## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functionality and Polymerizability

The **functionality (f)** of a monomer dictates the **topology** of the resulting polymer:

- If $f = 2$ (bifunctional, e.g., $\text{HO}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{OH}$): The polymer grows in **two directions only**, producing a **linear or branched** chain.
- If $f = 3$ (trifunctional, e.g., glycerol, phenol-formaldehyde resin precursor): The monomer introduces a **branching point**; multiple such monomers can join to form a **network (crosslinked)** polymer.
- If $f \geq 3$ and is present in significant proportion, a **three-dimensional network** is formed, leading to thermoset behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?**
> A linear polymer (e.g., HDPE) is **thermoplastic** — it can be remelted. A crosslinked network (e.g., vulcanized rubber, Bakelite) is **thermosetting** — once set, it cannot be remelted without decomposition. This single property difference originates from the **functionality** of the monomers.

### 2.2 Types of Molecular (Covalent) Structure

**1. Linear Polymers**
Monomers join end-to-end in a single continuous chain. No side branches. Example: **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene), PVC, Nylon 6,6**.

**2. Branched Polymers**
Side chains grow off the main backbone. Two sub-types:
- **Short-chain branches** (e.g., **LDPE** — Low-Density Polyethylene) — branches of 2–4 carbons.
- **Long-chain branches** (e.g., **LLDPE**) — branches of comparable length to the main chain.

**3. Crosslinked (Network) Polymers**
Adjacent chains are joined by covalent bonds. The polymer becomes a single giant molecule. Example: **Vulcanized rubber (sulfur crosslinks), phenolic resins, epoxies**.

**4. Interpenetrating Networks (IPNs)**
Two crosslinked networks physically interlocked but not covalently bonded to each other.

### 2.3 Isomerism in Polymers

Isomers are compounds with the **same molecular formula but different structural arrangement**. In polymers, two main types are critical:

#### A. Structural Isomerism
This arises from differences in the **covalent connectivity** of atoms.

| Sub-type | Definition | Example |
|:---------|:-----------|:--------|
| **Positional Isomerism** | Same substituent on different carbons of the repeat unit | Poly(vinyl alcohol) vs. poly(vinyl acetal) |
| **Geometric (cis–trans) Isomerism** | Restricted rotation around a C=C double bond in the backbone | cis-1,4-polyisoprene (natural rubber) vs. trans-1,4-polyisoprene (gutta-percha) |
| **Regioisomerism** | Different orientations of monomer addition (head-to-tail vs. head-to-head) | Head-to-tail PVC (dominant) vs. head-to-head PVC (defect) |

#### B. Stereo Isomerism (Stereoisomerism)
Same connectivity but different **3D spatial arrangement** of atoms.

- **Tacticity (in vinyl polymers, $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHR}-$):**
  - **Isotactic:** All R groups on the same side of the chain. Highly **crystalline**.
  - **Syndiotactic:** R groups alternate sides. Moderately crystalline.
  - **Atactic:** R groups random. Usually **amorphous**.

### 2.4 Intermolecular Forces in Polymers

The strength of secondary (intermolecular) forces determines mechanical and thermal properties:

| Force Type | Bond Energy (kJ/mol) | Example Polymer |
|:-----------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| Van der Waals (London dispersion) | $\mathbf{0.5 - 8}$ | Polyethylene, polyisobutylene |
| Dipole–Dipole | $\mathbf{5 - 25}$ | PVC, PMMA |
| Hydrogen Bonding | $\mathbf{10 - 40}$ | Nylon, poly(vinyl alcohol), cellulose |
| Ionic | $\mathbf{40 - 200}$ | Ionomers (e.g., Surlyn) |
| Covalent (in crosslinks) | $\mathbf{150 - 400}$ | Vulcanized rubber, epoxy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The melting temperature $T_m$ and glass transition temperature $T_g$ of a polymer are **strongly correlated** with the strength and density of intermolecular forces. Strong secondary forces (e.g., hydrogen bonding in Nylon 6,6) raise $T_m$ and improve mechanical strength.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------------|:------|:------|
| 1 | Degree of Polymerization (from $M_n$) | $\text{DP} = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit |
| 2 | Number-average Molecular Weight | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | g/mol | $N_i$ = number of chains of mass $M_i$ |
| 3 | Weight-average Molecular Weight | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | g/mol | Always $M_w \geq M_n$ |
| 4 | Polydispersity Index | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | dimensionless | PDI = 1 for monodisperse |
| 5 | Carothers Equation (extent) | $p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$ | dimensionless | $N_0$ = initial monomers, $N$ = remaining |
| 6 | Carothers DP (step polymerization) | $\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | dimensionless | For $r = 1$ (stoichiometric) |
| 7 | Carothers DP (non-stoichiometric) | $\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | dimensionless | $r = N_A/N_B \leq 1$ |
| 8 | End-group analysis | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\text{End-group equivalents} \times 1000}{\text{moles of polymer}}$ | g/mol | For linear chains |
| 9 | Functionality in network | $\text{Average } f = \dfrac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | dimensionless | Predicts gel point |
| 10 | Crystallinity fraction | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{sample}}}{\Delta H_{\text{100\% crystal}}} \times 100\%$ | % | From DSC |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Carothers Equation (Step-Growth Polymerization DP)

**Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction (p):**
The extent of reaction is defined as the fraction of functional groups that have reacted at time $t$.

$$p = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$$

where $N_0$ is the **initial number of functional groups** and $N$ is the **number of functional groups remaining** at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Count the number of molecules (N molecules):**
At any point, the number of unreacted functional groups is $N$. Since each molecule (chain) has **at least 2 unreacted end groups** (in a linear system), the total number of molecules is:

$$N_{\text{molecules}} = \frac{N}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Initial condition:**
At the start, the total number of unreacted functional groups is $2N_0$ (each monomer has 2 groups), so the number of monomer molecules initially is $N_0$.

**Step 4 — Define the degree of polymerization:**
The number-average degree of polymerization $\text{DP}_n$ is the **initial number of molecules** divided by the **number of molecules at time $t$**:

$$\text{DP}_n = \frac{N_0}{N_{\text{molecules}}} = \frac{N_0}{N/2} = \frac{2 N_0}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Express $N$ in terms of $N_0$ and $p$:**
From the definition in Step 1:

$$N = N_0(1 - p)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into DP equation:**

$$\text{DP}_n = \frac{2 N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{2}{1-p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 2 appears because each monomer has 2 functional groups. In the **more general derivation** that counts functional groups rather than monomers directly, the form is often written as:
> $$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$
> where $p$ is the fraction of functional groups reacted. This is the form typically used in textbooks for **stoichiometric step-growth** polymerization.

**Step 7 — Generalization for non-stoichiometric case:**
Let $r = \dfrac{N_A}{N_B} \leq 1$ be the ratio of functional groups. The total number of functional groups initially is $N_A + N_B$. The fraction $p$ now refers to the extent of reaction of the **minority** groups. Working through algebra:

$$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

**Step 8 — Limiting cases:**
- As $p \to 1$ and $r = 1$: $\text{DP}_n \to \infty$. This shows that in a perfect step-growth, you need **near-complete conversion** (> 99%) to obtain high molecular weight.
- For $r < 1$: $\text{DP}_n$ saturates at a finite maximum $\dfrac{1+r}{1-r}$ as $p \to 1$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Number-Average Molecular Weight

By definition of number average:

$$M_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i}$$

If we consider all repeat units are part of chains, and the total mass of polymer $W$ is conserved, then:

$$W = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i = N_0 M_0$$

where $M_0$ is the **molar mass of the repeat unit** and $N_0$ is the **initial number of monomers** (assuming complete incorporation).

The total number of molecules is (from Step 3 above):

$$N_{\text{total}} = \frac{N_0(1 - p)}{2} \quad \text{(for difunctional stoichiometric system)}$$

Therefore:

$$M_n = \frac{W}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{N_0 M_0}{N_0(1-p)/2} = \frac{2 M_0}{1 - p}$$

Equivalently:

$$M_n = \text{DP}_n \times M_0$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A linear polyester is formed from 1 mol of adipic acid and 1.001 mol of ethylene glycol. After 2 hours of reaction, the carboxylic acid value indicates that 95% of the COOH groups have reacted. Calculate the number-average degree of polymerization.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the stoichiometric ratio:
$$r = \frac{N_{\text{acid}}}{N_{\text{glycol}}} = \frac{1.000}{1.001} = 0.999$$

**Step 2:** Identify the extent of reaction:
$$p = 0.95$$

**Step 3:** Apply the generalized Carothers equation:

$$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1 + 0.999}{1 + 0.999 - 2(0.999)(0.95)}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the numerator:
$$1 + 0.999 = 1.999$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the denominator:
$$1 + 0.999 - 2(0.999)(0.95) = 1.999 - 1.8981 = 0.1009$$

**Step 6:** Compute the result:

$$\text{DP}_n = \frac{1.999}{0.1009} \approx 19.81$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Even a tiny imbalance ($r = 0.999$) and a 95% conversion gives a DP of only ~20. This is a **classic KTU board question** designed to show that **both stoichiometric balance AND high conversion** are essential for high molecular weight.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Structure vs. Property Correlation

| Structural Feature | Effect on $T_g$ | Effect on Crystallinity | Effect on Solubility | Effect on Mechanical Strength |
|:-------------------|:----------------|:------------------------|:---------------------|:------------------------------|
| Linear chain (no branches) | Reference baseline | High | Low (semicrystalline) | High (when crystalline) |
| Short-chain branches (LDPE) | Slight decrease | Low | Higher | Lower |
| Crosslinks | Slight increase | Prevents crystallization | Very low (swelling only) | Increases modulus, prevents flow |
| Strong H-bonding (Nylon) | Large increase | Moderate-High | Low | High |
| Aromatic backbone (PET, PS) | High | Variable (PS is amorphous) | Low | High rigidity |
| Isotactic configuration | Increases $T_m$ | High | Low | High |
| Atactic configuration | Decreases $T_m$ | Very low | High (amorphous) | Low to moderate |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECHT523]**
**Define functionality of a monomer. How does the functionality of monomers determine the structure of a polymer?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Functionality (f)** is defined as the **number of reactive functional groups** (active bonds) in a monomer molecule that can participate in polymerization.

The functionality determines the polymer architecture as follows:

- **f = 2 (bifunctional, e.g., $\text{HOOC}-\text{R}-\text{COOH}$):** Produces **linear or branched** polymers. The chain grows only in two directions.
- **f = 3 (trifunctional, e.g., glycerol, $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}$):** Introduces **branching points**; with sufficient concentration, leads to **crosslinked network** polymers.
- **f ≥ 3** in high proportion: Produces **three-dimensional thermoset networks** (e.g., phenol-formaldehyde resin, epoxies).

Hence, functionality is the **molecular-level control parameter** that dictates whether a polymer is thermoplastic, elastomeric, or thermosetting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT523]**
**Distinguish between configuration and conformation in polymers with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Configuration | Conformation |
|:---------|:--------------|:-------------|
| Definition | Spatial arrangement of atoms that **cannot be changed without breaking covalent bonds** | Spatial arrangement that **can be changed by rotation about single bonds** |
| Stability | Fixed once polymer is synthesized | Changes with temperature, solvent, stress |
| Energy barrier | High ($\sim 100$ kJ/mol) | Low ($\sim 12$ kJ/mol per bond) |
| Example | **Tacticity** (isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic) in polypropylene; cis vs. trans in 1,4-polyisoprene | Random coil in amorphous polymer; zig-zag in crystalline PE; helix in isotactic PP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**C**onfiguration = **C**ovalent bonds must break" ; "**C**onformation = **C**an be rotated freely."

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following Question A or Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, PECHT523]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of **molecular (covalent) structure of polymers** with suitable examples. Discuss how the structure affects the physical and mechanical properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The molecular structure of a polymer describes the **covalent connectivity** of the repeat units in a polymer chain. There are **four major types**:

**1. Linear Polymers (7 marks content for sub-part a):**
In linear polymers, the monomers are joined end-to-end in a single continuous chain without any branches. The polymer molecules can pack closely together, leading to high intermolecular forces, high density, and high crystallinity. Example: **High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)**, polyvinyl chloride (PVC), Nylon 6,6. These polymers are **thermoplastic** — they can be melted and reprocessed.

**2. Branched Polymers:**
Branched polymers contain side chains of varying length growing off the main backbone. The branches prevent close packing, reduce crystallinity, and lower density.
- **Short-chain branches** (e.g., LDPE): Branches of 2–4 carbons; reduces crystallinity; lower density than HDPE.
- **Long-chain branches** (e.g., LLDPE): Branches of similar length to the main chain; intermediate properties.

**3. Crosslinked (Network) Polymers:**
Adjacent polymer chains are joined by covalent bonds (crosslinks), typically formed during vulcanization or curing. The result is a single **giant macromolecule** with three-dimensional network architecture. Example: **vulcanized rubber** (sulfur crosslinks between polyisoprene chains), phenol-formaldehyde resin, epoxies. These are **thermosets** — they cannot be remelted. Crosslinking **eliminates crystallinity**, increases elastic modulus, and prevents viscous flow.

**4. Interpenetrating Networks (IPNs):**
Two separately crosslinked networks are physically interlocked (not covalently bonded to each other). Example: Polyurethane–epoxy IPNs used in damping materials.

**Effect on Physical and Mechanical Properties:**

| Structure | Density | Crystallinity | $T_m$ | Mechanical Behaviour |
|:----------|:--------|:--------------|:------|:---------------------|
| Linear | High | High | High | Strong, rigid, semicrystalline |
| Branched | Low | Low | Lower | Softer, more flexible |
| Crosslinked | Variable | Amorphous | None (does not melt) | Elastic (rubbery) or rigid (thermoset) |

> **Key principle:** Crosslink density is the **master variable** controlling the transition from rubber-like to rigid thermoset behaviour.

---

**(b)** What is **tacticity**? With neat sketches, explain the different types of tacticities in vinyl polymers. Discuss the influence of tacticity on crystallinity and mechanical properties, with reference to polypropylene as an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Tacticity is the **stereoregular arrangement** of the pendant substituent (R group) on the chiral carbon of a vinyl polymer repeat unit of the form $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHR}-$. Tacticity is a **configuration** property — it cannot be altered without breaking covalent bonds.

**Types of Tacticity (with neat sketches):**

- **Isotactic:** All R groups are on the **same side** of the polymer backbone plane. The chain has a regular, helical conformation. The structure is highly regular, so chains pack efficiently.
  $$\text{R—CH}_2\text{—CHR—CH}_2\text{—CHR—}$$

- **Syndiotactic:** R groups **alternate** between two sides of the backbone plane. The structure is also regular and can crystallize, though often with different morphology than isotactic.
  $$\text{R—CH}_2\text{—CHR—CH}_2\text{—CHR— (alternating)}$$

- **Atactic:** R groups are placed **randomly** on both sides. The irregular structure prevents efficient chain packing.
  $$\text{Random placement — no regular pattern}$$

**Influence on Properties (Reference: Polypropylene, PP):**

| Tacticity | Crystallinity | Density (g/cm³) | $T_m$ (°C) | Application |
|:----------|:--------------|:----------------|:-----------|:------------|
| Isotactic PP (iPP) | High (50–70%) | 0.90–0.91 | $\sim 165$ | Fibres, moulded parts |
| Syndiotactic PP (sPP) | Moderate | 0.88 | $\sim 130$ | Specialty films |
| Atactic PP (aPP) | Amorphous | 0.85 | Soft tacky solid | Adhesives, sealants |

> **Conclusion:** **Stereoregular catalysts** (Ziegler–Natta and metallocene catalysts) revolutionized polymer technology because they allowed the synthesis of **isotactic** and **syndiotactic** polymers, opening the door to high-strength, high-melting engineering plastics that were impossible with the older free-radical polymerization methods.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, PECHT523]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **intermolecular forces** present in polymers. How do these forces influence the physical, thermal, and mechanical properties of polymers? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Intermolecular forces in polymers are **secondary bonds** (non-covalent) that act between adjacent chains. Although individually weak, their cumulative effect over a long chain determines the **bulk properties** of the polymer.

**1. Van der Waals (London Dispersion) Forces:**
Present in **all** polymers, but dominant in non-polar ones (polyethylene, polyisobutylene). Energy: **0.5–8 kJ/mol**. These are weak, instantaneous dipole-induced dipole attractions. In HDPE, the regular chain packing allows many of these weak forces to act cooperatively, producing a strong, semicrystalline material.

**2. Dipole–Dipole Interactions:**
Present in **polar polymers** (PVC, PMMA, polyacrylonitrile). The permanent dipoles on adjacent chains align partially, creating additional attractive force. Energy: **5–25 kJ/mol**. This is why PVC is **stronger and stiffer** than polyethylene, despite both having a $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHX}-$ backbone.

**3. Hydrogen Bonding:**
A particularly strong dipole–dipole interaction involving H bonded to F, O, or N. Energy: **10–40 kJ/mol**. Present in Nylon, poly(vinyl alcohol), cellulose, proteins. Nylon 6,6 has strong H-bonds between amide groups, raising $T_m$ to $\sim 265\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and giving high tensile strength.

**4. Ionic Interactions:**
Present in **ionomers** (e.g., Surlyn, a copolymer of ethylene with methacrylic acid, partially neutralized with $\text{Na}^+$ or $\text{Zn}^{2+}$). Energy: **40–200 kJ/mol**. The ionic clusters act as physical crosslinks, giving high transparency and toughness.

**5. Covalent Crosslinks:**
Not strictly intermolecular, but intermolecular in effect. Energy: **150–400 kJ/mol**. Vulcanized rubber, epoxies, phenolics. The crosslinks prevent chain slippage and impart **elastic recovery** (rubber) or **rigidity** (thermoset).

**Influence on Properties:**

- **$T_g$ and $T_m$:** Increase with stronger intermolecular forces (Nylon > PVC > PE).
- **Crystallinity:** Strong, regular forces promote crystallinity (HDPE, Nylon).
- **Mechanical Strength:** Higher secondary forces → higher tensile strength and modulus.
- **Solubility:** Strong forces → low solubility; the solvent must overcome secondary bonds (Nylon dissolves only in strong acids or phenols).
- **Viscosity of melt:** Stronger forces → higher melt viscosity, harder to process.

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of **isomerism in polymers**. Discuss **structural isomerism** (positional, geometric) and **stereoisomerism** (tacticity) with one example for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Isomerism:**
Isomers are compounds with the **same molecular formula** but **different structural or spatial arrangement** of atoms. In polymers, isomerism arises at the repeat-unit level and profoundly affects properties.

**A. Structural (Constitutional) Isomerism:**

**1. Positional Isomerism:**
The same substituent group occupies **different positions** on the polymer backbone.
*Example:* In poly(vinyl chloride), chlorine can be on every carbon (normal head-to-tail: $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHCl}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHCl}-$) or on every alternate carbon (hypothetical 1,3- or 1,1-arrangement). The properties differ significantly.

**2. Geometric (cis–trans) Isomerism:**
Restricted rotation around the **C=C double bond** in the polymer backbone forces substituents to be on the same side (cis) or opposite sides (trans).
*Example:* **1,4-Polyisoprene** exists in two natural forms:
- **cis-1,4-polyisoprene (Natural Rubber):** Elastic, soft, $T_g \approx -73\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- **trans-1,4-polyisoprene (Gutta-Percha):** Hard, crystalline, $T_m \approx 65\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

This single difference in geometric configuration transforms a soft **elastomer** into a hard, rigid plastic.

**3. Regioisomerism (Head-to-Tail vs. Head-to-Head):**
Monomers can link in different orientations. In free-radical polymerization of styrene, ~99% head-to-tail; the small fraction of head-to-head linkages are **structural defects** that reduce crystallinity.

**B. Stereoisomerism (Stereoregular Isomerism):**

**Tacticity** (discussed in detail in Question A above):
- **Isotactic PP** — high crystallinity, $T_m \approx 165\ ^\circ\text{C}$, strong fibres.
- **Syndiotactic PP** — moderate crystallinity.
- **Atactic PP** — amorphous, soft, used in adhesives.

> **Synthesis control:** **Ziegler–Natta catalysts** and **metallocene catalysts** permit stereospecific polymerization, producing predominantly isotactic or syndiotactic polymers — a breakthrough of the 1950s that won Karl Ziegler and Giulio Natta the **Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1963)**.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in 'Structure of Polymers' questions:**
> 1. **Confusing configuration and conformation** — this is the **#1 KTU marking trap**. Configuration requires bond breaking; conformation does not. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting head-to-tail vs. head-to-head** as a form of structural isomerism — most students mention tacticity but skip regioisomerism.
> 3. **No neat diagrams** — in 7-mark questions, KTU examiners expect **at least 2–3 schematic sketches** (linear, branched, crosslinked; or isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic). A text-only answer caps at ~5/7 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up functionality with degree of polymerization** — functionality is a monomer property; DP is a polymer property.
> 5. **Missing numerical substitution** in Carothers' equation — students often quote the formula but fail to substitute $r$ and $p$ properly in numerical problems.
> 6. **Writing "crosslinked = thermoset"** without explaining the molecular reason — always state that crosslinks prevent chain slippage, hence no flow on heating.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Structure of Polymers**
> - [ ] **Functionality (f)** = number of reactive groups per monomer; f = 2 → linear, f ≥ 3 → network.
> - [ ] **Molecular structures:** Linear, branched (short/long), crosslinked, IPN.
> - [ ] **Configuration** (requires bond breaking): Tacticity, cis-trans, head-tail.
> - [ ] **Conformation** (bond rotation): Random coil, zig-zag, helix.
> - [ ] **Tacticity:** Isotactic (same side, high crystallinity), Syndiotactic (alternating), Atactic (random, amorphous).
> - [ ] **Cis-trans isomerism:** cis-1,4-polyisoprene = natural rubber; trans = gutta-percha.
> - [ ] **Intermolecular forces order (strength):** Van der Waals < Dipole < H-bonding < Ionic < Covalent crosslink.
> - [ ] **Stronger secondary forces → higher $T_g$, $T_m$, tensile strength; lower solubility.**
> - [ ] **Carothers' DP equations:** $\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (stoichiometric) and $\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ (non-stoichiometric).
> - [ ] **Step-growth polymerization** requires very high conversion (>99%) for high molecular weight.
> - [ ] **Stereoregular polymerization** (Ziegler–Natta / metallocene) enabled isotactic PP and the modern plastics industry.
> - [ ] **Molecular weight averages:** $M_n$, $M_w$, and PDI = $M_w/M_n$ (PDI = 1 for monodisperse).
> - [ ] **Crosslinks transform** a thermoplastic (linear) into a thermoset (network) — single most important structural concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Polymer Applications

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PECHT523 – Polymer Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1)**, the topic *Application* refers to the systematic study of how polymeric materials are selected, processed, and utilized across engineering, biomedical, electronic, packaging, automotive, and aerospace industries based on their **physical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, and rheological property profiles**. The study encompasses the mapping of **structure–property–processing–performance (SPPP)** relationships to specific end-use requirements, enabling rational material selection for engineering design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Applications):**
> Selection of polymers for engineering applications is governed by the **4-pillar framework**: (1) Polymer type (thermoplastic, thermoset, elastomer), (2) Molecular architecture (linear, branched, crosslinked, network), (3) Reinforcement/filler system (composites, blends, alloys), and (4) Processing route (injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, compression molding, 3D printing, casting). Mastery of this framework is essential for 14-mark application questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy – "The Polymer Toolbox"**
> Think of polymers as a **Swiss Army Knife** in an engineer's toolbox. Just as a Swiss Army Knife has different blades (knife, screwdriver, scissors, corkscrew) for different tasks, different polymers serve different functions:
> - **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** = The strong bucket (milk cans, chemical tanks)
> - **PTFE (Teflon)** = The non-stick pan (cookware, gaskets)
> - **PMMA (Plexiglas/Acrylic)** = The transparent window (aircraft canopies, aquariums)
> - **Natural Rubber** = The elastic band (tyres, vibration dampers)
> - **Epoxy** = The structural glue (aerospace composites, electronics encapsulation)
> Just as you would not use a corkscrew to tighten a screw, an engineer must select the **right polymer for the right application** based on the working conditions (temperature, load, chemical environment, electrical stress, etc.).

## 1.3 Key Classification of Polymer Applications (KTU Standard)

Polymer applications are typically classified into the following **major engineering domains** (as per KTU Module 1 syllabus):

| **SI. No.** | **Application Domain** | **Representative Polymers** | **Critical Property Demanded** |
|-------------|------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Packaging | LDPE, HDPE, PP, PET, PVC | Clarity, flexibility, barrier properties |
| 2 | Automotive | PP, ABS, Nylon-6,6, PBT, PC | Impact strength, heat resistance |
| 3 | Aerospace | Epoxy-Carbon Fiber, PEEK, BMI | Strength-to-weight ratio, Tg |
| 4 | Electrical & Electronics | PVC, PE, PTFE, Epoxy, Silicone | Dielectric strength, insulation |
| 5 | Biomedical | PMMA, UHMWPE, PDMS, PVA, PLA | Biocompatibility, sterilizability |
| 6 | Construction | PVC, UPVC, HDPE, Epoxy | Weatherability, mechanical strength |
| 7 | Textile & Fibers | PET, Nylon, Acrylic, PP | Tensile strength, dyeability |
| 8 | Adhesives & Coatings | Epoxy, Phenolic, Acrylic, PU | Adhesion, chemical resistance |
| 9 | Agriculture | LDPE, LLDPE (mulch films) | UV stability, cost |
| 10 | Defence & Specialty | Aramid (Kevlar), UHMWPE (Dyneema) | Ballistic protection, ballistic limit |

## 1.4 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Outcomes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Definitions (Memorize for 3-Mark Questions):**
> - **Engineering Polymer:** A polymer whose properties (mechanical, thermal, chemical) make it suitable for structural or functional load-bearing applications.
> - **Commodity Polymer:** A high-volume, low-cost polymer used in everyday applications (PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET).
> - **High-Performance Polymer:** A polymer exhibiting superior thermal stability (T_g > 150 °C), mechanical strength, and chemical resistance (PEEK, PI, PPS, PAI, PSU).
> - **Functional Polymer:** A polymer designed for a specific non-structural function such as conductivity, optical activity, or biological response.
> - **Smart/Intelligent Polymer:** A polymer that responds reversibly to external stimuli (pH, temperature, light, electric/magnetic field).
> - **Biodegradable Polymer:** A polymer that decomposes into CO₂, H₂O, and biomass under microbial action (PLA, PHB, PCL, PVA).

## 1.5 Polymer Selection: The 5-Sieve Model

Every engineering application requires the candidate polymer to pass through **5 sieves** (this is the *de facto* exam-answer framework):

1. **Sieve 1 – Thermal Sieve:** Glass transition temperature (T_g) and melting temperature (T_m) must exceed the service temperature.
2. **Sieve 2 – Mechanical Sieve:** Tensile strength, modulus, impact strength, and fatigue resistance must meet design load.
3. **Sieve 3 – Chemical Sieve:** Resistance to solvents, acids, alkalis, UV, and oxidation at service conditions.
4. **Sieve 4 – Processing Sieve:** Polymer must be processable by an economically viable route.
5. **Sieve 5 – Economic & Regulatory Sieve:** Cost, recyclability, FDA approval, REACH compliance, and environmental impact.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Structure → Property → Application

The cornerstone of polymer application engineering is the **SPPP chain** (Structure–Property–Processing–Performance). For the KTU 2024 Scheme, every 14-mark question on applications will implicitly test whether you can trace an application back to its structural origin.

### Step-by-Step Logic Chain (Bulleted Breakdown)

- **Step 1 – Molecular Structure Determination**
  - The **repeat unit** defines the chemical identity.
  - **Tacticity** (isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic) governs crystallinity and therefore mechanical/optical properties.
  - Example: Isotactic PP is semi-crystalline and used for containers; atactic PP is amorphous and used only as an adhesive.

- **Step 2 – Molecular Weight & Distribution**
  - Number-average molecular weight (M_n) → End-group analysis, colligative properties.
  - Weight-average molecular weight (M_w) → Light scattering, mechanical strength.
  - Polydispersity Index (PDI = M_w / M_n) → Breadth of molecular weight distribution.
  - High M_w → Higher tensile strength, viscosity, impact resistance.
  - Narrow PDI → Predictable processing and mechanical performance.

- **Step 3 – Morphology & Crystallinity**
  - % Crystallinity (% X_c) governs stiffness, opacity, density, and chemical resistance.
  - Crystalline polymers (HDPE, PP, Nylon, PET) → Used for fibers, containers, gears.
  - Amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA, PC, ABS) → Used for transparent parts, impact goods.

- **Step 4 – Glass Transition (T_g) & Melt Temperature (T_m)**
  - Below T_g → Glassy, rigid, brittle.
  - Above T_g (but below T_m for semi-crystalline) → Rubbery, tough.
  - Above T_m → Viscous flow (processable state).
  - Rule of thumb: T_g ≈ (1/2 to 2/3) × T_m (in Kelvin) for symmetric polymers.

- **Step 5 – Additives & Compounding**
  - Plasticizers → Increase flexibility (PVC → flexible films).
  - Fillers → Increase stiffness, reduce cost (CaCO₃, talc, glass fiber).
  - Stabilizers → UV, thermal, antioxidant protection.
  - Flame retardants → Critical for electronics and construction.
  - Reinforcements → Carbon fiber, glass fiber, Kevlar for composites.

- **Step 6 – Processing Route Selection**
  - Injection molding → Complex 3D parts (ABS, PP, Nylon).
  - Extrusion → Continuous profiles (pipes, sheets, films).
  - Blow molding → Hollow parts (PET bottles, HDPE tanks).
  - Compression molding → Thermosets (Phenolic, Melamine, BMC).
  - 3D printing (FDM, SLA) → Prototyping and custom parts.
  - Thermoforming → PS, PET, PVC sheets for trays, blisters.

- **Step 7 – End-Use Performance Validation**
  - Creep resistance, fatigue life, weathering, hydrolytic stability.
  - ASTM/ISO standard testing (ASTM D638 for tensile, D790 for flexural, D256 for Izod impact).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> Although the topic is "Application," the KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the following **molecular-weight and property-correlation equations** as 3-mark sub-parts inside 14-mark application questions.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Application Context** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------------|
| $\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | End-group titration, osmometry |
| $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | Light scattering, sedimentation |
| $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$ | Polydispersity Index | Monodisperse polymer: PDI = 1.0 |
| $DP_n = \dfrac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$ | Degree of polymerization (number-average) | Cellulose (DP ~ 1000), PE (DP ~ 10⁴–10⁵) |
| $\% X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | Crystallinity (density method) | HDPE: ~70–80%, LDPE: ~40–50% |
| $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (K) | Empirical Bueche rule | For symmetric, linear polymers |
| $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{\ln(\eta_r)}{c}$ | Inherent viscosity | Intrinsic viscosity via extrapolation |
| $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Mark-Houwink-Sakurada equation | MWD determination by viscometry |
| $\sigma_t = \sigma_{t,0} \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$ | Strength build-up with cure time | Thermoset cure kinetics |
| $E \propto \rho \cdot T_g$ (qualitative) | Modulus–density–T_g correlation | Stiff polymer selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars, modulus bars, and conditional bars in formula text have been replaced with the LaTeX-safe command `\vert` or descriptive words like "modulus" / "absolute value" to prevent markdown table corruption, in accordance with the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10** rendering protocol.

## 2.3 Application-Driven Property Matrix (Engineering Decision Tool)

This matrix is the **single most important analytical tool** for KTU 14-mark application questions. Memorize and reproduce it cleanly.

| **Property Required** | **Best Polymer Family** | **Typical Use Case** |
|-----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Highest T_g, dimensional stability | Polyimide (PI), PEEK | Aerospace bushings, jet engine parts |
| Optical clarity | PMMA, PC, PS | Lenses, aircraft canopies, displays |
| Chemical resistance to acids/alkalis | PTFE, PVDF, HDPE | Chemical tanks, pipe linings |
| Lowest coefficient of friction | PTFE, UHMWPE | Bearings, slide plates, artificial joints |
| Highest impact strength | ABS, PC, UHMWPE | Helmets, phone cases, knee implants |
| Electrical insulation | PE, XLPE, PTFE, Epoxy | Cable insulation, PCB substrates |
| Biocompatibility | UHMWPE, PMMA, PDMS, PLA, PEEK | Bone cements, contact lenses, scaffolds |
| High tensile strength (fiber) | Kevlar (Aramid), UHMWPE (Dyneema), Carbon Fiber | Bulletproof vests, ropes, composites |
| Food contact safety | PP, HDPE, PET, LDPE | Bottles, containers, cling films |
| Flame retardancy | Phenolic, Melamine, PVC, PEEK | Switchboards, aircraft interiors |
| Elastic recovery (rubbery) | Natural Rubber, SBR, EPDM, Silicone | Tyres, gaskets, seals, medical tubes |
| 3D printing (FDM) | PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon | Prototyping, dental models, jigs |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Polymer composites have reduced vehicle weight by 30–40%, improving fuel efficiency (e.g., BMW i-series, Tesla Model 3 use extensive polymer-carbon fiber body panels).
- **Aerospace:** Boeing 787 Dreamliner is 50% composite material by weight, with epoxy-carbon fiber dominating the fuselage and wings.
- **Biomedical:** UHMWPE hip liners have wear rates < 0.1 mm/year, lasting 20+ years in vivo.
- **Renewable Energy:** Epoxy-encapsulated photovoltaic (PV) modules have lifetimes > 25 years, with EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate) as the encapsulant.
- **Electronics:** PTFE and LCP (Liquid Crystal Polymer) enable 5G high-frequency circuit boards with Dk < 3.0.
- **3D Printing:** The global polymer 3D printing market exceeded **$15 billion (2024)**, driven by PLA, ABS, and photopolymer resins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 3 Objective:** Provide *exhaustive* derivations and worked numerical examples for the formulas listed in Section 2, ensuring KTU 2024 Scheme 14-mark questions can be solved in full.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Polymerization from Molecular Weight

### Problem Setup
A sample of **polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** has a number-average molecular weight of **62,500 g/mol**. The repeat unit of PVC is –CH₂–CHCl–. Calculate:
1. The molecular weight of the repeat unit (M₀).
2. The number-average degree of polymerization (DP_n).
3. Comment on whether this PVC is suitable for **rigid pipe applications**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify atomic composition of repeat unit**

The repeat unit of PVC is:
$$-\text{CH}_2 - \text{CHCl} -$$

Atomic masses (standard, g/mol):
- C = 12.011
- H = 1.008
- Cl = 35.453

**Step 2 — Calculate M₀**

$$M_0 = 2 \times M_C + 3 \times M_H + 1 \times M_{Cl}$$

Substituting:

$$M_0 = 2(12.011) + 3(1.008) + 1(35.453)$$

$$M_0 = 24.022 + 3.024 + 35.453$$

$$M_0 = 62.499 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute DP_n**

$$\overline{DP_n} = \dfrac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$$

$$\overline{DP_n} = \dfrac{62{,}500}{62.499}$$

$$\overline{DP_n} \approx 1000$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation**

A DP_n of ~1000 corresponds to a **medium-to-high molecular weight PVC** (K-value 57–65, per ISO 1628-2 classification). This grade possesses:
- Tensile strength ≈ 50–60 MPa
- Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT) ≈ 65–70 °C
- Sufficient impact strength for **rigid pressure pipe applications** (ASTM D1785, Class 1).

> **Conclusion:** Yes, this PVC grade is suitable for **rigid pipe applications** (e.g., cold-water plumbing, drainage). For hot-water pipes (CPVC), chlorination is required to raise HDT to ~100 °C.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Polydispersity Index from SEC Data (Size Exclusion Chromatography)

### Problem Setup
A polymer sample analyzed by size exclusion chromatography (SEC) shows three fractions with the following data:

| **Fraction** | **N_i (mol fraction)** | **M_i (g/mol)** |
|--------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 0.20 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 0.30 | 100,000 |

Calculate **M_n**, **M_w**, and **PDI**. Comment on the **processing behavior**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute M_n**

$$\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i = (0.20)(10{,}000) + (0.50)(50{,}000) + (0.30)(100{,}000)$$

$$= 2{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 57{,}000$$

$$\sum N_i = 0.20 + 0.50 + 0.30 = 1.00$$

$$\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{57{,}000}{1.00} = 57{,}000 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute M_w**

$$\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^2 = (0.20)(10{,}000)^2 + (0.50)(50{,}000)^2 + (0.30)(100{,}000)^2$$

$$= (0.20)(1.0 \times 10^8) + (0.50)(2.5 \times 10^9) + (0.30)(1.0 \times 10^{10})$$

$$= 2.0 \times 10^7 + 1.25 \times 10^9 + 3.0 \times 10^9$$

$$= 4.45 \times 10^9$$

$$\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{4.45 \times 10^9}{57{,}000} = 78{,}070 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute PDI**

$$PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \dfrac{78{,}070}{57{,}000} = 1.37$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation**

- A PDI of **1.37** indicates a **moderately broad molecular weight distribution**.
- Such a polymer exhibits:
  - Good **melt strength** (suitable for blow molding, thermoforming).
  - Moderate **flow** (acceptable for injection molding, but may show weld-line weakness).
  - Good **processability** with a wide processing window.
- A PDI > 2.0 would suggest poor mechanical reproducibility; PDI = 1.0 is monodisperse (only achievable in anionic living polymerization).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Crystallinity from Density Measurement

### Problem Setup
A sample of **HDPE** has the following measured data:
- Density of sample (ρ_s) = 0.952 g/cm³
- Density of fully crystalline HDPE (ρ_c) = 1.004 g/cm³
- Density of fully amorphous HDPE (ρ_a) = 0.853 g/cm³

Calculate the **% crystallinity** and predict the **primary application** of this HDPE grade.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the density-based crystallinity equation**

$$\% X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$\% X_c = \dfrac{1.004 \times (0.952 - 0.853)}{0.952 \times (1.004 - 0.853)} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.004 \times 0.099 = 0.0994$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator**

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.952 \times 0.151 = 0.1438$$

**Step 5 — Compute crystallinity**

$$\% X_c = \dfrac{0.0994}{0.1438} \times 100 \approx 69.1\%$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation**

A crystallinity of **~69%** classifies this as **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)**, Type II per ASTM D1248. Suitable applications:
- **Blow-molded containers** (chemical tanks, fuel tanks).
- **Pressure pipes** (PE-100 grade for water/gas distribution, SDR 11/17).
- **Injection-molded crates** and industrial parts.
- **Geomembranes** for landfill liners (high chemical resistance).

> [!NOTE]
> For comparison: LDPE has %X_c ≈ 40–50% (ρ_s = 0.910–0.925 g/cm³), and LLDPE has %X_c ≈ 50–60%. Higher crystallinity → higher density, stiffness, chemical resistance, and lower permeability.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mark–Houwink–Sakurada Viscosity Equation Application

### Problem Setup
The intrinsic viscosity of a polymer solution is measured at **25 °C** in toluene. Using the Mark–Houwink parameters:
- K = 1.7 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g
- a = 0.69

If the intrinsic viscosity [η] = 1.20 dL/g, calculate the **viscosity-average molecular weight (M_v)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Mark–Houwink equation**

$$[\eta] = K M_v^a$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for M_v**

$$M_v = \left(\dfrac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/a}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

$$M_v = \left(\dfrac{1.20}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.69}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio inside the parenthesis**

$$\dfrac{1.20}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}} = 7{,}058.8$$

**Step 5 — Apply the exponent**

$$M_v = (7{,}058.8)^{1/0.69} = (7{,}058.8)^{1.4493}$$

$$M_v \approx 7.06 \times 10^{4.32} \approx 7.06 \times 20{,}893$$

$$M_v \approx 1.47 \times 10^{5} \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation**

A viscosity-average M_v of **~1.47 × 10⁵ g/mol** indicates a high-molecular-weight polymer suitable for **film extrusion, blow molding, and fiber spinning** (high melt strength, good mechanical properties).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Polymer Selection for a Hot-Water Pipe

### Problem
A chemical plant requires a polymer for a **hot-water transport pipe (continuous service at 90 °C, 6 bar pressure, exposure to mild acids)**. Recommend the best polymer with full justification.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the 5-Sieve Selection Model**

| **Sieve** | **Requirement** | **Best Candidate** | **Why** |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|---------|
| Thermal | T_g > 90 °C, continuous use temp > 90 °C | **CPVC** (T_g ~ 115 °C) or **PP-R** (T_m ~ 165 °C) | CPVC is chlorinated PVC; PP-R is random copolymer PP |
| Mechanical | Pressure rating ≥ 6 bar, hydrostatic strength | **PE-100 HDPE** or **CPVC** | PE-100 has MRS = 10 MPa |
| Chemical | Acid resistance (HCl, H₂SO₄ dilute) | **CPVC**, **PVDF**, **PP** | CPVC: excellent acid resistance up to 80 °C |
| Processing | Extrudable into pipes | **PVC/CPVC extrusion**, **PP-R extrusion** | Standard thermoplastic processing |
| Economic | Cost-effective, long service life | **CPVC** (best balance) | Lower cost than PVDF, better than PE for hot water |

**Step 2 — Final Selection: CPVC (Chlorinated Polyvinyl Chloride)**
- Continuous service temperature: 90–100 °C
- Pressure rating: 6–10 bar at 90 °C
- Acid/alkali resistance: Excellent (excellent chlorinated backbone)
- Standards: ASTM F441, IS 15778
- Application: Industrial hot water lines, chemical handling, fire sprinkler systems

> **Final Answer:** **CPVC** is the recommended polymer for this application.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table: Application-to-Property Mapping

| **Application** | **T_g (°C)** | **Crystallinity** | **Key Polymer(s)** | **Critical Property** |
|-----------------|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| Hot-water pipe | > 100 | Amorphous | CPVC, PP-R | Heat deflection, pressure |
| Bulletproof vest | > 250 | Highly crystalline | Kevlar, Dyneema | Energy absorption, tensile modulus |
| Artificial hip joint | > 130 | Semi-crystalline | UHMWPE | Wear resistance, biocompatibility |
| Aircraft canopy | > 100 | Amorphous | PMMA, PC | Optical clarity, impact |
| Food packaging film | < 0 (flexible) | Semi-crystalline | LDPE, LLDPE | Flexibility, sealability |
| 5G circuit board | > 200 (high freq.) | Highly crystalline | PTFE, LCP | Low dielectric loss |
| 3D printing filament | > 60 | Amorphous/semi | PLA, ABS, PETG | Low warpage, dimensional stability |
| Tyre tread | < –50 (rubbery) | Amorphous | SBR, BR, NR | Elastic recovery, abrasion |
| Solar panel encapsulant | –30 to 90 | Amorphous | EVA, POE | UV stability, adhesion |
| Chemical tank liner | > 100 | Semi-crystalline | HDPE, PP | Chemical resistance, weldability |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define the term "engineering polymer" and give two examples with one typical application for each.

**Model Answer:**
An engineering polymer is a polymeric material that exhibits superior mechanical, thermal, chemical, and dimensional stability properties compared to commodity polymers, making it suitable for **load-bearing and functional structural applications** where metals were traditionally used.

**Examples:**
1. **Polyamide (Nylon 6,6):** Used for automotive under-the-hood components, gears, and bearings (high strength, heat resistance, low friction).
2. **Polycarbonate (PC):** Used for aircraft canopies, safety helmets, and electronic housings (high impact strength, optical clarity, T_g ≈ 150 °C).

> *(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – example 1, 1 mark – example 2)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between commodity polymers and high-performance polymers. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Commodity Polymer** | **High-Performance Polymer** |
|---------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | High-volume, low-cost, general-purpose | Specialty, high-cost, engineered for extreme service |
| T_g / T_m | Low to moderate (typically < 150 °C) | High (typically > 150 °C) |
| Mechanical strength | Moderate | Very high |
| Cost | $1–3/kg | $30–500/kg |
| Production volume | Millions of tons/year | Hundreds to thousands of tons/year |
| Example | Polyethylene (PE) — packaging films, bottles | Polyetheretherketone (PEEK) — aerospace bearings |

> *(1 mark – commodity definition + example, 1 mark – high-perf definition + example, 1 mark – differentiation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Question A]**

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Structure–Property–Processing–Performance (SPPP) chain** in polymer applications. With a neat flowchart, describe how the molecular structure of polyethylene influences its end-use applications. Compare LDPE, LLDPE, and HDPE in terms of density, crystallinity, and two specific applications of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polymer sample has the following SEC data:

| Fraction | N_i (mol fraction) | M_i (g/mol) |
|----------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | 0.30 | 20,000 |
| 2 | 0.45 | 80,000 |
| 3 | 0.25 | 200,000 |

Calculate (i) number-average molecular weight (M_n), (ii) weight-average molecular weight (M_w), and (iii) polydispersity index (PDI). Comment on the processing behavior of this polymer and recommend two applications.

---

### Model Answer — Part (a): SPPP Chain & Polyethylene Comparison

**Step 1 — Define the SPPP Chain [2 marks]**
The SPPP chain establishes the causal relationship:
- **Structure** (repeat unit, molecular weight, branching, tacticity)
- → **Properties** (T_g, T_m, crystallinity, modulus, strength)
- → **Processing** (injection, extrusion, blow, thermoforming)
- → **Performance** (in-service mechanical, thermal, chemical behavior)
- → **Application** (specific industrial use)

**Step 2 — Polyethylene: Molecular Influence [2 marks]**
- Repeat unit: –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–
- Branching (short-chain and long-chain) reduces crystallinity.
- Linear chains pack tightly → higher crystallinity → higher density.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table [3 marks]**

| **Property** | **LDPE** | **LLDPE** | **HDPE** |
|--------------|----------|-----------|----------|
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.910–0.925 | 0.918–0.940 | 0.941–0.965 |
| Branching | Long, frequent | Short, uniform | None (linear) |
| Crystallinity (%) | 40–50 | 50–60 | 70–80 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 10–20 | 15–25 | 25–40 |
| T_m (°C) | 105–115 | 120–125 | 130–135 |
| Application 1 | Squeeze bottles, films | Stretch wrap films | Chemical containers, pipes |
| Application 2 | Cable insulation, liners | Agricultural mulch films | Geomembranes, fuel tanks |

**Flowchart (text representation for answer script):**

```
Repeat unit –CH₂–CH₂–
       ↓
Branching (Short or Long) → affects Crystallinity → affects Density
       ↓                                              ↓
LDPE (high branching) ← LLDPE (uniform short) ← HDPE (linear, no branching)
       ↓                                              ↓
Soft, flexible films                   Stiff, strong containers/pipes
```

---

### Model Answer — Part (b): SEC Numerical Computation

**Step 1 — Calculate Σ(N_i) [0.5 mark]**
$$\sum N_i = 0.30 + 0.45 + 0.25 = 1.00$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Σ(N_i × M_i) [1 mark]**
$$\sum N_i M_i = (0.30)(20{,}000) + (0.45)(80{,}000) + (0.25)(200{,}000)$$
$$= 6{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 92{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Calculate M_n [0.5 mark]**
$$\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{92{,}000}{1.00} = 92{,}000 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Σ(N_i × M_i²) [1.5 marks]**
$$\sum N_i M_i^2 = (0.30)(20{,}000)^2 + (0.45)(80{,}000)^2 + (0.25)(200{,}000)^2$$
$$= (0.30)(4.0 \times 10^8) + (0.45)(6.4 \times 10^9) + (0.25)(4.0 \times 10^{10})$$
$$= 1.20 \times 10^8 + 2.88 \times 10^9 + 1.00 \times 10^{10}$$
$$= 1.30 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate M_w [0.5 mark]**
$$\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{1.30 \times 10^{10}}{92{,}000} = 141{,}304 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate PDI [0.5 mark]**
$$PDI = \dfrac{141{,}304}{92{,}000} = 1.54$$

**Step 7 — Processing behavior and applications [2.5 marks]**

- A PDI of **1.54** indicates a **moderately broad molecular weight distribution**.
- Processing implications:
  - Good melt strength → suitable for **blow molding, film extrusion, thermoforming**.
  - Reasonable flow → acceptable for injection molding with proper mold design.
- Recommended applications:
  1. **Blow-molded HDPE containers** (chemical drums, milk cans).
  2. **Extruded LLDPE/HDPE films** for packaging.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Question B (Alternative Choice)]**

### **Question B: 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **applications of polymers in the biomedical field**. With examples, explain the role of (i) UHMWPE in total joint replacements, (ii) PMMA in bone cement and intraocular lenses, and (iii) PLA in biodegradable sutures and tissue engineering scaffolds.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sample of **nylon 6,6** is to be used for an automotive radiator end-tank (continuous service at 130 °C, exposure to glycol–water coolant, dimensional stability required). Justify the polymer selection using the **5-sieve methodology**. Compare the suitability of nylon 6,6 with (i) polypropylene (PP), (ii) polyphenylene sulfide (PPS), and (iii) polyetheretherketone (PEEK) for this application.

---

### Model Answer — Part (a): Biomedical Polymer Applications

**Introduction [1 mark]**
Biomedical polymers (biomaterials) must satisfy **biocompatibility, sterilizability, mechanical adequacy, and chemical stability** in physiological environments. They are classified as **inert, bioactive, or biodegradable**.

**(i) UHMWPE in Joint Replacements [2 marks]**
- Molecular weight: 3–6 million g/mol.
- Highly crystalline, highly crosslinked (in modern grades).
- Used as the **acetabular cup liner** in total hip replacements and as the **tibial insert** in knee replacements.
- Properties: Extremely low wear rate (< 0.1 mm/year), low friction, high impact strength, biocompatibility (ISO 5834).
- Vitamin E-stabilized UHMWPE (e.g., E1® by Zimmer Biomet) further reduces oxidation.

**(ii) PMMA in Bone Cement and IOLs [2 marks]**
- Bone cement: PMMA powder + MMA liquid monomer mixed in situ, polymerizes exothermically (T_max ≈ 70–80 °C), sets in 10–15 min. Used to fix hip stems to bone.
- Intraocular lenses (IOLs): Foldable PMMA or acrylic lenses replace cataractous lenses. Excellent optical clarity, refractive index ~ 1.49, and long-term biostability.

**(iii) PLA in Sutures and Scaffolds [2 marks]**
- Polylactic acid (PLA) is a **biodegradable, biocompatible** aliphatic polyester.
- Bioresorbable sutures: PLA fibers lose 50% tensile strength in 6–12 months and are fully resorbed in 2–5 years.
- Tissue engineering scaffolds: PLA, PLGA, and PCL are fabricated by electrospinning or 3D printing to mimic extracellular matrix, supporting cell attachment and growth.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b): Nylon 6,6 Selection for Radiator End-Tank

**Step 1 — 5-Sieve Methodology Application [4 marks]**

| **Sieve** | **Requirement (130 °C, glycol, dimensional)** | **Nylon 6,6 Performance** | **Pass/Fail** |
|-----------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Thermal | Continuous T_use > 130 °C | T_m = 255 °C, T_g = 50 °C, HDT @ 1.82 MPa = 75 °C | **Marginal** (heat-stabilized grades needed) |
| Mechanical | High stiffness, creep resistance | Tensile strength = 80 MPa, flexural modulus = 2.8 GPa | **Pass** |
| Chemical | Glycol/water resistance (pH 7–9) | Excellent; minor hydrolysis over time | **Pass** (heat-stabilized) |
| Processing | Injection moldable (BMC/SMC) | Easily injection-molded at 280–290 °C | **Pass** |
| Economics | Cost-effective vs. metals/PPS/PEEK | $4–6/kg | **Pass** |

**Conclusion [1 mark]:** **Heat-stabilized, glass-fiber-reinforced (33% GF) Nylon 6,6** is the optimal cost-effective choice (e.g., BASF Ultramid® A3WG6 HR or DuPont Zytel® 70G33HSL).

**Step 2 — Comparative Analysis [2 marks]**

| **Polymer** | **T_m (°C)** | **Cost ($/kg)** | **Verdict for Radiator Tank** |
|-------------|--------------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| Nylon 6,6 (33% GF) | 255 | 4–6 | **Selected** — best balance of heat, cost, processability |
| PP (30% GF) | 165 | 2–3 | **Reject** — T_m too low, softens at 130 °C |
| PPS (40% GF) | 280 | 10–15 | **Backup** — superior heat & chemical resistance, higher cost |
| PEEK (30% CF) | 343 | 80–120 | **Reject for automotive, accept for aerospace** — excellent but uneconomical |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in "Application" Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Vague justifications** (e.g., "Nylon is strong, so it can be used"). Examiners expect **quantitative property–application linkage** (T_g, T_m, tensile strength, % elongation, chemical resistance data). Always cite a numeric property value.
>
> 2. **Confusing LDPE, LLDPE, and HDPE.** LDPE is high-pressure, free-radical polymerized; LLDPE uses a comonomer (butene, hexene, octene); HDPE is low-pressure, Ziegler–Natta or Phillips catalyzed. **Density must be quoted correctly.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the cost-and-processability sieve.** Selecting PEEK for a commodity packaging application will cost you marks. Always perform the 5-sieve analysis.
>
> 4. **Incorrect unit usage:** Tensile strength in **MPa** (not psi), M_w in **g/mol** (not Daltons or amu), T_g in **°C or K (with conversion shown)**.
>
> 5. **Missing the engineering "Why":** Don't just name the polymer; explain **why** the repeat unit, crystallinity, or crosslink density makes it suitable.
>
> 6. **Failing to cite standards:** Mentioning ASTM/ISO standards (e.g., ASTM D638 for tensile, ASTM D648 for HDT, ISO 5834 for UHMWPE) earns bonus marks.
>
> 7. **Ignoring recyclability and sustainability:** KTU 2024 places emphasis on **circular economy and ESG**. Mention recycling codes (PET #1, HDPE #2, PVC #3, LDPE #4, PP #5, PS #6, Other #7) wherever possible.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 – "Application of Polymers" — Final Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Application domains (10 main):** Packaging, Automotive, Aerospace, Electrical & Electronics, Biomedical, Construction, Textiles & Fibers, Adhesives & Coatings, Agriculture, Defence.
>
> ✅ **Polymer classification (must remember):** Commodity, Engineering, High-Performance, Specialty/Functional, Biodegradable.
>
> ✅ **5-Sieve Selection Model:** Thermal → Mechanical → Chemical → Processing → Economic/Regulatory.
>
> ✅ **SPPP Chain:** Structure → Property → Processing → Performance → Application.
>
> ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:**
> - $M_n$, $M_w$, PDI = $M_w$ / $M_n$
> - $DP_n$ = $M_n$ / $M_0$
> - % Crystallinity = $\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)$ / $[\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)] \times 100$
> - Mark–Houwink: $[\eta] = K M_v^a$
>
> ✅ **Application–polymer must-know pairings:**
> - Bulletproof vest → Kevlar (Aramid) or Dyneema (UHMWPE fibers)
> - Hot-water pipe → CPVC
> - Artificial hip → UHMWPE
> - Aircraft canopy → PMMA or PC
> - 5G PCB → PTFE, LCP
> - 3D printing → PLA, ABS, PETG
> - Tyre → NR, SBR, BR
> - Fuel tank (automotive) → HDPE
> - Bone cement → PMMA
> - Biodegradable suture → PLA, PGA
> - Cable insulation → XLPE, PVC
> - Chemical tank → HDPE, PP, PVDF
> - Solar encapsulant → EVA, POE
> - Non-stick cookware → PTFE
> - Window frame → uPVC
> - Pressure pipe (water/gas) → HDPE PE-100
>
> ✅ **Crystallinity rule:** Higher crystallinity → higher density, stiffness, T_m, chemical resistance, opacity. Lower crystallinity → higher flexibility, transparency, impact strength.
>
> ✅ **T_g and T_m rule:** Application service temperature < T_g (for amorphous) or < T_m (for crystalline). Above T_g, amorphous polymer becomes rubbery; above T_m, semi-crystalline polymer flows.
>
> ✅ **Recycling codes (Resin Identification Codes – RIC):** 1 = PET, 2 = HDPE, 3 = PVC, 4 = LDPE, 5 = PP, 6 = PS, 7 = Other.
>
> ✅ **Standards to cite in answers:** ASTM D638 (tensile), D790 (flexural), D256 (Izod impact), D648 (HDT), D1238 (MFI), ISO 1628 (viscosity), ISO 5834 (UHMWPE for implants), FDA 21 CFR (food contact).
>
> ✅ **Circular economy hot topics (KTU 2024 ESG emphasis):** Mechanical recycling, chemical recycling (depolymerization), pyrolysis to fuel, biodegradable polymers (PLA, PHB), bio-based polymers (bio-PE from sugarcane).

topics.title: Thermal Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Behaviour of Polymers** refers to the systematic response of polymeric materials — both amorphous and semi-crystalline — to applied thermal energy, encompassing **thermal transitions** (glass transition, melting, crystallization), **thermo-mechanical responses** (thermal expansion, heat deflection), **thermal stability** (degradation and decomposition), and **calorimetric signatures** obtained through techniques such as **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)**, **Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)**, **Thermomechanical Analysis (TMA)**, and **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic is mapped to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Understand the fundamental structure–property relationships in polymers."* It forms the foundation for subsequent modules dealing with processing, mechanical behaviour, and end-use performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Train Compartment" Model

Imagine a commuter train packed with passengers (representing polymer chains):

- **At low temperatures (below $T_g$)** — the passengers stand rigid, frozen in awkward positions. They cannot slide past each other. This is the **glassy state**: brittle, hard, and dimensionally frozen.
- **As temperature rises (approaching $T_g$)** — passengers begin shifting their shoulders, small cooperative movements appear in groups of 5–10 atoms (the "**segmental motion**"). The train is still crowded, but now it can *creep* around bends. This is the **rubbery/leathery transition zone**.
- **Well above $T_g$** — passengers flow freely; whole-chain motions and reptation become possible.
- **For semicrystalline polymers** — the "standing passengers with suitcases" represent the **ordered crystalline lamellae** which only melt at a sharp $T_m$, regardless of the crowd dynamics below them.

This analogy helps cement why **amorphous polymers show a gradual $T_g$ transition** while **semicrystalline polymers show a sharp $T_m$** superimposed on a $T_g$.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Constants for Polymer Thermal Analysis:**
> - **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$
> - **Free-volume fraction at $T_g$** $f_g \approx 0.025$ (Doolittle / Williams–Landel–Ferry standard value)
> - **WLF Universal Constants** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6 \text{ K}$ (referenced to $T_g$)
> - **Standard Heating Rate** $10 \text{ K·min}^{-1}$ (ASTM D3418 DSC protocol)
> - **Reference Crystallinity for HDPE** $\approx 70\text{–}80\%$
> - **Reference Crystallinity for LDPE** $\approx 40\text{–}55\%$

## 1.4 Categorization of Thermal Transitions

| Transition | Symbol | Nature | Occurs In |
|------------|:------:|--------|-----------|
| Glass Transition | $T_g$ | Second-order (step change in $C_p$) | Amorphous & semicrystalline |
| Melting | $T_m$ | First-order (endothermic peak) | Semicrystalline only |
| Crystallization (cooling) | $T_c$ | First-order (exothermic peak) | Semicrystalline only |
| Decomposition | $T_d$ | Irreversible mass loss | All polymers |
| Sub-$T_g$ transitions | $T_\beta$, $T_\gamma$ | Local mode relaxations | All polymers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Polymer Motion

Polymer thermal behaviour is fundamentally governed by **the hierarchy of molecular motions** unlocked at successively higher temperatures:

1. **Vibrational motion** (< 50 K) — bond stretching and bending; no chain mobility.
2. **Side-group / pendant group rotation** ($T_\gamma$ region, ~100–200 K) — localized motion of small substituents (e.g., methyl group rotation in PMMA at ~$T_\gamma \approx -173^\circ\text{C}$).
3. **Local cooperative motion (4–8 backbone atoms)** ($T_\beta$ region) — crankshaft and kink transitions.
4. **Long-range segmental motion** ($T_g$, ~200–400 K) — 10–50 monomer units cooperate; the polymer softens over a 10–20 K range.
5. **Whole-chain flow / reptation** ($T > T_g + 50 \text{ K}$) — viscous flow; relevant for processing.
6. **Melting of crystallites** ($T_m$) — only for semicrystalline polymers; endothermic first-order transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $T_g$ is not a sharp thermodynamic transition:** Unlike $T_m$, the glass transition is **kinetic, not thermodynamic**. The observed $T_g$ shifts with heating rate by approximately **3–5 K per decade** of heating rate, because it represents a *relaxation* process governed by the Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) time–temperature superposition.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Signatures — First-Order vs. Second-Order

| Property | First-Order (e.g., $T_m$, $T_c$) | Second-Order (e.g., $T_g$) |
|---------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Enthalpy $H$ | Discontinuous jump | Continuous |
| Entropy $S$ | Discontinuous jump | Continuous, slope change |
| Heat capacity $C_p$ | Approaches infinity (peak) | Step change (discontinuity) |
| Volume $V$ | Discontinuous jump | Slope change (kink) |
| Latent heat | Yes (endothermic / exothermic) | No |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes every equation you must memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic. Internal pipeline characters have been converted to `\vert` for markdown safety.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_g = \dfrac{2}{3}\,T_m$ (K) | Beaman–Boyer rule (empirical) | K |
| 2 | $T_m = \dfrac{2}{3}\,T_g$ (K) | Inverse Beaman–Boyer | K |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{1}{T_g^{\circ}} + \dfrac{K}{M_n}$ | Fox–Flory (molecular weight effect) | K$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | Fox equation (miscible blends / copolymers) | K$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{K\,w_1 w_2}{T_{g1}}$ | Kelley–Bueche (plasticizer, $w_1$ = polymer) | K$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\log_{10}(a_T) = -\dfrac{C_1\,(T-T_g)}{C_2 + (T-T_g)}$ | WLF equation (time–temperature shift) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f = f_g + \alpha_f\,(T - T_g)$ | Free-volume fraction (linear expansion) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_g = T_g(\infty) - \dfrac{K}{\alpha_c}$ | Effect of crosslink density $\alpha_c$ | K |
| 9 | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \times 100\%$ | Degree of crystallinity from DSC | % |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dT_g}{d\ln q} = $ slope | Heating-rate dependence of $T_g$ | K |
| 11 | $\alpha_f = \alpha_r - \alpha_g$ | Difference in expansion coefficients | K$^{-1}$ |
| 12 | $E_a = R\,\dfrac{d(\ln q)}{d(1/T_g)}$ | Activation energy from heating rate | J·mol$^{-1}$ |

**Notation key:**
- $T_g^{\circ}$ = glass transition at infinite molecular weight
- $M_n$ = number-average molecular weight
- $a_T$ = horizontal shift factor
- $f_g \approx 0.025$ (free-volume fraction at $T_g$)
- $\alpha_f$ = free-volume thermal expansion coefficient
- $X_c$ = percent crystallinity
- $\Delta H_f$ = measured heat of fusion
- $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ = theoretical heat of fusion for 100% crystalline polymer
- $q$ = heating rate (K·min$^{-1}$)
- $E_a$ = apparent activation energy of the glass transition

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Injection Moulding & Extrusion:** $T_m + 30\text{–}80 \text{ K}$ sets the processing temperature.
2. **Heat-Deflection Temperature (HDT, ASTM D648):** Directly correlated with $T_g$ for amorphous polymers — determines the upper service temperature.
3. **Sterilization of Medical Devices:** For poly(propylene) syringes, autoclaving at $121^\circ\text{C}$ requires one to remain well below $T_m \approx 165^\circ\text{C}$ but above $T_g \approx -10^\circ\text{C}$.
4. **Design of Hot-Water Pipes:** Cross-linked polyethylene (PEX) raises $T_g$ and $T_m$ to permit continuous service at $95^\circ\text{C}$.
5. **Thermal Recycling:** TGA decomposition profiles guide pyrolysis and feedstock-recovery operations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Fox–Flory Equation for Molecular-Weight Dependence of $T_g$

### Statement
For a homologous series of linear polymers, the glass transition temperature $T_g$ increases with molecular weight and approaches a limiting value $T_g^{\circ}$ as $M_n \to \infty$, according to:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{1}{T_g^{\circ}} + \dfrac{K}{M_n}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free-volume framework.**
Fox and Flory (1950) proposed that each chain end contributes *excess free volume* $V_e$ relative to an interior segment. A polymer chain of degree of polymerization $x$ contains $(x-2)$ interior segments and $2$ end groups. The total chain-end free volume $\theta$ is therefore proportional to the number of ends:

$$
\theta \propto \dfrac{2}{x} \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{M_n}
$$

**Step 2 — Free-volume requirement at $T_g$.**
A glass transition occurs when the free-volume fraction $f$ reaches a critical value $f_g \approx 0.025$. The free-volume fraction contains a thermal part (linearly dependent on $T$) and a structural part (chain ends):

$$
f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f\,(T - T_g^{\circ}) + \dfrac{\beta}{M_n}
$$

where $\beta$ is the chain-end excess free volume per unit molecular weight and $\alpha_f$ is the free-volume thermal expansion coefficient.

**Step 3 — Apply the glass-transition condition.**
At $T = T_g$, we have $f(T_g) = f_g$. Therefore:

$$
f_g = f_g + \alpha_f\,(T_g - T_g^{\circ}) + \dfrac{\beta}{M_n}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**
Subtract $f_g$ from both sides:

$$
0 = \alpha_f\,(T_g - T_g^{\circ}) + \dfrac{\beta}{M_n}
$$

Solve for $(T_g - T_g^{\circ})$:

$$
T_g - T_g^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha_f}\cdot \dfrac{1}{M_n}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify constant and invert.**
Define $K = \beta/\alpha_f$ (a positive constant with units of K·g·mol$^{-1}$). Note the minus sign disappears when we rewrite in *reciprocal* form. Dividing both sides by $T_g \, T_g^{\circ}$ and applying the identity $(T_g - T_g^{\circ})/(T_g T_g^{\circ}) = (1/T_g^{\circ}) - (1/T_g)$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{1}{T_g^{\circ}} + \dfrac{K}{M_n}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{1}{T_g^{\circ}} + \dfrac{K}{M_n}\;}
$$

This is the **Fox–Flory equation**, where the constant $K \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ K·g·mol$^{-1}$ for many common vinyl polymers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) Equation

### Statement
The horizontal shift factor $a_T$, used to superpose polymer viscoelastic data taken at different temperatures, obeys:

$$
\log_{10}(a_T) = -\dfrac{C_1\,(T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Doolittle viscosity equation.**
The viscosity $\eta$ of a polymer melt depends on free volume through Doolittle's equation:

$$
\ln \eta = \ln A + B\,\left(\dfrac{1}{f} - 1\right)
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants and $f$ is the free-volume fraction.

**Step 2 — Free-volume expansion.**
Assume $f$ increases linearly above $T_g$:

$$
f = f_g + \alpha_f\,(T - T_g)
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of viscosities at two temperatures.**
The shift factor is defined as $a_T = \eta(T)\,T_g\,\rho_g / [\eta(T_g)\,T\,\rho]$; for narrow temperature ranges the density and $T$ corrections are negligible, so $a_T \approx \eta(T)/\eta(T_g)$. Using Doolittle's equation:

$$
\ln a_T = B\,\left[\dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{f_g}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute free-volume expression.**
With $f = f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)$:

$$
\ln a_T = B\,\left[\dfrac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)} - \dfrac{1}{f_g}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic simplification.**
Take common denominator:

$$
\ln a_T = B\,\left[\dfrac{f_g - f_g - \alpha_f(T-T_g)}{f_g\,[f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)]}\right] = -\dfrac{B\,\alpha_f\,(T-T_g)}{f_g\,[f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)]}
$$

**Step 6 — Define universal constants.**
Divide numerator and denominator by $\alpha_f$:

$$
\ln a_T = -\dfrac{(B/f_g)\,(T-T_g)}{(f_g/\alpha_f) + (T-T_g)}
$$

Define the **WLF universal constants**:

$$
C_1 = \dfrac{B}{2.303\,f_g}, \qquad C_2 = \dfrac{f_g}{\alpha_f}
$$

(the factor $2.303 = \ln 10$ converts natural to base-10 logarithm).

**Step 7 — Final WLF form.**

$$
\boxed{\;\log_{10}(a_T) = -\dfrac{C_1\,(T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}\;}
$$

**Universal values (referenced to $T_g$):**
$$
C_1 = 17.44, \qquad C_2 = 51.6 \text{ K}
$$

These constants are remarkably *universal* across many amorphous polymers, supporting the free-volume interpretation of $T_g$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Degree of Crystallinity from DSC

The enthalpy of fusion $\Delta H_f$ measured by DSC for a semicrystalline polymer is proportional to its crystalline fraction $X_c$. If $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ is the theoretical enthalpy for the 100% crystalline polymer:

$$
X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \times 100\%
$$

**Sample Calculation:**
A sample of Nylon 6,6 weighing 5.0 mg produces a melting endotherm with area $\Delta H_f = 31.5$ J·g$^{-1}$. Given $\Delta H_f^{\circ} = 196$ J·g$^{-1}$ for 100% crystalline Nylon 6,6:

$$
X_c = \dfrac{31.5}{196}\times 100\% = 16.07\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Activation Energy of Glass Transition

The Moynihan–Landel relationship links the heating rate $q$ to $T_g$ via:

$$
\dfrac{d(\log_{10} q)}{d(1/T_g)} = -\dfrac{E_a}{2.303\,R}
$$

**Worked Example (PS):**
DSC at $q_1 = 5$ K·min$^{-1}$ gives $T_{g1} = 370$ K; at $q_2 = 20$ K·min$^{-1}$ gives $T_{g2} = 374$ K.

$$
\ln\dfrac{q_2}{q_1} = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{g1}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{g2}}\right)
$$

$$
\ln(4) = \dfrac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{370} - \dfrac{1}{374}\right)
$$

$$
1.386 = \dfrac{E_a}{8.314}\,(2.890\times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
E_a = \dfrac{1.386 \times 8.314}{2.890\times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.99\times 10^{5} \text{ J·mol}^{-1} \approx 400 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}
$$

This high $E_a$ is consistent with cooperative segmental motion.

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Thermal Technique

| Engineering Question | Best Technique | What It Measures |
|----------------------|----------------|------------------|
| What is $T_g$? | DSC (heat flow step) or DMA (tan $\delta$ peak) | Heat capacity / mechanical loss |
| What is $T_m$ and crystallinity? | DSC | Enthalpy of fusion |
| Will it survive $200^\circ\text{C}$ long-term? | TGA (isothermal at 200°C) | Mass loss vs. time |
| What is the heat-deflection temperature? | HDT tester (ASTM D648) | Deflection under load |
| What is the coefficient of thermal expansion? | TMA | Dimensional change |
| Is the polymer crosslinked? | DMA (rubbery plateau modulus) | Equilibrium modulus |
