# 📝 Molecular Weight of Polymers — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Number Average ($\bar{M}_n$) and Weight Average ($\bar{M}_w$) molecular weights of polymers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | $\bar{M}_n$ | $\bar{M}_w$ |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Definition | $\sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ | $\sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ |
| Sensitivity | Sensitive to **low** molecular weight species | Sensitive to **high** molecular weight species |
| Experimental method | Osmometry, end-group analysis, VPO | Light scattering, sedimentation |
| Physical meaning | Counts each molecule equally | Weighs each molecule by its mass |
| Order in hierarchy | Smallest | $\bar{M}_n < \bar{M}_v < \bar{M}_w$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For a monodisperse sample, $\bar{M}_n = \bar{M}_w$, so PDI = 1.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Polydispersity Index. What is its significance in polymer characterization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** is defined as:

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$$

**Significance:**
1. It quantifies the **breadth of the molecular weight distribution** in a polymer sample.
2. PDI = 1 → **monodisperse** (theoretical, only for biological polymers).
3. PDI = 2 → typical of free-radical polymerization (most probable distribution).
4. PDI is critical for **processing behavior**: a narrow PDI gives better flow predictability; a broad PDI improves impact strength but worsens melt uniformity.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 🟢 Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable equations, explain the **osmotic pressure method** for the determination of number average molecular weight ($\bar{M}_n$) of a polymer. Discuss the role of the second virial coefficient $A_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polymer sample shows the following composition. Calculate $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, and PDI.

| Fraction | Mass (g) | Molecular Weight (g/mol) |
|----------|----------|---------------------------|
| A | 0.40 | 20,000 |
| B | 0.35 | 60,000 |
| C | 0.25 | 100,000 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to A(a)

The osmotic pressure method exploits the **colligative property** of polymer solutions. The semi-permeable membrane allows solvent molecules to pass but not the large polymer chains.

**Governing Equation (Virial Form):**

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = RT\left[\frac{1}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 c + A_3 c^2 + \cdots\right]$$

For dilute solutions, the higher-order terms are negligible:

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + RT \cdot A_2 \cdot c$$

**Experimental Procedure:**
1. Place a polymer solution of known concentration $c$ on one side of a **semipermeable membrane** (e.g., regenerated cellulose), with pure solvent on the other.
2. Solvent flows into the solution compartment until osmotic equilibrium; measure the hydrostatic head $\pi$.
3. Repeat for 4–5 different concentrations.

**Data Analysis:**
- Plot $\pi/c$ vs. $c$.
- Obtain a straight line with:
  - **Intercept** at $c = 0$: $\dfrac{RT}{\bar{M}_n}$ → calculate $\bar{M}_n$.
  - **Slope**: $RT \cdot A_2$ → calculate second virial coefficient $A_2$.

**Role of Second Virial Coefficient $A_2$:**
- $A_2$ reflects **polymer–solvent interaction**.
- $A_2 > 0$: good solvent (coil expands).
- $A_2 = 0$: theta solvent (ideal, unperturbed coil).
- $A_2 < 0$: poor solvent (aggregation).

**Useful Range:** $\bar{M}_n$ from **20,000 to 500,000 g/mol** — molecules below 20,000 Da pass through the membrane; above 500,000 Da, $\pi$ is too small to measure accurately.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Convert mass to number of molecules** using $N_i = \dfrac{W_i}{M_i} \times N_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= \frac{0.40}{20{,}000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol} \\
N_B &= \frac{0.35}{60{,}000} = 5.833 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol} \\
N_C &= \frac{0.25}{100{,}000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

(We can work in mol-units directly; the $N_A$ cancels.)

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{M}_n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n &= \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} \\
&= \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5})(20{,}000) + (5.833 \times 10^{-6})(60{,}000) + (2.5 \times 10^{-6})(100{,}000)}{2.0 \times 10^{-5} + 5.833 \times 10^{-6} + 2.5 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{0.400 + 0.350 + 0.250}{2.833 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{1.000}{2.833 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 35{,}294 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = 35{,}294 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\bar{M}_w$ using mass fractions $w_i$:**

Since total mass = 1.0 g, mass fractions $w_A = 0.40$, $w_B = 0.35$, $w_C = 0.25$.

$$\bar{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i = (0.40)(20{,}000) + (0.35)(60{,}000) + (0.25)(100{,}000)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 8{,}000 + 21{,}000 + 25{,}000 \\
&= 54{,}000 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_w = 54{,}000 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute PDI:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{54{,}000}{35{,}294} = 1.53$$

$$\boxed{\text{PDI} \approx 1.53}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** PDI of 1.53 indicates a moderately broad molecular weight distribution. The sample has a substantial high-molecular-weight component (fraction C with $M = 100{,}000$ contributes 25% by mass).

---

### 🟢 Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **viscosity-average molecular weight ($\bar{M}_v$)** determination using the Mark-Houwink equation. Discuss the physical significance of constants $K$ and $a$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The intrinsic viscosity of a polystyrene sample in toluene at 25 °C is measured to be $[\eta] = 0.85$ dL/g. Using $K = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g and $a = 0.69$, calculate the viscosity-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_v$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to B(a)

**Principle of Viscometry:**
The viscosity of a polymer solution depends on the size (hydrodynamic volume) of polymer chains. By measuring the flow time of dilute polymer solutions in a **capillary viscometer** (e.g., Ubbelohde), one can extract the intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$, which is then related to molecular weight through the Mark-Houwink equation.

**Mark-Houwink-Sakurada (MHS) Equation:**

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$$

Where:
- $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity (dL/g)
- $M$ = viscosity-average molecular weight
- $K$ = constant related to polymer–solvent system
- $a$ = Mark-Houwink exponent

**Determination Procedure:**

1. Prepare 4–5 polymer solutions of known concentration $c$.
2. Measure flow times $t$ (solution) and $t_0$ (pure solvent) in an Ubbelohde viscometer.
3. Compute specific viscosity: $\eta_{sp} = \dfrac{t - t_0}{t_0}$.
4. Compute reduced viscosity: $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$.
5. Plot $\eta_{sp}/c$ vs. $c$ (Huggins plot). Extrapolate to $c = 0$ to get $[\eta]$.
6. Use $[\eta] = K M^a$ to compute $\bar{M}_v$.

**Physical Significance of $K$ and $a$:**

- **Exponent $a$:** Indicates the shape of polymer coil in solution.
  - $a = 0.5$: theta solvent (unperturbed random coil).
  - $a = 0.5 - 0.8$: good solvent (expanded coil).
  - $a \approx 1.0$ : very good solvent.
  - $a \approx 2.0$: rigid rod (theoretical limit for stiff chains).
- **Constant $K$:** Encodes polymer-specific hydrodynamic properties; depends on temperature and solvent.

**Advantages of Viscometry:**
- Simple apparatus; fast measurements.
- Excellent for routine industrial QC.
- Wide applicable range: $10^4$ to $10^7$ g/mol.
- Requires only small sample quantities.

**Limitation:** Requires pre-calibration of $K$ and $a$ using samples of known $\bar{M}$ (from absolute methods).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to B(b)

**Given:**
- $[\eta] = 0.85$ dL/g
- $K = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g  
- $a = 0.69$

**Step 1:** Apply the Mark-Houwink equation:

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M^a \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_v = \left(\frac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/a}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$M_v = \left(\frac{0.85}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.69}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio inside the brackets:

$$\frac{0.85}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.85}{0.00017} = 5000$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $1/a$:

$$\frac{1}{0.69} = 1.4493$$

**Step 5:** Raise 5000 to the power 1.4493:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(M_v) &= 1.4493 \times \log_{10}(5000) \\
\log_{10}(5000) &= 3.6990 \\
\log_{10}(M_v) &= 1.4493 \times 3.6990 = 5.3607 \\
M_v &= 10^{5.3607}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Final calculation:

$$M_v = 2.295 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_v \approx 2.30 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol} = 230{,}000 \text{ g/mol}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** For polystyrene in toluene with $a = 0.69$, a value of 230,000 g/mol corresponds to a medium-high molecular weight — consistent with commercial-grade injection-molding polystyrene.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Molecular Weight" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{M}_w$:** A student who writes $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ (instead of $\sum w_i M_i$ or $\sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$) loses 3 marks immediately.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units:** $K$ has units of dL/g (or mL/g) and $a$ is dimensionless. Report $\bar{M}_v$ in g/mol. Examiners explicitly look for correct units.
> 
> 3. **Misreading data tables:** If a problem gives mass fractions, **DO NOT** compute $\bar{M}_n$ directly without first converting mass to moles. A 1-mark deduction is standard.
> 
> 4. **Wrong sign for $A_2$:** The second virial coefficient's sign must be interpreted (good/theta/poor solvent). Examiners award 1 mark for this discussion.
> 
> 5. **Mark-Houwink exponent confusion:** $a$ is **NOT** a function of molecular weight — it is a polymer-solvent-temperature specific constant. Students who write "$a$ depends on $M$" lose a conceptual mark.
> 
> 6. **Missing the inequality hierarchy:** For any polydisperse sample, the inequality $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_v \leq \bar{M}_w$ must hold. If a calculated $\bar{M}_v$ violates this, you made an arithmetic error — check the work.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it twice before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ Polymers are **polydisperse** — every chain has a different length; we use statistical averages.
- ✅ $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ — sensitive to low MW tail; measured by **osmometry and end-group analysis**.
- ✅ $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ — sensitive to high MW tail; measured by **light scattering**.
- ✅ $\bar{M}_v$ lies between $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{M}_w$; measured by **viscometry** using $[\eta] = K M^a$.
- ✅ Strict inequality: $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_v \leq \bar{M}_w \leq \bar{M}_z \leq \bar{M}_{z+1}$.
- ✅ **PDI = $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$**; equals 1.0 for monodisperse, 2.0 for most probable distribution.
- ✅ Mark-Houwink exponent $a$: 0.5 (theta), 0.5–0.8 (good solvent), ~1.0 (very good), ~2.0 (rigid rod).
- ✅ Osmometry equation: $\pi/c = RT/\bar{M}_n + RT A_2 c$ — intercept gives $\bar{M}_n$, slope gives $A_2$.
- ✅ Light scattering (Zimm plot) gives $\bar{M}_w$ via double extrapolation to $c = 0$ and $\theta = 0$.
- ✅ GPC/SEC gives the **full distribution** and all averages in a single run — most widely used modern technique.
- ✅ Carothers equation for step-growth polymers: $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$, giving $\bar{M}_n = M_o/(1-p)$.
- ✅ Property dependencies: tensile ↑ with $M$; melt viscosity ↑↑ with $M_w$; $T_g$ ↑ slightly with $M$.
- ✅ For monodisperse biological polymers (proteins, DNA), PDI = 1; synthetic polymers always have PDI > 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Average and Number Average Molecular Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weight Average and Number Average Molecular Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In polymer science, a synthetic polymer sample is never composed of molecules of a single, identical molecular weight. Instead, it contains a **statistical distribution** of chain lengths, and therefore a distribution of molecular weights. To characterize such a polydisperse (heterogeneous) sample, average molecular weights are used as representative scalar descriptors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT523 – Module 2):**
> The **Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$)** is the arithmetic mean of molecular weights, weighted by the *number* of molecules of each species present. The **Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_w$)** is the arithmetic mean weighted by the *mass* (weight) of each molecular species. These two averages are the most fundamental parameters for describing polymer molecular weight distributions (MWD).

**Mathematical compact form:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i} N_i} = \frac{W}{N}$$

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum_{i} w_i M_i}{\sum_{i} w_i}$$

where $N_i$ = number of molecules of species $i$, $M_i$ = molecular weight of species $i$, $w_i = N_i M_i$ = total weight of species $i$, $W$ = total weight of the sample, and $N$ = total number of molecules.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Classroom Marks Story"**
> Imagine a classroom of **100 students** appearing for an exam. Each student is a "molecule" and his/her mark is the "molecular weight".
> - **Number Average (Mn)** is like computing the *simple arithmetic mean* — we treat every student equally, irrespective of whether he scored 40 or 95. This is the **"democratic" average** where each molecule casts one vote.
> - **Weight Average (Mw)** is like computing a *weighted mean* where a student's score is multiplied by his own score before averaging. The topper (heavier molecule) now contributes disproportionately more. This is the **"elitist" average** where bigger molecules dominate.
> - Because of this, **Mw is always greater than or equal to Mn**. The ratio Mw/Mn (the Polydispersity Index) tells us how *unequal* the student body is. A value of exactly **1** means every student scored the same — a monodisperse polymer.

### 1.3 Physical Significance in KTU Context

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Board Insight:** Examiners frequently award marks for explicitly stating that *Mn* is sensitive to the *presence of many small molecules* (e.g., residual monomers, oligomers), while *Mw* is sensitive to the *presence of a few very large molecules* (high molecular weight tail). Always write this contrast in your answer scripts.

---

### 1.4 Definition Callout Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Definitions to Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$):** Total mass of all molecules divided by the total number of molecules. Determined experimentally by **colligative property methods** — membrane osmometry, vapor pressure osmometry (VPO), cryoscopy, ebulliometry, and end-group analysis (for known-structure polymers like polyesters, polyamides).
> 2. **Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_w$):** Sum of the weights of size fractions each multiplied by their respective molecular weights, divided by total weight. Determined experimentally by **static (classical) light scattering**, sedimentation equilibrium, and small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS).
> 3. **Polydispersity Index (PDI = $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$):** A dimensionless measure of the breadth of the molecular weight distribution. **PDI = 1** is the theoretical limit for a perfectly monodisperse polymer (all chains of identical length).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Deconstruction — Why Two Averages?

Synthetic polymers are produced by chain-growth and step-growth mechanisms that follow statistical laws (e.g., Flory's most probable distribution for step-growth, Schulz–Zimm distribution for chain-growth with termination). Because of this statistical nature:

1. A *single* number cannot fully describe a polymer.
2. Different experimental techniques "see" different moments of the molecular weight distribution.
3. **Number-average** methods count molecules → respond to the **zeroth moment** of the distribution.
4. **Weight-average** methods measure mass contributions → respond to the **first moment** (squared-weighted).
5. The difference between them mathematically reveals the **width** of the distribution.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Partition the sample into monodisperse slices:**
Divide the polymer sample into imaginary fractions, each containing molecules of a single molecular weight $M_i$, with $N_i$ molecules in fraction $i$.

**Step 2 — Compute the total mass:**
The total mass of fraction $i$ is $w_i = N_i M_i$. The total mass of the sample is $W = \sum_i w_i$.

**Step 3 — Compute total number of molecules:**
$$N = \sum_i N_i$$

**Step 4 — Define Number Average Molecular Weight (Mn):**
A "per-molecule" arithmetic mean. Each molecule contributes one count.

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

**Step 5 — Define Weight Average Molecular Weight (Mw):**
A "per-gram" arithmetic mean. Each gram (or unit weight) of fraction contributes once.

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum w_i M_i}{\sum w_i}$$

**Step 6 — Polydispersity Index (PDI):**
The dimensionless ratio that quantifies distribution breadth.

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} \geq 1$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Utility:**
- **Mn** → governs melt flow, mechanical strength at low strain, end-group concentration, and fiber dyeing properties.
- **Mw** → governs melt viscosity, impact strength, tensile strength, and processing behavior.
- **PDI** → dictates the **breadth of the MWD curve** and hence processability, film uniformity, and aging.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a high-yield revision block — reproduce it during last-minute prep before KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Experimental Method | SI Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|----------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Number Average MW | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | $N_i$ = number of molecules of mass $M_i$ | Osmometry, VPO, End-group titration | g/mol |
| 2 | Number Average MW (alt) | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{W}{N}$ | $W$ = total weight, $N$ = total number of molecules | — | g/mol |
| 3 | Weight Average MW | $\bar{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Squared weighting emphasizes heavy molecules | Static light scattering, sedimentation | g/mol |
| 4 | Weight Average MW (alt) | $\bar{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i M_i}{\sum w_i}$ | $w_i$ = weight of fraction $i$ | — | g/mol |
| 5 | Polydispersity Index | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Breadth of distribution | Ratio of Mw to Mn | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Monodisperse Limit | $\text{PDI} = 1$ | All molecules identical | Theoretical | — |
| 7 | Most Probable (Flory) PDI | $\text{PDI} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n}$ | Step-growth, $\bar{X}_n$ = number-average DP | — | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Inequality Rule | $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_w \leq \bar{M}_z \leq \bar{M}_{z+1}$ | General ordering of moments | — | g/mol |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall — Pipe Symbol in Tables:** In KTU 2024 answer scripts (and in digital LaTeX templates), never use the bare vertical bar $\vert$ inside table cells. Use \vert or \mid to ensure the markdown table does not break. This has been a recurring issue in the official answer-key PDF templates.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection (for full-mark KTU answers):**
> - In **polyethylene film extrusion**, narrow PDI gives uniform bubble stability and gauge control.
> - In **injection-molded polypropylene**, high Mw improves impact resistance but excessive PDI causes warpage.
> - In **Nylon-6,6 fiber spinning**, Mn (not Mw) controls dye uptake because dye molecules diffuse in proportion to free end-groups.
> - In **PMMA optical lenses**, narrow PDI is essential for high light transmittance and absence of haze.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU 2024 ESE Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Model Question):**
> A polymer sample is fractionated into four monodisperse fractions. The data are given below. Calculate $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, and the Polydispersity Index (PDI). Comment on the breadth of the distribution.

| Fraction $i$ | Mass $w_i$ (g) | Molecular Weight $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.20 | 1.0 × 10⁴ |
| 2 | 0.30 | 5.0 × 10⁴ |
| 3 | 0.30 | 1.0 × 10⁵ |
| 4 | 0.20 | 2.0 × 10⁵ |

**Total mass of the sample:**
$$W = \sum w_i = 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00 \text{ g}$$

**Step A — Compute $N_i$ for each fraction using $N_i = w_i / M_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= \frac{0.20}{1.0 \times 10^{4}} = 2.000 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol} \\
N_2 &= \frac{0.30}{5.0 \times 10^{4}} = 6.000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol} \\
N_3 &= \frac{0.30}{1.0 \times 10^{5}} = 3.000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol} \\
N_4 &= \frac{0.20}{2.0 \times 10^{5}} = 1.000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total number of moles:**
$$
N = \sum N_i = (2.000 \times 10^{-5}) + (6.000 \times 10^{-6}) + (3.000 \times 10^{-6}) + (1.000 \times 10^{-6})
$$
$$
N = 20.000 \times 10^{-6} + 6.000 \times 10^{-6} + 3.000 \times 10^{-6} + 1.000 \times 10^{-6} = 30.000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol}
$$
$$N = 3.000 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}$$

**Step B — Compute Number Average Molecular Weight $\bar{M}_n$ using $\bar{M}_n = W / N$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n &= \frac{1.00 \text{ g}}{3.000 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}} = \frac{1.00}{3.000 \times 10^{-5}} \\
\bar{M}_n &= 3.333 \times 10^{4} \text{ g/mol} = 33{,}333 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute Weight Average Molecular Weight $\bar{M}_w$ using the weight-fraction form $\bar{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i / \sum w_i$:**

We can directly use $W = 1.00$ g and weight fractions $x_i = w_i / W$:

| $i$ | $w_i$ (g) | $M_i$ (g/mol) | $w_i M_i$ (g²/mol) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.20 | 10 000 | 2.000 × 10³ |
| 2 | 0.30 | 50 000 | 1.500 × 10⁴ |
| 3 | 0.30 | 100 000 | 3.000 × 10⁴ |
| 4 | 0.20 | 200 000 | 4.000 × 10⁴ |
| **Σ** | **1.00** | — | **9.000 × 10⁴** |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{\sum w_i M_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{9.000 \times 10^{4}}{1.00} \\
\bar{M}_w &= 9.000 \times 10^{4} \text{ g/mol} = 90{,}000 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Polydispersity Index (PDI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDI} &= \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{9.000 \times 10^{4}}{3.333 \times 10^{4}} \\
\text{PDI} &= 2.70
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Interpretation for the Answer Script:**
> - $\bar{M}_n = 3.33 \times 10^{4}$ g/mol is pulled downward by the abundant low-MW chains (fraction 1: $2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ mol dominates the count).
> - $\bar{M}_w = 9.00 \times 10^{4}$ g/mol is pulled upward by the heavy chains (fraction 4: $M = 2 \times 10^{5}$ g/mol contributes the largest $w_i M_i$).
> - **PDI = 2.70** is well above 1, confirming a **broad polydisperse distribution**, typical of step-growth polymers (which approach PDI = 2 at high conversion).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of All Molecular Weight Averages

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is a KTU-board-tested comparative table; reproduce it in long-answer questions for full marks.**

| Property | Number Average $\bar{M}_n$ | Weight Average $\bar{M}_w$ | Z-Average $\bar{M}_z$ | Viscosity Avg $\bar{M}_v$ |
|----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Definition** | $\sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ | $\sum N_i M_i^{2} / \sum N_i M_i$ | $\sum N_i M_i^{3} / \sum N_i M_i^{2}$ | $\left(\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}/\sum N_i M_i\right)^{1/a}$ |
| **Weighting Basis** | Number of molecules | Mass of fraction | Mass × $M_i$ | Intrinsic viscosity |
| **Sensitivity to Small Molecules** | **Very High** | Low | Negligible | Low |
| **Sensitivity to Large Molecules** | Low | Moderate | **Very High** | Moderate |
| **Experimental Method** | Osmometry, Ebulliometry, End-group analysis | Static Light Scattering, Sedimentation | Sedimentation, GPC (with calibration) | Viscometry (Mark–Houwink) |
| **Typical KTU Formula** | $\bar{M}_n = W/N$ | $\bar{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i / \sum w_i$ | $\bar{M}_z = \sum N_i M_i^{3}/\sum N_i M_i^{2}$ | $[\eta] = K \bar{M}_v^{a}$ |
| **Value Range vs Mn** | Lowest | $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$ | $\bar{M}_z \geq \bar{M}_w$ | $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_v \leq \bar{M}_w$ |
| **Unit** | g/mol | g/mol | g/mol | g/mol |

### 3.3 Algebraic Derivation — From Fundamental Distribution to PDI = 1 + 1/Xn

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation: PDI of a Flory Most-Probable Distribution (Step-Growth Polymerization):**
> This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** worth 4–5 marks when asked "derive PDI for step-growth polymerization".

For step-growth polymerization, the number-fraction of $x$-mers is given by the Flory distribution:

$$
p_x = (1 - p) \, p^{x-1}
$$

where $p$ = extent of reaction and $x$ = degree of polymerization.

The number-average degree of polymerization:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}
$$

The weight-fraction of $x$-mers:

$$
w_x = \frac{x \, p^{x-1} (1-p)^{2}}{1} = x(1-p)^{2} p^{x-1}
$$

The weight-average degree of polymerization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_w &= \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot w_x = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^{2}(1-p)^{2} p^{x-1} \\
&= (1-p)^{2} \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^{2} p^{x-1} \\
&= (1-p)^{2} \cdot \frac{1 + p}{(1-p)^{3}} = \frac{1 + p}{1 - p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Polydispersity Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDI} &= \frac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1 + p
\end{aligned}
$$

As $p \to 1$ (full conversion for stoichiometric step-growth):

$$
\boxed{\text{PDI}_{\text{step-growth}} \to 2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 2.** Memorize the final boxed result and the three intermediate equations. Most students lose marks by skipping the geometric series identity $\sum x^{2} p^{x-1} = (1+p)/(1-p)^{3}$.

### 3.4 Algebraic Derivation — Why Mw ≥ Mn (Mathematical Proof)

**Statement:** For any non-negative distribution of molecular weights, $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$, with equality only if all molecules are identical.

**Proof using the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^{2} &= \left(\sum_i \sqrt{N_i} \cdot \sqrt{N_i} M_i\right)^{2} \\
&\leq \left(\sum_i N_i\right)\left(\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\left(\sum_i N_i\right)\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)$:

$$
\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} \leq \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

The left-hand side is $\bar{M}_n$ and the right-hand side is $\bar{M}_w$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_w}
$$

Equality holds only when $M_i$ is the same for all $i$ (i.e., monodisperse case, $\text{PDI} = 1$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part A consists of compulsory 3-mark questions. Answers should be **30–50 words** with at least **one formula**.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$) and Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_w$). State the relationship between them for a monodisperse polymer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$\bar{M}_n$ is defined as $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$, the total weight of polymer divided by the total number of molecules. $\bar{M}_w$ is defined as $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i^{2} / \sum N_i M_i$, weighted by the mass of each species. For a **monodisperse** polymer (all chains of identical length), $\bar{M}_n = \bar{M}_w$, and the **PDI = 1**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is the Polydispersity Index (PDI)? Mention the PDI value for a polymer obtained by step-growth condensation at 100 % conversion.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Polydispersity Index is the dimensionless ratio $\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ that quantifies the breadth of the molecular weight distribution; it is always $\geq 1$. For a step-growth polymer at 100 % conversion (Flory most-probable distribution), $\text{PDI} = 1 + p = 1 + 1 = \mathbf{2}$. This is the theoretical limit for condensation polymers like Nylon-6,6 and PET.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes** (writing Mn as g instead of g/mol) — a 1-mark deduction each time.
> 2. **Mixing up the divisor** in Mn (must be $\sum N_i$, not $\sum w_i$) and Mw (must be $\sum w_i$, not $\sum N_i$).
> 3. **Skipping the units** in the final answer — always write g/mol explicitly.
> 4. **Not commenting on PDI value** after computation — this fetches 1–2 bonus marks in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Confusing the experimental method** for each average — examiners specifically check "Mn is determined by which method?" in long answers.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full KTU Board Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define the following terms clearly, giving the relevant mathematical expressions: (i) Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$), (ii) Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_w$), and (iii) Polydispersity Index (PDI). Explain why $\bar{M}_w$ is always greater than or equal to $\bar{M}_n$ for any real polymer sample. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample is composed of three monodisperse fractions with the following composition: 0.5 g of chains with $M = 2 \times 10^{4}$ g/mol, 0.3 g of chains with $M = 5 \times 10^{4}$ g/mol, and 0.2 g of chains with $M = 1 \times 10^{5}$ g/mol. Calculate $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, and the Polydispersity Index. Comment on the breadth of the distribution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Number Average Molecular Weight:**
The total weight of all polymer molecules in the sample divided by the total number of molecules. Expressed mathematically as:

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{W}{N}$$

It represents the **arithmetic mean** weighted by the *number* of molecules of each species. **Determined by colligative property methods** such as membrane osmometry, vapor pressure osmometry, and end-group analysis.

**(ii) Weight Average Molecular Weight:**
The sum of the products of the molecular weight of each species and the weight of that species, divided by the total weight. Expressed as:

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum w_i M_i}{\sum w_i}$$

It is the arithmetic mean weighted by the *mass* of each species, so heavier molecules contribute disproportionately. **Determined by static light scattering** and sedimentation equilibrium.

**(iii) Polydispersity Index:**
A dimensionless ratio that measures the breadth of the molecular weight distribution:

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} \geq 1$$

PDI = 1 for a monodisperse system (theoretical limit), and PDI > 1 for all real polymers.

**Proof of $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$ (Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^{2} &= \left(\sum_i \sqrt{N_i} \cdot \sqrt{N_i} M_i\right)^{2} \\
&\leq \left(\sum_i N_i\right)\left(\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\left(\sum_i N_i\right)\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)$:

$$\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} \leq \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \implies \bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_w$$

Equality holds only when all $M_i$ are identical (monodisperse case). ∎

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**

| Fraction $i$ | $w_i$ (g) | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.5 | 20 000 |
| 2 | 0.3 | 50 000 |
| 3 | 0.2 | 100 000 |

**Step 1: Total mass:**
$$W = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Compute $N_i = w_i / M_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= \frac{0.5}{2 \times 10^{4}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol} \\
N_2 &= \frac{0.3}{5 \times 10^{4}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol} \\
N_3 &= \frac{0.2}{1 \times 10^{5}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol} \\
N &= (2.5 \times 10^{-5}) + (6.0 \times 10^{-6}) + (2.0 \times 10^{-6}) \\
N &= 3.30 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Number Average Molecular Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n &= \frac{W}{N} = \frac{1.0}{3.30 \times 10^{-5}} \\
\bar{M}_n &= 3.03 \times 10^{4} \text{ g/mol} = 30{,}303 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sum w_i M_i$:**

| $i$ | $w_i$ (g) | $M_i$ (g/mol) | $w_i M_i$ (g²/mol) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.5 | 20 000 | 10 000 |
| 2 | 0.3 | 50 000 | 15 000 |
| 3 | 0.2 | 100 000 | 20 000 |
| **Σ** | **1.0** | — | **45 000** |

**Step 5: Weight Average Molecular Weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_w &= \frac{\sum w_i M_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{45{,}000}{1.0} \\
\bar{M}_w &= 4.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ g/mol} = 45{,}000 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Polydispersity Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDI} &= \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{4}}{3.03 \times 10^{4}} \\
\text{PDI} &= 1.485
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Comment (for the 1-mark conclusion):**
Since PDI = 1.485, the polymer is **polydisperse** (the value is significantly greater than 1). This breadth of distribution is consistent with a typical step-growth polymer (PDI approaches 2 at 100 % conversion). The sample contains a wide range of chain lengths, with the lower-MW fraction (1) being most abundant in number but the higher-MW fraction (3) dominating the mass-based average.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear derivation, show that for a step-growth polymer following the Flory most-probable distribution, the Polydispersity Index approaches 2 at 100 % conversion. State the assumptions made in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the experimental techniques used to determine $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{M}_w$. Give one specific example for each and explain why the two averages generally differ for synthetic polymers. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Assumptions of the Flory Most-Probable Distribution:**
1. All functional groups are equally reactive.
2. The reactivity of a functional group is independent of molecular size.
3. No cyclization side reactions occur.
4. Equimolar stoichiometry of bifunctional monomers.

**Flory Number-Fraction Distribution:**
The probability that a randomly chosen chain has exactly $x$ repeat units is:

$$p_x = (1 - p) \, p^{x-1}$$

where $p$ is the extent of reaction.

**Number-Average Degree of Polymerization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n &= \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \, p_x = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x(1-p)p^{x-1} \\
&= (1-p) \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x p^{x-1} = (1-p) \cdot \frac{1}{(1-p)^{2}} = \frac{1}{1-p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Weight-Fraction Distribution:**

$$w_x = \frac{x(1-p)^{2} p^{x-1}}{1} = x(1-p)^{2} p^{x-1}$$

**Weight-Average Degree of Polymerization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_w &= \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot w_x = (1-p)^{2} \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^{2} p^{x-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the identity $\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^{2} p^{x-1} = (1+p)/(1-p)^{3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_w &= (1-p)^{2} \cdot \frac{1+p}{(1-p)^{3}} = \frac{1+p}{1-p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Polydispersity Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDI} &= \frac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1 + p
\end{aligned}
$$

**At 100 % conversion** ($p = 1$):

$$\boxed{\text{PDI}_{\max} = 1 + 1 = 2}$$

This is the most famous result in polymer science for step-growth polymerization, first derived by Paul Flory (Nobel Prize, 1974). ∎

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Methods for $\bar{M}_n$ (Colligative Property Methods):**

| Method | Principle | Typical MW Range |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Membrane Osmometry** | Measures osmotic pressure $\pi$ of polymer solution; $\pi/c = RT/\bar{M}_n + A_2 RT c + \ldots$ | $2 \times 10^{4}$ to $2 \times 10^{6}$ g/mol |
| **Vapor Pressure Osmometry (VPO)** | Measures temperature difference due to lowered vapor pressure | Up to $5 \times 10^{4}$ g/mol |
| **End-Group Analysis** | Titration of functional end-groups (e.g., –COOH, –OH); only valid for linear polymers with known end-groups | Up to $5 \times 10^{4}$ g/mol |
| **Cryoscopy / Ebulliometry** | Freezing-point depression / boiling-point elevation | Up to $5 \times 10^{4}$ g/mol |

**Methods for $\bar{M}_w$ (Light Scattering / Sedimentation):**

| Method | Principle | Typical MW Range |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Static Light Scattering (SLS)** | Zimm plot: $Kc/R_\theta = (1/\bar{M}_w) + 2 A_2 c$ | $10^{4}$ to $10^{7}$ g/mol |
| **Sedimentation Equilibrium** | Equilibrium concentration gradient in ultracentrifuge | $10^{4}$ to $10^{7}$ g/mol |
| **Small-Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS)** | Scattering intensity extrapolation to zero angle | $10^{4}$ to $10^{6}$ g/mol |

**Why the Two Averages Differ:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concluding Explanation (for full marks):**
> The two averages differ because **$\bar{M}_n$ counts every molecule equally** (one molecule = one vote), while **$\bar{M}_w$ weights each molecule by its own mass** (a heavy molecule contributes more to the sum because of the squared term $M_i^{2}$). For a polydisperse sample, the few very large chains disproportionately raise $\bar{M}_w$, while the many small chains disproportionately lower $\bar{M}_n$. As a result, $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$, with the ratio PDI = $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ serving as a quantitative measure of the distribution breadth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print This & Keep Before the KTU ESE):**

- **Mn** is the **"count-based"** average: $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i = W/N$. Sensitive to **low MW species** (monomers, oligomers).
- **Mw** is the **"mass-based"** average: $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i^{2} / \sum N_i M_i = \sum w_i M_i / \sum w_i$. Sensitive to **high MW species**.
- The **general ordering** is $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_v \leq \bar{M}_w \leq \bar{M}_z \leq \bar{M}_{z+1}$.
- **PDI = $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$** is **dimensionless** and **always $\geq 1$**. PDI = 1 only for monodisperse systems.
- For **step-growth (Flory)**: $\text{PDI} = 1 + p \to 2$ as $p \to 1$ (100 % conversion).
- For **free-radical chain-growth** (termination by disproportionation): $\text{PDI} = 1.5$ to 2.0.
- For **living polymerization** (anionic, ATRP, RAFT): $\text{PDI} \to 1$ (narrow distribution).
- **Mn** is measured by **colligative methods**: osmometry, VPO, cryoscopy, ebulliometry, end-group titration.
- **Mw** is measured by **light scattering**, sedimentation, SAXS.
- **Mark–Houwink equation** $[\eta] = K M^{a}$ gives the **viscosity-average** $M_v$, with $M_n \leq M_v \leq M_w$ (because $0 < a < 1$ typically).
- **GPC (Gel Permeation Chromatography)** is the most common modern technique; it gives the **entire MWD curve** and can yield all averages simultaneously after proper calibration.
- Always include **units (g/mol)** in numerical answers.
- For KTU valuation, **commenting on PDI** (broad vs narrow, step-growth vs living) is a **bonus mark** that distinguishes a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation and Viscosity Average Molecular Weights. Experimental Methods for Molecular Weight Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sedimentation and Viscosity Average Molecular Weights. Experimental Methods for Molecular Weight Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Average Molecular Weights — A Critical Polymer Concept

A polymer sample is **never composed of molecules of a single, uniform size**. Instead, it contains chains of varying lengths, leading to a **molecular weight distribution (MWD)**. Therefore, we use **statistical average molecular weights** to characterize the polymer.

#### 1.1.1 The Common Statistical Averages

| Symbol | Average | Mathematical Expression | Type of Average |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $M_n$ | Number-Average | $\frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Sensitive to small molecules |
| $M_w$ | Weight-Average | $\frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Sensitive to large molecules |
| $M_z$ | Z-Average | $\frac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$ | Heavily weighted by large molecules |
| $M_{sed}$ | Sedimentation-Average | $\frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Lies between $M_n$ and $M_w$ |
| $M_v$ | Viscosity-Average | $\left(\frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i^a}\right)$ | Lies between $M_n$ and $M_w$ |

Where:
- $N_i$ = number of molecules with molecular weight $M_i$
- $a$ = Mark-Houwink exponent (typically 0.5 ≤ a ≤ 1.0)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Hierarchy (for a = 1 case):** $M_n \le M_{sed} \le M_v \le M_w \le M_z$
> The polydispersity index (PDI) is defined as: $PDI = M_w / M_n \ge 1$

### 1.2 Sedimentation Average Molecular Weight ($M_{sed}$)

#### Formal Definition
The **sedimentation average molecular weight** is the molecular weight determined using sedimentation velocity measurements in an analytical ultracentrifuge. It is obtained by relating the sedimentation coefficient of each species to its molecular weight and computing a specific statistical mean.

$$M_{sed} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice that $M_{sed} = M_w$ by definition! However, in practice, the *experimentally measured* value from sedimentation velocity is actually an **effective average** that depends on hydrodynamic factors and friction. Strictly speaking, the sedimentation-transport method yields an average between $M_n$ and $M_w$, depending on the experimental setup (sedimentation velocity vs. sedimentation equilibrium).

#### Conceptual Analogy
> **Imagine a marathon with runners of different fitness levels.** In a **centrifuge's gravitational field**, heavier/larger molecules (the fittest runners) "settle" faster than lighter ones. By observing how fast the *boundary* of a polymer solution moves in the centrifuge tube, we get a measurement that is *biased toward the heavier, faster-settling species*. This bias gives us a different kind of average than simply counting molecules.

### 1.3 Viscosity Average Molecular Weight ($M_v$)

#### Formal Definition
The **viscosity average molecular weight** is determined from the intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ of a polymer solution using the **Mark-Houwink equation**:

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^a$$

It is the specific molecular weight that, when substituted into the Mark-Houwink equation, gives the observed intrinsic viscosity of a polydisperse sample.

$$M_v = \left[ \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^a} \right]^{1/a} \quad \text{or} \quad M_v^a = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i^a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Position of $M_v$ in the Hierarchy:** Since $0.5 \le a \le 1.0$, the value of $M_v$ always lies **between $M_n$ and $M_w$**. As $a \rightarrow 1$, $M_v \rightarrow M_w$. As $a \rightarrow 0.5$, $M_v$ approaches a value closer to (but not equal to) $M_n$.

#### Conceptual Analogy
> **Think of viscosity as a "traffic jam" measurement.** A single very long chain (a long truck) in the middle of a polymer solution disrupts flow much more than many small chains (small cars). Viscosity is highly *sensitive to the size of the largest chains present*. So $M_v$ weights larger molecules more heavily than $M_n$ does, but the weighting is "tunable" via the exponent $a$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sedimentation Theory — The Ultracentrifuge Approach

#### 2.1.1 Physical Principle
When a polymer solution is placed in a high-speed ultracentrifuge, the polymer molecules experience a centrifugal force. The **sedimentation coefficient** $s$ is defined as the sedimentation velocity per unit centrifugal field:

$$s = \frac{dx/dt}{\omega^2 x}$$

Where:
- $dx/dt$ = velocity of the sedimenting boundary
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $x$ = distance from the axis of rotation
- Units: **Svedbergs (S)** where $1\,S = 10^{-13}$ seconds

#### 2.1.2 Svedberg Equation (Molecular Weight from Sedimentation)
For a single monodisperse species, the molecular weight is:

$$M = \frac{s \cdot N_A \cdot f}{1 - \bar{v}\rho}$$

Where:
- $s$ = sedimentation coefficient
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number
- $f$ = frictional coefficient
- $\bar{v}$ = partial specific volume of the polymer
- $\rho$ = density of the solvent

For real polymer solutions, the frictional coefficient $f$ depends on molecular size, shape, and solvent viscosity. Using the **Flory-Mandelkern equation**, we can express M in terms of measurable quantities:

$$M^{2/3} = \frac{s \cdot [\eta]^{1/3} \cdot N_A \cdot \eta_0}{(1 - \bar{v}\rho) \cdot \Phi^{1/3}}$$

Where $\Phi$ is the Flory universal constant ($\approx 2.5 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$ for $[\eta]$ in dL/g) and $\eta_0$ is the solvent viscosity.

#### 2.1.3 Sedimentation Average Derivation (Heuristic)
For a polydisperse sample, the sedimentation boundary is composed of many species. Each species $i$ contributes to the boundary movement proportional to $c_i s_i$ (concentration times sedimentation coefficient). The total weight-average sedimentation coefficient is:

$$\bar{s}_w = \frac{\sum c_i s_i}{\sum c_i} = \frac{\sum c_i M_i^{(1-a)}}{\sum c_i M_i^{-a}}$$

Substituting the Svedberg relation, the resulting **sedimentation average** is:

$$M_{sed} = \left( \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i^a} \right)^{1/a_{sed}}$$

This places $M_{sed}$ between $M_n$ and $M_w$, with its exact position depending on the relationship between $s$ and $M$.

### 2.2 Viscosity Theory — From Flow to Molecular Weight

#### 2.2.1 Intrinsic Viscosity Concept
The **intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta]$ is the limiting viscosity number as concentration approaches zero (eliminating polymer-polymer interactions):

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \left( \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} \right) = \lim_{c \to 0} \left( \frac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} \right)$$

Where:
- $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$ = specific viscosity
- $\eta_{rel} = \eta / \eta_0$ = relative viscosity
- $c$ = concentration (typically in g/dL)
- Units of $[\eta]$: **dL/g** or **cm³/g**

#### 2.2.2 Huggins and Kraemer Equations (Extrapolation to c=0)

$$ \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c \quad \text{(Huggins)} $$

$$ \frac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} = [\eta] - k_K [\eta]^2 c \quad \text{(Kraemer)} $$

A **double extrapolation** (plotting both vs $c$) and finding the common y-intercept gives $[\eta]$.

#### 2.2.3 Mark-Houwink-Sakurada Equation (The Master Equation)

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^a$$

Where $K$ and $a$ are **empirical constants** that depend on:
- The polymer-solvent pair
- Temperature

Typical values of $a$:
- $a = 0.5$ : Theta solvent (coiled chain, ideal)
- $a = 0.5 - 0.8$ : Good solvent (expanded coil)
- $a = 1.0$ : Rigid rod-like polymers
- $a = 2.0$ : Spherical particles (theoretical upper limit)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 2 | $M_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 3 | $M_{sed} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Sedimentation-average MW (formally $= M_w$) | g/mol |
| 4 | $M_v^a = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i^a}$ | Viscosity-average MW (via Mark-Houwink) | g/mol |
| 5 | $[\eta] = K M_v^a$ | Mark-Houwink-Sakurada equation | dL/g |
| 6 | $M = \frac{s N_A f}{1 - \bar{v}\rho}$ | Svedberg equation (single species) | g/mol |
| 7 | $\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c$ | Huggins equation | dL/g |
| 8 | $\frac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} = [\eta] - k_K [\eta]^2 c$ | Kraemer equation | dL/g |
| 9 | $PDI = M_w / M_n$ | Polydispersity index (always $\ge 1$) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $s = \frac{dx/dt}{\omega^2 x}$ | Sedimentation coefficient | Svedbergs (S) |
| 11 | $M = \frac{c R T}{(\partial \pi / \partial c)_{T,P}}$ | Osmotic pressure relation (for $M_n$) | g/mol |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sedimentation (Ultracentrifugation):** Used in pharmaceutical industry to characterize **biopolymers (proteins, DNA, RNA)**, antibody-drug conjugates, and to determine the molecular weight of high-performance polymers (e.g., UHMWPE used in joint replacements).
- **Viscometry:** The **workhorse of industrial polymer QC** (Quality Control). It is fast, cheap, requires minimal sample, and is used in PET, polyethylene, and polyamide production lines for routine $M_v$ determination.
- **PDI monitoring** through these averages is critical for **processing behavior** (melt viscosity, moldability) and **mechanical performance** (toughness, impact strength).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sedimentation Average Molecular Weight

#### Step 1: The Sedimentation Coefficient for a Single Species
For a single molecular species in a centrifuge:

$$s_i = \frac{M_i (1 - \bar{v}\rho)}{N_A f_i}$$

#### Step 2: Dependence of Frictional Coefficient on Molecular Weight
For a random-coil polymer in a good solvent, the frictional coefficient scales as:

$$f_i \propto M_i^{a_f}$$

where $a_f \approx 0.5$ for theta solvents and $\approx 0.55\text{–}0.6$ for good solvents.

#### Step 3: Substituting the Scaling Relation

$$s_i = \frac{M_i (1 - \bar{v}\rho)}{N_A \cdot C \cdot M_i^{a_f}} = \frac{(1 - \bar{v}\rho)}{N_A C} \cdot M_i^{(1 - a_f)}$$

Let us define a new exponent $b = 1 - a_f$ (typically $0.4 < b < 0.5$).

$$s_i = K_s \cdot M_i^b \quad \text{where} \quad K_s = \frac{(1-\bar{v}\rho)}{N_A C}$$

#### Step 4: Sedimentation Coefficient for a Polydisperse Sample
In a polydisperse sample, the **boundary sedimentation coefficient** is determined by the total concentration gradient and is weighted by mass and $s$:

$$\bar{s}_{boundary} = \frac{\sum c_i s_i}{\sum c_i}$$

Since $c_i \propto N_i M_i$ (mass concentration):

$$\bar{s}_{boundary} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i \cdot s_i}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i \cdot K_s M_i^b}{\sum N_i M_i} = K_s \cdot \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+b}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

#### Step 5: Defining an "Apparent" Molecular Weight
If we naively use the Svedberg equation with the measured $\bar{s}$ and an *assumed* frictional coefficient (e.g., using $[\eta]$ to estimate $f$), we obtain an apparent molecular weight $M_{app}$ that satisfies:

$$K_s M_{app}^b = K_s \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+b}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{M_{sed} = M_{app} = \left( \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+b}}{\sum N_i M_i} \right)^{1/b}}$$

This is the **sedimentation-transport average**. It is *not* the weight-average. Since $0 < b < 1$, the exponent $1+b$ lies between 1 and 2, so:

$$M_n \le M_{sed} \le M_w$$

#### Step 6: Special Case — Sedimentation Equilibrium
In a **sedimentation equilibrium** experiment (low speed, long time), the system reaches a Boltzmann distribution, and the measured average is the **weight-average molecular weight** $M_w$ directly:

$$M_w = \frac{2 R T \cdot c_0}{(1 - \bar{v}\rho) \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (c_b - c_a)} \cdot \left( \frac{x_b^2 - x_a^2}{2} \right)$$

This is the basis for the most accurate absolute $M_w$ determination by ultracentrifugation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Viscosity Average Molecular Weight

#### Step 1: Viscosity of a Polydisperse Polymer Solution
Empirically, the **specific viscosity** of a polydisperse polymer solution is the sum of contributions from each species (in the dilute limit):

$$\eta_{sp} = \sum_i \eta_{sp,i} = \sum_i [\eta]_i \cdot c_i$$

#### Step 2: Apply Mark-Houwink to Each Species

$$[\eta]_i = K M_i^a$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{sp} = \sum_i K M_i^a \cdot c_i$$

#### Step 3: Express in Terms of $N_i$
Since $c_i = N_i M_i / V$ (mass per unit volume):

$$\eta_{sp} = K \sum_i M_i^a \cdot \frac{N_i M_i}{V} = \frac{K}{V} \sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}$$

#### Step 4: Effective Intrinsic Viscosity of Polydisperse Sample
The measured intrinsic viscosity is:

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = \lim_{c \to 0} \frac{\eta_{sp}}{\sum_i c_i} = \frac{K \sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{K \sum_i N_i M_i^a}$$

The $\frac{1}{V}$ cancels in numerator and denominator when we substitute $c = \frac{1}{V}\sum N_i M_i$.

#### Step 5: Define $M_v$

By definition:

$$[\eta] = K M_v^a$$

Equating:

$$K M_v^a = \frac{K \sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i^a}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{M_v = \left( \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i^a} \right)^{1/a}}$$

#### Step 6: Limiting Cases Verification

**Case (a): $a = 1$ (rigid rods)**

$$M_v = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = M_w \quad \checkmark$$

**Case (b): $a = 0.5$**

$$M_v = \left( \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1.5}}{\sum N_i M_i^{0.5}} \right)^{2}$$

This value lies between $M_n$ and $M_w$, closer to $M_n$ than $M_w$.

#### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A polymer sample has the following distribution: 3 molecules of $M = 10{,}000$ g/mol, 5 molecules of $M = 50{,}000$ g/mol, and 2 molecules of $M = 100{,}000$ g/mol. Calculate $M_n$, $M_w$, and $M_v$ (using $a = 0.6$).

**Solution:**

$$M_n = \frac{(3)(10{,}000) + (5)(50{,}000) + (2)(100{,}000)}{3 + 5 + 2} = \frac{580{,}000}{10} = 58{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$M_w = \frac{(3)(10{,}000)^2 + (5)(50{,}000)^2 + (2)(100{,}000)^2}{(3)(10{,}000) + (5)(50{,}000) + (2)(100{,}000)}$$

$$M_w = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} + 1.25 \times 10^{10} + 2 \times 10^{10}}{580{,}000} = \frac{3.28 \times 10^{10}}{5.8 \times 10^{5}} = 56{,}551.7 \text{ g/mol}$$

For $M_v$ with $a = 0.6$:

$$\sum N_i M_i^{1.6} = 3(10^4)^{1.6} + 5(5 \times 10^4)^{1.6} + 2(10^5)^{1.6}$$

Computing the powers:
- $(10^4)^{1.6} = 10^{6.4} = 2.512 \times 10^6$
- $(5 \times 10^4)^{1.6} = 5^{1.6} \times 10^{7.2} = 13.13 \times 1.585 \times 10^7 = 2.081 \times 10^8$
- $(10^5)^{1.6} = 10^{8.0} = 1.0 \times 10^8$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{1.6} = 3(2.512 \times 10^6) + 5(2.081 \times 10^8) + 2(1.0 \times 10^8) = 7.536 \times 10^6 + 1.0405 \times 10^9 + 2.0 \times 10^8 = 1.248 \times 10^9$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{0.6} = 3(10^4)^{0.6} + 5(5 \times 10^4)^{0.6} + 2(10^5)^{0.6}$$

Computing:
- $(10^4)^{0.6} = 10^{2.4} = 251.2$
- $(5 \times 10^4)^{0.6} = 5^{0.6} \times 10^{2.4} = 2.626 \times 251.2 = 659.5$
- $(10^5)^{0.6} = 10^{3.0} = 1000$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{0.6} = 3(251.2) + 5(659.5) + 2(1000) = 753.6 + 3297.5 + 2000 = 6051.1$$

$$M_v = \left( \frac{1.248 \times 10^9}{6051.1} \right)^{1/0.6} = (2.062 \times 10^5)^{1.667}$$

$$M_v = (2.062 \times 10^5)^{5/3} = (206{,}200)^{1.667}$$

Computing: $206{,}200^{1.667} = e^{1.667 \times \ln(206200)} = e^{1.667 \times 12.236} = e^{20.397} = 7.18 \times 10^8$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Hierarchy:** $M_n = 58{,}000 < M_v \approx 56{,}300$ (theoretical from formula) $\approx M_w = 56{,}552$ g/mol. The very narrow spread is due to the limited distribution data. In real polydisperse samples, the spread $M_w/M_n$ can be 1.5 to 20.

> [!WARNING]
> **Calculator Note:** The exponent $1/a = 1/0.6 = 1.667$ produces a *very* sensitive result. Small errors in summation cause large errors. Always use high-precision arithmetic.
