> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 Examination:**
> 
> **Core Concepts (Must Memorize):**
> - **8 Types of Degradation:** Thermal, Oxidative, Photo, Mechanical, Chemical, Biological, Radiation, Ozonolysis.
> - **3 Sub-mechanisms of Thermal:** Random scission, Unzipping (zipper), Side-group stripping.
> - **Auto-oxidation cycle:** 4 stages — Initiation, Propagation, Branching, Termination.
> - **Stabilizer Classes:** Primary antioxidants (chain breakers), Secondary antioxidants (hydroperoxide decomposers), HALS, UV absorbers, Antiozonants.
> 
> **Key Equations (Must Derive on Demand):**
> - $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ — Arrhenius.
> - $\overline{M}_n = M_{n,0} / (1 + kt)$ — Random scission.
> - $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$ — First-order half-life.
> - $1/\overline{DP}_n = 1/\overline{DP}_{n,0} + kt$ — Linear scission form.
> 
> **Polymer-Specific Behavior (Frequently Tested):**
> - **PMMA, PTFE, Poly(α-methyl styrene)** → Unzipping (high monomer yield).
> - **PE, PP, PS** → Random scission.
> - **PVC, PVAc** → Side-group stripping (polyene formation).
> - **PET, Nylon-6,6, PC** → Hydrolytic degradation.
> - **NR, SBR, NBR** → Ozonolysis at C=C.
> - **PE** → Crosslinking under radiation.
> - **PMMA, PP** → Scission under radiation.
> - **PLA, PGA, PCL** → Biodegradation.
> 
> **Numerical Constants (Always Carry in Mind):**
> - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
> - $E_a$ range for most polymers: **80 – 250 kJ/mol**
> - UV threshold: $\lambda \leq 290$ nm, $E_{photon} \geq 413$ kJ/mol for C–H.
> 
> **Examiner's Favorite Short Questions:**
> - "Distinguish between random and chain-end scission."
> - "Explain the role of HALS in polymer stabilization."
> - "Why does PVC discolor on heating?"
> - "What is the Bolland-Gee cycle?"
> - "Give two examples of biodegradable polymers."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Reinforcement on the Properties. Properties of Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Reinforcement on the Properties. Properties of Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymer Composites and Reinforcement — The Formal Definition

In the context of **Polymer Technology (PECHT523, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, a **reinforced polymer** is a two-phase heterogeneous material system (a **polymer composite**) in which a continuous **matrix phase** (the polymer resin — thermoplastic or thermoset) is combined with a discrete **reinforcement phase** (fibres, whiskers, particulates, or nanofillers) to produce a synergistic combination of properties that cannot be achieved by the matrix or the reinforcement acting alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT523 Module 2):** "Reinforcement is the incorporation of a stronger, stiffer, or tougher secondary phase into a polymer matrix to enhance one or more engineering properties such as tensile strength, modulus, impact resistance, heat deflection temperature, dimensional stability, fatigue life, and creep resistance, without substantially altering the processability of the base resin."

The **general classification** of reinforcement is:

| Reinforcement Type | Geometry | Aspect Ratio (L/D) | Primary Property Boost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous fibres** | Long, aligned | $\infty$ | Highest stiffness & strength |
| **Short (discontinuous) fibres** | Chopped strands | 10 – 1000 | Balanced strength/processability |
| **Whiskers** | Single-crystal rods | > 1000 | High modulus |
| **Particulate fillers** | Spheres/flakes | ~ 1 | Stiffness, HDT, reduce cost |
| **Nanofillers** | Nano-tubes/clays | > 1000 | Multifunctional (electrical, barrier) |

## 1.2 The Three "M"s of a Composite — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Concrete & Steel Rebar Model"**
> 
> Imagine a **concrete slab** — concrete on its own is strong in compression but brittle and weak in tension. When you embed **steel rebar** inside it, the steel carries the tensile loads (just like the iron rods in RCC buildings), while the concrete holds everything in shape and protects the steel from corrosion.
> 
> In the **polymer world**:
> - The **concrete** = the **polymer matrix** (e.g., epoxy, polyester, nylon, polypropylene) — it is soft, flexible, easy to process, and protects the fibres.
> - The **steel rebar** = the **reinforcement** (glass fibre, carbon fibre, aramid, natural fibre) — it is stiff, strong, and load-bearing.
> - The **bond between them** = the **interfacial adhesion** (assisted by **silanes / coupling agents**) — without a good bond, the rebar simply slips out of the concrete.
> 
> **Result:** The composite slab (or polymer composite) carries loads that neither component could carry alone.

## 1.3 The Six Universal "Rules" of Reinforcement

> [!TIP]
> **The 6 Golden Rules Every KTU Examiner Tests:**
> 1. **Rule of Mixtures** governs upper-bound properties (parallel loading).
> 2. **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** governs lower-bound properties (perpendicular loading).
> 3. **Aspect Ratio (L/D)** of the fibre controls whether it acts as a true load-bearer.
> 4. **Critical Fibre Length ($l_c$)** is the minimum length for effective stress transfer.
> 5. **Fibre Orientation** — aligned > random > perpendicular (for strength).
> 6. **Interfacial Shear Strength ($\tau_i$)** determines load transfer efficiency from matrix → fibre.

## 1.4 Defining the Engineering Properties of Polymers

The "**Properties of Polymers**" component of this topic is a survey of the **physical, mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical, optical, and rheological** properties that define a polymer's fitness for an engineering application. Each property is quantified by standardized tests (ASTM/ISO) and is sensitive to molecular architecture (molecular weight, crystallinity, Tg, crosslink density, branching, copolymer composition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Property of a Polymer:**
> A measurable response of a polymeric material to an applied stimulus (mechanical force, heat, electric field, chemical, light), described by both an **intensive quantity** (independent of sample size) and a **structural interpretation** (how the polymer's molecular structure causes that response).

The **five families of polymer properties** covered under KTU PECHT523 Module 2 are:

1. **Mechanical Properties** — tensile strength, Young's modulus, elongation at break, impact strength, hardness, fatigue, creep.
2. **Thermal Properties** — glass transition temperature ($T_g$), melting temperature ($T_m$), Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT), Vicat softening point, Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE), thermal conductivity, thermal stability (LOI, TGA).
3. **Electrical Properties** — volume/surface resistivity, dielectric strength, dielectric constant, dissipation factor, arc resistance.
4. **Chemical & Physical Properties** — density, moisture absorption, solvent resistance, permeability, weathering, chemical resistance.
5. **Rheological & Processing Properties** — Melt Flow Index (MFI), viscosity, mold-shrinkage, spiral flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Reinforcement Works — The Micromechanical Picture

A pure polymer matrix is held together by **weak secondary bonds** (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding). When a load is applied, polymer chains slide past each other because the inter-chain bond energy is only **~ 5 – 40 kJ/mol**, compared with **covalent C–C bonds of 350 kJ/mol** in the fibre.

A **reinforcing fibre** has:
- Near-perfect atomic alignment (e.g., crystalline graphite planes in carbon fibre).
- Covalent bonds running along the fibre axis.
- Tensile strength of **2,000 – 7,000 MPa** (carbon) vs. **30 – 100 MPa** for an unfilled polymer.

**Mechanism of Reinforcement** (step-by-step):

1. **Matrix deforms** under load.
2. **Matrix transfers load** to the fibre through **shear stresses** at the matrix–fibre interface (this is the **Kelly–Tyson shear-lag model**).
3. **Fibre carries the axial load** along its high-strength covalent backbone.
4. **Fibre debonds / pulls out / fractures** only when the ultimate strength of the fibre-matrix interface is exceeded.
5. **Crack propagation is hindered** by the fibre, increasing toughness.

## 2.2 Quantitative Effects of Reinforcement on Each Property

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect on TENSILE STRENGTH**
> 
> Tensile strength of the composite ($\sigma_c$) follows the **Rule of Mixtures** (parallel-isostrain model):
> 
> $$\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f$$
> 
> where $V_m + V_f = 1$. Unidirectional continuous fibres can boost tensile strength by **5× to 20×**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect on YOUNG'S MODULUS (Stiffness)**
> 
> - **Longitudinal (isostrain / Voigt model):** $E_{cL} = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$
> - **Transverse (isostress / Reuss model):** $\dfrac{1}{E_{cT}} = \dfrac{V_m}{E_m} + \dfrac{V_f}{E_f}$
> 
> Real short-fibre composites are modeled with the **Halpin–Tsai equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect on IMPACT STRENGTH (Toughness)**
> 
> Fibres absorb energy through **three mechanisms**:
> 1. Fibre **fracture**.
> 2. Fibre **pull-out** (the dominant energy sink).
> 3. Fibre **debonding** at the interface.
> 
> Toughness gain: **3× – 10×** depending on fibre length and interface quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect on HEAT DEFLECTION TEMPERATURE (HDT)**
> 
> HDT of a polymer rises dramatically with fibre reinforcement because the fibres **constrain chain mobility**. Example: neat nylon 6,6 HDT ≈ **75 °C**; with 30 % glass fibre → **255 °C** — a **3.4× increase**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect on THERMAL EXPANSION (CTE)**
> 
> Fibres have very low CTE (carbon fibre: $-0.5$ to $0$ µm/m·K). CTE of composite:
> 
> $$\alpha_c \approx \alpha_m V_m + \alpha_f V_f$$
> 
> Used to produce **dimensionally stable** aerospace and electronic substrates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect on CREEP & FATIGUE**
> 
> Fibres drastically **reduce creep** because the matrix flow is restrained by the stiffer fibre network. Fatigue endurance limit can rise by **2× – 3×**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect on ELECTRICAL & BARRIER PROPERTIES (with conductive fillers)**
> 
> Carbon black, CNTs, or graphite nanoplatelets above the **percolation threshold ($\phi_c$)** form a continuous conductive network — used in **EMI shielding, antistatic, and flexible electrodes**.

## 2.3 The Critical Fibre Length — The Most Tested Concept

The **critical fibre length ($l_c$)** is the minimum length required for the fibre to be loaded to its ultimate tensile strength before the matrix fails in shear at the interface.

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{fu}$ = ultimate tensile strength of the fibre
- $d$ = fibre diameter
- $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength (matrix–fibre)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** If $L < l_c$ → fibre **pulls out** (sub-optimal reinforcement). If $L \geq 15 \cdot l_c$ → fibre behaves as **continuous** (full reinforcement efficiency).

## 2.4 The Coupling Agent — The Invisible Hero

Without surface treatment, polymers and most inorganic fibres (glass, silica) are chemically incompatible. **Silanes** (e.g., γ-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, **γ-APS**) form a chemical bridge:

$$\text{Inorganic fibre} \;\; \underset{\substack{\vert \\ \text{Si-OH}}}{\longrightarrow} \;\; \text{Si–O–Si} \;\; \underset{\substack{\vert \\ \text{Organofunctional group R}}}{\longrightarrow} \;\; \text{Polymer matrix}$$

The **R group** reacts with the matrix (epoxy, polyester, PP-maleic anhydride), creating a true **covalent + mechanical + chemical** bond.

## 2.5 Survey of Polymer Properties — Engineering Quantities & Tests

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Standard Test | Typical Polymer Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | $\sigma_t$ | Max stress before break | ASTM D638 | LDPE: 10 MPa; Nylon 6,6: 80 MPa; CF-Epoxy: 2400 MPa |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | Stress / strain in elastic region | ASTM D638 | LDPE: 0.2 GPa; PS: 3 GPa; CF-Epoxy: 150 GPa |
| Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b$ | Strain at fracture | ASTM D638 | LDPE: 400 %; Epoxy: 4 % |
| Impact Strength (Izod) | $I$ | Energy to break notched specimen | ASTM D256 | PS: 21 J/m; PC: 850 J/m; CF-PA66: 250 J/m |
| Hardness (Rockwell M) | HRM | Indentation resistance | ASTM D785 | PP: R90; PMMA: M95 |
| Glass Transition Temp. | $T_g$ | Amorphous chain mobility onset | DSC, DMA | PE: -120 °C; PVC: 80 °C; PS: 100 °C; PC: 150 °C |
| Melting Temp. | $T_m$ | Crystalline phase melting | DSC | HDPE: 130 °C; Nylon 6: 220 °C; PTFE: 327 °C |
| Heat Deflection Temp. | HDT | Temp. at 0.25/0.45 MPa deflection | ASTM D648 | PP: 100 °C; 30%GF-PP: 155 °C; PEEK: 160 °C |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | CTE | $\Delta L / L \cdot \Delta T$ | ASTM D696 | PE: 100–200; CF-Epoxy: 0–1 µm/m·°C |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | Heat flux per °C | ASTM C177 | PS: 0.13; PE: 0.33; CF-Epoxy: 1.0–10 W/m·K |
| Volume Resistivity | $\rho_v$ | Resistance to DC current | ASTM D257 | PE: $10^{16}$; Nylon 6: $10^{13}$ Ω·cm |
| Dielectric Strength | $E_d$ | Max electric field before breakdown | ASTM D149 | PE: 20; PTFE: 60; PVC: 14 kV/mm |
| Density | $\rho$ | Mass per unit volume | ASTM D792 | PP: 0.90; PVC: 1.40; CF-Epoxy: 1.55–1.60 g/cm³ |
| Melt Flow Index | MFI | Mass extruded in 10 min at $T$ and load | ASTM D1238 | LDPE: 25; PP: 12; PEEK: 3 g/10 min |
| Water Absorption | WA | % weight gain at saturation | ASTM D570 | Nylon 6,6: 8.5 %; PP: 0.02 % |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAM**

| # | Property | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rule of Mixtures (Strength)** | $\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f$ | Upper bound, unidirectional continuous fibres |
| 2 | **Rule of Mixtures (Modulus, Long.)** | $E_{cL} = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ | Voigt model — isostrain |
| 3 | **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** | $1/E_{cT} = V_m/E_m + V_f/E_f$ | Reuss model — isostress, transverse |
| 4 | **Halpin–Tsai (Modulus)** | $E_c = E_m \dfrac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, $\eta = \dfrac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2}$ | Short-fibre composites |
| 5 | **Critical Fibre Length** | $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / 2\tau_i$ | Min. fibre length for full loading |
| 6 | **Density of Composite** | $\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$ | Used in specific strength & modulus |
| 7 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_c / \rho_c$ | Strength-to-weight ratio |
| 8 | **Specific Modulus** | $E_c / \rho_c$ | Stiffness-to-weight ratio |
| 9 | **Voigt–Reuss Bounds** | $E_{cT} \le E_c \le E_{cL}$ | True modulus always lies between |
| 10 | **Percolation Threshold** | $\sigma = \sigma_0 (\phi - \phi_c)^t$ | Conductive filler network onset |
| 11 | **Glass Transition (Fox Eqn.)** | $1/T_g = w_1/T_{g1} + w_2/T_{g2}$ | Copolymer Tg from monomer weights |
| 12 | **Tg (Crystalline Polymer) — Beaman** | $T_g \approx 2/3 \, T_m$ (K) | Empirical, for symmetric polymers |
| 13 | **Crystallinity by DSC** | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\%$ | % crystallinity from melting enthalpy |
| 14 | **HDT Increase (Rule of Thumb)** | HDT$_{composite}$ ≈ $\approx$ 1.5–3 × HDT$_{neat}$ | Quick design check |
| 15 | **Mark–Houwink** | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Intrinsic viscosity → molecular weight |
| 16 | **Eyring (Viscosity-Temp.)** | $\eta = A \exp(E_\eta / RT)$ | Process viscosity at any temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **NEVER write absolute value bars as `\|` inside the table cells of your answer sheet — examiners dock marks for formatting errors. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rule of Mixtures for Modulus (Voigt / Isostrain Model)

We consider a unidirectional continuous-fibre composite where the fibre and the matrix are bonded perfectly, and both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ when a tensile load is applied parallel to the fibre axis (this is the **isostrain** condition, also called the **Voigt model**).

**Step 1 — Define the geometric arrangement:**

A composite of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A_c$ contains fibres of total cross-section $A_f$ and matrix of total cross-section $A_m$, so:

$$A_c = A_f + A_m$$

**Step 2 — Volume fractions:**

$$V_f = \frac{A_f}{A_c}, \quad V_m = \frac{A_m}{A_c}, \quad V_f + V_m = 1$$

**Step 3 — Force equilibrium:**

When the load $F$ is applied, the total force is shared between the fibre and the matrix:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

Dividing throughout by $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply the isostrain condition:**

Because both phases are bonded and deform together:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

Using Hooke's law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$):

$$E_f \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \varepsilon \cdot V_m = E_c \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\varepsilon$ (since $\varepsilon \neq 0$):**

$$\boxed{\,E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m\,} \quad \text{(Voigt / Rule of Mixtures for Modulus)}$$

This represents the **upper bound** of the composite modulus.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Reuss / Isostress Model)

For loading applied **perpendicular** to the fibres, both phases experience the **same stress** but **different strains** (this is the **isostress** or **Reuss model**).

**Step 1 — Isostress condition:**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Strain compatibility (additive strains):**

The total elongation is the sum of fibre and matrix elongations weighted by thickness:

$$\Delta L_c \, t_c = \Delta L_f \, t_f + \Delta L_m \, t_m$$

In terms of strain ($\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$):

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law for each phase:**

$$\varepsilon = \sigma / E$$

$$\frac{\sigma}{E_{cT}} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\sigma$:**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{1}{E_{cT}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}\,} \quad \text{(Reuss / Inverse Rule of Mixtures)}$$

This is the **lower bound** of the composite modulus. Therefore:

$$E_{cT} \le E_c \le E_{cL}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Critical Fibre Length $l_c$

Consider a single short fibre of length $L$ and diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix, loaded in tension.

**Step 1 — Force on the fibre at breaking point:**

If the fibre is to be loaded to its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$, the axial tensile force is:

$$F = \sigma_{fu} \cdot A_f = \sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Force transferred by the interface (shear-lag):**

The load enters and exits the fibre through **shear stress $\tau_i$** acting on the **cylindrical surface area** of the embedded length. The shear force at one end is:

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot A_{interface} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

(Here $L/2$ is the half-length on which shear acts at one end, since load is symmetric.)

**Step 3 — Equate tensile force to twice the shear force (both ends):**

$$\sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 2 \cdot \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

Wait — a cleaner derivation uses the full length $L$ (load must be transferred from **both** ends):

$$\sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L$:**

$$L = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}$$

This is the **minimum length** for the fibre to reach $\sigma_{fu}$ simultaneously at its **mid-point**. Conventionally, the **critical fibre length** is defined as **twice** this value, so that the **entire fibre** (not just the mid-point) is at $\sigma_{fu}$:

$$\boxed{\,l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio check:**

If $L \geq 15 \, l_c$, the fibre behaves as **continuous**; if $L < l_c$, the fibre **pulls out** without reaching its ultimate strength.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:**
A unidirectional glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite contains **60 % by volume of glass fibres**. Given:
- $E_{fibre} = 72$ GPa, $\sigma_{fibre} = 3.5$ GPa
- $E_{matrix} = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{matrix} = 60$ MPa
- Fibre diameter $d = 10 \, \mu m$
- Interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 40$ MPa

Calculate:
1. Longitudinal modulus $E_{cL}$
2. Transverse modulus $E_{cT}$
3. Longitudinal tensile strength $\sigma_{cL}$
4. Critical fibre length $l_c$

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Longitudinal Modulus (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40)$$

$$E_{cL} = 43.2 + 1.4 = 44.6 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (b) — Transverse Modulus (Inverse Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{cT}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.60}{72} + \frac{0.40}{3.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{cT}} = 0.00833 + 0.11429 = 0.12262 \, \text{GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{cT} = \frac{1}{0.12262} = 8.156 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (c) — Longitudinal Tensile Strength:**

Assuming fibre strain-to-failure $\varepsilon_{fu} = 2.5\%$ and matrix strain-to-failure $\varepsilon_{mu} = 2.0\%$, the **matrix fails first** (smaller strain). Strength is governed by the matrix strain:

$$\sigma_{cL} = \sigma_f V_f \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{mu}}{\varepsilon_{fu}} + \sigma_m V_m$$

But if both fail at the same strain, simply use the rule of mixtures:

$$\sigma_{cL} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m = (3500)(0.60) + (60)(0.40)$$

$$\sigma_{cL} = 2100 + 24 = 2124 \text{ MPa} \approx 2.12 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (d) — Critical Fibre Length:**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(3.5 \times 10^9)(10 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (40 \times 10^6)}$$

$$l_c = \frac{35{,}000}{80 \times 10^6} = 4.375 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.4375 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{l_c = 437.5 \, \mu m \approx 0.44 \text{ mm}}$$

**For full reinforcement, the fibre length must be:**

$$L \geq 15 \, l_c = 6.56 \text{ mm}$$

This is why **chopped glass strands** used in injection-moulded composites are typically **3–6 mm**, with the longer end approaching full efficiency.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Calculating % Crystallinity by DSC

**Given:** A DSC scan of polypropylene gives $\Delta H_f = 90$ J/g. The literature value for 100 % crystalline PP is $\Delta H_f^0 = 207$ J/g.

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$\chi_c (\%) = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\chi_c (\%) = \frac{90}{207} \times 100 = 43.5 \%$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:**

The PP sample is **43.5 % crystalline**; the rest (56.5 %) is the amorphous phase, which controls properties like $T_g$, impact strength, and transparency.

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: Glass Transition by Fox Equation for a Copolymer

**Given:** A styrene–butadiene copolymer contains $w_1 = 0.30$ (PS fraction) and $w_2 = 0.70$ (PB fraction). $T_{g1}$ (PS) = 373 K, $T_{g2}$ (PB) = 173 K.

**Step 1 — Write the Fox equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.30}{373} + \frac{0.70}{173} = 8.04 \times 10^{-4} + 4.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 4.85 \times 10^{-3} \, K^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Invert:**

$$T_g = \frac{1}{4.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 206 \text{ K} = -67 \, °C$$

This explains why SBR (styrene-butadiene rubber) remains a **rubber at room temperature**, while pure PS is a hard, glassy solid at 25 °C.

**Interpretation:**
- The **slope** of the linear elastic region represents Young's modulus.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** 2–3 sentences with a diagram or formula. Marks awarded for: (1) Definition 1 mark, (2) Explanation 1 mark, (3) Example/Application 1 mark.

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **Rule of Mixtures** as applied to fibre-reinforced polymer composites. Write the formula for the longitudinal Young's modulus.

**Model Answer:**

The Rule of Mixtures states that, for a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite loaded parallel to the fibre axis (isostrain condition), the longitudinal modulus $E_{cL}$ is a weighted average of the fibre modulus $E_f$ and the matrix modulus $E_m$, with the volume fractions $V_f$ and $V_m$ acting as the weights:

$$E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

This equation represents the **upper bound** of the composite modulus and is valid for perfectly bonded, continuous, aligned fibres. *Example:* 30 % glass-fibre-reinforced PP has $E_{cL}$ ≈ 6 GPa, against ≈ 1.5 GPa for neat PP.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the **critical fibre length**? Mention its significance in designing short-fibre composites.

**Model Answer:**

The critical fibre length $l_c$ is the minimum length of a short fibre required for the fibre to be loaded up to its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ through shear transfer at the matrix–fibre interface:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

**Significance:** If the actual fibre length $L < l_c$, the fibre pulls out of the matrix and never reaches its full strength, resulting in poor reinforcement. Hence, for effective reinforcement, chopped fibres used in injection-moulded composites must have $L \geq 15 \cdot l_c$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Long Answer with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Full derivation, formula, and numerical evaluation are required. Internal choice means students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the **Rule of Mixtures** for the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional continuous-fibre-reinforced polymer composite. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.45$, $E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, fibre diameter $d = 12 \, \mu m$, fibre ultimate strength $\sigma_{fu} = 3.0$ GPa, and interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 35$ MPa. Calculate the **longitudinal modulus**, the **critical fibre length**, and the **minimum fibre length** required for full reinforcement. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Rule of Mixtures (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. Fibres are continuous, uniform, and perfectly aligned with the load.
2. Perfect bonding at the matrix–fibre interface (no slip).
3. Both phases experience the same strain (isostrain / Voigt model).
4. The composite is homogeneous and free of voids.

*Derivation:*

Consider a composite of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A_c = A_f + A_m$.

By force equilibrium, the load on the composite equals the sum of the loads carried by fibre and matrix:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

Dividing by $A_c$ and defining $V_f = A_f / A_c$, $V_m = A_m / A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m \quad \text{...(1)}$$

By the isostrain assumption, $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$.

Applying Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) in Equation (1):

$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \varepsilon \cdot V_m$$

Cancelling $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{\,E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m\,}$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures** (Voigt upper bound) for the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional continuous-fibre composite.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

*Given:*
$V_f = 0.45, \; V_m = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$
$E_f = 70$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
$d = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\sigma_{fu} = 3.0 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\tau_i = 35 \times 10^6$ Pa

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (70)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)$$

$$E_{cL} = 31.5 + 1.925 = 33.43 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Critical Fibre Length:**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^9)(12 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (35 \times 10^6)}$$

$$l_c = \frac{36{,}000}{70 \times 10^6} = 5.143 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$l_c = 0.5143 \text{ mm} \approx 514.3 \, \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Length for Full Reinforcement:**

$$L_{min} = 15 \times l_c = 15 \times 0.5143 = 7.71 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $E_{cL} = 33.43$ GPa
- $l_c = 0.514$ mm
- $L_{min} = 7.71$ mm

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the effect of **fibre reinforcement** on the following properties of a polymer composite: (i) tensile strength, (ii) modulus, (iii) impact strength, (iv) heat deflection temperature, and (v) coefficient of thermal expansion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of **coupling agents** (e.g., silanes) in polymer composites. With a suitable sketch, explain how a silane molecule forms a chemical bridge between a glass fibre and an epoxy matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Effects of Fibre Reinforcement (7 marks):**

| Property | Effect | Mechanism | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Tensile strength | Increases significantly | Load transferred to high-strength fibre via interfacial shear | 5× to 20× increase (e.g., 30 % CF-epoxy) |
| (ii) Modulus (stiffness) | Increases | Fibres are 10–100× stiffer than matrix; load shared per rule of mixtures | 3× to 50× increase |
| (iii) Impact strength | Increases (for ductile fibres) or decreases (for brittle ones) | Energy absorbed by fibre pull-out, fracture, debonding | 3× to 10× increase with aramid/CF |
| (iv) HDT | Increases sharply | Fibres constrain polymer chain mobility above $T_g$ | Neat PA66 = 75 °C; 30 % GF = 255 °C |
| (v) CTE | Decreases (often by 10×) | Fibres (e.g., carbon: $-0.5$ µm/m·K) dominate dimensional change | 10× to 100× reduction |

The effect is governed by:
- The **Rule of Mixtures** (for properties parallel to fibre).
- The **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** (for properties perpendicular to fibre).
- The **Halpin–Tsai equation** (for short/discontinuous fibres).

**Key design equation** (longitudinal strength, continuous fibre):

$$\sigma_{cL} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Key design equation** (transverse modulus):

$$\frac{1}{E_{cT}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

---

**(b) Role of Coupling Agents (7 marks):**

A **coupling agent** is a bifunctional molecule applied as a thin surface coating on the reinforcement (especially glass fibres) to:
1. **Improve wetting** of the fibre by the polymer.
2. **Form a chemical bond** between the inorganic fibre surface and the organic polymer.
3. **Reduce moisture ingress** at the interface.
4. **Increase interfacial shear strength** $\tau_i$, and hence reduce $l_c$.

**Silanes (e.g., γ-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, γ-APS):**

A silane molecule has the general structure:

$$(\text{RO})_3\text{Si} - (\text{CH}_2)_n - \text{R}'$$

where:
- $\text{RO}$ = alkoxy group (e.g., $\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5$) — reacts with the **inorganic fibre (Si–OH)**.
- $\text{R}'$ = organofunctional group (e.g., $-\text{NH}_2$ for epoxies) — reacts with the **organic polymer**.

**Mechanism (step-by-step):**

1. **Hydrolysis** of silane in water: $(\text{RO})_3\text{Si–R}' \rightarrow (\text{HO})_3\text{Si–R}'$
2. **Hydrogen bonding** with Si–OH on the glass surface: $\text{Si–OH} \cdots \text{HO–Si–R}'$
3. **Condensation** on drying: $\text{Si–O–Si} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (covalent bond formed)
4. **Reaction of R' with matrix:** for example, $\text{–NH}_2$ opens the **epoxide ring** of the epoxy resin, forming a covalent C–N bond.

The result is a **covalently bonded bridge**:

$$\underbrace{\text{Glass–O–Si(CH}_2)_3\text{–NH}_2}_{\text{reacts with glass}} \;\; \longrightarrow \;\; \underbrace{\text{...–CH}_2\text{–NH–CH}_2\text{–CH(OH)–}}_{\text{reacts with epoxy}}$$

```
       GLASS SURFACE                  EPOXY MATRIX
  ┌─────────────────┐           ┌────────────────────┐
  │   Si — OH       │           │                    │
  │   Si — OH       │   ━━╋━━   │   O   CH2          │
  │   Si — O — Si   ←——┤       │   ‖   |            │
  │        |        │  SILANE  │   C — C             │
  │        (CH2)3   │ BRIDGE   │   |   |            │
  │        |        │           │   NH — CH2 — ...   │
  │        NH2      │           │                    │
  └─────────────────┘           └────────────────────┘
```

**Significance:**
- Without silane, glass–epoxy interface has $\tau_i \approx 10$ MPa (poor).
- With silane, $\tau_i \approx 50$ MPa (excellent) — a 5× increase.
- $l_c$ is halved, so shorter fibres can be used effectively.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing longitudinal and transverse modulus** — these follow **different** equations. Longitudinal → Rule of Mixtures. Transverse → Inverse Rule of Mixtures. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 
> 2. **Wrong use of volume fraction** — always use $V_f$, not **weight fraction** $w_f$. To convert: $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting units in critical fibre length derivation** — $\sigma_{fu}$ must be in Pa, $d$ in metres, $\tau_i$ in Pa. Final answer in metres; convert to mm.
> 
> 4. **Assuming $l_c$ is the optimum length** — actually $L = 15 \cdot l_c$ is needed for full reinforcement efficiency. $l_c$ is just the **minimum** length for the mid-point to reach $\sigma_{fu}$.
> 
> 5. **Writing modulus in MPa instead of GPa** — always convert: $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa}$.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up $T_g$ and $T_m$** — $T_g$ is for **amorphous** polymers; $T_m$ is for **crystalline** polymers. Semi-crystalline polymers have **both** ($T_g < T_m$).
> 
> 7. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in derivations — Voigt/Reuss models require explicit mention of "isostrain/isostrain" and "perfect bonding" assumptions. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for missing assumptions.
> 
> 8. **Plotting curves without units or labels** — stress–strain curves must have axes labelled with units (MPa, %) and curves identified (neat vs. composite).

---

## 5.4 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **📌 One-Page Rapid Revision — Module 2 Highlights**

- ✅ A **reinforced polymer** = matrix (continuous) + reinforcement (discrete), giving properties neither phase has alone.
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures** (longitudinal, isostrain): $E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — **upper bound**.
- ✅ **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** (transverse, isostress): $1/E_{cT} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — **lower bound**.
- ✅ **Critical fibre length**: $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / 2 \tau_i$ — minimum length for full fibre loading.
- ✅ **Effective reinforcement** requires $L \geq 15 \cdot l_c$.
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures for strength**: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$.
- ✅ **Halpin–Tsai equation** for short-fibre composites.
- ✅ **Coupling agents (silanes)** create a chemical bridge between inorganic fibre and organic matrix.
- ✅ **Fibre orientation ranking (for strength)**: unidirectional > cross-ply > random planar > perpendicular.
- ✅ **Higher $V_f$** → higher strength/modulus but **lower ductility, toughness, and processability**.
- ✅ **Reinforcement effects on thermal properties**: HDT ↑, CTE ↓, thermal conductivity ↑ (for carbon/metal fillers).
- ✅ **Six families of polymer properties**: mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical, optical, rheological.
- ✅ **Key mechanical properties**: tensile strength ($\sigma_t$), Young's modulus ($E$), elongation at break ($\varepsilon_b$), impact strength ($I$), hardness, fatigue, creep.
- ✅ **Key thermal properties**: $T_g$, $T_m$, HDT, Vicat, CTE, $k$, TGA, LOI.
- ✅ **Key electrical properties**: volume resistivity ($\rho_v$), dielectric strength ($E_d$), dielectric constant ($\varepsilon'$), $\tan \delta$.
- ✅ **Tg by Fox equation** (copolymers): $1/T_g = w_1/T_{g1} + w_2/T_{g2}$.
- ✅ **Beaman's rule** (symmetric crystalline polymers): $T_g \approx 2/3 \cdot T_m$ (in K).
- ✅ **% Crystallinity** (DSC): $\chi_c = \Delta H_f / \Delta H_f^0 \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Mark–Houwink equation** gives molecular weight from intrinsic viscosity: $[\eta] = K M^a$.
- ✅ **Specific strength** $(\sigma/\rho)$ and **specific modulus** $(E/\rho)$ are key composite selection metrics for aerospace.
- ✅ **HDT increases ~ 1.5–3×** with 30 % glass fibre; this is a classic KTU exam question.
- ✅ **Carbon fibre composite CTE** can be made **zero or negative** by adjusting $V_f$ and orientation — used in space structures.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheology - Viscous Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rheology - Viscous Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Rheology and Viscous Flow

**Rheology** is the branch of physics and materials science that studies the **deformation and flow of matter**, particularly the complex viscoelastic behavior of polymers that exhibit both viscous (liquid-like) and elastic (solid-like) responses under applied stress. The term was coined by Eugene Bingham in 1920, derived from the Greek word *rheo* meaning "to flow."

**Viscous Flow** in polymer science refers to the irreversible, time-dependent deformation of a polymer melt or concentrated solution under an applied shear stress, where the energy is dissipated as heat rather than stored. For a polymer to undergo viscous flow, it must be heated above its characteristic transition temperatures—either above the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** for amorphous polymers or above the **melting temperature ($T_m$)** for semi-crystalline polymers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Rheology - Viscous Flow is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 2. Students are expected to master Newton's Law of Viscosity, distinguish Newtonian from non-Newtonian behavior, derive the Power Law model, and apply the WLF/Arrhenius equations to solve numerical problems.

## 1.2 Fundamental Concepts and Units

The three foundational variables in rheology are:

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$)**: The force per unit area applied tangentially to a fluid layer, measured in **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²**.
- **Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$ or $\gamma'$)**: The velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction, measured in **reciprocal seconds (s⁻¹)**.
- **Viscosity ($\eta$)**: The internal resistance of a fluid to flow, measured in **Pascal-seconds (Pa·s)** or **Poise (P)** where 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Honey & Water Comparison

Imagine pouring **water** versus **honey** from a container onto a flat surface. Water flows out rapidly, spreading quickly into a thin film. Honey, in contrast, oozes out sluggishly, holding its shape for a longer time. Both are liquids, but honey has a much higher **viscosity**—it resists deformation and flow more strongly.

Now imagine **ketchup** that refuses to come out of the bottle, but once you shake it, it flows freely. This is **non-Newtonian behavior**—the viscosity changes with applied shear rate. Polymer melts and solutions almost always display this kind of complex, shear-dependent flow behavior, which is why rheology is a cornerstone of polymer processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering impact:** Understanding viscous flow is critical in polymer processing operations such as **extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, film blowing, and fiber spinning**. The viscosity dictates the pressure required, the throughput rate, and the energy consumption of the process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity

The foundational equation of viscous flow was formulated by Sir Isaac Newton in 1687. For a fluid confined between two parallel plates separated by a distance $dy$, where the top plate moves with velocity $dv$ relative to the bottom (stationary) plate:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \cdot \frac{dv}{dy}$$

**Where:**
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa)
- $\eta$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = Shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $\frac{dv}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (s⁻¹)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fluid is classified as **Newtonian** when the viscosity $\eta$ remains **constant** regardless of the applied shear rate. Examples: water, air, glycerin, low-molecular-weight oils.

## 2.2 Classification of Fluids Based on Flow Behavior

### 2.2.1 Newtonian Fluids
- The shear stress versus shear rate plot is a **straight line passing through the origin**.
- The slope (viscosity) is constant.
- Examples: Water (1 mPa·s at 20°C), air (0.018 mPa·s), mercury.

### 2.2.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids
Most polymer melts and solutions are non-Newtonian, meaning viscosity changes with shear rate. They are classified as **time-independent** or **time-dependent** based on whether the viscosity changes with the duration of shearing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Newton's Law | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ in Pa, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹ | Defining viscosity in simple fluids |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$ | $\nu$ in m²/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³ | Comparing flow behavior independent of density |
| 3 | Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ), $n$ = flow behavior index | Extrusion die design, melt processing |
| 4 | Apparent Viscosity | $\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Pa·s | Real viscosity at a given shear rate |
| 5 | Bingham Plastic Model | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_y$ = yield stress (Pa), $\eta_p$ = plastic viscosity | Toothpaste, drilling mud, filled polymers |
| 6 | Casson Model | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\eta_\infty$ = infinite-shear viscosity | Chocolate, printing inks |
| 7 | Arrhenius Equation (T-dependence) | $\eta = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_\eta$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Predicting viscosity above $T_m$ or $T_g + 100°C$ |
| 8 | WLF Equation (T-dependence near $T_g$) | $\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$ | $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K | Polymer melt processing below $T_g + 100°C$ |
| 9 | Zero-Shear Viscosity vs M.W. | $\eta_0 = K \cdot \bar{M}_w^{3.4}$ | For $M > M_c$ (critical entanglement M.W.) | Predicting processability from molecular weight |
| 10 | Mark-Houwink Equation | $[\eta] = K \cdot M^a$ | $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity (dL/g), $0.5 \leq a \leq 0.8$ | Determining molecular weight from solution viscosity |
| 11 | Carreau Model | $\eta = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\left[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2\right]^{(n-1)/2}$ | $\lambda$ = time constant (s), $\eta_0$, $\eta_\infty$ | Full-range shear-rate behavior of polymers |
| 12 | Free Volume Fraction | $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$ | $\alpha_f$ = thermal expansion coefficient of free volume | Explaining WLF equation theoretically |
| 13 | Recoverable Shear Strain | $S_R = \frac{\tau}{\eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}} = \frac{1}{n} - 1$ (for power law) | Dimensionless | Die swell (extrudate swell) prediction |
| 14 | Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (capillary) | $\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s), $R$ = tube radius (m) | Capillary rheometry calculations |
| 15 | Rabinowitsch Correction | $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_{app} \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)$ | $n$ = power law index | Correcting for non-Newtonian flow in capillary dies |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION WARNING:** When writing shear-rate, viscosity, or temperature in tables, never use the vertical bar `|` (e.g., $|\dot{\gamma}|$) as it breaks markdown table syntax. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value notation, or simply $\eta$, $\dot{\gamma}$, $T$ without delimiters.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Extrusion**: The Power Law model determines the pressure drop and throughput in an extruder die.
- **Injection Molding**: WLF/Arrhenius equations predict melt viscosity at the filling temperature, dictating the injection pressure and cycle time.
- **Blow Molding & Film Blowing**: The Carreau model captures the full shear-rate response, including shear-thinning at high rates.
- **Quality Control**: Zero-shear viscosity is correlated with molecular weight distribution, enabling rapid QC of polymer grades.
- **Process Simulation**: Software like Moldflow, Polyflow, and ANSYS Fluent all require the above constitutive equations as inputs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Apparent Viscosity from the Power Law Model

The Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) model is the most commonly used constitutive equation for polymer melts in the shear rate range of typical processing operations (10 to 10,000 s⁻¹).

**Starting Equation (Power Law):**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

**Step 1 — Recall Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

By definition, apparent viscosity is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Power Law expression for $\tau$:**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent on $\dot{\gamma}$:**

Since $\frac{\dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$:

$$\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

- If $n = 1$: $\eta_{app} = K$ → Newtonian behavior (K becomes the constant viscosity).
- If $n < 1$: $\eta_{app}$ **decreases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → **Pseudoplastic / Shear-thinning** behavior.
- If $n > 1$: $\eta_{app}$ **increases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → **Dilatant / Shear-thickening** behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> Most polymer melts exhibit $n$ between **0.2 and 0.6**, indicating strong shear-thinning. This is highly beneficial in processing because the melt flows easily under high shear (in the die) but retains shape at low shear (after extrusion).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rabinowitsch Correction for Non-Newtonian Flow in a Capillary

In capillary rheometry, the **apparent** shear rate at the wall is calculated assuming Newtonian flow. For non-Newtonian fluids, a correction must be applied.

**Step 1 — Apparent wall shear rate (Newtonian assumption):**

For a fluid flowing through a circular tube of radius $R$ with volumetric flow rate $Q$:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — True wall shear rate from the Rabinowitsch-Mooney equation:**

For a power-law fluid, the true shear rate at the wall is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_{app} \left( \frac{3n + 1}{4n} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Verification with limiting case $n = 1$:**

If the fluid is Newtonian ($n = 1$):

$$\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_{app} \left( \frac{3(1) + 1}{4(1)} \right) = \dot{\gamma}_{app} \left( \frac{4}{4} \right) = \dot{\gamma}_{app}$$

This correctly recovers the Newtonian result, validating the derivation.

**Step 4 — Practical Example (KTU Problem Format):**

Given: $Q = 10 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$, $R = 1 \text{ mm}$, $n = 0.4$.

First, compute the apparent shear rate:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{4 \times 10 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}{\pi \times (1 \text{ mm})^3} = \frac{40}{\pi} \text{ s}^{-1} \approx 12.73 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Then apply the Rabinowitsch correction:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{true} = 12.73 \times \frac{3(0.4) + 1}{4(0.4)} = 12.73 \times \frac{2.2}{1.6} = 12.73 \times 1.375 \approx 17.51 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Dependence of Viscosity (WLF Equation)

**Step 1 — Start with Doolittle equation for viscosity:**

$$\eta = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

where $f$ is the fractional free volume and $A$, $B$ are empirical constants.

**Step 2 — Express free volume as a function of temperature:**

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $f_g$ is the free volume at $T_g$ and $\alpha_f$ is the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

**Step 3 — Take the ratio at two temperatures to eliminate constants:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = B\left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $f = f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = B \cdot \frac{f_g - f}{f \cdot f_g} = -\frac{B}{f_g} \cdot \frac{(T - T_g)}{(f_g / \alpha_f) + (T - T_g)}$$

**Step 5 — Define universal constants:**

Setting $C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \cdot f_g} \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f} \approx 51.6$ K, and converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

This is the **Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF) equation**, valid in the range $T_g$ to $T_g + 100$ K.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why WLF is important:** The WLF equation is universal for most polymers, meaning $C_1$ and $C_2$ are nearly constant (17.44 and 51.6) for nearly all amorphous polymers. This makes it incredibly useful for predicting the processing behavior of any polymer once $T_g$ is known.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect of Molecular Weight on Zero-Shear Viscosity

**Step 1 — Empirical observation:**

For polymer melts, the zero-shear viscosity $\eta_0$ depends strongly on the weight-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_w$.

**Step 2 — Below the critical entanglement molecular weight $M_c$:**

For $M < M_c$, viscosity scales linearly:

$$\eta_0 \propto \bar{M}_w^{1.0}$$

**Step 3 — Above $M_c$, entanglement effects dominate:**

For $M > M_c$, the scaling becomes a **3.4 power law**:

$$\eta_0 = K \cdot \bar{M}_w^{3.4}$$

**Step 4 — Combined form (Fox-Flory):**

$$\eta_0 = K_1 \cdot \bar{M}_w \quad \text{for } M \leq M_c$$

$$\eta_0 = K_2 \cdot \bar{M}_w^{3.4} \quad \text{for } M > M_c$$

**Engineering Insight:** A small change in molecular weight (e.g., a 10% increase) can cause a **35% increase** in viscosity (since $1.10^{3.4} \approx 1.38$). This makes molecular weight control critical in polymer manufacturing.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Behavior Type | $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ Shape | $\eta$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ | Typical $n$ Value | Example |
|---------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|---------|
| Newtonian | Straight line through origin | Constant | $n = 1$ | Water, glycerol |
| Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) | Concave down | Decreases | $n < 1$ | Polymer melts, blood, paint |
| Dilatant (Shear-thickening) | Concave up | Increases | $n > 1$ | Cornstarch + water slurry |
| Bingham Plastic | Linear with yield stress | Constant (after yielding) | $n = 1$, $\tau_y > 0$ | Toothpaste, ketchup |
| Herschel-Bulkley | Curved with yield stress | Shear-thinning with $\tau_y$ | $n < 1$, $\tau_y > 0$ | Blood, drilling mud, filled polymers |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Behavior Type | Definition | Engineering Example |
|---------------|------------|---------------------|
| Rheopectic | Viscosity **increases** with time of shearing | Printer's ink, gypsum paste |
| Thixotropic | Viscosity **decreases** with time of shearing, recovers at rest | Paint, coatings, whipped cream |
| Viscoelastic | Exhibits both viscous flow and elastic recovery | All polymer melts & solutions |

## 3.7 Comprehensive Worked Example — WLF Calculation (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A polymer has $T_g = 100°C$ and $\eta_{T_g} = 10^{12}$ Pa·s. Calculate the viscosity at $T = 150°C$ using the WLF equation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T - T_g = 150 - 100 = 50 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the WLF equation with universal constants:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{17.44 \times 50}{51.6 + 50}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator and numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 17.44 \times 50 = 872$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 51.6 + 50 = 101.6$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{872}{101.6} = -8.583$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the viscosity ratio:**

$$\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}} = 10^{-8.583} = 2.61 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the absolute viscosity:**

$$\eta_{150°C} = 10^{12} \times 2.61 \times 10^{-9} = 2.61 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa·s} \approx 2610 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The viscosity drops by **9 orders of magnitude** with a 50 K increase above $T_g$, demonstrating the extreme temperature sensitivity of polymer melts near the glass transition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shear Flow Between Parallel Plates — Physical Setup

The following mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical shear flow geometry used to derive Newton's law of viscosity. The top plate moves with velocity $v$ while the bottom plate is stationary; the fluid fills the gap of height $h$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stationary Bottom Plate: v = 0] --> B[Fluid Layer dy Above Bottom]
    B --> C[Velocity v = dv at height y]
    C --> D[Fluid Layer dy Above]
    D --> E[Moving Top Plate: v = V]
    
    F[Applied Force F] --> G[Top Plate Area A]
    G --> H[Shear Stress tau = F divided by A]
    
    I[Top Plate Velocity V] --> J[Plate Separation h]
    J --> K[Shear Rate gamma dot = V divided by h]
    
    H --> L[Newton Law: tau = eta times gamma dot]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Viscosity eta in Pa times s]
```

## 4.2 Flow Curve Classification — Rheological Behavior Map

This schematic categorizes the eight major flow behaviors by plotting shear stress vs. shear rate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shear Stress tau on Y axis] --> B[Shear Rate gamma dot on X axis]
    B --> C{Behavior Type}
    
    C --> D[Newtonian: Linear, n = 1]
    C --> E[Pseudoplastic: Concave down, n less than 1]
    C --> F[Dilatant: Concave up, n greater than 1]
    C --> G[Bingham: Linear with yield stress tau y]
    C --> H[Herschel Bulkley: Curved with tau y]
    C --> I[Thixotropic: Hysteresis loop, time dependent]
    C --> J[Rheopectic: Viscosity increases with time]
    C --> K[Viscoelastic: Recovers shape after stress removed]
    
    D --> D1[Example: Water, Glycerin]
    E --> E1[Example: Polymer melts, Blood, Paint]
    F --> F1[Example: Cornstarch suspension]
    G --> G1[Example: Toothpaste, Ketchup]
    H --> H1[Example: Filled polymers, Mud]
    I --> I1[Example: Paints, Coatings]
    J --> J1[Example: Gypsum paste, Inks]
    K --> K1[Example: All polymer melts, Silly putty]
```

## 4.3 Viscosity vs. Shear Rate — Universal Polymer Melt Behavior

The Carreau model captures the three distinct regions of polymer melt rheology. The architecture diagram below explains each region and its physical origin.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Polymer Melt Viscosity Curve] --> B[Region 1: Zero Shear Plateau]
    A --> C[Region 2: Shear Thinning Transition]
    A --> D[Region 3: Infinite Shear Plateau]
    
    B --> B1[Constant viscosity eta 0 at low shear rates]
    B1 --> B2[Entangled chains in equilibrium, full network intact]
    B2 --> B3[Flow is linear and recoverable]
    
    C --> C1[Viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate]
    C1 --> C2[Entanglements disentangle, chains align in flow direction]
    C2 --> C3[Power law region: eta = K times gamma dot to power n minus 1]
    
    D --> D1[Constant viscosity eta infinity at very high shear]
    D1 --> D2[Complete chain alignment, no further structural change]
    D2 --> D3[Second Newtonian plateau]
    
    E[Carreau Equation] --> E1[eta = eta infinity + eta 0 minus eta infinity times bracket 1 plus lambda gamma dot squared bracket to power n minus 1 divided by 2]
```

## 4.4 Free Volume Theory — Molecular Picture of Viscous Flow

This schematic depicts how temperature affects free volume, which governs viscous flow in polymers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Polymer Chains at Low Temperature] --> B[Close Packing, Low Free Volume]
    B --> C[High Viscosity, No Flow, Glassy State]
    
    D[Polymer Chains at High Temperature] --> E[Expanded Free Volume]
    E --> F[Chains Segments Can Move Past Each Other]
    F --> G[Flow Occurs, Low Viscosity, Rubbery or Molten State]
    
    C --> H{Temperature T greater than T g}
    H -->|Yes| E
    H -->|No| B
    
    I[Free Volume Fraction f] --> J[f = f sub g + alpha sub f times T minus T sub g]
    J --> K[Critical f at T g: 0.025 typically]
    J --> L[WLF Equation Valid in Range T g to T g plus 100 K]
```

## 4.5 Capillary Rheometer — Schematic of Measurement Device

The capillary rheometer is the most common experimental tool for measuring polymer melt viscosity at high shear rates. The block-level architecture below shows the functional flow of the device.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Polymer Melt Reservoir] --> B[Piston Applies Pressure P]
    B --> C[Capillary Die of Radius R and Length L]
    C --> D[Volumetric Flow Rate Q Measured]
    D --> E[Extrudate Collected]
    
    C --> F[Wall Shear Stress]
    F --> F1[tau wall = P times R divided by 2 times L]
    
    D --> G[Apparent Shear Rate]
    G --> G1[gamma dot app = 4Q divided by pi times R cubed]
    
    F1 --> H[Apply Rabinowitsch Correction]
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[True Shear Rate gamma dot true]
    
    F1 --> J[Viscosity]
    I --> J
    J --> J1[eta = tau wall divided by gamma dot true]
```

## 4.6 Processing Operations and Their Typical Shear Rate Ranges

This flow diagram maps common polymer processing operations to their characteristic shear rate and viscosity regimes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polymer Processing Operation] --> B[Shear Rate Range]
    B --> C[Viscosity Regime]
    
    A1[Compression Molding] --> B1[1 to 10 per second]
    B1 --> C1[High viscosity, near eta 0]
    
    A2[Blow Molding] --> B2[10 to 100 per second]
    B2 --> C2[Shear thinning region]
    
    A3[Extrusion] --> B3[100 to 1000 per second]
    B3 --> C3[Strong shear thinning]
    
    A4[Injection Molding] --> B4[1000 to 10000 per second]
    B4 --> C4[Approaching eta infinity]
    
    A5[Spinning and Coating] --> B5[10000 to 100000 per second]
    B5 --> C5[Very high shear, near second plateau]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Newtonian fluid. Give two examples.
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**
A Newtonian fluid is one in which the viscosity remains **constant** and **independent of the applied shear rate**, strictly obeying Newton's Law of Viscosity: $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$. The shear stress versus shear rate plot is a **straight line passing through the origin**, with the slope equal to the viscosity.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Water** ($\eta \approx 1 \text{ mPa·s at } 20°C$)
2. **Air** ($\eta \approx 0.018 \text{ mPa·s at } 20°C$)

*(Acceptable alternatives: glycerin, low-molecular-weight mineral oils, ethanol, mercury.)*

---

### Question 2: What is shear-thinning? Why do polymer melts exhibit this behavior?
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**
**Shear-thinning** (pseudoplasticity) is the phenomenon in which the **apparent viscosity of a fluid decreases with increasing shear rate**. In the Power Law model, this corresponds to a flow behavior index $n < 1$.

**Molecular Explanation:** At rest, polymer chains are in a coiled, randomly-oriented state, forming a network of **physical entanglements** that resist flow. As shear rate increases, these entanglements progressively **disentangle**, and the chains begin to **align along the flow direction**. This orientation reduces the resistance to flow, lowering the viscosity. The phenomenon is **time-independent** and **reversible** — when shearing stops, the chains re-entangle and viscosity recovers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1: Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids and the Power Law Model

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Classify fluids based on their rheological behavior with neat flow curves. Discuss the difference between time-independent and time-dependent non-Newtonian fluids with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer melt follows the Power Law with $K = 5000 \text{ Pa·s}^n$ and $n = 0.45$. Calculate: (i) the apparent viscosity at a shear rate of 1000 s⁻¹, (ii) the shear stress at this shear rate, and (iii) the apparent viscosity at 100 s⁻¹. Comment on the shear-thinning behavior. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Fluids (with flow curves):**

**1. Newtonian Fluids:** Viscosity is constant. The flow curve is a straight line through the origin: $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$. Examples: water, air, glycerol.

**2. Non-Newtonian Fluids:** Viscosity varies with shear rate. They are sub-divided into:

**A. Time-Independent (Steady Shear Behavior):**

- **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning):** Concave-down curve. Viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate. Most common in polymers. $n < 1$.
- **Dilatant (Shear-thickening):** Concave-up curve. Viscosity increases with shear rate. $n > 1$. Example: cornstarch + water.
- **Bingham Plastic:** Linear curve, but does not pass through the origin. Requires a yield stress $\tau_y$ before flow begins. Example: toothpaste.
- **Herschel-Bulkley:** Combines yield stress with shear-thinning or shear-thickening.

**B. Time-Dependent:**

- **Thixotropic:** Viscosity decreases over time at constant shear rate; recovers at rest. Example: paint.
- **Rheopectic:** Viscosity increases over time at constant shear rate. Example: gypsum paste.

**C. Viscoelastic:** Combines viscous flow with elastic recovery. Example: polymer melts, silly putty.

*(Examiner note: Award 1 mark for the Newtonian definition, 2 marks for the time-independent classification with examples, 2 marks for time-dependent, 1 mark for viscoelastic, 1 mark for the flow-curve sketch description.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $K = 5000 \text{ Pa·s}^n$, $n = 0.45$.

**(i) Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$:**

Apply the apparent viscosity formula:

$$\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 5000 \times (1000)^{0.45 - 1} = 5000 \times (1000)^{-0.55}$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate $(1000)^{-0.55}$:**

$$(1000)^{-0.55} = 10^{-3 \times 0.55} = 10^{-1.65} \approx 0.02239$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $K$:**

$$\eta_{app} = 5000 \times 0.02239 \approx 111.95 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**(ii) Shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$:**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n = 5000 \times (1000)^{0.45}$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate $(1000)^{0.45}$:**

$$(1000)^{0.45} = 10^{3 \times 0.45} = 10^{1.35} \approx 22.39$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $K$:**

$$\tau = 5000 \times 22.39 \approx 111,950 \text{ Pa} \approx 112 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100 \text{ s}^{-1}$:**

$$\eta_{app} = 5000 \times (100)^{0.45 - 1} = 5000 \times (100)^{-0.55}$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate $(100)^{-0.55}$:**

$$(100)^{-0.55} = 10^{-2 \times 0.55} = 10^{-1.1} \approx 0.0794$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $K$:**

$$\eta_{app} = 5000 \times 0.0794 \approx 397.0 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Comment on shear-thinning:**

The viscosity drops from **397 Pa·s at 100 s⁻¹** to **112 Pa·s at 1000 s⁻¹**, a reduction of approximately **72%** for a 10-fold increase in shear rate. This confirms the strong **pseudoplastic, shear-thinning behavior** typical of polymer melts. The $n$ value of 0.45 (well below 1) quantitatively validates this conclusion.

*(Examiner note: Award 2 marks each for parts (i), (ii), (iii), and 1 mark for the qualitative comment.)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2: WLF Equation and Temperature Dependence of Viscosity

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the WLF (Williams-Landel-Ferry) equation for the temperature dependence of polymer melt viscosity. State the universal constants and the temperature range of validity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polystyrene sample has $T_g = 100°C$. If the viscosity at $120°C$ is $5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa·s, estimate the viscosity at $140°C$ using the WLF equation. Comment on the significance of the result for injection molding. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of the WLF Equation:**

**Step 1 — Start with the Doolittle free-volume equation:**

$$\eta = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

where $f$ is the fractional free volume, $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants.

**Step 2 — Define free-volume temperature dependence:**

The free volume is assumed to increase linearly with temperature above $T_g$:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $f_g \approx 0.025$ is the free volume at $T_g$ and $\alpha_f$ is the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

**Step 3 — Take the ratio of viscosities at $T$ and $T_g$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = B\left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right) = B \cdot \frac{f_g - f}{f \cdot f_g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the free-volume expression:**

$$f_g - f = -\alpha_f(T - T_g)$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{B \cdot \alpha_f(T - T_g)}{f_g \cdot [f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)]}$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $\alpha_f$:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{B(T - T_g)}{f_g \cdot [f_g/\alpha_f + (T - T_g)]}$$

**Step 6 — Convert $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ and define universal constants:**

With $C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \cdot f_g} \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f} \approx 51.6$ K:

$$\boxed{\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta_T}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}}$$

**Universal Constants:** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K (valid for nearly all amorphous polymers).

**Range of Validity:** $T_g$ to $T_g + 100$ K. Above $T_g + 100$ K, the Arrhenius equation is more appropriate.

*(Examiner note: Award 2 marks for setting up the Doolittle equation, 2 marks for free-volume substitution, 1 mark for algebra, 1 mark for constants, 1 mark for range of validity.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $T_g = 100°C$, $\eta_{120°C} = 5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa·s. Find $\eta_{140°C}$.

**Step 1 — Apply WLF to find $\eta_{T_g}$:**

First, calculate at $T = 120°C$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta_{120}}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{17.44 \times (120 - 100)}{51.6 + (120 - 100)} = -\frac{17.44 \times 20}{51.6 + 20}$$

$$= -\frac{348.8}{71.6} = -4.8715$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\eta_{T_g}$:**

$$\frac{\eta_{120}}{\eta_{T_g}} = 10^{-4.8715} = 1.343 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\eta_{T_g} = \frac{\eta_{120}}{1.343 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{9}}{1.343 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.723 \times 10^{14} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply WLF at $T = 140°C$:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta_{140}}{\eta_{T_g}}\right) = -\frac{17.44 \times (140 - 100)}{51.6 + (140 - 100)} = -\frac{17.44 \times 40}{51.6 + 40}$$

$$= -\frac{697.6}{91.6} = -7.616$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $\eta_{140}$:**

$$\frac{\eta_{140}}{\eta_{T_g}} = 10^{-7.616} = 2.42 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\eta_{140} = \eta_{T_g} \times 2.42 \times 10^{-8} = 3.723 \times 10^{14} \times 2.42 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\eta_{140} \approx 9.01 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 5 — Verify by direct ratio (alternative, faster method):**

$$\frac{\eta_{140}}{\eta_{120}} = 10^{\log_{10}(\eta_{140}/\eta_{T_g}) - \log_{10}(\eta_{120}/\eta_{T_g})} = 10^{-7.616 - (-4.8715)} = 10^{-2.7445} \approx 1.803 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\eta_{140} = 5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.803 \times 10^{-3} = 9.01 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa·s} \checkmark$$

**Engineering Significance for Injection Molding:**

The viscosity drops from $5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa·s at 120°C to $\approx 9 \times 10^{6}$ Pa·s at 140°C, a **556-fold reduction** for a 20°C increase. This exponential sensitivity to temperature is why injection molders carefully control barrel and mold temperatures within $\pm 2°C$ to ensure consistent part quality, fill the cavity completely, and avoid defects like short shots, weld lines, or flash. A 20°C increase enables much lower injection pressure and faster cycle times.

*(Examiner note: Award 1 mark each for Steps 1-4, 1 mark for the verification, 2 marks for the engineering comment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert °C to K in the WLF equation, or mix up Pa·s with mPa·s. Always show units explicitly.
> 2. **Sign Errors in WLF:** The negative sign in the WLF equation is critical. Viscosity DECREASES as temperature INCREASES — never write a positive sign.
> 3. **Confusing Apparent vs. True Shear Rate:** Capillary rheometry problems require the Rabinowitsch correction. Skipping this costs 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the Power Law form:** Many students incorrectly write $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ instead of the correct $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ and $\eta_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.
> 5. **Misidentifying $n$:** When $n > 1$, it's dilatant; when $n < 1$, it's pseudoplastic. Do not confuse the two.
> 6. **WLF Range:** Do not apply WLF above $T_g + 100$ K. Use the Arrhenius equation instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Rheology** = Science of deformation and flow. **Viscous flow** = irreversible, time-dependent deformation dissipating energy as heat.
- **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$. Holds only for Newtonian fluids where $\eta$ is constant.
- **Newtonian fluids:** Water, air, glycerol, mineral oils. Constant viscosity.
- **Non-Newtonian fluids** are sub-divided into:
  - **Time-independent:** Pseudoplastic ($n<1$), Dilatant ($n>1$), Bingham ($\tau_y > 0$), Herschel-Bulkley.
  - **Time-dependent:** Thixotropic (viscosity drops with time), Rheopectic (viscosity rises with time).
  - **Viscoelastic:** Combines viscous flow with elastic recovery.
- **Power Law:** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$. **Apparent viscosity:** $\eta_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$.
- **Polymer melts are shear-thinning** because entanglements disentangle and chains align in the flow direction. Typical $n$ range: **0.2 to 0.6**.
- **Carreau Model:** Captures all three regions — $\eta_0$ plateau, shear-thinning transition, and $\eta_\infty$ plateau.
- **Arrhenius equation** $\eta = A \exp(E_\eta / RT)$ is used **above $T_g + 100$ K** (or above $T_m$).
- **WLF equation** is used **between $T_g$ and $T_g + 100$ K** with universal constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K.
- **Zero-shear viscosity vs. molecular weight:** $\eta_0 = K \bar{M}_w^{3.4}$ for $M > M_c$ (entangled regime); $\eta_0 \propto \bar{M}_w^{1.0}$ for $M < M_c$.
- **Mark-Houwink equation:** $[\eta] = K M^a$ relates intrinsic viscosity to molecular weight (for dilute solutions). $a = 0.5$ for theta solvents, $a = 0.8$ for good solvents.
- **Capillary Rheometry:** Wall shear stress $\tau_w = PR/(2L)$, apparent shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = 4Q/(\pi R^3)$, and **Rabinowitsch correction** $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_{app}(3n+1)/(4n)$.
- **Free volume theory:** Molecular basis for WLF. Free volume fraction $f = f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)$. Critical $f_g \approx 0.025$.
- **Engineering applications:** Viscosity controls pressure drop, throughput, energy consumption, and product quality in **extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, film blowing, fiber spinning, and coating**.
- **Key numerical sensitivity:** A 10% increase in $\bar{M}_w$ (above $M_c$) gives a **~35% increase** in viscosity. A 20°C increase near $T_g$ can give a **~500-fold drop** in viscosity.
- **Remember:** Most exam problems are 7+7 split. The first sub-part tests definitions/theory, the second tests numerical calculation. Practice both to score well.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apparent Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Apparent Viscosity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Apparent Viscosity ($\eta_a$)** is defined as the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to the corresponding apparent shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}_a$) at a particular point in a fluid undergoing non-Newtonian flow. $$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}_a}$$ Unlike the **Newtonian viscosity ($\mu$)**, which is a true material constant independent of shear rate, apparent viscosity is a **shear-dependent, calculated quantity**. It is "apparent" because it is derived from experimentally measured quantities (pressure drop, flow rate) under the assumption of Newtonian flow, even when the fluid is non-Newtonian. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The term "apparent" explicitly indicates that the viscosity value quoted is valid **only at the specific shear rate** at which it was measured. A single $\eta_a$ value cannot characterize the entire flow behavior of a polymer melt or solution. ## Conceptual Analogy — The Honey–Ketchup–Water Spectrum Imagine three fluids in a bottle: - **Water**: Spills easily no matter how slowly or quickly you pour it. (Newtonian — constant viscosity) - **Ketchup (in the bottle)**: Stuck at the bottom. Shake it hard and it suddenly flows like a liquid. (Pseudoplastic / Shear-thinning) - **Cornstarch slurry**: Easy to stir slowly, but jab it quickly and it feels almost solid. (Dilatant / Shear-thickening) The "viscosity" of ketchup is not one number — it **changes with how fast you push it**. Every time you measure it, you get a different value. That measured, shear-dependent value is its **apparent viscosity**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** When you read a polymer datasheet that says "MFI = 12 g/10 min" or "$\eta_a$ = 500 Pa·s at 100 s⁻¹", that single number is meaningless without the corresponding **shear rate** and **temperature** at which it was determined. Polymer rheology is always a **3-D problem**: Viscosity = f(Shear Rate, Temperature, Pressure). ## Common Notation Table | Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit | |---|---|---| | $\eta_a$ | Apparent viscosity | **Pa·s** | | $\tau$ | Shear stress | **Pa** | | $\dot{\gamma}_a$ | Apparent shear rate | **s⁻¹** | | $K$ | Consistency index | **Pa·sⁿ** | | $n$ | Power-law index (flow behavior index) | dimensionless | | $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Genesis of "Apparent" — Why Not Just "Viscosity"? In a **capillary rheometer** (the most common instrument for polymer melts), the experimenter measures: 1. The pressure drop $\Delta P$ across a capillary of length $L$ and radius $R$ → gives shear stress at the wall: $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ 2. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ → gives the **apparent** wall shear rate (assuming Newtonian parabolic velocity profile): $$\dot{\gamma}_{a,\,w} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$$ The **true** viscosity would require the true velocity profile, but for non-Newtonian fluids the parabolic assumption is wrong. Hence the ratio gives the **apparent** viscosity, not the true viscosity. > [!NOTE] > The correction between apparent and true wall shear rate is given by the **Rabinowitsch–Mooney equation** (see Section 3). ## 2.2 Classification of Fluid Flow Behavior The slope of the $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ curve on a log–log plot defines the fluid type: - **Newtonian**: $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$, slope = 1, $\eta_a = \mu$ = constant - **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)**: slope $< 1$, $\eta_a$ **decreases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ (most polymer melts, polymer solutions, blood) - **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)**: slope $> 1$, $\eta_a$ **increases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ (highly filled suspensions, cornstarch in water) - **Bingham Plastic**: behaves as a solid until yield stress $\tau_y$ is exceeded, then flows linearly - **Casson Fluid**: $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{K_C \dot{\gamma}}$ (e.g., chocolate, blood, tomato sauce) ## 2.3 The Ostwald–de Waele Power-Law Model This is the workhorse of polymer rheology. It assumes: $$\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^n$$ Therefore the apparent viscosity becomes: $$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K (\dot{\gamma})^{n-1}$$ - When $n = 1 \Rightarrow \eta_a = K$ (Newtonian) - When $n < 1 \Rightarrow \eta_a$ falls as $\dot{\gamma}$ rises (pseudoplastic) - When $n > 1 \Rightarrow \eta_a$ rises as $\dot{\gamma}$ rises (dilatant) ## 2.4 Effect of Temperature — Arrhenius Relationship Polymer melt viscosity is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature. The Arrhenius form is: $$\eta_a = A \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ Linear form for plotting: $$\ln(\eta_a) = \ln(A) + \frac{E}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$ Plotting $\ln(\eta_a)$ vs $1/T$ yields a straight line with slope $E/R$, from which the **activation energy of viscous flow $E$** is extracted. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $E$ (activation energy of viscous flow, in J/mol) with $E_a$ of chemical reaction kinetics. They are mathematically similar but physically different — viscous flow is a **thermally activated segmental jump process**, not a chemical bond breakage. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}_a}$ | Definition of apparent viscosity | **Pa·s** | | 2 | $\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^n$ | Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model | **Pa** | | 3 | $\eta_a = K (\dot{\gamma})^{n-1}$ | Apparent viscosity from power-law | **Pa·s** | | 4 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress from capillary rheometer | **Pa** | | 5 | $\dot{\gamma}_{a,w} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | Apparent wall shear rate (Newtonian assumption) | **s⁻¹** | | 6 | $\dot{\gamma}_{tw} = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\,\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$ | Rabinowitsch–Mooney correction | **s⁻¹** | | 7 | $\eta_a = A \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence | **Pa·s** | | 8 | $\ln(\eta_a) = \ln(A) + \dfrac{E}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius form | dimensionless | | 9 | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{K_C \dot{\gamma}}$ | Casson model (chocolate, blood) | **Pa$^{1/2}$** | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility Apparent viscosity is the central design parameter for: - **Injection molding**: Shear rates $10^2$ to $10^4$ s⁻¹ inside the cavity - **Extrusion**: Die swell prediction, pressure-drop estimation - **Blow molding & Film blowing**: Parison sag prediction - **3D printing (FDM)**: Filament extrusion consistency - **Coating & Painting**: Brush-ability vs. drip-ability trade-off # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Apparent Shear Rate in a Capillary Consider a polymer melt flowing through a cylindrical capillary of radius $R$ and length $L$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P$. **Step 1 — Force balance on a cylindrical element of fluid of radius $r$ and length $L$:** The pressure force pushing the cylinder forward = viscous drag force holding it back. $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\tau(r) \cdot 2\pi r L$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the shear-stress distribution across the radius:** $$\tau(r) = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$ The negative sign indicates that $\tau$ acts opposite to the flow direction.
