Taking magnitudes: $$\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{2L}\,r$$ **Step 3 — Identify the wall shear stress (at $r = R$):** $$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Newtonian definition to get the apparent shear rate:** If we (falsely) assume the fluid is Newtonian, then $\tau_w = \mu \dot{\gamma}_{aw}$, so: $$\dot{\gamma}_{aw} = \frac{\tau_w}{\mu} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$$ The last step comes from integrating the parabolic velocity profile $v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$ over the cross-section to obtain the volumetric flow rate $Q$. > [!NOTE] > The symbol $Q$ here is the **volumetric flow rate**, not heat. In KTU heat-transfer problems the same symbol means heat — keep contexts separate. **Step 5 — Form the apparent viscosity:** $$\eta_a \equiv \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_{aw}} = \frac{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}}{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q}$$ This is essentially the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** rearranged, and the resulting $\eta_a$ is the **apparent viscosity** at wall conditions. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Rabinowitsch–Mooney Correction The Newtonian assumption gave us $\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$. For a true power-law fluid, the velocity profile is **flatter** than parabolic, and the **true wall shear rate** is given by: $$\dot{\gamma}_{tw} = \frac{3n+1}{4n}\,\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$$ **Derivation (sketched rigorously):** **Step 1:** Start with the Rabinowitsch equation relating wall shear stress to wall shear rate for any fluid: $$-\frac{dv}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = \dot{\gamma}_{tw} = \frac{3}{4}\dot{\gamma}_{aw} + \frac{1}{4}\tau_w \frac{d\dot{\gamma}_{aw}}{d\tau_w}$$ **Step 2:** For a power-law fluid, $\tau_w = K(\dot{\gamma}_{aw})^n$, so: $$\frac{d\dot{\gamma}_{aw}}{d\tau_w} = \frac{1}{nK^{1/n}}\,\tau_w^{(1-n)/n} = \frac{\dot{\gamma}_{aw}}{n\,\tau_w}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the Rabinowitsch equation: $$\dot{\gamma}_{tw} = \frac{3}{4}\dot{\gamma}_{aw} + \frac{1}{4}\tau_w \cdot \frac{\dot{\gamma}_{aw}}{n\,\tau_w} = \frac{3}{4}\dot{\gamma}_{aw} + \frac{1}{4n}\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$$ **Step 4:** Factor out $\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$: $$\dot{\gamma}_{tw} = \dot{\gamma}_{aw}\!\left(\frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4n}\right) = \frac{3n+1}{4n}\,\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When $n = 1$ (Newtonian), the factor $\frac{3n+1}{4n} = 1$, so $\dot{\gamma}_{tw} = \dot{\gamma}_{aw}$ — as expected. When $n = 0.4$ (typical pseudoplastic polymer), the factor $= \frac{2.2}{1.6} = 1.375$, meaning the true shear rate is **37.5% higher** than the apparent one. **Always correct for this in lab calculations.** ## 3.3 Derivation of Arrhenius Form for $\eta_a(T)$ **Step 1:** Define a reference state $(T_0, \eta_{a0})$. Divide the Arrhenius form at $T$ by the form at $T_0$: $$\frac{\eta_a(T)}{\eta_a(T_0)} = \frac{A \exp(E/RT)}{A \exp(E/RT_0)} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $\eta_a(T)$: $$\eta_a(T) = \eta_a(T_0) \, \exp\!\left[\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$ **Step 3:** Linearize for graphical analysis: $$\ln(\eta_a) = \ln(\eta_{a0}) + \frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$ Plot $\ln(\eta_a)$ on the $y$-axis and $1/T$ on the $x$-axis → straight line, slope $= E/R$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Values | Polymer | $n$ (typical) | $K$ (Pa·sⁿ) | Behaviour | |---|---|---|---| | LDPE melt (190 °C) | 0.30 – 0.45 | $\sim 10^4$ | Highly shear-thinning | | HDPE melt | 0.50 – 0.60 | $\sim 5\times 10^3$ | Moderately shear-thinning | | PS melt (200 °C) | 0.25 – 0.35 | $\sim 10^4$ | Strongly shear-thinning | | PP melt | 0.35 – 0.45 | $\sim 7\times 10^3$ | Shear-thinning | | Glycerol (room T) | 1.00 | $\sim 0.9$ | **Newtonian** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification of Fluid Flow Behaviour (Log–Log Conceptual Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD StartA([Fluid subjected to shear]) --> Q1{Is viscosity
constant?} Q1 -- Yes --> NewT[Newtonian Fluid
tau equals mu times gamma dot
n equals 1] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does viscosity
decrease with
shear rate?} Q2 -- Yes --> Pseu[Pseudoplastic
Shear Thinning
n less than 1
Most polymer melts] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does viscosity
increase with
shear rate?} Q3 -- Yes --> Dil[Dilatant
Shear Thickening
n greater than 1
Concentrated suspensions] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is there a
yield stress tau y?} Q4 -- Yes --> Bing[Bingham Plastic
tau equals tau y plus mu p times gamma dot] Q4 -- No --> Cas[Non Newtonian
without yield stress
complex viscoelastic] NewT --> ApV1[Apparent Viscosity
equals true viscosity
eta a equals mu] Pseu --> ApV2[Apparent Viscosity
decreases with shear rate
eta a equals K times gamma dot to n minus 1] Dil --> ApV3[Apparent Viscosity
increases with shear rate] Bing --> ApV4[Apparent Viscosity
defined only above yield] Cas --> ApV5[Apparent Viscosity
measured at fixed gamma dot] ``` ## 4.2 Apparent Viscosity Measurement Workflow (Capillary Rheometer) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Melt extruded
through capillary] --> nodeB[Measure delta P
across length L] nodeB --> nodeC[Compute wall
shear stress tau w] nodeC[tau w equals delta P R over 2 L] --> nodeD[Measure volumetric
flow rate Q] nodeD --> nodeE[Compute apparent
wall shear rate gamma aw] nodeE[gamma aw equals 4 Q over pi R cubed] --> nodeF[Compute eta a
equals tau w over gamma aw] nodeF --> nodeG{Is fluid
power law?} nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Apply Rabinowitsch
correction factor 3n plus 1 over 4n] nodeH --> nodeI[True wall
shear rate gamma tw] nodeI --> nodeJ[Plot log eta a
versus log gamma tw] nodeG -- No --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK[Extract K and n
from slope and intercept] ``` ## 4.3 Temperature Effect on Apparent Viscosity — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeT1[Increase T] --> nodeT2[Easier segmental
motion of polymer chains] nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Lower resistance
to flow] nodeT3 --> nodeT4[eta a decreases
exponentially] nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Quantified by Arrhenius
eta a equals A exp E over RT] nodeT5 --> nodeT6[Plot ln eta a
versus 1 over T] nodeT6 --> nodeT7[Slope gives E over R
Intercept gives ln A] nodeT7 --> nodeT8[Engineers use E to
predict processing window] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define apparent viscosity. Why is it called "apparent" and not "true" viscosity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Apparent viscosity is defined as the ratio of shear stress to the apparent shear rate at a particular point of a non-Newtonian fluid: $$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}_a}$$ It is termed "apparent" because the shear rate is calculated assuming Newtonian flow (e.g., parabolic velocity profile in a capillary), whereas the fluid is actually non-Newtonian. Therefore, $\eta_a$ is **not a true material property** but a calculated quantity that depends on the prevailing shear rate, temperature, and measurement geometry. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **A polymer melt exhibits power-law index $n = 0.4$ at 200 °C. State whether it is shear-thinning or shear-thickening. Justify.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Since $n = 0.4 < 1$, the polymer melt is **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**. **Justification:** For a power-law fluid, $\eta_a = K(\dot{\gamma})^{n-1}$. When $n < 1$, the exponent $(n-1)$ is negative, so $\eta_a$ decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases. This is the defining characteristic of pseudoplastic, shear-thinning behavior — typical of most polymer melts due to progressive chain disentanglement at higher shear rates. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` **(a)** With the aid of a labelled flow-curve diagram, classify the different types of non-Newtonian fluid behaviour. Give one engineering example of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A polymer melt is forced through a capillary rheometer of radius $R = 1.0$ mm and length $L = 50$ mm. At a particular flow rate, the pressure drop is $\Delta P = 5$ MPa. If the volumetric flow rate is $Q = 4.17 \times 10^{-9}$ m³/s, calculate: (i) The wall shear stress $\tau_w$ (ii) The apparent wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$ (iii) The apparent viscosity $\eta_a$ Assume the fluid behaviour is Newtonian for the calculation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Newtonian Fluid:** $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$, slope = 1 on log–log plot. Example: water, glycerol, low-MW mineral oil. **(1 mark)** **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning):** Viscosity decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$, slope $< 1$ on log–log plot. Example: polymer melts (LDPE, PS), polymer solutions, blood. **(1.5 marks)** **Dilatant (Shear-thickening):** Viscosity increases with $\dot{\gamma}$, slope $> 1$ on log–log plot. Example: highly filled suspensions, cornstarch–water slurry. **(1.5 marks)** **Bingham Plastic:** Behaves as rigid solid below yield stress $\tau_y$; flows linearly above. Equation: $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$. Example: toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise. **(1.5 marks)** **Viscoelastic / Non-Newtonian-without-yield:** Show partial elastic recovery (e.g., die swell, Weissenberg effect). Example: silicone putty, polymer solutions. **(1 mark)** **Diagram:** Log–log $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ plot showing all four curves diverging from a common origin, with the Newtonian line at 45°. **(0.5 mark)** --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $R = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 50 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $Q = 4.17 \times 10^{-9}$ m³/s. **(i) Wall shear stress (1 mark):** $$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{6})(1.0 \times 10^{-3})}{2(50 \times 10^{-3})}$$ $$\tau_w = \frac{5000}{0.1} = 5.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} = 50 \text{ kPa}$$ **(ii) Apparent wall shear rate (2 marks):** $$\dot{\gamma}_{aw} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \frac{4(4.17 \times 10^{-9})}{\pi (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$ $$\dot{\gamma}_{aw} = \frac{1.668 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.668 \times 10^{-8}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 5.31 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **(iii) Apparent viscosity (2 marks):** $$\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_{aw}} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{4}}{5.31} \approx 9.42 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Final answer (2 marks for full working + units):** $\tau_w = 50$ kPa, $\dot{\gamma}_{aw} \approx 5.31$ s⁻¹, $\eta_a \approx 9.42 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — How Marks are Lost:** > 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to convert mm → m before cubing $R^3$ costs **2 full marks** in part (ii). > 2. **Wrong formula factor:** Writing $\dot{\gamma} = 8Q/\pi R^3$ (slip-flow form) instead of $4Q/\pi R^3$ → 1 mark deducted. > 3. **No unit on final $\eta_a$:** Writing "9420" without "Pa·s" costs 0.5 mark. > 4. **Assuming Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop** instead of using $\tau_w = \Delta P R/2L$ directly → loses 1 mark in part (i). --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` **(a)** Discuss the effect of temperature on the apparent viscosity of polymer melts. Derive the Arrhenius-type relationship and explain its significance in polymer processing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A polymer melt has apparent viscosity $500$ Pa·s at 200 °C. The activation energy for viscous flow is $E = 40$ kJ/mol. Using the Arrhenius equation, calculate the apparent viscosity at: (i) 220 °C (ii) 180 °C Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Take a reference temperature of $T_0 = 200$ °C = 473 K. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Statement (1 mark):** Polymer melt viscosity decreases sharply with temperature due to enhanced segmental mobility of polymer chains. **Phenomenological origin (1 mark):** As $T$ rises, the free volume in the melt increases, reducing the resistance to chain slippage past one another. Above $T_g + 100$ °C, this follows Arrhenius behaviour. **Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from the Arrhenius-type postulate: $$\eta_a(T) = A \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ At a reference temperature $T_0$: $$\eta_a(T_0) = A \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{RT_0}\right)$$ Dividing: $$\frac{\eta_a(T)}{\eta_a(T_0)} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$ $$\eta_a(T) = \eta_a(T_0)\, \exp\!\left[\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$ **Significance in processing (2 marks):** Determines the **processing window** for extrusion, injection molding, blow molding. A 10 °C change can alter viscosity by 30–50%, so precise temperature control is critical. Higher $E$ (e.g., rigid-chain polymers like PS) are more temperature-sensitive than low-$E$ polymers (e.g., flexible-chain PE). --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $\eta_a(T_0) = 500$ Pa·s, $T_0 = 473$ K, $E = 40000$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **General formula (1 mark):** $$\eta_a(T) = \eta_a(T_0)\, \exp\!\left[\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$ **(i) At T = 220 °C = 493 K (3 marks):** $$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{493} - \frac{1}{473} = 0.002028 - 0.002114 = -8.61 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = \frac{40000}{8.314} \times (-8.61 \times 10^{-5}) = 4811.6 \times (-8.61 \times 10^{-5}) = -0.4143$$ $$\eta_a(493\text{ K}) = 500 \times \exp(-0.4143) = 500 \times 0.6607 \approx 330.4 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **(ii) At T = 180 °C = 453 K (3 marks):** $$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{453} - \frac{1}{473} = 0.002208 - 0.002114 = 9.36 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{E}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 4811.6 \times 9.36 \times 10^{-5} = 0.4503$$ $$\eta_a(453\text{ K}) = 500 \times \exp(0.4503) = 500 \times 1.5688 \approx 784.4 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Result summary:** $\eta_a(220°C) \approx 330$ Pa·s (decreased by ~34%), $\eta_a(180°C) \approx 784$ Pa·s (increased by ~57%). This shows the exponential sensitivity of polymer melt viscosity to temperature. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — How Marks are Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** when plugging into Arrhenius → entire solution wrong, loses 4–5 marks. > 2. **Sign error on the exponent**: confusing $(1/T - 1/T_0)$ with $(1/T_0 - 1/T)$. At $T > T_0$, the difference is **negative** → viscosity must **decrease**, not increase. > 3. **Forgetting to use $\eta_a(T_0)$ as the base** and computing $A$ from scratch → wastes 2 marks. > 4. **Not writing units** (Pa·s) on the final answer → -0.5 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Apparent Viscosity** - ☐ **Definition:** $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}_a$ — always shear-rate-dependent for non-Newtonian fluids. - ☐ **Newtonian** is a special case where $\eta_a = \mu$ (true, constant viscosity). - ☐ **Power-law model:** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ → $\eta_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Check $n$: $< 1$ pseudoplastic, $= 1$ Newtonian, $> 1$ dilatant. - ☐ **Wall shear stress in capillary:** $\tau_w = \Delta P R / (2L)$. - ☐ **Apparent wall shear rate (Newtonian assumption):** $\dot{\gamma}_{aw} = 4Q/(\pi R^3)$. - ☐ **True shear rate** (Rabinowitsch–Mooney): $\dot{\gamma}_{tw} = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\dot{\gamma}_{aw}$ — always apply this for non-Newtonian data reduction. - ☐ **Arrhenius temperature dependence:** $\eta_a = A\exp(E/RT)$. Always use **absolute temperature (K)**. Plot $\ln \eta_a$ vs $1/T$ → straight line, slope = $E/R$. - ☐ **Engineering examples of shear-thinning:** LDPE, PS, PP melts — chain disentanglement mechanism. - ☐ **Yield-stress fluids** (Bingham, Casson) need a minimum $\tau_y$ before flow begins. - ☐ **Unit discipline:** Pa·s for viscosity, s⁻¹ for shear rate, K for temperature, Pa for stress. - ☐ **Remember:** $\eta_a$ is **not a material constant**; it is a **measurement context** parameter.

topics.title: Rubber Like Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rubber Like Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rubber-like elasticity** (also termed **elastomeric elasticity** or **entropy elasticity**) is the unique ability of a lightly cross-linked polymeric network to undergo **large, reversible, nearly isochoric (constant-volume) deformations** under the action of a relatively small applied stress, with the elastic recovery being governed primarily by **changes in conformational entropy** of the polymer chains rather than by changes in internal energy (enthalpy).

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, materials that exhibit this behaviour are called **elastomers**. They are characterized by:
- A **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** well below the use temperature (so the material is in the rubbery plateau region).
- **Low cross-link density** ($\nu \sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ mol/cm³) so that chain segments retain mobility.
- **High extensibility** — elongation at break often exceeds **100 %** and may reach **1000 %** for natural rubber.
- A **low elastic modulus** ($E \sim 1$ to $10$ MPa) compared to metals ($E \sim 100$ GPa) and even to glassy plastics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In metals, elasticity arises from small reversible atomic bond distortions (enthalpic). In elastomers, the dominant restoring force is **entropic in origin** — the chains resist being straightened because there are far fewer microstates available to a stretched chain than to a coiled one.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **tangled bundle of thin steel wool** packed loosely into a small pouch. When you pull the pouch open, the wires slide over each other and the bundle extends several times its original length. The extension is not because the wires themselves are stretching (like a metal spring), but because the **tangled coils are being pulled apart into a more ordered configuration**. As soon as you release the pull, thermal motion (Brownian motion) of the chains pushes them back to their statistically preferred, highly coiled state.

This is the essence of rubber elasticity:
- **The polymer chain is a writhing, coiling random walker** that constantly samples a huge number of conformations.
- **A relaxed chain is coiled** because the random coil has an astronomically larger number of available conformations than a stretched one.
- **Stretching the network straightens the chains**, drastically reducing the number of accessible conformations, and the **entropy drop** manifests as a **macroscopic retractive force**.

> [!TIP]
> A useful mental check: a piece of natural rubber warms up when suddenly stretched (because the tension has an entropic component) — this is the **Gough-Joule effect**, and is a fingerprint of entropy-elasticity.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | $8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| $N_A$ | Avogadro's number | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$ |
| $\rho$ | Density of elastomer | $\sim 900$ to $1100$ kg/m³ |
| $T_g$ | Glass transition temperature | Below ambient (e.g., $-73$ °C for NR) |
| $E$ | Young's modulus (rubber) | $\sim 1$ to $10$ MPa |
| $G$ | Shear modulus (rubber) | $\sim 0.3$ to $3$ MPa |
| $\lambda_{max}$ | Maximum extension ratio | $5$ to $10$ |
| $\nu$ | Crosslink density (active chains per unit volume) | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ mol/cm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **nearly incompressible** nature of rubber (Poisson's ratio $\nu_{p} \approx 0.499$) means that the bulk modulus $K$ is $\sim 10^{3}$ times larger than the shear modulus $G$. This fact is exploited in finite-element analysis of elastomers and is the reason we use the three-term invariant $I_1$, $I_2$ in hyperelastic constitutive models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Pre-Requisites: Behaviour of a Single Chain

A flexible polymer chain in a $\theta$-solvent or in the bulk amorphous state behaves as a **random coil**. The mean-square end-to-end distance for a chain of $N$ freely-jointed links of length $l$ is:

$$\langle r^{2} \rangle = N l^{2}$$

For a real chain with fixed bond angle and hindered rotation, this becomes:

$$\langle r^{2} \rangle = C_{\infty} \, n \, l_{0}^{2}$$

where $n$ is the number of backbone bonds, $l_{0}$ is the bond length, and $C_{\infty}$ is the **characteristic ratio** (typically $5$ to $10$ for vinyl polymers).

### 2.2 The Statistical (Gaussian) Foundation

The probability density for the end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$ of a Gaussian chain is:

$$P(\vec{r}) = \left(\frac{3}{2 \pi N l^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}\right)$$

The number of conformations corresponding to an end-to-end distance $r$ is:

$$\Omega(r) = \text{const} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}\right)$$

By Boltzmann's relation, the **configurational entropy** of a single chain is:

$$S = k_{B} \ln \Omega(r) = S_{0} - \frac{3 k_{B} r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}$$

where $S_{0} = k_{B}\ln(\text{const})$ is a reference entropy for the un-stretched chain.

### 2.3 From a Single Chain to the Network

In a cross-linked network, the chain ends are pinned at crosslinks. Affine network theory (assuming crosslink displacements follow macroscopic strain) gives the elastic free energy density as:

$$\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right)$$

where $\nu$ is the number density of **elastically active network chains** per unit volume and $\lambda_i$ are the principal extension ratios.

For **incompressible** elastomers, the constraint is $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$. The free-energy expression above is the **Gaussian (Neo-Hookean) elastic strain energy** function.

### 2.4 The Thermodynamic Equation of State (Canonical Derivation)

The tension $f$ in a stretched rubber strip held at length $L$ and absolute temperature $T$ is obtained from the **Helmholtz free energy** $A$:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Substituting the entropic expression, with the additional recognition that there is a small **internal-energy contribution** $f_e$:

$$f = f_{e} + f_{s} = f_{e} - T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

The **thermoelastic inversion phenomenon** allows $f_e$ and $f_s$ to be measured experimentally.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **exam-ready equations** for Rubber-Like Elasticity. Use these as the canonical reference for solving numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean-square end-to-end distance | $\langle r^{2} \rangle = N l^{2}$ | Gaussian random coil, freely jointed |
| 2 | Configurational entropy of one chain | $S(r) = S_{0} - \dfrac{3 k_{B} r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}$ | J/K per chain |
| 3 | Elastic free energy density (Gaussian) | $\Delta A = \dfrac{1}{2} \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right)$ | J/m³, $\nu$ in m$^{-3}$ |
| 4 | Neo-Hookean shear modulus | $G = \nu k_{B} T$ | Pa |
| 5 | Young's modulus (incompressible) | $E = 3 G = 3 \nu k_{B} T$ | Pa, uses $\nu_{p} = 0.5$ |
| 6 | Nominal (engineering) stress, uniaxial | $\sigma = \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)$ | Pa |
| 7 | True (Cauchy) stress, uniaxial | $\sigma_{T} = \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1}\right)$ | Pa |
| 8 | Entropic tension component | $f_{s} = T \left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$ | N |
| 9 | Energy component of tension | $f_{e} = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$ | N |
| 10 | Thermo-elastic inversion identity | $\left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{p,L} = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,p}$ | Maxwell relation |
| 11 | Volume fraction of active chains | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho \, N_{c}}{M_{c}}$ | chains/m³ |
| 12 | Constraint of incompressibility | $\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2} \lambda_{3} = 1$ | Valid for $K \gg G$ |
| 13 | Number of network chains per unit volume | $\nu = \dfrac{N_{A} \rho}{2 \, \bar{M}_{c}}$ | where $\bar{M}_{c}$ is the number-average chain mass between crosslinks |
| 14 | Flory-Huggins interaction parameter (related) | $\chi$ | Dimensionless, controls swelling |

> [!WARNING]
> The symbols $\nu$ (crosslink density) and $\nu_p$ (Poisson's ratio) are **distinct**. Do not confuse them. In a typical rubber, $\nu_p \approx 0.499$ and $\nu \approx 10^{26}$ chains/m³.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rubber-like elasticity is not a laboratory curiosity — it underpins:
- **Automotive tyres** — the cornering force on a pneumatic tyre depends on the shear modulus $G = \nu k_B T$ and is **directly proportional to absolute temperature**, which is why tyre-pressure specifications (gauge pressure) decrease by $\sim 1$ psi per 10 °F temperature rise.
- **Vibration isolators and engine mounts** — designed using the small-strain regime $G \approx E/3$.
- **Biomedical elastomers** — silicone rubber, polyurethane and nitrile rubber for artificial heart valves, catheters, and gaskets — all use the **nearly isochoric** property.
- **Damping and energy-absorption** — elastomers can store $\sim 90$ % of mechanical energy reversibly, leading to their use in seismic bearings and energy-harvesting devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Configurational Entropy of a Single Gaussian Chain

We wish to derive the entropy $S$ of a single freely-jointed chain with one end fixed at the origin and the other at $\vec{r}$, starting from the random-walk distribution.

**Step 1 — Express the probability density.**

For a 3-D random walk of $N$ links of length $l$, the probability of the chain end being within a small volume element $dV$ at position $\vec{r}$ is:

$$P(\vec{r})\, dV = \left(\frac{3}{2 \pi N l^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}\right) dV$$

**Step 2 — Define the number of microstates.**

The number of conformations $\Omega(\vec{r})$ that yield the end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$ is proportional to $P(\vec{r})$:

$$\Omega(\vec{r}) = \Omega_{0} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Boltzmann entropy relation.**

The entropy of the chain is the logarithm of the number of accessible microstates:

$$S(\vec{r}) = k_{B} \ln \Omega(\vec{r}) = k_{B} \ln \Omega_{0} - \frac{3 k_{B} r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Define a reference entropy and simplify.**

Set $S_0 = k_B \ln \Omega_0$. Then:

$$S(r) = S_{0} - \frac{3 k_{B}}{2 N l^{2}}\, r^{2}$$

This is the **canonical entropy–distance relation** for a Gaussian chain. Note that:
- $S$ depends on $r^2$ — longer extensions are exponentially less probable.
- The proportionality constant $\dfrac{3 k_B}{2 N l^2}$ will become the **spring constant** of the chain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Entropy Elastic Force on a Single Chain

We now compute the tensile force required to hold one end of a Gaussian chain at a fixed distance $r$ from the other (i.e., the chain behaves as a single-molecule spring).

**Step 1 — Express the Helmholtz free energy of the chain (assuming $U$ is independent of $r$).**

For a chain with no enthalpic elasticity (purely entropic), $U(r) = U_0$ is a constant. Therefore:

$$A(r) = U_{0} - T S(r) = U_{0} - T S_{0} + \frac{3 k_{B} T}{2 N l^{2}}\, r^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Take the derivative with respect to $r$ to obtain the force.**

The tensile force $f$ required to keep the chain stretched is:

$$f = \frac{\partial A}{\partial r} = \frac{3 k_{B} T}{N l^{2}}\, r$$

**Step 3 — Identify the chain spring constant.**

Defining the **single-chain spring constant**:

$$k_{\text{chain}} = \frac{3 k_{B} T}{N l^{2}}$$

we obtain the **entropic spring equation**:

$$f = k_{\text{chain}}\, r = \frac{3 k_{B} T}{N l^{2}}\, r$$

> [!NOTE]
> The chain stiffness **increases with temperature** (opposite to an enthalpic spring). This is a hallmark of entropy elasticity: heating a rubber band makes it *stiffer*, not softer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Stress–Strain Relation for an Affine Network (Uniaxial Extension)

We start from the elastic free energy density of a Gaussian network:

$$\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the constraint of incompressibility.**

For an incompressible elastomer, $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$. For a **uniaxial extension** along axis 1 with extension ratio $\lambda$, we have:

$$\lambda_{1} = \lambda, \quad \lambda_{2} = \lambda_{3} = \lambda^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the free energy.**

$$\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda^{2} + 2 \lambda^{-1} - 3\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the engineering (nominal) stress.**

The nominal stress $f^{*}$ (force per original cross-sectional area) is obtained from the partial derivative of the free energy per unit volume with respect to $\lambda$:

$$f^{*} = \frac{\partial \Delta A}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_{B} T \left(2 \lambda - 2 \lambda^{-2}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\, f^{*} = \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right) \,}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to true (Cauchy) stress.**

The true stress $\sigma_T$ is the nominal stress multiplied by $\lambda$:

$$\sigma_{T} = \lambda f^{*} = \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the small-strain limit.**

For small $\lambda - 1 = \varepsilon \ll 1$, expand using $\lambda \approx 1 + \varepsilon$ and $\lambda^{-2} \approx 1 - 2\varepsilon$:

$$f^{*} \approx \nu k_{B} T \left(1 + \varepsilon - 1 + 2 \varepsilon\right) = 3 \nu k_{B} T \, \varepsilon$$

This recovers the **neo-Hookean shear modulus** $G = 3 \nu k_B T / 3 = \nu k_B T$ via $f^* = E \varepsilon$ and $E = 3G$ (incompressible case). ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation: The Thermo-Elastic (Force–Temperature) Equation

Consider a rubber strip of original length $L_0$ stretched to length $L$ at constant pressure $p$. The Helmholtz free energy is $A(T, L)$, and its total differential is:

$$dA = -S\, dT + f\, dL$$

**Step 1 — Define the entropic and energetic components of the force.**

The force $f$ is conventionally split as:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} - T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = f_{e} + f_{s}$$

where $f_e$ is the **energetic** component and $f_s$ the **entropic** component.

**Step 2 — Take the temperature derivative at fixed length.**

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = \frac{f_{s}}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to obtain the entropic force.**

$$\boxed{\, f_{s} = T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L} \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **direct, experimentally accessible relation**. By measuring the tension $f$ at constant $L$ as a function of $T$, one can compute $f_s$ and $f_e = f - f_s$ without any microscopic model. For natural rubber, $f_e / f \approx 0.10$ to $0.20$, confirming that rubber elasticity is **predominantly entropic**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A natural-rubber sample has a crosslink density $\nu = 1.20 \times 10^{26}$ chains/m³. Calculate:
1. The shear modulus $G$ at $T = 300$ K.
2. The engineering stress required to stretch it uniaxially to $\lambda = 4$.
3. The Young's modulus $E$ (assume incompressibility).
4. The single-chain end-to-end RMS distance for a chain of $N = 200$ links of length $l = 0.154$ nm (C–C bond).

**Solution:**

**(1) Shear modulus:**

$$G = \nu k_{B} T = (1.20 \times 10^{26}) \times (1.381 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300$$

$$G = 1.20 \times 1.381 \times 300 \times 10^{3} = 4.97 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.497 \text{ MPa}$$

**(2) Engineering stress at $\lambda = 4$:**

$$f^{*} = \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right) = (4.97 \times 10^{5}) \times \left(4 - 4^{-2}\right)$$

$$f^{*} = (4.97 \times 10^{5}) \times (4 - 0.0625) = (4.97 \times 10^{5}) \times 3.9375$$

$$f^{*} \approx 1.957 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 1.96 \text{ MPa}$$

**(3) Young's modulus:**

$$E = 3G = 3 \times 0.497 \text{ MPa} = 1.49 \text{ MPa}$$

**(4) RMS end-to-end distance:**

$$\sqrt{\langle r^{2} \rangle} = \sqrt{N}\, l = \sqrt{200} \times 0.154 \text{ nm} = 14.142 \times 0.154 \text{ nm} \approx 2.18 \text{ nm}$$

This is consistent with the typical size of a network strand in lightly cross-linked natural rubber. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: Origins of Rubber-Like Elasticity

The following block diagram maps the conceptual architecture of rubber-like elasticity — from microscopic chain conformations up to macroscopic mechanical response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MICROSCOPIC INPUT]:::input
    B[Polymer chain: freely jointed coil]:::process
    C[Gaussian statistics: P exp of minus 3r2 over 2Nl2]:::process
    D[Configurational entropy S of r]:::process
    E[NETWORK LEVEL]:::level
    F[Crosslinks pin chain ends at fixed points]:::process
    G[Affine deformation: crosslink moves with macroscopic strain]:::process
    H[Free energy density: half nu kB T times lambda sum minus 3]:::process
    I[MACROSCOPIC OUTPUT]:::output
    J[Engineering stress f star equals nu kB T times lambda minus lambda power minus 2]:::output
    K[Thermoelastic response: f increases linearly with T at constant L]:::output
    L[Shear modulus G equals nu kB T proportional to T]:::output
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    I --> K
    I --> L

    classDef input fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef process fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef level fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef output fill:#FAE8FF,stroke:#A21CAF,stroke-width:2px,color:#581C87
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Decomposition of the Total Elastic Force

The total retractive force $f$ in a stretched rubber sample can be decomposed into **energetic** and **entropic** components. The figure below shows the functional flow from thermodynamic potentials to the measurable force.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Helmholtz Free Energy A of T L]:::src
    M2[Internal Energy U of T L]:::src
    M3[Entropy S of T L]:::src
    M4[Partial derivative of A with respect to L at constant T]:::proc
    M5[Total force f]:::out
    M6[Energetic force fe = partial U over partial L at T]:::out
    M7[Entropic force fs = minus T times partial S over partial L at T]:::out
    M8[Maxwell identity: partial f over partial T at L equals fs over T]:::check
    M9[Experimental verification: Gough Joule effect]:::check

    M1 --> M4
    M2 --> M6
    M3 --> M7
    M4 --> M5
    M6 --> M5
    M7 --> M5
    M5 --> M8
    M5 --> M9

    classDef src fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef proc fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#A16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713F12
    classDef out fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#500724
    classDef check fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064E3B
```

### 4.3 Architecture of the Neo-Hookean Constitutive Model

The neo-Hookean model is the canonical constitutive model for moderate-strain rubber elasticity. The functional architecture is mapped below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Input: macroscopic deformation gradient F]:::in
    N2[Compute principal stretches lambda 1, lambda 2, lambda 3]:::proc
    N3[Apply incompressibility constraint: product of lambdas equals 1]:::proc
    N4[Compute elastic free energy density delta A]:::proc
    N5[Derive second Piola Kirchhoff stress tensor S]:::proc
    N6[Push forward to Cauchy stress sigma]:::out
    N7[Update shear modulus G equals nu kB T]:::in

    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> N6
    N7 --> N4

    classDef in fill:#CFFAFE,stroke:#0E7490,stroke-width:2px,color:#164E63
    classDef proc fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef out fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,stroke-width:2px,color:#3B0764
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exams, students are expected to draw **labelled free-body sketches** of stretched networks showing crosslinks and chain vectors, but a block-diagram (as above) is also acceptable for full marks when the question is on the **mathematical structure** of the theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Set]**
*Distinguish between **enthalpic (energy) elasticity** and **entropic elasticity**. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Enthalpic elasticity** originates from reversible changes in **internal energy** (bond stretching, bending) under deformation. The restoring force **decreases** with temperature. *Example:* Steel spring, glassy polymer below $T_g$.
- **Entropic elasticity** originates from the **decrease in conformational entropy** when a flexible polymer chain is forced from a coiled to a stretched state. The restoring force **increases** with temperature. *Example:* Vulcanized natural rubber, silicone elastomer.
- Key physical fingerprint: a rubber band **heats up on rapid stretching** (Gough–Joule effect) because work done on the system increases the population of fewer higher-energy chain states, which then equilibrate by releasing heat.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Set]**
*Write down the expression for the **configurational entropy of a single Gaussian chain** and explain why a rubber band becomes stiffer when heated.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The configurational entropy of a Gaussian chain with end-to-end distance $r$ is:

$$S(r) = S_{0} - \frac{3 k_{B}}{2 N l^{2}}\, r^{2}$$

**Reason for stiffening on heating:**

The entropic tensile force on a chain is $f = \left(\frac{3 k_B T}{N l^2}\right) r$, which is **directly proportional to absolute temperature** $T$. As $T$ rises, the random thermal agitation of chain segments becomes more vigorous, and the chain exerts a stronger retractive force to resist being held in a low-entropy stretched conformation. Hence the rubber feels **stiffer** at higher temperature — opposite to the behaviour of an ordinary metal spring.

---

### 5.2 PART B: Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

> **KTU ESE Format Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student is expected to attempt **ONE** of the two full-question choices (Choice A **or** Choice B).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **configurational entropy of a single Gaussian chain** of $N$ freely-jointed segments of length $l$, end-to-end distance $r$. Starting from this expression, obtain the **entropic spring force** in such a chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **cross-linked elastomeric network** with chain density $\nu$ (chains per unit volume) at temperature $T$, derive the **neo-Hookean stress–strain relation** for uniaxial extension. State clearly the assumption of **affine network deformation** and the **incompressibility constraint** you have used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. The probability density of a random-walk end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$ is:

$$P(\vec{r}) = \left(\frac{3}{2 \pi N l^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}\right)$$

2. Microstates $\Omega(\vec{r}) \propto P(\vec{r})$, so:

$$\Omega(\vec{r}) = \Omega_{0} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}\right)$$

3. Boltzmann entropy:

$$S(r) = k_{B} \ln \Omega = k_{B} \ln \Omega_{0} - \frac{3 k_{B} r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}} = S_{0} - \frac{3 k_{B} r^{2}}{2 N l^{2}}$$

4. Internal energy $U$ is taken as independent of $r$ for a flexible chain (purely entropic), so the Helmholtz free energy is:

$$A(r) = U_{0} - T S(r) = \text{const} + \frac{3 k_{B} T}{2 N l^{2}}\, r^{2}$$

5. The entropic spring force is:

$$f = \frac{\partial A}{\partial r} = \frac{3 k_{B} T}{N l^{2}}\, r$$

The **chain spring constant** is $k_{\text{chain}} = \dfrac{3 k_B T}{N l^2}$, confirming the **linear, temperature-dependent restoring force** characteristic of entropy springs.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

1. **Affine network assumption:** The macroscopic deformation affinely maps the positions of crosslinks; i.e., a vector $\vec{R}_0$ between two crosslinks in the undeformed state transforms to $\vec{R} = \vec{F} \vec{R}_0$ in the deformed state.

2. **Incompressibility constraint:** $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$, so the volume is preserved under deformation.

3. The elastic free energy density of a Gaussian network is:

$$\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right)$$

4. For **uniaxial extension** along axis 1, set $\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$:

$$\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda^{2} + 2 \lambda^{-1} - 3\right)$$

5. The nominal (engineering) stress is:

$$f^{*} = \frac{\partial \Delta A}{\partial \lambda} = \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)$$

6. The true (Cauchy) stress is:

$$\sigma_{T} = \lambda f^{*} = \nu k_{B} T \left(\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1}\right)$$

7. **Limiting cases:**
   - At small strain: $f^* \approx 3 \nu k_B T \cdot \varepsilon$ giving $E = 3 \nu k_B T$ and $G = \nu k_B T$.
   - At large $\lambda$: $f^* \approx \nu k_B T \lambda$ — the upturn is the classic stiffening of rubber at high extensions (though the Gaussian model under-predicts this and is replaced by the Mooney–Rivlin or Ogden models for large strains).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]*

**(a)** Explain the **thermodynamic equation of state** for an elastomer. Define the **entropic** and **energetic** components of the retractive force and derive the **thermo-elastic inversion** identity that allows them to be measured experimentally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural-rubber test piece has a crosslink density $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{26}$ chains/m³. At $T = 298$ K, calculate:
  (i) The **shear modulus** $G$.
  (ii) The **Young's modulus** $E$ (incompressibility assumed).
  (iii) The **nominal tensile stress** required to stretch it to $\lambda = 3$.
  (iv) The **RMS end-to-end distance** of a network strand with $N = 250$ freely-jointed links of bond length $l = 0.154$ nm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. The thermodynamic state of a rubber strip under tension $f$, length $L$, temperature $T$, and pressure $p$ is described by the Helmholtz free energy $A(T, L)$ (at constant $p$).

2. The total retractive force is:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = \underbrace{\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T}}_{f_e} + \underbrace{\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T}}_{f_s \cdot (-1)} = f_e - T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T}$$

3. Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $L$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = \frac{f_{s}}{T}$$

4. The **thermo-elastic inversion identity**:

$$\boxed{\, f_{s} = T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L} \,}$$

5. **Experimental measurement:** Hold the rubber strip at fixed length, vary the temperature, and measure the tension $f(T)$. Plot $f$ vs $T$; the slope gives $f_s$ directly. Then $f_e = f - f_s$.

6. For natural rubber, $f_e / f \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$ at room temperature, demonstrating that rubber elasticity is **dominated by entropy** with only a small enthalpic correction arising from bond-angle distortion and van der Waals interactions.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Shear modulus:**

$$G = \nu k_{B} T = (1.5 \times 10^{26}) (1.381 \times 10^{-23}) (298)$$

$$G = 1.5 \times 1.381 \times 298 \times 10^{3} = 6.17 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.617 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Young's modulus:**

$$E = 3 G = 3 \times 0.617 \text{ MPa} = 1.85 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Nominal stress at $\lambda = 3$:**

$$f^{*} = G \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right) = (6.17 \times 10^{5}) \left(3 - 3^{-2}\right) = (6.17 \times 10^{5}) (3 - 0.1111)$$

$$f^{*} = (6.17 \times 10^{5}) \times 2.8889 = 1.782 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 1.78 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iv) RMS end-to-end distance:**

$$\sqrt{\langle r^{2} \rangle} = \sqrt{N}\, l = \sqrt{250} \times 0.154 \text{ nm} = 15.811 \times 0.154 \text{ nm} \approx 2.43 \text{ nm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units matter — half the marks vanish** if the student uses $\nu$ in mol/cm³ but does not convert to chains/m³ before multiplying by $k_B T$.
> 2. **Stress vs. strain ambiguity** — clearly state whether the answer is the **engineering (nominal)** or **true (Cauchy)** stress; both are valid but the question dictates which.
> 3. **Confusion between $\nu$ (chain density) and $\nu_p$ (Poisson's ratio)** — these are unrelated symbols in different chapters; rubric specifically deducts 1 mark for misuse.
> 4. **The factor of 3** between $E$ and $G$ comes from **incompressibility**; if the student forgets the incompressibility assumption, $E$ should not be $3G$.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section for last-mile revision the night before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Rubber-like elasticity** = large, reversible, nearly isochoric deformation governed by **entropy change** of flexible chains.
- ✅ A relaxed polymer chain is a **Gaussian random coil** with $\langle r^2 \rangle = N l^2$.
- ✅ **Configurational entropy** of a chain: $S(r) = S_0 - \dfrac{3 k_B r^2}{2 N l^2}$ — quadratic in $r$, hence chain acts as a Hookean spring.
- ✅ **Chain spring force** $f = \dfrac{3 k_B T}{N l^2} r$ — proportional to $T$ (entropy signature).
- ✅ **Network free energy density** (Gaussian, neo-Hookean): $\Delta A = \dfrac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left(\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3\right)$.
- ✅ **Incompressibility**: $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$; **affine network**: crosslinks follow macroscopic strain.
- ✅ **Shear modulus** $G = \nu k_B T$ — increases linearly with $T$.
- ✅ **Young's modulus** $E = 3G$ for incompressible rubber.
- ✅ **Uniaxial nominal stress** $f^* = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$; **true stress** $\sigma_T = G(\lambda^2 - \lambda^{-1})$.
- ✅ **Thermo-elastic identity** $f_s = T (\partial f / \partial T)_L$ — directly measurable.
- ✅ For natural rubber, $f_e / f \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$ → **80–90 % entropic**.
- ✅ **Gough–Joule effect**: rubber heats on rapid stretching, cools on release.
- ✅ **Crosslink density** $\nu = \dfrac{N_A \rho}{2 \bar{M}_c}$ — small for elastomers, large for thermosets.
- ✅ Neo-Hookean model valid for **$\lambda \le 1.5$ to $2$**; for larger strains use **Mooney–Rivlin** or **Ogden** models.
- ✅ Typical elastomer properties: $E \sim 1$–$10$ MPa, $\lambda_{max} \sim 5$–$10$, $T_g$ well below ambient.
- ✅ **Real-world examples**: pneumatic tyres, engine mounts, O-rings, biomedical silicone, seismic isolators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Strain Behaviour of Elastomers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress Strain Behaviour of Elastomers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elastomers** are a class of polymeric materials that exhibit **large elastic deformations** (strains typically in the range of **100 % to 1000 %**) under small applied stress, and recover their original shape almost completely upon removal of the load. This behaviour arises from the **entropy-driven elasticity** of a crosslinked polymer network rather than from bond-stretching.

The **stress–strain behaviour** of an elastomer refers to the relationship between the applied **engineering (or true) stress** $\sigma$ and the resulting **engineering (or true) strain** $\varepsilon$ (or stretch ratio $\lambda = 1 + \varepsilon$) when the elastomer is subjected to a uniaxial, biaxial or pure shear deformation mode. Because strains are very large, elastomers are described mathematically by **hyperelastic constitutive models** rather than the classical Hookean law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hyperelasticity**: A non-linear elastic constitutive behaviour for which the stress is derived from a **strain energy density function** $W$ that depends only on the instantaneous deformation, not on the deformation history.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Ball-Pit Rubber Sheet" Analogy

Imagine a giant rubber sheet pinned at the edges, with thousands of tiny balls (representing polymer chain segments) trapped inside it in constant, random **Brownian motion**.

* When you **pull the sheet** (apply stress), you do not stretch the covalent C–C bonds significantly — instead, you **straighten out the coiled chains** and **reduce the number of microstates** (configurations) the chains can adopt.
* The chains resist this loss of freedom, giving rise to a **retractive force**. This is essentially an **entropy-driven restoring force** — the chains "want" to go back to their disordered, high-entropy state.
* When you **release the stress**, the chains spontaneously coil back to the configuration of maximum entropy (maximum number of possible conformations) and the sheet returns to its original dimensions.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Take-away:** In metals, elasticity is **enthalpic** (bond stretching). In elastomers, elasticity is **entropic** (chain uncoiling). This is why the Young's modulus of rubber (~**1 MPa**) is ~1000× lower than that of steel (~**200 GPa**), even though both are "elastic".

---

## 1.3 Salient Features of the Elastomeric Stress–Strain Curve

| Feature | Typical Magnitude | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Elongation at break ($\varepsilon_b$) | **200 % – 1000 %** | Far higher than thermoplastics (~10–100 %) and metals (~5–30 %). |
| Initial Young's modulus ($E$) | **0.5 – 5 MPa** | Very low — gives the "soft" feel. |
| Tensile strength | **5 – 30 MPa** | Crosslinking raises strength. |
| Yield point | **Absent** | No permanent plastic deformation region. |
| Recovery on unloading | **> 90 %** | Energy dissipation is small but non-zero (hysteresis). |
| Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) | **≈ 0.49** | Nearly incompressible — volume change during deformation is negligible. |
| Effect of temperature on modulus | **Opposite to metals** | Modulus **increases** with $T$ for ideal rubber; for metals it decreases. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incompressibility ($\nu \approx 0.49$)** is the most distinctive mechanical fingerprint of elastomers. Under uniaxial tension, the material compensates for the axial elongation by a large lateral contraction, so that $\det(\mathbf{F}) = 1$ (where $\mathbf{F}$ is the deformation gradient tensor). This condition simplifies every hyperelastic constitutive equation.

---

## 1.4 Comparison with Other Material Classes

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Elastomers Behave Differently:**
> 1. **Cross-linked, amorphous network** — chains are tied at junction points but are otherwise free to rotate about $\sigma$ bonds.
> 2. **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$) is well below the service temperature** — the material is in a rubbery state, with segmental mobility.
> 3. **Long, flexible chains** — the contour length $L_c$ is much larger than the end-to-end distance $r$, giving a high flexibility ratio.

This means that under normal service conditions the elastomer sits in its **rubbery plateau**, well above $T_g$ but below the degradation temperature, where chain segments can rearrange quickly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of the Elastomeric Stress–Strain Curve

A typical uniaxial tensile curve of a lightly-crosslinked elastomer (e.g., natural rubber) consists of three distinct regions:

1. **Region I — Low-Strain (Hookean) Region** ($\varepsilon \approx 0$ – 10 %): A near-linear, very steep initial response. This region is governed by the **uncross-linked entanglement network** and **finite chain extensibility**.
2. **Region II — Strain-Induced Softening / Plateau** ($\varepsilon \approx 10$ – 200 %): The slope decreases dramatically, often called the **"strain-softening" or "Mullins effect"** region. The chains begin to align along the load direction.
3. **Region III — Strain-Hardening / Up-Turn Region** ($\varepsilon > 200$ %): The slope rises steeply again because the chains approach their **finite extensibility limit** ($r \to L_c$). Approaching this limit requires stretching the covalent bonds themselves, which is enthalpically very expensive.

> [!TIP]
> The **up-turn** in Region III is the primary safeguard against catastrophic failure in a tough elastomer. Without it, the material would simply fail at the end of Region II.

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Foundation — The Equation of State for Rubber Elasticity

The Helmholtz free energy per unit volume $A$ of an elastomer at temperature $T$ is split into an **energetic** and an **entropic** part:

$$A = U - TS$$

For an **ideal rubber** ($U$ is independent of deformation, i.e. $\left(\partial U / \partial L\right)_{T,V} = 0$), the tensile force $f$ is purely entropic:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = -T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

The more general **thermodynamic equation of state** for rubber is:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

The two contributions are commonly reported as the **energy contribution $f_e$** and the **entropy contribution $f_s$**:

$$f = f_e + f_s, \quad f_e = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}, \quad f_s = -T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For natural rubber at small strains, $f_s \approx 0.85 f$ and $f_e \approx 0.15 f$, confirming that elasticity is **predominantly entropic**. The small enthalpic contribution arises from the slight stiffening of bond angles and bond lengths under deformation.

---

## 2.3 The Affine Network Model — Gaussian Chain Statistics

In the **affine network model** (James & Guth, 1943), the following assumptions are made:

* Each **active chain** has $n$ freely-jointed Kuhn segments, each of length $l$.
* The **end-to-end vector** of a chain in the unstrained state follows a **Gaussian distribution**.
* The **microscopic deformation** of each chain equals the **macroscopic deformation gradient** $\mathbf{F}$ (the junctions move affinely with the bulk).
* The network contains $\nu$ active chains per unit volume.

For a chain in the unstressed state, the **mean-square end-to-end distance** is:

$$\langle r_0^2 \rangle = n l^2$$

After deformation with principal stretch ratios $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$, the new mean-square end-to-end distance becomes:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = n l^2 \left( \frac{\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2}{3} \right)$$

The **entropy change per chain** is:

$$\Delta S_{chain} = -\frac{1}{2} k_B n \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

Summing over all $\nu$ chains per unit volume and adding the elastic strain energy:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

This $W$ is the **Gaussian strain energy density function**.

---

## 2.4 Constitutive Stress–Strain Relations

For **uniaxial tension** ($\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$, by incompressibility), differentiation of $W$ with respect to $\lambda$ gives the **engineering stress**:

$$\sigma = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

This is the famous **neo-Hookean** stress–strain relation in uniaxial tension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The neo-Hookean equation is the **analogue of Hooke's law for large-strain rubber elasticity**. It is the simplest hyperelastic model that captures the physics of Gaussian network deformation.

The corresponding **true (Cauchy) stress** is:

$$\sigma_{true} = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda^2 - \lambda^{-1} \right)$$

For **pure shear** ($\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = 1$, $\lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1}$):

$$\sigma = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-1} \right)$$

For **equibiaxial tension** ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$, $\lambda_3 = \lambda^{-2}$):

$$\sigma = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-4} \right)$$

---

## 2.5 The Mooney–Rivlin Phenomenological Model

The neo-Hookean model cannot capture the **strain-softening** behaviour in Region II. The empirical **Mooney–Rivlin** model introduces an additional material constant $C_2$:

$$\sigma = 2 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) \left( C_1 + \frac{C_2}{\lambda} \right)$$

The corresponding **strain energy density** is:

$$W = C_1 \left( I_1 - 3 \right) + C_2 \left( I_2 - 3 \right)$$

where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the **first and second invariants** of the left Cauchy–Green deformation tensor $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^T$:

$$I_1 = \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2, \quad I_2 = \lambda_1^2 \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_2^2 \lambda_3^2 + \lambda_3^2 \lambda_1^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case:** Setting $C_2 = 0$ recovers the neo-Hookean model. The Mooney–Rivlin model is widely used for **carbon-black-filled rubbers** (tyres, engine mounts) up to stretches of $\lambda \approx 3$.

---

## 2.6 Finite Extensibility — The Langevin Chain Model

For very large stretches (Region III), the Gaussian assumption fails because the chain end-to-end distance $r$ cannot exceed the contour length $L_c = n l$. The correct chain statistics are described by the **inverse Langevin function** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}(x) = \coth(x) - 1/x$.

The single-chain free energy becomes:

$$A_{chain} = k_B T n \left[ \frac{r}{n l} \beta + \ln \frac{\beta}{\sinh \beta} \right]$$

where $\beta = \mathcal{L}^{-1}(r/nl)$ is obtained from $\mathcal{L}(\beta) = r/nl$.

The network-level stress is obtained by averaging over all chains, and for uniaxial tension it can be written as:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{3} \nu k_B T n^{1/2} \left[ \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left( \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{n}} \right) - \lambda^{-3/2} \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left( \frac{1}{\lambda^{1/2} \sqrt{n}} \right) \right]$$

> [!TIP]
> The $\sqrt{n}$ dependence explains why **highly-entangled or high-molecular-weight** rubbers reach the up-turn region only at very large stretches, while short chains show up-turn at small strains.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Symbols | Valid Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Engineering stress (uniaxial) | $\sigma = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$ | $\nu$ = chains/vol, $k_B$ = Boltzmann const, $T$ = absolute temp | $\lambda \lesssim 2$ (small/intermediate strain) |
| 2 | True (Cauchy) stress | $\sigma_{true} = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda^2 - \lambda^{-1} \right)$ | Same as above | Same |
| 3 | Gaussian strain energy density | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left( I_1 - 3 \right)$ | $I_1 = \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2$ | All strains (Gaussian limit) |
| 4 | Mooney–Rivlin stress | $\sigma = 2 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) \left( C_1 + C_2 / \lambda \right)$ | $C_1$, $C_2$ = empirical constants (Pa) | $\lambda \lesssim 3$ |
| 5 | Mooney–Rivlin energy | $W = C_1 \left( I_1 - 3 \right) + C_2 \left( I_2 - 3 \right)$ | $I_2$ = second invariant | Same |
| 6 | Initial Young's modulus | $E_0 = 3 \nu k_B T = 3 n_{chains} k_B T$ | $n_{chains}$ = no. of chains / unit volume | $\lambda \to 1$ |
| 7 | Shear modulus (small strain) | $G = \nu k_B T = n_{chains} k_B T$ | Same | Small strain |
| 8 | Incompressibility | $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ | — | All strains |
| 9 | Mean-square end-to-end distance | $\langle r_0^2 \rangle = n l^2$ | $n$ = segments/chain, $l$ = segment length | Unstrained Gaussian chain |
| 10 | Entropy per chain (affine) | $\Delta S = -\tfrac{1}{2} k_B \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$ | — | Affine, Gaussian |
| 11 | Crosslink density relation | $\nu = \rho N_A / (2 M_c)$ | $\rho$ = density, $M_c$ = avg. chain mol. wt. between crosslinks, $N_A$ = Avogadro no. | Ideal network |
| 12 | Equation of state | $f = \left( \partial U / \partial L \right)_{T,V} + T \left( \partial f / \partial T \right)_{L,V}$ | — | General |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Do NOT use $|\lambda|$ or $|x|$ notation inside a markdown table — this will break the rendering. Always use $\lambda$ alone (stretch ratios are positive by definition) or write `\vert \lambda \vert` if a modulus is genuinely needed.

---

## 2.8 Engineering Real-World Utility

1. **Tyre technology:** Tyre tread and sidewall compounds are designed using the Mooney–Rivlin model. The up-turn region (Region III) prevents the tyre from "bottoming out" at extreme cornering loads.
2. **Elastomeric bearings & dampers:** Base isolators in earthquake engineering use the hyperelastic stress–strain curve (often Ogden model) to absorb seismic energy.
3. **Soft robotics & biomedical devices:** Silicone elastomers (PDMS) used in prosthetics and microfluidic valves are characterized using neo-Hookean / Mooney–Rivlin parameters.
4. **Inflatable structures:** Air-supported roofs and inflatable boats require accurate prediction of biaxial stress–stretch response (equibiaxial stretch formula).
5. **3D-printed rubber-like materials:** Finite-element simulation of TPU and silicone prints relies on hyperelastic constitutive models.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Gaussian Chain Entropy

### Step 1: Probability distribution of end-to-end distance
For a freely-jointed chain of $n$ segments each of length $l$, the probability that the chain has an end-to-end vector $\mathbf{r}$ is given by the Gaussian distribution:

$$P(\mathbf{r}) = \left( \frac{3}{2 \pi n l^2} \right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{3 r^2}{2 n l^2} \right)$$

### Step 2: Boltzmann's relation for entropy
The configurational entropy is obtained from the Boltzmann equation:

$$S = k_B \ln \Omega \approx k_B \ln P(\mathbf{r}) + \text{constant}$$

Substituting the Gaussian probability and discarding the constant (since entropy differences are physically meaningful):

$$S(\mathbf{r}) = S_0 - \frac{3 k_B r^2}{2 n l^2}$$

where $S_0$ is the entropy of the chain in the unperturbed reference state.

### Step 3: Entropy change upon deformation
Let the chain's end-to-end vector change from $\mathbf{r}_0$ to $\mathbf{r}$. The change in entropy is:

$$\Delta S = S(\mathbf{r}) - S(\mathbf{r}_0) = -\frac{3 k_B}{2 n l^2} \left( r^2 - r_0^2 \right)$$

Using the **affine** assumption that $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{r}_0$, we obtain $r^2 = r_0^2 \left( \lambda_1^2 \cos^2\theta + \lambda_2^2 \sin^2\theta \right)$ in 2D, and after averaging over all chain orientations in 3D:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \frac{n l^2}{3} \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 \right)$$

### Step 4: Average entropy change per chain
Substituting into the entropy change:

$$\Delta S_{chain} = -\frac{1}{2} k_B \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

This is the **affine-network entropy change per chain**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strain Energy Density of the Affine Network

### Step 1: Elastic free energy of a single chain
Since $U$ is taken as independent of deformation for an ideal rubber, the Helmholtz free energy per chain is:

$$A_{chain} = U_0 - T \Delta S_{chain}$$

$$\Rightarrow A_{chain} = A_0 + \frac{1}{2} k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

### Step 2: Multiply by network chain density
If there are $\nu$ active chains per unit volume, the strain energy density $W$ (free energy per unit volume of the network) is:

$$W = \nu A_{chain} = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

Dropping the reference constant, we obtain the canonical **Gaussian strain energy density function**:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left( I_1 - 3 \right)$$

### Step 3: Identification with shear modulus
The small-strain shear modulus is identified as:

$$G = \nu k_B T$$

so that the compact form is:

$$W = \frac{G}{2} \left( I_1 - 3 \right)$$

### Step 4: Initial Young's modulus
For an isotropic incompressible material, $E_0 = 3G$:

$$E_0 = 3 \nu k_B T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Result:** $E_0$ is **directly proportional to absolute temperature** for an ideal rubber — this is the opposite of metals, where $E$ decreases with $T$. This temperature dependence is a direct fingerprint of entropic elasticity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uniaxial Neo-Hookean Stress

### Step 1: Impose uniaxial deformation
Let the specimen be stretched along the $x_1$ direction by stretch ratio $\lambda$, and let it contract freely in $x_2$ and $x_3$:

$$\lambda_1 = \lambda, \quad \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$$

(derived from the incompressibility condition $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ and symmetry $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3$).

### Step 2: Compute the strain invariant

$$I_1 = \lambda^2 + 2 \lambda^{-1}$$

### Step 3: Strain energy density

$$W = \frac{G}{2} \left( \lambda^2 + 2 \lambda^{-1} - 3 \right)$$

### Step 4: Engineering stress from the second Piola–Kirchhoff framework
The nominal (engineering) stress $P_{11}$ is the derivative of $W$ with respect to the stretch in the loading direction:

$$P_{11} = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \lambda} = G \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

This is the **neo-Hookean stress in uniaxial tension**.

### Step 5: Conversion to true (Cauchy) stress
The true stress relates to the engineering stress by $\sigma_{true} = \lambda \sigma$:

$$\sigma_{true} = G \left( \lambda^2 - \lambda^{-1} \right)$$

### Step 6: Verification of small-strain limit
Expanding $\lambda = 1 + \varepsilon$ for small $\varepsilon$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= G \left[ (1+\varepsilon) - (1+\varepsilon)^{-2} \right] \\
&= G \left[ 1 + \varepsilon - \left( 1 - 2\varepsilon + 3\varepsilon^2 - \cdots \right) \right] \\
&= G \left( 3\varepsilon + \mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^2) \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Since the small-strain Young's modulus of an incompressible material is $E_0 = 3G$, this correctly reduces to $\sigma = E_0 \varepsilon$ as $\varepsilon \to 0$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mooney–Rivlin Uniaxial Stress from Energy Function

### Step 1: Strain energy density function

$$W = C_1 \left( I_1 - 3 \right) + C_2 \left( I_2 - 3 \right)$$

### Step 2: Compute invariants for uniaxial stretch

$$I_1 = \lambda^2 + 2 \lambda^{-1}, \quad I_2 = 2\lambda + \lambda^{-2}$$

### Step 3: Take derivative w.r.t. $\lambda$

$$\frac{\partial I_1}{\partial \lambda} = 2\lambda - 2\lambda^{-2}, \quad \frac{\partial I_2}{\partial \lambda} = 2 - 2\lambda^{-3}$$

### Step 4: Engineering stress

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{\partial W}{\partial \lambda} \\
&= C_1 \left( 2\lambda - 2\lambda^{-2} \right) + C_2 \left( 2 - 2\lambda^{-3} \right) \\
&= 2 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) C_1 + 2 \left( 1 - \lambda^{-3} \right) C_2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Algebraic simplification
Factor out $\left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$. Note that:

$$1 - \lambda^{-3} = \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) \frac{1 + \lambda^{-1} + \lambda^{-2}}{3}$$

But the **commonly-used engineering form** retains the natural factorisation:

$$\sigma = 2 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) \left( C_1 + \frac{C_2}{\lambda} \right)$$

This is the canonical **Mooney–Rivlin uniaxial stress equation** used throughout rubber engineering.

### Step 6: Recovery of neo-Hookean limit
Setting $C_2 = 0$ gives $\sigma = 2 C_1 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$, with $2 C_1 = G = \nu k_B T$, recovering the affine network result. ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Relation Between Crosslink Density and Modulus

### Step 1: Affine network prediction

$$G = \nu k_B T$$

### Step 2: Express $\nu$ in terms of measurable quantities
For a network with $N$ chains per unit volume, mass density $\rho$, and average molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$:

$$\nu = \frac{\rho N_A}{2 M_c}$$

The factor of 2 in the denominator arises because each crosslink connects 4 chain ends in a tetrafunctional network, giving $2$ chain-ends per crosslink, hence $2$ crosslinks per chain.

### Step 3: Substitute back

$$G = \frac{\rho N_A k_B T}{2 M_c} = \frac{\rho R T}{2 M_c}$$

where $R = N_A k_B$ is the gas constant.

### Step 4: Initial Young's modulus

$$E_0 = 3 G = \frac{3 \rho R T}{2 M_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern:** For a natural rubber vulcanisate with $\rho = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$, $M_c = 5000 \text{ g/mol}$, the calculated modulus is:
> $$E_0 = \frac{3 \times 920 \times 8.314 \times 300}{2 \times 5.0} \approx 0.69 \text{ MPa}$$
> This matches the experimentally observed modulus of soft natural rubber.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Stress Contribution of Finite Chain Extensibility (Brief Sketch)

For a non-Gaussian chain, the free energy per chain is given by the **Langevin expression**:

$$A_{chain} = k_B T \left[ \frac{r}{l} \beta + \ln \frac{\beta}{\sinh \beta} \right]$$

where $\beta = \mathcal{L}^{-1}(r / L_c)$ and $L_c = n l$.

The tension in a single chain is:

$$f_{chain} = \frac{\partial A_{chain}}{\partial r} = \frac{k_B T}{l} \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left( \frac{r}{n l} \right)$$

For $r \ll n l$, expanding $\mathcal{L}^{-1}(x) \approx 3x + \tfrac{9}{5}x^3 + \tfrac{297}{175}x^5 + \cdots$ gives the Gaussian limit. As $r \to n l$, $\mathcal{L}^{-1}(x) \to \infty$, producing the **up-turn** in Region III of the stress–strain curve.

Network averaging then yields a stress that diverges as $\lambda \to \sqrt{n}$, the maximum possible stretch.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Affine Network | Phantom Network | Mooney–Rivlin (Phenomenological) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Crosslink fluctuation | **Suppressed** (junctions move with bulk) | **Allowed** (junctions fluctuate freely) | n/a — empirical |
| Modulus equation | $G = \nu k_B T$ | $G = (\nu - h) k_B T$, $h$ = cycle rank | $G = 2(C_1 + C_2)$ |
| Strain energy | $\tfrac{G}{2}(I_1 - 3)$ | Same form, different prefactor | $C_1(I_1 - 3) + C_2(I_2 - 3)$ |
| Captures up-turn? | No (Gaussian) | No (Gaussian) | No |
| Captures strain-softening? | No | No | **Yes** |
| KTU 2024 expected? | Yes (must-derive) | Yes (mention + modulus) | Yes (engineering formula) |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — The Elastomer Stress–Strain Block

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **flow of mechanical response** in an elastomer from external loading to macroscopic stress–strain output, and the mapping of each region to the underlying physics and constitutive model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load sigma] --> B[Network Junction Affine Motion]
    B --> C[Chain End to End Vector r]
    C --> D{Strain Magnitude?}
    D -- Small lambda less than 1.2 --> E[Region I Hookean Gaussian]
    D -- Medium lambda 1.2 to 3 --> F[Region II Strain Softening]
    D -- Large lambda greater than 3 --> G[Region III Finite Extensibility Up turn]
    E --> H[Neo Hookean Model sigma = G lambda minus lambda inv2]
    F --> I[Mooney Rivlin Model sigma = 2 lambda minus lambda inv2 times C1 plus C2 over lambda]
    G --> J[Langevin Non Gaussian Chain Model]
    H --> K[Output Stress Strain Curve]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Validation via Uniaxial Tensile Test]
```

---

## 4.2 Three-Region Stress–Strain Schematic

The following Mermaid chart depicts the **three characteristic regions** of an elastomeric stress–strain curve, the molecular mechanism dominating each region, and the corresponding constitutive model used to describe it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Region_I
        R1A[Region I: 0 to 10 percent strain] --> R1B[Slope = E0 = 3 nu kB T]
        R1B --> R1C[Affine Gaussian Network]
        R1C --> R1D[Neo Hookean]
    end
    subgraph Region_II
        R2A[Region II: 10 to 200 percent strain] --> R2B[Strain Softening Mullins Effect]
        R2B --> R2C[Chain Alignment and Entanglement Release]
        R2C --> R2D[Mooney Rivlin with C1 and C2]
    end
    subgraph Region_III
        R3A[Region III: above 200 percent strain] --> R3B[Strain Hardening Up turn]
        R3B --> R3C[Chain Approaches Contour Length Lc]
        R3C --> R3D[Langevin Inverse Function]
    end
    Region_I --> Region_II
    Region_II --> Region_III
    Region_III --> R4[Failure at lambda max]
```

---

## 4.3 Network Hierarchy — From Chain to Continuum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Kuhn Segment length l] --> M2[Single Chain n segments]
    M2 --> M3[Active Chain with two crosslink ends]
    M3 --> M4[Crosslink Junction]
    M4 --> M5[Network of nu chains per unit volume]
    M5 --> M6[Continuum Point with deformation gradient F]
    M6 --> M7[Macroscopic Stress sigma via W lambda]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each level of the hierarchy contributes one term to the final constitutive equation. The Kuhn segment length $l$ sets the statistical length scale; the single chain defines the Gaussian distribution; the active chain connects two junctions; the junction forms the network; the network gives the continuum a strain energy density; the strain energy density produces the macroscopic stress.

---

## 4.4 Deformation-Mode Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Loading Mode] --> P1{Type of Stretch?}
    P1 -- One direction --> Q1[Uniaxial: lambda1 = lambda, lambda2 = lambda3 = lambda inv 1/2]
    P1 -- Two directions --> Q2[Equibiaxial: lambda1 = lambda2 = lambda, lambda3 = lambda inv 2]
    P1 -- One fixed --> Q3[Pure Shear: lambda1 = lambda, lambda2 = 1, lambda3 = lambda inv 1]
    Q1 --> R1[sigma = G times lambda minus lambda inv 2]
    Q2 --> R2[sigma = G times lambda minus lambda inv 4]
    Q3 --> R3[sigma = G times lambda minus lambda inv 1]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 trick question alert:** "Equibiaxial" stretching of a sheet clamped at the edges gives $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-4})$, NOT the uniaxial formula. Be sure to identify the loading mode from the boundary conditions before applying any formula.
