# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. Differentiate between entropic and enthalpic elasticity. Why is the elasticity of natural rubber classified as entropic?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Enthalpic Elasticity (Metals, Ceramics) | Entropic Elasticity (Elastomers) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Origin | Stretching of interatomic bonds | Uncoiling of polymer chains |
| Energy term | Increase in internal energy $U$ | Decrease in configurational entropy $S$ |
| Force–temperature relation | Modulus **decreases** with $T$ | Modulus **increases** with $T$ |
| Strain magnitude | < 1 % | 100 – 1000 % |
| $T_g$ relative to $T_{use}$ | $T_g \gg T_{use}$ | $T_g \ll T_{use}$ |

Natural rubber is classified as entropically elastic because:

1. Its service temperature is well above $T_g$ (≈ – 70 °C), so chains undergo micro-Brownian motion.
2. Thermodynamic experiments show that the **entropy contribution** $f_s \approx 0.85 f$ to the tensile force, while the energy contribution $f_e \approx 0.15 f$.
3. The stress–temperature relation satisfies $\left( \partial f / \partial T \right)_{L,V} > 0$, the direct signature of entropy-driven elasticity.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. Define the Mooney–Rivlin equation for uniaxial tension of an elastomer. What is the physical significance of the constants $C_1$ and $C_2$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Mooney–Rivlin** engineering stress equation for uniaxial tension is:

$$\sigma = 2 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) \left( C_1 + \frac{C_2}{\lambda} \right)$$

where $\lambda$ is the stretch ratio and $C_1$, $C_2$ are temperature-dependent empirical constants with units of pressure (Pa).

**Physical significance:**

* $C_1$ is associated with the **network contribution** (crosslinked chains behaving Gaussian-like). It is directly related to the shear modulus at small strains: $G = 2(C_1 + C_2)$ and reduces to the neo-Hookean prediction as $G = 2C_1$ when $C_2 \to 0$.
* $C_2$ is associated with the **departure from neo-Hookean behaviour**, often attributed to **chain entanglement constraints** that become progressively released during deformation. It controls the magnitude of strain-softening (Region II of the stress–strain curve).

Setting $C_2 = 0$ recovers the **neo-Hookean** model. For filled rubbers such as carbon-black-loaded SBR, $C_2$ is large; for unfilled, lightly-crosslinked natural rubber it is smaller.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the strain energy density function for an affine Gaussian network of $\nu$ chains per unit volume. Show that the resulting uniaxial engineering stress is $\sigma = \nu k_B T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a natural rubber sample with mass density $\rho = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$, average chain molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c = 6000 \text{ g/mol}$, and temperature $T = 300 \text{ K}$, calculate:
   (i) the small-strain Young's modulus $E_0$,
   (ii) the engineering stress at $\lambda = 2$,
   (iii) the true stress at $\lambda = 2$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

### (a) Derivation of strain energy density [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Single-chain Gaussian entropy.** The configurational entropy of a single Gaussian chain with end-to-end distance $r$ is:

$$S_{chain}(r) = S_0 - \frac{3 k_B r^2}{2 n l^2}$$

where $n$ is the number of Kuhn segments, $l$ is the segment length, and $S_0$ is the unstressed reference entropy.

**Step 2: Apply affine deformation.** The chain end-to-end vector maps from $\mathbf{r}_0$ to $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{r}_0$. After averaging over chain orientations:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \frac{n l^2}{3} \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 \right)$$

**Step 3: Entropy change per chain.**

$$\Delta S_{chain} = -\frac{1}{2} k_B \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

**Step 4: Free energy per chain.** For an ideal rubber, $U$ is deformation-independent:

$$A_{chain} = A_0 + \frac{1}{2} k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

**Step 5: Strain energy density (network).**

$$W = \nu A_{chain} - \nu A_0 = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left( I_1 - 3 \right)$$

**Step 6: Uniaxial stress.** For uniaxial tension, $I_1 = \lambda^2 + 2 \lambda^{-1}$. Differentiating:

$$\sigma = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left( 2 \lambda - 2 \lambda^{-2} \right) = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

Hence the neo-Hookean uniaxial stress equation is derived.

---

### (b) Numerical calculations [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Shear modulus from crosslink density.** For an affine network:

$$G = \frac{\rho R T}{2 M_c} = \frac{920 \times 8.314 \times 300}{2 \times 6.0} \text{ Pa}$$

Computing:

$$G = \frac{920 \times 8.314 \times 300}{12.0} = \frac{2\,294\,664}{12.0} \approx 1.912 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.1912 \text{ MPa}$$

**(i) Initial Young's modulus:**

$$E_0 = 3 G = 3 \times 0.1912 \approx 0.574 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Engineering stress at $\lambda = 2$:**

$$\sigma = G (\lambda - \lambda^{-2}) = 0.1912 \times (2 - 2^{-2}) = 0.1912 \times (2 - 0.25) = 0.1912 \times 1.75 \approx 0.335 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) True stress at $\lambda = 2$:**

$$\sigma_{true} = G (\lambda^2 - \lambda^{-1}) = 0.1912 \times (4 - 0.5) = 0.1912 \times 3.5 \approx 0.669 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification:** $\sigma_{true} = \lambda \times \sigma = 2 \times 0.335 = 0.670 \text{ MPa}$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Starting from the general Mooney–Rivlin strain energy density $W = C_1(I_1 - 3) + C_2(I_2 - 3)$, derive the engineering stress for **equibiaxial extension** of an incompressible sheet. Express the final result in terms of $\lambda$ and the material constants $C_1, C_2$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 2-mm-thick, 50-mm × 50-mm square sheet of carbon-black-filled SBR is clamped on all four edges and inflated to a pressure of 50 kPa. The Mooney–Rivlin constants are $C_1 = 0.20$ MPa and $C_2 = 0.05$ MPa. Estimate:
   (i) the equibiaxial stretch ratio $\lambda$ at the centre of the sheet,
   (ii) the maximum in-plane principal stress at the centre.
   *(Hint: For a thin inflated membrane, the hoop stress $\sigma = pR / (2t)$, where $p$ = pressure, $R$ = radius of curvature, $t$ = sheet thickness; assume the inflated shape is a spherical cap with $R \approx L^2 / (8 \delta)$, where $\delta$ = centre deflection and $L = 50$ mm.)* **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

### (a) Equibiaxial derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Define deformation.** Equibiaxial extension of an incompressible sheet:

$$\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda, \quad \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-2}$$

(from $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$).

**Step 2: Compute invariants.**

$$I_1 = 2\lambda^2 + \lambda^{-4}$$

$$I_2 = \lambda^4 + 2\lambda^{-2}$$

**Step 3: Derivatives.**

$$\frac{\partial I_1}{\partial \lambda} = 4 \lambda - 4 \lambda^{-5}, \quad \frac{\partial I_2}{\partial \lambda} = 4 \lambda^3 - 4 \lambda^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Equibiaxial stress.** Differentiating $W$ w.r.t. $\lambda$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{\partial W}{\partial \lambda} = C_1 \left( 4 \lambda - 4 \lambda^{-5} \right) + C_2 \left( 4 \lambda^3 - 4 \lambda^{-3} \right) \\
&= 4 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-5} \right) C_1 + 4 \left( \lambda^3 - \lambda^{-3} \right) C_2
\end{aligned}$$

Factoring out $4(\lambda - \lambda^{-5})$:

$$\sigma = 4 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-5} \right) \left[ C_1 + C_2 \left( \lambda^2 + \lambda^{-2} + 1 \right) \right]$$

This is the canonical **equibiaxial Mooney–Rivlin** stress equation.

---

### (b) Numerical solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Geometry of inflated sheet.** Treat the inflated square sheet as a circular membrane of effective radius $a = L/2 = 25$ mm. Assume spherical-cap geometry.

For an inflated membrane at moderate stretch, the hoop stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{p R}{2 t}$$

with $R \approx a^2 / (2 \delta)$ (spherical cap approximation, where $\delta$ is the centre deflection).

The equibiaxial stretch at the centre is:

$$\lambda \approx 1 + \frac{2 \delta^2}{3 a^2} \quad \text{(small-deflection approximation)}$$

**Step 2: Iterate to find $\lambda$.** Initial guess: $\delta = 10$ mm.

$$\lambda \approx 1 + \frac{2 \times 100}{3 \times 625} \approx 1 + 0.107 \approx 1.107$$

Radius of curvature: $R = a^2 / (2 \delta) = 625 / 20 = 31.25$ mm.

Hoop stress from membrane theory:

$$\sigma = \frac{p R}{2 t} = \frac{50 \times 10^3 \times 31.25 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1562.5}{0.004} \approx 0.391 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Verify using the Mooney–Rivlin equibiaxial equation.** With $\lambda = 1.107$:

$$\lambda - \lambda^{-5} = 1.107 - (1.107)^{-5} = 1.107 - 0.595 = 0.512$$

$$\lambda^2 + \lambda^{-2} + 1 = 1.225 + 0.816 + 1 = 3.041$$

$$\sigma = 4 \times 0.512 \times [0.20 + 0.05 \times 3.041] = 2.048 \times [0.20 + 0.152] = 2.048 \times 0.352 \approx 0.721 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Iterate** — the predicted $\sigma$ (0.721 MPa) exceeds the membrane-theory estimate (0.391 MPa), so the actual $\delta$ must be larger. Repeat with $\delta = 15$ mm:

$$\lambda \approx 1 + \frac{2 \times 225}{3 \times 625} = 1.240$$

$$R = 625 / 30 = 20.83 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma_{membrane} = 50{,}000 \times 0.0208 / 0.004 = 0.260 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\lambda - \lambda^{-5} = 1.240 - 0.341 = 0.899$$

$$\sigma_{MR} = 4 \times 0.899 \times [0.20 + 0.05 \times (1.538 + 0.650 + 1)] = 3.596 \times [0.20 + 0.159] = 3.596 \times 0.359 \approx 1.291 \text{ MPa}$$

The convergence is slow due to the spherical-cap assumption. The converged solution (within 5 %) is approximately:

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 1.30, \quad \sigma \approx 1.0 \text{ MPa}}$$

For KTU valuation, full numerical iteration is **not** required; the methodology and at least **two iterations** must be shown to earn full marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Incompressibility misuse** — Forgetting that $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ and using $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 1$ in equibiaxial/pure-shear problems. This is the **single most common error** (loss of up to 4 marks).
>
> 2. **Confusing engineering vs. true stress** — The neo-Hookean formula $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ gives the **engineering (Piola–Kirchhoff) stress**, not the Cauchy stress. Mixing the two in numerical problems leads to a factor-of-$\lambda$ error.
>
> 3. **Wrong Mooney–Rivlin form** — Writing the equibiaxial formula as $\sigma = 2(\lambda - \lambda^{-5})(C_1 + C_2/\lambda)$ is a frequent mistake copied from the uniaxial form. The correct **equibiaxial** formula has a factor of 4 and a different $C_2$-multiplier.
>
> 4. **Dropping the constant** — Students often write $W = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k_B T (I_1 - 3)$ without justification. The derivation **must include** the Boltzmann entropy step and the affine assumption explicitly.
>
> 5. **Using $E_0 = 3G$ wrongly** — This relation holds only for **incompressible** materials. For compressible elastomers (e.g., foamed rubber), $E_0 = 2G(1+\nu)$ with $\nu \neq 0.49$ must be used.
>
> 6. **Sign of temperature coefficient** — Writing "$E$ decreases with $T$" for rubber loses 1 mark; the correct answer is the **opposite** of metals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Stress Strain Behaviour of Elastomers**

- **Definition:** Elastomers exhibit **> 100 % recoverable strain** under small stress, governed by **entropic elasticity** of crosslinked networks.
- **Three-region curve:** Initial Hookean (Gaussian), strain-softening plateau, finite-extensibility up-turn.
- **Incompressibility:** $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.49$.
- **Neo-Hookean (uniaxial):** $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$, $G = \nu k_B T = \rho R T / (2 M_c)$.
- **Neo-Hookean (equibiaxial):** $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-4})$.
- **Neo-Hookean (pure shear):** $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-1})$.
- **Mooney–Rivlin (uniaxial):** $\sigma = 2(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})(C_1 + C_2/\lambda)$.
- **Mooney–Rivlin (equibiaxial):** $\sigma = 4(\lambda - \lambda^{-5})[C_1 + C_2(\lambda^2 + \lambda^{-2} + 1)]$.
- **Strain energy density (Gaussian):** $W = \tfrac{1}{2} G (I_1 - 3) = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k_B T (I_1 - 3)$.
- **Entropy change per chain (affine):** $\Delta S = -\tfrac{1}{2} k_B (\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3)$.
- **Thermodynamic equation of state:** $f = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V} + T (\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$.
- **Energy vs entropy split:** For NR, $f_s / f \approx 0.85$, $f_e / f \approx 0.15$.
- **Temperature dependence:** $E_0 \propto T$ for ideal rubber (opposite of metals).
- **Crosslink–modulus link:** $G = \rho R T / (2 M_c)$, $E_0 = 3G$.
- **Finite chain extensibility:** Captured by the inverse Langevin function; produces Region III up-turn.
- **Mullins effect:** Stress-softening on first loading; absent on subsequent cycles below the previous maximum strain.
- **Payne effect:** Strain-amplitude dependence of dynamic modulus in filled rubbers.
- **Hysteresis:** Energy loss per cycle due to viscoelastic dissipation; area inside stress–strain loop.
- **KTU keyword trigger words to memorise:** *affine network, Gaussian chain, neo-Hookean, Mooney–Rivlin, equibiaxial, strain invariant, hyperelastic, strain energy density, crosslink density, entropic elasticity*.
- **Most-tested formula on KTU papers (Module 2):** $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ — must be derivable from $W = \tfrac{1}{2} G (I_1 - 3)$.
- **Numerical pattern:** $G$ in MPa range for soft rubbers (0.1–1 MPa); $C_1 \approx G/2$ in Mooney–Rivlin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic Recap.** This concludes the KTU 2024 Scheme premium notes for **Stress Strain Behaviour of Elastomers** (Module 2, PECHT523). All derivations, formulae, diagrams, and valuation-pitfall callouts are aligned with the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework and the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply, Analyse, Evaluate).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscoelasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viscoelasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscoelasticity** is the property of materials that exhibit both **viscous** (fluid-like, time-dependent, dissipative) and **elastic** (solid-like, recoverable, energy-storing) characteristics when subjected to deformation. A viscoelastic material stores part of the deformation energy (elastic response) and dissipates the remaining part as heat through internal friction (viscous response).

Mathematically, the stress in a viscoelastic body is a function not only of the instantaneous strain but also of the **strain rate**, the **entire strain history**, and **temperature**. This makes viscoelasticity a **history-dependent** mechanical behavior, which is intermediate between the classical Hookean solid (purely elastic) and the Newtonian fluid (purely viscous).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Viscoelasticity is the response of a material that combines the characteristics of an elastic solid and a Newtonian fluid. The stress in a viscoelastic material is time-dependent, and the strain produced depends on the rate of loading as well as the magnitude of the applied stress."*

In KTU board examinations, students frequently lose marks because they mention "viscoelasticity" but fail to explicitly state the **dual nature** (viscous + elastic) and the **time-dependence** of the response.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Silly Putty" Mental Model

Imagine a ball of **Silly Putty** (silicone polymer) lying on a table.

- If you **stretch it slowly** with your hands over 10 seconds, it flows like a thick honey and may even break under its own weight — **viscous behavior dominates**.
- If you **strike it sharply** with a hammer, it shatters like glass — **elastic behavior dominates** because there is no time for viscous flow to occur.
- If you **roll it into a ball and drop it**, it bounces like rubber — it stores energy elastically and returns it.
- If you **leave the ball on the table for an hour**, it flattens into a puddle — **irreversible viscous flow**.

This single experiment captures every important aspect of viscoelasticity:

| Loading Condition | Dominant Behavior | Energy Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Slow, gradual loading | Viscous | Energy dissipated as heat |
| Fast, sudden loading | Elastic | Energy stored and recovered |
| Sustained constant load | Creep (time-dependent strain) | Mixed |
| Constant deformation | Stress relaxation | Mixed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why polymers are viscoelastic:** Long-chain macromolecules can uncoil (elastic, instantaneous) and slide past each other (viscous, time-dependent). Both mechanisms occur simultaneously, giving rise to the viscoelastic response.

### 1.3 The Five Classical Signatures of Viscoelastic Behavior

When a polymer is subjected to a step change in stress (or strain), five hallmark phenomena appear. Examiners frequently test these directly:

1. **Creep** — Continuous deformation under constant applied stress over time.
2. **Stress Relaxation** — Gradual decay of stress under constant applied strain.
3. **Stress-Strain Hysteresis** — The loading and unloading curves do not coincide; the enclosed area equals the energy dissipated as heat.
4. **Strain-Rate Sensitivity** — A polymer loaded rapidly appears stiffer and stronger than the same polymer loaded slowly.
5. **Dynamic Mechanical Damping** — Under cyclic loading, the material exhibits a phase lag ($\delta$) between stress and strain.

### 1.4 Engineering Significance

The viscoelastic response governs the **service life**, **dimensional stability**, **load-bearing capacity**, and **energy dissipation** of virtually every polymeric product:

- **Tire treads**: must dissipate energy (hysteresis) for grip, but also store energy elastically for fuel efficiency.
- **Pipeline gaskets**: must creep under sustained pressure without leaking.
- **Vibration dampers**: rely on the loss modulus $E''$ to absorb mechanical noise.
- **Biomedical implants** (UHMWPE hip liners): viscoelasticity determines long-term wear.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Elementary Mechanical Analogues

Because the governing equations of viscoelasticity are mathematically identical to those of certain mechanical circuits, KTU examiners universally expect students to master the **Maxwell model** and the **Kelvin–Voigt model**, and then understand their combination into the **Standard Linear Solid (SLS) Model**.

#### 2.1.1 Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in Series)

**Construction:** A Hookean spring of modulus $E$ connected in series with a Newtonian dashpot of viscosity $\eta$.

- Under an applied strain $\varepsilon$, the total strain rate is the sum of the strain rates in each element:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt}$$

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

This is the **constitutive equation** of the Maxwell model. It is the foundational differential equation of linear viscoelasticity.

- **Relaxation time** (the characteristic time of the model):
$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} \quad [\text{units: seconds}]$$

#### 2.1.2 Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in Parallel)

**Construction:** A Hookean spring of modulus $E$ in parallel with a Newtonian dashpot of viscosity $\eta$.

- Both elements experience the **same strain**, while the **stresses add**:

$$\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

- **Retardation time**:
$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} \quad [\text{units: seconds}]$$

#### 2.1.3 Standard Linear Solid (SLS) Model (Zener Model)

The Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt models individually fail to describe both **creep** and **stress relaxation** accurately. The **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** model combines them (either as a Maxwell element in parallel with a spring, or a Kelvin element in series with a spring) and successfully reproduces both phenomena.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of the Creep Response

When a constant stress $\sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$:

1. **At $t = 0^+$** — The dashpot cannot respond instantaneously; the spring carries the entire load. An **instantaneous elastic strain** $\sigma_0 / E_1$ appears.
2. **For $0 < t < \infty$** — The dashpot begins to flow, transferring load gradually to the second branch.
3. **At $t \rightarrow \infty$** — Equilibrium is reached. The total strain equals the long-term compliance $\sigma_0 / E_2$.

The **creep compliance** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ is the ratio of time-dependent strain to applied stress.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of the Stress-Relaxation Response

When a constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ is applied at $t = 0$:

1. **At $t = 0^+$** — The spring stretches instantaneously; initial stress equals $E \cdot \varepsilon_0$.
2. **For $0 < t < \infty$** — The dashpot elongates, relieving the spring of part of its load.
3. **At $t \rightarrow \infty$** — Stress decays to zero (Maxwell) or to a finite equilibrium (SLS).

The **relaxation modulus** $E(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_0$ decays exponentially.

### 2.4 Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) — The Frequency-Domain Picture

When a sinusoidal stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied, a viscoelastic solid responds with a **phase-lagged** strain:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$$

This phase lag $\delta$ is the basis for defining two moduli:

- **Storage modulus** $E'$ — the in-phase (elastic) component.
- **Loss modulus** $E''$ — the out-of-phase (viscous) component.
- **Loss tangent** $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ — the ratio of dissipated to stored energy per cycle.

### 2.5 Time–Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)

Because increasing temperature is **equivalent** (in its molecular effects) to increasing the observation time, the entire viscoelastic response curve at one temperature can be **horizontally shifted** along the log-time axis to construct a **master curve** at a reference temperature $T_0$.

The horizontal shift factor $a_T$ is given empirically by the **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation**:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_0)}{C_2 + (T - T_0)}$$

where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are **universal constants** for a given polymer (approximately $C_1 \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K when $T_0$ is the glass transition temperature $T_g$).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Reference for KTU Board Examinations — Viscoelasticity**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units / Key Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maxwell constitutive equation | $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ | SI units throughout |
| 2 | Maxwell relaxation time | $\tau = \eta / E$ | seconds |
| 3 | Maxwell stress relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | stress decays exponentially |
| 4 | Maxwell creep (under $\sigma_0$) | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta}$ | unbounded viscous flow |
| 5 | Kelvin–Voigt constitutive equation | $\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | — |
| 6 | Kelvin retardation time | $\tau = \eta / E$ | seconds |
| 7 | Kelvin–Voigt creep | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | bounded, asymptotic |
| 8 | Kelvin–Voigt stress relaxation | Not physically meaningful (strain must be ramped) | — |
| 9 | Standard Linear Solid creep | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_2} + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_1}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | $\tau = \eta / E_1$ |
| 10 | SLS stress relaxation | $\sigma(t) = E_2 \varepsilon_0 + (E_1 \varepsilon_0)\, e^{-t/\tau}$ | bounded decay |
| 11 | Complex modulus | $E^* = E' + i\, E''$ | Pa |
| 12 | Storage modulus | $E' = \vert E^* \vert \cos \delta$ | Pa |
| 13 | Loss modulus | $E'' = \vert E^* \vert \sin \delta$ | Pa |
| 14 | Loss tangent | $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | WLF shift factor | $\log_{10} a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_0)}{C_2 + (T - T_0)}$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Five regions of viscoelastic behavior | Glassy, transition, rubbery, rubbery flow, viscous flow | — |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Maxwell model is used in industry to predict **stress relaxation in rubber seals** and **pre-stressed concrete**. The Kelvin–Voigt model is the basis for understanding **creep in pipeline-grade HDPE** (PE-100). The SLS model is the engineering standard for **finite-element simulations** of thermoplastic components under sustained load. Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) is the laboratory technique used to identify the **glass transition temperature $T_g$** from the peak of the $\tan\delta$ vs. $T$ curve.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Every algebraic step below is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. Every transition is annotated with the underlying physical or mathematical principle. This level of detail is what earns full marks on a KTU 14-mark derivation question.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maxwell Model: Stress Relaxation Under Constant Strain

**Given:** A Maxwell element (spring $E$ in series with dashpot $\eta$) is subjected to a step strain $\varepsilon_0$ applied instantaneously at $t = 0$.

**To find:** The relaxation modulus $E(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_0$.

**Step 1 — State the constitutive equation of the Maxwell model:**

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

*Justification:* Because the spring and dashpot are in series, they share the **same stress $\sigma$**, and their strains add: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$. Differentiating gives the equation above.

**Step 2 — Apply the constant-strain condition.**

Because $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ is constant for $t \geq 0$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$$

*Justification:* The total strain is held fixed by the testing machine; hence its time derivative vanishes.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the constitutive equation:**

$$0 = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\,\sigma$$

*Justification:* This is a first-order linear homogeneous ODE for $\sigma(t)$.

**Step 4 — Identify the relaxation time.**

Let $\tau = \eta / E$. Then $E / \eta = 1 / \tau$, and:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{\sigma}{\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$) to a generic time $t$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma'}{\sigma'} = -\frac{1}{\tau}\int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to obtain the final stress-relaxation response:**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Express the relaxation modulus:**

Since $E(t) = \sigma(t) / \varepsilon_0$ and $\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon_0$:

$$\boxed{\;E(t) = E\, e^{-t/\tau}\;}$$

*Physical interpretation:* The stress decays exponentially with characteristic time $\tau$. After one relaxation time ($t = \tau$), the stress has fallen to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value. The Maxwell element cannot support a sustained load indefinitely — it is a **fluid-like** response.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maxwell Model: Creep Under Constant Stress

**Given:** Apply a constant stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$ and measure the resulting strain $\varepsilon(t)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the constant-stress condition:**

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the constitutive equation:**

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\cdot 0 + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate with respect to time:**

$$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt'}\, dt' = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\, dt'$$

$$\varepsilon(t) - \varepsilon(0) = \frac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the initial condition.** At $t = 0$, only the spring deforms instantaneously, so $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0 / E$:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Express the creep compliance:**

$$\boxed{\;J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta}\;}$$

*Physical interpretation:* The strain grows without bound as $t \rightarrow \infty$ — characteristic of a fluid. The Maxwell model captures the **instantaneous elastic** response correctly, but its unbounded viscous flow is unphysical for a solid polymer. This is a known **limitation** and a frequent KTU exam question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kelvin–Voigt Model: Creep Under Constant Stress

**Given:** A Kelvin–Voigt element subjected to a constant stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$.

**Step 1 — State the constitutive equation:**

$$\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constant-stress condition:**

$$\sigma_0 = E\,\varepsilon + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta}\,\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the retardation time $\tau = \eta / E$:**

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear first-order ODE using an integrating factor $\mu(t) = e^{t/\tau}$:**

Multiply both sides by $e^{t/\tau}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\varepsilon \cdot e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\, e^{t/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$:**

$$\varepsilon(t)\, e^{t/\tau} - \varepsilon(0)\, e^{0} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\int_{0}^{t} e^{t'/\tau}\, dt'$$

The integral evaluates to:

$$\int_{0}^{t} e^{t'/\tau}\, dt' = \tau\!\left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the initial condition.** Because the dashpot cannot deform instantaneously, $\varepsilon(0) = 0$:

$$\varepsilon(t)\, e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0 \, \tau}{\eta}\!\left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0 \, \tau}{\eta}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\tau = \eta / E$ and simplify:**

$$\frac{\tau}{\eta} = \frac{\eta/E}{\eta} = \frac{1}{E}$$

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\;}$$

**Step 8 — Express the creep compliance:**

$$\boxed{\;J(t) = \frac{1}{E}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\;}$$

*Physical interpretation:* The strain **asymptotically approaches** the finite limit $\sigma_0/E$. The Kelvin–Voigt model captures the **bounded** creep of a solid correctly, but it **fails to predict instantaneous elastic strain** because the dashpot cannot respond at $t = 0$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Storage Modulus, Loss Modulus, and Loss Tangent

**Given:** Sinusoidal stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ applied to a linear viscoelastic solid.

**Step 1 — Express the strain response with phase lag $\delta$:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$$

*Justification:* The strain lags behind the stress because viscous dissipation takes time.

**Step 2 — Expand the strain using the trigonometric identity:**

$$\sin(\omega t - \delta) = \sin(\omega t)\cos\delta - \cos(\omega t)\sin\delta$$

Therefore:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) - \varepsilon_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the in-phase and out-of-phase amplitudes:**

$$\varepsilon'(t) = \varepsilon_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(in-phase, elastic)}$$

$$\varepsilon''(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(out-of-phase, viscous)}$$

**Step 4 — Define the storage and loss moduli from the stress-strain ratios of the respective components:**

$$E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta = \vert E^* \vert \cos\delta$$

$$E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta = \vert E^* \vert \sin\delta$$

**Step 5 — Construct the complex modulus:**

$$E^* = E' + i\,E''$$

**Step 6 — Form the loss tangent:**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{\sin\delta}{\cos\delta}$$

*Physical interpretation:* $\tan\delta$ is the **ratio of energy dissipated to energy stored per cycle**. It peaks near the glass transition temperature $T_g$, making DMA the most sensitive technique for locating $T_g$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Mechanical Models

| Property | Maxwell | Kelvin–Voigt | Standard Linear Solid (SLS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring + Dashpot arrangement | Series | Parallel | Maxwell $\parallel$ spring, or Kelvin $-$ spring |
| Constitutive equation | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dot{\sigma}/E + \sigma/\eta$ | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dot{\varepsilon}$ | $\sigma + (\eta/E_1)\dot{\sigma} = E_2 \varepsilon + \eta(1 + E_2/E_1)\dot{\varepsilon}$ |
| Creep under $\sigma_0$ | $\sigma_0/E + \sigma_0 t/\eta$ (unbounded) | $(\sigma_0/E)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ (bounded) | Bounded with both instant + delayed elastic |
| Stress relaxation under $\varepsilon_0$ | $\sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ (decays to 0) | Requires ramp loading | Decays to a finite equilibrium $E_2 \varepsilon_0$ |
| Instantaneous elasticity | Yes ($\sigma_0/E$) | No | Yes |
| Long-term behavior | Viscous flow | Elastic equilibrium | Elastic equilibrium |
| Suitable for | Stress relaxation in fluids, polymer melts | Creep in elastomers, concrete | Solid polymers — most realistic |
| Number of parameters | 2 ($E$, $\eta$) | 2 ($E$, $\eta$) | 3 ($E_1$, $E_2$, $\eta$) |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Five Viscoelastic Regions (Master Curve)

| Region | Temperature Range | Modulus Behavior | Molecular Motion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Glassy | $T \ll T_g$ | $E' \sim 10^9$ – $10^{10}$ Pa, plateau | Only vibrations; segmental motion frozen |
| 2. Glass transition | $T \approx T_g$ | $E'$ drops by 3 decades; $\tan\delta$ peaks | Long-range segmental motion unlocks |
| 3. Rubbery | $T_g < T < T_g + 50$ K | $E' \sim 10^6$ Pa, plateau | Entanglements act as temporary crosslinks |
| 4. Rubbery flow | $T_g + 50$ K $< T < T_g + 100$ K | $E'$ decreases steadily | Entanglements slip; chain disentanglement begins |
| 5. Viscous flow | $T \gg T_g$ | $E' \rightarrow 0$; material flows | Complete chain slippage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Selecting a Mechanical Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify loading condition"] --> q1{"Is strain held constant?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> stress["Stress Relaxation Problem"]
    q1 -- No --> q2{"Is stress held constant?"}
    q2 -- Yes --> creep["Creep Problem"]
    q2 -- No --> q3{"Is loading sinusoidal?"}
    q3 -- Yes --> dma["Dynamic Mechanical Analysis"]
    q3 -- No --> general["General history-dependent loading"]

    stress --> q4{"Does stress decay to zero?"}
    q4 -- Yes --> m1["Use Maxwell Model"]
    q4 -- No --> q5{"Does stress decay to a finite value?"}
    q5 -- Yes --> m2["Use Standard Linear Solid Model"]

    creep --> q6{"Does strain grow without bound?"}
    q6 -- Yes --> m3["Use Maxwell Model"]
    q6 -- No --> q7{"Is there an instantaneous elastic strain?"}
    q7 -- No --> m4["Use Kelvin-Voigt Model"]
    q7 -- Yes --> m5["Use Standard Linear Solid Model"]

    dma --> dyn["Compute E prime, E double prime, tan delta from phase lag delta"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of the Three Mechanical Analogues

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["MAXWELL MODEL"]
        sp1["Spring E"] --- dash1["Dashpot eta"] --- load1["Applied Stress sigma"]
    end

    subgraph M2["KELVIN-VOIGT MODEL"]
        sp2["Spring E"]
        dash2["Dashpot eta"]
        load2["Applied Stress sigma"]
        sp2 --- commonNode["Shared Strain epsilon"]
        dash2 --- commonNode
        load2 --- commonNode
    end

    subgraph M3["STANDARD LINEAR SOLID MODEL"]
        branchA["Maxwell Branch Spring E1 + Dashpot eta"]
        branchB["Spring E2 in parallel with branchA"]
        load3["Applied Stress sigma"]
        branchA --- nodeB["Common Strain node"]
        branchB --- nodeB
        load3 --- nodeB
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Stress-Relaxation Timeline in a Maxwell Element

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Test Machine
    participant S as Spring E
    participant D as Dashpot eta
    participant M as Maxwell Element

    T->>M: Apply step strain epsilon0 at t=0
    M->>S: Spring stretches instantaneously
    S-->>T: Instant stress = E * epsilon0
    Note over S,D: Time t = 0+
    D->>D: Dashpot begins to elongate
    S-->>T: Spring force decays as sigma0 e power negative t over tau
    D-->>T: Dashpot absorbs the deformation
    Note over S,D: Time t large
    S-->>T: Spring stress approaches zero
    D-->>T: Dashpot carries all deformation
    Note over M: Asymptotic state: stress equals zero
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Dynamic Mechanical Analysis Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input["Sinusoidal Stress sigma t equals sigma0 sin omega t"] --> sample["Viscoelastic Polymer Specimen"]
    sample --> response["Strain Response epsilon t equals epsilon0 sin omega t minus delta"]
    response --> splitter{"Decompose by Phase"}
    splitter --> inphase["In-Phase Component amplitude epsilon0 cos delta"]
    splitter --> outphase["Out-of-Phase Component amplitude epsilon0 sin delta"]
    inphase --> estore["Storage Modulus E prime = sigma0 over epsilon0 times cos delta"]
    outphase --> eloss["Loss Modulus E double prime = sigma0 over epsilon0 times sin delta"]
    estore --> ratio["Loss Tangent tan delta = E double prime over E prime"]
    eloss --> ratio
    ratio --> output["Plot tan delta versus Temperature T to locate Tg"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Examiners:** In the Maxwell element diagram, note that the spring and dashpot are arranged end-to-end (series), so the **same force** passes through both. In the Kelvin–Voigt diagram, both elements experience the **same deformation** (parallel). The SLS model is the only one that has a **permanent (equilibrium) branch** — represented by the spring $E_2$ in parallel with the Maxwell arm.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, 3 Marks]**
> *Define viscoelasticity. List any four characteristic features of viscoelastic materials.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Viscoelasticity is the time-dependent mechanical response of a material that exhibits **both elastic (solid-like) and viscous (fluid-like) behavior** simultaneously when subjected to an applied stress or strain.

**Four characteristic features:**

1. **Creep** — continuous deformation under constant applied stress.
2. **Stress relaxation** — decay of stress under constant applied strain.
3. **Strain-rate sensitivity** — apparent stiffness increases with loading rate.
4. **Hysteresis** — energy is dissipated as heat during cyclic loading (loading and unloading curves do not coincide).

*(Additional valid features include: stress-strain phase lag under dynamic loading; partial strain recovery upon unloading; temperature-rate equivalence.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 3 Marks]**
> *Differentiate between the Maxwell model and the Kelvin–Voigt model.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Maxwell Model | Kelvin–Voigt Model |
|---|---|---|
| Arrangement | Spring and dashpot in **series** | Spring and dashpot in **parallel** |
| Governing equation | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dot{\sigma}/E + \sigma/\eta$ | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dot{\varepsilon}$ |
| Creep behavior | Strain grows **unboundedly** with time | Strain **asymptotically approaches** $\sigma_0/E$ |
| Stress relaxation | Stress decays **exponentially to zero** | Cannot describe true relaxation; requires ramp loading |
| Instantaneous elasticity | **Yes** | **No** |
| Suitable for describing | Polymer melts, fluids | Solid elastomers under creep |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic diagram, derive the governing differential equation of the **Maxwell model** and obtain its expression for **stress relaxation** under a step strain $\varepsilon_0$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A Maxwell element has $E = 5 \times 10^8$ Pa and $\eta = 5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa·s. If a step strain of $\varepsilon_0 = 0.02$ is applied, calculate: (i) the relaxation time $\tau$, (ii) the initial stress $\sigma_0$, and (iii) the stress remaining after 20 seconds.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (Full Marks 7):**

**Schematic (described in words; draw in exam):** A spring of modulus $E$ connected end-to-end with a dashpot of viscosity $\eta$. Both share the same stress $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Strain additivity (series):**

$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 2 — Spring and dashpot constitutive laws:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\sigma}{E} \quad ; \quad \dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{dashpot}} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate spring equation and sum:**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

This is the **governing differential equation of the Maxwell model** (1 mark).

**Step 4 — Apply constant strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$:**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve with initial condition $\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_0$:**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon_0 \, e^{-t/\tau} \quad \text{where} \;\; \tau = \eta/E\;}$$

(3 marks for solving the ODE, 1 mark for stating the boundary condition, 1 mark for the final boxed result, 1 mark for the schematic.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (Full Marks 7):**

**Given:**
- $E = 5 \times 10^8$ Pa
- $\eta = 5 \times 10^9$ Pa·s
- $\varepsilon_0 = 0.02$ (dimensionless)
- $t = 20$ s

**Step 1 — Compute the relaxation time:**

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^8} = 10 \;\; \text{seconds}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute the initial stress:**

$$\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 = (5 \times 10^8)(0.02) = 1 \times 10^7 \;\; \text{Pa} = 10 \;\; \text{MPa}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Compute the stress at $t = 20$ s:**

$$\sigma(20) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau} = (1 \times 10^7)\, e^{-20/10}$$

$$= (1 \times 10^7)\, e^{-2}$$

$$= (1 \times 10^7)(0.1353)$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(20) \approx 1.353 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{Pa} \approx 1.35 \;\; \text{MPa}\;}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** After one relaxation time ($t = \tau = 10$ s), the stress would have decayed to $36.8\%$ of $\sigma_0$. After $2\tau = 20$ s, the stress is $\approx 13.5\%$ of $\sigma_0$, demonstrating the exponential stress-relaxation behavior characteristic of a viscoelastic Maxwell fluid.

**Step 5 — Dimensional check (1 mark):** All quantities carry correct SI units (Pa for stress, seconds for time, dimensionless for strain).

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Standard Linear Solid (SLS) model**. Draw the schematic and derive expressions for both **creep** and **stress relaxation**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Define **storage modulus** ($E'$), **loss modulus** ($E''$), and **loss tangent** ($\tan\delta$). Explain how DMA is used to determine the **glass transition temperature** ($T_g$) of a polymer. Sketch the typical variation of $E'$, $E''$, and $\tan\delta$ with temperature.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Standard Linear Solid Model (Full Marks 7):**

**Schematic:** A Maxwell arm (spring $E_1$ in series with dashpot $\eta$) placed in parallel with a free spring $E_2$. Total stress is the sum of the stresses in each branch; total strain is the same in both branches.

**Step 1 — Branch stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = \text{stress in Maxwell arm}, \quad \sigma_2 = E_2 \varepsilon \;\;(\text{parallel spring})$$

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_1(t) + E_2 \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 2 — Maxwell arm constitutive law:**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}_1}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_1}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $\sigma_1$ to obtain the SLS constitutive equation:**

After algebraic manipulation (valid exam answer: cite the result directly):

$$\sigma + \frac{\eta}{E_1}\,\dot{\sigma} = E_2\,\varepsilon + \eta\!\left(1 + \frac{E_2}{E_1}\right)\dot{\varepsilon}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Creep response** (apply $\sigma = \sigma_0$, $\dot{\sigma} = 0$, initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0/(E_1+E_2)$):

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_1 + E_2} + \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2}\!\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]\;}$$

where $\tau = \eta/E_1 + \eta/E_2$ (effective retardation time).

**Step 5 — Stress relaxation response** (apply $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$, $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$, initial condition $\sigma(0) = (E_1 + E_2)\varepsilon_0$):

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = E_2\,\varepsilon_0 + E_1\,\varepsilon_0\, e^{-t/\tau}\;}$$

*(3 marks for derivations, 2 marks for schematic, 1 mark for initial conditions.)*

**Key feature:** SLS combines the strengths of Maxwell (instantaneous elasticity) and Kelvin–Voigt (bounded long-term response), making it the **most realistic 3-parameter model** for solid polymers.

---

**Part (b) — Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (Full Marks 7):**

**Step 1 — Define $E'$ (Storage Modulus, 1 mark):**

The **in-phase** component of the complex modulus, representing the **elastic** (energy-storing) response:

$$E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta$$

**Step 2 — Define $E''$ (Loss Modulus, 1 mark):**

The **out-of-phase** component, representing the **viscous** (energy-dissipating) response:

$$E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta$$

**Step 3 — Define $\tan\delta$ (Loss Tangent, 1 mark):**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$

It is the **ratio of energy dissipated to energy stored per cycle** — a dimensionless damping metric.

**Step 4 — Locate $T_g$ using DMA (2 marks):**

The glass transition temperature $T_g$ is located at the **peak of the $\tan\delta$ vs. $T$ curve** (or alternatively, the peak of $E''$ vs. $T$, or the inflection point of the $E'$ vs. $T$ curve).

**Step 5 — Sketch the curves (2 marks):**

> **Required sketch features (any one valid description):**
> - $E'$ is high and nearly constant in the glassy region, drops sharply (over 2–4 decades) through $T_g$, and levels off in the rubbery plateau.
> - $E''$ is small in the glassy region, rises to a maximum near $T_g$, then decays in the rubbery region.
> - $\tan\delta$ similarly peaks near $T_g$; its peak temperature is typically 10–20 °C above the $E''$ peak.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the initial condition** when solving the ODEs. Marks are specifically allotted for stating $\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_0$ (Maxwell) or $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ (Kelvin–Voigt). Missing this costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the relaxation time and retardation time** — they are mathematically identical ($\tau = \eta/E$) but arise from different physical contexts. Name them correctly.
> 3. **Stating the WLF constants** without identifying $T_0$ as the **reference temperature** (often $T_g$). Always write the WLF equation with $T_0$ explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up the Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt creep formulas.** Memorize: Maxwell creep = $\sigma_0/E + \sigma_0 t/\eta$ (unbounded); Kelvin creep = $(\sigma_0/E)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ (bounded).
> 5. **Drawing the SLS schematic incorrectly.** The Maxwell arm must be in parallel with the **second spring**, not in series. The series–parallel topology is worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units** on numerical answers. KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks for missing SI units (Pa, s).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Viscoelasticity (Module 2)**

**Core Definitions**

- Viscoelasticity = elastic (solid) + viscous (fluid) response, **time-dependent**.
- Creep = time-dependent strain under constant stress.
- Stress relaxation = time-dependent stress under constant strain.

**Mechanical Models (memorize all three)**

- **Maxwell** — spring $\parallel^{?}$ NO — spring and dashpot **in series**. Creep unbounded; stress decays to zero.
- **Kelvin–Voigt** — spring and dashpot **in parallel**. Creep bounded; cannot predict stress relaxation properly.
- **SLS (Zener)** — Maxwell arm + parallel spring. Three parameters: $E_1$, $E_2$, $\eta$. Best for solid polymers.

**Key Time Constant**

- **Relaxation/Retardation time:** $\tau = \eta / E$ — units of seconds. After time $\tau$, exponential decay reaches $1/e \approx 36.8\%$.

**Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)**

- Apply sinusoidal stress at frequency $\omega$; strain lags by phase angle $\delta$.
- $E' = \vert E^* \vert \cos\delta$ (storage, elastic).
- $E'' = \vert E^* \vert \sin\delta$ (loss, viscous).
- $\tan\delta = E'' / E'$ peaks at $T_g$.

**Time–Temperature Superposition**

- Raising temperature $\equiv$ lengthening time (for amorphous polymers above $T_g$).
- Master curve constructed by horizontal shift along log-time axis.
- WLF equation: $\log_{10} a_T = -C_1(T - T_0) / [C_2 + (T - T_0)]$, with $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K when $T_0 = T_g$.

**Five Viscoelastic Regions of the Master Curve**

- Glassy → Glass transition (steep modulus drop) → Rubbery plateau → Rubbery flow → Viscous flow.

**Engineering Applications**

- Stress relaxation → rubber seals, gaskets.
- Creep → HDPE pipes, polymer bearings.
- DMA → $T_g$ measurement, damping materials.
- TTSP → accelerated lifetime prediction.

**Most-Frequently-Tested Derivations on KTU Exams**

1. Maxwell stress relaxation (4–5 marks).
2. Kelvin–Voigt creep (4–5 marks).
3. SLS creep and relaxation (7–10 marks).
4. Storage and loss modulus from sinusoidal loading (5–7 marks).
5. WLF equation application (numerical, 3–5 marks).

**Common Mnemonic — "Maxwell Squirms, Kelvin Holds"**

- **Maxwell** creeps **unboundedly** (squirms out).
- **Kelvin–Voigt** creeps **boundedly** (holds firm).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Relaxation and Creep
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress Relaxation and Creep

# Stress Relaxation and Creep in Polymers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Viscoelastic Behavior

Polymers occupy a unique position in materials science because they exhibit **viscoelastic behavior** — a hybrid response combining the elastic characteristics of a solid spring (Hookean behavior) and the viscous flow of a liquid dashpot (Newtonian behavior). This dual nature is the origin of two of the most industrially important time-dependent phenomena in polymer engineering: **Stress Relaxation** and **Creep**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT523 – Module 2):** The study of viscoelastic models (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, and Burgers), and the time-dependent mechanical response of polymers expressed through **creep compliance J(t)** and **relaxation modulus G(t)** forms the core of this module.

### 1.2 Stress Relaxation — Formal Definition

**Stress Relaxation** is defined as the time-dependent **decay of internal stress** in a polymeric specimen held under a **constant strain** at a fixed temperature.

Mathematically, the phenomenon is characterized by the **Relaxation Modulus**, denoted as:

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ is the constant imposed strain and $\sigma(t)$ is the decaying stress response.

### 1.3 Creep — Formal Definition

**Creep** is defined as the time-dependent **progressive increase in strain** experienced by a polymeric specimen subjected to a **constant applied stress** at a fixed temperature.

The property is quantified using **Creep Compliance**:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the constant stress and $\varepsilon(t)$ is the time-dependent strain.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Chewing Gum Experiment"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Take a piece of chewing gum and stretch it quickly to a fixed length, then hold it at that length with both hands.
>
> 1. You will *feel* the force (tension) in your hands **decrease** with time, even though the length of the gum has not changed. This is **Stress Relaxation**.
> 2. Now, take a fresh piece of gum, hang a constant weight from it, and watch. The gum will **gradually elongate** over minutes and hours. This is **Creep**.
>
> In both cases, the polymer chains are slowly rearranging (sliding past each other) to relieve the imposed mechanical disturbance.

### 1.5 Industrial Significance

| Phenomenon | Real-world Consequence |
|-------------|------------------------|
| Stress Relaxation | Loss of pre-tension in bolted polymer joints, gaskets, and O-rings |
| Creep | Sagging of plastic pipes, deformation of polymer gears, drape in textile fibres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Unit Note:** The **Relaxation Modulus G(t)** has units of **Pa (Pascals)**, while **Creep Compliance J(t)** has units of **Pa⁻¹ (Inverse Pascals)**. They are reciprocal functions only in the simplest linear viscoelastic cases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Analogue Models for Viscoelasticity

To mathematically describe stress relaxation and creep, we use idealized combinations of two fundamental mechanical elements:
- **Spring (Hookean element):** Represents ideal elastic behavior. Stress is directly proportional to strain: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$.
- **Dashpot (Newtonian element):** Represents ideal viscous flow. Stress is proportional to strain rate: $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$.

By combining these elements in **series** or **parallel**, we can represent different polymer behaviors.

### 2.2 The Maxwell Model (Series Spring + Dashpot)

The **Maxwell model** is a **spring and dashpot connected in series**. It is the foundational model for describing **stress relaxation**.

**Why series?** In a series connection, the **strain is additive**, but the **stress is the same** in both elements at all times.

**Governing Differential Equation:**

The total strain rate is the sum of the strain rate in the spring and the strain rate in the dashpot:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_{spring}}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$$

Substituting the constitutive laws:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

### 2.3 The Kelvin–Voigt Model (Parallel Spring + Dashpot)

The **Kelvin–Voigt model** connects a spring and dashpot in **parallel**. It is the foundational model for describing **creep**.

**Why parallel?** In a parallel connection, the **strain is identical** in both elements, but the **stresses are additive**.

**Governing Differential Equation:**

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}$$

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

### 2.4 The Burgers Model (Four-Element Model)

For accurate representation of both phenomena simultaneously, a **four-element model** is used:
- A Maxwell element (spring + dashpot in series) connected **in series** with a Kelvin–Voigt element (spring + dashpot in parallel).

This model captures:
1. **Instantaneous elastic deformation** (Maxwell spring)
2. **Irreversible flow** (Maxwell dashpot)
3. **Delayed viscoelastic recovery** (Kelvin–Voigt element)

### 2.5 The Critical Parameter — Relaxation Time $\tau$

The **Relaxation Time** $\tau$ is the single most important parameter in viscoelastic analysis. It is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = Viscosity of the dashpot (**Pa·s**)
- $E$ = Elastic modulus of the spring (**Pa**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of $\tau$:** The relaxation time is the characteristic time over which the polymer "forgets" the imposed deformation. When $t = \tau$, the stress in stress relaxation has decayed to approximately **36.8%** of its initial value ($\sigma = \sigma_0/e$).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Governing Equation | Boundary / Initial Condition | Units |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------------|-------|
| Maxwell Model (Stress Relaxation) | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$ | $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$ at constant $\varepsilon_0$ | Pa |
| Maxwell Model (Decay Modulus) | $G(t) = G_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$ | $G(0) = G_0$ | Pa |
| Kelvin–Voigt Model (Creep Strain) | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - \exp(-t/\tau)\right]$ | $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ at constant $\sigma_0$ | dimensionless |
| Kelvin–Voigt Model (Creep Compliance) | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[1 - \exp(-t/\tau)\right]$ | $J(0) = 0$ | Pa⁻¹ |
| Relaxation Time | $\tau = \eta / E$ | Always positive | seconds (s) |
| Burgers Model (Total Strain) | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1}t + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_2}\left[1 - \exp(-t/\tau_2)\right]$ | $\tau_2 = \eta_2 / E_2$ | dimensionless |
| Retardation Time (Kelvin–Voigt) | $\tau' = \eta / E$ | Same form as $\tau$ | seconds (s) |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility of These Models

1. **Polymer Pipe Design:** Long-term creep rupture data (using J(t) curves) determines the maximum safe working stress for HDPE pipes used in municipal water supply.
2. **Pre-stressed Concrete Anchorage:** Stress relaxation curves for polymer tendons predict the loss of locking force over decades.
3. **Vibration Damping:** The Kelvin–Voigt element models the energy dissipation in rubber mounts used in automotive suspensions.
4. **Aging Studies:** Time-temperature superposition (TTS) uses these models to predict long-term behavior from short-term tests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stress Relaxation from the Maxwell Model

### Objective:
To derive the time-dependent stress $\sigma(t)$ for a Maxwell model subjected to a constant applied strain $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$.

**Step 1 — Constitutive Laws of the Two Elements**

For the spring (Hooke's law):

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{spring}$$

For the dashpot (Newton's law of viscosity):

$$\sigma = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Series Connection Constraint**

In a series connection, the **strain adds up**, but the **stress is uniform** across both elements:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_{spring}}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Express Each Strain Rate in Terms of Stress**

From the spring equation:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{spring}}{dt} = \frac{1}{E} \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{dt}$$

From the dashpot equation:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the Master Differential Equation**

Substituting the above into Step 2:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Constant-Strain Boundary Condition**

If the polymer is held at a **constant strain** $\varepsilon_0$, then $\dfrac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = 0$. The equation simplifies to:

$$0 = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\sigma = -\frac{\sigma}{\tau}$$

where $\tau = \eta / E$ is the relaxation time.

**Step 6 — Solve the First-Order Linear ODE**

Separating variables:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

Integrating from $\sigma_0$ to $\sigma(t)$ and from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dt}{\tau}$$

This yields:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 7 — Final Exponential Decay Expression**

Exponentiating both sides, we obtain the celebrated stress relaxation equation:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Express as Relaxation Modulus G(t)**

Dividing both sides by the constant strain $\varepsilon_0$:

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = G_0 \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** At $t = \tau$, the stress has decayed to $\sigma(\tau) = \sigma_0/e \approx 0.368\,\sigma_0$. At $t = 5\tau$, the stress is essentially zero ($\approx 0.0067\,\sigma_0$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Creep from the Kelvin–Voigt Model

### Objective:
To derive the time-dependent strain $\varepsilon(t)$ for a Kelvin–Voigt model subjected to a constant stress $\sigma_0$.

**Step 1 — Constitutive Laws**

Spring strain: $\varepsilon_{spring} = \sigma / E$

Dashpot strain rate: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt} = \sigma / \eta$

**Step 2 — Apply the Parallel Connection Constraint**

In a parallel connection, the **strain is identical** in both elements, and the **stresses add up**:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}$$

$$\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard ODE Form**

$$\eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + E \cdot \varepsilon = \sigma_0$$

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta}\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Solve Using an Integrating Factor**

The integrating factor is $\mu(t) = e^{t/\tau}$. Multiplying both sides:

$$e^{t/\tau}\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}e^{t/\tau}$$

The left side becomes an exact derivative:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\varepsilon \cdot e^{t/\tau}\right) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}e^{t/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Both Sides**

$$\varepsilon \cdot e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\int e^{t/\tau}\,dt = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot \tau \cdot e^{t/\tau} + C$$

Since $\tau = \eta / E$, we have $\tau/\eta = 1/E$:

$$\varepsilon \cdot e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}e^{t/\tau} + C$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Initial Condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$**

At $t = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -\frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Final Creep Equation**

Substituting back and solving for $\varepsilon(t)$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\right]$$

**Step 8 — Express as Creep Compliance J(t)**

Dividing by the constant stress $\sigma_0$:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** At $t = \tau$, the strain has reached $\varepsilon(\tau) = (\sigma_0/E)(1 - 1/e) \approx 0.632\,\varepsilon_{\infty}$, where $\varepsilon_{\infty} = \sigma_0/E$ is the asymptotic equilibrium strain.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Burgers Model — Total Creep Strain

The Burgers model gives the **total strain** as the sum of three contributions:

1. Instantaneous elastic strain (Maxwell spring): $\varepsilon_1 = \sigma_0 / E_1$
2. Irreversible viscous flow (Maxwell dashpot): $\varepsilon_2 = (\sigma_0 / \eta_1) \cdot t$
3. Delayed viscoelastic response (Kelvin–Voigt element): $\varepsilon_3 = (\sigma_0 / E_2)[1 - e^{-t/\tau_2}]$

**Summing all three contributions:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1}t + \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_2}\right)\right]$$

This equation captures all four stages of polymer creep behavior observed experimentally.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Maxwell Model — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Constant Strain Input epsilon0] --> B[Spring E1]
    B --> C[Dashpot eta1]
    C --> D[Stress Output sigma t]
    E[Time axis t] --> C
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Functional Logic:** The input strain is shared between the spring and the dashpot. The spring stretches immediately; over time, the dashpot flows, transferring more strain to itself and causing the stress (resistance) to relax.

---

## 4.2 Kelvin–Voigt Model — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Constant Stress Input sigma0] --> B[Split into two parallel paths]
    B --> C[Spring E2]
    B --> D[Dashpot eta2]
    C --> E[Strain Output epsilon t]
    D --> E
    F[Time axis t] --> D
    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Functional Logic:** The constant stress is carried jointly by the spring and dashpot. The dashpot delays deformation, producing the characteristic delayed creep response that asymptotically approaches $\sigma_0/E$.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Polymer Creep Response Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initial Loading at t equals 0] --> B0[Stage 1: Instantaneous Elastic Strain]
    B0 --> C0[Stage 2: Primary Creep Region]
    C0 --> D0[Stage 3: Secondary Steady State Creep]
    D0 --> E0[Stage 4: Tertiary Creep leading to Failure]
    E0 --> F0[Creep Rupture of Polymer Sample]
    style A0 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
    style B0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style C0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style D0 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style E0 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style F0 fill:#8B0000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

**Description of Each Stage:**

| Stage | Physical Mechanism | Mathematical Equivalent |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Stage 1 | Bond stretching, chain uncoiling | Maxwell spring: $\sigma_0/E_1$ |
| Stage 2 | Slow chain sliding, entanglement adjustment | Kelvin–Voigt term: $(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ |
| Stage 3 | Steady viscous flow | Maxwell dashpot: $t/\eta_1$ |
| Stage 4 | Crazing, microvoid coalescence | Empirical polynomial fit |

---

## 4.4 Comparative Time-Response Curves — Stress Relaxation vs Creep

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stress Relaxation Curve] --> A2[sigma t equals sigma0 exp of minus t by tau]
    A2 --> A3[Exponential Decay]
    A3 --> A4[Asymptote at zero]
    B1[Creep Compliance Curve] --> B2[J t equals one by E times one minus exp of minus t by tau]
    B2 --> B3[Exponential Rise]
    B3 --> B4[Asymptote at one by E]
    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style A2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style A3 fill:#FF7F50,stroke:#000
    style A4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style B2 fill:#4682B4,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style B3 fill:#1E90FF,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style B4 fill:#00008B,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reminder:** The stress relaxation curve is a **mirror image** of the creep compliance curve when both are normalized and plotted on the same time axis. This symmetry arises because both phenomena are governed by the same relaxation time $\tau = \eta / E$.

---

## 4.5 Burgers Model — Comprehensive Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Constant Stress sigma0] --> M1[Maxwell Element: Spring E1]
    M1 --> M2[Maxwell Element: Dashpot eta1]
    M2 --> K1[Kelvin Element: Spring E2]
    K1 --> K2[Kelvin Element: Dashpot eta2 in parallel with E2]
    K2 --> OUT[Total Strain epsilon t]
    style A2 fill:#FF6347,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style M1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style M2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style K1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style K2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```
