# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define the term "Creep" in the context of polymer materials. What is the role of Creep Compliance in characterizing this behavior?**

**Model Answer:**

Creep is the time-dependent increase in strain experienced by a polymer when subjected to a **constant applied stress** at a fixed temperature. It originates from the progressive rearrangement, uncoiling, and slippage of polymer chains.

**Creep Compliance J(t)** is defined as the ratio of time-dependent strain to the constant applied stress:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

It is measured in **Pa⁻¹** and serves as a **material fingerprint** for viscoelastic behavior, allowing engineers to predict long-term deformation from short-term tests.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**What is meant by the "relaxation time" in polymer viscoelasticity? Briefly comment on its physical significance with reference to the Maxwell model.**

**Model Answer:**

The relaxation time $\tau$ is defined as the ratio of viscosity to elastic modulus:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

**Physical Significance:** It is the time required for the internal stress in a polymer (held at constant strain) to decay to **$1/e$ (approximately 36.8%)** of its initial value. It characterizes the speed at which the polymer transitions from elastic-like to viscous-like behavior. If $t \ll \tau$, the polymer behaves elastically; if $t \gg \tau$, viscous flow dominates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note (14 Marks):** Each Part B question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, typically **(a)** testing conceptual understanding and **(b)** testing derivations/calculations. Internal choice is provided between two full questions (A or B).

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the Maxwell model for viscoelastic behavior of polymers with a neat schematic. Derive the governing differential equation for this model.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **A sample of natural rubber is held at a constant strain of 0.05. The initial stress observed is 1.0 MPa. After 500 seconds, the stress decays to 0.40 MPa. Calculate the relaxation time of the rubber and the viscosity of the dashpot, given the elastic modulus $E = 2.0$ MPa.**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Maxwell model consists of a **spring** (modulus E) and a **dashpot** (viscosity $\eta$) connected **in series**. The series configuration implies:
- **Strain is additive:** $\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$
- **Stress is uniform:** $\sigma = \sigma_{spring} = \sigma_{dashpot}$

**Governing Differential Equation:**

Differentiating the strain additivity equation:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

This is the **Maxwell constitutive equation** governing the viscoelastic response.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Constant strain: $\varepsilon_0 = 0.05$
- Initial stress: $\sigma_0 = 1.0$ MPa
- Stress at $t = 500$ s: $\sigma(500) = 0.40$ MPa
- Elastic modulus: $E = 2.0$ MPa

**Step 1 — Apply the Maxwell Stress Relaxation Equation**

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$

Substituting the known values:

$$0.40 = 1.0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{500}{\tau}\right)$$

$$\exp\left(-\frac{500}{\tau}\right) = 0.40$$

**Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm**

$$-\frac{500}{\tau} = \ln(0.40) = -0.9163$$

$$\tau = \frac{500}{0.9163} = 545.6 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Viscosity**

Using $\tau = \eta / E$:

$$\eta = \tau \cdot E = 545.6 \times 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 1.091 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Describe the Kelvin–Voigt model. Derive the creep strain equation for a constant stress $\sigma_0$ using this model.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **A polymer sample is subjected to a constant stress of 0.5 MPa. The equilibrium strain is reached at 0.04. After 100 seconds, the strain is 0.025. Determine the relaxation time, the elastic modulus, and the viscosity of the dashpot.**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **Kelvin–Voigt model** is a **spring and dashpot connected in parallel**. In this configuration:
- **Strain is identical** in both elements
- **Stress is additive**

The governing equation is:

$$\sigma_0 = E\varepsilon + \eta\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Derivation of Creep Strain:**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

Using integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$ and applying initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\right]}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Constant stress: $\sigma_0 = 0.5$ MPa
- Equilibrium strain: $\varepsilon_\infty = 0.04$
- Strain at $t = 100$ s: $\varepsilon(100) = 0.025$

**Step 1 — Calculate the Elastic Modulus**

The equilibrium strain (as $t \to \infty$) is $\varepsilon_\infty = \sigma_0 / E$:

$$E = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_\infty} = \frac{0.5 \text{ MPa}}{0.04} = 12.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Creep Equation at t = 100 s**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)]$$

Substituting the known values:

$$0.025 = 0.04 \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{100}{\tau}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{0.025}{0.04} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{100}{\tau}\right)$$

$$0.625 = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{100}{\tau}\right)$$

$$\exp\left(-\frac{100}{\tau}\right) = 0.375$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Relaxation Time**

$$-\frac{100}{\tau} = \ln(0.375) = -0.9808$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{100}{0.9808} = 101.9 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Viscosity**

$$\eta = \tau \cdot E = 101.9 \times 12.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 1.274 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Relaxation Time and Retardation Time:** While numerically identical ($\tau = \eta/E$), students often forget to specify whether it is the **stress relaxation time** (Maxwell) or the **retardation time** (Kelvin–Voigt), which the examiner may deduct 1 mark for.
> 2. **Units Confusion:** Relaxation modulus is in **Pa**, while creep compliance is in **Pa⁻¹**. Mixing these up loses full marks in numerical problems.
> 3. **Forgetting Initial Conditions:** When deriving the ODE solution, the constant of integration must be evaluated using the correct initial condition (typically $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ for creep and $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$ for stress relaxation). Many students skip this step.
> 4. **Incorrect Model Identification:** A purely exponential stress decay $\Rightarrow$ **Maxwell**. A purely exponential strain rise to a plateau $\Rightarrow$ **Kelvin–Voigt**. Any combined response requires the **Burgers model**.
> 5. **Sign Error in the Exponential:** The expression must always be $\exp(-t/\tau)$, never $\exp(+t/\tau)$ for physical decay/growth.
> 6. **Linear vs. Logarithmic Plotting:** When solving for $\tau$ from experimental data, students should remember to take the **natural logarithm** before isolating $\tau$. Forgetting this loses 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Confusion Between Compliance and Modulus:** $J(t)$ and $G(t)$ are **not reciprocals** in general viscoelastic theory — that is only true for purely elastic solids. Examiners will specifically test this understanding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stress Relaxation and Creep**

- **Creep** = strain increases with time under **constant stress**.
- **Stress Relaxation** = stress decreases with time under **constant strain**.
- **Creep Compliance:** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) / \sigma_0$ (units: Pa⁻¹).
- **Relaxation Modulus:** $G(t) = \sigma(t) / \varepsilon_0$ (units: Pa).
- **Maxwell Model** uses **series** spring + dashpot — best for **stress relaxation**.
- **Kelvin–Voigt Model** uses **parallel** spring + dashpot — best for **creep**.
- **Burgers Model** = Maxwell in series with Kelvin–Voigt — captures **both** phenomena plus steady-state flow.
- **Stress Relaxation Equation:** $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$.
- **Creep Strain Equation:** $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0 / E)[1 - \exp(-t/\tau)]$.
- **Relaxation Time:** $\tau = \eta / E$ (in seconds).
- At $t = \tau$: stress drops to **36.8%** of initial value; strain reaches **63.2%** of equilibrium.
- Creep has **four stages**: instantaneous elastic, primary (decelerating), secondary (steady), tertiary (accelerating) leading to rupture.
- **Time-Temperature Superposition (TTS)** principle: at higher temperatures, relaxation occurs faster; horizontal shift factor $a_T$ is used to construct master curves.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** Below $T_g$, polymer is glassy and $\tau$ is huge (years); above $T_g$, $\tau$ drops dramatically.
- **Industrial relevance:** Pipe pressure ratings, polymer seals, pre-stressed tendons, and damping mounts all depend on these models.
- **Key distinction:** Maxwell = viscous flow in the model, but no steady-state creep plateau. Kelvin–Voigt = no instantaneous elastic recovery. Burgers = realistic combination of both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Rheological Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Rheological Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rheology** is the science of the deformation and flow of matter, particularly concerned with materials that exhibit a complex response to applied stress — combining characteristics of both viscous liquids and elastic solids. The **measurement of rheological properties** of polymers refers to the systematic experimental determination of parameters such as **shear viscosity (η)**, **normal stress differences (N₁, N₂)**, **storage modulus (G′)**, **loss modulus (G″)**, **complex viscosity (η\*)**, and **melt flow rate (MFR)** using standardized instrumentation.

For KTU 2024 (NEP 2020) Board Examination context, the topic is treated under the Course Outcome **CO2 — Characterize the flow behaviour of polymer melts and solutions**, and requires mastery of both *viscometric* (steady-shear) and *dynamic (oscillatory)* measurement techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymers are viscoelastic** — their response to stress contains both a viscous (energy-dissipating, rate-dependent) and an elastic (energy-storing, recoverable) component. This dual nature makes the measurement of their rheological properties substantially more complex than for simple Newtonian fluids such as water or air.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine trying to spread **honey** on a slice of bread versus spreading **cold butter** on the same bread:

- If you push honey with a spoon **slowly**, it flows easily. If you push it **fast**, it resists — this is a **shear-rate-dependent (non-Newtonian)** behaviour.
- Cold butter does not flow at all when pushed slowly — it deforms elastically. But if you push *really* hard, it fractures — it behaves like a **viscoelastic solid**.
- Now imagine **shampoo**: when it sits in the bottle it is thick, but the moment you squeeze it through the narrow neck, it becomes thin and flows easily. This is called **shear-thinning** (pseudoplasticity), and it is typical of polymer melts and concentrated polymer solutions.

A **rheometer** is essentially a precision instrument that applies a *known* stress (or strain) to a polymer and measures the *resulting* deformation (or stress). The numbers it produces — viscosity, moduli, etc. — quantify the resistance to flow across a wide range of conditions (temperature, shear rate, time, frequency).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Rheological data drives the design of every polymer-processing operation — **extrusion** (pipe, film, sheet), **injection moulding**, **blow moulding**, **fibre spinning**, and **3D printing**. Without accurate rheological measurement, predicting how a polymer will behave inside a die, mould, or spinneret is impossible.

## 1.3 Why Measurement (and not just calculation)?

The rheological behaviour of polymers is governed by their **molecular architecture** (chain length, branching, molecular weight distribution, entanglements), and these influences are too complex to predict purely from first principles. Hence, **direct experimental measurement** is the only reliable route for:

- Quality control in polymer production.
- Selection of polymers for specific processing methods.
- Generation of constitutive model parameters (e.g., Cross, Carreau, Power-law).
- Predicting **die swell**, **melt fracture**, and **processability** windows.

---

**Key Standardized Test Methods (commit to memory):**

| Standard | Property Measured |
|---|---|
| **ASTM D1238 / ISO 1133** | Melt Flow Rate (MFR) / Melt Volume Rate (MVR) |
| **ASTM D3835** | Capillary rheometry of thermoplastics |
| **ASTM D4440** | Dynamic mechanical properties by oscillation |
| **ASTM D1708** | Tensile properties of plastics |
| **ISO 11443** | Capillary rheometry of plastics |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Rheological Measurement Techniques

The measurement techniques are classified along two axes: **type of deformation imposed** and **type of response measured**.

### 2.1.1 Steady-Shear (Viscometric) Tests
A constant shear rate γ̇ is imposed and the resulting steady shear stress τ is measured.

| Technique | Geometry | Typical Range of γ̇ (s⁻¹) | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capillary Rheometer** | Long die, circular cross-section | **10⁰ – 10⁶** | Polymer melts, concentrated solutions; *high-shear processing simulation* |
| **Cone-and-Plate Rheometer** | Truncated cone on a flat plate | **10⁻³ – 10³** | Moderate-viscosity polymer melts; normal-stress measurement |
| **Parallel-Plate Rheometer** | Two parallel discs | **10⁻³ – 10³** | Viscoelastic melts; gap-dependent analysis |
| **Concentric-Cylinder (Couette) Rheometer** | Inner cylinder rotating in outer | **10⁻² – 10³** | Low-viscosity polymer solutions, dispersions |
| **Falling-Ball / Falling-Needle Viscometer** | Gravity-driven body in fluid | **Quasi-static** | Dilute polymer solutions |

### 2.1.2 Dynamic (Oscillatory) Tests — Small Amplitude Oscillatory Shear (SAOS)
A small sinusoidal strain γ(t) = γ₀ sin(ωt) is imposed; the resulting stress σ(t) = G′γ₀ sin(ωt) + G″γ₀ cos(ωt) is decomposed into in-phase (elastic) and out-of-phase (viscous) components.

This is the **most powerful method** for characterising viscoelastic polymers in the linear regime.

### 2.1.3 Transient Tests
- **Creep test:** Apply constant stress, monitor strain vs. time.
- **Stress relaxation test:** Apply constant strain, monitor stress vs. time.
- **Start-up of steady shear:** Monitor stress growth upon sudden application of γ̇.

## 2.2 Capillary Rheometer — Operational Logic

The **capillary rheometer** is the workhorse instrument for characterising polymer melts at the high shear rates encountered in industrial processing.

**Operational Steps (in order):**
1. A polymer melt is extruded from a reservoir (barrel) by a piston moving at a controlled velocity through a circular die of known radius **R** and length **L**.
2. The volumetric flow rate **Q** is computed from the piston velocity: $Q = v_p \cdot A_{barrel}$.
3. The pressure drop **ΔP** across the capillary is measured by a pressure transducer mounted just before the die entrance.
4. The shear stress at the wall is calculated assuming fully-developed laminar flow (Poiseuille flow).
5. The apparent shear rate at the wall is calculated assuming Newtonian behaviour.
6. The **Rabinowitsch** and **Bagley** corrections are then applied to obtain the *true* shear rate and *true* shear stress, since polymer melts are non-Newtonian and exhibit entrance effects.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield, Board-Exam Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises every equation you need to confidently solve any Part A or Part B problem on this topic. Internalise it before the exam.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variable Definitions | Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric flow rate** | $Q = v_p A_b$ | $v_p$ = piston velocity; $A_b$ = barrel cross-section | m³/s |
| 2 | **Wall shear stress (Newtonian assumption)** | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across die; $R$ = die radius; $L$ = die length | Pa |
| 3 | **Apparent wall shear rate (Newtonian)** | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | **Apparent viscosity** | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a}$ | ratio of stress to rate | Pa·s |
| 5 | **True (Rabinowitsch-corrected) shear rate** | $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \left( \dfrac{3n' + 1}{4n'} \right)$ | $n' = \dfrac{d \ln \tau_w}{d \ln \dot{\gamma}_a}$ = slope of log-log flow curve | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | **Power-law / Ostwald–de Waele model** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | $K$ = consistency index; $n$ = power-law index | Pa, — |
| 7 | **Bagley correction (entry pressure)** | $\tau_{true} = \dfrac{(\Delta P - P_{entry}) R}{2L}$ | $P_{entry}$ from Bagley plot extrapolation | Pa |
| 8 | **Melt Flow Rate (MFR)** | $\text{MFR} = \dfrac{600 \cdot m}{t}$ | $m$ = mass extruded (g); $t$ = time of extrusion (s) | g / 10 min |
| 9 | **Cone-and-plate shear rate** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\Omega}{\alpha}$ | $\Omega$ = angular velocity; $\alpha$ = cone angle (rad) | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | **Storage modulus (SAOS)** | $G' = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} \cos \delta$ | $\sigma_0$ = stress amplitude; $\gamma_0$ = strain amplitude; $\delta$ = phase angle | Pa |
| 11 | **Loss modulus (SAOS)** | $G'' = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} \sin \delta$ | — | Pa |
| 12 | **Complex viscosity** | $\eta^* = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0 \omega}$ | $\omega$ = angular frequency | Pa·s |
| 13 | **Magnitude of complex viscosity** | $\vert \eta^* \vert = \dfrac{\sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}}{\omega}$ | — | Pa·s |
| 14 | **Loss tangent** | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{G''}{G'}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 15 | **Cox–Merz rule** | $\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \vert \eta^*(\omega) \vert \Big\vert_{\omega = \dot{\gamma}}$ | links steady-shear to oscillatory data | Pa·s |
| 16 | **First normal-stress difference** | $N_1 = \sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}$ | principal stress difference | Pa |
| 17 | **Die swell ratio** | $B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | measure of elastic recovery | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility of these equations:** Equations 2, 3, 5, 6 govern die design in extrusion. Equation 8 is the universal **factory quality-control** test. Equations 10–14 drive molecular-weight and molecular-weight-distribution determination. Equation 15 is used to predict processing behaviour from laboratory oscillatory data. Equation 17 controls final-product dimensional accuracy (e.g., in cable insulation, film blowing).

## 2.4 Why These Specific Corrections?

- The **Newtonian assumption** fails for polymers because the velocity profile in the die is *blunter* than the parabolic Poiseuille profile. The Rabinowitsch correction accounts for this by introducing the local slope $n'$ of the log-log flow curve.
- The **Bagley correction** accounts for the extra pressure drop at the die entrance (converging flow, elastic stretching of chains, and viscous dissipation in the entry region). Without it, the calculated wall stress is artificially high.
- These two corrections together yield the **true flow curve** $\eta(\dot{\gamma}_{true})$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}_{true}$, which is the fundamental material function of the polymer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. KTU board examiners reward students who write the logic of each transition, not just the final expression.

## 3.1 Derivation — Wall Shear Rate in a Capillary (Newtonian)

**Assumption:** Steady, laminar, isothermal, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid in a fully developed circular tube of radius $R$.

**Starting point — Navier–Stokes equation in the $z$-direction (cylindrical coordinates):**

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \eta \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right]
$$

where $\eta$ is the (constant) viscosity and $v_z(r)$ is the axial velocity.

**Step 1 — Re-arrange.** Because the flow is fully developed, $\partial P/\partial z$ is constant. Let $\partial P/\partial z = -\Delta P / L$ (negative because pressure decreases in the flow direction).

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \eta \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once with respect to $r$.** Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate:

$$
\int \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} r \, dr = \eta \int \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) dr
$$

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2} = \eta \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) + C_1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the symmetry boundary condition.** At the centreline ($r = 0$), the velocity gradient must be zero (no-slip at the wall is *not* used here; rather, symmetry means $dv_z/dr = 0$ at $r=0$).

This forces $C_1 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Solve for the velocity gradient.**

$$
\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} r
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate a second time to obtain the velocity profile.**

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} r^2 + C_2
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition at the wall.** $v_z = 0$ at $r = R$ gives $C_2 = -\dfrac{1}{4\eta} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} R^2$.

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \left( r^2 - R^2 \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the volumetric flow rate** $Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_0^R \frac{1}{4\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \left( r^2 - R^2 \right) 2\pi r \, dr \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{\pi}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \int_0^R \left( r^3 - R^2 r \right) dr \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{\pi}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \left[ \frac{R^4}{4} - \frac{R^4}{2} \right] \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{\pi}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \left( -\frac{R^4}{4} \right) \\[6pt]
  &= -\frac{\pi R^4}{8\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $\partial P/\partial z = -\Delta P / L$** to obtain the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}
$$

**Step 9 — Identify the wall shear stress.** A force balance on a cylindrical element of the fluid gives:

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}
$$

**Step 10 — Identify the wall shear rate for a Newtonian fluid.** From the velocity gradient at the wall:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \left. -\frac{dv_z}{dr} \right|_{r=R} = -\frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} R = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 \eta L}
$$

**Step 11 — Obtain the shear rate in terms of $Q$.** Eliminating $\Delta P$ between the two equations:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^3}
$$

This is the famous **Newtonian wall-shear-rate formula** for a capillary — your starting point for every apparent-viscosity calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Rabinowitsch Correction

**Motivation:** For a non-Newtonian fluid, the velocity profile inside the capillary is *not* parabolic. Therefore, Eq. 11 above underestimates the true wall shear rate.

**Starting point — general non-Newtonian flow.** For any generalised Newtonian fluid, the shear stress at the wall is still given by the force balance:

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}
$$

This is **independent** of the fluid model — it is a pure force balance, not a constitutive assumption. Good.

**Definition of true wall shear rate.** The true wall shear rate is the local velocity gradient at the wall:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \left. -\frac{dv_z}{dr} \right|_{r=R}
$$

**Step 1 — Express $Q$ in terms of the velocity profile.**

$$
Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $Q$ with respect to $R$ using Leibniz's rule.** This is the trick that avoids needing the full velocity profile.

For a fluid whose shear stress at the wall is $\tau_w(R) = \Delta P R / (2L)$:

$$
\frac{dQ}{dR} = 2\pi R \cdot v_z(R) + \int_0^R 2\pi r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial R} dr
$$

But $v_z(R) = 0$ (no-slip), and through a careful manipulation (see e.g., standard texts on transport phenomena) one can show:

$$
\frac{d(\ln Q)}{d(\ln \tau_w)} = n' = \frac{d \ln \tau_w}{d \ln \dot{\gamma}_a}
$$

**Step 3 — The Mooney–Rabinowitsch result** is obtained:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \left( \frac{3 n' + 1}{4 n'} \right)
$$

where $n'$ is the local slope of the apparent flow curve on a log–log plot of $\tau_w$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}_a$.

**Step 4 — Limiting cases (sanity check).**

- For a Newtonian fluid: $\tau_w \propto \dot{\gamma}_a$, so $n' = 1$. Then $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot (3+1)/4 = \dot{\gamma}_a$. ✓
- For a strongly shear-thinning fluid: $n' \to 0.3$, so $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = 1.83 \, \dot{\gamma}_a$ (the true rate is higher than the apparent rate).

This correction is **mandatory** when reporting rheological data for polymer melts in any publication or technical report.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Melt Flow Rate

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU-style 7-mark sub-question. Work through it.

**Problem:** A polymer is tested in a melt flow indexer (ASTM D1238) at 190 °C with a 2.16 kg load. In 60 seconds, 1.25 g of polymer is extruded through the die. Calculate the **Melt Flow Rate (MFR)** in g/10 min.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.**
- Mass extruded: $m = 1.25$ g
- Time of extrusion: $t = 60$ s

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of MFR.** MFR is defined as the mass (in grams) of polymer extruded in 10 minutes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MFR} &= \frac{m \times 600}{t} \\[4pt]
           &= \frac{1.25 \, \text{g} \times 600 \, \text{s}}{60 \, \text{s}} \\[4pt]
           &= 12.5 \, \text{g} / 10 \, \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.** A MFR of 12.5 g/10 min indicates a **relatively low-viscosity** polymer (e.g., suitable for injection moulding). High-MFR polymers (low molecular weight) flow easily; low-MFR polymers (high molecular weight) are tougher but harder to process.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Capillary Rheometer

**Problem:** A polymer melt is extruded through a capillary rheometer with $R = 1.0$ mm and $L = 40$ mm. The pressure drop is $\Delta P = 4.5$ MPa. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 8.0 \times 10^{-8}$ m³/s. Calculate the apparent shear rate, apparent wall shear stress, and apparent viscosity. The slope of the log-log flow curve is $n' = 0.45$.

**Step 1 — Apparent wall shear stress.**

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.625 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apparent wall shear rate.**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^3} = \frac{4 \cdot 8.0 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi (10^{-3})^3} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 10^{-9}} = 1.019 \times 10^2 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Apparent viscosity.**

$$
\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{5.625 \times 10^4}{1.019 \times 10^2} = 5.52 \times 10^2 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Step 4 — Rabinowitsch correction.**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \left( \frac{3 n' + 1}{4 n'} \right) = 101.9 \times \left( \frac{3(0.45) + 1}{4(0.45)} \right) = 101.9 \times \frac{2.35}{1.80} = 133.0 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — True viscosity.**

$$
\eta_{true} = \frac{5.625 \times 10^4}{133.0} = 423 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The true viscosity is about 23 % lower than the apparent viscosity — a non-trivial correction that must be applied for publication-quality data.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Physical drawings of rheometer geometries are rendered as **block-level functional architecture flows** to comply with the Mermaid-safe compiler requirements.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Capillary Rheometer Measurement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer melt in barrel] --> B[Piston applies controlled force F]
    B --> C[Piston descends at velocity v_p]
    C --> D[Volumetric flow rate Q computed]
    D --> E[Polymer flows through circular die of radius R and length L]
    E --> F[Pressure transducer measures delta P]
    F --> G[Compute apparent wall shear stress tau_w]
    F --> H[Compute apparent wall shear rate gamma dot a]
    G --> I[Compute apparent viscosity eta a]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Log-log slope n' known?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Apply Rabinowitsch correction]
    K --> L[Compute true viscosity eta true vs gamma dot true]
    L --> M[Generate true flow curve]
    J -- No --> M
    M --> N[Plot in specialised software e.g. origin]
    N --> O[Output Master Curve and Cross Carreau model parameters]
    O --> P[Feed into process simulation e.g. POLYFLOW]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Cone-and-Plate Rotational Rheometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer sample placed on lower plate] --> B[Lower plate held at controlled temperature T]
    B --> C[Upper cone lowered to fixed gap h]
    C --> D[Motor applies controlled angular velocity Omega or torque M]
    D --> E{Measurement mode}
    E -- Steady shear --> F[Measure steady torque M]
    E -- Oscillatory --> G[Measure complex torque amplitude and phase delta]
    F --> H[Compute shear stress tau and shear rate gamma dot from cone geometry]
    G --> I[Compute storage modulus G prime and loss modulus G double prime]
    H --> J[Plot viscosity vs shear rate]
    I --> K[Plot G prime G double prime and tan delta vs frequency omega]
    J --> L[Determine zero-shear viscosity and relaxation spectrum]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Cross over point analysis to identify gel point and molecular weight distribution]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Master Decision Map for Selecting a Measurement Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: polymer sample available] --> Q1{State of polymer?}
    Q1 -- Molten --> Q2{Viscosity range?}
    Q1 -- Solution --> R1[Concentric cylinder or falling ball]
    Q1 -- Solid --> R2[Dynamic mechanical analyser DMA in torsion bending]
    Q2 -- Low to moderate --> R3[Cone and plate rheometer]
    Q2 -- High or processing rate --> R4[Capillary rheometer]
    R3 --> S1{High shear rate needed?}
    R4 --> S1
    S1 -- Yes up to 10^6 --> R5[Capillary rheometer]
    S1 -- No --> S2[Rotational rheometer]
    R5 --> T1[Measure shear viscosity and die swell]
    S2 --> T2[Measure G prime G double prime and N1]
    R1 --> T3[Measure intrinsic viscosity and molecular weight]
    R2 --> T4[Measure glass transition and storage modulus]
    T1 --> OUT[Engineering data for processing]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT2[Molecular characterisation data]
    T4 --> OUT2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Rabinowitsch correction** when reporting the true wall shear rate of a non-Newtonian fluid. *Always* state the value of $n'$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing MFI and MFR.** MFI is a single-point viscosity index; MFR is the *rate* (in g/10 min). The ASTM D1238 procedure may quote both.
> 3. **Mixing up $G'$ and $G''$.** $G'$ is the **storage** (elastic) modulus — energy stored and recovered. $G''$ is the **loss** (viscous) modulus — energy dissipated as heat. The mnemonic: **$G'$ ↔ Elastic (prime = real part of $G^*$)**.
> 4. **Using $\eta_a$ instead of $\eta_{true}$** in any calculation that is meant to be used for engineering design. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> 5. **Ignoring the phase angle $\delta$** in oscillatory data — many students forget that $\delta = 0°$ means perfectly elastic and $\delta = 90°$ means perfectly viscous.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert units.** Pressure in MPa must be converted to Pa; radii in mm to m. This single oversight costs 1–2 marks per question.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]**
Define the **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** of a polymer. State the standard testing conditions for polyethylene and polypropylene as per ASTM D1238.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **MFI (Melt Flow Index)** is defined as the mass of polymer (in grams) extruded in 10 minutes through a standard capillary die of fixed dimensions under a specified load and temperature, as per **ASTM D1238**.

Standard test conditions:

| Polymer | Temperature | Load |
|---|---|---|
| **Polyethylene (PE)** | 190 °C | 2.16 kg |
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | 230 °C | 2.16 kg |

MFI is widely used as a **quality control parameter** and an indirect indicator of molecular weight: a higher MFI corresponds to a lower molecular weight (lower viscosity) and easier processability.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for at least one polymer's standard conditions, 1 mark for one application/interpretation.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between the **storage modulus ($G'$)** and the **loss modulus ($G''$)** of a polymer. What is the significance of the **crossover point** ($G' = G''$)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Storage modulus $G'$:** Represents the **elastic (solid-like)** response of the polymer. It is the in-phase component of the complex modulus and quantifies the energy stored per cycle of oscillation. Unit: Pa.
- **Loss modulus $G''$:** Represents the **viscous (liquid-like)** response. It is the out-of-phase component and quantifies the energy dissipated as heat per cycle. Unit: Pa.
- **Crossover point ($G' = G''$):** This is the frequency at which the polymer transitions from a predominantly elastic to a predominantly viscous response. It is used to identify:
  - The **gel point** during crosslinking/curing.
  - The longest polymer relaxation time $\tau = 1/\omega_{crossover}$.
  - A characteristic molecular relaxation time scale.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Capillary Rheometry and Rabinowitsch Correction

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Adapted from past paper pattern]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **wall shear rate** in a capillary rheometer for a **Newtonian fluid**. State clearly the assumptions made.

(b) **[7 marks]** A polymer melt is tested in a capillary rheometer with a die radius of **1.5 mm** and die length of **30 mm**. The pressure drop across the die is **3.6 MPa** and the volumetric flow rate is **$2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s**. The local slope of the log-log flow curve is **$n' = 0.40$**.

Compute:
- (i) The apparent wall shear stress and apparent wall shear rate.
- (ii) The true wall shear rate after applying the Rabinowitsch correction.
- (iii) The apparent and true viscosities.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]**

The complete step-by-step derivation is given in **Section 3.1** above. For the answer script, the student should present the following condensed logic:

1. State the assumptions: **steady, laminar, isothermal, incompressible, fully-developed** flow in a horizontal tube; **Newtonian** behaviour; **no-slip** at the wall; **symmetry** at the centreline.
2. Write the **Navier–Stokes equation** in the $z$-direction for a horizontal tube (no gravity component along $z$):

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \eta \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right]
$$

3. Integrate twice using the boundary conditions $v_z(0) = \text{finite}$ and $v_z(R) = 0$ to obtain the **parabolic velocity profile**:

$$
v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)
$$

4. Integrate the velocity profile over the cross-section to obtain the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}
$$

5. Differentiate $v_z$ at $r = R$ to obtain the **wall shear rate**:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^3}
$$

*(1 mark for each of the 5 steps; examiner's key suggests 1 additional mark for clearly stating all assumptions.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given:** $R = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $L = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $\Delta P = 3.6 \times 10^6$ Pa; $Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s; $n' = 0.40$.

**(i) Apparent shear stress and shear rate [3 marks]**

Apparent wall shear stress:

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{5400}{0.060} = 9.0 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}
$$

Apparent wall shear rate:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^3} = \frac{4 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^3} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \cdot 3.375 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0603 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 94.3 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**(ii) True shear rate (Rabinowitsch) [2 marks]**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \left( \frac{3 n' + 1}{4 n'} \right) = 94.3 \times \left( \frac{3 \cdot 0.40 + 1}{4 \cdot 0.40} \right) = 94.3 \times \frac{2.20}{1.60} = 129.7 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**(iii) Apparent and true viscosities [2 marks]**

Apparent viscosity:

$$
\eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^4}{94.3} = 954 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

True viscosity:

$$
\eta_{true} = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_{true}} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^4}{129.7} = 694 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The Rabinowitsch-corrected (true) viscosity is approximately **27 % lower** than the apparent viscosity, reflecting the highly non-Newtonian, shear-thinning character of the polymer melt.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Oscillatory Rheometry and Viscoelastic Characterisation

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted from past paper pattern]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Explain the principle of **small-amplitude oscillatory shear (SAOS)** testing. Derive the expressions for the **storage modulus ($G'$)**, **loss modulus ($G''$)**, and **complex viscosity ($\eta^*$)** in terms of the stress amplitude, strain amplitude, phase angle, and angular frequency.

(b) **[7 marks]** A polymer melt is tested in oscillatory shear at 200 °C. At a frequency of $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s, the measured stress amplitude is $\sigma_0 = 1.5 \times 10^3$ Pa for an imposed strain amplitude of $\gamma_0 = 0.05$. The phase angle is $\delta = 35°$. Calculate $G'$, $G''$, $\tan \delta$, and $\vert \eta^* \vert$. Comment on whether the polymer behaves more like an elastic solid or a viscous liquid at this frequency.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — SAOS Principle and Derivations [7 marks]**

**Principle [2 marks]:** In SAOS, a sinusoidal strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ of *small* amplitude is imposed on the polymer (small enough to remain in the **linear viscoelastic regime**). The resulting stress response is also sinusoidal but lags by a phase angle $\delta$:

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)
$$

Expanding this and decomposing into in-phase and out-of-phase components:

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cos \delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) + \sigma_0 \sin \delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Definition of $G'$ [1 mark]:** The storage modulus is the **in-phase** stress-to-strain ratio — it represents elastic energy storage:

$$
G' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} \cos \delta
$$

**Definition of $G''$ [1 mark]:** The loss modulus is the **out-of-phase** stress-to-strain ratio — it represents viscous energy dissipation:

$$
G'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} \sin \delta
$$

**Definition of complex viscosity [2 marks]:** The complex modulus is $G^* = G' + iG''$. The complex viscosity is defined as:

$$
\eta^* = \frac{G^*}{i\omega} = \frac{G''}{\omega} - i \frac{G'}{\omega}
$$

The magnitude of the complex viscosity is:

$$
\vert \eta^* \vert = \frac{\sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}}{\omega}
$$

**Significance [1 mark]:** SAOS is the *gold-standard* linear viscoelastic characterisation. It probes the material structure over many decades of frequency (and hence, time) without destroying the sample and without leaving the linear regime. Master curves are constructed by **time–temperature superposition (TTS)**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given:** $\sigma_0 = 1.5 \times 10^3$ Pa; $\gamma_0 = 0.05$; $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s; $\delta = 35°$.

**Step 1 — Compute the modulus amplitude.**

$$
G^*_{mag} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\gamma_0} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^3}{0.05} = 3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Storage modulus $G'$ [2 marks].**

$$
G' = G^*_{mag} \cos \delta = 3.0 \times 10^4 \cdot \cos(35°) = 3.0 \times 10^4 \cdot 0.8192 = 2.46 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Loss modulus $G''$ [2 marks].**

$$
G'' = G^*_{mag} \sin \delta = 3.0 \times 10^4 \cdot \sin(35°) = 3.0 \times 10^4 \cdot 0.5736 = 1.72 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Loss tangent [1 mark].**

$$
\tan \delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{1.72 \times 10^4}{2.46 \times 10^4} = 0.70
$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude of complex viscosity [1 mark].**

$$
\vert \eta^* \vert = \frac{\sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}}{\omega} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^4}{1.0} = 3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation [1 mark].** Since $G' > G''$ (i.e., $G' \approx 1.43 \cdot G''$) and $\tan \delta = 0.70 < 1$, the polymer at $\omega = 1$ rad/s and 200 °C behaves **more like a viscoelastic solid** (elastically dominated) than a viscous liquid. However, the relatively high $\tan \delta$ shows that the viscous dissipation is significant — the polymer is on the verge of the terminal flow region.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision — read this out loud twice before the exam.**

**Core Concepts:**
- Rheology = study of deformation + flow of viscoelastic materials (polymers).
- Polymers are **viscoelastic** — they store *and* dissipate energy.
- Two broad categories of measurement: **steady-shear (viscometric)** and **dynamic (oscillatory)**.

**Capillary Rheometer — must-know equations:**
- Apparent wall shear stress: $\tau_w = \Delta P \cdot R / (2L)$
- Apparent wall shear rate: $\dot{\gamma}_a = 4Q / (\pi R^3)$
- Apparent viscosity: $\eta_a = \tau_w / \dot{\gamma}_a$
- Rabinowitsch correction: $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot (3n' + 1)/(4n')$
- Best for **high shear rates** ($10^0$ to $10^6$ s⁻¹) — relevant to processing.

**Rotational Rheometers:**
- Cone-and-plate: uniform shear rate; best for moderate viscosity melts; measures $N_1$.
- Parallel-plate: gap-dependent shear rate; useful for suspensions and structured fluids.
- Couette: best for low-viscosity solutions.

**Oscillatory (SAOS) — must-know equations:**
- Storage modulus: $G' = (\sigma_0/\gamma_0) \cos \delta$
- Loss modulus: $G'' = (\sigma_0/\gamma_0) \sin \delta$
- Complex viscosity: $\vert \eta^* \vert = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}/\omega$
- Loss tangent: $\tan \delta = G''/G'$
- $G' > G''$ → elastic solid-like; $G'' > G'$ → viscous liquid-like.

**Melt Flow Index (MFI/MFR):**
- Standard test: ASTM D1238.
- MFR = (600 · m) / t, in g/10 min.
- Higher MFR → lower molecular weight → easier processing.
- 190 °C / 2.16 kg for PE; 230 °C / 2.16 kg for PP.

**Engineering / Processing Links:**
- Extrusion design → capillary rheometer data.
- Mould filling simulation → Cross/Carreau model fit to $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$.
- Wire-coating, film-blowing → die swell ratio $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$.
- Product performance → $G'(\omega)$ and $\tan \delta(\omega)$ master curves (TTS).

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- Forgetting to convert mm to m, MPa to Pa.
- Confusing $G'$ (storage, elastic) with $G''$ (loss, viscous).
- Reporting apparent viscosity as true viscosity without Rabinowitsch correction.
- Using Newtonian formulas for non-Newtonian polymer melts without verification.
- Mixing up phase angle conventions (some texts use $e^{i\omega t}$, others $e^{-i\omega t}$).

**Quick-Reference Numerical Sanity Checks:**
- A typical LDPE melt at processing shear rates: $\eta \approx 10^2$–$10^3$ Pa·s.
- A typical PP melt: $\eta \approx 10^3$ Pa·s.
- Die swell of LDPE: $B \approx 1.2$–1.6 (highly elastic).
- Die swell of HDPE: $B \approx 1.05$–1.2 (less elastic).

**Key Terms to Define in Two Sentences (for 1-mark sub-questions):**
1. *Shear-thinning (pseudoplasticity)*
2. *Yield stress*
3. *Die swell / extrudate swell*
4. *Cox–Merz rule*
5. *Time–temperature superposition (TTS)*
6. *Newtonian vs. non-Newtonian fluid*
7. *Linear viscoelastic regime*
8. *Crossover frequency* $\omega_c$

**Mastering these is sufficient for full marks on Module 2's "Measurement of Rheological Properties" section of the KTU 2024 ESE.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Melt Flow Index (MFI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Melt Flow Index (MFI) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** — also called **Melt Mass-Flow Rate (MFR)** — is formally defined as the **mass of polymer melt (in grams)** that is extruded through a **standardized capillary die** of specified dimensions under a **prescribed load (dead weight)** at a **specified temperature** over a **standard time interval of 10 minutes**. The governing standards are: - **ASTM D 1238** (American Standard) - **ISO 1133** (International Standard) $$\text{MFI} \;=\; \frac{m \;(\text{grams})}{t_{\text{norm}} \;(\text{minutes})} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{normalized to 10 min}$$ Where: - $m$ = mass of extrudate cut and weighed (grams) - $t_{\text{norm}}$ = standardized test interval (extrapolated or interpolated to 10 minutes) --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Plain-English Explanation) Imagine a **toothpaste tube**. Squeeze hard → a lot of paste comes out fast. Squeeze gently → slow trickle. Now, instead of your hand, a **standard weight** presses the piston, and instead of a wide opening, the polymer exits through a **tiny nozzle of fixed size**. The amount that comes out in 10 minutes **is** the MFI. - A **runny, low-viscosity polymer** (like water from a tap) → **HIGH MFI** (it pours easily). - A **thick, honey-like polymer** → **LOW MFI** (it resists flowing). > [!NOTE] > Think of MFI as the **"pourability score"** of a polymer melt. Higher MFI = easier to flow = lower molecular weight on average. --- ## 1.3 Standard Test Conditions (Bold Metrics) | Parameter | Standard Specification | |---|---| | **Capillary diameter** | **2.095 ± 0.005 mm** | | **Capillary length** | **8.000 ± 0.025 mm** | | **Test temperature** | **190 °C** (polyethylene), **230 °C** (polypropylene), **300 °C** (polycarbonate/PA) | | **Standard applied load** | **2.16 kg** (sometimes 5 kg, 10 kg, 21.6 kg) | | **Preheating time** | **4–6 minutes** (to ensure complete melting) | | **Cut intervals** | **typically every 30 s to 5 min** | | **Unit of MFI** | **g/10 min** | --- ## 1.4 Classification Note — MFI vs. MFR vs. MVR > [!NOTE] > - **MFI / MFR** → mass-based rate (g/10 min) — *most commonly used in industry.* > - **MVR (Melt Volume Rate)** → volume-based rate (cm³/10 min) — *used when melt density variations matter.* > Conversion: $\text{MFR} = \text{MVR} \times \rho_{\text{melt}}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic) The Melt Flow Indexer (also called an **extrusion plastometer**) works on a controlled capillary-rheometry principle. Here is the underlying logic, broken down for clarity: 1. **Charging** — A precisely weighed quantity of polymer granules (typically 3–8 g) is introduced into a **vertical, electrically heated steel barrel**. 2. **Compaction & Preheating** — A piston is inserted, and the polymer is **held under light contact pressure** for **4–6 minutes** to expel trapped air and ensure uniform melting at the set temperature. 3. **Application of Load** — A **calibrated dead-weight** (typically **2.16 kg**) is placed on the piston, generating a constant downward **hydrostatic pressure** on the molten polymer. 4. **Steady-State Extrusion** — The molten polymer is forced through the **standard capillary die** at the bottom. After the preheating period, the operator begins **cutting extrudate strands** at timed intervals using a cutting blade. 5. **Weighing** — Each cut strand is **weighed accurately (to 0.0001 g)** after cooling. 6. **Calculation** — The average mass per cut interval is extrapolated or normalized to **10 minutes** to obtain MFI. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **"Why" behind the test**: MFI is essentially a **single-point viscosity measurement at very low shear rate**. It is **not** a fundamental rheological property (it does not give viscosity vs. shear rate), but it is an **incredibly fast, cheap, and reliable** quality-control proxy for **average molecular weight** and **processability**. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Unit / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{m \times 600}{t}$ | Mass-time conversion to 10-min basis | g/10 min | | 2 | $\text{MFI} \propto \dfrac{1}{\eta}$ | MFI is **inversely** proportional to melt viscosity | g/10 min | | 3 | $\text{MFI} \propto \dfrac{1}{\bar{M}_w^{\,n}}$ where $n \approx 3.4$ | Empirical tie with weight-average MW (for polyolefins) | — | | 4 | $\text{FRR} = \dfrac{\text{MFI}_{21.6}}{\text{MFI}_{2.16}}$ | Flow Rate Ratio → indicates **MWD** | dimensionless | | 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{F \cdot r}{2 \pi R^2 L}$ | Wall shear stress in capillary (from load) | Pa | | 6 | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi r^3}$ | Apparent wall shear rate | s⁻¹ | | 7 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w}$ | Apparent viscosity | Pa·s | | 8 | $\text{MVR} \times \rho_{\text{melt}} = \text{MFR}$ | Mass–volume conversion | g/10 min | | 9 | $\eta_0 = K \cdot \bar{M}_w^{3.4}$ | Zero-shear viscosity vs. MW (3.4-power law) | Pa·s | | 10 | Higher load → higher MFI | Shear-thinning confirmation | — | > [!NOTE] > **Critical exam pitfall:** MFI is measured at a **single, very low shear rate (~1–10 s⁻¹)**. It is therefore **not equal to viscosity at processing shear rates** (~100–10 000 s⁻¹). Use the **Cox-Merz rule** to relate if needed. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Quality control** in polymer manufacturing (batch-to-batch consistency). - **Incoming raw-material inspection** for injection-moulding/film-extrusion plants. - **Selecting processing conditions** — high-MFI grades are easier to mould (lower pressure, faster cycles) but have lower toughness; low-MFI grades are tougher but harder to process. - **Predicting molecular weight** indirectly — used in R&D and process development. - **Specifying grades** for end-use applications (e.g., blow-moulding HDPE has low MFI; injection-moulding PP has high MFI). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion from Cut Mass to MFI (g/10 min) **Problem context:** A polymer strand of mass $m$ grams is collected over a time interval $t$ seconds. Express MFI in g/10 min. **Step 1 — Identify the proportionality.** By definition, MFI is the mass extruded in **600 seconds (10 minutes)**: $$\text{MFI} \;\propto\; \frac{m}{t}$$ **Step 2 — Set up the dimensional scale.** We want the equivalent mass for a duration of 600 s. Using simple proportion: $$\frac{\text{MFI}}{600 \; \text{s}} \;=\; \frac{m}{t \; \text{s}}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange for MFI.** Multiplying both sides by 600 s: $$\boxed{\text{MFI} \;=\; \frac{m \times 600}{t}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical illustration.** If $m = 0.35$ g of extrudate is collected in $t = 60$ s: $$\text{MFI} = \frac{0.35 \times 600}{60} = 3.50 \; \text{g/10 min}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shear Stress at the Capillary Wall **Problem context:** Show that the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ generated by a load $F$ applied on a piston of barrel radius $R$ and capillary length $L$ is given by: $$\tau_w = \frac{F \cdot r}{2 \pi R^2 L}$$ **Step 1 — Pressure at piston.** The load $F$ is applied on the piston of cross-sectional area $A_p = \pi R^2$. The pressure at the top of the melt column is: $$P_0 = \frac{F}{\pi R^2}$$ **Step 2 — Pressure drop across the capillary.** For an incompressible melt in a long capillary, the pressure at the capillary exit equals atmospheric ($P_{\text{atm}}$). Therefore, the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the capillary length $L$ is: $$\Delta P = P_0 - P_{\text{atm}} = \frac{F}{\pi R^2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Hagen–Poiseuille capillary flow relation.** For fully developed laminar flow in a circular tube, the wall shear stress is related to the pressure drop by: $$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$ where $r$ is the capillary radius. **Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta P$.** $$\tau_w = \frac{\left(\dfrac{F}{\pi R^2}\right) \cdot r}{2 L} = \frac{F \cdot r}{2 \pi R^2 L}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{F \, r}{2 \pi R^2 L}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical check for a standard test.** For $F = 2.16 \times 9.81 = 21.195$ N, $R = 4.75$ mm (barrel), $r = 1.0475$ mm (capillary), $L = 8$ mm: $$\tau_w = \frac{21.195 \times 1.0475 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \pi (4.75 \times 10^{-3})^2 (8 \times 10^{-3})}$$ Numerically: $$\tau_w = \frac{0.02221}{2 \pi \times 1.125 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 31.43 \; \text{kPa}$$ This is a **low shear stress** — confirming MFI is a **low-shear test**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Apparent Wall Shear Rate For a Newtonian fluid in a capillary, the wall shear rate is: $$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4 Q}{\pi r^3}$$ where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate ($Q = \text{MVR}/600$ cm³/s). For non-Newtonian polymer melts, the **Rabinowitsch correction** is applied: $$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{true}} = \frac{(3n+1)}{4n} \cdot \dot{\gamma}_w$$ where $n$ is the power-law index. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How MFI Varies with Polymer Property | Polymer Property | Effect on MFI | Engineering Consequence | |---|---|---| | ↑ Molecular weight ($M_w$) | MFI decreases sharply (∝ $1/M_w^{3.4}$) | Higher toughness, harder processing | | ↑ Molecular weight distribution (MWD) | FRR = $M_{21.6}/M_{2.16}$ increases | Better melt strength, easier extrusion | | ↑ Branching | Generally raises MFI (reduces entanglements) | Lower density, better transparency | | ↑ Temperature | MFI rises exponentially (Arrhenius) | Lower viscosity, easier moulding | | ↑ Applied Load | MFI rises linearly (shear-thinning) | Used for FRR determination | | ↑ Crosslinking | MFI decreases / material stops flowing | Thermoset behaviour | | ↑ Plasticizer content | MFI increases | Lower $T_g$, more flexible | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Melt Flow Indexer (Extrusion Plastometer) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Weighed polymer granules
3 to 8 g] --> B[Charging into heated steel barrel
T set at 190 C or 230 C] B --> C[Insert piston
Preheating 4 to 6 min
expel trapped air] C --> D[Apply calibrated dead weight
2.16 kg or 5 kg or 21.6 kg] D --> E[Steady melt flow
under constant hydrostatic pressure] E --> F[Extrusion through capillary die
d 2.095 mm, L 8 mm] F --> G[Cut extrudate strands
at fixed time intervals
e.g. every 30 s] G --> H[Weigh each cut strand
analytical balance 0.0001 g] H --> I[Calculate MFI
MFI equals m times 600 over t
unit g per 10 min] I --> J{Quality control decision} J -->|Within spec| K[Accept batch
release to production] J -->|Out of spec| L[Reject batch
rework or scrap] style A fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e style F fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f style I fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d style J fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843 ``` --- ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of the Test Barrel (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BARREL [INSULATED HEATED BARREL] P1[Dead weight 2.16 kg
acts on piston] --> P2[Steel piston
transmits load] P2 --> P3[Molten polymer column
at test temperature T] P3 --> P4[Capillary die
d equals 2.095 mm
L equals 8 mm] P4 --> P5[Extrudate strand
cut and weighed] P3 -.->|thermocouple| P6[Temperature controller
plus minus 0.1 C] end style P1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e style P4 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d style P5 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Interpreting MFI Results ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Measure MFI
g per 10 min] --> B{Compare to
specification range} B -->|Within range| C[Grade is acceptable
record on QC sheet] B -->|Below spec| D[High viscosity
Possible high MW
Check processing settings] B -->|Above spec| E[Low viscosity
Possible degradation
or low MW] D --> F[Increase melt temp
or injection pressure] E --> G[Reduce melt temp
or check for contamination] style B fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e style C fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534 style D fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b style E fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Melt Flow Index. State the standard conditions used for the test.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** MFI is defined as the mass of polymer (in grams) extruded through a standard capillary die under a prescribed load at a specified temperature in 10 minutes. - **Standard capillary (1 mark):** Diameter = 2.095 mm, Length = 8 mm. - **Standard conditions (1 mark):** For polyethylene T = 190 °C; load = 2.16 kg; preheating = 4–6 min. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **How is MFI related to (a) molecular weight, and (b) viscosity of a polymer melt?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(a) Molecular weight (1.5 marks):** MFI is inversely proportional to the weight-average molecular weight raised to a power of approximately 3.4 (for polyolefins): $$\text{MFI} \propto \frac{1}{\bar{M}_w^{\,3.4}}$$ Therefore, **higher MFI → lower molecular weight**. - **(b) Viscosity (1.5 marks):** MFI is inversely proportional to the melt viscosity. A more viscous (thicker) melt flows slowly and gives a **lower MFI**: $$\text{MFI} \propto \frac{1}{\eta}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation**, Part B Module-2 questions carry an internal choice. The examiner sets **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are shown below for practice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]* #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a Melt Flow Indexer (extrusion plastometer) used for measuring MFI of thermoplastics. State ASTM/ISO standard conditions.** **Model Solution:** 1. **Construction (3 marks)** - **Heated barrel:** Vertical cylindrical steel barrel with temperature controller (accuracy ± 0.1 °C), thermocouple feedback. - **Piston:** Steel piston with a low-friction fit, on which calibrated dead weights are placed. - **Capillary die:** Replaceable, made of hardened steel; diameter 2.095 mm, length 8 mm. - **Cutting blade & receiving platform:** For timed collection of extrudate. - **Analytical balance:** Reads to 0.0001 g for weighing cut strands. 2. **Working (3 marks)** - Charge weighed granules (3–8 g) into the preheated barrel. - Insert piston and preheat for 4–6 minutes. - Place standard load (2.16 kg) → constant downward pressure. - Cut extrudate at timed intervals (typically 30 s). - Weigh each cut, average, and normalize to 10 min using: $$\text{MFI} = \frac{m \times 600}{t}$$ - Record MFI in g/10 min. 3. **Standards (1 mark):** ASTM D 1238 / ISO 1133. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **For a polypropylene sample at 230 °C under a 2.16 kg load, an extrudate mass of 0.42 g is collected in 30 seconds. Calculate the MFI. If the same sample yields 1.85 g in 30 s under a 21.6 kg load, determine the Flow Rate Ratio (FRR) and comment on the molecular weight distribution.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — MFI at 2.16 kg load (3 marks):** $$\text{MFI}_{2.16} = \frac{m \times 600}{t} = \frac{0.42 \times 600}{30} = \frac{252}{30} = 8.40 \; \text{g/10 min}$$ **Step 2 — MFI at 21.6 kg load (2 marks):** $$\text{MFI}_{21.6} = \frac{1.85 \times 600}{30} = \frac{1110}{30} = 37.00 \; \text{g/10 min}$$ **Step 3 — Flow Rate Ratio (1 mark):** $$\text{FRR} = \frac{\text{MFI}_{21.6}}{\text{MFI}_{2.16}} = \frac{37.00}{8.40} \approx 4.40$$ **Step 4 — Comment on MWD (1 mark):** Since FRR > 3, the polymer has a **broad molecular weight distribution (high polydispersity)**. Such grades show **pronounced shear-thinning** and are well suited for processes like **blow-moulding and film extrusion** where good melt strength is needed. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]* #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the significance of Melt Flow Index as a quality-control parameter in polymer processing. Discuss how MFI varies with molecular weight, temperature, and applied load.** **Model Solution:** 1. **Significance in QC (2 marks):** - Single-number proxy for **average molecular weight** and **processability**. - Used to **accept/reject batches** before moulding; out-of-spec MFI → defective parts. - Helps **select correct grade** for a given process (injection, blow, extrusion, thermoforming). 2. **Variation with Molecular Weight (2 marks):** $$\text{MFI} \propto \frac{1}{\bar{M}_w^{\,3.4}}$$ A **doubling** of $M_w$ reduces MFI by a factor of $\approx 2^{3.4} \approx 10.6$. Hence small MW variations produce large MFI shifts — making MFI a sensitive QC indicator. 3. **Variation with Temperature (1.5 marks):** MFI follows an **Arrhenius-type** behaviour with temperature: $$\text{MFI} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$ Raising T lowers viscosity, so MFI increases sharply. 4. **Variation with Load (1.5 marks):** Increasing the applied load raises the shear stress and shear rate. Because polymer melts are **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**, MFI rises **super-linearly** with load. This is exploited in the FRR test for MWD characterization. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A polyethylene grade tested at 190 °C under 2.16 kg gives an extrudate of 0.20 g in 60 s. (i) Calculate its MFI. (ii) If the melt density is 0.78 g/cm³, calculate the MVR in cm³/10 min. (iii) Comment on whether this grade is suitable for injection moulding or blow moulding.** **Model Solution:** **(i) MFI calculation (2.5 marks):** $$\text{MFI} = \frac{0.20 \times 600}{60} = \frac{120}{60} = 2.0 \; \text{g/10 min}$$ **(ii) MVR calculation (2.5 marks):** $$\text{MVR} = \frac{\text{MFR}}{\rho_{\text{melt}}} = \frac{2.0}{0.78} \approx 2.56 \; \text{cm}^3/10 \; \text{min}$$ **(iii) Application suitability (2 marks):** - MFI = 2.0 g/10 min is in the **typical blow-moulding HDPE range** (MFI ≈ 0.3–2.5). - For injection moulding, MFI should typically be **> 10 g/10 min** for easy filling. - **Conclusion:** This grade is **better suited for blow moulding** (containers, drums) where higher melt strength is needed, **not** for thin-walled injection moulding. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in MFI questions:** > 1. **Unit confusion (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert seconds → minutes, or cut interval → 10-min basis. Always write the formula explicitly: > $$\text{MFI} = \frac{m \times 600}{t}$$ > 2. **Stating MFI without units (–0.5 mark):** Always specify **g/10 min**. > 3. **Confusing MFI with viscosity (–1 mark):** MFI is **not** viscosity. It is the *inverse* of viscosity (empirically). Do not equate the two. > 4. **Wrong standard conditions (–1 mark):** Different polymers use different test temperatures (PE → 190 °C; PP → 230 °C; PC → 300 °C). > 5. **Ignoring preheating time (–0.5 mark):** The 4–6 min preheat is critical; cutting before steady-state is reached yields incorrect (lower) values. > 6. **FRR confusion (–1 mark):** FRR uses the **ratio of MFI at two different loads**, not two different temperatures. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **MFI definition:** Mass (g) of polymer extruded through a 2.095 mm × 8 mm capillary under a specified load and temperature in **10 minutes**. Unit: **g/10 min**. - ✅ **Governing standards:** **ASTM D 1238** and **ISO 1133**. - ✅ **Standard load:** **2.16 kg** (also used: 5 kg, 10 kg, 21.6 kg). - ✅ **Standard temperatures:** **190 °C** (PE), **230 °C** (PP), **300 °C** (PC, PA). - ✅ **Conversion formula:** $\text{MFI} = \dfrac{m \times 600}{t}$, where $t$ is in seconds. - ✅ **MFI inversely related to viscosity** and to $M_w^{3.4}$ — high MFI = low viscosity = low $M_w$. - ✅ **FRR (Flow Rate Ratio):** $\text{MFI}_{21.6}/\text{MFI}_{2.16}$ — indicates **molecular weight distribution (MWD)**. Higher FRR → broader MWD. - ✅ **MVR × ρ_melt = MFR** — for volume-vs-mass conversions. - ✅ **MFI is a low-shear test** (~1–10 s⁻¹) — does **not** represent processing-shear viscosity; Cox-Merz rule must be applied if conversion is needed. - ✅ **Wall shear stress in capillary:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{F \, r}{2 \pi R^2 L}$. - ✅ **Apparent wall shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi r^3}$; use **Rabinowitsch correction** for non-Newtonian melts. - ✅ **Higher temperature → exponentially higher MFI** (Arrhenius dependence). - ✅ **Application tip:** Low-MFI grades → blow moulding/extrusion (tough parts); high-MFI grades → injection moulding, thin-wall parts, nonwovens. - ✅ **Pitfall to avoid:** Never equate MFI with viscosity; never skip the 4–6 min preheat; always convert cut time to 10-min basis.

topics.title: Capillary Rheometers. Estimation of Tensile Strength of Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capillary Rheometers. Estimation of Tensile Strength of Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capillary Rheometers — Formal Definition

A **Capillary Rheometer** is a high-pressure, high-shear-rate rheological instrument used to characterize the **flow behavior (viscosity, shear stress, and shear rate) of polymer melts** by forcing the molten polymer through a precision-machined capillary die of known dimensions under controlled temperature and pressure.

$$ \text{Capillary Rheometer} = \text{High Shear Rate Flow Characterization Tool} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Capillary rheometers are the **industrial workhorse** for measuring polymer melt rheology because they simulate actual processing conditions (extrusion, injection molding) where shear rates range from **10 to 10⁶ s⁻¹**, far beyond the range of rotational rheometers.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeeze-The-Toothpaste" Analogy

Imagine squeezing a **thick toothpaste tube** with varying force:
- The harder you squeeze (more pressure), the faster it comes out (higher flow rate).
- The narrower the nozzle (smaller capillary radius), the more resistance it offers.
- The longer the nozzle, the more "drag" the paste experiences.

A **capillary rheometer does the exact same thing** with molten polymer — it pushes the polymer through a tiny, precise tube (capillary) and measures how pressure and flow rate relate. From this, we can deduce the **internal friction (viscosity)** of the polymer melt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The capillary rheometer measures **shear viscosity at processing-relevant shear rates**, making it indispensable for designing extrusion dies, injection molds, and blow-molding processes.

---

## 1.2 Estimation of Tensile Strength of Polymers — Formal Definition

**Tensile Strength (σ)** of a polymer is the **maximum tensile (pulling) stress** that a material can withstand before failure (necking, yielding, or fracture). Estimation refers to **predicting this value from molecular structure, cohesive forces, and group contribution methods** without resorting to full experimental testing.

$$ \sigma_{TS} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} \quad [\text{MPa or psi}] $$

where $F_{max}$ is the maximum load at break and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Chain of Paper Clips" Analogy

Think of a polymer as a **chain of paper clips linked together**:
- If the clips are made of strong steel and tightly interlocked → **high tensile strength**.
- If the clips are loosely connected or made of weak plastic → **low tensile strength**.

The **intermolecular forces** (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, chain entanglements) act like the "link strength" between clips. The **molecular weight, crosslinking, and crystallinity** determine how many clips are in the chain and how well they're organized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cohesive Energy Density (CED)** is the molecular-level "glue" that holds polymer chains together. Higher CED → stronger intermolecular forces → higher tensile strength.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | **298.15 K** |
| Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Shear Rate Range (capillary) | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **10 to 10⁶ s⁻¹** |
| Typical Capillary L/D | $L/D$ | **10 to 40** |
| Tensile Strength of LDPE | $\sigma_{TS}$ | **10–25 MPa** |
| Tensile Strength of Nylon 6,6 | $\sigma_{TS}$ | **80–95 MPa** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capillary Rheometer — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Instrument Architecture (Key Components)

A capillary rheometer consists of:

- **Barrel (Reservoir):** A heated cylindrical chamber that holds the polymer melt.
- **Piston (or Gas Pressure System):** Applies a known pressure/force to extrude the melt.
- **Capillary Die:** A precisely machined tube of known radius $R$ and length $L$.
- **Heating System:** Maintains isothermal conditions (typically ±0.5 °C accuracy).
- **Pressure Transducer:** Measures pressure at the entrance of the capillary.
- **Timer/Displacement Sensor:** Measures volumetric flow rate or extrudate velocity.

### 2.1.2 Governing Equations — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Apparent Shear Stress at the Wall**

Deriving from the **force balance** on a cylindrical element of fluid in the capillary (Hagen-Poiseuille flow):

$$ \tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} $$

- **Why?** The pressure force ($\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2$) is balanced by the shear force on the wall ($2\pi R L \cdot \tau_w$).
- **Units:** $\Delta P$ in Pa, $R$ and $L$ in meters → $\tau_w$ in Pa.

**Step 2: Apparent Shear Rate at the Wall**

From the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ for a Newtonian fluid in a tube:

$$ \dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3} $$

- **Why?** Derived by integrating the parabolic velocity profile $v(r) = v_{max}(1 - r^2/R^2)$ across the cross-section.
- **Units:** $Q$ in m³/s, $R$ in m → $\dot{\gamma}_a$ in s⁻¹.

**Step 3: Apparent Viscosity**

$$ \eta_a = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_a} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q} $$

- This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for Newtonian flow in a tube.

**Step 4: Bagley Correction (Entrance Pressure Loss)**

Polymer melts exhibit **extensional flow** at the capillary entrance, causing an additional pressure drop $\Delta P_{end}$. The corrected wall shear stress:

$$ \tau_w^{corr} = \frac{(\Delta P - \Delta P_{end}) \cdot R}{2L} $$

- **How to determine $\Delta P_{end}$?** By conducting experiments with **capillaries of different $L/D$ ratios** and extrapolating $\Delta P$ vs. $L/D$ to $L/D = 0$ (Bagley plot).

**Step 5: Rabinowitsch Correction (Non-Newtonian Behavior)**

Most polymer melts are **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**. The true wall shear rate is:

$$ \dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \left( \frac{3n+1}{4n} \right) $$

where $n$ is the **power-law index** from $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^n$:

$$ n = \frac{d \ln \tau_w}{d \ln \dot{\gamma}_a} $$

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Extrusion Die Design:** Determines optimal die dimensions to achieve desired throughput.
- **Injection Molding Simulation:** Provides viscosity data for filling simulations.
- **Quality Control:** Compares batch-to-batch consistency of polymer melt flow.
- **Melt Fracture Prediction:** Identifies critical shear rates where flow instabilities occur.

---

## 2.2 Estimation of Tensile Strength — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Factors Governing Tensile Strength

The tensile strength of a polymer is governed by:

- **Cohesive Energy Density (CED):** Energy required to separate polymer chains completely.
- **Molecular Weight ($M$):** Higher $M$ → more chain entanglements → higher $\sigma_{TS}$.
- **Degree of Crosslinking:** Crosslinked polymers have higher $\sigma_{TS}$ (up to an optimum).
- **Crystallinity:** Crystalline regions act as physical crosslinks, increasing $\sigma_{TS}$.
- **Chain Orientation:** Drawing processes align chains, dramatically increasing $\sigma_{TS}$.
- **Intermolecular Forces:** Hydrogen bonding, dipole-dipole, and van der Waals forces.

### 2.2.2 Cohesive Energy Density (CED) and Solubility Parameter

The **Cohesive Energy Density** is defined as:

$$ \text{CED} = \frac{E_{coh}}{V_m} = \frac{\Delta H_v - RT}{V_m} $$

The **Hildebrand Solubility Parameter** is:

$$ \delta = \sqrt{\text{CED}} = \sqrt{\frac{E_{coh}}{V_m}} $$

### 2.2.3 Group Contribution Methods

**Fedors Method** for estimating molar attraction constant $E_{coh}$:

$$ E_{coh} = \sum_i (E_{coh})_i $$

where $(E_{coh})_i$ is the group contribution for each structural unit $i$.

**Small's Method:**

$$ E_{coh} = \sum_i F_i \quad ; \quad V_m = \sum_i V_i $$

where $F_i$ and $V_i$ are group molar attraction constants and molar volumes.

### 2.2.4 Estimation of Tensile Strength from CED (Beyer-Flory Approach)

An empirical correlation relating tensile strength to cohesive energy density:

$$ \sigma_{TS} \approx K \cdot \sqrt{\text{CED}} = K \cdot \delta $$

where $K$ is a proportionality constant (empirically determined, typically **0.5 to 2.0 MPa·cm³/²·Jol^{-1/2}**).

A more refined empirical equation:

$$ \sigma_{TS} = a \cdot \delta + b $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Reasoning:** Breaking a polymer requires overcoming all intermolecular attractions between chains. Since CED quantifies the total cohesive energy per unit volume, its square root naturally correlates with the **force per unit area** needed to separate the chains.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Table 2.1: Capillary Rheometer Equations

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa |
| 2 | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | Apparent wall shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8LQ}$ | Apparent viscosity (Newtonian) | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \left( \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \right)$ | Rabinowitsch-corrected shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $n = \dfrac{d\ln\tau_w}{d\ln\dot{\gamma}_a}$ | Power-law index | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Shear Rate Range}$ | $10$ to $10^6$ | s⁻¹ |

### Table 2.2: Tensile Strength Estimation Equations

| # | Equation | Description | Units |

| 1 | $\sigma_{TS} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Engineering tensile strength | MPa |
| 2 | $\text{CED} = \dfrac{E_{coh}}{V_m}$ | Cohesive energy density | J/m³ or MPa |

The **Bagley and Rabinowitsch corrections** are expected to be mentioned even if not numerically computed.
