# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between acrylates and methacrylates with two suitable examples of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Acrylates | Methacrylates |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Structure** | Derived from $\text{CH}_2=\text{CHCOOR}$ | Derived from $\text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOR}$ |
| **α-Carbon Substitution** | **No methyl group** at α-carbon | **One methyl group** at α-carbon |
| **Polymer Steric Hindrance** | Lower → softer, flexible polymers | Higher → rigid, glassy polymers |
| **Polymerization Rate** | Faster | Slower (steric retardation) |
| **Tg of poly(methyl) ester** | **+10 °C** (PMA) | **+105 °C** (PMMA) |
| **Example 1 (Monomer)** | Methyl acrylate / Butyl acrylate | Methyl methacrylate (MMA) |
| **Example 2 (Polymer)** | Poly(ethyl acrylate) — adhesive base | PMMA — Plexiglas® |
| **Example 3 (Application)** | Latex paint binder | Optical lenses, dentures |

*(Expected answer length: 8–10 lines; award 1 mark for table differences, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for distinguishing application.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What are the salient features of Poly(methyl methacrylate) that make it suitable as a glass substitute?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Optical Clarity:** Light transmittance of **92%** is significantly higher than that of ordinary silicate glass (~85%). Refractive index of **1.49** makes it ideal for lenses.
2. **Low Density:** **1.18 g/cm³** is approximately half the density of glass (2.5 g/cm³), reducing structural load.
3. **Impact Resistance:** It withstands impacts **10–20 times greater** than glass of equivalent thickness, with only "safe" breakage into blunt fragments (no sharp shards).
4. **UV Stability & Weatherability:** Excellent resistance to UV-induced yellowing and outdoor weathering.
5. **Easy Processing:** Can be cast, extruded, injection-molded, and thermoformed.
6. **Limitation:** Lower heat resistance ($T_g \approx 105$ °C) and poor solvent resistance to acetone/chlorinated solvents.

*(Award 1 mark for each of any 3 distinguishing features with values.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the free radical polymerization mechanism of methyl methacrylate (MMA) to produce poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). Discuss the three stages — initiation, propagation, and termination — with relevant chemical equations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A bulk polymerization of MMA is carried out at 77 °C using AIBN as initiator. The following data is given:
- $[\text{M}]_0 = 9.0 \text{ M}$, $[\text{I}]_0 = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$
- $k_d = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $f = 0.5$, $k_p = 7.1 \times 10^{2} \text{ L mol}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$, $k_t = 1.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ L mol}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$

Calculate:
(i) Rate of polymerization $R_p$
(ii) Kinetic chain length $\nu$
(iii) Number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{X}_n$ assuming termination by combination **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Initiation (1.5 marks):**
The initiator **AIBN** decomposes thermally:
$$\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C(CN)N=NC(CN)(CH}_3\text{)}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta, k_d} 2(\text{CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C}^{\bullet}\text{CN} + \text{N}_2$$
The primary radical combines with a monomer:
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{R}-\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3$$

**Propagation (1.5 marks):**
The chain radical attacks successive monomers, with each step adding one MMA unit:
$$\text{R}-[\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3]_n-\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3 + \text{M} \xrightarrow{k_p} \text{R}-[\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3]_{n+1}-\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{C}}(\text{CH}_3)\text{COOCH}_3$$

**Termination (2 marks):**
Two growing chains terminate by either:
- **Combination:** $\text{P}_1^{\bullet} + \text{P}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{P}_1-\text{P}_2$
- **Disproportionation:** $\text{P}_1^{\bullet} + \text{P}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{P}_1\text{=CH}_2 + \text{P}_2\text{-H}$ (one saturated, one unsaturated end)

**Kinetic Summary (2 marks):**
Rate equation: $R_p = k_p \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}} \cdot [\text{M}]$
Chain length: $\nu = \dfrac{k_p [\text{M}]}{2\sqrt{f k_d k_t [\text{I}]}}$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Rate of polymerization (3 marks):**
Apply steady-state approximation:

$$[\text{M}^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$[\text{M}^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 10^{-6}}{1.6 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{8.75 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.96 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol L}^{-1}$$

$$R_p = k_p \cdot [\text{M}^{\bullet}] \cdot [\text{M}] = 7.1 \times 10^2 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-7} \times 9.0$$

$$R_p = 1.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Kinetic chain length (2 marks):**

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [\text{I}] = 2 \times 0.5 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{1.89 \times 10^{-3}}{2.8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 675$$

**(iii) Degree of polymerization (2 marks):**
For combination: $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu = 2 \times 675 = 1350$

$$\overline{M}_n = 1350 \times 100.12 = 135{,}162 \text{ g/mol} \approx 1.35 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the synthesis, properties, and applications of **Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)** as a precursor for carbon fiber. Compare the properties of PAN with PMMA in a structured table. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **acrylic copolymers** (e.g., methyl methacrylate–butyl acrylate, MMA–acrylonitrile) in industrial coating applications. How does the Fox equation help in designing a copolymer with a target $T_g$? **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Synthesis of PAN (2.5 marks):**
Acrylonitrile ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CHCN}$) is polymerized via:
- **Free radical polymerization** in solution (DMF solvent) or by **redox-initiated aqueous slurry** (used industrially by Asahi Kasei, Courtaulds).
- Common initiators: AIBN, persulfates, or redox systems like $\text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8$/$\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$.

$$\text{n CH}_2=\text{CHCN} \xrightarrow{\text{AIBN, } 60-80^{\circ}\text{C}} \left[ -\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CN})- \right]_n$$

**Properties of PAN (2 marks):**
- $T_g = 95$ °C; strong dipole interactions from the CN group.
- Excellent solvent/chemical resistance.
- Yellow, opaque appearance; brittle.
- Excellent precursor for carbon fiber because of:
  - High carbon yield (~50%)
  - Ability to form a stable ladder polymer during thermal stabilization (200–300 °C in air)
  - Cyclization of nitrile groups forms a thermally stable aromatic ladder structure

**Comparison with PMMA (2.5 marks):**

| Property | PAN | PMMA |
|----------|-----|------|
| Tg (°C) | **95** | **105** |
| Transparency | Yellow, opaque | Water-clear |
| Solvent resistance | Excellent | Moderate |
| Carbon yield on pyrolysis | ~50% | <5% |
| Primary use | Carbon fiber | Optical/glazing |
| Monomer molar mass | 53 g/mol | 100 g/mol |

**Carbon Fiber Process (built into properties):**
1. Spinning PAN solution into fibers
2. Thermal oxidative stabilization at 200–300 °C (forms ladder polymer)
3. Carbonization at 1000–1500 °C in inert atmosphere (forms graphite-like structure)
4. Optional graphitization at 2000–3000 °C (high-modulus fiber)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Acrylic Copolymers in Coatings (3.5 marks):**

Industrial latex paints (water-based coatings) almost universally use acrylic copolymers as binders. The most common architecture is a **gradient/staged copolymer** of:
- **Soft monomer:** Butyl acrylate ($T_g = -54$ °C) — provides film formation, flexibility, and adhesion at room temperature.
- **Hard monomer:** Methyl methacrylate ($T_g = 105$ °C) — provides hardness, scrub resistance, and dirt pickup resistance.
- **Functional monomer:** Methacrylic acid (~1–2%) — improves pigment dispersion and emulsion stability.
- **Optional:** Acrylonitrile, acrylamide for specific properties.

The composition can be tuned to deliver a specific $T_g$ for different climates — for example, exterior paints in hot climates need $T_g > 25$ °C to resist dirt pickup, while cold-climate paints may use lower $T_g$ binders.

**Fox Equation for $T_g$ Design (3.5 marks):**

The Fox equation predicts the $T_g$ of a random copolymer from the $T_g$ values of the constituent homopolymers:

$$\frac{1}{T_g(\text{copolymer})} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}} + \cdots$$

where $w_i$ is the **weight fraction** of monomer $i$, and $T_{gi}$ are in **Kelvin**.

**Worked Example (1.5 marks within subpart b):**
Design a coating with $T_g = 20$ °C = 293 K using MMA and butyl acrylate.
Given: $T_{g,\text{MMA}} = 105$ °C = 378 K, $T_{g,\text{BA}} = -54$ °C = 219 K.

$$\frac{1}{293} = \frac{w_{\text{MMA}}}{378} + \frac{(1 - w_{\text{MMA}})}{219}$$

$$3.413 \times 10^{-3} = 2.646 \times 10^{-3} w_{\text{MMA}} + 4.566 \times 10^{-3} (1 - w_{\text{MMA}})$$

$$3.413 \times 10^{-3} = 4.566 \times 10^{-3} - 1.920 \times 10^{-3} w_{\text{MMA}}$$

$$1.920 \times 10^{-3} w_{\text{MMA}} = 1.153 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$w_{\text{MMA}} = 0.600, \quad w_{\text{BA}} = 0.400$$

So a 60:40 MMA:BA copolymer by weight will give a $T_g$ of 20 °C, perfect for general-purpose exterior latex coatings.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Acrylics Exam Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing PMMA with poly(methyl acrylate):** PMMA has a **methyl group on the α-carbon** — its repeat unit is $-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3)-$. Poly(methyl acrylate) is $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{COOCH}_3)-$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units of $T_g$ in Fox equation:** The Fox equation requires **Kelvin**, not Celsius. A common error loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Sign error in $\overline{X}_n$ for combination vs. disproportionation:** For combination, $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$; for disproportionation, $\overline{X}_n = \nu$. Examiners specifically check this.
>
> 4. **Calling AIBN a "peroxide":** AIBN is an **azo initiator** (azobisisobutyronitrile). BPO (benzoyl peroxide) is a peroxide. Each yields different radical structures.
>
> 5. **Skipping the steady-state approximation derivation:** Just writing $R_p = k_p \sqrt{(f k_d [\text{I}])/k_t} \cdot [\text{M}]$ without showing the SSA derivation loses 2 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Acrylics (Module 3)**
>
> ☐ **Acrylics = polymers of monomers with the acryloyl group ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CO}-$)**.
>
> ☐ **PMMA repeat unit:** $-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3)-_{n}$ — has an α-methyl group and an ester group.
>
> ☐ **Key monomers:** MMA (boiling point 100 °C), Acrylic acid (141 °C), Acrylonitrile (77 °C).
>
> ☐ **MMA production:** Acetone-Cyanohydrin (ACH) route is dominant (~80% globally).
>
> ☐ **Polymerization mechanism:** Free Radical Polymerization (FRP) with Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
>
> ☐ **Key equation:** $R_p = k_p \sqrt{f k_d [\text{I}] / k_t} \cdot [\text{M}]$ — derived from Steady-State Approximation (SSA).
>
> ☐ **Kinetic chain length:** $\nu = R_p / R_i = k_p [\text{M}] / (2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [\text{I}]})$.
>
> ☐ **Fox Equation (copolymer $T_g$):** $1/T_g = \sum w_i / T_{gi}$ (in Kelvin).
>
> ☐ **PMMA properties:** $T_g = 105$ °C, density 1.18 g/cm³, light transmittance 92%, refractive index 1.49.
>
> ☐ **PAN properties:** $T_g = 95$ °C, yellow, opaque, primary use as carbon fiber precursor (Boeing 787, F1 cars).
>
> ☐ **Acrylic coating monomers:** Soft BA (Tg -54°C) + hard MMA (Tg +105°C) + functional MAA (~1-2%).
>
> ☐ **Termination:** Combination gives $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$; Disproportionation gives $\overline{X}_n = \nu$.
>
> ☐ **Initiators for MMA:** AIBN (azo, 60-70 °C) and BPO (peroxide, 80-95 °C).
>
> ☐ **PMMA applications:** Optical lenses, aircraft glazing, dental prosthetics, intraocular lenses, LCD backlights, signage.
>
> ☐ **PAN applications:** Carbon fiber precursor, Orlon® acrylic fiber, SAN/ABS resin component.
>
> ☐ **Acrylate applications:** Latex paint binder, pressure-sensitive adhesives, leather finishing, textile coatings.
>
> ☐ **Carothers equation** (step-growth): $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ — applies to **condensation** polymerization, NOT FRP of MMA.
>
> ☐ **Mark-Houwink:** $[\eta] = K M^a$ — for PMMA in toluene, $K = 7.0 \times 10^{-5}$, $a = 0.71$.
>
> ☐ **Weatherability:** PMMA retains >90% tensile strength after 10 years of outdoor exposure — one of the best among transparent plastics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cellulose Acetate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cellulose Acetate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cellulose Acetate (CA)** is an **organic ester of cellulose**, manufactured by the partial or complete acetylation of the three free hydroxyl groups present on each anhydroglucose repeating unit of cellulose, using **acetic anhydride** in the presence of an acid catalyst (typically sulfuric or perchloric acid). It is classified as a **semi-synthetic, thermoplastic, linear polymer** belonging to the broader family of **cellulosic plastics**.

The substitution is quantified by the **Degree of Substitution (DS)**, which represents the **average number of hydroxyl groups (–OH) replaced by acetyl groups (–OCOCH₃)** per anhydroglucose unit. The theoretical maximum value of DS is **3.00** (corresponding to cellulose triacetate).

| Commercial Form | Degree of Substitution (DS) | Acetyl Content (% by weight) | Solvent for Processing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cellulose Triacetate (Primary Acetate) | 2.80 – 3.00 | 43.0 – 44.8 | Chloroform, Methylene chloride |
| Cellulose Diacetate (Secondary Acetate) | 2.20 – 2.70 | 38.5 – 42.5 | Acetone |
| Cellulose Monoacetate | 1.20 – 2.20 | 21.0 – 38.0 | Water (limited), Methanol |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The most commercially significant grade is the **secondary cellulose acetate (diacetate)** because it is soluble in cheap, low-toxicity solvents such as **acetone**, making it ideal for fibre spinning, film casting, and injection moulding.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine the cellulose polymer as a long **bamboo ladder** (where the rungs and rails are made of sugar units, and the side handles are the –OH groups). Now imagine that each handle is "sticky" — it attracts water strongly and makes the polymer rigid and insoluble in common solvents.

When a chemist pours **acetic anhydride** over this ladder in the presence of a catalyst, the sticky –OH handles are replaced by smoother **"acetate handles" (–OCOCH₃)**. The ladder still looks the same, but:

1. The **stickiness disappears** → the polymer now dissolves in acetone.
2. The ladder becomes **slightly more flexible and transparent**.
3. It gains **water resistance**, but loses some of its original high crystallinity.

This is exactly what happens in a cellulose fibre (from cotton linters or wood pulp) during industrial acetylation.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Connection**: The transparent film in old photography reels, the filters in almost every cigarette sold worldwide, and many spectacle frames you wear are all cellulose acetate. So when you pick up a pair of sunglasses, you are holding chemistry in action!

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key metrics used universally in the cellulose acetate industry and in KTU valuation:

- **Molecular weight of anhydroglucose (AGU) unit**: **162 g/mol**
- **Molecular weight of an acetyl group (–OCOCH₃)**: **43 g/mol**
- **Mass increment per substitution**: **42 g/mol** (since one H is replaced by one –COCH₃)
- **Maximum DS**: **3.00**
- **Glass transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub>)**: **180 – 210 °C** (for DS ≈ 2.5)
- **Density**: **1.27 – 1.34 g/cm³**
- **Refractive Index**: **1.47 – 1.50**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Acetylation — The Core Reaction

Cellulose contains **three hydroxyl groups per anhydroglucose unit**:

- **C-2 hydroxyl** (secondary)
- **C-3 hydroxyl** (secondary)
- **C-6 hydroxyl** (primary)

The acetylation reaction proceeds as a **nucleophilic acyl substitution** where the oxygen of the –OH attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetic anhydride:

$$\text{Cell–OH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{Cell–OCOCH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

**The "Why" behind each step:**

- **Why acetic anhydride?** — It is reactive enough to acetylate under mild conditions, yet cheap enough for industrial scale.
- **Why sulfuric acid?** — It protonates the carbonyl oxygen of the anhydride, making the carbonyl carbon strongly electrophilic.
- **Why dilute acid later?** — Excess H₂SO₄ catalyzes hydrolysis of acetate groups back, allowing **partial hydrolysis** to achieve desired DS values.

---

## 2.2 Process Logic — Stepwise Industrial Flow

1. **Pretreatment of cellulose**: Cotton linters or purified wood pulp is broken down and dried (moisture content < **5%**) to avoid side reactions.
2. **Activation**: Cellulose is soaked in glacial acetic acid to open up its crystalline structure.
3. **Acetylation**: Cellulose + acetic anhydride + H₂SO₄ catalyst, at **25 – 50 °C**, until fully acetylated (DS = 3).
4. **Hydrolysis (Ageing)**: Water is added to partially hydrolyze the triacetate to the desired DS (≈ 2.4 – 2.5).
5. **Precipitation & Washing**: The polymer is precipitated using dilute acetic acid, then washed thoroughly to remove catalyst residues.
6. **Stabilization & Drying**: Anti-acid stabilizers are added; the polymer is dried and pelletized.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all the formulas you must memorize for KTU valuation.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\% \text{ Acetyl} = \dfrac{43 \times DS}{162 + 42 \times DS} \times 100$ | 162 = M.W. of AGU, 43 = M.W. of acetyl, 42 = mass increment | Determines purity / grade from lab titration |
| 2 | $M_{\text{repeat unit}} = 162 + 42 \times DS$ | Mass per anhydroglucose unit | Used in stoichiometric yield calculations |
| 3 | $DS = \dfrac{162 \times \%A}{43 \times 100 - 42 \times \%A}$ | Rearrangement of (1) | Inverse calculation when %Acetyl is known |
| 4 | Acetyl Value $= \dfrac{\%A}{0.0234}$ | Empirical correlation used in industry | Quick estimation |
| 5 | $\%\text{ Combined Acetic Acid} = \dfrac{60 \times DS}{162 + 42 \times DS} \times 100$ | 60 = M.W. of CH₃COOH | Used in regulatory specifications |
| 6 | $\text{Intrinsic Viscosity } [\eta] = K \cdot M_v^a$ | Mark–Houwink equation | For molecular weight determination |
| 7 | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{sample}}}{\Delta H_{100\% \text{ cryst}}} \times 100$ | Crystallinity from DSC | Thermal characterization |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical industry**: Cellulose acetate is the dominant material for spectacle frames because it can be polished, dyed, and shaped with low stress.
- **Tobacco industry**: ~**90%** of cigarette filters worldwide are made of cellulose acetate tow.
- **Photography & display**: Was the standard film base for over 80 years.
- **Coatings**: Lacquers for automobiles, wood finishes, nail polishes.
- **Reverse osmosis membranes**: CA membranes are widely used due to their high salt rejection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — % Acetyl Content from Degree of Substitution

**Problem Statement:** Show that for a cellulose acetate sample, the weight percentage of acetyl groups (–OCOCH₃) is given by:

$$\%\text{ Acetyl} = \frac{43 \times DS}{162 + 42 \times DS} \times 100$$

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

**Step 1 — Identify the molecular weight of one anhydroglucose unit (AGU)**

The AGU has the empirical formula C₆H₁₀O₅ with three free –OH groups:

$$M_{\text{AGU}} = (6 \times 12) + (10 \times 1) + (5 \times 16) = 72 + 10 + 80 = 162 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Determine what happens when one –OH is replaced by –OCOCH₃**

- Mass of original –OH group = 1 + 16 = **17 g/mol**
- Mass of incoming –OCOCH₃ group = 12 + 16 + 12 + (3 × 1) = **43 g/mol**
- Net mass increment per substitution = 43 − 17 = **26 g/mol**? ❌

> **Wait** — we must subtract only the **H (1 g/mol)**, because the **O** of the original –OH is retained.

$$\text{Mass increment} = \underbrace{43}_{\text{OCOCH}_3} - \underbrace{1}_{\text{H removed}} = 42 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass of fully substituted AGU when DS = 3**

$$M_{\text{cellulose triacetate AGU}} = 162 + (3 \times 42) = 162 + 126 = 288 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize for any DS**

$$M_{\text{substituted AGU}} = 162 + 42 \cdot DS$$

**Step 5 — Mass contributed by acetyl groups alone**

For DS acetyl groups per AGU:

$$m_{\text{acetyl}} = 43 \cdot DS$$

**Step 6 — Express as weight percentage**

$$\%\text{ Acetyl} = \frac{m_{\text{acetyl}}}{M_{\text{substituted AGU}}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{ Acetyl} = \frac{43 \cdot DS}{162 + 42 \cdot DS} \times 100$$

**Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solving the Reverse Problem (DS from % Acetyl)

**Given:** A cellulose acetate sample is found to contain **40.0 %** acetyl groups by weight. Find the Degree of Substitution.

**Step 1 — Start from the derived formula:**

$$\%\text{ Acetyl} = \frac{43 \cdot DS}{162 + 42 \cdot DS} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute %Acetyl = 40.0:**

$$40.0 = \frac{43 \cdot DS}{162 + 42 \cdot DS} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$40.0 \times (162 + 42 \cdot DS) = 100 \times 43 \cdot DS$$

$$6480 + 1680 \cdot DS = 4300 \cdot DS$$

**Step 4 — Collect DS terms on one side:**

$$6480 = 4300 \cdot DS - 1680 \cdot DS$$

$$6480 = 2620 \cdot DS$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$DS = \frac{6480}{2620} = 2.473$$

**Interpretation:** The sample is **cellulose diacetate** (commercial secondary acetate). ✔️

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stoichiometric Yield of Triacetate from Cellulose

**Given:** Start with **100 g of dry cellulose** (pure AGU basis). Calculate the **mass of cellulose triacetate** obtained assuming complete acetylation (DS = 3).

**Step 1 — Moles of AGU**

$$n_{\text{AGU}} = \frac{100 \text{ g}}{162 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.6173 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of one fully acetylated AGU**

$$M_{\text{triacetate AGU}} = 162 + (3 \times 42) = 288 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of triacetate produced**

$$m_{\text{triacetate}} = 0.6173 \times 288 = 177.78 \text{ g}$$

**Conclusion:** 100 g of cellulose yields **177.78 g** of pure cellulose triacetate — a **77.78% mass gain** due to acetyl substitution.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. DS Relationship

| Property | DS = 1.0 | DS = 2.0 | DS = 2.5 | DS = 3.0 (Triacetate) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water Solubility | High | Low | Insoluble | Insoluble |
| Acetone Solubility | Partial | High | High | Low |
| Chloroform Solubility | None | None | Low | High |
| Melting Point | Decomposes | 230 °C | 240 °C | 290 °C |
| Moisture Regain (%) | 12 | 7 | 4 | 2 |
| Density (g/cm³) | — | 1.31 | 1.32 | 1.34 |
| UV Resistance | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition for KTU Valuation**: Lower DS → higher hydrophilicity, lower melting point, easier solubility in polar solvents like acetone. Higher DS → more hydrophobic, better chemical resistance, requires non-polar solvents like chloroform.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Manufacture of Cellulose Diacetate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawMat[Raw Material: Cotton Linters / Wood Pulp] --> pretreat[Pretreatment: Bleaching and Drying]
    pretreat --> activate[Activation: Glacial Acetic Acid Soak]
    activate --> acetylate[Acetylation Reactor: Acetic Anhydride + H2SO4 Catalyst, 25-50 C]
    acetylate --> triacet[Intermediate Cellulose Triacetate DS approx 3.0]
    triacet --> age[Ageing Tank: Controlled Partial Hydrolysis with Water]
    age --> diacet[Cellulose Diacetate DS 2.4 - 2.5]
    diacet --> precip[Precipitation: Dilute Acetic Acid]
    precip --> wash[Washing: Removal of Catalyst and Acid]
    wash --> stab[Stabilization: Addition of Anti-acid Stabilizers]
    stab --> dry[Drying and Pelletization]
    dry --> finalProd[Final Product: CA Pellets / Flakes]
    finalProd --> app1[Application: Cigarette Filters]
    finalProd --> app2[Application: Spectacle Frames]
    finalProd --> app3[Application: Films and Membranes]
    finalProd --> app4[Application: Lacquers and Coatings]
```

---

## 4.2 Reaction Pathway Diagram — Acetylation to Triacetate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cell[Cellulose AGU with 3 OH groups] --> r1[Step 1: Activation with Acetic Acid]
    r1 --> r2[Step 2: Acetylation with Acetic Anhydride and H2SO4]
    r2 --> r3[Step 3: Hydrolysis to Target DS]
    r3 --> dacet[Diacetate DS 2.4]
    r3 --> mact[Monoacetate DS 1.5]
    r3 --> tact[Triacetate DS 3.0]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cellulose Acetate Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    coreCA[Cellulose Acetate Polymer] --> physProp[Physical Properties Block]
    coreCA --> chemProp[Chemical Properties Block]
    coreCA --> thermProp[Thermal Properties Block]

    physProp --> trans[Optical Transparency]
    physProp --> mech[Mechanical Strength]
    physProp --> flex[Flexibility with Plasticizers]

    chemProp --> sol[Solubility in Acetone]
    chemProp --> res[Chemical Resistance]
    chemProp --> hyd[Hydrophobicity Increases with DS]

    thermProp --> tg[Glass Transition 180-210 C]
    thermProp --> melt[Melting 230-290 C]
    thermProp --> dec[Thermal Decomposition above 250 C]

    trans --> appA[Spectacle Lenses and Frames]
    mech --> appB[Tool Handles and Grips]
    sol --> appC[Fibers via Dry Jet Wet Spinning]
    sol --> appD[Films via Solvent Casting]
    flex --> appE[Cigarette Filter Tow]
    res --> appF[Automotive Lacquers]
    hyd --> appG[Reverse Osmosis Membranes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define cellulose acetate. Mention any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Cellulose acetate is a semi-synthetic thermoplastic polymer obtained by the esterification of cellulose with acetic anhydride in the presence of a mineral acid catalyst. The hydroxyl groups on the cellulose backbone are replaced by acetyl groups, with the degree of substitution typically ranging between 2.0 and 3.0.

**Industrial applications:**
1. **Cigarette filters** — Cellulose acetate tow is the world standard for cigarette filtration.
2. **Spectacle frames** — Used for its transparency, lightweight nature, and ability to be dyed in vibrant colours.

*(Any two valid applications accepted – also accepted: photographic film base, lacquers, textile fibres, RO membranes.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is meant by the Degree of Substitution (DS) in cellulose acetate? Why is its control important?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Degree of Substitution (DS)** is defined as the average number of hydroxyl groups (–OH) replaced by acetyl groups (–OCOCH₃) per anhydroglucose unit of cellulose. The maximum possible value is **3.00**.

**Importance of DS control:**
- Determines **solubility** — DS 2.0–2.5 dissolves in acetone; DS = 3.0 requires chloroform.
- Controls **mechanical properties**, **moisture absorption**, and **thermal behaviour**.
- Determines the **end-use application** — fibre spinning, film casting, moulding, or coating.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Internal choice (Q-A or Q-B) is provided at the module level. Both choices given below are equally valid.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Past Paper Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **manufacture of cellulose diacetate** from cotton linters with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of catalyst and hydrolysis step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **structure–property relationship** of cellulose acetate as a function of DS. How does it influence solubility and mechanical behaviour? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The manufacture of cellulose diacetate consists of the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Pretreatment:**
Cotton linters (or purified wood pulp) are mechanically shredded, bleached with sodium hypochlorite, and dried to reduce moisture content below **5%**. Excess moisture hydrolyzes acetic anhydride wastefully.

**Step 2 — Activation:**
The dried cellulose is treated with **glacial acetic acid** at 25–30 °C for 1–2 hours. This swells the cellulose fibres, opens the crystalline regions, and makes the –OH groups accessible for the upcoming reaction.

**Step 3 — Acetylation:**
The activated cellulose is charged into a stainless-steel reactor along with **acetic anhydride** (about 2.5–3.0 times the stoichiometric requirement) and **sulfuric acid catalyst (0.5–1.5% on cellulose weight)**. The temperature is maintained at **25–50 °C** for 2–6 hours until complete acetylation occurs, giving cellulose triacetate (DS ≈ 3.0).

$$\text{Cell–(OH)}_3 + 3\,(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cell–(OCOCH}_3)_3 + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

**Role of catalyst:** Sulfuric acid protonates the carbonyl oxygen of acetic anhydride, activating it for nucleophilic attack by the cellulose –OH groups. Without the catalyst, the reaction is impractically slow.

**Step 4 — Partial Hydrolysis (Ageing):**
To convert triacetate to the commercially valuable diacetate, controlled amounts of water are added and the mixture is held at 40–60 °C for several hours. The H₂SO₄ also catalyzes this hydrolysis, cleaving some acetyl groups until DS drops to **2.4 – 2.5**.

**Step 5 — Precipitation, Washing & Drying:**
The reaction mass is diluted with dilute acetic acid, precipitating the polymer as flakes. These flakes are washed thoroughly to remove residual acid and catalyst, then dried in a hot-air dryer.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Structure–property relationship with DS:**

| DS | Structure | Solubility | Mechanical Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 (Monoacetate) | Partly acetylated, hydrogen-bonded network retained | Water-soluble | Brittle, hard |
| 2.0–2.5 (Diacetate) | Hydrogen bonds partially disrupted | Acetone-soluble | Tough, flexible |
| 3.0 (Triacetate) | Fully acetylated, hydrophobic | Chloroform-soluble | Rigid, high-melting |

**Influence on solubility:**
- At **DS < 2.0**, unreacted –OH groups maintain extensive inter- and intramolecular hydrogen bonding → polymer is hydrophilic and water-soluble.
- At **DS 2.0–2.5**, most –OH groups are replaced; polymer dissolves in polar organic solvents like **acetone** due to reduced H-bonding.
- At **DS = 3.0**, all –OH groups are acetylated → polymer is hydrophobic and requires non-polar solvents like **chloroform or methylene chloride**.

**Influence on mechanical behaviour:**
- Higher DS increases **chain stiffness** (due to bulky acetyl groups hindering rotation) → higher tensile strength but lower impact resistance.
- Diacetate, when plasticized with **diethyl phthalate** or **triphenyl phosphate**, achieves the ideal balance of flexibility and strength used in spectacle frames.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice Model]**

**(a)** What are the different **plasticizers** used with cellulose acetate? Explain their function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major **applications of cellulose acetate** with specific examples, and explain why it has lost some market share in photographic films. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Plasticizers** are low-molecular-weight substances added to cellulose acetate to:
- Lower the glass transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub>) for easier processing.
- Improve flexibility, impact resistance, and elongation at break.
- Reduce brittleness of the finished product.

**Common plasticizers for cellulose acetate:**

| Plasticizer | Full Name | Typical Loading (%) | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DEP** | Diethyl phthalate | 20 – 35 | Excellent for clarity in films |
| **DMP** | Dimethyl phthalate | 20 – 30 | High plasticizing efficiency |
| **DBP** | Dibutyl phthalate | 15 – 30 | Low volatility |
| **TPP** | Triphenyl phosphate | 10 – 25 | Flame-retardant bonus |
| **Triacetin** | Glyceryl triacetate | 15 – 30 | FDA-approved for food contact |
| **PEG** | Polyethylene glycol | 5 – 15 | Used for fibre spinning |

**Function (mechanism):**
Plasticizer molecules insert themselves between polymer chains, increasing the free volume. This weakens secondary bonding (van der Waals, dipole interactions), allowing chains to slide past one another more easily under stress → increased flexibility, decreased T<sub>g</sub> and stiffness.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Major applications:**

1. **Cigarette filters** — Cellulose acetate tow, processed through fibre-bundling, accounts for over **90%** of the global cigarette filter market. The fibres trap tar and particulate matter efficiently.

2. **Spectacle frames** — Fashion eyewear uses CA because it is lightweight, hypoallergenic, easily polished, and can be made in vibrant colours.

3. **Textile fibres** — Commercial name "**acetate rayon**" or "**Estron**". Used in women's apparel, lining fabrics, and decorative items.

4. **Films and membranes** — Cast films for packaging, photographic film base, reverse osmosis membranes for water desalination.

5. **Coatings and lacquers** — CA dissolved in acetone is brushed or sprayed as protective coatings on automobiles, paper, and wood.

6. **Tool handles, toys, and pen barrels** — Injection-moulded CA is used for its toughness and glossy finish.

**Why CA lost share in photographic films:**
- The rise of **digital photography** drastically reduced the demand for photographic film base.
- Polyester (PET) films have superior dimensional stability and tear strength for specialized films.
- CA is more moisture-sensitive than polyester, leading to degradation under tropical conditions.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students up to 4–5 marks per question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DS with degree of polymerization (DP)** — DS refers to acetyl substitution; DP refers to chain length. Examiners deduct 1 mark if mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting units in derivation** — Always write g/mol, °C, etc. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong molecular weight for AGU** — Use **162**, not 180 (180 is for glucose, not the anhydro form).
> 4. **Saying "triacetate is most used commercially"** — This is FALSE. **Diacetate** is the workhorse. Triacetate is a niche product.
> 5. **Drawing arrow diagrams in reverse** — In flow diagrams, arrows should flow *from raw material to product*, not the reverse. Marks lost for unclear flow.
> 6. **No mention of catalyst recovery** — KTU examiners appreciate mention of H₂SO₄ recovery and effluent treatment in industrial questions.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cellulose acetate** is a **semi-synthetic polymer** made by acetylating cellulose with acetic anhydride + H₂SO₄ catalyst.
- **Degree of Substitution (DS)** ranges from 0 to 3. **Diacetate (DS ≈ 2.5)** is the most commercially important.
- **M.W. of AGU = 162 g/mol**; **mass increment per substitution = 42 g/mol**; **mass per acetyl = 43 g/mol**.
- **Key formula**: %Acetyl = (43 × DS) / (162 + 42 × DS) × 100.
- **Process flow**: Pretreatment → Activation → Acetylation → Hydrolysis → Precipitation → Washing → Drying.
- **Plasticizers** (DEP, DMP, TPP, triacetin) reduce T<sub>g</sub> and improve flexibility.
- **Top applications**: Cigarette filters, spectacle frames, textile fibres, films, lacquers.
- **Solvent rule**: Low DS → water; Mid DS (2.0–2.5) → acetone; High DS (3.0) → chloroform.
- **100 g cellulose gives 177.78 g triacetate** at complete conversion.
- **Catalyst role**: H₂SO₄ activates acetic anhydride; same catalyst also promotes hydrolysis for DS control.
- **Comparison with nitrocellulose**: CA is **less flammable** than nitrocellulose → safer handling and storage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluoropolymers (PTFE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluoropolymers (PTFE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fluoropolymers — The Definitional Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Fluoropolymers** are a specialized class of high-performance engineering thermoplastics in which the hydrogen atoms of the parent hydrocarbon polymer chain are partially or completely replaced by **fluorine atoms**. This atomic substitution imparts extraordinary chemical inertness, thermal stability, and surface characteristics that no other polymer family can match.

Among all fluoropolymers, **Poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE)** is the most industrially significant and historically the first to be commercialized (1938, DuPont). It is also popularly known by its trade names **Teflon® (DuPont)**, **Hostaflon® (3M/Dyneon)**, and **Fluon® (Asahi Glass)**.

## 1.2 Poly(tetrafluoroethylene) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PTFE is a fully fluorinated, linear, semi-crystalline thermoplastic homopolymer derived from the free-radical addition polymerization of the gaseous monomer tetrafluoroethylene (TFE, C₂F₄). Its repeating constitutional unit is –[CF₂–CF₂]–ₙ, with a repeat unit molar mass of exactly 100 g·mol⁻¹.**

Key molecular and physical parameters of PTFE:

| Parameter | Value (with units) |
|---|---|
| Repeat unit formula | **–[CF₂–CF₂]–** |
| Repeat unit molar mass | **100 g·mol⁻¹** |
| C–F bond energy | **485 kJ·mol⁻¹** |
| C–F bond length | **1.32 Å (0.132 nm)** |
| F–C–F bond angle | **108.5°** |
| Density (crystalline) | **2.302 g·cm⁻³** |
| Density (amorphous) | **2.00 g·cm⁻³** |
| Density (commercial) | **2.13 – 2.19 g·cm⁻³** |
| Melting point (Tₘ) | **327 °C (600 K)** |
| Glass transition (T_g) | **~ −97 °C** |
| Continuous service temp. | **−260 °C to +260 °C** |
| Tensile strength | **14 – 35 MPa** |
| Dielectric constant (1 MHz) | **2.0 – 2.1** |
| Dielectric strength | **60 kV·mm⁻¹** |
| Volume resistivity | **>10¹⁸ Ω·cm** |
| Water absorption (24 h) | **< 0.01 %** |
| Coefficient of friction (vs. steel) | **0.05 – 0.10** |

## 1.3 The Historical Discovery (A Student-Friendly Story)

> [!NOTE]
> **Serendipity in Polymer Science:** In **April 1938**, a young DuPont chemist named **Roy J. Plunkett** was working with tetrafluoroethylene gas stored in pressurized cylinders. When he opened the valve of a cylinder that appeared empty, no gas came out — yet the cylinder's mass had not changed. Inside, he found a white, waxy, slippery solid: the world's first PTFE. The TFE had spontaneously polymerized under pressure. This chance discovery gave birth to the entire fluoropolymer industry.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Invisible Shield" Analogy:** Imagine you take an ordinary polyethylene chain — like the plastic used in grocery bags — and you wrap every single hydrogen atom in a thick, electron-rich "armor" of fluorine. Fluorine is the most electronegative element on the periodic table (χ = 3.98 on the Pauling scale). This armor makes the chain chemically invulnerable: acids, bases, solvents, and oxidizers simply **cannot** find an electron to attack. The chain is also stiff, smooth, and slippery, much like a snake that has been coated in Teflon spray. This is why a PTFE-coated pan can survive years of contact with boiling oil, acid marinades, and dishwashers without degrading. The fluorine "armor" is the source of PTFE's legendary non-stick and corrosion-proof behavior.

## 1.5 Why the Fluorine Atom is the Key

The **fluorine atom (9F)** brings three unique advantages that no other substituent can match:

1. **Smallest atomic radius after hydrogen** — so it fits sterically along the chain without distorting bond geometry.
2. **Highest electronegativity (3.98)** — polarizes the C–F bond strongly, producing a chemically inert, low-surface-energy surface.
3. **Strongest single bond to carbon in organic chemistry (C–F ≈ 485 kJ·mol⁻¹)** — provides exceptional thermal and oxidative stability.

Combined, these three effects mean that once a C–F bond forms, it almost never breaks under normal service conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Analysis of PTFE's Unique Behavior

### Step 1 — The Molecular Architecture

PTFE is a **linear, unbranched homopolymer** with the simplest possible repeat unit among all fluoropolymers. The chain is essentially a **fully fluorinated polyethylene**:

$$\text{Polymerization: } n \text{CF}_2\!=\!\text{CF}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Free radical initiator}} -[\text{CF}_2-\text{CF}_2]_n-$$

### Step 2 — Why the Helical Conformation Forms

Unlike polyethylene (which adopts a planar **zig-zag** conformation), the bulky fluorine atoms physically prevent PTFE from lying flat. The chain twists into a **helical (spiral) conformation** with a twist of about **13.8° per CF₂ unit**, giving roughly **13 CF₂ groups per half-turn of the helix**, repeating every **1.68 nm**. This helical sheath of fluorine atoms:

- Creates a smooth, cylindrical "Teflon surface" with no reactive sites.
- Shields the carbon backbone from chemical attack.
- Explains PTFE's very low coefficient of friction.

### Step 3 — Polymerization Mechanism (Free-Radical Addition)

PTFE is **exclusively** synthesized by **free-radical polymerization** of TFE. The mechanism is the classical three-stage process:

1. **Initiation** — A peroxide (e.g., ammonium persulfate, APS) decomposes to give radicals R•.
2. **Propagation** — The radical adds to the C=C of TFE; the new radical adds another TFE, and so on.
3. **Termination** — Combination or disproportionation of two chain radicals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical point for KTU:** Because TFE is a gas (boiling point = −76.3 °C), polymerization is carried out in **aqueous medium under high pressure (10–100 atm)** either by **suspension** (granular PTFE) or **emulsion/dispersion** (fine powder PTFE).

### Step 4 — Why PTFE Cannot Be Melt-Processed Conventionally

PTFE has an exceptionally **high melt viscosity** — about **10¹⁰ to 10¹² Pa·s at 380 °C**, which is 10⁶–10⁸ times higher than polyethylene at its melting point. Below its melting point (327 °C), PTFE does not flow. Above it, it becomes a transparent gel that does not flow either, because of its extremely high molecular weight (Mₙ ≈ 10⁶ – 10⁷ g·mol⁻¹).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** PTFE must be fabricated by **non-conventional techniques** — compression molding + sintering, ram extrusion, paste extrusion (with naphtha or white-oil lubricant), isostatic molding, and coagulation casting. Conventional injection molding, blow molding, and extrusion are **not feasible** for virgin PTFE.

### Step 5 — The Three-Phase Polymorphism of PTFE

PTFE exhibits **three solid phases** between room temperature and its melting point, a feature unique among commodity polymers:

| Phase | Temperature range | Conformation | Crystal system |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase II** | Below **19 °C** | Twisted zigzag (helical, 13/6) | Triclinic |
| **Phase IV** | **19 – 30 °C** | Slightly untwisted helix | Hexagonal |
| **Phase I** | **30 – 327 °C** | Untangled helix | Pseudo-hexagonal |

This is why PTFE articles undergo a **2.0 % volume expansion** when cooled below 19 °C — engineers must allow for this in cryogenic seal design.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key formulas a KTU student must memorize for the Fluoropolymers module are summarized below.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_0 = 2(12) + 4(19) = 100$ | Repeat unit molar mass of PTFE | g·mol⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | Number-average degree of polymerization | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\rho_{\text{PTFE}} = 2.13 - 2.19$ | Commercial PTFE density | g·cm⁻³ |
| 4 | $\rho_c = 2.302,\; \rho_a = 2.000$ | Crystalline & amorphous reference densities | g·cm⁻³ |
| 5 | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$ | Degree of crystallinity (density method) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | $\rho_s = \dfrac{\rho_c \rho_a}{\rho_c - \chi_c(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$ | Sample density from crystallinity | g·cm⁻³ |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{19} \approx 2.0 \, \%$ | Volume change at 19 °C phase transition | % |
| 8 | $E_{C-F} \approx 485$ | C–F bond dissociation energy | kJ·mol⁻¹ |
| 9 | $T_m = 327 \,°C$ | Equilibrium melting point | °C |
| 10 | $\tan \delta_{\text{max}}$ at $10^{2} \text{ Hz} \approx 10^{-4}$ | Loss tangent (extremely low dissipation) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{melt}} = 10^{10} - 10^{12}$ | Melt viscosity at 380 °C | Pa·s |
| 12 | $\% \text{ Crystallinity} = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | Crystallinity in percentage | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for KTU answer sheets:** Always retain at least **3 significant figures** when reporting crystallinity or molecular weights of PTFE. Examiners penalize rounding to 1–2 figures.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Concepts

| Concept | Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| Crystallinity of PTFE | Predicts barrier properties, wear resistance, hardness in seals |
| Helical conformation | Explains non-stick behavior in cookware, low friction in bearings |
| C–F bond energy (485 kJ·mol⁻¹) | Justifies use in nuclear, aerospace, and aggressive chemical plants |
| Phase II↔IV transition at 19 °C | Critical for cryogenic valve and gasket design (LNG, liquid N₂) |
| High melt viscosity | Forces special processing routes — paste extrusion of wire insulation |
| Low dielectric constant (2.05) | Makes PTFE the gold standard for **high-frequency PCB substrates** (Teflon-based microwave/RF boards) |
| Volume resistivity > 10¹⁸ Ω·cm | Used as **primary insulation** in aerospace wire harnesses |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical goal:** In this section, every algebraic and logical transition is written out in full so that a student can reproduce the derivation in an exam without skipping a single step.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Repeat-Unit Molar Mass of PTFE

The repeating unit of PTFE contains **two carbon atoms** and **four fluorine atoms**.

**Step 1:** Look up standard atomic masses:
- C (carbon) = 12.011 g·mol⁻¹
- F (fluorine) = 18.998 g·mol⁻¹

**Step 2:** Multiply atomic masses by the number of atoms in the repeat unit:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0(\text{C}) &= 2 \times 12.011 = 24.022 \ \text{g·mol}^{-1} \\
M_0(\text{F}) &= 4 \times 18.998 = 75.992 \ \text{g·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Add the two contributions:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0(\text{PTFE}) &= 24.022 + 75.992 = 100.014 \ \text{g·mol}^{-1} \\
&\approx 100 \ \text{g·mol}^{-1} \quad \text{(rounded)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering convention:** The repeat-unit molar mass of PTFE is universally taken as exactly **100 g·mol⁻¹** in all KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Degree of Polymerization (DP) from Mₙ

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of number-average degree of polymerization:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the PTFE repeat-unit mass:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{100 \, \text{g·mol}^{-1}}$$

**Numerical example (typical of KTU problems):**
If a sample of commercial granular PTFE has $\overline{M}_n = 4.0 \times 10^{6}$ g·mol⁻¹, then

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \frac{4.0 \times 10^{6}}{100} \\
&= 4.0 \times 10^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

So each chain has **40,000 repeat units on average** — a very high molecular weight, which is why melt flow is essentially impossible.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crystallinity by the Density Method (KTU's most-asked fluoropolymer numerical)

The two-phase model of a semi-crystalline polymer assumes the specific volume is additive:

$$v_s = v_a(1 - \chi_c) + v_c \chi_c$$

where $v$ is specific volume (= 1/ρ) and subscripts $s, a, c$ denote sample, amorphous, and crystalline.

**Step 1:** Invert the equation to express in densities:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_s} = \frac{1 - \chi_c}{\rho_a} + \frac{\chi_c}{\rho_c}$$

**Step 2:** Combine into a single fraction:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_s} = \frac{\rho_c(1 - \chi_c) + \rho_a \chi_c}{\rho_a \rho_c}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply:

$$\rho_a \rho_c = \rho_s [\rho_c - \rho_c \chi_c + \rho_a \chi_c]$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $\chi_c$:

$$\rho_a \rho_c = \rho_s \rho_c - \rho_s \chi_c (\rho_c - \rho_a)$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:

$$\rho_s \chi_c (\rho_c - \rho_a) = \rho_s \rho_c - \rho_a \rho_c = \rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\chi_c$:

$$\boxed{\chi_c = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)}}$$

### Worked Example (typical KTU 7-mark sub-question)

> A sample of PTFE has a measured density of $\rho_s = 2.165$ g·cm⁻³. Given $\rho_c = 2.302$ g·cm⁻³ and $\rho_a = 2.000$ g·cm⁻³, calculate the **percentage crystallinity**.

**Step 1:** Substitute the values:

$$\chi_c = \frac{2.302 \times (2.165 - 2.000)}{2.165 \times (2.302 - 2.000)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the differences:

$$\chi_c = \frac{2.302 \times 0.165}{2.165 \times 0.302}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator:

$$2.302 \times 0.165 = 0.37983$$

**Step 4:** Denominator:

$$2.165 \times 0.302 = 0.65383$$

**Step 5:** Divide:

$$\chi_c = \frac{0.37983}{0.65383} = 0.5810$$

**Step 6:** Express as percentage:

$$\% \chi_c = 0.5810 \times 100 = 58.1 \, \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A commercial PTFE with ~58 % crystallinity is a typical "as-sintered" grade. Highly oriented, sintered-and-quenched grades can reach 75–80 % crystallinity.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quantitative Comparison of C–H vs C–F Bond Energy (KTU's frequent theory question)

**Step 1:** Use the Pauling electronegativity relationship for bond energy correction.

**Step 2:** Note that the standard C–H bond energy is **413 kJ·mol⁻¹**, while the C–F bond energy is **485 kJ·mol⁻¹**.

**Step 3:** The percentage increase is:

$$\Delta = \frac{485 - 413}{413} \times 100 = \frac{72}{413} \times 100 = 17.4 \, \%$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: The C–F bond is **~17 % stronger** than C–H, which translates into dramatically higher thermal, oxidative, and chemical stability for PTFE versus polyethylene.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Theoretical Repeat-Unit Length of PTFE Helix

**Step 1:** The C–C bond length in PTFE = 1.54 Å (sp³ carbon).
**Step 2:** The F–C–F bond angle = 108.5°.
**Step 3:** The projection of one C–C bond on the helix axis is:

$$d = 1.54 \times \cos\!\left(\frac{108.5°}{2}\right) = 1.54 \times \cos(54.25°)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$d = 1.54 \times 0.5844 = 0.900 \, \text{Å} \ \text{per CF}_2 \ \text{unit}$$

**Step 5:** Two CF₂ units per repeat, so the c-axis spacing per chemical repeat is:

$$c = 2 \times 0.900 = 1.80 \, \text{Å (theoretical)}$$

The experimentally measured value is **~1.69 Å** (Helical 13/6 conformation), confirming that the chain is slightly compressed from the theoretical zig-zag projection — a fact that KTU examiners sometimes ask as a "comment on" question.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Fluoropolymers

| Property | PTFE | FEP | PFA | ETFE | PVDF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit | –[CF₂–CF₂]– | –[CF₂–CF₂/CF₂–CFCF₃]– | –[CF₂–CF₂/CF₂–CF(OR)]– | –[CH₂–CH₂/CF₂–CF₂]– | –[CH₂–CF₂]– |
| M₀ (g·mol⁻¹) | 100 | varies | varies | 64 | 64 |
| Tₘ (°C) | 327 | 260 | 305 | 270 | 165–172 |
| Melt-processable? | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Max service (°C) | 260 | 200 | 260 | 150 | 150 |
| Chemical resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Very good | Good |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 2× | 4× | 1.5× | 0.6× |
| KTU weightage | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are framed strictly in line with the **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's-level distribution** (L1–L2 for Part A, L3–L4 for Part B) and the **14-mark module-internal-choice pattern** used in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1: Define fluoropolymers. Give the structure and two characteristic properties of PTFE. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fluoropolymers are a class of polymers in which one or more hydrogen atoms of the hydrocarbon backbone are replaced by fluorine atoms, resulting in enhanced chemical resistance, thermal stability, and low surface energy.
PTFE (Poly-tetrafluoroethylene) is a fully fluorinated linear polymer with the repeat unit **–[CF₂–CF₂]–** and repeat-unit molar mass = **100 g·mol⁻¹**.
Two characteristic properties:
1. Operating temperature range: −260 °C to +260 °C.
2. Coefficient of friction = 0.05–0.10 (very low) and chemical resistance to virtually all solvents and acids.

### Question 2: Why does PTFE exhibit a helical conformation rather than a planar zig-zag? [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fluorine atoms in PTFE are larger and more electron-rich than hydrogen atoms. Steric repulsion between adjacent CF₂ groups forces the chain to twist out of the planar zig-zag conformation adopted by polyethylene. The result is a **helical sheath** with ~13 CF₂ groups per half-turn and a torsion angle of about 13.8° per CF₂ unit. This helix exposes only the inert C–F surface to the outside environment, which is the molecular origin of PTFE's low friction and non-stick behavior.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B** in the allotted module slot.

### ⭐ Question A (14 marks)

#### (a) Discuss the free-radical polymerization of tetrafluoroethylene (TFE). Compare suspension and emulsion routes for the production of PTFE. [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Monomer:** TFE, CF₂=CF₂, a colorless gas, bp = −76.3 °C, must be polymerized under pressure.
2. **Initiation:** A water-soluble peroxide (e.g., ammonium persulfate, (NH₄)₂S₂O₈) decomposes thermally to give sulfate radical-anions:
   $\text{S}_2\text{O}_8^{2-} \rightarrow 2 \text{SO}_4^{\bullet -}$
3. **Propagation:** The radical adds to TFE, generating a new radical that successively adds TFE units:
   $\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CF}_2\!=\!\text{CF}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–CF}_2\text{–CF}_2^{\bullet}$
4. **Termination:** By combination (R–R) or disproportionation of two chain radicals.

**Comparison of routes:**

| Aspect | Suspension Polymerization | Emulsion / Dispersion |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Aqueous, no surfactant | Aqueous + surfactant (e.g., PFOS-free fluorosurfactant) |
| Initiator | Water-soluble peroxide | Water-soluble peroxide |
| Product form | Granular resin (100–600 μm) | Aqueous dispersion (0.1–0.3 μm) |
| Agitation | Mild | Vigorous |
| KTU phrase | "Granular PTFE" | "Fine powder / dispersion PTFE" |
| Typical use | Compression molding, ram extrusion | Paste extrusion, coatings |
| Mₙ range | 10⁶ – 10⁷ | 10⁶ – 10⁷ |

#### (b) Explain why PTFE cannot be processed by conventional melt-processing techniques. List any four special processing methods used for PTFE with one application of each. [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

PTFE cannot be melt-processed because:
1. Its **molecular weight is very high (Mₙ ≈ 10⁶–10⁷ g·mol⁻¹)**, producing chain entanglements that never disentangle below the decomposition temperature.
2. Its **melt viscosity at 380 °C is 10¹⁰–10¹² Pa·s**, which is 10⁶–10⁸ times higher than that of polyethylene under comparable conditions.
3. **Above 327 °C, PTFE becomes a transparent gel that does not flow** but instead degrades before viscous flow can occur.
4. It has **no useful glass transition below 327 °C** that could enable rubbery flow.

**Four special processing methods:**

| Method | Brief description | One application |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression molding + sintering** | Powder pressed in a mold at 25–35 MPa, then sintered at 360–380 °C | Gaskets, valve seats |
| **Paste extrusion** | Fine powder mixed with 15–20 % lubricant (naphtha), extruded, then lubricant removed and sintered | Wire insulation, thin-walled tubes |
| **Ram extrusion** | Granular PTFE compacted by a ram through a heated die | Continuous rods and tubes |
| **Isostatic molding** | Powder in a flexible mold is pressed uniformly from all sides in a high-pressure fluid | Large, complex-shaped chemical parts |

### ⭐ Question B (14 marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

#### (a) Describe the helical structure and three-phase polymorphism of PTFE. How does the 19 °C phase transition affect engineering design of cryogenic seals? [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Helical Structure:**
PTFE chains adopt a **helical conformation** because the bulky, electronegative fluorine atoms force the C–C backbone out of the planar zig-zag. The chain has a helical pitch of ~1.68 nm with about 13 CF₂ units per 6 full turns. This exposes only the inert fluorine "sheath" externally, yielding the very low surface energy of ~18 mN·m⁻¹.

**Three Solid Phases of PTFE:**

| Phase | Temperature Range | Conformation | Crystal System |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase II | Below 19 °C | Twisted helix, 13/6 | Triclinic |
| Phase IV | 19–30 °C | Slightly untwisted helix | Hexagonal |
| Phase I | 30–327 °C | Untwisted, untangled helix | Pseudo-hexagonal |

**Effect on Cryogenic Seal Design:**
At 19 °C, PTFE undergoes a ~**2.0 % volume change** on cooling. For a cryogenic LNG seal that operates at −160 °C, this transition has been crossed. The designer must:
- Provide **axial and radial clearance** of at least 2 % to avoid tensile cracking.
- Choose **filled grades (e.g., 25 % glass-filled PTFE)** with reduced phase-transition expansion.
- Avoid **glassy fillers with mismatched CTE** that could amplify stresses.

#### (b) A commercial PTFE sample has a measured density of 2.175 g·cm⁻³. Given ρc = 2.302 g·cm⁻³ and ρa = 2.000 g·cm⁻³, calculate (i) the percentage crystallinity, and (ii) the average degree of polymerization for a number-average molecular weight of 5.0 × 10⁶ g·mol⁻¹. [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Percentage crystallinity:**

$$\chi_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} = \frac{2.302(2.175 - 2.000)}{2.175(2.302 - 2.000)}$$

Compute numerator:

$$2.302 \times 0.175 = 0.40285$$

Compute denominator:

$$2.175 \times 0.302 = 0.65685$$

Divide:

$$\chi_c = \frac{0.40285}{0.65685} = 0.6133$$

**Percentage crystallinity ≈ 61.3 %.**

**(ii) Average degree of polymerization:**

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^6}{100} = 5.0 \times 10^4$$

**$\overline{X}_n$ = 50,000 repeat units per chain on average.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this type of question:**
> 1. **Do not mix up $\rho_a$ and $\rho_c$** — always keep the crystalline density (2.302) in the numerator term multiplied by $(\rho_s - \rho_a)$, never swap.
> 2. **Always show the units** in the answer (g·cm⁻³ for density, g·mol⁻¹ for molar mass, dimensionless for DP).
> 3. **Do not forget the ×100** when reporting percentage crystallinity.
> 4. **State M₀ = 100 g·mol⁻¹ explicitly** at the start of the calculation; this is a 1-mark gift from the examiner.
> 5. **Round to three significant figures** for crystallinity. 61.3 % is acceptable; 61 % is acceptable; 0.61 is acceptable but discouraged.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid revision checklist — print this and read 10 minutes before the exam:**

- **PTFE repeat unit:** –[CF₂–CF₂]– ; **M₀ = 100 g·mol⁻¹**.
- **Monomer:** TFE (tetrafluoroethylene, C₂F₄, gas at room temperature).
- **Discovery:** Roy Plunkett, 1938, DuPont (accident).
- **Trade names:** Teflon® (DuPont), Hostaflon® (3M), Fluon® (AGC).
- **Synthesis:** Free-radical polymerization only (suspension → granular; emulsion → dispersion).
- **C–F bond:** strongest single bond in organic chemistry, **485 kJ·mol⁻¹**.
- **Tₘ = 327 °C ; T_g ≈ −97 °C** ; continuous service up to **260 °C**.
- **Density (commercial) = 2.13–2.19 g·cm⁻³**; $\rho_c$ = 2.302, $\rho_a$ = 2.000.
- **Crystallinity formula:** $\chi_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)}$ — a guaranteed numerical every year.
- **Conformation:** helical, ~13 CF₂ per 6 turns; this gives low friction and non-stick behavior.
- **Phases of PTFE:** Phase II (< 19 °C), Phase IV (19–30 °C), Phase I (30–327 °C); 19 °C transition causes ~2 % volume change.
- **Melt viscosity at 380 °C = 10¹⁰ – 10¹² Pa·s** — cannot be melt-processed.
- **Special processing routes:** compression molding + sintering, paste extrusion, ram extrusion, isostatic molding.
- **Copolymers for melt processability:** FEP, PFA, ETFE, ECTFE.
- **Dielectric constant ≈ 2.05** — PTFE is the gold-standard substrate for microwave/RF printed circuit boards.
- **Chemical resistance:** attacked only by molten alkali metals, fluorine gas at high T, and a few exotic fluorinating agents.
- **Drawbacks:** poor creep resistance, low wear resistance, poor radiation resistance, cannot be solvent-bonded, and very high cost.
- **Applications to remember for viva/ESE:** non-stick cookware, gaskets, aerospace wire insulation, HF chemical plant linings, surgical implants, plumbing thread tape (Teflon tape), architectural PTFE-coated glass-fibre membranes (e.g., stadium roofs).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
