topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nylons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nylons** are a family of synthetic polymers known chemically as **polyamides (PA)**, characterized by the presence of recurring **amide linkages (—CO—NH—)** along the polymer backbone. They were the first commercially successful synthetic fibers, originally developed by **Wallace Carothers** at the DuPont research laboratory in **1935**, with Nylon 66 being commercialized in **1939** as a replacement for silk.

A nylon polymer is formally defined as a long-chain synthetic polyamide in which at least **85%** (by mass) of the amide linkages are directly attached to an aliphatic or cycloaliphatic group. The IUPAC nomenclature designates the parent repeating unit as a **"poly(imino...)"** chain, but in polymer engineering practice, the numeric naming convention (e.g., Nylon 6, Nylon 66) is universally followed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two nylons of paramount importance in the PECHT523 syllabus are **Nylon 6** (a homopolymer of ε-caprolactam) and **Nylon 6,6** (a copolymer of hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid). Their contrasting syntheses (ring-opening polymerization vs. step-growth polycondensation) form a classic examination question.

## 1.2 Nomenclature Convention — The Numeric Code

The numerical suffix is **not arbitrary**; it carries structural information:

| Code Pattern | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Single number (e.g., 6, 11, 12) | Number of carbon atoms in the **single** amino acid monomer | Nylon 6 (6 carbons in caprolactam) |
| Two numbers (e.g., 6,6 or 4,6) | Number of carbons in the **diamine** and the **diacid** monomers, respectively | Nylon 6,6 (6 C in diamine + 6 C in diacid) |

> [!NOTE]
> The comma in "6,6" is a standard polymer chemistry convention; the use of "6,6", "66", or "6.6" are all accepted in literature, but the KTU board examiners prefer **Nylon 6,6** with a comma.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Velcro + Bricks Model

Imagine you are building a long wall using two alternating types of bricks:

- **Type A bricks** have a "hook" surface (representing the **—COOH** carboxylic acid group).
- **Type B bricks** have a "loop" surface (representing the **—NH₂** amine group).

When the wall is built, every hook locks firmly into the next loop, forming an extremely strong **amide bond**. This hook–loop locking represents the **hydrogen bonding** between adjacent nylon chains. Just as a Velcro-fastened wall is far stronger than a wall held by friction alone, nylon chains are held together not merely by weak van der Waals forces but by a dense network of **inter-chain hydrogen bonds** between the carbonyl oxygen (C=O) of one chain and the amide hydrogen (N—H) of the adjacent chain.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication of Hydrogen Bonding:** This dense hydrogen-bond network is the single most important reason why nylons are **crystalline, high-melting, high-tenacity, and hygroscopic** (water-absorbing). Engineering plastics designers exploit this to make gears, bearings, and textile fibers.

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Commercial Metrics

- **Repeat Unit Mass of Nylon 6,6:** **226.32 g/mol**
- **Repeat Unit Mass of Nylon 6:** **113.16 g/mol**
- **Theoretical Water Loss per Repeat Unit (Nylon 6,6):** **1 mole H₂O (18 g/mol)**
- **Melting Point (Tm):** Nylon 6,6 ≈ **265 °C**; Nylon 6 ≈ **225 °C**
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** Nylon 6,6 ≈ **57 °C**; Nylon 6 ≈ **53 °C**
- **Hydrogen Bond Density:** ~**1 H-bond per 2.5 Å** along the chain axis (highest among commodity polymers)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Classification of Nylons

Nylons are classified along three orthogonal axes that frequently appear in KTU short-answer questions:

### A. By Monomer Origin
1. **AB Type (Homopolyamides):** A single monomer contains both —NH₂ and —COOH (or their cyclic precursor). Example: **Nylon 6** from ε-caprolactam.
2. **AABB Type (Copolyamides):** Two different bifunctional monomers — a diamine and a dicarboxylic acid. Example: **Nylon 6,6** from HMDA + adipic acid.

### B. By Skeletal Structure
1. **Aliphatic (Fatty) Polyamides:** All-carbon backbone — Nylon 6, 6,6, 4,6, 11, 12. Most common.
2. **Aromatic (Aramid) Polyamides:** Phenylene rings in backbone — Kevlar® (poly-p-phenylene terephthalamide) and Nomex®. These are ultra-high-performance fibers, not the focus of the basic syllabus.
3. **Semi-aromatic Polyamides:** Mixed aliphatic-aromatic, e.g., Nylon 6,T.

### C. By Crystalline Behavior
- **Crystalline nylons** (Nylon 6, 6,6) — used as engineering thermoplastics.
- **Amorphous nylons** (Nylon 6,I, Nylon 6,3,T) — used as transparent barrier films.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Formation

The two production pathways are conceptually different but produce structurally similar amide bonds.

### Pathway 1 — Step-Growth Polycondensation (Nylon 6,6)

The exact mechanism follows classical **Carothers' theory (1934)**. The reaction proceeds via nucleophilic acyl substitution:

**Step 1 — Protonation:** The amine lone pair attacks the carbonyl carbon of adipic acid; the carbonyl oxygen is protonated to give a tetrahedral intermediate.

**Step 2 — Proton Transfer:** The —OH₂⁺ group is lost as water; the resulting cation is neutralized by a base or by proton transfer to the original amine.

**Step 3 — Amide Bond Formation:** A new C—N bond is established, giving an amide and releasing H₂O.

### Pathway 2 — Ring-Opening Polymerization (Nylon 6)

ε-Caprolactam is a **7-membered cyclic amide (lactam)**. The reaction is a **ring-opening chain-growth (or pseudo-chain) process** initiated by water or a strong base:

1. **Hydrolysis:** Caprolactam + H₂O → ε-aminocaproic acid.
2. **Propagation Step 1:** The —NH₂ of the amino acid attacks another caprolactam ring (C=O), opening it and transferring the chain end.
3. **Propagation Step 2:** This repeats, transferring the active hydrogen to the next ring, building the linear polyamide chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Distinction:** Nylon 6,6 formation is a **step-growth** polymerization (each step has roughly equal reactivity and the molecular weight builds up slowly), whereas Nylon 6 formation is a **chain-growth** (ring-opening) polymerization (molecular weight builds up rapidly after a long induction period). This distinction is **favourite board exam material**.

## 2.3 Carothers' Equation for Degree of Polymerization

For step-growth polymerization of nylon 6,6, Carothers' equation relates the extent of reaction (p) to the number-average degree of polymerization (Xn):

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

For **stoichiometrically imbalanced** AA + BB systems, where the mole ratio `r = N_A / N_B ≤ 1`:

$$X_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2 r p}$$

At complete conversion (p = 1):

$$X_{n, \max} = \frac{1 + r}{1 - r}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $p$ | Extent of reaction | (moles reacted) / (initial moles) | dimensionless |
| $X_n$ | Number-average DP | $X_n = 1 / (1 - p)$ | dimensionless |
| $r$ | Stoichiometric ratio (AA/BB) | $N_A / N_B$ | dimensionless |
| $\bar{M}_n$ | Number-average molecular weight | $X_n \cdot M_0$ | g/mol |
| $M_0$ | Repeat unit molar mass | Nylon 6,6: 226.32; Nylon 6: 113.16 | g/mol |
| $T_m$ | Melting point | Empirical: $1/T_m = 1/T_m^0 + (R/\Delta H_f)\ln X_A$ | K |
| $T_g$ | Glass transition | Nylon 6,6 ≈ 330 K; Nylon 6 ≈ 326 K | K |
| $E_H$ | H-bond energy per amide | ≈ 8 – 24 kJ/mol in nylon (≈ 20 typical) | kJ/mol |
| $n_w$ | Moles of water lost per repeat unit | 1 (nylon 6,6); 0 (nylon 6 ring-opening) | mol |

> [!WARNING]
> **Math Typing Rule:** In markdown tables, the absolute value `|x|` must be typed as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nylon 6,6 Fibers:** Tire cord, parachute fabric, carpets, seat belts — utilizes the high tenacity and fatigue resistance.
- **Nylon 6 Moldings:** Gears, cams, bearings, zip fasteners, electrical connectors — utilizes the toughness, self-lubrication, and abrasion resistance.
- **Nylon 11 & 12:** Automotive fuel lines, pneumatic tubing — chosen for very low moisture absorption and chemical resistance.
- **Nylon 6,10 & 6,12:** Electrical insulation and brush bristles — chosen for lower density of amide groups and hence lower moisture pickup.

> [!TIP]
> The **degree of crystallinity** in nylon parts can be controlled by **mold temperature** during injection molding. A high mold temperature (≈ 80–120 °C) yields a highly crystalline part with higher strength and better chemical resistance; a low mold temperature gives an amorphous skin with better transparency and ductility. This concept bridges Module 3 with Module 4 (processing) in the KTU syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Nylon 6,6 Polycondensation Mass Balance

### Problem Setup
Synthesize **1000 kg of Nylon 6,6** from **hexamethylenediamine (HMDA, H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂)** and **adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH)** at stoichiometric balance (r = 1) and **p = 0.99** conversion. Determine the actual mass of each monomer required and the mass of water released.

### Step A — Stoichiometric Requirement at r = 1

At stoichiometric balance, the molar ratio of HMDA to adipic acid is **exactly 1:1**. We therefore first compute the moles of repeat unit required in the final polymer.

Let the desired number of repeat units be `n`. Since the target polymer mass `W_p` is 1000 kg:

$$n = \frac{W_p}{M_0} = \frac{1000 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{226.32 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$n = 4418.55 \text{ mol repeat units}$$

### Step B — Conversion Factor for Monomer Excess

The Carothers equation gives:

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.99} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

This means that on average **100 monomer units combine to form 1 polymer chain**, but the **total moles of monomer consumed is much greater** than `n` because at p = 0.99 a small fraction (1%) of monomers remain unreacted.

The total initial moles of each bifunctional monomer that must be charged to the reactor:

$$N_0 = \frac{n \cdot (1 + r)}{2 r p} = \frac{4418.55 \times (1 + 1)}{2 \times 1 \times 0.99}$$

$$N_0 = \frac{8837.10}{1.98} = 4463.18 \text{ mol of each monomer}$$

### Step C — Mass of Each Monomer Charged

- **Molar mass of adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH):** 14 + 2(45) + 4(14) = 146.14 g/mol.
- **Molar mass of HMDA (H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂):** 2(16) + 6(14) + 2(1) = 116.21 g/mol.

$$m_{\text{adipic}} = 4463.18 \times 146.14 = 652.30 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{\text{HMDA}} = 4463.18 \times 116.21 = 518.66 \text{ kg}$$

### Step D — Mass of Water Released

For **r = 1**, every amide bond formed releases **1 mole of water**. The total number of amide bonds formed is essentially `n` (negligible end-group difference at high Xn):

$$m_{H_2O} = n \times 18.015 = 4418.55 \times 18.015$$

$$m_{H_2O} = 79.60 \text{ kg of water}$$

### Step E — Mass Balance Verification

$$m_{\text{adipic}} + m_{\text{HMDA}} = 652.30 + 518.66 = 1170.96 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_p + m_{H_2O} = 1000.00 + 79.60 = 1079.60 \text{ kg}$$

The discrepancy of **~91.36 kg** corresponds to the **1% unreacted monomer** that remains in the reactor at p = 0.99 and is typically removed/recycled industrially.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Nylon 6 Ring-Opening Polymerization Kinetics

The kinetics of ε-caprolactam polymerization is treated as a combination of three reversible reactions, but for the introductory KTU level, the simplified integrated rate expression is sufficient.

### Problem Setup
Initiate the reaction of ε-caprolactam with **2 mol% water** at **250 °C**. The effective rate constant at 250 °C for the chain-growth step is $k_p = 1.2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$. Determine the time required to reach **90% conversion** of monomer.

### Step A — Identify Rate Law
The simplified rate of monomer consumption follows pseudo-first-order kinetics because water concentration is held constant by the hydrolysis equilibrium:

$$-\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p \cdot [M]$$

### Step B — Integrate
Integrate from initial concentration $[M]_0$ to concentration $[M]$ at time t:

$$\int_{[M]_0}^{[M]} \frac{d[M]}{[M]} = -k_p \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{[M]}{[M]_0} \right) = -k_p \cdot t$$

### Step C — Solve for Time at 90% Conversion

The fraction unreacted, $(1 - p)$, is:

$$1 - p = \frac{[M]}{[M]_0} = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

$$\ln(0.10) = -k_p \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.10)}{k_p} = \frac{2.3026}{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$t = 191.88 \text{ seconds} \approx 3.2 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step D — Engineering Interpretation
A mere 3 minutes at 250 °C with 2 mol% water is sufficient to reach 90% conversion — this is why industrial Nylon 6 reactors (VK-tubes, also called "VK columns" for *Vereinfacht Kontinuierlich* — German for "simplified continuous") are typically only **10–15 meters tall** despite high throughputs.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Nylon 6 vs. Nylon 6,6

| Property | Nylon 6 | Nylon 6,6 | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synthesis type | Chain-growth (ring-opening) | Step-growth (condensation) | 7-ring vs. AA+BB |
| Repeat unit mass | **113.16 g/mol** | **226.32 g/mol** | Single vs. dual monomer |
| $T_m$ | 225 °C | 265 °C | More symmetric, denser H-bond |
| H-bond regularity | Even spacing (every 6 C) | Even spacing (every 6 C, but offset) | Both 100% H-bond in crystalline domains |
| Water release | **0** (ring-opening) | **1 mol** per repeat unit | Cyclic vs. condensation |
| Moisture regain | ≈ 9.5 % | ≈ 8.0 % | Nylon 6 has slightly higher amide density |
| Impact strength | Slightly higher | Slightly lower | Nylon 6 has lower crystallinity |
| Molding ease | Easier (lower Tm) | Requires higher melt T | Processability trade-off |
| Price (commercial) | Lower | Higher | Single monomer = lower capex |
| Fiber application | Carpets, apparel | Tire cord, industrial | Higher modulus of Nylon 6,6 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **higher melting point of Nylon 6,6** despite having the same number of CH₂ groups as Nylon 6 is due to the **better packing of chain ends**: in Nylon 6, the amide groups all point the same direction along the chain, causing some strain at the fold, whereas in Nylon 6,6 the amide groups alternate direction, giving a more stable crystal lattice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram: Nylon 6 vs. Nylon 6,6 Production Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Crude Oil Refinery] --> A2[Benzene Extraction]
    A2 --> A3[Cyclohexane]
    A3 --> A4[Oxidation to KA Oil]
    A4 --> A5[Adipic Acid HOOC-CH2-4-COOH]
    A5 --> A6[React with HMDA]
    A7[Ammonia + Butadiene] --> A8[Adiponitrile]
    A8 --> A9[Hexamethylenediamine H2N-CH2-6-NH2]
    A9 --> A6
    A6 --> A10[Nylon 6,6 Salt AABB]
    A10 --> A11[Evaporator]
    A11 --> A12[Autoclave 280C 18 atm]
    A12 --> A13[Polycondensation Reactor]
    A13 --> A14[Finisher Melt at 290C]
    A14 --> A15[Nylon 6,6 Polymer Melt]
    A15 --> A16[Spinneret or Mold]
    A16 --> A17[Fiber or Engineering Part]

    B1[Phenol Hydrogenation] --> B2[Cyclohexanone]
    B2 --> B3[Beckmann Rearrangement with HONH2]
    B3 --> B4[Epsilon-Caprolactam 7-membered ring]
    B4 --> B5[VK Column Initiator water 0.2 mol%]
    B5 --> B6[Ring Opening Polymerization 250C]
    B6 --> B7[Nylon 6 Polymer Melt]
    B7 --> B16

    A17 --> C1[Final Product: Fiber or Plastic Part]
    B7 --> C1
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Hydrogen-Bonded Sheet in Nylon 6,6 Crystal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Chain 1 Direction] -->|parallel| S2[Chain 2 Direction]
    S2 -->|antiparallel| S3[Chain 3 Direction]
    S3 -->|parallel| S4[Chain 4 Direction]
    S1 -. CO H-N H-bond .-> S2
    S2 -. CO H-N H-bond .-> S3
    S3 -. CO H-bond .-> S4
    S1 -->|amide N-H| A1[H-bond donor]
    S2 -->|amide C=O| A2[H-bond acceptor]
    A1 <-->|8 to 24 kJ/mol| A2
    S1 -->|hydrocarbon spacer CH2-4| B1[Flexible Spacer]
    S2 -->|hydrocarbon spacer CH2-4| B1
    B1 -->|crystalline register| C1[Triclinic Unit Cell]
    C1 -->|repeats| D1[Macroscopic Crystallite]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Industrial Nylon 6 VK-Tube

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Caprolactam Melt Feed] --> P2[Preheater 90C]
    P2 --> P3[Top of VK Column 250C]
    P3 --> P4[Ring Opening Initiator Zone]
    P4 --> P5[Polymerization Zone 1 - Falling Film]
    P5 --> P6[Polymerization Zone 2 - Bubbling Bed]
    P6 --> P7[Polymerization Zone 3 - Finishing]
    P7 --> P8[Polymer Discharge Pump]
    P8 --> P9[Extruder and Pelletizer]
    P9 --> P10[Nylon 6 Chips]
    P3 -->|vapor| V1[Water + Monomer Vapor]
    V1 --> V2[Condenser]
    V2 --> V3[Refining Column]
    V3 -->|recycle| P1
    P10 --> P11[Spinning or Injection Molding]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between Nylon 6 and Nylon 6,6 with respect to their monomer systems, polymerization mechanism, and water evolution."**

**Model Answer:**

- **Monomer system:** Nylon 6 is prepared from a **single monomer — ε-caprolactam** (a 7-membered cyclic amide). Nylon 6,6 is prepared from **two monomers — hexamethylenediamine (HMDA) and adipic acid** (AABB system).
- **Polymerization mechanism:** Nylon 6 is synthesized by **ring-opening (chain-growth) polymerization**. Nylon 6,6 is synthesized by **step-growth polycondensation**.
- **Water evolution:** Nylon 6 synthesis **does not release water** because it is a ring-opening process. Nylon 6,6 synthesis **releases 1 mole of water per repeat unit** formed.
- **Molar mass of repeat unit:** Nylon 6 = 113.16 g/mol; Nylon 6,6 = 226.32 g/mol.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Why are nylons highly crystalline in nature? Justify with reference to hydrogen bonding."**

**Model Answer:**
Nylons possess the **amide group —CO—NH—** in every repeat unit. The **N—H** of one chain acts as a strong hydrogen-bond donor to the **C=O** of an adjacent chain. Because this donor-acceptor geometry is **linear and directional**, the chains can pack into **planar sheets** held together by a dense network of hydrogen bonds. This regular, directional secondary bonding forces the chains into ordered crystalline regions, giving nylons their characteristic **high crystallinity (typically 35–55%)**, high melting point, and high tensile strength. The presence of a regular (CH₂)n spacer sequence between amide groups further enhances crystallizability by providing a flexible, non-polar segment that folds into a stable crystal lattice.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks]

**"Discuss the manufacturing, properties, and applications of Nylon 6,6 in detail."**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Manufacturing of Nylon 6,6

The commercial production of Nylon 6,6 proceeds through the following sequence:

**Step 1 — Nylon Salt Preparation:** Adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine (HMDA) are dissolved separately in hot methanol. They are mixed in **exactly 1:1 molar ratio** to form **"Nylon salt"** — a white crystalline adduct (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH · H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂) with a pH of about 7.6. The salt melts at 190 °C and is easy to purify, ensuring perfect stoichiometry.

**Step 2 — Concentration:** The salt solution is evaporated in a stainless-steel vacuum evaporator to about 60–75% solids.

**Step 3 — Polycondensation:** The concentrated salt is charged to a stainless-steel autoclave under a nitrogen blanket. It is heated to **220 °C** at 18 atm pressure, where the salt loses water and condenses. The temperature is then raised to **270–280 °C** to drive the molecular weight to commercially acceptable values (Xn ≈ 100–200, Mn ≈ 20,000–45,000).

**Step 4 — Finishing:** The melt is passed to a "finisher" vessel held at **290 °C** under reduced pressure (50–60 mm Hg) for 30 minutes to push the reaction to the desired molecular weight. The melt is then extruded as a ribbon, cooled, and chipped.

**Step 5 — Spinning (for fibers):** The chips are remelted and extruded through a **spinneret** with hundreds of tiny holes. The filaments are air-cooled, drawn (stretched 4–5×), and wound.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Properties and Applications of Nylon 6,6

**Properties:**
- **Mechanical:** High tensile strength (≈ 80 MPa dry), high modulus, high impact resistance, excellent abrasion resistance. Tensile strength drops by about 30% when wet due to plasticization by absorbed water.
- **Thermal:** $T_m = 265 \text{ °C}$, $T_g = 57 \text{ °C}$. Continuous service temperature up to 150 °C.
- **Chemical:** Excellent resistance to hydrocarbons, oils, and alkalis; poor resistance to strong acids and phenols.
- **Moisture:** Absorbs ≈ 8% moisture at 50% RH — affects dimensional stability and mechanical properties.
- **Electrical:** Good dielectric properties; dissipates static.
- **Flammability:** Self-extinguishing; LOI ≈ 28.

**Applications:**
- **Fibers:** Tire cord (replaced rayon in 1960s), carpets, seat belts, parachutes, conveyor belts, ropes.
- **Moldings:** Gears, cams, bearings, rollers, zip fasteners, power-tool housings, automotive radiator tanks.
- **Films:** Vacuum bags, food packaging (as multilayer with PE for oxygen barrier).
- **Electrical:** Switch housings, terminal blocks, plug connectors.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 14 Marks]

**"Explain the synthesis of Nylon 6 by ring-opening polymerization. Discuss the kinetics and molecular weight control."**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Ring-Opening Synthesis of Nylon 6

**Monomer:** ε-Caprolactam, a 7-membered cyclic amide with melting point 69 °C, produced from cyclohexanone via Beckmann rearrangement.

**Mechanism:** The reaction is initiated by **water or a strong base**. The overall process combines three equilibria:
1. Ring opening of caprolactam with water → ε-aminocaproic acid.
2. Polycondensation of amino acid → polyamide + water.
3. Polyaddition of caprolactam to amine chain ends (chain-growth).

**Process Steps:**

**Step 1 — Melting and charging:** Caprolactam flakes are melted at 80–90 °C and mixed with **0.2–2 mol% water** (initiator) and a small amount of acetic acid (chain stopper for molecular weight control).

**Step 2 — Polymerization:** The mixture is fed to the top of a **VK column** (vertical tube reactor) at **250–270 °C** under a nitrogen blanket. The reaction takes 12–24 hours of residence time.

**Step 3 — Devolatilization:** The polymer melt is passed through a thin-film evaporator at 270 °C and 10–20 mbar to remove unreacted monomer (typically 8–10%) and water. The recovered monomer is recycled.

**Step 4 — Pelletizing:** The melt is extruded, cooled in water, and cut into cylindrical chips.

**Step 5 — Leaching (optional):** For textile-grade fibers, chips are leached in hot water at 95 °C to extract residual monomer to below 0.5%.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Kinetics and Molecular Weight Control

**Kinetics:** The simplified kinetic model is **pseudo-first-order** in monomer concentration:

$$-\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p \cdot [M]$$

The integrated form yields:

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{[M]/[M]_0}$$

The observed activation energy is $E_a ≈ 85 \text{ kJ/mol}$, so a 10 °C rise in temperature approximately doubles the rate.

**Molecular Weight Control:** The number-average molecular weight at equilibrium is governed by the **monomer-to-initiator (water) ratio** and the **chain-stopper** concentration:

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{[M]_0 - [M]_e}{[C]_0 + [H_2O]_0 - [H_2O]_e}$$

where $[C]_0$ is the initial chain-stopper (e.g., acetic acid) concentration. Typical commercial Nylon 6 has $\bar{M}_n$ of **18,000–35,000 g/mol**.

**Equilibrium monomer concentration:** $[M]_e$ at 250 °C is about **0.08–0.10 mol fraction** — this is why devolatilization is essential.

**Effect of additives:**
- **Acetic acid (0.5–1 mol%):** Caps chain ends with —COCH₃ groups, limiting DP to ≈ 150–200.
- **Excess water:** Pushes equilibrium back, lowers DP.
- **Reduced water:** Higher DP but risk of thermal degradation at > 280 °C.
- **Branching agents (trimesic acid):** Introduce branches for impact-modified grades.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing chain-growth vs. step-growth:** Many students incorrectly call Nylon 6 "step-growth." It is **ring-opening chain-growth**.
> 2. **Forgetting water release:** Nylon 6,6 releases water, Nylon 6 does **not**.
> 3. **Ignoring stoichiometry:** Always mention that adipic acid and HMDA are mixed in 1:1 ratio to form the "nylon salt."
> 4. **Skipping kinetics:** Even in descriptive questions, citing Carothers' equation ($X_n = 1/(1-p)$) and the $r$-ratio earns extra credit.
> 5. **Not mentioning H-bonding:** Nylon's properties cannot be justified without H-bonding as the structural cause.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nylons = polyamides** with **—CO—NH—** linkage; invented by **Carothers (1935)**, commercialized **1939**.
- **Naming convention:** Single number = C atoms in one amino-acid monomer; Two numbers = C atoms in diamine and diacid respectively.
- **Nylon 6** = ring-opening polymerization of ε-caprolactam — **chain-growth, no water release, repeat unit 113.16 g/mol, $T_m$ = 225 °C**.
- **Nylon 6,6** = step-growth polycondensation of HMDA + adipic acid — **AABB type, releases 1 mol H₂O per repeat, repeat unit 226.32 g/mol, $T_m$ = 265 °C**.
- **Carothers' equation:** $X_n = 1/(1-p)$; with imbalance: $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$.
- **Hydrogen bonding** between C=O and N—H of adjacent chains is the **primary cause** of crystallinity, high $T_m$, and high strength.
- **Equilibrium monomer** in Nylon 6 is ~8–10%, requiring devolatilization.
- **Moisture absorption** ≈ 8–9% causes plasticization, lowering modulus and $T_g$.
- **Major applications:** Tire cord (6,6), carpets (6), gears and bearings (6 & 6,6), fuel lines (11, 12), aramids (Kevlar) for ballistic armor.
- **Nylon salt** is a 1:1 adduct of diamine and diacid, used to ensure perfect stoichiometry in Nylon 6,6 synthesis.
- **VK column** is the standard industrial reactor for Nylon 6 ring-opening polymerization.
- **Higher mold temperature** in injection molding → higher crystallinity → better mechanical properties.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polycarbonate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polycarbonate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polycarbonate (PC)** is a linear, amorphous, aromatic thermoplastic polymer whose repeat unit contains the **carbonate group (-O-(C=O)-O-)** linked between aromatic diol moieties. The industrially dominant grade is **Bisphenol-A Polycarbonate (BPA-PC)**, where the carbonate group bridges two *p*-substituted 2,2-diphenylpropane units. Its repeat unit is:

$$-\!\left[\,\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\overset{\displaystyle O}{\overset{\|}{\text{C}}}-\right]_n-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Polycarbonate belongs to the family of **engineering thermoplastics** and is the textbook example of a polymer prepared by **step-growth (condensation) polymerization** where a diol is reacted with a derivative of carbonic acid (phosgene or diphenyl carbonate).

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (Syllabus Relevance):** Hermann Schnell at Bayer (Germany, 1953) and Daniel Fox at General Electric (USA, 1955) independently invented commercial polycarbonate. This dual discovery explains the existence of two parallel industrial processes — the **phosgene route (interfacial)** and the **transesterification route (melt)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **train track made of LEGO bricks**. Each LEGO brick is a *bisphenol-A unit* (a strong, rigid block with two phenyl rings). Between every brick sits a **flexible, small carbonate coupler (-O-CO-O-)** — like a soft rubber hinge. The result: a chain that is **mostly rigid** (because the phenyl rings resist bending) but **just flexible enough** at the carbonate linkages to absorb sudden blows without shattering. This is exactly why a PC sheet can be hammered without breaking, yet is glass-clear like a window.

- The **phenyl rings** → provide **rigidity, transparency, and high Tg**.
- The **C(CH₃)₂ group** → provides **internal mobility** (no crystallinity).
- The **carbonate group** → provides **toughness and impact absorption**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of Polycarbonate as **"transparent steel for plastics"** — a very common industrial nickname that captures its twin virtues of optical clarity and mechanical toughness.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics of BPA-Polycarbonate

| Property | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Density (ρ) | **1.20** | g/cm³ |
| Glass Transition Temp (Tg) | **145 – 150** | °C |
| Heat Deflection Temp (HDT @ 1.82 MPa) | **130 – 140** | °C |
| Refractive Index (nD) | **1.586** | – |
| Light Transmittance | **~ 90** | % |
| Notched Izod Impact | **600 – 850** | J/m |
| Tensile Strength | **55 – 75** | MPa |
| Dielectric Strength | **> 30** | kV/mm |
| Water Absorption (24 h) | **0.15 – 0.35** | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Melt Flow Index (MFI)** of PC is typically low (3–12 g/10 min at 300 °C/1.2 kg), which means PC has a **narrow processing window** and is sensitive to overheating — a frequent viva question.

---

### 1.4 Nomenclature and Types of Polycarbonates

1. **Aromatic Polycarbonates (most common):**
   - **BPA-PC** (Bisphenol-A based) — 95% of world production.
   - **BPZ-PC** (Bisphenol-Z based) — higher Tg, used in optics.
2. **Aliphatic Polycarbonates:**
   - Poly(ethylene carbonate), Poly(propylene carbonate) — biodegradable, low Tg.
3. **Copolycarbonates** — for tailored Tg and flow (e.g., BPA + BPZ, or BPA + isosorbide).

For KTU Module 3 focus, we study **BPA-PC** in detail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Monomer Preparation: Bisphenol-A (BPA)

**Bisphenol-A** is the universal diol monomer for aromatic polycarbonates. It is synthesized by the **acid-catalyzed condensation of phenol with acetone** in the presence of HCl or an ion-exchange resin.

#### 2.1.1 Chemical Reaction

$$\text{2 C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{HCl / H}^+ \text{ or ion-exchange resin}} \text{HO-C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Mechanism Highlights:**
- Phenol first attacks the protonated carbonyl of acetone → carbinol intermediate.
- A second phenol attacks the benzylic carbocation.
- Dehydration gives BPA.
- An **excess of phenol** is used (phenol : acetone ≈ 4 : 1) to suppress tris-phenol formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Purity Requirement:** The BPA must be > 99.5% pure, with strict limits on *o,p*-isomer content (< 0.05%) because impurities drastically lower Tg and degrade color stability.

---

### 2.2 Polymerization Routes for Polycarbonate

There are **four industrially important** methods. Each is examinable.

#### Route A — Interfacial Polycondensation (Phosgene Process)

- Reactants: **Bisodium salt of BPA + Phosgene (COCl₂)**.
- Solvents: Aqueous NaOH (aq. phase) + CH₂Cl₂ (org. phase).
- Catalyst: **Triethylamine (Et₃N)** or quaternary ammonium salts (phase-transfer catalysts).
- Temperature: **20 – 40 °C**.
- Polymer forms at the **aqueous-organic interface** as a white film.

$$\text{NaO-C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{ONa} + \text{COCl}_2 \longrightarrow \left[-\text{O-C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O-CO}-\right]_n + 2n\,\text{NaCl}$$

**Advantages:**
- Ambient temperature, low energy.
- Very high molecular weight (M̄n up to **200 000 g/mol**).
- Direct isolation by steam precipitation of the organic phase.

**Disadvantages:**
- Uses **highly toxic phosgene**.
- Methylene chloride is a regulated VOC.

#### Route B — Melt Transesterification (Non-Phosgene, Current Bayer/Covestro process)

- Reactants: **BPA + Diphenyl carbonate (DPC)**.
- Catalyst: **Lithium hydroxide (LiOH)** or sodium / Caesium acetate (10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁴ mol/mol BPA).
- Stages:
  1. **Oligomerization at 180 – 220 °C**, reduced pressure → phenol distilled out.
  2. **Polycondensation at 250 – 300 °C**, high vacuum (< 1 mbar) → further phenol removal, build-up of M̄n.
- Final M̄n ≈ **25 000 – 35 000 g/mol** (lower than interfacial route).

$$n\,\text{HO-R-OH} + n\,\text{(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O)}_2\text{CO} \longrightarrow \left[-\text{O-R-O-CO}-\right]_n + 2n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Compare phosgene and non-phosgene routes."* Memorise: **Interfacial = high MW, ambient T, toxic; Melt = solvent-free, low MW, high T, modern.**

#### Route C — Solution Polymerization
BPA + phosgene in pyridine (acts as both solvent and HCl scavenger). Outdated for commercial PC; used in laboratory scale.

#### Route D — Solid-State Polymerization
Pre-formed PC oligomer chips are heated **below Tg (180 – 220 °C)** under N₂ for several hours. Increases M̄n and improves end-group removal. Used for **optical-grade PC** (CD/DVD substrates).

---

### 2.3 Structure–Property Relationships (KTU High-Yield)

| Structural Feature | Resulting Property | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Rigid phenyl rings in backbone | High Tg (145 °C), high modulus | Dimensional stability at elevated temperature |
| Amorphous, irregular structure | Transparency | Optical applications |
| Polar carbonate group + phenyl rings | Strong dipole-dipole interactions | High impact strength, high tensile |
| Bulky C(CH₃)₂ group | Prevents close packing | Amorphous, transparent |
| Aromatic backbone | UV sensitivity | Requires UV stabilizers outdoors |
| Ester (carbonate) linkage | Hydrolyzable by strong base | Poor alkali resistance |
| Absence of crystallinity | No sharp melting point | Wide thermoforming window |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| # | Equation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carothers: $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Step-growth degree of polymerization |
| 2 | $\overline{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n$ | Number-average molecular weight |
| 3 | Flory–Stockmayer: $\overline{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | Weight-average DP for AA+BB systems |
| 4 | PDI $= \dfrac{\overline{X}_w}{\overline{X}_n} = 1 + p$ | Polydispersity index |
| 5 | $T_g(\text{blend}) = \dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ (Fox eq.) | Tg of miscible PC blends |
| 6 | Mark–Houwink: $[\eta] = K M^a$ with $K=0.012,\ a=0.82$ for PC in CH₂Cl₂ at 25 °C | Viscosity-average MW |
| 7 | Fox–Flory: $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Effect of M̄n on Tg |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{\text{process}} = \Delta H_{\text{poly}} = -n \cdot \Delta H_{\text{bond}}$ | Heat of polymerization estimate |

> [!CAUTION]
> **For PC, $\overline{X}_n$ must be > 100** to obtain useful mechanical strength. This is the same threshold as in Nylon-6,6, and is a standard KTU follow-up question.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Optical media**: The 1.586 refractive index and < 1 % birefringence make PC the substrate of choice for **CD, DVD, Blu-ray discs** (12 cm disc, 1.2 mm thick, moulded in 4 s).
- **Automotive**: Headlamp lenses (replaced glass in 1990s, weight saving ≈ 50 %).
- **Aerospace**: Aircraft cabin windows (e.g., Airbus A350 — stretched PC).
- **Electronics**: Mobile phone housings, connectors, capacitor films.
- **Medical**: Hemodialysers, surgical instrument housings (must be sterilizable — PC withstands γ-radiation and EtO).
- **Glazing**: Riot shields, police visors, machine guards.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Carothers Equation for Polycarbonate Step-Growth

Polycarbonate synthesis via interfacial or melt route is a **classic A–B + A–B (or A–A + B–B) step-growth polymerization**. The relationship between conversion (p) and degree of polymerization (X̄n) is derived below in full.

**Assumption (Carothers, 1929):**
- All functional groups are equally reactive.
- No cyclization (valid because BPA is rigid and the carbonate group is planar).
- The system reacts with two complementary functional groups: **phenolate (–O⁻) and carbonyl chloride (–COCl)** OR **phenol (–OH) and phenyl carbonate (–OC₆H₅)**.

**Step 1.** Let $N_0$ = total number of functional groups (–OH = –COCl = $N_0/2$ for AA + BB).
- At conversion $p$, reacted groups = $N_0 p$.
- Unreacted groups = $N_0 (1 - p)$.

**Step 2.** The number of molecules remaining in the system, $N$, equals the number of unreacted end groups divided by 2 (since each molecule has 2 ends):

$$N = \frac{N_0(1-p)}{2}$$

**Step 3.** The total number of molecules originally present (i.e., monomer molecules before reaction) is $N_0/2$. Therefore, the number-average degree of polymerization is:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0/2}{N} = \frac{N_0/2}{N_0(1-p)/2} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

This is the **Carothers equation**, a cornerstone of step-growth kinetics.

**Step 4.** Substitute $M_0$ = molecular weight of the repeat unit (BPA-PC repeat unit = 254.3 g/mol) to obtain:

$$\overline{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n = \frac{254.3}{1-p}$$

**Step 5.** Numerical illustration for PC:

| Conversion p | $\overline{X}_n$ | $\overline{M}_n$ (g/mol) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 2 | 509 | Oligomer — brittle, unusable |
| 0.90 | 10 | 2 543 | Wax-like, low strength |
| 0.95 | 20 | 5 086 | Borderline mechanical use |
| 0.99 | 100 | 25 430 | Engineering plastic grade |
| 0.995 | 200 | 50 860 | Optical-grade PC |
| 0.999 | 1 000 | 254 300 | Ultra-high MW, solution-cast film |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU trap:** For *p = 0.99*, only 1 % of end groups remain unreacted. To remove the last 1 % of phenol, the reactor must be under **< 1 mbar vacuum at 290 °C** — a non-trivial engineering challenge in the melt process.

---

### 3.2 Flory Polydispersity for PC Step-Growth

Following the same statistical argument used by Flory, the probability that a randomly chosen molecule has degree of polymerization $x$ is the probability of finding $x$ reacted units in sequence:

$$P(x) = (1-p) \cdot p^{x-1}$$

This is a **geometric (Schulz–Zimm-type) distribution**. The first moment gives $\overline{X}_n$ and the second moment gives $\overline{X}_w$:

$$\overline{X}_n = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot P(x) = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

$$\overline{X}_w = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot P(x) / \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot P(x) = \frac{1+p}{1-p}$$

Therefore the **polydispersity index** is:

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{X}_w}{\overline{X}_n} = 1 + p$$

For PC at $p = 0.99$, **PDI = 1.99** — very close to the ideal step-growth value of 2.

---

### 3.3 Fox–Flory Equation for the Effect of Molecular Weight on Tg

For low-molecular-weight polymers, $T_g$ depends on $\overline{M}_n$ because the chain ends act as **internal plasticizers**. The Fox–Flory equation:

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$$

For BPA-PC:
- $T_{g,\infty} = 150 \,°C$ (high-MW limit)
- $K = 2.5 \times 10^5 \,\text{K·g/mol}$ (typical value for PC)

**Numerical example — KTU-style problem:**

> *A batch of PC has $\overline{M}_n$ = 20 000 g/mol. Predict its Tg.*

$$T_g = 423\,\text{K} - \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{20\,000} = 423 - 12.5 = 410.5\,\text{K} = 137.5 \,°C$$

This is why **low-MW PC grades** (e.g., injection-moulding optical grade) have a *slightly* lower Tg than high-MW extrusion grades.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Polycarbonate Routes

| Criterion | Interfacial (Phosgene) | Melt (Transesterification) | Solution | Solid-State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Achieved $\overline{M}_n$ (g/mol) | Up to 200 000 | 25 000 – 35 000 | 30 000 – 50 000 | > 50 000 |
| Reaction T (°C) | 20 – 40 | 180 – 300 | 30 – 80 | 180 – 220 |
| Catalyst | Et₃N / PTC | LiOH / NaOAc | Pyridine | None / LiOH |
| Solvent | CH₂Cl₂ + H₂O | None | Pyridine / CH₂Cl₂ | None |
| By-product | NaCl | Phenol | HCl·Py | Phenol |
| Toxicity | High (phosgene) | Low | Medium | Low |
| Process continuity | Batch | Continuous | Batch | Batch |
| Used by (modern) | SABIC (Lexan) | Covestro, Mitsubishi (Eupalon) | Lab scale | Specialty optical |

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks: *Which process is preferred for optical-grade PC and why?* Answer: **Solid-state polymerization**, because it minimizes thermal degradation, gives uniform M̄n, and eliminates gel particles that scatter light.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Polycarbonate vs Other Engineering Plastics

| Property | PC | PMMA | Polysulfone | Nylon-6,6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Repeat-Unit Connectivity (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A [BPA segment]
        A1[Phenyl ring 1]
        A2[C with two methyl groups]
        A3[Phenyl ring 2]
        A1 --- A2 --- A3
    end
    subgraph B [Carbonate bridge]
        B1[O atom left]
        B2[Carbonyl C]
        B3[O atom right]
        B1 --- B2 --- B3
    end
    A1 -- para bond --> B1
    A3 -- para bond --> B3
    A2 -. quaternary C with 2 CH3 .-> A2
    B2 -. double bond to O .-> B2
    noteRight[Repeat unit MW = 254.3 g/mol]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two phenyl rings are connected *para* (1,4) to the central quaternary carbon (which carries two CH₃ groups). Each ring is further linked *para* to a carbonate group (-O-CO-O-). This 1,4-para arrangement is essential for **linear, rigid-rod behaviour**; ortho substitution would prevent polymerisation.

---

### 4.2 Industrial Process Flowchart: Melt Transesterification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Phenol + Acetone] --> R2[Acid-catalyzed condensation 50-70 C]
    R2 --> R3[Bisphenol A crystals]
    R3 --> R4[Diphenyl carbonate DPC synthesis]
    R4 --> P1[Oligomerization stage 180-220 C, vacuum]
    P1 --> P2[Polycondensation stage 250-300 C, high vacuum 1 mbar]
    P2 --> P3[Melt PC extrusion and pelletization]
    P3 --> P4[Solid state polymerization for optical grade]
    P4 --> P5[Optical PC pellets]
    P3 --> P6[General purpose PC pellets]
    P2 -. phenol recycle .-> R1
    P1 -. phenol recycle .-> R1
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Why PC Has High Impact Strength

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[External impact force] --> S2[Stress distribution along rigid aromatic backbone]
    S2 --> S3[Carbonate linkage rotates slightly to absorb energy]
    S3 --> S4[C CH3 2 group provides internal torsional mobility]
    S4 --> S5[Diphenyl propane segment acts as rigid energy-absorbing unit]
    S5 --> S6[Energy dissipated as heat, no crack propagation]
    S6 --> O1[Material remains intact and transparent]
    O1 --> O2[Impact strength 600-850 J per m, 20x higher than PMMA]
```

---

### 4.4 Process Selection Logic (Phosgene vs Non-Phosgene)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Target MW > 50 000 g/mol?}
    Q1 -- yes --> Q2{Tolerate phosgene use?}
    Q1 -- no --> P1[Choose melt transesterification]
    Q2 -- yes --> P2[Choose interfacial phosgene process]
    Q2 -- no --> P3[Choose solid state polymerization upgrade on melt route]
    P2 --> P4[High MW PC for sheet and film]
    P1 --> P5[General purpose moulding grade PC]
    P3 --> P6[Optical grade PC for CD DVD lenses]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"INTERface gives HIGH MW; MELT gives MEDIUM MW; SOLID gives OPTICAL MW."*

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is polycarbonate? Write the repeat-unit structure of Bisphenol-A polycarbonate."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Polycarbonate is a linear, aromatic thermoplastic polymer containing the **carbonate group (-O-CO-O-)** in the repeat unit. The Bisphenol-A grade (BPA-PC) is synthesised by the condensation of **Bisphenol-A** with **phosgene (interfacial route)** or with **diphenyl carbonate (melt route)**.

The repeat unit is:

$$-\!\left[\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{CO}\right]-$$

with repeat-unit molecular weight $M_0 = 254.3 \,\text{g/mol}$.

(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — structure, 1 mark — repeat-unit MW / route).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List four important properties of polycarbonate that make it suitable for engineering applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High impact strength** (600 – 850 J/m, ~ 20× that of PMMA) → riot shields, helmets.
2. **Transparency** (~ 90 % light transmission) → CD/DVD, lenses, aircraft windows.
3. **High Tg (145 °C) and high HDT (130 – 140 °C)** → hot-fill applications, automotive under-bonnet parts.
4. **Good electrical insulation** (dielectric strength > 30 kV/mm) → capacitor films, electrical housings.
5. **Self-extinguishing, UL94 V-2 rated** → electrical enclosures.

(Any 4 properties — 3 marks; ½ mark deducted for missing the engineering-application link).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

## ✦ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q. (a)** Describe the **interfacial polycondensation process** for the manufacture of Bisphenol-A polycarbonate. Discuss the chemistry, role of the catalyst, and operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **melt transesterification process** for polycarbonate manufacture. Compare the two processes with respect to molecular weight achieved, safety, and product purity. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Interfacial Polycondensation (7 marks)

**Chemistry (2 marks):** Bisphenol-A is first dissolved in aqueous NaOH to form the bisodium phenolate:

$$\text{HO-Ar-C(CH}_3)_2\text{-Ar-OH} + 2\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaO-Ar-C(CH}_3)_2\text{-Ar-ONa} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Phosgene gas is passed simultaneously through the methylene chloride layer:

$$\text{NaO-Ar-C(CH}_3)_2\text{-Ar-ONa} + \text{COCl}_2 \rightarrow \left[-\text{O-Ar-C(CH}_3)_2\text{-Ar-O-CO-}\right]_n + 2n\,\text{NaCl}$$

**Role of Catalyst (1 mark):** A phase-transfer catalyst such as **triethylamine (Et₃N)** or **benzyltriethylammonium chloride (BTEAC)** is added in 0.1 – 1.0 mol % to facilitate transfer of the phenolate ion from the aqueous to the organic phase, where the polymerisation occurs at the interface.

**Operating Conditions (2 marks):**
- Aqueous phase: 5 – 10 wt % BPA, 5 – 10 wt % NaOH.
- Organic phase: methylene chloride, BPA : COCl₂ molar ratio ≈ 1 : 1.05.
- Temperature: 20 – 40 °C.
- Agitation: vigorous (Reynolds > 10 000) to maximise interfacial area.
- pH: 12 – 13 (maintained by NaOH feed).

**Mechanism Note (1 mark):** Reaction proceeds via Schotten–Baumann-type acylation; the byproduct NaCl remains in the aqueous phase, simplifying polymer isolation.

**Isolation (1 mark):** The polymer-rich organic layer is washed with dilute HCl and water, then PC is precipitated by steam-stripping the CH₂Cl₂. Result: white powder, $\overline{M}_n$ up to 200 000 g/mol.

---

#### (b) Melt Transesterification (7 marks)

**Chemistry (2 marks):** BPA + diphenyl carbonate (DPC) react via phenolysis:

$$n\,\text{HO-R-OH} + n\,(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O})_2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} \left[-\text{O-R-O-CO-}\right]_n + 2n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

**Process Stages (2 marks):**
1. **Oligomerization** (180 – 220 °C, 50 – 100 mbar): BPA and DPC melt, catalyst (LiOH, 10⁻⁴ mol/mol BPA) initiates transesterification, phenol distilled off.
2. **Polycondensation** (250 – 300 °C, < 1 mbar): vacuum progressively deepened; final M̄n = 25 000 – 35 000 g/mol.
3. Optional **solid-state upgrade** (180 – 220 °C) to optical grade.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Interfacial | Melt Transesterification |
|---|---|---|
| Toxicity | High (phosgene) | Low |
| Solvent | CH₂Cl₂ | None |
| $\overline{M}_n$ | Up to 2 × 10⁵ | 2.5 – 3.5 × 10⁴ |
| Catalyst | Et₃N / PTC | LiOH |
| Product purity | Good, may retain solvent | Excellent |
| Continuous? | Batch | Continuous possible |
| Capex | High (corrosion resistant) | Lower |
| Modern usage | SABIC Lexan | Covestro, Mitsubishi |

---

## ✦ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **structure of Bisphenol-A polycarbonate** and discuss how the structure influences its **optical transparency and impact strength**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **solution and solid-state polymerisation methods** for polycarbonate. Under what circumstances is solid-state polymerisation preferred? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Structure – Property Relationships (7 marks)

**Structure (3 marks):** The repeat unit contains:
- Two *para*-linked **phenyl rings** (rigid, planar, UV-absorbing at 254 nm).
- A central **quaternary carbon C(CH₃)₂** (bulky, disrupts crystallisation).
- An **aromatic carbonate group -O-(C=O)-O-** (polar, rotatable).

The aromatic rings are in conjugation with the carbonate carbonyl, which restricts rotation and contributes to the high Tg.

**Optical Transparency (2 marks):**
- The polymer is **amorphous** because the bulky C(CH₃)₂ group prevents close packing — no crystallites to scatter light.
- The refractive index (1.586) is isotropic.
- Result: ~ 90 % light transmission across the visible spectrum.
- The aromatic carbonate can undergo **photo-Fries rearrangement** under UV, generating yellowing — hence UV stabilisers (benzotriazoles) are added for outdoor grades.

**Impact Strength (2 marks):**
- The aromatic backbone is rigid → high modulus.
- The carbonate linkage is slightly rotatable, allowing small-scale segmental motion that **dissipates impact energy**.
- The C(CH₃)₂ group provides additional torsional mobility.
- This combination gives notched Izod values of **600 – 850 J/m**, about 20× higher than PMMA.
- PC therefore fails in a **ductile manner** (no sharp crack propagation).

---

#### (b) Solution and Solid-State Polymerisation (7 marks)

**Solution Polymerisation (3 marks):**
- BPA + phosgene dissolved in **pyridine** (acts as both solvent and HCl acceptor).
- Temperature 30 – 80 °C; reaction is exothermic, controlled by cooling.
- Product precipitates as a white powder.
- Disadvantage: pyridine is toxic, recovery is energy-intensive, and MW is moderate (M̄n 30 000 – 50 000).
- Used mainly in **laboratory scale** synthesis and in the historical GE process.

**Solid-State Polymerisation (SSP) (3 marks):**
- Pre-formed PC oligomer (M̄n ~ 8 000 – 12 000 g/mol, crystallised/granulated) is heated **below Tg** (180 – 220 °C) in a fluidised bed or rotary dryer under **inert N₂**.
- Phenol continues to diffuse out; chain-ends react; M̄n rises to > 50 000 g/mol.
- Heating time: 8 – 24 h.
- **No thermal degradation** because temperature is below Tg (no melting).
- The process is **slower**, but gives exceptional optical clarity (no gel particles, uniform M̄n).

**When SSP is Preferred (1 mark):**
- For **optical-grade PC** (CD, DVD, Blu-ray substrates, eyeglass lenses).
- When **minimum gel content** is required (laser-readable discs reject any gel particle).
- When the **highest possible purity and lowest yellowness index** is essential.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (mark-losing zones):**
> 1. **Confusing repeat unit with monomer:** The repeat unit MW of BPA-PC is 254.3 g/mol, not the BPA MW (228.3) and not the phosgene MW (99). Examiners deliberately test this.
> 2. **Writing "polycarbonate = thermoplastic" without naming the carbonate group** — at least ½ mark is deducted for an incomplete definition.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of Et₃N / PTC** in the interfacial route — students often list NaOH as the catalyst, but NaOH is the **acid scavenger**, not the catalyst.
> 4. **Melt route temperature error:** Students frequently write 300 – 350 °C, but the actual upper limit is **300 °C** to avoid Fries rearrangement and yellowing.
> 5. **No mention of the Carothers equation** when discussing degree of polymerisation — the *p* = 0.99 → X̄n = 100 fact is a KTU favourite.
> 6. **Spelling "Bisphenol" as "Bisfenol"** or "Phosgene" as "Fosgene" — automatic half-mark cut.
> 7. **In structure-property questions**, always link the property to the structural feature; merely listing properties without structural justification loses 50 % of the marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polycarbonate (PC)** is a **linear, amorphous, aromatic thermoplastic** whose repeat unit contains the **carbonate group -O-(C=O)-O-** linking two *p*-substituted bisphenol units.
- The dominant commercial grade is **BPA-PC**, $M_0 = 254.3 \,\text{g/mol}$.
- **Tg ≈ 145 – 150 °C**, density 1.20 g/cm³, refractive index 1.586, light transmittance ~ 90 %.
- **BPA** is made from **phenol + acetone (acid-catalysed)**; *o,p*-isomer impurity must be < 0.05 %.
- **Two main industrial routes:**
  - *Interfacial (phosgene)* → M̄n up to 2 × 10⁵, ambient T, toxic.
  - *Melt (DPC transesterification)* → M̄n 2.5–3.5 × 10⁴, 250 – 300 °C, safe, continuous.
- **Minor routes:** solution (pyridine, lab scale); solid-state (post-upgrade for optical PC).
- **Carothers equation:** $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$; for *p* = 0.99, $\overline{X}_n = 100$ and PDI ≈ 2.
- **PDI = 1 + p** (Flory) for step-growth polymerisation of PC.
- **Fox–Flory:** $T_g$ falls as M̄n decreases (end-group plasticisation).
- **Applications:** optical discs, headlamp lenses, safety glasses, riot shields, medical devices, aircraft canopies, capacitor films, mobile-phone housings.
- **Limitations:** poor alkali resistance (carbonate hydrolysis), UV-yellowing, notch-sensitive under prolonged stress (solvated stress-cracking).
- **Mnemonic — "INTERface HIGH, MELT MEDIUM, SOLID OPTICAL"** for MW achieved by each route.
- **Always mention** Carothers equation, repeat-unit MW = 254.3 g/mol, and Tg ≈ 145 °C in any PC answer — these are the three most-tested KTU facts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyurethanes. Thermosetting Plastics - Epoxy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polyurethanes. Thermosetting Plastics - Epoxy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polyurethanes (PUR / PU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Polyurethanes are a class of heterochain step-growth polymers containing the characteristic carbamate (urethane) linkage (–NH–CO–O–) in the repeating backbone, formed by the addition reaction between di- or polyfunctional isocyanates and polyols containing active hydrogen atoms.*

The characteristic reaction responsible for polyurethane formation is the **isocyanate-polyol addition (step-growth polyaddition)**:

$$R-N=C=O + R'-OH \longrightarrow R-NH-CO-O-R'$$

The polymer can be **thermoplastic or thermoset** depending on the functionality of monomers used.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Velcro"

Imagine building a chain-link fence where every alternate link is a hook (isocyanate) and every other link is an eyelet (polyol). When hooks snap into eyelets, they form the **urethane linkage**. By using hooks and eyelets with **more than two ends**, you create a 3-D network (rigid foam or thermoset PU). With exactly two ends on each, you get a flexible linear chain (thermoplastic elastomer or flexible foam). The "blowing agent" (water) acts like steam rising from a boiling pot — it puffs the mass into foam before it solidifies.

### 🔑 Key Building Blocks

| Component | Role | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Diisocyanate** | Hard segment | TDI, MDI, HDI, IPDI |
| **Polyol** | Soft segment | Polyether (PPG, PEG), Polyester (adipate) |
| **Chain Extender** | Bridge hard–soft segments | 1,4-Butanediol, Ethylenediamine |
| **Blowing Agent** | Generates gas for foam | Water, pentane, HFCs |
| **Catalyst** | Accelerates gelling/foaming | Tertiary amines, Tin (Sn) carboxylates |
| **Surfactant** | Stabilises cell structure | Silicone–polyether copolymers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard segment (HS)** = isocyanate + chain extender → rigid, polar, H-bonded domains.
> **Soft segment (SS)** = polyol → flexible, rubbery, low-Tg matrix.
> Microphase separation between HS and SS is the origin of the remarkable mechanical properties of PU elastomers.

---

## 1.2 Epoxy Resins (Thermosetting Plastics)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Epoxy resins are low-molecular-weight prepolymers containing at least one terminal epoxide (oxirane) ring per molecule, which, on reaction with a suitable curing agent (hardener), undergo ring-opening crosslinking to form a densely networked, infusible, three-dimensional thermoset.*

The simplest epoxide ring has the structure:

$$\underset{\text{oxirane ring}}{\begin{array}{c}\overset{\displaystyle O}{\overbrace{\diagup\!\!\!\!\!\text{C—C}\!\!\!\!\!\diagdown}}\\\diagdown\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\diagup\\\text{H}\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\text{H}\end{array}}$$

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Handed Handshake"

The oxirane ring is a strained three-membered ring with one **eager-to-react carbon** in each "hand" of the molecule. The hardener (say an amine) brings its own hydrogen hands. When the resin's strained rings meet the hardener's active hydrogens, **each handshake (C–N bond) is permanent** — they cannot pull apart without breaking. With many bifunctional hands on each molecule, you rapidly build a tangled 3-D molecular "knot" — the cured thermoset.

### 🔑 Commercial Standard: DGEBA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DGEBA** = **Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A** — the workhorse of the epoxy industry (>75 % of global epoxy production).
> Epoxy equivalent weight (**EEW**) = grams of resin containing 1 mole of epoxide groups. It is the single most cited quality parameter for DGEBA, with commercial grades typically in the range **170–190 g/eq**.

---

## 1.3 High-Yield Quick Snapshot

| Property | Polyurethane | Epoxy (cured DGEBA) |
|---|---|---|
| Polymer class | Step-growth heterochain | Step-growth thermoset |
| Functional group | Urethane (–NH–CO–O–) | Ether + hydroxyl |
| Cure mechanism | Addition (no by-product) | Ring-opening addition |
| Density (g/cm³) | **0.02 – 1.2** (foam → solid) | **1.1 – 1.4** |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 20 – 80 | 35 – 100 |
| Glass transition Tg | -50 to +150 °C | 80 – 200 °C |
| Major applications | Foams, elastomers, coatings, adhesives | Coatings, composites, electronics |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Polyurethane Formation

### 2.1.1 Primary Urethane Linkage Formation

The classical **isocyanate–hydroxyl** reaction produces the urethane (carbamate) group:

$$R-N=C=O + HO-R' \longrightarrow R-NH-\overset{\displaystyle O}{\underset{\displaystyle \|}{C}}-O-R'$$

This is a **nucleophilic addition** of the –OH oxygen to the electrophilic carbon of –N=C=O. Hydrogen shifts from O (alcohol) to N (isocyanate).

### 2.1.2 Secondary Reactions Used in Foam Manufacture

**(a) Isocyanate–Water Reaction (CO₂ Generation — "Chemical Blowing")**

$$R-N=C=O + H_2O \longrightarrow [R-NH-COOH] \longrightarrow R-NH_2 + CO_2\uparrow$$

The unstable carbamic acid spontaneously decarboxylates, releasing **carbon dioxide gas** that physically blows the polymerising mass into foam. The amine formed immediately reacts with another isocyanate, forming a **disubstituted urea** linkage:

$$R-NH_2 + R'-N=C=O \longrightarrow R-NH-CO-NH-R'$$

**(b) Isocyanate–Amine (Urea Formation)**

The above urea reaction contributes to the hard-segment content and increases rigidity, crosslink density and Tg.

**(c) Trimerisation of Isocyanate (Isocyanurate)**

$$3\ R-N=C=O \longrightarrow \underset{\text{isocyanurate ring}}{R-N\overset{\displaystyle \underset{\displaystyle C}{\overset{\displaystyle O}{\diagup\!\!\diagdown}}\!\!\!\!\!\diagup\!\!\diagdown}{C}\!\!\!\!\diagup\!\!\!\diagdown\!\!\!\!\diagdown\!\!\!\diagup}N-R$$

This isocyanurate ring is highly thermally stable and flame-retardant — it forms the basis of **rigid PIR foams**.

### 2.1.3 Catalysis Mechanism

- **Tertiary amine catalysts** (DABCO, DMEA): selectively accelerate the **isocyanate–water (blowing)** reaction.
- **Organotin catalysts** (dibutyltin dilaurate, stannous octoate): selectively accelerate the **isocyanate–polyol (gelling)** reaction.

The proper balance of these two catalyst families (called the **blow-to-gel ratio**) is the art of PU foam manufacture. Wrong ratio → foam collapse or shrinkage.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Polyurethanes

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol/Unit Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number-average DP (Carothers, equimolar) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = extent of reaction |
| 2 | $\bar{M}_n$ of step-growth polymer | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{M_0}{1-p}$ | $M_0$ = average monomer mass |
| 3 | Isocyanate Index | $I = \dfrac{N_{NCO,actual}}{N_{NCO,stoich}} \times 100\ \%$ | $I \approx 105$ typical for foam |
| 4 | OH value of polyol | $OH\# = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{M_n}$ | mg KOH per g polyol |
| 5 | Equivalent weight of isocyanate | $EW_{NCO} = \dfrac{42.0 \times 1000}{\%NCO}$ | 42 = M(NCO), in g/eq |
| 6 | Foam density | $\rho_f = \dfrac{m_{polymer}}}{V_{foam}}$ | g/cm³ |
| 7 | Hard-segment weight fraction | $w_{HS} = \dfrac{m_{diiso} + m_{extender}}}{m_{total}}} \times 100\ \%$ | dictates modulus |
| 8 | % NCO in isocyanate | $\%NCO = \dfrac{42.0 \times n}{M_{iso}}} \times 100$ | $n$ = no. of NCO groups |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Isocyanate Index $I$** is the single most important formulation variable — the polymer technologist controls every property through it. When $I < 100$, soft thermoplastic PUR dominates; when $I > 100$, allophanate/biuret crosslinks form → thermoset PUR.

---

## 2.3 Chemistry of Epoxy Resins

### 2.3.1 Synthesis of DGEBA (Bisphenol-A + Epichlorohydrin)

The classical synthesis is a **two-step Williamson etherification / dehydrohalogenation**:

**Step 1 — Alkoxide formation**

$$HO-C_6H_4-C(CH_3)_2-C_6H_4-OH + 2\ NaOH \longrightarrow NaO-C_6H_4-C(CH_3)_2-C_6H_4-ONa + 2\ H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Ring-opening addition to epichlorohydrin**

$$NaO-Ar + Cl-CH_2-\underset{\overline{O}}{\overset{\diagup\;\;\diagdown}{CH}-CH_2} \longrightarrow NaO-Ar-CH_2-CH(OH)-CH_2-Cl$$

**Step 3 — Intramolecular epoxide ring closure (dehydrohalogenation)**

$$-CH(OH)-CH_2-Cl + NaOH \longrightarrow \underset{\text{epoxide}}{-CH-CH_2 \atop \diagup\;\;\diagdown \atop O} + NaCl + H_2O$$

Repeating this sequence with the second phenolic OH gives the diglycidyl ether — **DGEBA**, $n = 0$ monomer. For $n \geq 1$, higher-molecular-weight homologues form:

$$\underset{\text{DGEBA, }n=0,1,2,\dots}{CH_2-CH-CH_2-O-[C_6H_4-C(CH_3)_2-C_6H_4-O]_n-CH_2-CH(OH)-CH_2-O-[C_6H_4-C(CH_3)_2-C_6H_4-O]_n-CH_2-CH-CH_2\atop \diagup\;\;\;\diagdown\atop \overline{O}\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\diagup\;\;\;\;\;\;\diagdown\atop \overline{O}}$$

### 2.3.2 Curing of Epoxy with Amines

Primary amine (e.g., DETA = diethylenetriamine) — **two H's** per N open the epoxide:

$$\underset{\text{epoxide}}{-CH(O)CH_2-} + H_2N-R \longrightarrow -CH(OH)-CH_2-NH-R$$

The newly formed secondary amine further reacts with another epoxide:

$$-CH(OH)-CH_2-NH-R + \underset{\text{epoxide}}{-CH(O)CH_2-} \longrightarrow -CH(OH)-CH_2-N(R)-CH_2-CH(OH)-$$

A **tertiary amine** is now formed — and the network is **fully crosslinked**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each **primary amine H** consumes **one epoxide ring**. Each **primary amine NH₂** thus consumes **2 epoxides**; each **NH** (secondary) consumes **1**. The stoichiometric hardener quantity = 100 × (eq. wt. of resin) / (eq. wt. of hardener).

### 2.3.3 Other Hardener Families

| Hardener Type | Cure T (°C) | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aliphatic polyamine (DETA, TETA) | RT to 80 | Fast, RT-cure | Civil adhesives, civil coatings |
| Aromatic amine (MDA, DDS) | 150 – 200 | High Tg, chemical resistant | Aerospace composites |
| Anhydride (phthalic, nadic) | 150 – 220 | Long pot life, high Tg | Electrical castings |
| Polyamide | RT | Flexible, moisture-tolerant | Marine coatings |
| Dicyandiamide (DICY) | 150 – 180 | Latent one-pack | Powder coatings, prepregs |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Epoxy

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Epoxy Equivalent Weight | $EEW = \dfrac{M_{resin}}{n_{epoxide}}$ | g/eq |
| 2 | Epoxy Value | $EV = \dfrac{1}{EEW}$ | eq/g |
| 3 | Theoretical Hardener Demand | $H_{phr} = \dfrac{100 \times EEW}{EW_{hardener}}$ | parts per hundred resin |
| 4 | Functionality average | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{\Sigma n_i f_i}{\Sigma n_i}$ | for resin + hardener mix |
| 5 | Carothers gel point | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$ | for $A_f + B_f$ system |
| 6 | Volume shrinkage on cure | $\%\Delta V = \dfrac{\rho_{liq}-\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{liq}} \times 100$ | epoxy typically 2 – 5 % |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Carothers Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation for PUR Polyaddition

The Carothers equation is the foundation for designing the molecular weight of any step-growth polymer — including polyurethanes. We derive it explicitly.

### Step 1 — Define extent of reaction $p$

Let $N_0$ = initial total number of functional groups (–NCO + –OH combined, assuming stoichiometric feed).

Let $N$ = number of unreacted functional groups remaining at time $t$.

$$p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = N_0(1-p)$$

### Step 2 — Number of molecules present

Each reaction event (one NCO + one OH) reduces the total count of molecules by exactly **one** (two molecules merge into one). The number of reactions that have occurred = $N_0 - N = N_0 p$.

Therefore the total number of molecules currently in the system is:

$$M_{tot} = M_0 - N_0 p$$

where $M_0$ = initial number of molecules (monomers + oligomers).

### Step 3 — Definition of $\bar{X}_n$

The number-average degree of polymerisation is the average number of structural repeat units per molecule:

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{initial total structural units}}{\text{number of molecules now present}} = \dfrac{M_0}{M_0 - N_0 p}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $M_0$:

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{N_0 p}{M_0}}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce stoichiometric ratio $r$

If the two bifunctional monomers are mixed in stoichiometric ratio $r$ ($r \le 1$):

$$r = \dfrac{N_{OH,0}}{N_{NCO,0}}$$

Then $N_0 = N_{NCO,0}(1 + r)$ and the limit $M_0 \to N_{NCO,0}$ (each monomer contributes one molecule). Hence:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}\;}$$

For exact stoichiometry, $r = 1$:

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{2}{2(1-p)} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$$

### Step 5 — Number-average molecular weight

$$\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \times M_0'$$

where $M_0'$ = average molar mass per structural unit (e.g., for the [–O–R–O–CO–NH–R′–NH–CO–] repeat unit).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** To get $\bar{M}_n = 30\,000$ g/mol with $M_0' = 250$ g/mol, you need $p = 0.9917$ — i.e., **99.17 % conversion**. This is precisely why step-growth polymers require exceptional monomer purity and slow, careful processing — every 1 % loss of conversion halves $\bar{M}_n$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Polyurethane

A polyurethane is synthesised from **MDI** ($M = 250$ g/mol, 2 NCO groups) and **1,4-butanediol** ($M = 90$ g/mol, 2 OH groups). Stoichiometric ratio $r = 1$. If the final reaction extent is $p = 0.98$, compute $\bar{M}_n$.

**Solution:**

Repeat-unit mass:

$$M_0' = \dfrac{M_{MDI} + M_{BD}}{2} = \dfrac{250 + 90}{2} = 170\ \text{g/mol}$$

Compute $\bar{X}_n$:

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.98} = \dfrac{1}{0.02} = 50$$

Compute $\bar{M}_n$:

$$\bar{M}_n = 50 \times 170 = 8500\ \text{g/mol}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Epoxy Curing Stoichiometry

**Problem:** A DGEBA resin has $EEW = 185$ g/eq. It is to be cured with **DETA** ($M = 146$ g/mol, 5 active H's per molecule — i.e., 2 primary NH₂ × 2 + 1 secondary NH = 5 active H's). Compute the theoretical stoichiometric phr of DETA.

**Step 1 — Equivalent weight of DETA:**

$$EW_{DETA} = \dfrac{M}{\text{number of active H}} = \dfrac{146}{5} = 29.2\ \text{g/eq}$$

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometric formula:**

$$H_{phr} = \dfrac{100 \times EEW_{resin}}{EW_{hardener}} = \dfrac{100 \times 185}{29.2}$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$H_{phr} = \dfrac{18500}{29.2} \approx 633.6$$

Wait — re-check. Each **gram-equivalent** of resin requires one gram-equivalent of hardener. So:

$$\dfrac{100\ \text{g resin}}{185\ \text{g/eq}} = 0.5405\ \text{eq resin} \rightarrow 0.5405\ \text{eq hardener}$$

$$\text{Mass of DETA} = 0.5405 \times 29.2 = 15.78\ \text{g}$$

$$\boxed{H_{phr} \approx 15.8\ \text{phr DETA}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PU vs Epoxy

| Criterion | Polyurethane | Epoxy (DGEBA) |
|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit | –NH–CO–O– | –CH(OH)–CH₂–O–Ar– |
| Polymerisation type | Step-growth polyaddition | Step-growth polyaddition |
| Cure by-product | None (water consumed → CO₂ in foams) | None |
| Crosslink control | NCO index + functionality | Hardener type + functionality |
| Tg range | -50 °C to +150 °C | +80 °C to +200 °C |
| Foaming capability | Native (water/isocyanate reaction) | None inherently |
| Adhesion strength | High | Very high (benchmark) |
| Chemical resistance | Moderate | Excellent |
| UV resistance | Poor (aromatic) → Good (aliphatic) | Moderate (chalking) |
| Toughness | Elastomeric options very tough | Brittle unless toughened |
| Reformulation flexibility | Extremely wide (foam ↔ elastomer ↔ rigid) | Narrower |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Polyurethane Foam Manufacturing Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Storage\nDiisocyanate + Polyol] --> B[Pre-blending\nPolyol + Blowing Agent + Catalyst + Surfactant]
    A --> C[Isocyanate Tank\nMDI or TDI]
    B --> D[High-pressure\nMixing Head]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Pour / Inject\ninto Mould]
    E --> F[Reaction Step\nGelling and Blowing]
    F --> G[Polymerisation\nNetwork Build-up]
    G --> H[Curing Oven\nPost-cure 80 to 120 C]
    H --> I[Demoulding\nTrimming]
    I --> J[Finished Foam\nSlabstock or Moulded]

    style A fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1a4f80
    style B fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#806000
    style C fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#800000
    style D fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6b2c
    style J fill:#e0c3ff,stroke:#4b1e80
```

> **Reading guide:** The mix-head combines two streams that react on contact — the entire foam structure forms within 30 – 120 seconds.

---

## 4.2 DGEBA Synthesis Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bisphenol A\nBPA, solid flakes] --> C[Reactor Vessel\nwith NaOH]
    B[Epichlorohydrin\nECH, liquid] --> C
    C --> D[Step 1\nAlkoxide Attack]
    D --> E[Step 2\nRing Opening + Cl Release]
    E --> F[Step 3\nDehydrohalogenation\nNaOH]
    F --> G[Wash and\nPhase Separation]
    G --> H[Strip Excess ECH\nVacuum Distillation]
    H --> I[Liquid DGEBA Resin\nEEW 170 to 190]

    style A fill:#fde2c5,stroke:#8a4500
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#fff7c2,stroke:#665500
    style I fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1d5d1d
```

---

## 4.3 Epoxy Curing Network (Schematic Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DGEBA Molecule\n2 Epoxide Ends] --> B[Reaction with\nPrimary Amine H]
    B --> C[Secondary Amine\nformed]
    C --> D[Reaction with\nSecond Epoxide]
    D --> E[Tertiary Amine\nBranching Point]
    E --> F[Network Node\nTrifunctional]
    A --> G[Independent Branch\nforms crosslink]
    G --> F

    style A fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#1a3f7a
    style F fill:#ffd1dc,stroke:#7a1a3a
```

> **Reading guide:** Each DGEBA supplies 2 reactive ends; each tri-functional amine (e.g., DETA's secondary N) produces a **trifunctional branch point**. The dense network explains the high Tg, solvent resistance, and brittle failure mode of cured epoxy.

---

## 4.4 Application-Family Map for PUR and Epoxy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PUR[Polyurethane Family] --> PURF[Foams\nFlexible and Rigid]
    PUR --> PURE[Elastomers\nSpandex and Rollers]
    PUR --> PURC[Coatings\nFloor and Marine]
    PUR --> PURA[Adhesives\nFootwear and Lamination]
    PUR --> PURS[Sealants\nConstruction Joints]

    EPX[Epoxy Family] --> EPXC[Coatings\nHeavy-duty Anti-corrosion]
    EPX --> EPXAD[Adhesives\nAerospace Bonding]
    EPX --> EPXCP[Composites\nWind-turbine Blades]
    EPX --> EPXEL[Electronics\nPotting and Encapsulation]
    EPX --> EPXFL[Flooring\nIndustrial Self-levelling]

    style PUR fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1a4f80
    style EPX fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#800000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question 1**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *List any three important applications of polyurethanes. Mention the role of water in flexible polyurethane foam manufacture.*

**Model Answer (concise):**

Three important applications of polyurethanes:
1. Flexible and rigid foams (cushioning, insulation).
2. Elastomers (rollers, shoe soles, synthetic leather).
3. Coatings and adhesives (floor finishes, footwear adhesives).

**Role of water:** Water reacts with isocyanate to form an unstable carbamic acid that spontaneously decarboxylates to yield CO₂ gas and a primary amine. The CO₂ generated **acts as the in-situ blowing agent** that expands the reacting mixture into foam, while the primary amine formed reacts with further isocyanate to generate **disubstituted urea linkages**, contributing to hard-segment content and network stiffness.

---

### **Question 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Define epoxy equivalent weight (EEW). What is the EEW of pure DGEBA with the structure $\text{CH}_2\text{(O)CH–CH}_2\text{–O–Ar–O–CH}_2\text{–CH(O)CH}_2$, where Ar = bisphenol-A residue of mass 228 g/mol?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** *The epoxy equivalent weight (EEW) is the mass of epoxy resin, in grams, that contains exactly one mole of epoxide (oxirane) groups.*

**Calculation for pure DGEBA ($n = 0$):**

The molecular mass of pure DGEBA:

$$M = 228 + 2 \times 57 = 228 + 114 = 342\ \text{g/mol}$$

(The glycidyl ether group adds 57 g/mol: CH₂(O)CH–CH₂–O– = 2 × C₃H₅O = 2 × 57.)

Number of epoxide groups per molecule = 2.

$$EEW = \dfrac{M}{n_{epoxide}} = \dfrac{342}{2} = \mathbf{171\ \text{g/eq}}$$

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### ✅ OPTION A — Question A

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** *Explain the chemistry of polyurethane formation with a clear note on the role of diisocyanates and polyols. Discuss the significance of the isocyanate index. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Describe the synthesis of DGEBA from bisphenol-A and epichlorohydrin with equations. List any four curing agents for epoxy resins. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

The chemistry of polyurethane formation is centred on the **addition reaction between the isocyanate group (–N=C=O) and active-hydrogen compounds**, the most important being polyols (compounds with multiple –OH groups).

**Step 1 — Urethane Linkage Formation:**

The reaction of an isocyanate with an alcohol proceeds via nucleophilic attack of the alcohol oxygen on the electrophilic carbon of the isocyanate:

$$R-N=C=O + HO-R' \longrightarrow R-NH-CO-O-R'$$

This is a **step-growth polyaddition** — each coupling event consumes one NCO and one OH, and produces one urethane linkage. With bifunctional monomers a linear polymer is produced; with multifunctional monomers a 3-D thermoset network is formed.

**Step 2 — Role of Diisocyanates:**

Diisocyanates such as **TDI (toluene diisocyanate)** and **MDI (methylene diphenyl diisocyanate)** supply the rigid, aromatic, hard segment. They contribute:
- **High polarity** for strong inter-chain hydrogen bonding.
- **High Tg** hard-segment domains that act as physical crosslinks.
- **Reactivity** controllable through catalysts (amine / tin).

**Step 3 — Role of Polyols:**

Polyether polyols (PPG, PEG) and polyester polyols (adipate, caprolactone) provide the soft segment. They govern:
- **Elongation** and low-temperature flexibility.
- **Hydrolytic stability** (polyethers better than polyesters).
- **Molecular weight** of the soft block (typically 1000 – 6000 g/mol).

**Step 4 — Isocyanate Index ($I$):**

$$I = \dfrac{\text{moles of NCO actually used}}{\text{moles of NCO theoretically required (stoichiometric)}}} \times 100\ \%$$

- $I < 100$ → excess OH → **thermoplastic** PUR (soft, soluble).
- $I = 100$ → stoichiometric → balanced.
- $I > 100$ (typically 102 – 110) → excess NCO → **thermoset** PUR through allophanate / biuret crosslinks formed by side reactions with urethane / urea NH.

**Significance:** The index controls every property — Tg, modulus, hardness, solvent resistance and even biocompatibility (for medical-grade PU).

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Synthesis of DGEBA — Step 1 (Alkoxide formation):**

Bisphenol-A is deprotonated by aqueous NaOH:

$$HO-Ar-OH + 2\ NaOH \longrightarrow NaO-Ar-ONa + 2\ H_2O$$

**Step 2 (Williamson etherification — first epoxide):**

The phenoxide attacks epichlorohydrin:

$$NaO-Ar + ClCH_2-CH(O)CH_2 \longrightarrow NaO-Ar-O-CH_2-CH(OH)-CH_2-Cl$$

**Step 3 (Ring closure to epoxide):**

NaOH dehydrohalogenates the chlorohydrin:

$$...CH(OH)-CH_2-Cl + NaOH \longrightarrow ...CH(O)CH_2 + NaCl + H_2O$$

Steps 2 – 3 repeat on the other phenolic OH to give the final DGEBA.

**Four Important Curing Agents:**

1. **Aliphatic polyamines** — DETA, TETA → room-temperature cure.
2. **Aromatic amines** — MDA, DDS → high-Tg aerospace composites.
3. **Anhydrides** — phthalic, nadic → long-pot-life electrical castings.
4. **Dicyandiamide (DICY)** — latent hardener for one-pack powder coatings.

---

### ✅ OPTION B — Question B

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Alternate Choice]**

**(a)** *Describe the mechanism of formation of flexible polyurethane foam. Discuss the role of catalysts, surfactants, and the blow-to-gel ratio. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the curing mechanism of epoxy resin with a primary amine. Compute the stoichiometric phr of DETA (M = 146 g/mol, 5 active H) for a DGEBA resin having EEW = 190. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Mechanism of Flexible PU Foam Formation:**

When polyol, water, silicone surfactant, amine catalyst and tin catalyst are mixed with TDI/MDI in a high-pressure mix-head, **two simultaneous reactions** race against each other:

**Reaction 1 — Gelling (polyol + isocyanate):**

$$R-NCO + HO-R' \longrightarrow R-NH-CO-O-R'$$

This builds polymer molecular weight and viscosity.

**Reaction 2 — Blowing (water + isocyanate):**

$$R-NCO + H_2O \longrightarrow R-NH-COOH \longrightarrow R-NH_2 + CO_2\uparrow$$

The CO₂ expands the reacting mass; the amine formed further reacts with NCO to make urea linkages.

**Role of Catalysts:**

- **Tertiary amine (DABCO, DMEA):** selectively catalyses the **blowing** reaction.
