- **Organotin (dibutyltin dilaurate):** selectively catalyses the **gelling** reaction.

**Role of Surfactants (silicone–polyether):**

- Lower surface tension of the rising foam.
- Stabilise cell windows against rupture and collapse.
- Promote uniform, open cell structure essential for flexible foam.

**Blow-to-Gel Ratio:**

The ratio of the rates of blowing to gelling must be carefully balanced:
- If **blow >> gel** → foam rises but collapses (over-blown, no strength to hold structure).
- If **gel >> blow** → foam sets before it can rise (tight, dense, no foam).
- Properly balanced → foam rises, stabilises, then fully cures.

A typical commercial flexible slabstock formulation uses an amine catalyst for blowing and a tin catalyst for gelling in a carefully tuned molar ratio (often around **2 : 1 amine : tin**).

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Curing Mechanism with Primary Amine (DETA):**

DETA has the structure H₂N–CH₂CH₂–NH–CH₂CH₂–NH₂. It carries **5 active hydrogens** (2 on each terminal NH₂ and 1 on the central NH).

**Step 1 — First ring-opening (primary H):**

$$\underset{\text{epoxide}}{-CH-CH_2\atop \diagup\;\;\diagdown\atop O} + H_2N-R \longrightarrow -CH(OH)-CH_2-NH-R$$

**Step 2 — Second ring-opening (second primary H on same N):**

$$-CH(OH)-CH_2-NH-R + \underset{\text{epoxide}}{-CH-CH_2\atop \diagup\;\;\diagdown\atop O} \longrightarrow \text{tertiary amine branching point}$$

**Step 3 — Secondary amine (central NH) reacts with another epoxide:**

The central secondary N also opens further epoxide rings, generating crosslinks and the 3-D network. The hydroxyl groups formed along the chain participate in extensive H-bonding, contributing to the high Tg and adhesive strength.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Equivalent weight of DETA:

$$EW_{DETA} = \dfrac{M}{\text{active H}} = \dfrac{146}{5} = 29.2\ \text{g/eq}$$

Stoichiometric phr of DETA:

$$H_{phr} = \dfrac{100 \times EEW_{resin}}{EW_{hardener}} = \dfrac{100 \times 190}{29.2} = 650.7$$

Mass of DETA per 100 g resin:

$$m_{DETA} = \dfrac{100}{EEW} \times EW_{hardener} = \dfrac{100}{190} \times 29.2 = 15.37\ \text{g}$$

$$\boxed{H_{phr} \approx 15.4\ \text{phr of DETA}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **EEW confusion:** Many students write $EEW = M/n$ where $n$ is number of repeat units — WRONG. $n$ must be the **number of epoxide groups** per molecule.
> 2. **Isocyanate index sign error:** Index $> 100$ means **excess isocyanate**, not excess polyol. The UPH vocabulary is unforgiving.
> 3. **Carothers equation misuse:** Students often forget that $p$ must be very close to 1 (e.g., 0.99+) to get commercial molecular weights. A value of $p = 0.90$ gives $\bar{M}_n$ that is far too low for engineering plastics.
> 4. **DETA H count:** DETA has **5 active hydrogens** (often miscounted as 4 or 6). Memorise this — KTU loves it.
> 5. **Skipping the role of water in PU foam:** A very common 2-mark loss. Always mention both the carbamic-acid intermediate and the resulting urea linkage, not just "CO₂ is released".

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Polyurethane** = heterochain step-growth polymer with **–NH–CO–O–** linkage formed from **diisocyanate + polyol**.
- ✅ **Hard segment** (diisocyanate + chain extender) provides rigidity; **soft segment** (polyol) provides flexibility; **microphase separation** governs properties.
- ✅ **TDI, MDI, HDI, IPDI** are the major commercial diisocyanates.
- ✅ **Isocyanate Index ($I$)** is the master formulation variable — controls thermoplastic vs thermoset character.
- ✅ **Water + isocyanate → CO₂** is the in-situ chemical blowing mechanism for PU foams.
- ✅ **Tertiary amine catalysts** promote blowing; **organotin catalysts** promote gelling; **silicone surfactants** stabilise foam cells.
- ✅ **PIR (polyisocyanurate)** = trimerised isocyanate rings → flame-retardant rigid foam.
- ✅ **Epoxy resin** carries the strained **oxirane ring**; **DGEBA** is the industrial standard.
- ✅ **DGEBA synthesis** = BPA + epichlorohydrin + NaOH in three coupled steps (alkoxide formation → chlorohydrin → epoxide ring closure).
- ✅ **Epoxy curing** = ring-opening addition of amines; primary H opens first, then secondary H, generating tertiary amine branch points.
- ✅ **Epoxy Equivalent Weight (EEW)** = mass (g) per mole of epoxide groups; commercial DGEBA typically **170 – 190 g/eq**.
- ✅ **Hardener phr** $= 100 \times EEW_{resin} / EW_{hardener}$; **DETA** has $EW = 29.2$ g/eq (5 active H's).
- ✅ **Carothers equation:** $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$; high $M_n$ requires $p \geq 0.99$.
- ✅ **Comparison tip:** PUR is the **most formulation-flexible** polymer family; epoxy is the **highest-modulus, lowest-shrinkage** thermoset for structural applications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phenol Formaldehyde
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phenol Formaldehyde

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Phenol Formaldehyde (PF) Resins — Core Technical Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phenol Formaldehyde (PF) resins** are the condensation products obtained by the step-growth polymerization reaction between **phenol (C₆H₅OH)** and **formaldehyde (HCHO)** in the presence of an acid or an alkaline catalyst. They belong to the class of **thermosetting polymers** and represent the **first fully synthetic plastic** ever commercialized (under the trade name **Bakelite** by Leo Baekeland in **1907**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PF resins are the most historically and commercially significant thermoset in the family of phenolic resins. They cure irreversibly into a hard, infusible, three-dimensional crosslinked network upon the application of heat and pressure, and they are non-conductors of electricity — making them indispensable in the electrical and electronics industry.

### Key Identifiers

- **Polymer Class:** Phenolic thermoset (network polymer)
- **IUPAC-based Description:** Poly(oxy-1,4-phenylene-methylene)
- **Reaction Type:** Step-growth (condensation) polymerization with elimination of **water (H₂O)** as a by-product
- **Crosslink Density:** High (fully crosslinked upon cure)
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** Generally above **200 °C** for fully cured resin
- **Density:** ~1.25–1.30 g/cm³
- **Trade Names:** *Bakelite*, *Durez*, *Resinox*, *Novolac* (linear), *Resole* (single-stage)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The 3D Tinkertoy Glue"

Imagine you have thousands of tiny three-pronged **stars** (the phenol rings with three reactive positions: *ortho* and *para* to the –OH group). Now imagine pouring over them a sticky **two-pronged glue** (formaldehyde, H₂C=O). Each glue molecule grabs one star, and as the glue cures, it bridges to another star — and then to another. Within minutes, you no longer have loose stars; you have one **giant, inseparable, rock-hard single molecule** shaped like a tangled 3D web.

That is precisely what happens in a PF resin:
- **Phenol = the multi-reactive hub**
- **Formaldehyde = the bridging glue**
- **Heat + catalyst = the trigger that fuses everything into one crosslinked "super-molecule"**

Once the crosslinked network is formed, **melting is impossible** (no more chains to slide past each other). Hence the name: **thermo-set** (sets permanently with heat).

## 1.3 Historical and Industrial Significance

> [!NOTE]
> - **1907** – Leo Hendrik Baekeland (USA) patented the first synthetic plastic, "Bakelite", by reacting phenol and formaldehyde under pressure.
> - PF was the world's first **fully synthetic commercial polymer**, predating polyethylene, PVC, and polystyrene by decades.
> - It set the architectural template for the entire **thermoset plastics industry**.

**Common Applications (KTU Board Favourite List):**

1. **Electrical switchboards, plugs, sockets, and insulators** (excellent dielectric strength ~10–15 kV/mm).
2. **Adhesives** for plywood, particle board, and laminates (e.g., aircraft interior panels).
3. **Molded articles** — telephones, radio cabinets, cooker handles, camera bodies.
4. **Coatings and lacquers** — chemically resistant paints, varnishes.
5. **Foundry sand binders** in metal casting.
6. **Brake linings, clutch facings, and abrasive binders** (phenolic friction materials).
7. **Ion-exchange resins** (after sulfonation).
8. **Carbon foam and activated carbon** precursors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — Types, Reactions, and Cure Behaviour

## 2.1 The Reactivity of Phenol Toward Formaldehyde

The phenol ring is **activated** toward electrophilic aromatic substitution because the –OH group is a strong **ortho/para director** (electron-donating by resonance). This activates three positions on the ring:

- Two *ortho* positions (C-2 and C-6)
- One *para* position (C-4)

Hence, the **maximum functionality of phenol = 3**. Formaldehyde (H₂C=O) acts as the **electrophile** (carbonyl carbon bears a partial positive charge). This 3:1 functionality mismatch is precisely what allows **three-dimensional crosslinking** to occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Functionality (f)** is the average number of reactive sites per monomer. For PF to form a 3D network, the average functionality of the system must be **≥ 2**. Phenol (f = 3) + Formaldehyde (f = 2) easily satisfies the **Carothers gelation criterion** (average f > 2).

## 2.2 Two Industrial Varieties of PF Resins

The product obtained depends entirely on the **catalyst used** and the **molar ratio** of phenol to formaldehyde.

### A. Novolac Resins (Two-Stage Resin)

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Catalyst | Acidic (e.g., HCl, H₂SO₄, oxalic acid, p-toluenesulfonic acid) |
| P:F Molar Ratio | **> 1:1** (phenol in excess, typically 1.0 : 0.75 to 0.85) |
| Structure | **Linear** thermoplastic; *never* crosslinked on its own |
| Curing | Requires a second addition of a **hardener** (usually **hexamethylenetetramine / hexa**) and heat |
| Storage | Indefinitely stable at room temperature (no self-curing) |
| Molecular Weight | 500 – 5000 g/mol (oligomer) |

> [!TIP]
> Because novolac has excess phenol, **all formaldehyde is consumed** during synthesis, leaving no free –CH₂OH groups to self-crosslink. Hence novolac is **permanently fusible and soluble** — a true thermoplastic behavior *until* a hardener is added.

### B. Resole Resins (Single-Stage Resin)

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Catalyst | Basic / alkaline (e.g., NaOH, KOH, Ba(OH)₂, NH₄OH) |
| P:F Molar Ratio | **< 1:1** (formaldehyde in excess, typically 1.0 : 1.2 to 2.0) |
| Structure | **Branched**, containing free methylol (–CH₂OH) and benzyl-ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–) groups |
| Curing | Self-curing upon heating (no hardener required) |
| Storage | Limited shelf life at room temp; refrigerated storage preferred (called **A-stage resin** → B-stage → C-stage) |
| Molecular Weight | 200 – 1000 g/mol (A-stage liquid) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** If an examiner asks *"Why does resole cure on its own while novolac does not?"*, the answer is: **resole has unreacted –CH₂OH groups (because excess HCHO is present), which can further condense with available *ortho/para* H atoms of other phenol rings — leading to self-crosslinking on heating. Novolac has no such unreacted methylols.**

## 2.3 The Three Reaction Stages of a Resole (KTU Favourite!)

| Stage | Name | Physical State | Chemistry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Resol | Liquid / fusible, soluble in alcohol | Linear oligomers, contains –CH₂OH and –CH₂OCH₂– groups |
| **B** | Resitol | Rubber-like, partially soluble, swells in solvent | Branched, partially crosslinked, softens with heat |
| **C** | Resite | Infusible, insoluble, hard solid | Fully crosslinked, infusible 3D network; **end product** |

This **A → B → C** progression is what gives the resin its working life — phenolic laminates, for example, are impregnated at the **A-stage** and cured to **C-stage** in the press.

## 2.4 The Two Fundamental Chemical Steps

The PF reaction proceeds in **two universally-applicable elementary steps**, regardless of catalyst type:

### Step 1 — Methylolation (Addition Reaction)

$$ \text{Phenol} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{Hydroxymethyl phenol (Methylol phenol)} $$

The carbonyl carbon of HCHO attacks an *ortho* or *para* position of the activated phenol ring, producing a **methylol (–CH₂OH)** intermediate.

### Step 2 — Condensation (Bridge Formation)

$$ 2 \times \text{Hydroxymethyl phenol} \longrightarrow \text{Methylene-bridged dimer} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

OR

$$ \text{Hydroxymethyl phenol} + \text{Phenol} \longrightarrow \text{Methylene-bridged dimer} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

Two methylol groups (or one methylol and one free ring position) join, eliminating **water** to form a **–CH₂– (methylene) bridge** between two phenol rings.

> [!WARNING]
> In **alkaline resoles**, a *third* competing pathway also occurs: two methylol groups can condense to form a **dibenzyl ether bridge (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** with loss of water. This ether bridge is **less thermally stable** than the methylene bridge and can re-arrange to a methylene bridge + free HCHO at high cure temperatures (a "self-healing" feature of phenolic networks).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

| Symbol / Concept | Expression / Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Average functionality $\bar{f}$ | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Determines crosslink potential (must be $\geq 2$ for gelation) |
| Carothers gel point $p_c$ | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$ | Critical extent of reaction for network formation |
| Number-average DP (linear novolac) | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$ | $r = N_A/N_B \leq 1$; for stoichiometric imbalance |
| Phenol:Formaldehyde (Novolac) | **1.0 : 0.75 – 0.85** | Phenol always in excess |
| Phenol:Formaldehyde (Resole) | **1.0 : 1.2 – 2.0** | Formaldehyde always in excess |
| Curing temperature | **120 – 180 °C** | Typical compression molding range |
| Curing pressure | **15 – 25 MPa** | For compression-molded Bakelite parts |
| Dielectric strength | **10 – 15 kV/mm** | High electrical insulation value |
| Water eliminated per bridge | **1 mole H₂O** | Confirms step-growth (condensation) mechanism |
| Hexa (C₆H₁₂N₄) usage in novolac | ~**8 – 15 phr** | Parts per hundred parts of resin (by mass) |

> [!NOTE]
> **phr** = parts per hundred parts of resin. A typical novolac molding powder contains 8–15 phr hexa as the curing agent.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PF resins are ubiquitous in engineering because of four "hero" properties:
1. **Outstanding heat resistance** — char yield > 50% at 700 °C (used in ablative heat shields of spacecraft).
2. **Excellent flame retardancy** — phenolic chars instead of melting/dripping.
3. **Superior chemical resistance** — to solvents, acids, and bases.
4. **High dimensional stability and rigidity** — perfect for precision-molded parts.

The **ablative heat shield** of NASA's *Apollo* command module used a glass-fiber-reinforced phenolic composite — a textbook application of PF thermoset performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Reaction Mechanism, Derivations, and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Acid-Catalyzed Mechanism (Novolac Route)

The acid mechanism is an **electrophilic aromatic substitution** on the activated phenol ring.

### Step 1 — Protonation of Formaldehyde

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{H}_2\text{C}{=}\text{O} \;+\; \text{H}^+ \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{C}{=}\overset{+}{\text{O}}\text{H} \\
&\text{(Formaldehyde)} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(Protonated carbonyl, a strong electrophile)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Electrophilic Attack on Phenol

The protonated formaldehyde attacks the *para* position of the phenol (or one of the *ortho* positions), forming a **carbocation intermediate** (a *Wheland* / arenium ion), followed by loss of a proton to restore aromaticity:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{C}{=}\overset{+}{\text{O}}\text{H} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \;+\; \text{H}^+
\end{aligned}$$

The product is **p-hydroxymethyl phenol** (or its *ortho* isomer, *o*-hydroxymethyl phenol). The H⁺ is *regenerated*, confirming acid as a true **catalyst**.

### Step 3 — Condensation to Methylene Bridge

A second phenol molecule attacks the benzylic carbon of the methylol, displacing –OH (which leaves as water after protonation):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \;+\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}^+}\; \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

The result is a **methylene (–CH₂–) bridged diphenol** (4,4′-dihydroxydiphenylmethane). Repeating this process builds the **linear novolac oligomer** when phenol is in excess.

## 3.2 Base-Catalyzed Mechanism (Resole Route)

The base mechanism proceeds through a **phenoxide ion**, which is even more activated than neutral phenol.

### Step 1 — Phenol → Phenoxide

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; \text{OH}^- \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

The phenoxide ion has high electron density at the *ortho* and *para* positions.

### Step 2 — Nucleophilic Addition to Formaldehyde

The phenoxide (as a carbanion-like resonance form) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of HCHO:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{C}{=}\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{O}^-
\end{aligned}$$

Protonation by water gives the **methylol phenol**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{O}^- \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \;+\; \text{OH}^-
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Condensation: Methylene Bridge Formation

Two methylol groups condense, releasing a hydroxide ion:

$$\begin{aligned}
2\;\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} \;+\; \text{HCHO} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **formaldehyde is regenerated** in this base-catalyzed condensation — that is why resoles with excess HCHO can keep reacting. The regenerated HCHO goes on to attack another *ortho/para* position, eventually consuming all three reactive sites of phenol and giving a 3D crosslinked network.

### Step 4 (Alkaline, Resole-specific) — Ether Bridge Formation

Two methylol groups can also condense to form a **dibenzyl ether** with loss of water:

$$\begin{aligned}
2\;\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

Ether bridges are **kinetically favoured at low cure temperatures** (< 130 °C) and rearrange to **methylene bridges** at higher temperatures (> 150 °C).

## 3.3 Carothers Equation for Novolac Molecular Weight

For a step-growth polymerization with **stoichiometric imbalance** $r < 1$ (where $r = N_{\text{phenol}}/N_{\text{HCHO}}$), the Carothers equation gives the number-average degree of polymerization:

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}
\end{aligned}$$

where $p$ is the extent of reaction of the limiting reactant (HCHO, since phenol is in excess for novolac). When $p = 1$ (all HCHO consumed):

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1 + r}{1 - r}
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Example (typical novolac):**
For $r = 1.00/0.85 = 1.176$ (excess phenol):

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + 1.176}{1 - 1.176 \cdot p}
\end{aligned}$$

At $p = 0.99$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{2.176}{1 - 1.164} = \dfrac{2.176}{-0.164}
\end{aligned}$$

This *negative* sign in the denominator confirms that the novolac *cannot* reach the gel point — it will always remain **finite and linear** because phenol is in excess (formaldehyde is the limiting reactant that will be exhausted). This is a beautiful mathematical confirmation of the two-stage novolac behavior.

## 3.4 Hexamethylenetetramine (Hexa) Curing of Novolac — Mechanistic Notes

When hexa (C₆H₁₂N₄) is heated with novolac, it decomposes to release **formaldehyde and ammonia**, both of which act as crosslinking agents:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{N}_4 \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; 6\,\text{HCHO} \;+\; 4\,\text{NH}_3
\end{aligned}$$

The released HCHO performs *in situ* methylolation and condensation on the phenol rings, while NH₃ participates in producing **benzylamine (–CH₂–NH–CH₂–)** crosslinks as well. This is why novolac molding powders always package hexa as the latent hardener.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Quick Glance for Exams)

| Feature | Novolac | Resole |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst | Acid | Alkali |
| P:F ratio | Phenol excess (> 1:1) | HCHO excess (< 1:1) |
| Resin structure | Linear | Branched |
| Methylol groups | Absent (all reacted) | Abundant |
| Hardener required? | Yes (hexa) | No |
| Crosslink site | At *ortho/para* via hexa-released HCHO | Direct –CH₂OH condensation |
| Storage stability | Excellent | Limited (A-stage) |
| Final product | Molding powder + hexa | Resol varnish/resin |
| Application | Molding, laminates, abrasives | Adhesives, foams, coatings |

## 3.6 Properties of the Fully Cured Network

After the **C-stage** is reached, the cured phenolic is characterized by:

- **3D Network Connectivity:** every phenol is connected to **three neighbors** through methylene (–CH₂–) bridges.
- **High Char Yield:** > 50% by mass at 700 °C in inert atmosphere (outstanding fire performance).
- **Dielectric Strength:** 10–15 kV/mm (excellent insulator).
- **Hardness:** Rockwell M ~ 100+ (very hard surface).
- **Chemical Resistance:** Resistant to most organic solvents, dilute acids, and bases.
- **Limitation:** Dark brown/black color (due to conjugated quinone-methide structures); **not** recyclable by remelting (thermoset).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams and Process Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Reaction Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Phenol C6H5OH] --> A1[Formaldehyde HCHO]
    A1 --> A2[Reactor with Catalyst]
    A2 --> A3{Catalyst Type?}
    A3 -- Acidic HCl/H2SO4 --> B1[Novolac Resin Linear Thermoplastic]
    A3 -- Alkaline NaOH/KOH --> B2[Resole Resin Branched with CH2OH groups]
    B1 --> C1[Add Hexamethylenetetramine Hexa Hardener]
    C1 --> D1[Compression Molding 150-180 C, 15-25 MPa]
    B2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Final Cured Phenolic Resin 3D Crosslinked Network]
    E1 --> F1[Applications: Switches, Adhesives, Brake Linings, Ablatives]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Reaction Pathway Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Phenol + HCHO] --> step2[Methylolation CH2OH attached ortho or para]
    step2 --> step3{Methylol reacts with...}
    step3 -- Another phenol ring --> step4[Methylene bridge CH2 condensation H2O released]
    step3 -- Another methylol --> step5[Dibenzyl ether bridge CH2-O-CH2 H2O released]
    step4 --> step6[Linear or Branched Oligomer]
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7{Excess reactant?}
    step7 -- Phenol excess Novolac --> step8[Linear Novolac, needs Hexa to cure]
    step7 -- HCHO excess Resole --> step9[Resole with free CH2OH, self-curing on heating]
    step8 --> step10[Compression molding with Hexa and heat]
    step9 --> step10
    step10 --> step11[Cured PF Resin 3D Network]
```

## 4.3 Stage Progression Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stageA[A Stage: Resol Liquid, soluble, fusible, low MW ~ 200-1000]
    stageB[B Stage: Resitol Rubbery, partially crosslinked, swells in solvent]
    stageC[C Stage: Resite Hard solid, infusible, insoluble, fully crosslinked]
    stageA -- Heat 100-130 C --> stageB
    stageB -- Heat 150-180 C --> stageC
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Cured Phenolic Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    hubA[Phenol Ring 1 Crosslink Hub] -- CH2 bridge --> hubB[Phenol Ring 2]
    hubB -- CH2 bridge --> hubC[Phenol Ring 3]
    hubC -- CH2 bridge --> hubA
    hubA -- CH2 bridge --> hubD[Phenol Ring 4]
    hubB -- CH2 bridge --> hubD
    hubC -- CH2 bridge --> hubD
    hubD -- CH2 bridge --> hubE[Phenol Ring 5]
    hubE -- CH2 bridge --> hubA
    hubE -- CH2 bridge --> hubB
    note1[Note: Every phenol has 3 connections up to 3 neighbors via ortho/ortho/para CH2 bridges forming a rigid 3D web]:::note
    hubA -.- note1
    classDef note fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#f5b400,color:#663c00
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between Novolac and Resole phenolic resins in terms of catalyst, P:F ratio, structure, and curing behaviour.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark structured response):**

| Property | Novolac | Resole |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst | Acidic (HCl, H₂SO₄) | Alkaline (NaOH, NH₄OH) |
| Phenol : Formaldehyde | > 1:1 (phenol excess) | < 1:1 (HCHO excess) |
| Structure | Linear oligomer | Branched oligomer |
| Curing | Requires hardener (hexa) | Self-curing on heating |
| Storage | Indefinite | Limited (must be refrigerated) |
| Final stage | Two-stage resin | Single-stage resin |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is phenol-formaldehyde classified as a thermosetting polymer? Mention any three characteristic properties of cured PF resin.*

**Model Answer:**

Phenol has three reactive sites (two *ortho* + one *para*) and formaldehyde acts as a bifunctional crosslinker, producing a **3D crosslinked network** upon curing. The crosslinks are covalent and irreversible — once formed, the polymer **cannot be remelted or reshaped** by heating, hence classified as a **thermoset**.

Three characteristic properties of cured PF resin:

1. **High heat resistance** — char yield > 50% at 700 °C; no melting/dripping.
2. **Excellent electrical insulation** — dielectric strength ~10–15 kV/mm.
3. **Superior chemical and solvent resistance** — to acids, alkalis, and most organic solvents.

(Other acceptable points: hard, rigid, dark brown colour, low smoke emission.)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / KTU Model Paper 2024]** *With suitable reaction schemes, explain the preparation, mechanism, and properties of Phenol-Formaldehyde resin. Compare Novolac and Resole resins.*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** *Describe the acid-catalysed (Novolac) preparation of PF resin with mechanism.*

**Model Answer:**

**Raw Materials:** Phenol (C₆H₅OH) and 37–40% aqueous formaldehyde (formalin) in the molar ratio 1.0 : 0.75 to 0.85. Catalyst: 0.5–2% hydrochloric acid or oxalic acid.

**Procedure:**
1. Phenol and formalin are charged into a glass-lined steel reactor fitted with a stirrer, reflux condenser, and temperature-control jacket.
2. Acid catalyst is added slowly with stirring.
3. The mixture is heated to **80–95 °C** for 2–4 hours under reflux.
4. The reaction proceeds in **two stages**:
   - **Methylolation (addition):** HCHO attacks the *ortho/para* positions, giving hydroxymethyl phenols.
   - **Condensation:** Methylol groups condense with each other (or with free phenol) to give **–CH₂– (methylene) bridges** + H₂O.
5. Water is removed by distillation; the molten resin is poured, cooled, and ground into a powder.

**Key Mechanism Steps (acid-catalysed):**
- Protonation of HCHO → activated electrophile $^+\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$.
- Electrophilic aromatic substitution on phenol ring at *o/p* positions → arenium ion → methylol phenol.
- Condensation: methylol + phenol → methylene bridge + H₂O (linear, since phenol is in excess).

**Novolac Properties:** Linear, low MW (~500–5000), thermoplastic, soluble in alcohol, requires hexa to cure.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Describe the base-catalysed (Resole) preparation with mechanism, A/B/C stages, and applications.*

**Model Answer:**

**Raw Materials:** Phenol : Formaldehyde molar ratio 1.0 : 1.2 to 2.0. Catalyst: 1–3% NaOH, Ba(OH)₂, or NH₄OH.

**Procedure:**
1. Phenol, formalin, and alkali catalyst are charged into the reactor.
2. Heated to **60–95 °C** for 1–2 hours.
3. Excess water is removed under vacuum to obtain a viscous **A-stage resole** resin.
4. The resole is cooled and stored (it will self-cure on further heating).

**Mechanism (base-catalysed):**
- Phenol + OH⁻ → phenoxide ion (highly activated).
- Phenoxide attacks HCHO → methylol phenoxide → methylol phenol.
- Condensation: 2 methylols → methylene bridge + HCHO (regenerated, perpetuating crosslinking).
- Side reaction: 2 methylols → dibenzyl ether bridge + H₂O.

**A → B → C Stage Progression:**

| Stage | State | Properties |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Liquid / low MW (200–1000) | Soluble, fusible, still moldable |
| **B** | Rubbery, partially crosslinked | Swells in solvents, softens on heating |
| **C** | Hard, infusible solid | Fully crosslinked, insoluble, dimensionally stable |

**Applications:** Adhesives for plywood, foundry sand binders, laminating varnishes, foam insulation, coatings, and brake linings.

**Comparison Novolac vs. Resole:**
(See Part A answer table for full comparison.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 expected pattern]** *Explain the curing mechanism of Novolac resin using hexamethylenetetramine (hexa). Discuss the structure–property relationship, applications, and limitations of cured PF resin.*

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** *Curing of Novolac with hexa — mechanism and chemistry.*

**Model Answer:**

**Why hexa is needed:** Novolac is linear with no reactive –CH₂OH groups left (because phenol was used in excess). A latent source of formaldehyde is required for in-situ crosslinking. **Hexamethylenetetramine (C₆H₁₂N₄)** — a cage-like amine — is the industry-standard hardener.

**Mechanism:**
1. On heating to ~**120 °C**, hexa decomposes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{N}_4 \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; 6\,\text{HCHO} \;+\; 4\,\text{NH}_3
\end{aligned}$$

2. The liberated HCHO performs methylolation at the available *ortho/para* positions of the novolac chain.
3. The liberated NH₃ reacts with benzyl alcohol intermediates to form **benzylamine (–CH₂–NH–CH₂–) bridges** between chains.
4. The combined methylene and benzylamine bridges create the **3D crosslinked network**.

A typical molding powder contains **8–15 phr hexa** — enough to give ~5–10% crosslink density.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Structure–property relationship, applications, and limitations.*

**Model Answer:**

**Structure–Property Relationship:**

| Structural Feature | Resulting Property |
|---|---|
| 3D crosslinked network (high crosslink density) | Hardness, rigidity, no flow on heating |
| Aromatic rings | High thermal stability, high char yield |
| –OH and –CH₂– bridges | Polar → excellent adhesion to fillers, fibers |
| Dense network (no free chain ends) | Chemical and solvent resistance |
| Conjugated quinone-methide (during cure) | Dark brown/black colour |

**Key Applications:**

1. **Electrical industry:** switches, plugs, MCBs, switchgear (high dielectric strength + flame retardance).
2. **Adhesives:** exterior-grade plywood and aircraft interior panels (WBP — Weather and Boil Proof).
3. **Molded goods:** telephones, kitchenware handles, camera bodies, ashtrays.
4. **Friction materials:** brake pads, clutch facings (thermal stability under friction).
5. **Abrasives:** grinding wheels (phenol-formaldehyde bonds SiC / Al₂O₃ grit).
6. **Ablative heat shields** (Apollo, space capsules).
7. **Foundry sand binders** (shell molding process).

**Limitations:**

1. **Dark colour** (only brown/black) — cannot be used where light shades are required.
2. **Brittleness** — phenolics are hard but have low impact strength (often reinforced with wood flour, glass, or paper).
3. **Emission of HCHO and phenol** during cure (health hazards — well-ventilated molding required).
4. **Non-recyclable** (thermoset).
5. **Difficult to process** — requires high temperature, high pressure molding; cannot be injection-molded like thermoplastics.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PF Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the catalysts** — students often write "NaOH is used for novolac" or "HCl for resole". Examiner deducts **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the by-product** — the question says "condensation polymerization" — students must explicitly mention **H₂O is eliminated**, or lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the second stage** — for novolac, always write "**requires hardener (hexa)**"; for resole, always write "**self-curing**". Examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Wrong P:F ratio** — Novolac = phenol excess (P:F > 1:1); Resole = formaldehyde excess (P:F < 1:1). Reversed ratio = full credit loss.
> 5. **Skipping the A/B/C stages** — when discussing resole, examiners expect the A → B → C progression table. Missing this costs 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning –CH₂OH (methylol) intermediate** — without this, the mechanism is incomplete and you lose 1 mark.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phenol Formaldehyde = the world's first fully synthetic commercial plastic (Bakelite, 1907).**
- **Reaction type:** step-growth condensation polymerization; **by-product = H₂O** (one mole per methylene bridge).
- **Two industrial types:** **Novolac** (acid catalyst, phenol excess, linear, two-stage with hexa hardener) and **Resole** (alkali catalyst, HCHO excess, branched, single-stage self-curing).
- **Functionality:** phenol = 3, formaldehyde = 2 → enables 3D crosslinked network (Carothers gel point easily reached in resole, *not* reached in novolac due to stoichiometric imbalance).
- **Two key elementary steps:** **Methylolation (addition)** then **Condensation (bridge formation with H₂O release)**.
- **Bridges formed:** mainly **methylene (–CH₂–)**, sometimes **dibenzyl ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** in resoles at low temperatures; ether rearranges to methylene at high T.
- **Curing stages of resole:** **A** (liquid) → **B** (rubbery) → **C** (hard infusible solid).
- **Hardener for novolac:** **Hexamethylenetetramine (hexa, C₆H₁₂N₄)** at 8–15 phr; decomposes on heating to release HCHO and NH₃ for crosslinking.
- **Cure conditions:** **120–180 °C**, **15–25 MPa** compression pressure.
- **Hero properties:** heat resistance (char yield > 50% at 700 °C), flame retardance, chemical resistance, dielectric strength (10–15 kV/mm), rigidity.
- **Major applications:** electrical switchgear, plywood adhesives, brake linings, ablative heat shields, abrasive binders, foundry sand binders.
- **Limitations:** dark brown/black colour, brittleness (need fillers like wood flour), HCHO/phenol emissions during cure, non-recyclable.
- **Industrial trade names:** Bakelite, Durez, Resinox, Varcum, Durite.
- **Polymer class:** Phenolic **thermoset**; not to be confused with thermoplastic phenoxy resins.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Urea Formaldehyde
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Urea Formaldehyde

# Urea Formaldehyde (UF) Resin — A Comprehensive KTU Module 3 Study Module

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT523 — Module 3):** Urea Formaldehyde is a key **thermosetting amino resin** classified under the family of **aminoplasts**. It is formed by the step-growth condensation of **urea (NH₂–CO–NH₂)** with **formaldehyde (HCHO)** in the presence of an acid or base catalyst.

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Urea Formaldehyde (UF)** is a non-crystalline, cross-linked **thermosetting condensation polymer** synthesized from urea and formaldehyde through successive methylolation and condensation reactions. The cured resin has a three-dimensional network structure formed by methylene (–CH₂–), methylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–), and minor uron linkages. It is the **cheapest and most widely produced** amino resin in the world and is primarily used as an **adhesive in wood-based panels (plywood, particleboard, MDF)**, as well as in laminates, surface coatings, and molded articles.

**Empirical Structural Identity of Repeating Unit:**

$$\text{–[–NH–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{–]}_n\text{–}$$

Cross-linked network contains methylene bridges: **–NH–CH₂–NH–** and ether bridges **–NH–CH₂–O–CH₂–NH–**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Spider-Web"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of Urea Formaldehyde as a **"molecular Velcro net"** 🕸️. Imagine urea molecules as small rings and formaldehyde as tiny connector clips. When these clips snap the rings together **repeatedly in every direction** (a process called cross-linking), they form a rigid, irreversible **three-dimensional web**. Once this web is "baked" (cured under heat and acid), it becomes permanently hard — you can hit it, heat it, or boil it, but it will **never melt or reshape** again. This is exactly the behaviour of a **thermoset** — a polymer that hardens once and stays hard forever, like a boiled egg that cannot be un-boiled.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Identifiers

| Property | Standard Metric (Bolded) |
|---|---|
| **Density** | **1.5 g/cm³** (cured resin) |
| **Refractive Index** | **1.54 – 1.56** |
| **Service Temperature** | **< 100 °C** (continuous) |
| **Formaldehyde/Urea Molar Ratio** | Typically **1.2 : 1 to 1.6 : 1** for adhesives |
| **Colour** | Translucent / Milky white → Pale yellow cured |
| **CAS Number** | **9011-05-6** |

### 1.4 Classification Position

$$\text{Aminoplasts (N-containing thermosets)} \;\supset\; \text{UF, MF (Melamine–Formaldehyde), UF-MF blends}$$

UF is the **lowest cost** member of the amino resin family but is **less water-resistant and releases more free formaldehyde (HCHO emissions)** than Melamine Formaldehyde (MF) or Phenol Formaldehyde (PF).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Raw Materials — The Two Building Blocks

**A. Urea — CO(NH₂)₂**
- White, crystalline, water-soluble, **MW = 60.06 g/mol**, **m.p. = 133 °C**
- Difunctional (two reactive –NH₂ groups)

**B. Formaldehyde (HCHO) — usually as 37 % aqueous solution called "Formalin"**
- **MW = 30.03 g/mol**, **b.p. = –19 °C**
- Highly reactive carbonyl carbon (electrophilic)

### 2.2 The Two-Stage Reaction Mechanism

UF formation is a **step-growth condensation polymerization** that proceeds in two distinct, controllable stages.

---

#### **Stage 1 — Methylolation (Addition Stage, pH ≈ 7.5 – 8.0, mildly alkaline)**

Urea's amine nitrogen attacks formaldehyde's electrophilic carbonyl carbon. This produces **methylol ureas** (hydroxymethyl ureas).

**Step 1: Monomethylol urea**
$$\text{H}_2\text{N–CO–NH}_2 + \text{HCHO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{N–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{OH}$$

**Step 2: Dimethylol urea (DMU) — the key product**
$$\text{H}_2\text{N–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HOCH}_2\text{–NH–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{OH}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Trimethylol and tetramethylol ureas can also form when F/U ratio > 2, but the **dimethylol urea** is the most industrially important intermediate for adhesive applications.

---

#### **Stage 2 — Condensation (Cross-linking Stage, pH ≈ 4 – 5, acidic, with heat)**

Methylol groups condense with each other or with free –NH– groups, releasing **water** as condensate. This builds the polymer chain and ultimately the 3-D network.

**Reaction Pathway A — Methylene bridge formation (primary, releases H₂O):**
$$\text{–NH–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{H–NH–} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}^+,\;\Delta}\; \text{–NH–CH}_2\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Reaction Pathway B — Ether bridge formation (secondary, releases H₂O):**
$$\text{–NH–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HOCH}_2\text{–NH–} \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}^+,\;\Delta}\; \text{–NH–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ether bridge is **unstable at high curing temperatures** and converts to the methylene bridge with loss of formaldehyde:
> $$\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; \text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{HCHO} \uparrow$$
> This is the **major source of free-formaldehyde emission** in UF-bonded boards.

### 2.3 Curing & Network Formation

> [!TIP]
> **Curing** is the final, irreversible hardening where the linear/branched pre-polymer is converted into a **rigid, infusible 3-D network** by application of heat (≈ 100 – 150 °C) and an **acid catalyst** (commonly **NH₄Cl** — ammonium chloride — which hydrolyses to release HCl in situ).

$$\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NH}_4\text{OH} + \text{HCl}$$

The liberated **HCl** drops the local pH, accelerating the condensation reactions and rapidly building molecular weight toward infinity (gel point).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **F/U Molar Ratio** | $\dfrac{n_{\text{HCHO}}}{n_{\text{Urea}}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{UF, theory}}}{M_{\text{Urea}}} \cdot \dfrac{w_{\text{urea}}}{w_{\text{formal}}}$ | Typically **1.2 – 1.6** for wood adhesives |
| 2 | **Extent of reaction (p)** | $p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$ | Used in Carothers' analysis |
| 3 | **Carothers' gel point (f̄ = 3 network)** | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$ | $\bar{f}$ = average functionality |
| 4 | **For UF (urea f = 2, DMU f = 4):** | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$ | Average f̄ ≈ 3 → $p_c \approx 0.667$ |
| 5 | **Water released per methylene bridge** | $1\;\text{mol H}_2\text{O per 1 mol –CH}_2\text{–}$ | Stoichiometric condensate |
| 6 | **Theoretical water of condensation** | $W_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18 \times N_{\text{bridges}}\;\text{g}$ | Useful for mass-balance problems |
| 7 | **Methylene / Ether bridge ratio** | Increases with cure temp & acid strength | Affects HCHO emission |
| 8 | **Storage life at 25 °C** | 2 – 6 weeks (with stabilizer) | Stabilized by adjusting to **pH 8 – 9** with NaOH / triethanolamine |
| 9 | **Free HCHO in resin** | 0.1 – 3.0 % (modern low-emission: < 0.3 %) | Determined by **KCN–AgNO₃ titration** or **sulfite method** |
| 10 | **Gel time at 100 °C** | 30 – 120 s | Determines press time |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use `|` in table cells** — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown. In LaTeX math, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute / modulus notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

UF resins are indispensable in:
- **Wood composites** — Particle Board, MDF, Plywood (≈ 70 % of global UF production)
- **Decorative laminates** (as impregnating resin for paper overlays — though MF dominates)
- **Foundry sand binders** (shell molding of cores)
- **Slow-release nitrogen fertilizers** (UF / Urea-Form granules)
- **Foamed-in-place insulation** (UF foam with CFC-free blowing agents)
- **Textile finishing** (crease-resistant cotton)
- **Electrical fittings** (lamp shades, switch boards — though PF/Bakelite dominates)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carothers' Average Functionality (f̄) for UF Resin

The **gel point** of a step-growth polymer network is predicted by Carothers' equation. To apply it to UF, we must compute the **average functionality** of the reacting mixture.

**Given:** A reaction mixture where urea ($f = 2$, difunctional via two –NH₂ groups) and dimethylol urea (DMU, $f = 4$) co-exist. Suppose the mixture contains $N_u$ moles of urea and $N_d$ moles of DMU.

**Step 1:** Total moles of monomer mixture
$$N_{\text{tot}} = N_u + N_d$$

**Step 2:** Total number of reactive functional groups (each –NH₂ or –NH–CH₂OH counts as one)
$$N_{\text{fun}} = 2N_u + 4N_d$$

**Step 3:** Average functionality
$$\bar{f} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{fun}}}{N_{\text{tot}}} \;=\; \dfrac{2N_u + 4N_d}{N_u + N_d}$$

**Step 4:** Carothers' gel point (statistical, no intramolecular loops)
$$p_c \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$$

**Step 5:** Carothers' gel point (Flory's modification, **strict stoichiometry**)
$$p_{c,\,\text{Flory}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(f - 1)} \quad \text{(for } \bar{f} = \bar{f}_A = \bar{f}_B \text{, and } r=1\text{)}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style problem):**
Let $N_u = 0.4$ mol urea, $N_d = 0.6$ mol DMU. Compute $p_c$.

$$\bar{f} = \dfrac{2(0.4) + 4(0.6)}{0.4 + 0.6} = \dfrac{0.8 + 2.4}{1.0} = 3.2$$

$$p_c = \dfrac{2}{3.2} = 0.625 \;(\text{i.e., 62.5 \% of functional groups must react before gelation})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Interpretation: Once ~62.5 % of the –NH/–CH₂OH groups have reacted, an **infinite 3-D network** appears and the system gels. This is the basis of resin "pot life" calculations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass-Balance for Water Released in Condensation

**Problem:** 100 g of urea ($M_u = 60.06$ g/mol) reacts with excess formaldehyde at F/U = 1.5 to give UF. Assuming all methylene bridges form, calculate the **theoretical mass of water** released.

**Step 1:** Moles of urea
$$n_u = \dfrac{100}{60.06} = 1.665\;\text{mol}$$

**Step 2:** Moles of HCHO
$$n_f = 1.5 \times 1.665 = 2.498\;\text{mol}$$

**Step 3:** Stoichiometry. Methylolation consumes all 2.498 mol HCHO producing methylol ureas. Then in the condensation stage, each methylene bridge releases 1 mol H₂O. The maximum bridges ≈ 1.665 mol (each urea can form 2 bridges, but cross-linking dominates). For a **linear pre-polymer** (one chain, 100 % methylene bridging), bridges ≈ $n_u - 1 \approx 1.665$.

**Step 4:** Mass of water
$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1.665 \times 18 = 29.97 \;\text{g} \approx 30\;\text{g}$$

**Conclusion:** Synthesizing 100 g of urea releases ~30 g of water — a key engineering parameter (must be removed by evaporation, otherwise causes porosity/blistering in the cured board).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — HCHO/Urea Molar Ratio Calculation from Solution Composition

**Problem:** A typical commercial UF resin syrup is made by mixing 200 kg urea with 450 kg of **37 % formalin** (aqueous HCHO). What is the **F/U molar ratio**?

**Step 1:** Mass of pure HCHO in 450 kg of 37 % formalin
$$m_{\text{HCHO}} = 0.37 \times 450 = 166.5\;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2:** Moles of HCHO
$$n_f = \dfrac{166.5 \times 10^3}{30.03} = 5545\;\text{mol}$$

**Step 3:** Moles of urea
$$n_u = \dfrac{200 \times 10^3}{60.06} = 3330\;\text{mol}$$

**Step 4:** Molar ratio
$$\dfrac{F}{U} = \dfrac{5545}{3330} = 1.665$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{F}{U} \approx 1.66}$$

This lies in the **typical adhesive-grade range (1.5 – 1.8)**, confirming a viable resin for wood panel manufacture.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property vs F/U Molar Ratio

| F/U Molar Ratio | Water Tolerance | Storage Life | Bond Strength (Dry) | HCHO Emission | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1.0 – 1.2** | Low | Long | Moderate | Very Low | Textile finishes |
| **1.3 – 1.5** | High | Long | High | Low | Molding powders |
| **1.5 – 1.8** | High | Moderate | Very High | Moderate | **Wood adhesives (PB, MDF)** |
| **1.8 – 2.5** | Very High | Short | High (wet strength ↑) | **High** | Paper impregnation |

> [!NOTE]
> Increasing F/U ratio improves **wet-strength** and reactivity but **increases free HCHO emission** — the central trade-off in modern UF formulation. Modern low-emission grades use **urea scavenging** (post-addition of urea) and **F/U ≈ 1.05 – 1.2** for interior-grade boards compliant with **E1 / E0 / CARB Phase 2** standards.

### 3.5 Cross-Linking Density Estimation (Flory's Theory)

For a perfect 3-D network, the **crosslink density** $\nu_e$ (mol/cm³) is related to average functionality and extent of reaction:

$$\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho}{M_0} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\bar{f}}{2}\right) \cdot \left(p - p_c\right)$$

where $\rho$ = density of cured resin (g/cm³), $M_0$ = molar mass per functional group segment.

For $\rho = 1.5\;\text{g/cm}^3$, $M_0 = 30$ g/mol, $\bar{f} = 3.2$, $p = 0.90$, $p_c = 0.625$:

$$\nu_e = \dfrac{1.5}{30} \cdot 1.6 \cdot (0.90 - 0.625) = 0.05 \cdot 1.6 \cdot 0.275 = 0.022 \;\text{mol/cm}^3$$

This high crosslink density (~ 1 crosslink per ~ 45 unit mass) explains UF's **brittleness, hardness, and lack of ductility**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Reaction Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["RAW MATERIALS INTAKE"]:::raw --> nodeB["UREA CO NH2 2\nMW 60.06 g per mol"]
```
