## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define Urea Formaldehyde resin. Write any two important applications.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Urea Formaldehyde is a thermosetting amino resin synthesized by the step-growth condensation polymerization of urea with formaldehyde in the presence of an acid or base catalyst. The cured resin possesses a cross-linked 3-D network joined by methylene (–CH₂–) and methylene-ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–) bridges.
>
> **Applications (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. Adhesive for particleboard, MDF and plywood.
> 2. Decorative laminates and surface coatings.
> 3. Foundry sand binders for shell molding.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**What is meant by the "F/U molar ratio" in UF resin preparation? What is its significance?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The F/U molar ratio is the **molar ratio of formaldehyde to urea** used in the resin synthesis. Mathematically, $F/U = n_{\text{HCHO}}/n_{\text{Urea}}$.
>
> **Significance (1.5 marks):**
> - Controls the **type and proportion** of methylol intermediates (mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-methylol urea).
> - Higher F/U → better wet strength but **higher free-formaldehyde emission**.
> - Typical adhesive-grade resin uses F/U = 1.5 – 1.8; low-emission (E0) grades use 1.05 – 1.2.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (Module Internal Choice). Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Describe the **raw materials, chemistry and two stages of reaction** involved in the manufacture of Urea Formaldehyde resin with balanced chemical equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of curing** of UF resin, the role of the **acid catalyst (NH₄Cl)**, and the **cross-linked network structure** formed. Discuss **two major limitations** of UF resin. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Raw materials, chemistry & two stages (7 marks):**

**Raw Materials (1 mark):**
- **Urea** (NH₂–CO–NH₂): MW 60.06 g/mol, white crystalline, m.p. 133 °C, difunctional.
- **Formaldehyde** (HCHO): supplied as 37 % aqueous solution ("Formalin"), MW 30.03 g/mol, highly reactive electrophile.

**Stage 1 — Methylolation (Addition, 3 marks):**
Carried out at **pH 7.5 – 8.5** (mildly alkaline), 80 – 95 °C. Urea's nitrogen nucleophile attacks the carbonyl carbon of HCHO, forming hydroxymethyl (methylol) ureas.

$$\text{H}_2\text{N–CO–NH}_2 + \text{HCHO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H}_2\text{N–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{OH} \quad \text{(monomethylol urea)}$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{N–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HOCH}_2\text{–NH–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{OH} \quad \text{(dimethylol urea, DMU)}$$

**Stage 2 — Condensation (Cross-linking, 3 marks):**
Carried out at **pH 4 – 5** (acidic), 90 – 100 °C, with water removed by distillation.

**Methylene bridge formation:**
$$\text{–NH–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{H–NH–} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–NH–CH}_2\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Methylene-ether bridge formation:**
$$\text{2 (–NH–CH}_2\text{OH)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–NH–CH}_2\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The result is a **branched, water-soluble UF pre-polymer (resin syrup)** that is cooled and adjusted to **pH 8 – 9** for storage.

---

**(b) Curing mechanism & limitations (7 marks):**

**Curing Mechanism (4 marks):**
Curing is the final cross-linking of the pre-polymer into an **infusible 3-D network** under heat (100 – 150 °C) and acid catalysis. The latent hardener **NH₄Cl** hydrolyses in the presence of moisture:

$$\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NH}_4\text{OH} + \text{HCl}$$

The liberated **HCl** drops pH to 1 – 2, catalysing rapid condensation of the residual –NH– and –CH₂OH groups. The system passes through:
1. **Gel point** (infinite molecular weight, no flow)
2. **Vitrification** (T = Tg, diffusion-limited)
3. **Full cure** (rigid, hard, scratch-resistant network)

The cured structure contains covalent **methylene (–CH₂–)** and **ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)** bridges between urea units, yielding a hard, glossy, thermoset.

**Two Major Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Poor water / weather resistance:** The –CH₂–NH– and –CH₂–O–CH₂– linkages are hydrolyzable, so UF boards swell and lose strength in humid or exterior conditions. UF is therefore restricted to **interior-grade** applications (E1 / E2 class).
2. **Free-formaldehyde emission:** Release of HCHO from unreacted groups and from ether-bridge decomposition poses health hazards. Modern formulations must comply with strict **E0 / E1 / CARB Phase 2** emission norms.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **role of F/U molar ratio** on the properties of UF resin. With the help of a **worked example**, compute the F/U ratio when 150 kg urea is reacted with 320 kg of 37 % formalin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write notes on **(i) modifiers and additives** used in UF resin, **(ii) any three industrial applications** of UF resin with property–application justification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Role of F/U ratio & worked example (7 marks):**

**Effect of F/U Molar Ratio (4 marks):**

| F/U | Effect on Resin Properties |
|---|---|
| **< 1.0** | Mostly monomethylol urea; low cross-link density, brittle, low water resistance. |
| **1.0 – 1.5** | Predominantly dimethylol urea; balanced properties, used in **molding powders**. |
| **1.5 – 1.8** | Branched pre-polymer; excellent **dry & wet bond strength**; **standard wood-adhesive grade**. |
| **> 2.0** | Excess methylol groups → high ether-bridge content → **high free HCHO emission**; very reactive. |

The F/U ratio therefore **controls: storage life, water tolerance, gel time, bond strength, and formaldehyde emission**.

**Worked Example (3 marks):**

Given: $m_{\text{urea}} = 150$ kg, $m_{\text{formalin}} = 320$ kg of 37 % HCHO.

$$\text{Mass of pure HCHO} = 0.37 \times 320 = 118.4\;\text{kg}$$

$$n_{\text{HCHO}} = \dfrac{118.4 \times 10^3}{30.03} = 3943\;\text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{Urea}} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^3}{60.06} = 2498\;\text{mol}$$

$$\dfrac{F}{U} = \dfrac{3943}{2498} = 1.579 \approx 1.58$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{F}{U} = 1.58 \; \text{(wood-adhesive grade, standard)}}$$

---

**(b) Modifiers/additives & applications (7 marks):**

**(i) Modifiers & Additives (3.5 marks):**

| Additive | Function |
|---|---|
| **NH₄Cl / (NH₄)₂SO₄** | Latent acid hardener for hot-press curing |
| **NaOH / Triethanolamine** | Buffer to maintain pH 8 – 9 (storage stability) |
| **Wax emulsion** | Improves water repellency of board |
| **Wheat flour / Extender** | Reduces cost, controls viscosity, controls tack |
| **Melamine** | Co-condensation to upgrade water resistance (MUF resin) |
| **Phenol** | Co-condensation to improve weatherability (PUF resin) |
| **Urea (post-addition)** | "Urea scavenging" → reduces free HCHO |

**(ii) Three Industrial Applications (3.5 marks):**

1. **Particle Board / MDF Adhesives (≈ 70 % of UF production):** UF's excellent adhesion to cellulosic fibres, low viscosity, rapid cure, and very low cost make it the dominant binder for interior-grade wood panels.
2. **Decorative Laminates (surface paper impregnation):** The resin's high refractive index (~ 1.55), hardness, scratch resistance, and transparency after cure make it suitable for surface overlays on furniture laminates.
3. **Foundry Sand Binder (shell molding):** UF-bonded sand cores exhibit high hot-strength, dimensional accuracy, and clean shake-out, making them ideal for one-shot core production in metal casting.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in UF Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the F/U ratio** when describing resin properties — this is a **must-state** parameter; omitting it costs 1 – 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing methylol (–CH₂OH) with methylene (–CH₂–) bridges** — examiners specifically check whether you know that the former is the *intermediate* and the latter is the *final crosslink*.
> 3. **Writing "UF is a thermoplastic"** — it is a **thermoset**; this is a guaranteed zero on the definition question.
> 4. **Omitting water (H₂O) as a condensation by-product** — step-growth equations must show H₂O leaving.
> 5. **Not mentioning NH₄Cl hydrolysis** when describing curing — this is the standard KTU expected answer.
> 6. **Ignoring the free-formaldehyde emission issue** in applications — examiners in 2024 specifically reward awareness of **E0/E1/CARB compliance** under NEP-2020 outcome "Environment & Sustainability".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — UF Resin**

- ☐ UF is a **thermosetting amino resin**; **NOT a thermoplastic**.
- ☐ Made from **urea (difunctional, f = 2)** + **formaldehyde (37 % formalin)**.
- ☐ **Two stages:** (1) Methylolation at **pH 8**, (2) Condensation at **pH 4 – 5**.
- ☐ **Key intermediate:** **Dimethylol urea (DMU)**.
- ☐ **Bridges formed:** Methylene (–CH₂–) **and** methylene-ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–).
- ☐ **Curing agent:** **NH₄Cl** (latent acid, releases HCl).
- ☐ **Cure condition:** **100 – 150 °C**, acidic pH.
- ☐ **F/U ratio:** Wood-adhesive grade = **1.5 – 1.8**; low-emission = **1.05 – 1.2**.
- ☐ **By-product:** **Water (H₂O)** — must be removed during cure to avoid porosity.
- ☐ **Gel point (Carothers):** $p_c = 2/\bar{f} \approx 0.62$ for typical resin.
- ☐ **Density of cured resin:** **1.5 g/cm³**; Refractive index **1.54 – 1.56**.
- ☐ **Free HCHO emission** is the **biggest drawback** → E0/E1/CARB compliance.
- ☐ **Main applications:** Particleboard, MDF, plywood adhesives (interior); laminates; foundry cores; UF foam insulation.
- ☐ **Modified versions:** **MUF** (Melamine-UF — better water resistance), **PUF** (Phenol-UF — better exterior durability).
- ☐ UF is the **cheapest amino resin** but the **least water-resistant**.
- ☐ Compare with: **MF** (more water-resistant, more expensive), **PF** (superior heat & water resistance, dark colour).
- ☐ **CAS Number:** **9011-05-6** (cite if asked in viva).
- ☐ **Use `\vert` or `\mid`, never `|`, in LaTeX inside tables** (markdown safety).
- ☐ **Aminoplast family:** UF + MF + UF/MF blends, all derived from HCHO + amino-group compound.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "UF ⇒ 2-4-2-3-D":**
> **2** raw materials, **4** methylol possibilities, **2** bridges (methylene + ether), **3-D** network. ✔
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyesters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Polyesters # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **polyester** is a polymeric material whose constitutional repeating units are joined together by the **ester functional group (–C(O)O–)**, which is the condensation product formed between a carboxylic acid (–COOH) and a hydroxyl group (–OH). The general IUPAC-style repeat unit of a polyester can be written as: $$\bigl[-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{R}_1-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{O})-\mathrm{R}_2-\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{O})-\bigr]_n$$ where **R₁** represents the diol-derived (glycol) residue and **R₂** represents the diacid-derived (or acid chloride/anhydride) residue. Polyesters are a sub-class of the broader **step-growth (condensation) polymers** and are therefore governed by the **Carothers equations** for molecular weight and extent of reaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Polyesters are categorized under *Step-Growth Polymerization – Condensation Polymers*. The two industrially dominant members of the polyester family are **Poly(ethylene terephthalate) [PET]** and **Poly(butylene terephthalate) [PBT]**, alongside specialty members like **Polycarbonate (PC)**, **Alkyd resins**, and **Unsaturated Polyester Resins (UPR)**. ## 1.2 Classification of Polyesters Polyesters can be classified along three orthogonal axes used by KTU examiners: **1. By Saturation of Backbone** - **Saturated polyesters:** no C=C double bonds in the main chain (e.g., PET, PBT, PC). They are thermoplastic and melt-processable. - **Unsaturated polyesters (UPR):** contain C=C double bonds in the chain (typically from maleic/fumaric anhydride). They are thermosetting after crosslinking with styrene. **2. By Chemical Nature of Diol/Diacid** - **Aliphatic polyesters:** both R₁ and R₂ are aliphatic (e.g., poly(ethylene adipate), PLA). Generally lower Tg/Tm, more biodegradable. - **Aromatic (semi-aromatic) polyesters:** at least one residue is aromatic (e.g., PET – aromatic diacid + aliphatic diol). Higher Tg/Tm, mechanically strong. - **Wholly aromatic polyesters (liquid crystalline polymers, LCPs):** both R₁ and R₂ aromatic (e.g., Vectra® – HBA/HNA copolyester). **3. By Method of Synthesis** - Direct esterification (acid + alcohol, with water removal). - Transesterification (ester + alcohol, with alcohol removal). - Acid chloride route (Schotten–Baumann, with HCl removal). - Ring-opening polymerization of lactones (e.g., ε-caprolactone → PCL). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Handshake Polymer"** > > Imagine a crowded room where every person has **two right hands** (a *diacid*) and another group of people has **two left hands** (a *diol*). Every time a right hand meets a left hand, they **shake** — and the handshake leaves behind a small "drop" of water (H₂O) on the floor. Each diacid must shake hands at **both** of its right hands, and each diol at **both** of its left hands, in order to form a long chain. If even a few people forget to shake with all their hands (low conversion), the chains remain short and the polymer is weak. **High conversion + exact stoichiometric balance = a long, strong chain.** This is precisely the essence of **Carothers' step-growth theory**. > [!TIP] > The same analogy also explains why a slight excess of one monomer (e.g., excess ethylene glycol in PET manufacture) is industrially used — it caps the chain ends, controlling molecular weight exactly, but it does so at the cost of reduced ultimate molecular weight. ## 1.4 Why Polyesters Matter in Engineering Polyesters are among the **top three commodity plastics** in the world by volume, sharing the podium with polyolefins and PVC. They dominate because: - The ester linkage is **polar yet hydrolyzable**, allowing tunable crystallinity. - They can be drawn into **high-tenacity fibers** (PET textile fiber, tire cord). - They are **bottle-grade recyclable** (PET bottles, ~30% global recycling stream). - Unsaturated polyesters form the **matrix of fiber-reinforced plastics (FRP)** used in boat hulls, wind-turbine blades, and bathroom fixtures. - Aromatic polyesters can be liquid-crystalline — used in **electrical/electronic connectors** that must survive lead-free soldering (260 °C). --- **Key constants / metrics used throughout this module:** | Quantity | Typical Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Bond energy of C–O (ester) | **~360 kJ mol⁻¹** | Stronger than C–C (~348) | | Tg of PET | **~80 °C** | Amorphous form | | Tm of PET | **~260 °C** | Crystalline form | | Tg of PBT | **~45 °C** | | | Tm of PBT | **~225 °C** | | | Density of amorphous PET | **1.33 g cm⁻³** | | | Density of crystalline PET | **1.45 g cm⁻³** | | | Intrinsic viscosity [η] (bottle grade) | **0.70 – 0.85 dL g⁻¹** | In o-chlorophenol at 25 °C | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Chemistry of Ester Linkage Formation The **ester group (–C(O)O–)** is the single most important functional group in this module. It can be formed by **four** distinct industrial / laboratory routes — each of which is a favourite KTU question stem. ### 2.1.1 Direct Esterification (Fischer Esterification at Polymer Scale) A carboxylic acid (–COOH) reacts with an alcohol (–OH) in the presence of an acid catalyst (H₂SO₄, p-toluenesulfonic acid, or titanate catalysts like Ti(OBu)₄): $$\mathrm{R{-}COOH} \; + \; \mathrm{HO{-}R'} \; \xrightleftharpoons[\text{cat.}]{\Delta,\; -\mathrm{H_2O}} \; \mathrm{R{-}C(O)O{-}R'} \; + \; \mathrm{H_2O}$$ - **Reversible** — water must be continuously removed (azeotropic distillation, molecular sieves, or N₂ sweep) to drive equilibrium to the right. - **Catalyst toxicity:** acid catalysts can cause discoloration and chain scission at >200 °C. PET manufacture therefore uses **antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃)** or **titanium(IV) butoxide (Ti(OBu)₄)**. ### 2.1.2 Transesterification (Alcoholysis) An ester reacts with a different alcohol; the leaving group is the **lower-boiling alcohol** (often methanol), which is distilled off: $$\mathrm{R{-}C(O)O{-}R_1} \; + \; \mathrm{HO{-}R_2} \; \xrightleftharpoons{\text{cat.}} \; \mathrm{R{-}C(O)O{-}R_2} \; + \; \mathrm{HO{-}R_1}$$ This is the **first stage of the industrial PET process** — dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) + excess ethylene glycol → bis(hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET) + 2 CH₃OH↑. ### 2.1.3 Acid Chloride Route (Schotten–Baumann) Acid chlorides react almost irreversibly with alcohols at low temperature, giving **HCl as byproduct** (neutralized with a tertiary amine like pyridine or triethylamine): $$\mathrm{R{-}COCl} \; + \; \mathrm{HO{-}R'} \; \longrightarrow \; \mathrm{R{-}C(O)O{-}R'} \; + \; \mathrm{HCl}$$ Used for **aromatic polyesters and polycarbonates** where the diacid chloride (e.g., terephthaloyl chloride, phosgene) is reactive enough. Enables **interfacial polymerization** at the water/organic interface. ### 2.1.4 Ring-Opening Polymerization (ROP) of Lactones / Lactides Cyclic esters (lactones, lactides) open under cationic, anionic, or coordination (e.g., Sn(Oct)₂) catalysis: $$\bigl[\mathrm{O{-}C(O){-}(CH_2)_n}\bigr]_m \;\xrightarrow{\text{cat.}}\; \bigl[-\mathrm{O}{-}(CH_2)_n{-}\mathrm{C(O)}{-}\bigr]_m$$ Used to make **polycaprolactone (PCL)**, **polylactic acid (PLA)**, **polyglycolide (PGA)** — all biodegradable polyesters. ## 2.2 The Carothers Framework for Polyesters Because polyesters are step-growth polymers, the **Carothers equation** is the analytical backbone. Two formulations appear in KTU papers. ### 2.2.1 Number-Average Degree of Polymerization, $X_n$ For a **stoichiometrically balanced** A–A + B–B system: $$X_n \;=\; \frac{1}{1-p}$$ For a **non-stoichiometric** system, with stoichiometric imbalance ratio $r = N_A / N_B \;\le\; 1$: $$X_n \;=\; \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$ where $p$ = extent of reaction of the **minority** functional groups. ### 2.2.2 Number-Average Molecular Weight $$\overline{M}_n \;=\; M_0 \cdot X_n$$ where $M_0$ is the average molar mass of one structural (repeat) unit, $M_0 = (M_{AA} + M_{BB})/2$ for an A–A + B–B system. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **every** high-yield formula for the polyesters topic that the KTU Board has asked in the past 5 years. Master these and you secure full marks on numerical questions. | # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | KTU Use | |:-:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ | Carothers, stoichiometric case | Most common | | 2 | $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ | Carothers, imbalanced case | $r \neq 1$ | | 3 | $\overline{M}_n = M_0 \cdot X_n$ | Number-average mol. wt. | Mwt. problems | | 4 | $\overline{X}_w = (1+p)/(1-p)$ | Weight-average DP | Polydispersity | | 5 | $PDI = \overline{X}_w / \overline{X}_n$ | Polydispersity index | PDI = 2 at high p (theory) | | | 6 | $r = N_A/N_B$ | Stoichiometric ratio | $\le 1$ by convention | | | 7 | $p_{\text{Stau}} = \frac{1-r}{2}$ | Gel point (Flory-Stockmayer) | Branched polyesters | | | 8 | Mark–Houwink: $[\eta] = K M^{a}$ | Intrinsic viscosity | PET quality control | | | 9 | $K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{[E][W]}{[A][G]}$ | Equilibrium constant (esterification) | Reactor design | | | 10 | $\eta_{\text{inh}} = \frac{\ln(t/t_0)}{c}$ | Inherent viscosity | Solution viscometry | | > [!NOTE] > **Markdown Safe-Encoding Note:** Throughout this module, absolute values and set-membership are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid$ — never the vertical bar `|`, which would otherwise break the KTU-rendered markdown table above. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice The Carothers equation has a brutal engineering consequence: **to reach $X_n = 100$, you need $p = 0.99$ (99 % reaction).** To reach $X_n = 200$, you need $p = 0.995$. This is why **PET reactors run at 280–290 °C under high vacuum** for hours — the last 1 % of unreacted end-groups dominates molecular weight. Industrial polycondensation reactors are essentially *devices to drive $p$ from 0.97 to 0.995.* The stoichiometric imbalance $r$ is also deliberately engineered. In bottle-grade PET, a slight **excess of ethylene glycol** is used (so $r < 1$) to cap chains with –OH ends, which prevents yellowing during subsequent melt processing. In fiber-grade PET, a closer-to-unity $r$ is used to maximize tensile strength. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Carothers Equation (Stoichiometric Case) This is the single most-tested derivation in the KTU Polymer Technology syllabus. We present it in **eight** logically connected steps. **Step 1 — Functional group bookkeeping.** Let $N_0$ = total initial number of A functional groups (which equals the total number of B groups, by stoichiometric balance). After reaction has proceeded to an **extent of reaction $p$**, the number of A groups that have reacted is $p N_0$. The number of A groups remaining (unreacted) is: $$N_A \;=\; N_0 - p\,N_0 \;=\; N_0(1-p)$$ **Step 2 — Total number of molecules remaining.** Every time a bond forms between one A and one B, the total count of *molecules* in the system drops by exactly 1. (Two molecules become one.) Since $pN_0$ A groups have reacted, $pN_0$ new bonds have formed, so the number of bonds formed equals $pN_0$ and the number of molecules has decreased by $pN_0$: $$N \;=\; N_0 - p\,N_0 \;=\; N_0(1-p)$$ **Step 3 — Definition of degree of polymerization.** By definition, the number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$ is the ratio of the *initial* number of molecules to the *current* number of molecules: $$X_n \;=\; \frac{N_0}{N} \;=\; \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1-p}$$ This is the **Carothers equation** in its most famous form. **Step 4 — Generalization to imbalanced stoichiometry.** Suppose the diacid (A–A) and diol (B–B) are *not* in exact balance. Let $N_A^0$ and $N_B^0$ be the initial numbers of A and B groups, with $N_A^0 \le N_B^0$ (so A is the minority). Define the **stoichiometric ratio**: $$r \;=\; \frac{N_A^0}{N_B^0} \quad \text{with} \quad 0 < r \le 1$$ **Step 5 — Unreacted groups at extent of reaction $p$ of the minority.** At extent $p$ of the **minority** A groups, the number of A groups reacted is $p N_A^0$, so the number of unreacted A groups is $N_A^0(1-p)$. The B groups also reacted to the same absolute number $p N_A^0$, so unreacted B groups equal $N_B^0 - p N_A^0$. The total molecules $N$ in the system equals the unreacted end-group molecules plus the new polymer molecules. By an exact balance: $$N \;=\; N_A^0(1-p) \;+\; \bigl[N_B^0 - p\,N_A^0\bigr] \;=\; N_A^0(1-p) \;+\; N_B^0(1-p r) \;-\;\text{etc.}$$ The standard Carothers algebra (omitted here for brevity — see any KTU textbook) yields the elegant result: $$X_n \;=\; \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$ **Step 6 — Limiting cases.** - If $r = 1$ (perfect balance): $X_n = (2)/(2-2p) = 1/(1-p)$. ✓ - If $p = 1$ (complete reaction of minority): $X_n = (1+r)/(1-r)$. This is the **maximum possible $X_n$** for that imbalance. - If $r \to 0$ (gross imbalance): $X_n \to 1$, i.e., *no polymer forms* — a tiny excess of one monomer **caps the chains** and prevents growth. **Step 7 — Conversion of $X_n$ to $\overline{M}_n$.** The number-average molecular weight is: $$\overline{M}_n \;=\; M_0 \cdot X_n \;=\; M_0 \cdot \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$ where $M_0$ is the average molecular weight of the *repeat unit*. For an A–A + B–B system: $$M_0 \;=\; \frac{M_{AA} + M_{BB}}{2}$$ For PET (C₁₀H₈O₄ repeat unit): $M_0 = (190.15 + 62.07)/2 \approx ... $ actually the repeat unit of PET is C₁₀H₈O₄ = 192.17 g mol⁻¹, so we use 192.17 directly because ethylene glycol contributes 1 oxygen and terephthalic acid contributes the other 3, with one molecule of water lost per repeat. **Step 8 — Polydispersity index.** For ideal step-growth with $r = 1$, the PDI is: $$PDI \;=\; \frac{\overline{X}_w}{\overline{X}_n} \;=\; 1 + p$$ As $p \to 1$, $PDI \to 2$. This is a **fingerprint of step-growth polymers** and is one of the most-tested KTU facts. ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Solved, Board Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2018, modified]:** > A polyester is synthesized from adipic acid (A–A, $M = 146$ g mol⁻¹) and ethylene glycol (B–B, $M = 62$ g mol⁻¹) with a 2 % molar excess of ethylene glycol. The reaction is stopped at $p = 0.970$ (extent of reaction of the acid groups). Calculate $X_n$, $\overline{M}_n$, and the maximum possible $\overline{M}_n$ if stoichiometry were perfect. **Solution:** Stoichiometric ratio: $r = N_A^0 / N_B^0$. With 2 % excess of B–B, $N_B^0 = 1.02\,N_A^0$, so $$r = \frac{1}{1.02} = 0.9804$$ Repeat unit mass: $M_0 = (146 + 62)/2 = 104$ g mol⁻¹. $$X_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1 + 0.9804}{1 + 0.9804 - 2(0.9804)(0.970)}$$ Computing denominator: $1 + 0.9804 - 1.9020 = 0.0784$. $$X_n = \frac{1.9804}{0.0784} \approx 25.26$$ $$\overline{M}_n = 104 \times 25.26 \approx 2\,627\;\text{g mol}^{-1}$$ If $r = 1$ (perfect balance) and $p = 0.970$: $$X_n^{\max} = \frac{1}{1-0.970} = 33.33 \qquad \overline{M}_n^{\max} = 104 \times 33.33 \approx 3\,466\;\text{g mol}^{-1}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** the 2 % imbalance cuts the achievable molecular weight by ~24 %. This is the **exact reason** why industrial PET reactors use precise computer-controlled feed ratios and on-line viscosity sensors. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix of Polyester Families | Polyester | Repeat Unit | Tg / Tm (°C) | Key Property | Engineering Use | |:---|:---|:---:|:---|:---| | **PET** (Polyethylene terephthalate) | –[O–CH₂CH₂–O–C(O)–C₆H₄–C(O)]– | 80 / 260 | High strength, transparent | Bottles, fibers, films | | **PBT** (Polybutylene terephthalate) | –[O–(CH₂)₄–O–C(O)–C₆H₄–C(O)]– | 45 / 225 | Fast crystallization | Electrical connectors, gears | | **PC** (Polycarbonate, BPA-based) | –[O–C₆H₄–C(CH₃)₂–C₆H₄–O–C(O)]– | 150 / – (amorphous) | High impact, transparent | CDs, helmets, glazings | | **PEN** (Polyethylene naphthalate) | –[O–CH₂CH₂–O–C(O)–C₁₀H₆–C(O)]– | 122 / 268 | Better barrier than PET | Hot-fill bottles | | **PLA** (Polylactic acid) | –[O–CH(CH₃)–C(O)]– | 58 / 175 | Biodegradable | 3-D printing, sutures | | **PCL** (Polycaprolactone) | –[O–(CH₂)₅–C(O)]– | –60 / 60 | Soft, low Tm | Drug delivery | | **UPR** (Unsaturated polyester) | –[O–(CH₂)₂–O–C(O)–CH=CH–C(O)]– | n/a (thermoset) | Crosslinkable | FRP, boat hulls | | **Alkyd resin** | Phthalic anhydride + glycerol + fatty acid | n/a (thermoset) | Air-drying | Paints, coatings | | **Vectra® (LCP)** | HBA/HNA copolyester | n/a (anisotropic) | Self-reinforcing | SMT electronics | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reaction Mechanism Flow (Mermaid) The block-level flow below summarizes the **four routes to the ester linkage** introduced in Section 2, and routes them into the polymer repeat-unit formation logic. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Diacid A-A
HOOC-R-COOH]:::raw A2[Diol B-B
HO-R-OH]:::raw A3[Diacid chloride A-A
ClOC-R-COCl]:::raw A4[Diester AA' / DMT
CH3OOC-R-COOCH3]:::raw A5[Lactone / Lactide
cyclic ester]:::raw R1[/Direct Esterification
acid + alcohol
loss H2O/]:::rxn R2[/Transesterification
ester + alcohol
loss CH3OH/]:::rxn R3[/Schotten-Baumann
acid chloride + alcohol
loss HCl/]:::rxn R4[/Ring-Opening Polymerization
lactone ring opening
no byproduct/]:::rxn CAT{{Catalyst
H2SO4 or Ti OBu 4
or Sb2O3}}:::cat H2O[(H2O removed)]:::by MEOH[(CH3OH removed)]:::by HCL[(HCl neutralised)]:::by PU[/Repeat unit formed
ester linkage -C O O-/]:::product POLY[/Step-growth polyester
long chain/]:::product IND[/Industrial PET reactor
280 C high vacuum/]:::product A1 --> R1 A2 --> R1 R1 --> H2O A4 --> R2 A2 --> R2 R2 --> MEOH A3 --> R3 A2 --> R3 R3 --> HCL A5 --> R4 CAT --- R1 CAT --- R2 R1 --> PU R2 --> PU R3 --> PU R4 --> PU PU --> POLY POLY --> IND classDef raw fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e classDef rxn fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f classDef cat fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,color:#581c87 classDef by fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d classDef product fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d ``` ## 4.2 Industrial PET Production Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FEED[Feed Preparation] F1[Ethylene Glycol]:::feed F2[Purified Terephthalic Acid PTA]:::feed F3[Dimethyl Terephthalate DMT]:::feed end subgraph ESTER[Esterification Stage 230 to 250 C] E1[Slurry mixing]:::unit E2[BHET monomer formation]:::unit E3[Byproduct water distilled off]:::by end subgraph POLY[Polycondensation Stage 275 to 290 C high vacuum] P1[Prepolymer reactor]:::unit P2[Finisher reactor vertical agitated]:::unit P3[EG vapor condensed and recycled]:::by end subgraph SSP[Solid-State Polycondensation] S1[Crystallizer]:::unit S2[Reactor 200 to 230 C N2]:::unit S3[IV rise 0.6 to 0.95 dL per g]:::unit end subgraph OUT[Product] O1[Bottle grade chips]:::out O2[Fiber grade chips]:::out O3[Film grade chips]:::out end F1 --> E1 F2 --> E1 F3 --> E1 E1 --> E2 --> E3 E2 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 P2 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 S3 --> O1 S3 --> O2 S3 --> O3 classDef feed fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#1e3a8a classDef unit fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#312e81 classDef by fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#7f1d1d classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d ``` ## 4.3 Polyester Type Decision Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Polyester repeat unit R1 and R2]:::start S --> Q1{Are there C=C double bonds in main chain?} Q1 -- Yes --> UNSAT[Unsaturated Polyester UPR
thermoset with styrene
FRP matrix]:::branch Q1 -- No --> SAT[Saturated Polyester
thermoplastic]:::branch SAT --> Q2{Is at least one of R1 or R2 aromatic?} Q2 -- Aromatic diacid only --> SEMI[Semi-aromatic polyester
e.g. PET, PBT, PEN]:::branch Q2 -- Both aromatic --> AR[Wholly aromatic polyester LCP
e.g. Vectra]:::branch Q2 -- Neither aromatic --> ALIPH[Aliphatic polyester
e.g. PLA, PCL, PGA]:::branch ALIPH --> Q3{Is the repeat unit a ring-opened lactone or lactid?} Q3 -- Yes --> BIO[Biodegradable polyester
used in medical and packaging]:::branch Q3 -- No --> ALIPH2[Conventional aliphatic polyester
flexible low Tm]:::branch SEMI --> Q4{Diacid is terephthalic acid?} Q4 -- Yes --> PETF[PET family
bottles fibers films]:::branch Q4 -- Naphthalenedicarboxylic acid --> PENF[PEN family
high barrier]:::branch classDef start fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#713f12 classDef branch fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#831843 ``` ## 4.4 The Ester Linkage: A Schematic Mapping Because the ester group itself is too small to physically draw in Mermaid, we present a **logical structural map** of its bonding and its breakdown under hydrolysis — a key KTU concept (degradation, recycling). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EST[Functional Ester Group C O O] E1[Carbonyl Carbon sp2]:::atom E2[Double bond to O]:::atom E3[Single bond to O ether side]:::atom E4[Two alkyl or aryl groups R and R prime]:::atom end subgraph HYD[Hydrolysis Reverse Reaction] H1[Water H-OH attacks carbonyl]:::step H2[Tetrahedral intermediate]:::step H3[R-prime OH leaves]:::step H4[Regenerates COOH and OH]:::step end EST -- exposed to hot water or base --> H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 classDef atom fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#1e1b4b classDef step fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#78350f ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Model answers below are written in the same concise register that a KTU external examiner expects — typically 8–12 lines, with at least one equation and one industrial example. --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Define the term "polyester." With a neat reaction scheme, explain the formation of poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) by the transesterification route.* **Model Answer:** A polyester is a polymer whose constitutional repeating units are joined by **ester (–COO–) groups**, formed by the condensation of polybasic acids (or their derivatives) with polyhydric alcohols. **Transesterification route to PET (also called the DMT route):** $$\mathrm{CH_3OOC{-}C_6H_4{-}COOCH_3} \; + \; 2\,\mathrm{HOCH_2CH_2OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{Zn(OAc)}_2]{200^\circ\mathrm{C}}\; \mathrm{HOCH_2CH_2OOC{-}C_6H_4{-}COOCH_2CH_2OH} \; + \; 2\,\mathrm{CH_3OH}\uparrow$$ The product **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)** is then heated at 270–290 °C under high vacuum (< 1 mbar) in the presence of Sb₂O₃, when transesterification between BHET molecules liberates ethylene glycol and builds up the long PET chain: $$n\,\mathrm{BHET} \;\longrightarrow\; \bigl[-\mathrm{OCH_2CH_2OOC{-}C_6H_4CO-}\bigr]_n \; + \; n\,\mathrm{HOCH_2CH_2OH}\uparrow$$ The byproduct methanol (in the first stage) and ethylene glycol (in the second) are removed continuously to drive equilibrium toward high molecular weight. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Distinguish between saturated and unsaturated polyesters. Give one example of each and one end-use.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Saturated Polyester | Unsaturated Polyester | |---|---|---| | C=C in main chain | Absent | Present (typically from maleic anhydride) | | Physical form | Thermoplastic | Thermoset (after crosslinking) | | Processing | Injection/stretch-blow molding | Casting/compression molding | | Example | Poly(ethylene terephthalate) PET | Propylene glycol–maleic anhydride–phthalic anhydride condensate dissolved in styrene | | End-use | Beverage bottles, textile fibers | Boat hulls, automotive panels, bathroom fixtures | The C=C bonds in the unsaturated polyester are **crosslinked by free-radical copolymerization with styrene** (initiated by MEKP/Co-naphthenate), giving a rigid 3-D network. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > (1) On numerical Carothers questions, **students almost always confuse the stoichiometric ratio convention** — remember $r = N_{\text{minority}}/N_{\text{majority}} \le 1$. If you flip it, you get $X_n < 1$ (impossible) and lose 3 marks instantly. > (2) When the question gives "2 % excess glycol," convert it to $r = 1/1.02 = 0.9804$, **not** $r = 1.02$. Excess of B means minority is A. > (3) On essay questions about PET manufacture, **students forget to mention the catalyst** (Sb₂O₃, Ti(OBu)₄ or germanium oxide). Mentioning it earns the "process" mark. > (4) For unsaturated polyesters, you must explain **why styrene is added** (acts as both crosslinker and viscosity reducer) — not just "to crosslink." --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2019, modified]* **(a) Derive the Carothers equation for the number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$ of a step-growth polymerization in the case of (i) equimolar functional groups, and (ii) non-equimolar functional groups. State clearly the meaning of every symbol.** **(7 marks)** **(b) In the synthesis of poly(ethylene adipate) from adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH) and ethylene glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH), an excess of 1.5 mol % of ethylene glycol is used. The reaction is stopped when 96.0 % of the acid groups have reacted. Calculate: (i) the stoichiometric ratio $r$, (ii) the number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$, (iii) the number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n$ (take the molecular weight of the adipic-acid residue as 112 g mol⁻¹ and the ethylene-glycol residue as 28 g mol⁻¹). Comment briefly on what would happen if the excess of ethylene glycol were increased to 5 mol %.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation** (i) **Equimolar case (r = 1):** Let $N_0$ be the initial number of A groups (= number of B groups by stoichiometry). After reaction has reached an extent $p$, the number of A groups remaining is $N_0(1-p)$, which equals the number of *molecules* in the system (since each unreacted A marks one chain end of a molecule). Initial molecules = $N_0$ (each A group is the end of a monomer/dimer/etc.). At extent $p$, each A–B bond formation reduces molecule count by 1, and $pN_0$ bonds have formed, hence molecules = $N_0 - pN_0 = N_0(1-p)$. Therefore $$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$ (ii) **Non-equimolar case:** Let $N_A$ and $N_B$ be the initial A and B groups, with $N_A < N_B$ and $r = N_A/N_B$. At extent $p$ of the A groups, the number of unreacted A groups is $N_A(1-p)$, the number of unreacted B groups is $N_B - pN_A$, and the number of molecules is the sum of unreacted A and B end molecules minus the polymer molecules (counted once each): $$N_{\text{mol}} = N_A(1-p) + \bigl[N_B - pN_A\bigr]$$ After algebraic simplification (counting each polymer chain once): $$X_n = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A(1-p) + N_B - pN_A} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_B + N_A(1-2p)} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$ **Symbols:** $N_0$ = initial number of molecules; $p$ = extent of reaction; $r$ = stoichiometric ratio (always $\le 1$); $X_n$ = number-average degree of polymerization. **Part (b) — Numerical** (i) $r = N_A/N_B$. With 1.5 mol % excess B (glycol), $N_B = 1.015 N_A$, so $$r = \frac{1}{1.015} = 0.9852$$ (ii) $p = 0.960$. $$X_n = \frac{1 + 0.9852}{1 + 0.9852 - 2(0.9852)(0.960)} = \frac{1.9852}{1.9852 - 1.8916} = \frac{1.9852}{0.0936} \approx 21.21$$ (iii) $M_0 = (M_{AA} + M_{BB})/2 = (112 + 28)/2 = 70$ g mol⁻¹. $$\overline{M}_n = 70 \times 21.21 \approx 1\,485\;\text{g mol}^{-1}$$ **Comment on 5 mol % excess glycol:** $r = 1/1.05 = 0.9524$, $X_n = (1+0.9524)/(1+0.9524-2\cdot0.9524\cdot0.960) = 1.9524/(1.9524 - 1.8286) = 1.9524/0.1238 \approx 15.77$, $\overline{M}_n \approx 70 \times 15.77 \approx 1\,104$ g mol⁻¹. The molecular weight **drops by ~25 %** as the imbalance grows — this is why industrial polyesters use the *minimum* excess needed to control chain ends, never a large excess. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2020, modified]* **(a) With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the industrial manufacture of poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) by the DMT route. Explain the role of each catalyst and justify why the polycondensation stage is carried out under high vacuum.** **(7 marks)** **(b) (i) What are alkyd resins? Write the general preparation scheme of a long-oil alkyd. (ii) Compare, in a tabular form, the properties of PET, PBT, and PEN with respect to their glass-transition temperature, melting point, and one major engineering application.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — PET by DMT route** **Flow diagram (textual form, since the actual Mermaid diagram is in Section 4.2 above):** DMT + EG → (transesterification, 200 °C, Zn(OAc)₂ catalyst) → BHET + 2 CH₃OH↑ → (polycondensation, 270–290 °C, Sb₂O₃ catalyst, < 1 mbar) → molten PET + EG↑ → (extrusion → chips → SSP at 220 °C) → bottle-grade chips. **Catalyst roles:** - **Zinc acetate [Zn(OAc)₂]** in the first stage: catalyzes the transesterification of DMT by EG at 180–210 °C. It is cheap and active at this temperature. - **Antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃)** in the second stage: stable and active at 270–290 °C, the temperature at which EG is removed and the chain builds. Antimony is preferred over Zn²⁺ because Zn²⁺ would catalyze **thermo-oxidative degradation** at the higher temperature, causing yellowing. - (In modern processes, **titanium(IV) butoxide [Ti(OBu)₄]** is used as a "dual" catalyst for both stages, or **germanium oxide (GeO₂)** for ultra-pure, low-color fiber-grade PET.) **Justification for high vacuum:** The equilibrium constant for PET polycondensation is unfavorable at high temperatures; the byproduct (ethylene glycol) must be *continuously removed* to drive the reaction forward. High vacuum reduces the partial pressure of EG below its equilibrium vapor pressure, ensuring rapid escape from the melt. It also minimizes thermal side reactions (degradation, acetaldehyde formation). **Part (b) — (i) Alkyd Resins** **Definition:** Alkyd resins are **oil-modified polyester resins** made by condensation of polybasic acids (phthalic anhydride or isophthalic acid), polyhydric alcohols (glycerol, pentaerythritol), and **fatty acids from drying/semi-drying oils** (linseed, soybean, coconut). **General preparation of a long-oil alkyd:** $$\text{Phthalic anhydride} \; + \; \text{Glycerol} \; + \; \text{Linseed-oil fatty acids} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Long-oil alkyd} \; + \; \mathrm{H_2O}$$ "Long-oil" means the oil length is **> 60 %** by mass, giving air-drying, flexible coatings (paints, varnishes). Curing occurs by **auto-oxidative crosslinking** of the unsaturated fatty-acid chains. **(ii) Tabular comparison:** | Property | PET | PBT | PEN | |---|---|---|---| | Glass-transition temperature, Tg | **80 °C** | **45 °C** | **122 °C** | | Melting point, Tm | **260 °C** | **225 °C** | **268 °C** | | Crystallization rate | Slow | Fast | Moderate | | Major application | Beverage bottles, textile fiber, magnetic-tape base | Automotive electrical connectors, precision gears | Hot-fill bottles, high-barrier films | **Why PBT has a lower Tg than PET:** the extra two –CH₂– groups in the 1,4-butanediol residue give a more flexible, less tightly packed chain, lowering both Tg and Tm. PEN is higher than PET because the **rigid naphthalene ring** restricts chain mobility and increases aromatic π–π interactions. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-revision checklist (final-night-read):** - **Definition:** Polyester = polymer with **–C(O)O–** in the backbone, formed by step-growth. - **Four routes to the ester group:** direct esterification, transesterification, Schotten–Baumann (acid chloride), ring-opening polymerization of lactones/lactides. - **Carothers equation, stoichiometric:** $X_n = 1/(1-p)$. - **Carothers equation, imbalanced:** $X_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$, where $r = N_{\min}/N_{\text{maj}} \le 1$. - **To get $X_n = 100$, need $p = 0.99$** — the "brutal arithmetic of step-growth." - **Theoretical PDI for step-growth = 2 at $p \to 1$** — distinguishes it from chain-growth (PDI ≈ 1.5–2 with living polymers being 1.0–1.1). - **Industrial PET process order:** (1) Esterification OR transesterification → BHET. (2) Polycondensation under high vacuum with Sb₂O₃. (3) Optional SSP for bottle grade. - **Catalysts:** Zn(OAc)₂ for transesterification, Sb₂O₃ or Ti(OBu)₄ for polycondensation. - **PET** repeat unit is C₁₀H₈O₄, $M_0 = 192.17$ g mol⁻¹. Tm = 260 °C, Tg = 80 °C. - **PBT** uses 1,4-butanediol (more flexible, lower Tg/Tm than PET). - **PEN** uses 2,6-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid (higher Tg/Tm, better barrier, used in hot-fill bottles). - **PC (polycarbonate):** aromatic polyester of bisphenol-A + phosgene or diphenyl carbonate; amorphous, Tg = 150 °C, used in CDs and safety helmets. - **Unsaturated polyester (UPR):** contains maleic anhydride-derived C=C, dissolved in styrene monomer, crosslinked with MEKP/Co initiator to give a thermoset FRP matrix. - **Alkyd resins:** oil-modified polyesters for paints — "long-oil" = > 60 % oil, "short-oil" = < 40 % oil. - **Biodegradable polyesters:** PLA, PCL, PGA, PHB — all derived from renewable feedstocks or lactone ROP, used in medical implants and 3-D printing. - **Theoretical gel point** (Stockmayer): $p_c = (1-r)/2$ — only relevant for branched polyesters, not linear ones. - **Hydrolysis of the ester group** is the *Achilles heel* of polyesters — why PET is sensitive to hot, humid processing and why recycling must remove water. > **End of KTU-Premier-Engine Notes for "Polyesters" — Module 3, PECHT523.**

topics.title: Silicones. Bioplastics - Poly Lactic Acid (PLA). Elastomers - Butyl Rubber - Nitrile Rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Silicones. Bioplastics - Poly Lactic Acid (PLA). Elastomers - Butyl Rubber - Nitrile Rubber # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Silicones (Polysiloxanes) **Formal Definition:** Silicones are a class of **semi-organic polymers** (technically *polyorganosiloxanes*) composed of an alternating backbone of **silicon (Si)** and **oxygen (O)** atoms, with organic side groups (typically methyl, phenyl, or vinyl) attached to the silicon atoms. The general repeating unit is given by the formula $(\text{R}_2\text{SiO})_n$, where R represents an organic substituent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Si–O–Si bond is one of the strongest single bonds in polymer chemistry with a bond dissociation energy of approximately **452 kJ/mol**, far higher than the C–C bond (~346 kJ/mol). This is the *fundamental reason* for the exceptional thermal stability of silicones. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of common plastics (polyethylene, PVC) as a **"beaded necklace"** where the beads are carbon atoms linked together. Now imagine replacing those carbon beads with **silicon atoms**, and inserting an **oxygen atom between every pair of silicon atoms** like tiny flexible springs. The result is a backbone that can wiggle, twist, and resist heat far better than carbon chains. The organic side groups (like methyl –CH₃) act like little "branches" that make the polymer compatible with oils, water-repellent surfaces, and flexible rubbers. This is precisely why silicone sealants stay flexible from −60 °C to +250 °C — a range where ordinary rubber would either freeze or melt. **Key Metrics & Constants:** | Property | Value/Range | |---|---| | Si–O bond length | **0.164 nm** | | Si–O–Si bond angle | **~143°** (highly flexible) | | Glass transition temperature (PDMS) | **−127 °C** | | Service temperature range | **−60 °C to +250 °C** | | Dielectric strength | **~16–20 kV/mm** | --- ## 1.2 Bioplastics — Poly(Lactic Acid) (PLA) **Formal Definition:** Poly(lactic acid) is a **biodegradable, aliphatic thermoplastic polyester** synthesized from renewable resources such as corn starch, sugarcane, or cassava. It is produced by the **ring-opening polymerization (ROP)** of the cyclic dimer *lactide*, which is itself derived from lactic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH(OH)COOH}$). > [!NOTE] > **Why "Bio"?** PLA is **bio-based** (made from renewable feedstocks) AND **biodegradable** (degrades under industrial composting conditions in ~3–6 months). This dual character distinguishes it from earlier "bioplastics" like bio-PE, which is bio-based but NOT biodegradable. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine **milk curdling** — the souring of milk produces lactic acid through bacterial fermentation. Now imagine stitching millions of these lactic acid molecules end-to-end like a chain of paper clips, each clip linked to the next by an **ester bond (–O–C(=O)–)**. The resulting chain is PLA. The "paper clip" backbone is rigid enough to be a solid plastic at room temperature, but the ester linkage has a built-in "weak switch" — enzymes in compost heaps can break these switches, making the entire material disappear into water and CO₂ within months. This is why PLA is the *workhorse* of modern 3D printing and the global bioplastic packaging market. **Key Metrics:** | Property | Value/Range | |---|---| | Glass transition ($T_g$) | **~55–60 °C** | | Melting point ($T_m$) | **~150–170 °C** | | Tensile strength | **~50–70 MPa** | | Density | **1.24 g/cm³** | | Biodegradation time (industrial compost) | **~90 days** | --- ## 1.3 Elastomers — Butyl Rubber (IIR) **Formal Definition:** Butyl rubber (IIR — *Isobutylene Isoprene Rubber*) is a **synthetic copolymer of isobutylene (≈98 mol%) and a small amount of isoprene (≈2 mol%)**, produced by **low-temperature cationic copolymerization** using $\text{AlCl}_3$ as a Lewis acid catalyst in methyl chloride solvent at approximately **−100 °C**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Magic" of Butyl Rubber:** The methyl groups on every other carbon of the backbone (from isobutylene) create a tightly packed, sterically hindered structure. This packing makes the polymer **virtually impermeable to gases** — its air permeability is roughly **8–10 times lower** than natural rubber. This is why it dominates the inner-tube and tire-liner market. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of natural rubber as a **"sponge" at the molecular level** — gases can squeeze between its loosely-packed chains. Now imagine a polymer where every other carbon carries **two bulky methyl groups (–CH₃)₂**, like a chain of dumbbells. Air molecules trying to sneak through must navigate a maze of these bulky groups, and they simply cannot. This dense, "molecular traffic jam" is what makes butyl rubber the *champion* of air retention. It is the same reason your bicycle tube holds air for months and your car tire's inner liner can survive potholes and temperature swings. **Key Metrics:** | Property | Value/Range | |---|---| | Isoprene content | **~1.5–2.0 mol%** | | Mooney viscosity (ML 1+8 @ 100 °C) | **45–80** | | Air permeability (vs NR = 100) | **~10** | | Service temperature | **−40 °C to +120 °C** | --- ## 1.4 Elastomers — Nitrile Rubber (NBR) **Formal Definition:** Nitrile rubber, also called **NBR (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber)**, **Buna-N**, or **acrylonitrile-butadiene rubber**, is a synthetic copolymer of **butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂)** and **acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH–CN)**. The acrylonitrile content typically ranges from **15% to 50%** by weight, which directly controls the oil resistance. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Higher acrylonitrile (ACN) content → better oil resistance, but worse low-temperature flexibility. This is a classic exam trade-off question. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a regular rubber band (natural rubber) dropped into a beaker of engine oil — within hours, it swells, softens, and falls apart because oil molecules sneak between the non-polar hydrocarbon chains. Now picture a rubber where **polar "anchors" (the –C≡N nitrile groups) are studded along the chain** like tiny magnets. When oil approaches, these magnets repel it because "like dissolves like" — polar attracts polar, non-polar attracts non-polar. Since engine oil is non-polar, the polar nitrile groups *refuse* to mix with it. The more anchors you add, the more oil-proof the rubber becomes. That is exactly why NBR gloves, fuel hoses, and O-rings survive years in gasoline and petroleum. **Key Metrics:** | Property | Value/Range | |---|---| | Acrylonitrile content | **15–50 wt%** | | Glass transition ($T_g$) | **−40 °C to −20 °C** (rises with ACN) | | Oil swell (ASTM Oil #3, 70 h @ 100 °C) | **−5% to +25%** | | Tensile strength | **7–25 MPa** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Silicones — Synthesis Routes ### 2.1.1 Direct Process (Rochow–Müller Process) The industrial workhorse for producing **methylchlorosilanes**, the precursors of silicones. $$\text{Si} + 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu catalyst, } 300\,°\text{C}} (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiCl}_2$$ The product $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiCl}_2$ is called **dimethyldichlorosilane (DDS)** — the *primary monomer* for silicone polymer synthesis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why copper catalyst?** Copper forms a Cu₃Si alloy with silicon at the surface, which is the active site for the reaction. Without Cu, the activation energy is too high and the reaction does not proceed at practical rates. ### 2.1.2 Hydrolysis & Condensation The chlorosilanes are hydrolyzed to silanols, which then condense to form siloxane bonds: $$\begin{aligned} (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiCl}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} &\rightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{Si(OH)}_2 + 2\,\text{HCl} \\ n\,(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{Si(OH)}_2 &\rightarrow [(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiO}]_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned}$$ ### 2.1.3 Grignard Method (Lab-scale) Used when a specific single monomer is needed (e.g., phenylsilicones): $$\text{SiCl}_4 + 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{MgBr} \rightarrow (\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)_2\text{SiCl}_2 + 2\,\text{MgBrCl}$$ **Real-world utility:** Silicones are used in **aerospace sealants, medical implants (breast implants, catheters), water-repellent coatings for masonry, antifoaming agents in food processing, and high-voltage insulators**. --- ## 2.2 PLA — Synthesis Pathways ### 2.2.1 Two-Step Industrial Route **Step 1:** Lactic acid fermentation from sugars: $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Lactobacillus}} 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CH(OH)COOH}$$ **Step 2:** Lactic acid → Lactide → PLA via ROP: $$\begin{aligned} 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CH(OH)COOH} &\xrightarrow{\text{dehydration, ZnO}} \text{Lactide (cyclic dimer)} \\ \text{Lactide} &\xrightarrow{\text{Sn(Oct)}_2, \, 180\,°\text{C}} \text{PLA} \end{aligned}$$ ### 2.2.2 Stereoisomerism — Why It Matters Lactic acid has a chiral center, so lactide exists in three forms: - **L-lactide** (two L-units) → **PLLA** (semi-crystalline, $T_m \approx 170\,°\text{C}$) - **D-lactide** (two D-units) → **PDLA** (semi-crystalline) - **meso-lactide** (one L, one D) → **PDLLA** (amorphous, $T_m$ disappears) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Optical purity of lactide directly determines PLA crystallinity, melting point, and degradation rate. Industrial PLA is typically 95–98% L-isomer. **Real-world utility:** PLA is the dominant material in **fused-deposition-modeling (FDM) 3D printing, biomedical sutures, drug-delivery microspheres, compostable food packaging (cups, cutlery), and absorbable bone-fixation screws**. --- ## 2.3 Butyl Rubber — Cationic Copolymerization ### 2.3.1 Mechanism The reaction proceeds via a **carbocationic chain-growth mechanism** initiated by $\text{AlCl}_3$ co-initiated with a trace of water (which generates $\text{H}^+\text{AlCl}_3\text{OH}^-$ as the true initiator): $$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C=CH}_2 + \text{CH}_2=\text{C(CH}_3)\text{CH=CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{AlCl}_3 / \text{CH}_3\text{Cl, } -100\,°\text{C}} \text{Polymer}$$ ### 2.3.2 Role of Each Monomer | Monomer | Function | Resulting Feature | |---|---|---| | **Isobutylene** (98%) | Provides saturated, sterically hindered backbone | Air impermeability, oxidative stability | | **Isoprene** (2%) | Provides residual C=C unsaturation | **Cure site for sulfur vulcanization** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Without isoprene, butyl rubber could NOT be vulcanized.** Pure polyisobutylene is a saturated polymer with no cure sites. The 2% isoprene provides the double bonds that allow sulfur crosslinking. **Real-world utility:** Tire inner liners, pharmaceutical stoppers, vacuum seals, chewing gum base, gas-mask bladders, pond liners, and shock absorbers for sensitive electronics. --- ## 2.4 Nitrile Rubber — Emulsion Copolymerization ### 2.4.1 The Reaction Conducted in **water** using a free-radical initiator (e.g., **potassium persulfate, K₂S₂O₈**) at 5–30 °C — this is the classic *cold emulsion* or *hot emulsion* process: $$n\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CH–CH=CH}_2 + m\,\text{CH}_2\text{=CH–CN} \xrightarrow{\text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8, \text{ soap, water}} \text{NBR}$$ ### 2.4.2 Why ACN Content Is the Master Variable | ACN Content | Effect | |---|---| | **Low (15–25%)** | Excellent low-temperature flexibility, poor oil resistance | | **Medium (25–35%)** | Balanced — most common "medium-NBR" grade | | **High (35–50%)** | Excellent oil/fuel resistance, poor low-T flexibility | > [!NOTE] > **The rule of thumb:** Each 1% increase in ACN content raises $T_g$ by approximately **0.7–1.0 °C** and reduces the oil swell by roughly **0.6%**. **Real-world utility:** Automotive fuel hoses, oil-resistant gaskets, O-rings, hydraulic seals, disposable nitrile gloves (the pandemic-era icon), printer rollers, and conveyor belts for oily food processing. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important summary** for KTU 2024 ESE preparation on this topic. Memorize the parameters and trade-offs. | Parameter | Silicones | PLA | Butyl Rubber | Nitrile Rubber | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Polymer type** | Polysiloxane (semi-inorganic) | Aliphatic polyester | Elastomer (copolymer) | Elastomer (copolymer) | | **Monomer(s)** | $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiCl}_2$ | Lactide (from lactic acid) | Isobutylene + Isoprene | Butadiene + Acrylonitrile | | **Polymerization** | Hydrolysis + polycondensation | Ring-opening polymerization (ROP) | Cationic (Lewis acid) | Free-radical emulsion | | **Catalyst** | $\text{HCl}$ (hydrolysis) | $\text{Sn(Oct)}_2$ | $\text{AlCl}_3$ | $\text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8$ | | **Temperature** | 300 °C (Rochow) | 180 °C (ROP) | −100 °C | 5–30 °C (cold) / 50 °C (hot) | | **Key property** | Thermal stability (−60 to +250 °C) | Biodegradability | Air impermeability | Oil resistance | | **$T_g$** | −127 °C (PDMS) | 55–60 °C | −67 °C | −40 to −20 °C | | **Tensile strength** | 5–10 MPa (elastomer grade) | 50–70 MPa | 7–21 MPa | 7–25 MPa | | **Master variable** | R-group on Si | Lactide stereochemistry | Isoprene % | ACN % | | **Headline application** | Sealants, implants | 3D printing, packaging | Tire inner tubes | Fuel hoses, gloves | --- ## 2.6 The "Why & How" Logic Chain (Syllabus-Aligned Reasoning) 1. **Why does Si–O backbone give thermal stability?** The Si–O bond (452 kJ/mol) is much stronger than C–C (346 kJ/mol). The Si–O–Si bond angle (~143°) is highly flexible, so the chain absorbs thermal energy as *bond bending* instead of breaking. 2. **Why is PLA biodegradable but bio-PE is not?** PLA contains hydrolyzable **ester bonds** that enzymes (e.g., *Burkholderia cepacia* lipase) can cleave. Bio-PE has a non-hydrolyzable C–C backbone, identical to fossil PE. 3. **Why is butyl rubber airtight?** Bulky gem-dimethyl groups $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}<$) along the chain create steric hindrance that **physically blocks** gas diffusion through the polymer matrix. 4. **Why does ACN control oil resistance in NBR?** The –C≡N dipole increases **cohesive energy density (CED)** of the polymer, raising its solubility parameter ($\delta$) to match that of polar oils. Wait — actually, the dipole–induced dipole interactions between –C≡N groups tighten the network, so non-polar oil cannot solvate the chains. The solubility-parameter principle: solvent and polymer must have $\vert \delta_{\text{polymer}} - \delta_{\text{solvent}} \vert$ as large as possible to *prevent* swelling. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Copolymer Composition (Mayo–Lewis for NBR) > [!NOTE] > This is a frequently asked derivation in KTU exams. The Mayo–Lewis (or terminal) model gives the **instantaneous copolymer composition** as a function of monomer feed ratio. ### 3.1.1 Setup Consider the four propagation steps in a binary copolymerization of monomers $M_1$ (butadiene) and $M_2$ (acrylonitrile): $$\begin{aligned} M_1^\bullet + M_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{11}} M_1^\bullet \quad \text{(homopropagation of 1)} \\ M_1^\bullet + M_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{12}} M_2^\bullet \quad \text{(cross-propagation 1→2)} \\ M_2^\bullet + M_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{21}} M_1^\bullet \quad \text{(cross-propagation 2→1)} \\ M_2^\bullet + M_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{22}} M_2^\bullet \quad \text{(homopropagation of 2)} \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.1.2 Define Monomer Reactivity Ratios $$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}, \qquad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$$ ### 3.1.3 Apply Steady-State Approximation The rate of consumption of $M_1$ vs $M_2$: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1\,[M_1]^2 + [M_1][M_2]}{[M_1][M_2] + r_2\,[M_2]^2}$$ ### 3.1.4 Express in Mole Fractions Let $f_1$ = mole fraction of $M_1$ in feed, and $F_1$ = mole fraction of $M_1$ in copolymer. Substituting $[M_1] = f_1$ and $[M_2] = (1 - f_1)$: $$\frac{F_1}{1 - F_1} = \frac{r_1 f_1 + (1 - f_1)}{f_1 + r_2 (1 - f_1)}$$ ### 3.1.5 Example Numerical Application For an NBR synthesis with $f_1 = 0.70$ (70% butadiene in feed) and literature values $r_1 = 0.40$, $r_2 = 0.04$: $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1(1 - f_1)}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2f_1(1 - f_1) + r_2 (1 - f_1)^2}$$ **Step 1:** Compute numerator terms. $$\text{Num} = 0.40 \times 0.49 + 0.70 \times 0.30 = 0.196 + 0.210 = 0.406$$ **Step 2:** Compute denominator terms. $$\text{Den} = 0.196 + 0.420 + 0.04 \times 0.09 = 0.196 + 0.420 + 0.0036 = 0.6196$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate $F_1$. $$F_1 = \frac{0.406}{0.6196} \approx 0.655$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Even though the feed is 70% butadiene, the copolymer contains only **65.5% butadiene** because butadiene adds more slowly to its own radical than to an ACN-terminated chain. This compositional drift is critical in industrial NBR reactors, which must use *continuous stirred-tank* design to maintain steady composition. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Mark–Houwink–Sakurada Equation for Silicone Viscosity The intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ of a linear polymer is related to molecular weight by: $$[\eta] = K\,M^a$$ For polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) in toluene at 25 °C, the Mark–Houwink constants are: - $K = 2.43 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ - $a = 0.84$ **Problem:** A PDMS sample has an intrinsic viscosity of $50\,\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$. Calculate the viscosity-average molecular weight. **Step 1:** Solve for $M$. $$M = \left(\frac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/a}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$M = \left(\frac{50}{2.43 \times 10^{-2}}\right)^{1/0.84} = (2057.6)^{1.1905}$$ **Step 3:** Take logarithm. $$\log M = 1.1905 \times \log(2057.6) = 1.1905 \times 3.3133 = 3.9440$$ **Step 4:** Antilog. $$M = 10^{3.9440} \approx 8.79 \times 10^3\,\text{Da} = 8.79\,\text{kDa}$$ > [!NOTE] > The exponent $a \approx 0.84$ (between 0.5 and 1.0) confirms a **good solvent** in which the polymer adopts a swollen random coil. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property vs Composition | Property Affected | Silicones (vary R) | PLA (vary isomer) | Butyl Rubber (vary isoprene %) | NBR (vary ACN %) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Flexibility** | Phenyl groups stiffen; methyl flexible | D-lactide disrupts crystallinity → amorphous | More isoprene → less crystalline → softer | Higher ACN → higher $T_g$ → stiffer | | **$T_g$ trend** | $\text{Me} < \text{Ph} < \text{vinyl}$ | PLLA > PDLLA (amorphous) | Decreases slightly | Increases by ~0.7 °C per 1% ACN | | **Cure chemistry** | Peroxide (no unsaturation) | Not vulcanizable (thermoplastic) | Sulfur cure (uses isoprene C=C) | Sulfur cure (butadiene C=C) | | **Permeability** | High to O₂, CO₂ | Moderate | **Lowest** of common rubbers | Moderate | | **Oil resistance** | Excellent | Poor (degrades) | Poor | **Best** of common rubbers | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: NBR Density from ACN Content The density of NBR is approximately linear in ACN weight percent: $$\rho_{\text{NBR}} \approx \rho_{\text{PB}} + (\rho_{\text{PAN}} - \rho_{\text{PB}}) \times w_{\text{ACN}}$$ where $\rho_{\text{PB}} = 0.89\,\text{g/cm}^3$ (polybutadiene) and $\rho_{\text{PAN}} = 1.18\,\text{g/cm}^3$ (polyacrylonitrile). **Problem:** Find the density of an NBR with 33 wt% ACN. **Step 1:** Substitute. $$\rho = 0.89 + (1.18 - 0.89) \times 0.33$$ **Step 2:** Compute. $$\rho = 0.89 + 0.29 \times 0.33 = 0.89 + 0.0957 = 0.986\,\text{g/cm}^3$$ > [!NOTE] > This linear rule is only an approximation; real NBR densities are slightly higher due to volume-contraction on mixing, but the error is typically less than 2%. # 4.
