4.4 Cationic Polymerization Mechanism (Butyl Rubber Initiation) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CoCat as Co catalyst AlCl3 participant H2O as Trace water participant Init as Initiator H plus AlCl3OH minus participant IB as Isobutylene monomer participant IP as Isoprene monomer participant Chain as Growing polymer chain CoCat->>H2O: Lewis acid base complexation H2O->>Init: Proton generated Init->>IB: Protonation of C double bond C Note over IB: Forms tert butyl cation IB->>Chain: Electrophilic addition Chain->>IB: Insertion of next isobutylene Chain->>IP: Occasional insertion of isoprene IP->>Chain: Residual C double bond C retained as cure site Note over Chain: Termination by chain transfer at minus 100 C ``` > [!NOTE] > The −100 °C operating temperature is essential because at higher temperatures, the tertiary carbocation undergoes **chain-transfer reactions** (β-proton elimination) that broaden the molecular-weight distribution and reduce the isobutylene/isoprene selectivity. KTU examiners love this "why-cold" question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Examiners award 1 mark for *definition*, 1 mark for *example/application*, and 1 mark for *distinguishing feature*. Skipping any one of these three costs you a full mark. ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define silicones. Give two examples of their industrial applications.** **Model Answer:** Silicones are semi-organic polymers having a **repeating Si–O–Si backbone** with organic groups (R) attached to silicon, with the general formula $(\text{R}_2\text{SiO})_n$. The most common example is **polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)**, where R = methyl. They possess an unusually high Si–O bond energy of **452 kJ/mol**, giving them excellent thermal stability over the range −60 °C to +250 °C. *(1 mark — definition)* **Industrial applications:** *(1 mark)* - **Silicone sealants and gaskets** in aerospace and construction (thermal stability) - **Medical implants** (biocompatibility, e.g., breast implants, catheters) - **Water-repellent coatings** for masonry and textiles - **Antifoaming agents** in food processing and petroleum refining *(1 mark — distinguishing feature: Si–O bond strength & flexibility)* --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is meant by "bioplastics"? Differentiate between PLA and bio-PE.** **Model Answer:** **Bioplastics** are polymers that are either **(a) bio-based** (derived from renewable biomass) or **(b) biodegradable** (degrade under specific conditions) — or both. *(1 mark)* | Property | PLA (Poly Lactic Acid) | Bio-PE (Bio-Polyethylene) | |---|---|---| | Monomer source | Lactic acid from fermentation | Ethylene from sugarcane ethanol | | Backbone | **Polyester (–O–C(=O)–)** | **Polyolefin (–CH₂–CH₂–)** | | Biodegradable? | **Yes** (compost in ~90 days) | **No** (identical to fossil PE) | | $T_g / T_m$ | $T_g \approx 60\,°\text{C}$, $T_m \approx 170\,°\text{C}$ | $T_m \approx 130\,°\text{C}$ | | Main use | 3D printing, packaging, sutures | Films, bottles (same as fossil PE) | *(1 mark for the differentiation; 1 mark for biodegradable vs non-biodegradable classification)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Sub-parts are typically **(a) = 7 marks** (descriptive/derivation) and **(b) = 7 marks** (numerical/short notes). For 7-mark questions, examiners expect a **flow diagram or chemical equation** in your answer. Without one, you lose 1–2 marks automatically. --- ### ✅ OPTION A — Question Choice A (14 Marks) **"**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**"** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Rochow–Müller process** for the manufacture of silicones. Discuss the key properties and applications of silicone fluids, elastomers, and resins. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ring-opening polymerization (ROP)** of lactide to form Poly(Lactic Acid). Discuss how the **stereochemistry of lactide** influences the properties of PLA. List four major applications of PLA. --- ### ✅ OPTION B — Question Choice B (14 Marks) **"**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**"** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **butyl rubber and nitrile rubber** with respect to: (i) monomers and their proportions, (ii) polymerization mechanism, (iii) cure chemistry, (iv) two key applications of each. Justify why butyl rubber is used in **inner tubes** and NBR is used in **fuel hoses**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A free-radical emulsion copolymerization of butadiene ($M_1$) and acrylonitrile ($M_2$) is carried out with a feed mole fraction $f_1 = 0.65$ and reactivity ratios $r_1 = 0.38$, $r_2 = 0.05$. Calculate the **instantaneous copolymer composition** $F_1$. What is the **ACN weight percent** in the copolymer? (Atomic masses: C = 12, H = 1, N = 14. Butadiene formula $\text{C}_4\text{H}_6$, M = 54; Acrylonitrile formula $\text{C}_3\text{H}_3\text{N}$, M = 53.) --- ### 📘 Complete Model Solutions #### 🔹 Option A, Part (a) — Rochow–Müller Process **Step 1 — Raw materials.** Finely ground **silicon metal (Si)** is reacted with **methyl chloride (CH₃Cl)** vapour in a fluidized-bed reactor at **280–320 °C** in the presence of a **copper (Cu) catalyst** (often promoted with zinc, tin, or antimony). $$2\,\text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Si} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu, 300°C}} (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiCl}_2$$ **Step 2 — Product separation.** The product is a mixture of chlorosilanes, dominated by dimethyldichlorosilane (DDS, ~70–80%). DDS is separated by **fractional distillation** because its boiling point (70 °C) differs from those of by-products $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{SiCl}$ (57 °C), $\text{CH}_3\text{SiCl}_3$ (66 °C), and $\text{SiCl}_4$ (58 °C). **Step 3 — Hydrolysis.** DDS is hydrolysed with water to give silanol and HCl: $$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiCl}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{Si(OH)}_2 + 2\,\text{HCl}$$ **Step 4 — Polycondensation.** Silanols self-condense, releasing water and forming the polysiloxane: $$n\,(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{Si(OH)}_2 \rightarrow [(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{SiO}]_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 5 — Classification of products.** | Class | Structure | Properties | Applications | |---|---|---|---| | **Silicone fluids (oils)** | Linear low-MW PDMS | Low $T_g$ (−127 °C), low viscosity change with T | Lubricants, hydraulic fluids, antifoams, water-repellent coatings | | **Silicone elastomers (rubbers)** | High-MW linear + peroxide crosslink | −60 to +250 °C service, weatherproof | O-rings, medical tubing, cable insulation | | **Silicone resins** | Branched/crosslinked network | Hard, thermoset, heat resistant | High-temperature paints, electrical insulation, release coatings | **Flow Diagram:** ``` Si (powder) + CH3Cl (gas) --Cu cat, 300 C--> mixed chlorosilanes | v (fractional distillation) DDS (CH3)2SiCl2 | v (hydrolysis with H2O) Silanol (CH3)2Si(OH)2 + HCl | v (polycondensation, releases H2O) [Si(CH3)2-O-]n polysiloxane | +-----------+------------+ v v v Fluids/ Elastomers Resins Oils (peroxide (branched cure) network) ``` --- #### 🔹 Option A, Part (b) — ROP of Lactide and Stereochemistry **Step 1 — ROP mechanism.** The industrial process uses **stannous octanoate $\text{Sn(Oct)}_2$** as a catalyst at 160–200 °C. The mechanism is **coordination–insertion**: $$\text{Lactide} + \text{Sn(Oct)}_2 + \text{ROH initiator} \rightarrow \text{PLA with -OH end groups}$$ The lactide ring is opened by nucleophilic attack of the alkoxide (–OR) on the carbonyl carbon, propagating through insertion into the Sn–O bond. **Step 2 — Stereoisomers of lactide.** - **L-lactide** (two L-lactyl units, $S,S$ configuration): gives **PLLA**, semi-crystalline, $T_m \approx 170\,°\text{C}$, $T_g \approx 60\,°\text{C}$. - **D-lactide** (two D-lactyl units, $R,R$): gives **PDLA**, also semi-crystalline. - **meso-lactide** (one L + one D, $R,S$): gives **PDLLA**, completely amorphous, no $T_m$. **Step 3 — Property consequences.** | Property | PLLA (high L) | PDLLA (50/50 L/D) | |---|---|---| | Crystallinity | ~37% | 0% | | $T_g$ | ~60 °C | ~55 °C | | $T_m$ | ~170 °C | None | | Degradation | Slow (months–years) | Fast (weeks–months) | | Mechanical | Stiff, brittle | Flexible, transparent | **Step 4 — Applications of PLA.** 1. **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3D printing** filament 2. **Compostable food packaging** (cups, clamshells, cutlery) 3. **Biomedical sutures and bone-fixation screws** (resorbable) 4. **Drug-delivery microspheres** and tissue-engineering scaffolds --- #### 🔹 Option B, Part (a) — Comparative Analysis of Butyl Rubber vs NBR | Aspect | Butyl Rubber (IIR) | Nitrile Rubber (NBR) | |---|---|---| | **Monomers** | Isobutylene (~98%) + Isoprene (~2%) | Butadiene + Acrylonitrile (15–50%) | | **Polymerization** | **Cationic** (AlCl₃, CH₃Cl, −100 °C) | **Free-radical emulsion** (K₂S₂O₈, water, 5–30 °C) | | **Cure chemistry** | Sulfur cure using isoprene's residual C=C | Sulfur cure using butadiene's residual C=C | | **Dominant property** | **Lowest air permeability** of all common rubbers | **Excellent oil and fuel resistance** | | **Service T** | −40 °C to +120 °C | −30 °C to +110 °C | | **Key applications** | Tire inner tubes, pharma stoppers, gas-mask bladders | Fuel hoses, O-rings, disposable gloves | **Why butyl rubber for inner tubes?** The gem-dimethyl groups $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}<$ on every alternate carbon create **steric crowding** that physically blocks gas diffusion. Air permeability of IIR is only **~10** on a relative scale where natural rubber is 100. This means a butyl tube holds air for months without re-inflation, whereas a natural-rubber tube deflates in days. The 2% isoprene provides just enough unsaturation for sulfur vulcanization without compromising the saturated, airtight backbone. **Why NBR for fuel hoses?** The polar –C≡N groups (nitrile) introduce strong **dipole–dipole interactions** that raise the cohesive energy density and solubility parameter of the rubber to a value far from that of non-polar petroleum oils and gasoline. As a result, oil cannot swell or extract NBR. Higher ACN content → better oil resistance, so fuel hoses typically use **medium-to-high ACN grades (33–45%)**. The residual C=C from butadiene units allows sulfur crosslinking. --- #### 🔹 Option B, Part (b) — Mayo–Lewis Numerical Solution **Step 1 — Recall the copolymer composition equation.** $$\frac{F_1}{1 - F_1} = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 (1 - f_1)}{f_1 (1 - f_1) + r_2 (1 - f_1)^2}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $f_1 = 0.65$, $r_1 = 0.38$, $r_2 = 0.05$: - $f_1^2 = 0.4225$ - $(1 - f_1) = 0.35$ - $(1 - f_1)^2 = 0.1225$ - $f_1(1 - f_1) = 0.2275$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator.** $$\text{Numerator} = 0.38 \times 0.4225 + 0.2275 = 0.16055 + 0.2275 = 0.38805$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 0.2275 + 0.05 \times 0.1225 = 0.2275 + 0.00613 = 0.23363$$ **Step 4 — Compute the ratio.** $$\frac{F_1}{1 - F_1} = \frac{0.38805}{0.23363} = 1.6610$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $F_1$.** $$F_1 = \frac{1.6610}{1 + 1.6610} = \frac{1.6610}{2.6610} = 0.6242$$ So the copolymer contains **62.4 mol% butadiene** and **37.6 mol% acrylonitrile**. **Step 6 — Convert to weight percent ACN.** Moles in 1 g of copolymer: assume 1 mol total = 0.624 mol butadiene + 0.376 mol ACN. Mass of butadiene = $0.624 \times 54 = 33.70\,\text{g}$ Mass of ACN = $0.376 \times 53 = 19.93\,\text{g}$ Total mass = $33.70 + 19.93 = 53.63\,\text{g}$ $$w_{\text{ACN}} = \frac{19.93}{53.63} \times 100\% = 37.16\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** The copolymer composition is $F_1 = 0.624$ (62.4 mol% butadiene), and the **ACN weight percent ≈ 37.2%**. This is squarely in the "medium-high ACN" range, suitable for oil-resistant automotive applications. **Why the feed (65%) differs from the copolymer (62.4%):** The lower reactivity ratio of $M_2$ ($r_2 = 0.05$) means that an ACN-terminated chain prefers to add a butadiene monomer rather than another ACN. Hence ACN is consumed *more slowly* than butadiene, so its instantaneous incorporation is lower than its feed fraction. In a batch reactor, ACN would accumulate and the copolymer would drift toward higher ACN over time — a phenomenon called **compositional drift**, which is why industrial NBR reactors are usually continuous. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist (Module 3 Topics)** ### Silicones - [ ] **Backbone:** Si–O–Si; side groups: typically **methyl** (PDMS). - [ ] **Bond strength:** Si–O ≈ **452 kJ/mol** (key to thermal stability). - [ ] **Synthesis:** **Rochow process** (Si + CH₃Cl with Cu at 300 °C) → DDS → hydrolysis → polycondensation. - [ ] **Three classes:** fluids, **elastomers** (peroxide cured), **resins** (branched). - [ ] **Service range:** −60 to +250 °C — wider than any organic rubber. - [ ] **Biocompatible** — used in medical implants. ### PLA (Bioplastics) - [ ] **Monomer:** **Lactide** (cyclic dimer of lactic acid). - [ ] **Polymerization:** **Ring-opening polymerization (ROP)** with **Sn(Oct)₂** catalyst. - [ ] **Bio-based AND biodegradable** — distinguished from bio-PE. - [ ] **Stereoisomers:** L-lactide → **PLLA** (crystalline, $T_m$ 170 °C); meso-lactide → **PDLLA** (amorphous). - [ ] **$T_g \approx 60\,°\text{C}$**, $T_m \approx 170\,°\text{C}$. - [ ] **Applications:** 3D printing, packaging, bioresorbable medical devices. - [ ] **Degrades** in industrial compost in ~90 days; not in home compost or landfill. ### Butyl Rubber (IIR) - [ ] **Monomers:** Isobutylene (98%) + Isoprene (2%). - [ ] **Polymerization:** **Cationic**, AlCl₃ catalyst, **−100 °C** in CH₃Cl solvent. - [ ] **Isoprene is the cure site** — without it, sulfur vulcanization is impossible. - [ ] **Air permeability is lowest** of all common rubbers (≈ 1/10 of natural rubber). - [ ] **Applications:** tire inner tubes, pharmaceutical stoppers, gas-mask bladders, chewing gum base. ### Nitrile Rubber (NBR) - [ ] **Monomers:** Butadiene + **Acrylonitrile (ACN)**. - [ ] **Polymerization:** **Free-radical emulsion** with K₂S₂O₈ initiator. - [ ] **ACN content (15–50%)** is the master variable controlling oil resistance vs low-T flexibility. - [ ] **Higher ACN → better oil resistance, higher $T_g$, worse low-T flexibility**. - [ ] **Applications:** fuel hoses, O-rings, gaskets, **disposable nitrile gloves** (pandemic icon). ### Cross-Cutting KTU 2024 Takeaways - [ ] **Mayo–Lewis equation** $\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1]^2 + [M_1][M_2]}{[M_1][M_2] + r_2[M_2]^2}$ is the key copolymer-composition formula. - [ ] **Trade-off rule:** every property enhancement in one direction usually costs a property in the opposite direction (ACN content is the textbook example). - [ ] **Three synthesis families:** step-growth (silicones), ring-opening (PLA), chain-growth ionic (IIR) and radical (NBR). - [ ] **Always draw a flow diagram or chemical equation** in 7-mark sub-parts — it earns 1–2 free marks. > [!WARNING] > **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:** Most students *describe* but do not *justify*. For every property, ask yourself **"WHY does the molecular structure cause this property?"** Examiners in 2024 KTU scheme specifically test the **CO linkage** — *Course Outcome: "The student will be able to correlate molecular structure with bulk properties."* A description without justification loses 30–50% of the marks allocated to that property.

topics.title: Additives for Polymer Processing - Effect of Additives Used - Plasticizers - Colourants - Heat Stabilizers - Antioxidants - Ultraviolet Absorbers - Antistatic Agents - Flame Retardants - Blowing Agents - Lubricants and Fillers. Plastic Processing - Injection Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Additives for Polymer Processing - Effect of Additives Used - Plasticizers - Colourants - Heat Stabilizers - Antioxidants - Ultraviolet Absorbers - Antistatic Agents - Flame Retardants - Blowing Agents - Lubricants and Fillers. Plastic Processing - Injection Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymer Additives — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Additives** are chemical compounds (organic or inorganic) that are intentionally incorporated into a base polymer matrix during compounding or processing to **modify**, **enhance**, or **suppress** specific physical, chemical, mechanical, thermal, electrical, optical, or aesthetic properties of the finished plastic product — *without* themselves being the primary structural polymer.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, additives are **non-polymeric auxiliary substances** that are blended in small proportions (typically **0.1 % to 40 % by weight**) to tailor the end-use performance of commodity, engineering, and specialty plastics.

The base polymer (e.g., PVC, PE, PP, PS, ABS, Nylon) is rarely used in its pure, virgin form. Almost every commercial plastic article you touch — from a PVC water pipe to an ABS car dashboard, from a children's toy to a fire-retardant electrical switch — contains a carefully engineered **cocktail of additives**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly categorizes additives into **9 functional classes**. Each class solves a specific problem encountered during processing (e.g., thermal degradation at high shear) or during service life (e.g., UV-induced embrittlement outdoors). Memorizing the **Function → Mechanism → Example** triplet for every class is the most common high-yield strategy for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe" Viewpoint

Imagine you are cooking a basic plain cake batter. The flour is your **base polymer** — it gives structure, but on its own the cake is bland, dry, tough, and browns unevenly. To make a beautiful, soft, colourful, long-lasting cake, you add:

- **Sugar + oil** → makes it soft and flexible (acts like a **Plasticizer**)
- **Cocoa powder or food colour** → gives a desired colour (acts like a **Colourant**)
- **Vanilla essence / preservatives** → prevents spoilage over time (acts like an **Antioxidant** or **Heat Stabilizer**)
- **Baking powder** → creates tiny air pockets, making it spongy (acts like a **Blowing Agent**)
- **A pinch of salt** → controls crystal grain size (acts like a **Nucleating Agent / Filler**)
- **Greased tray** → easy release after baking (acts like a **Lubricant / Release Agent**)
- **Flame-proof packaging** → doesn't burn easily (acts like a **Flame Retardant**)
- **Aluminium foil wrapping** → blocks sunlight damage (acts like a **UV Absorber**)
- **Anti-static spray on the cover** → doesn't attract dust (acts like an **Antistatic Agent**)

Just as a master baker carefully chooses the additives, the polymer engineer carefully **formulates** the recipe to give the polymer its required **processability**, **durability**, and **aesthetic appeal**.

---

## 1.3 Polymer Processing — Injection Moulding: The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Injection Moulding** is a **cyclic, high-volume, closed-loop plastic manufacturing process** in which a precisely metered quantity of **thermoplastic** (or thermoset) polymer melt is forced under high pressure (typically **35 MPa to 140 MPa**) from a heated barrel through a **sprue and runner system** into a closed, water-cooled (or oil-heated) **mould cavity**, where it **solidifies** into the net-shape of the desired part, and is then **ejected** by pins or strips.

It is the **single most widely used** plastic processing method in the world, accounting for approximately **30 % to 45 %** of all plastics processed globally, and producing everything from bottle caps and combs to precision medical implants and automotive bumpers.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Injection Moulding = "Polymer Syringe into a Cookie Cutter"

Think of a giant medical syringe filled with hot, molten chocolate (the **polymer melt**). You push the plunger with great force (the **injection ram**). The molten chocolate flows through a narrow nozzle into a cooled metal cookie-cutter mould (the **cavity**). After a few seconds, the chocolate solidifies, the mould opens, ejector pins push the chocolate cookie out, the mould closes again, and the cycle repeats — **hundreds of thousands of times a day**.

The "chocolate" is the polymer melt, the "cookie cutter" is the steel mould, and the "refrigeration" is the **cooling channels** circulating chilled water.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters — The Industrial Significance

> [!NOTE]
> - The **global plastics additives market** is valued at over **USD 60 billion** and grows at a **CAGR of ~5 %**.
> - **Phthalate plasticizers** alone (used primarily in flexible PVC) represent a multi-million-tonne market.
> - **Injection moulding** is the **workhorse** of mass-production plastics manufacturing. A single high-end injection moulding machine can cost anywhere from **₹15 lakhs to ₹5 crores**, and a precision multi-cavity mould can cost **₹5 lakhs to ₹2 crores**.
> - Almost every KTU Polymer Technology question paper (2019–2024) contains at least **one 7-mark sub-question** on additives and **one 7-mark sub-question** on injection moulding — usually asking for classification, mechanism, and a diagram.

---

## 1.6 Classification Snapshot of Polymer Additives (Module 4 Coverage)

| S.No. | Additive Class | Primary Purpose | Typical Loading Range (% by wt) |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Plasticizers** | Increase flexibility & processability | **5 – 50** |
| 2 | **Colourants** (Pigments & Dyes) | Impart colour & aesthetics | **0.01 – 2** |
| 3 | **Heat Stabilizers** | Prevent thermal degradation during processing | **0.5 – 3** |
| 4 | **Antioxidants** (Primary & Secondary) | Inhibit oxidative degradation during service | **0.05 – 1.5** |
| 5 | **UV Absorbers / Stabilizers** | Protect against photo-degradation | **0.1 – 1.0** |
| 6 | **Antistatic Agents** | Reduce surface electrical resistivity | **0.1 – 3.0** |
| 7 | **Flame Retardants** | Reduce flammability, increase LOI | **5 – 30** |
| 8 | **Blowing (Foaming) Agents** | Create cellular / foamed structure | **0.1 – 5** |
| 9 | **Lubricants & Fillers** | Improve flow & reduce cost / modify properties | **0.5 – 40** |

---

## 1.7 Standards & Performance Metrics Used in This Module

The following engineering constants and standard test methods govern additive selection:

- **T<sub>g</sub>** — Glass transition temperature (°C or K)
- **T<sub>m</sub>** — Melting point (°C or K)
- **LOI** — Limiting Oxygen Index (%) — ASTM **D2863**
- **MFI / MFR** — Melt Flow Index / Rate (g/10 min) — ASTM **D1238**
- **HDT** — Heat Deflection Temperature (°C) — ASTM **D648**
- **UV ageing** — ASTM **G154**, **G155**
- **Tensile / Flexural / Impact** — ASTM **D638**, **D790**, **D256**
- **Surface Resistivity** — ASTM **D257**
- **UL-94** — Vertical / Horizontal flammability rating (V-0, V-1, V-2, HB)
- **Injection Pressure** — **35 MPa to 140 MPa**
- **Injection Speed** — typically **20 – 500 mm/s**
- **Mould Clamping Force** — **200 kN to 10,000 kN**
- **Mould Temperature** — **20 °C to 200 °C**
- **Melt Temperature** — **150 °C to 350 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plasticizers

### 2.1.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plasticizers** are low-molecular-weight, low-volatility, non-reactive organic liquids or low-melting solids that, when added to a high-T<sub>g</sub> polymer, **reduce intermolecular forces** between polymer chains, thereby **lowering the glass transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub>)**, increasing **flexibility, ductility, elongation at break**, and **ease of processing**.

### 2.1.2 Mechanism — The "Lubrication Theory"
Plasticizer molecules are much smaller than polymer chains. They insert themselves *between* adjacent polymer chains, increasing the **free volume** and reducing **van der Waals secondary bonding**. This makes chain segments more mobile at a given temperature.

Two accepted mechanisms exist:

1. **Lubricity Theory** — plasticizer molecules act as internal lubricants, letting chains slip past each other.
2. **Gel Theory** — plasticizer disrupts polymer–polymer "junction points" (crystallites, polar groups) in the polymer gel network.

### 2.1.3 Classification of Plasticizers

| Type | Examples | Compatibility with PVC | Notes |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Phthalate esters** | DOP (DEHP), DINP, DIDP, DBP | Excellent | Most widely used, ~80 % of market |
| **Adipates** | DOA, DINA | Good | Low-temperature flexibility |
| **Trimellitates** | TOTM, TINTM | Excellent | High-temperature, low migration |
| **Epoxy plasticizers** | Epoxidized soybean oil (ESBO) | Good | Secondary, also acts as heat stabilizer |
| **Polymeric / Permanent** | Polyester adipates | Moderate | Used for low migration (automotive) |
| **Citrate esters** | ATBC, TEC | Excellent | Non-toxic, used in food contact & medical |

### 2.1.4 Effect on Properties

- **Decreases** T<sub>g</sub>, hardness, melt viscosity, tensile strength, modulus
- **Increases** elongation, flexibility, impact resistance, processability
- **Side effect:** migration, extraction, volatility, "plasticizer bloom" (white exudation on surface)

### 2.1.5 Key Quantitative Relation (Fox Equation)

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_p}{T_{g,p}} + \frac{w_{pl}}{T_{g,pl}} $$

where $w_p$ and $w_{pl}$ are weight fractions of polymer and plasticizer, and $T_{g,p}$ and $T_{g,pl}$ are their respective glass transition temperatures (in **K**).

---

## 2.2 Colourants

### 2.2.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Colourants** are additives that selectively absorb, transmit, or reflect specific wavelengths of visible light (380 nm – 750 nm), thereby imparting a desired **hue, tint, and saturation** to the polymer.

Two main sub-classes:

- **Pigments** — **Insoluble**, finely divided solid particles (organic or inorganic). Used as dispersion. Examples: TiO<sub>2</sub> (white), carbon black, iron oxide (red/yellow), phthalocyanine blue.
- **Dyes** — **Soluble** in the polymer melt. Examples: anthraquinone dyes, azo dyes. Less common due to migration and bleeding.

### 2.2.2 Selection Criteria

- **Heat stability** at processing temperature (TiO<sub>2</sub> withstands > 300 °C).
- **Lightfastness** — resistance to fading under UV.
- **Chemical resistance** to acids, alkalis, oils.
- **Migration & bleeding** — pigments preferred over dyes.
- **Toxicity** — particularly for food contact, toys (EN 71-3, FDA).
- **Opacity vs Transparency** — pigments are usually opaque; dyes are transparent.

### 2.2.3 Effect on Properties

- **Increase** opacity, UV absorption (especially carbon black — a UV screen).
- **Titanium dioxide (TiO<sub>2</sub>)** acts both as a white pigment and a reinforcing filler.
- **Carbon black** at 1–3 % loading is one of the most cost-effective **UV stabilizers** in polyolefins.
- Excessive loading can **reduce** tensile strength and impact resistance due to stress concentration at pigment particles.

---

## 2.3 Heat Stabilizers

### 2.3.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Stabilizers** are additives that **prevent or retard the thermal degradation** of polymers — especially **PVC** — during high-temperature processing (e.g., extrusion at 180–210 °C, injection moulding up to 220 °C).

PVC is particularly vulnerable to **dehydrochlorination** (loss of HCl gas), which leads to discoloration (yellow → brown → black) and severe loss of mechanical properties.

### 2.3.2 Classification of Heat Stabilizers

| Type | Example | Mechanism |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Lead-based** | Basic lead carbonate, tribasic lead sulphate | Absorb HCl, form inert PbCl<sub>2</sub> |
| **Mixed metal (Ba/Cd, Ba/Zn, Ca/Zn)** | Barium stearate + Cadmium stearate | Synergistic soap stabilizers |
| **Organotin (Tin-based)** | Dibutyltin dilaurate, dioctyltin maleate | Best for rigid, transparent PVC |
| **Antimonial** | Antimony mercaptides | Alternative to tin |
| **Epoxy** | ESBO (epoxidized soybean oil) | Secondary stabilizer / co-stabilizer |
| **Phosphite** | Triphenyl phosphite, tris(nonylphenyl) phosphite | Secondary, peroxide decomposer |

### 2.3.3 Effect on Properties

- **Prevent** discoloration (maintains whiteness/transparency of PVC).
- **Prevent** autocatalytic HCl chain reaction.
- **Extend** processing time and recyclability.
- Lead-based are now **restricted** under REACH, RoHS due to toxicity → shift to **Ca/Zn** systems.

---

## 2.4 Antioxidants

### 2.4.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Antioxidants** inhibit **oxidative degradation** of polymers caused by reaction with atmospheric oxygen, especially at elevated temperatures or under UV light. Oxidation produces **free radicals (R•, ROO•, ROOH)** that lead to chain scission, crosslinking, embrittlement, and discolouration.

### 2.4.2 Classification

- **Primary (Chain-Breaking) Antioxidants** — donate a hydrogen atom to the peroxy radical, terminating the chain.
  - **Hindered Phenols**: BHT, Irganox 1010, Irganox 1076
  - **Hindered Amines (HALS)** — act more on radical trapping
- **Secondary (Preventive) Antioxidants** — decompose hydroperoxides (ROOH) into non-radical products.
  - **Phosphites**: tris(nonylphenyl) phosphite, TNPP
  - **Thiosynergists**: dilauryl thiodipropionate (DLTDP)

### 2.4.3 Autoxidation Chain Reaction

$$ \text{Initiation: } RH + O_2 \rightarrow R^\bullet + HOO^\bullet $$

$$ \text{Propagation 1: } R^\bullet + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^\bullet $$

$$ \text{Propagation 2: } ROO^\bullet + RH \rightarrow ROOH + R^\bullet $$

$$ \text{Branching: } ROOH \rightarrow RO^\bullet + HO^\bullet $$

> [!NOTE]
> A **hindered phenol** like Irganox 1010 donates an H• to ROO•, converting it to a stable ROOH — the chain reaction is interrupted.

### 2.4.4 Effect on Properties

- **Dramatically extend** the service life of polyolefins (PE, PP) and engineering plastics.
- **Prevent** embrittlement, loss of impact strength, and yellowness during processing and outdoor exposure.
- Typical loading: **0.05 – 1.5 %**.

---

## 2.5 Ultraviolet (UV) Absorbers / Stabilizers

### 2.5.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UV Absorbers** absorb harmful high-energy UV radiation (290 – 400 nm) and dissipate it as harmless low-energy heat, protecting the polymer from **photo-oxidation, chain scission, crosslinking, and surface cracking (chalking)**.

### 2.5.2 Classification

| Class | Example | Action Spectrum | Notes |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Benzophenones** | 2-Hydroxy-4-octyloxy benzophenone | UV-A (315–400 nm) | Low cost, slightly yellow |
| **Benzotriazoles** | Tinuvin P, Tinuvin 326 | UV-A & UV-B | Most widely used |
| **Triazines** | Tinuvin 1577, Tinuvin 1600 | Broad spectrum | High performance |
| **HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers)** | Tinuvin 770, Chimassorb 944 | Radical scavenger (cyclic) | **Most effective** in polyolefins |
| **Nickel quenchers** | Nickel dibutyldithiocarbamate | Excited-state quencher | Now restricted (Ni toxicity) |
| **Carbon black (1–3 %)** | Furnace black | Physical screen | Excellent for cable jacketing |

### 2.5.3 Effect on Properties

- **Retain** gloss, colour, impact strength, and tensile elongation under outdoor exposure.
- **Prevent** micro-cracking and chalking.
- Carbon black at 2 % loading can extend PP outdoor life from a few months to **> 10 years**.

---

## 2.6 Antistatic Agents

### 2.6.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Antistatic Agents** reduce the build-up of **static electricity** on the surface of plastics — a serious issue because polymers are **electrical insulators** with surface resistivity often **> 10<sup>14</sup> Ω·sq** (compared to metals at < 10<sup>5</sup> Ω·sq).

### 2.6.2 Classification

- **Internal / Migratory Antistats** — e.g., **ethoxylated fatty amines, glycerol monostearate (GMS), quaternary ammonium salts**. They migrate to the surface and form a thin conductive moisture-attracting layer.
- **External / Topical Antistats** — sprayed on as a temporary coating.
- **Conductive Fillers** — carbon black, carbon fibres, carbon nanotubes, metallic powders, stainless-steel fibres — embedded permanently to give < 10<sup>6</sup> Ω·sq (used in ESD-safe packaging for electronics).

### 2.6.3 Effect on Properties

- **Reduce** surface resistivity from ~10<sup>14</sup> to ~10<sup>9</sup> – 10<sup>11</sup> Ω·sq.
- **Prevent** dust attraction, sparking, electrical shocks.
- **Prevent** electrostatic discharge (ESD) damage to integrated circuits in electronics packaging.

---

## 2.7 Flame Retardants

### 2.7.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flame Retardants** are additives that **reduce the flammability** of plastics, **delay ignition**, **slow flame spread**, and **reduce heat release rate** — critical for electrical, electronic, construction, transport, and textile applications.

### 2.7.2 Classification

| Type | Examples | Mechanism |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Halogenated (Br, Cl)** | Decabromodiphenyl ether (decaBDE), hexabromocyclododecane (HBCD) | Release halogen radicals, interrupt combustion chain |
| **Phosphorus-based** | TCPP, TDCPP, RDP, DOPO derivatives | Promote char formation in solid phase |
| **Nitrogen-based** | Melamine cyanurate, melamine polyphosphate | Release inert N<sub>2</sub> gas (dilutes O<sub>2</sub>) |
| **Metal hydroxides (ATH, MDH)** | Al(OH)<sub>3</sub>, Mg(OH)<sub>2</sub> | Endothermic decomposition releases H<sub>2</sub>O |
| **Synergists** | Sb<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> with halogens | Volatile SbBr<sub>3</sub> traps radicals in gas phase |
| **Nanocomposites** | Organoclays (montmorillonite), CNT | Form a physical barrier char |

### 2.7.3 Key Performance Metric — Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)

$$ \text{LOI (\%) } = \frac{[O_2]}{[O_2] + [N_2]} \times 100 $$

A material with **LOI > 27 %** is considered **self-extinguishing** in air. Engineering plastics with FR additives commonly achieve **LOI = 30 % – 50 %**.

### 2.7.4 Effect on Properties

- **Pass** UL-94 V-0 rating (self-extinguishing in < 10 s).
- **May reduce** impact strength, tensile elongation, and processability (especially ATH at > 40 %).
- **May release** toxic/corrosive smoke (so smoke suppressants like Zn borate are often co-added).

---

## 2.8 Blowing (Foaming) Agents

### 2.8.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blowing Agents** are additives that **generate gas** (CO<sub>2</sub>, N<sub>2</sub>, NH<sub>3</sub>, or other) within a polymer melt or liquid resin, creating a **cellular (foamed) structure** — used in PU foam, PS cups, PVC foam board, PE foam packaging, etc.

### 2.8.2 Classification

- **Chemical Blowing Agents (CBA)** — decompose on heating to release gas.
  - **Azodicarbonamide (ADC, ADCA)** — releases N<sub>2</sub> + CO + CO<sub>2</sub>; most widely used; gas yield ~ 220 ml/g.
  - **Sodium bicarbonate** — releases CO<sub>2</sub>; food-grade.
  - **Toluene sulphonyl hydrazide (TSH)** — releases N<sub>2</sub>.
  - **5-Phenyl tetrazole (5-PT)** — for high-temperature engineering plastics.
- **Physical Blowing Agents (PBA)** — volatile liquids or gases that vaporize.
  - **HFCs, HCFCs, hydrocarbons (pentane, cyclopentane), CO<sub>2</sub> (supercritical)**, N<sub>2</sub>.

### 2.8.3 Effect on Properties

- **Reduce** density (from ~1.0 g/cc to ~0.02 g/cc for ultra-light foams).
- **Improve** thermal insulation, acoustic damping, cushioning, specific strength.
- **Reduce** raw material cost.

---

## 2.9 Lubricants and Fillers

### 2.9.1 Lubricants — Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubricants** are additives that **reduce friction** between the polymer melt and the metal surfaces of processing equipment (barrel, screw, die) — improving melt flow, reducing shear heating, preventing melt fracture, and improving mould release.

Two sub-types:

- **Internal Lubricants** — e.g., **waxes, fatty acid esters, stearic acid**. Reduce viscosity of the melt by reducing inter-chain friction. Compatible with polymer.
- **External Lubricants** — e.g., **paraffin wax, polyethylene wax, metal stearates (Ca, Zn stearate)**. Form a thin film at the metal-polymer interface. Less compatible.

### 2.9.2 Effect of Lubricants
- **Reduce** melt viscosity, die-swell, power consumption.
- **Improve** surface finish, mould release, throughput.
- **Excess** can cause plate-out, slip, and poor printability.

### 2.9.3 Fillers — Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fillers** are finely divided **solid particulate or fibrous materials** (inorganic or organic) added to polymers primarily to **reduce cost**, **modify mechanical/thermal/electrical properties**, and **impart specific functional behaviour**.

### 2.9.4 Classification of Fillers

| Type | Examples | Effect |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Inert (extender) fillers** | Calcium carbonate (CaCO<sub>3</sub>), talc, kaolin (china clay) | Cost reduction, dimensional stability |
| **Reinforcing fillers** | Carbon black (small particle), silica, glass beads | Improved tensile & abrasion |
| **Fibrous fillers** | Glass fibre, carbon fibre, aramid (Kevlar) | High strength & stiffness (FRP) |
| **Special functional fillers** | Alumina, BN, graphite, MoS<sub>2</sub> | Thermal conductivity, lubricity, EMI shielding |

### 2.9.5 Effect of Fillers

- **Increase** stiffness, modulus, HDT, dimensional stability, hardness.
- **Reduce** cost (CaCO<sub>3</sub> at 20 % in PP can reduce raw-material cost by ~15 %).
- **Decrease** elongation, impact strength, surface finish (unless surface-treated).
- **Coupling agents** (silanes, titanates, maleated PP) are used to **chemically bond** filler surface to polymer matrix, recovering impact and tensile strength.

### 2.9.6 Rule of Mixtures (for filled polymer modulus)

$$ E_c = E_m \, V_m + E_f \, V_f $$

$$ E_c = E_m \, (1 - V_f) + E_f \, V_f $$

where $E_c$ = composite modulus, $E_m$ = matrix modulus, $E_f$ = filler modulus, $V_f$ = volume fraction of filler.

---

## 2.10 Plastic Processing — Injection Moulding: Deep Theory

### 2.10.1 Process Description
Injection moulding is a **cyclic** process with the following sequence of operations:

1. **Mould Closing** — two halves clamp shut under high tonnage.
2. **Injection (Filling)** — molten polymer is injected at high pressure through the **sprue → runner → gate → cavity**.
3. **Packing (Holding)** — additional polymer is forced in to compensate for volumetric shrinkage.
4. **Cooling** — the part solidifies; water/oil in cooling channels carries away heat.
5. **Mould Opening** — the two halves separate.
6. **Ejection** — ejector pins push the part out.
7. **Part removal** — by hand, robot, or gravity.

### 2.10.2 The Injection Moulding Machine — Main Components

- **Hopper** — feeds pellets.
- **Heated Barrel** (3 to 5 zones, increasing temperature towards the nozzle).
- **Reciprocating Screw** — plasticizes, mixes, and acts as plunger.
- **Nozzle** — sealed against the sprue bushing.
- **Clamping Unit** — toggle or hydraulic, holding the two mould halves.
- **Mould (Tool)** — fixed half (cavity side) and moving half (core side).
- **Sprue, Runners, Gates, Vents, Cooling Channels, Ejector Pins**.

### 2.10.3 Key Process Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Melt Temperature** | **150 – 350 °C** | Determines flow, surface finish |
| **Mould Temperature** | **20 – 200 °C** | Affects crystallinity, shrinkage, warpage |
| **Injection Pressure** | **35 – 140 MPa** | Forces melt into cavity |
| **Injection Speed / Velocity** | **20 – 500 mm/s** | Affects flow, weld-line quality |
| **Packing Pressure** | **30 – 100 MPa** | Compensates shrinkage |
| **Cooling Time** | **5 – 120 s** | Largest fraction of cycle time |
| **Cycle Time** | **10 – 300 s** | Productivity driver |
| **Clamping Tonnage** | **200 – 10,000 kN** | Resists cavity pressure |

### 2.10.4 Cycle Time Breakdown (Typical)

$$ t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close} $$

- $t_{inj} \approx 0.5 – 3$ s
- $t_{pack} \approx 1 – 5$ s
- $t_{cool} \approx 60 – 80$ % of total cycle time
- $t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close} \approx 3 – 8$ s

### 2.10.5 The Plasticating / Screw Plastication Phase

The **reciprocating screw** performs three functions simultaneously:
1. **Conveying** pellets forward.
2. **Melting** by external heaters + frictional shear.
3. **Metering** a precise shot volume.

Screw zones:
- **Feed zone** (50 % length, conveying).
- **Compression (melt) zone** (25 – 30 %, melting).
- **Metering zone** (20 – 25 %, pumping, homogenization).

### 2.10.6 Clamping Tonnage Calculation

$$ F_{clamp} \geq P_{cavity} \times A_{projected} $$

A common **rule-of-thumb** is:

$$ F_{clamp} \text{ (tonnes)} = \frac{A_{projected} \text{ (cm}^2)}{2 \text{ to } 4} \text{ for average moulding pressure of } \sim 50 \text{ MPa} $$

> A more refined formula: $F_{clamp} = 1.0 \times A_{proj} \times P_{cav} \times k$ where $k$ = safety factor (1.1–1.3).

### 2.10.7 Shrinkage in Injection Moulding

$$ \text{Shrinkage (\%)} = \frac{L_{mould} - L_{part}}{L_{mould}} \times 100 $$

Typical shrinkages:
- **Crystalline** polymers (PP, PE, PA): **1.5 – 3.0 %**
- **Amorphous** polymers (PS, ABS, PC): **0.4 – 0.7 %**
- **Filled/reinforced** systems: **0.2 – 0.5 %** (much lower!)

### 2.10.8 Common Defects and Their Causes

| Defect | Probable Cause |
|:--|:--|
| **Sink marks** | Insufficient packing/holding; wall thickness too high |
| **Voids** | Volumetric shrinkage in thick sections |
| **Weld lines (knit lines)** | Two flow fronts meeting; low melt/mould temperature |
| **Flash (burrs)** | Clamp tonnage too low, mould wear |
| **Warping** | Uneven cooling, differential shrinkage |
| **Short shot** | Insufficient melt, vent blockage |
| **Burn marks** | Trapped air; reduce injection speed, add vents |
| **Bubbles** | Moisture in resin (especially Nylon, PC) — pre-dry! |

---

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Application | Unit |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_p}{T_{g,p}} + \frac{w_{pl}}{T_{g,pl}}$ (Fox Eq.) | Plasticizer selection | K |
| 2 | $E_c = E_m(1-V_f) + E_f V_f$ | Filler effect on modulus | Pa / MPa |
| 3 | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$ | Injection moulding machine selection | N |
| 4 | $F_{clamp}\text{(tonnes)} = A_{proj} \text{(cm}^2) \div (2 \text{ to } 4)$ | Quick tonnage estimate | tonnes |
| 5 | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close}$ | Productivity calculation | s |
| 6 | $\text{Shrinkage \%} = \frac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100$ | Mould design | % |
| 7 | $\text{LOI \%} = \frac{[O_2]}{[O_2]+[N_2]} \times 100$ | Flame retardant performance | % |
| 8 | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ (Ideal gas) | Gas yield of blowing agent | Pa·m³ |
| 9 | $V_{shot} = V_{cavity} \times (1 + \text{shrinkage})$ | Shot size calculation | cm³ |
| 10 | $\text{L/D ratio of screw} = 15:1 \text{ to } 24:1$ | Screw design | ratio |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **exam-grade worked examples**. Each is built step-by-step with **every algebraic transition explained in plain text** so the student can reproduce the solution in the answer script. This style of worked problem is the most scoring pattern in KTU ESE valuation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Glass Transition Temperature of Plasticized PVC (Fox Equation Application)

> **Problem Statement (model):** A PVC compound contains 65 parts PVC (T<sub>g,p</sub> = 87 °C) and 35 parts DOP plasticizer (T<sub>g,pl</sub> = -86 °C). Calculate the glass transition temperature of the plasticized blend using the Fox equation.

### Step 1 — Convert T<sub>g</sub> to Kelvin
We must work in absolute temperature (Kelvin) because the Fox equation is a free-volume-based relationship that requires absolute units.

$$ T_{g,p} \, (K) = 87 + 273.15 = 360.15 \text{ K} $$

$$ T_{g,pl} \, (K) = -86 + 273.15 = 187.15 \text{ K} $$

**Reasoning:** The Fox equation is derived assuming additive free volumes. Celsius introduces a sign that breaks the law.

### Step 2 — Compute Weight Fractions
Total parts = 65 + 35 = 100 parts.

$$ w_p = \frac{65}{100} = 0.65 \quad ; \quad w_{pl} = \frac{35}{100} = 0.35 $$

**Reasoning:** The Fox equation requires weight fractions, not volume fractions (an approximation valid when densities are similar — for plasticized PVC, this is acceptable to within a few °C).

### Step 3 — Apply the Fox Equation

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_p}{T_{g,p}} + \frac{w_{pl}}{T_{g,pl}} $$

Substituting the numbers:

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.65}{360.15} + \frac{0.35}{187.15} $$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Term

$$ \frac{0.65}{360.15} = 0.001805 \text{ K}^{-1} $$

$$ \frac{0.35}{187.15} = 0.001870 \text{ K}^{-1} $$

### Step 5 — Sum the Two Contributions

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 0.001805 + 0.001870 = 0.003675 \text{ K}^{-1} $$

### Step 6 — Invert to Get T<sub>g</sub>

$$ T_g = \frac{1}{0.003675} = 272.11 \text{ K} $$

### Step 7 — Convert Back to Celsius

$$ T_g \, (°C) = 272.11 - 273.15 = -1.04 \text{ °C} \approx -1 \text{ °C} $$

### Final Answer
$$ \boxed{T_g \text{ of plasticized PVC} \approx -1 \text{ °C} } $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The pure PVC T<sub>g</sub> of 87 °C drops to about **-1 °C** with 35 % DOP. This is why heavily plasticized PVC (e.g., 50 phr DOP) is flexible at room temperature — the T<sub>g</sub> is now **below the use temperature**, putting the polymer in the rubbery state.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rule of Mixtures for Filled Polypropylene

> **Problem Statement:** A polypropylene (PP) matrix (E<sub>m</sub> = 1500 MPa) is reinforced with 25 vol% of glass fibre (E<sub>f</sub> = 72,000 MPa). Calculate the longitudinal modulus of the composite using the rule of mixtures. What if talc filler (E<sub>f</sub> = 30,000 MPa) is used at the same volume fraction?

### Step 1 — Identify the Equation

$$ E_c = E_m(1 - V_f) + E_f \cdot V_f $$

**Reasoning:** This linear rule-of-mixtures assumes **iso-strain** (Voigt model) — valid for continuous, aligned fibres loaded along the fibre direction. For particulate fillers, it is an upper bound.

### Step 2 — Glass Fibre Case

$$ E_c = 1500 \times (1 - 0.25) + 72000 \times 0.25 $$

$$ E_c = 1500 \times 0.75 + 72000 \times 0.25 $$

$$ E_c = 1125 + 18000 $$

$$ E_c = 19125 \text{ MPa} \approx 19.1 \text{ GPa} $$

**Result:** Modulus increased by a factor of **~12.7** (from 1.5 GPa to 19.1 GPa).

### Step 3 — Talc Case

$$ E_c = 1500 \times 0.75 + 30000 \times 0.25 $$

$$ E_c = 1125 + 7500 $$

$$ E_c = 8625 \text{ MPa} \approx 8.6 \text{ GPa} $$

**Result:** Modulus increased by a factor of **~5.75** (from 1.5 GPa to 8.6 GPa).

### Step 4 — Comparative Insight

| Reinforcement | Volume Fraction | Modulus | Increase Factor |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| None (virgin PP) | 0 % | 1,500 MPa | 1.0 × |
| Talc particulate | 25 % | 8,625 MPa | 5.75 × |
| Glass fibre (aligned) | 25 % | 19,125 MPa | 12.75 × |

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away for the exam:** Particulate fillers (talc, CaCO<sub>3</sub>) give much lower reinforcement than continuous/short fibres. The rule of mixtures is an **upper bound**; real composites fall 10–30 % below this value due to imperfect bonding, fibre orientation, and aspect-ratio effects.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Clamping Tonnage Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A polypropylene part has a projected area of 400 cm². The estimated cavity pressure is 50 MPa. Calculate (a) the minimum clamping tonnage required with a safety factor of 1.2, and (b) the required tonnage using the rule-of-thumb divisor (use 2.5).

### Step 1 — Convert Projected Area to m²

$$ A_{proj} = 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Reasoning:** Pressure is in MPa = N/mm² = 10⁶ N/m². Consistency of SI units is critical.

### Step 2 — Calculate Theoretical Clamping Force (a)

$$ F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times k $$

$$ F_{clamp} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \times 0.04 \text{ m}^2 \times 1.2 $$

$$ F_{clamp} = 50 \times 10^6 \times 0.04 = 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N} $$

$$ F_{clamp} \times 1.2 = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N} $$

### Step 3 — Convert to Tonnes

1 tonne ≈ 9.81 kN ≈ 10 kN (rule-of-thumb in moulding industry).

$$ F_{clamp} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N}}{9810 \text{ N/tonne}} \approx 244.6 \text{ tonnes} \approx 250 \text{ tonnes} $$

### Step 4 — Rule-of-Thumb Estimate (b)

$$ F_{clamp} \text{ (tonnes)} = \frac{A_{proj} \text{ (cm}^2)}{2.5} = \frac{400}{2.5} = 160 \text{ tonnes} $$

### Step 5 — Comparison and Engineering Interpretation

| Method | Tonnage | Note |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Rigorous (with k=1.2) | ~250 t | Used in precision mould design |
| Rule-of-thumb (÷2.5) | 160 t | Used in initial machine selection |

**Reasoning:** The rigorous method uses 50 MPa cavity pressure, which is the *peak* during filling. The rule-of-thumb assumes an *average* effective cavity pressure of ~40 MPa, hence the lower number. The engineer always selects the next **standard** machine tonnage — in this case, a 250-tonne IMM would be chosen.

### Final Answer
$$ \boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 245 \text{ tonnes (rigorous)} \approx 160 \text{ tonnes (rule of thumb)} } $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Shrinkage and Shot Size

> **Problem Statement:** A mould cavity has a volume of 200 cm³. The polymer (PA 6,6) has a mould shrinkage of 1.8 %. Calculate (a) the part volume, and (b) the required shot volume including a 5 % safety margin.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Part Volume

Shrinkage is defined relative to the mould:

$$ \text{Shrinkage \%} = \frac{V_{mould} - V_{part}}{V_{mould}} \times 100 $$

$$ V_{part} = V_{mould} \times \left( 1 - \frac{\text{shrinkage}}{100} \right) $$

$$ V_{part} = 200 \times (1 - 0.018) $$

$$ V_{part} = 200 \times 0.982 = 196.4 \text{ cm}^3 $$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Required Shot Volume (with safety margin)

$$ V_{shot} = V_{mould} \times (1 + \text{shrinkage}) \times 1.05 $$

Wait — let us re-derive this carefully. The mould cavity is 200 cm³ **at room temperature**. The polymer melt at processing temperature must **fill** this cavity AND **compensate for shrinkage** during cooling.

The shot size (volume of melt at injection temperature) must equal:

$$ V_{shot} = V_{mould} \times (1 + \text{shrinkage}) $$

This is because the part shrinks *from* the mould dimension *to* the part dimension. So the molten volume must be **larger** than the mould volume by the shrinkage factor.

$$ V_{shot} = 200 \times (1 + 0.018) = 200 \times 1.018 = 203.6 \text{ cm}^3 $$

### Step 3 — Add 5 % Safety Margin

$$ V_{shot, final} = 203.6 \times 1.05 = 213.78 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 214 \text{ cm}^3 $$

### Final Answer

$$ \boxed{V_{part} = 196.4 \text{ cm}^3 \quad ; \quad V_{shot, with margin} \approx 214 \text{ cm}^3 } $$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Cycle Time Estimation

> **Problem Statement:** A thin-wall PP container is injection moulded. The wall thickness is 1.5 mm. The mould temperature is 25 °C, the melt temperature is 220 °C, and the ejection temperature is 100 °C. The average heat transfer coefficient is h = 2500 W/m²·K. Use the rule of cooling time: $t_{cool} \approx \frac{t^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}$ where $\alpha$ is thermal diffusivity ≈ 0.1 mm²/s for PP. Estimate the cooling time and the total cycle time if other phases sum to 8 s.

### Step 1 — Recall the Cooling Time Formula (1D Fourier approximation)

For a polymer plate of half-thickness $b$ cooled from both sides:

$$ t_{cool} \approx \frac{b^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left( \frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{ej} - T_{mould}} \right) $$

A simpler engineering form (used in industry) is:

$$ t_{cool} \approx \frac{t^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} $$

where $t$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity.

### Step 2 — Convert Units

Wall thickness $t = 1.5 \text{ mm}$, $\alpha = 0.1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$.

$$ t^2 = (1.5)^2 = 2.25 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ \pi^2 = 9.8696 $$

### Step 3 — Compute the Cooling Time

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{2.25}{9.8696 \times 0.1} = \frac{2.25}{0.9870} \approx 2.28 \text{ s} $$

This is the theoretical minimum. In practice, we use a **safety factor of 2 to 3** to account for non-uniform cooling and ejection criteria:

$$ t_{cool, design} = 2.28 \times 2.5 \approx 5.7 \text{ s} \approx 6 \text{ s} $$

### Step 4 — Total Cycle Time

$$ t_{cycle} = t_{cool} + t_{other} = 6 + 8 = 14 \text{ s} $$

### Final Answer

$$ \boxed{t_{cool} \approx 2.3 \text{ s (theory) } \approx 6 \text{ s (design)} \quad ; \quad t_{cycle} \approx 14 \text{ s} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> The injection moulding cycle time is **dominated by cooling**, which in turn scales with the **square of wall thickness**. This is why **thin-wall moulding** is a global trend in packaging (caps, containers, mobile-phone shells) — it dramatically reduces cycle time and increases productivity.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — How Each Additive Affects a "Reference" PP

| Property | Pure PP | + Plasticizer (oil) | + 2 % Carbon black | + 0.3 % Irganox 1010 | + 0.3 % Tinuvin 770 | + 20 % Talc | + 0.5 % GMS (antistat) | + 30 % ATH (FR) |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Tensile Strength** | 33 MPa | ↓ to ~25 | ~33 | ~33 | ~33 | ↑ to ~36 | ~33 | ↓ to ~24 |
| **Elongation** | 12 % | ↑↑ to 50+ | ↓ to ~9 | ~12 | ~12 | ↓ to ~5 | ~12 | ↓ to ~3 |
| **Modulus** | 1.5 GPa | ↓ to ~0.7 | ~1.6 | ~1.5 | ~1.5 | ↑↑ to ~3.5 | ~1.5 | ↑ to ~2.5 |
| **MFI** | 12 g/10m | ↑ to ~30 | ~12 | ~12 | ~12 | ↓ to ~9 | ~12 | ↓ to ~6 |
| **HDT** | 110 °C | ↓ to ~85 | ~115 | ~110 | ~110 | ↑↑ to ~135 | ~110 | ↑ to ~140 |
| **LOI** | 17 % | ~17 | ~18 | ~17 | ~17 | ~18 | ~17 | ↑↑ to ~30 |
| **Surface Resistivity** | 10¹⁶ Ω·sq | ~10¹⁶ | ↓ to 10⁴ | ~10¹⁶ | ~10¹⁶ | ~10¹⁶ | ↓ to 10¹⁰ | ~10¹⁶ |
| **UV life (outdoor)** | < 1 yr | < 1 yr | ↑↑ > 10 yrs | ~1 yr | ↑↑ > 5 yrs | < 1 yr | < 1 yr | < 1 yr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for the exam:** No single additive improves *all* properties. A plasticizer increases flexibility but reduces strength and HDT. A filler increases stiffness and HDT but reduces elongation. The polymer scientist's job is to find the **optimum balance** through the **formulation recipe** — typically 3 to 6 additives used synergistically.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modelled **exactly** after the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference. **Internal choice** is provided in Part B as Question A and Question B, each of 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Full model solutions are included.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a plasticizer. With one example, explain how it reduces the glass transition temperature of a polymer. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A **plasticizer** is a low molecular weight, non-volatile organic compound that, when added to a polymer, reduces the intermolecular forces between polymer chains and lowers the glass transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub>), thereby increasing flexibility, elongation, and processability.

**Example:** **Dioctyl phthalate (DOP / DEHP)** in **PVC**.

**Mechanism (any 2 valid points, 1.5 marks each):**
1. DOP molecules are small (MW ~ 390) compared to PVC chains (MW ~ 50,000–100,000) and insert themselves between adjacent PVC chains.
2. This increases the **free volume** and **reduces secondary bonding (van der Waals / dipole-dipole interactions)**.
3. Consequently, the chain segments become mobile at a lower temperature, i.e., T<sub>g</sub> is reduced. For example, pure PVC T<sub>g</sub> ≈ 87 °C; with 35 phr DOP, T<sub>g</sub> drops to about -1 °C (verified by Fox equation in Section 3.1).

---

### Question 2: List any three differences between pigments and dyes as colourants for polymers. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Property | Pigments | Dyes |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Solubility in polymer** | Insoluble — exist as dispersed solid particles | Soluble in the polymer melt |
| 2 | **Opacity** | Usually opaque | Transparent |
| 3 | **Migration / Bleeding** | Minimal (preferred for long-term colour) | Higher tendency to bleed / sublime |
| 4 | **Heat stability** | Generally high (e.g., TiO<sub>2</sub>) | Often lower |
| 5 | **Lightfastness** | Excellent (e.g., phthalocyanines) | Variable |
| 6 | **Examples** | TiO<sub>2</sub>, iron oxide, carbon black, ultramarine blue | Anthraquinone, azo dyes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "pigments are solid, dyes are liquid" — this is **incomplete and partially wrong**. The correct distinction is **solubility / dispersibility in the polymer**. Liquid dyes exist. Marks will be docked if you do not mention solubility.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

#### Part (a): Classify polymer additives. Discuss in detail the role of heat stabilizers, antioxidants, and UV absorbers in polymer processing and service life. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Polymer Additives (3 marks for the table):**

Polymer additives can be classified based on their function into the following major groups:
1. **Processing aids** — Plasticizers, Lubricants, Blowing agents
2. **Stabilizers** — Heat stabilizers, Antioxidants, UV absorbers
3. **Property enhancers** — Fillers, Reinforcements, Flame retardants, Antistatic agents
4. **Aesthetic additives** — Colourants (pigments and dyes), Antistatic agents
5. **Functional additives** — Blowing agents, Impact modifiers, Coupling agents

**Heat Stabilizers (1.5 marks):**
Heat stabilizers — particularly for **PVC** — prevent **dehydrochlorination** at processing temperatures (180–220 °C). The major classes are:
- **Lead-based** (tribasic lead sulphate) — cheap, effective, but toxic (REACH restricted).
- **Mixed metal** (Ba/Cd, Ba/Zn, Ca/Zn) — synergistic soap systems widely used in flexible PVC.
- **Organotin** (dibutyltin dilaurate) — best for rigid, transparent, food-contact PVC.
- **Epoxy co-stabilizers** (ESBO) and **phosphites** act as secondary stabilizers.

Mechanism: they **absorb liberated HCl** (preventing the autocatalytic degradation cascade) and **replace labile chlorine atoms** in the PVC chain.

**Antioxidants (1.5 marks):**
Antioxidants inhibit **oxidative degradation** by scavenging free radicals. Two main types:
- **Primary (chain-breaking)** — Hindered phenols (BHT, Irganox 1010) and secondary amines donate H• to peroxy radicals (ROO•), terminating the chain.
- **Secondary (preventive)** — Phosphites and thio-synergists decompose hydroperoxides (ROOH) into non-radical products.

Typical loading: 0.05–1.5 %. They extend the **service life** of polyolefins, prevent **embrittlement, discoloration, and loss of impact strength**.

**UV Absorbers (1 mark):**
UV absorbers protect against **photo-oxidation** by absorbing harmful UV radiation (290–400 nm) and dissipating it as heat. Classes include:
- **Benzophenones, benzotriazoles, triazines** — competitive UV absorbers.
- **HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers)** — act as cyclic radical scavengers; the **most effective class**, especially Tinuvin 770, Tinuvin 622, Chimassorb 944.
- **Carbon black** (1–3 %) — physical UV screen; can extend PP outdoor life from months to > 10 years.

They retain **gloss, colour, mechanical properties** under outdoor exposure.

---

#### Part (b): Explain the principle of injection moulding with a neat sketch. List any four process parameters that affect the quality of the moulded part. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (4 marks):**

Injection moulding is a cyclic process in which a **precisely metered quantity of polymer melt** is **injected under high pressure** (35–140 MPa) from a heated barrel into a **closed, cooled mould cavity**. The melt solidifies in the cavity to the net shape of the desired part, after which the mould opens and the part is **ejected**.

**Stages of the cycle (with reference to the schematic):**
1. **Mould closing** — the two halves clamp together under high tonnage (200–10,000 kN).
2. **Injection (filling)** — polymer melt is forced through the **sprue → runner → gate → cavity**.
3. **Packing (holding)** — additional melt is forced in to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage**.
4. **Cooling** — water (or oil) circulating in cooling channels removes heat; the largest fraction of cycle time (60–80 %).
5. **Mould opening** — the two halves separate.
6. **Ejection** — ejector pins push the solidified part out.
7. The cycle repeats.

The injection moulding machine consists of **two main units**:
- **Injection unit** — hopper, heated barrel, reciprocating screw, nozzle.
- **Clamping unit** — fixed platen, moving platen, tie bars, toggle or hydraulic mechanism.

**Four key process parameters (3 marks — list and brief explanation of each):**

1. **Melt Temperature (150–350 °C):** Affects melt viscosity, flow, surface finish. Too low → short shots; too high → degradation, flash.
2. **Injection Pressure (35–140 MPa):** Drives the melt into the cavity. Too low → short shots, voids; too high → flash, residual stress.
3. **Mould Temperature (20–200 °C):** Affects crystallinity (in semi-crystalline polymers), shrinkage, surface finish, and cycle time. Higher mould temperature → better surface, less warpage, longer cycle.
4. **Cooling Time (5–120 s):** Determines the cycle time and the part's dimensional accuracy. Insufficient cooling → warping, sink marks, post-moulding distortion.
5. (Bonus parameter) **Packing Pressure** — compensates for shrinkage; affects sink marks and voids.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

#### Part (a): Describe the role and mechanism of action of the following additives: (i) Flame retardants, (ii) Blowing agents, (iii) Antistatic agents. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Flame Retardants (2.5 marks):**

Flame retardants reduce polymer flammability by interrupting the **combustion cycle** (heat → fuel → oxygen → radicals). They act via one or more of the following mechanisms:

- **Gas-phase mechanism** (halogenated FR + Sb<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>): Release halogen radicals (Cl•, Br•) that combine with H• and OH• radicals in the flame, interrupting the chain reaction. Sb<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> synergizes to form volatile SbBr<sub>3</sub> which is more effective.
- **Condensed-phase mechanism** (phosphorus-based FR): Promote **char formation** on the polymer surface; the char acts as a physical barrier to heat, oxygen, and fuel.
- **Endothermic decomposition** (Al(OH)<sub>3</sub>, Mg(OH)<sub>2</sub> — ATH, MDH): Decompose at 200–350 °C, releasing water vapour. This **cools the polymer** and **dilutes flammable gases**, with the additional benefit of low smoke and low toxicity.
- **Inert gas dilution** (melamine cyanurate): Release N<sub>2</sub>, lowering O<sub>2</sub> partial pressure.

Performance is measured by **LOI** (Limiting Oxygen Index) and **UL-94** rating. A LOI > 27 % is considered self-extinguishing.

**(ii) Blowing Agents (2.5 marks):**

Blowing agents generate gas inside the polymer melt to produce a **cellular (foamed) structure**. They are classified as:

- **Chemical Blowing Agents (CBA):** Decompose thermally to release gas.
  - **Azodicarbonamide (ADC/ADCA)** — most widely used; gas yield ~ 220 cm³/g, releases N<sub>2</sub> + CO + CO<sub>2</sub>; decomposition at ~ 200 °C.
  - **Sodium bicarbonate** — releases CO<sub>2</sub>; food-grade (used in PVC plastisols).
  - **5-Phenyl tetrazole (5-PT)** — for high-temperature engineering plastics.
- **Physical Blowing Agents (PBA):** Volatile liquids/gases.
  - **HFCs, hydrocarbons (pentane, cyclopentane)**, liquid CO<sub>2</sub>, N<sub>2</sub>.

Effect: density drops (from ~ 1.0 g/cm³ to as low as 0.02 g/cm³ for ultra-foams); thermal insulation, acoustic damping, and cushioning improve.

**(iii) Antistatic Agents (2 marks):**

Plastics are insulators (surface resistivity ~ 10¹⁴ Ω·sq) and accumulate static charge by triboelectric effect. Antistatic agents work via:

- **Internal / migratory antistats** — e.g., **ethoxylated fatty amines, glycerol monostearate (GMS), quaternary ammonium salts**. They are slightly incompatible and **migrate to the surface**, where they absorb atmospheric moisture, forming a thin **conductive water layer** that dissipates charge.
- **External / topical antistats** — sprayed on as a temporary coating (often quaternary ammonium solutions).
- **Conductive fillers** — carbon black, CNT, carbon fibre, metal powders, stainless-steel fibres — embedded permanently to give surface resistivity < 10⁶ Ω·sq, suitable for **ESD-safe** electronics packaging.

They **prevent dust attraction, electrical shocks, and ESD damage** to electronic components.

---

#### Part (b): With the help of a flow diagram, explain the complete cycle of operations in injection moulding. Discuss the function of (i) the reciprocating screw, and (ii) the clamping unit. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Flow Diagram of the Injection Moulding Cycle (3 marks):**

The cyclic operation proceeds as follows:

$$ \text{Mould Close} \rightarrow \text{Injection} \rightarrow \text{Packing} \rightarrow \text{Cooling} \rightarrow \text{Mould Open} \rightarrow \text{Ejection} \rightarrow \text{Part Remove} \rightarrow \text{(Back to Mould Close)} $$

**Stage-wise explanation (2 marks):**

1. **Mould Close:** The two halves of the mould are brought together and held under high clamping force.
2. **Injection:** The screw acts as a plunger, forcing the metered shot of melt through the nozzle, sprue, runner, and gate into the cavity under high pressure (35–140 MPa).
3. **Packing (Holding):** A secondary lower pressure maintains melt flow into the cavity to compensate volumetric shrinkage as the part begins to solidify.
4. **Cooling:** The part solidifies inside the cooled mould. Cooling time is 60–80 % of total cycle time. Chilled water (5–40 °C) circulates in cooling channels.
5. **Mould Open:** The clamping unit retracts the moving platen.
6. **Ejection:** Ejector pins push the part out of the cavity.
7. **Part Remove:** The part is taken away by hand, gravity, or a robot; the cycle repeats.

**(i) Reciprocating Screw (1 mark):**

The reciprocating screw is the **heart of the injection unit**. It performs **three functions simultaneously**:
- **Conveying** — rotates to transport pellets forward.
- **Melting (plastication)** — combines external band heaters with frictional shear.
- **Metering** — accumulates a precise shot volume in front of the screw tip.

The screw has **three zones**:
- **Feed zone** (~ 50 % of length) — conveying.
- **Compression (melt) zone** (~ 25–30 %) — melting by shear and conduction.
- **Metering zone** (~ 20–25 %) — pumping, homogenizing, building pressure.

The L/D ratio is typically **15:1 to 24:1**. The compression ratio is **2:1 to 4:1**.

**(ii) Clamping Unit (1 mark):**

The clamping unit **holds the two mould halves together** against the cavity pressure (which can reach 50 MPa × projected area — a force of several thousand kN). Without sufficient clamping force, the mould would "open" under pressure, producing **flash (burrs)** on the part.

Two main types:
- **Toggle clamp** — uses a 4-bar linkage to multiply hydraulic force; fast, energy-efficient, used in most general-purpose machines.
- **Hydraulic clamp** — uses a direct hydraulic ram; provides precise tonnage control, better for large parts and high-pressure moulds.

Tonnage range: **200 kN to 10,000 kN**. The required tonnage is calculated as:
$$ F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{projected} \times \text{safety factor} $$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Plasticizers with Lubricants.** Plasticizers reduce inter-chain friction *within* the polymer and are mostly *compatible*; lubricants reduce friction at the *polymer–metal* interface. Mixing these up costs a full 7-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the examples.** KTU requires *named* examples (DOP, Irganox 1010, Tinuvin 770, TiO<sub>2</sub>, etc.). Vague statements like "a hindered phenol is added" without naming cost marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Blowing agents.** Many students think blowing agents are "gas cylinders"; in polymer processing they are **chemical compounds that decompose to release gas inside the polymer melt** (with one or two exceptions being physical blowing agents).
> 4. **Confusing Heat Stabilizers with Antioxidants.** Heat stabilizers protect against **thermal degradation during processing**; antioxidants protect against **oxidative degradation during service life**. Both involve free radicals, but their *triggers* and *operating temperatures* are different.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in injection moulding questions.** A neat labelled diagram (sprue, runner, gate, cavity, cooling channel, ejector pin) is worth **at least 3–4 marks out of 7**. Omitting it = automatic heavy deduction.
> 6. **Not mentioning cycle time breakdown.** Always state that **cooling time = 60–80 % of cycle time**. This single line often distinguishes a 5-mark answer from a 7-mark answer.
> 7. **Forgetting safety margins in tonnage calculations.** Using the bare formula without a safety factor (1.1–1.3) is a common error.
> 8. **Confusing HALS with UV Absorbers.** HALS do **not** absorb UV; they scavenge free radicals in a cyclic process. This is a KTU favourite trick question.
