## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this section as a last-minute revision sheet the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Plasticizers** = reduce T<sub>g</sub>, increase flexibility. Mechanism = insertion between chains (lubricity / gel theory). Main example: **DOP (DEHP)** in PVC. Use **Fox equation** to calculate new T<sub>g</sub>.
- [x] **Colourants:** Pigments = insoluble, opaque, preferred. Dyes = soluble, transparent, bleed-prone. **TiO<sub>2</sub>** = most used white pigment. **Carbon black** = UV screen.
- [x] **Heat Stabilizers:** Critical for **PVC**. Classes = lead, mixed metal (Ba/Cd, Ca/Zn), organotin, epoxy, phosphite. They **absorb HCl** released during dehydrochlorination.
- [x] **Antioxidants:** Primary = **hindered phenols** (Irganox 1010, BHT). Secondary = **phosphites, thio-synergists**. Loading: 0.05–1.5 %. Protect against autoxidation.
- [x] **UV Absorbers:** Classes = **benzophenones, benzotriazoles, triazines, HALS, nickel quenchers, carbon black**. HALS most effective for polyolefins. Carbon black at 1–3 % is a "poor man's UV stabilizer".
- [x] **Antistatic Agents:** Surface resistivity 10¹⁴ → 10⁹–10¹¹ Ω·sq. Internal = **GMS, ethoxylated amines, quaternary ammonium salts**. External = topical spray. Permanent = **carbon black, CNT, metal fibres** for ESD.
- [x] **Flame Retardants:** Halogen + Sb<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> (gas phase). Phosphorus (condensed phase / char). **ATH, MDH** (endothermic, low smoke). **Melamine** (inert gas). LOI > 27 % = self-extinguishing.
- [x] **Blowing Agents:** Chemical = **ADC (azodicarbonamide)**, sodium bicarbonate, TSH, 5-PT. Physical = HFCs, hydrocarbons, CO<sub>2</sub>, N<sub>2</sub>. Gas yield of ADC ≈ 220 cm³/g.
- [x] **Lubricants:** Internal = waxes, fatty acid esters. External = paraffin wax, metal stearates. Reduce melt viscosity & mould sticking.
- [x] **Fillers:** Inert (CaCO<sub>3</sub>, talc), reinforcing (carbon black, silica), fibrous (glass, carbon fibre), functional (Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, BN, graphite). **Coupling agents (silanes, titanates)** improve filler–matrix bonding. Use **Rule of Mixtures** for modulus.
- [x] **Injection Moulding:** Cyclic process. **Injection unit** (hopper, barrel, screw, nozzle) + **Clamping unit** (toggle or hydraulic). Pressure: 35–140 MPa. Temperature: 150–350 °C melt, 20–200 °C mould.
- [x] **Cycle Stages:** Mould close → Injection → Packing → Cooling → Mould open → Ejection → Part remove.
- [x] **Screw Zones:** Feed (50 %) → Compression (25–30 %) → Metering (20–25 %). L/D = 15:1 to 24:1. Compression ratio = 2:1 to 4:1.
- [x] **Clamping Tonnage:** $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times k$. Rule of thumb: tonnes ≈ A(cm²) ÷ (2 to 4).
- [x] **Shrinkage:** Crystalline (PP, PE, PA) = 1.5–3.0 %. Amorphous (PS, ABS, PC) = 0.4–0.7 %. Filled systems = 0.2–0.5 %.
- [x] **Cooling time** = 60–80 % of total cycle; scales with **square of wall thickness**.
- [x] **Common defects:** Sink marks (low packing), weld lines (low T), warping (uneven cooling), flash (low clamp tonnage), short shot (insufficient material).
- [x] **Always draw a labelled diagram** of the injection mould (sprue, runner, gate, cavity, vents, cooling channels, ejector pins) — at least 3–4 marks are awarded for this.
- [x] **Always name at least one commercial example** for every additive class.
- [x] **Always use the KTU 2024 scheme verb conventions**: "Define", "Classify", "Explain with neat sketch", "Discuss", "Compare and contrast".

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — PECHT523 Polymer Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme).**
> **Best of luck for your End Semester Examination!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compression Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compression Moulding** is defined as a high-pressure thermoset moulding process in which a measured charge of moulding compound (powder, granules, preform, or dough-like mass) is placed directly into an open, heated mould cavity, the mould is then closed under pressure (**typically 7–70 MPa**), and the material is forced to flow, fill the cavity, and cure (crosslink) into a rigid, dimensionally stable finished part by the combined action of **heat (typically 120–200 °C)** and **pressure**.

It is the **oldest, simplest, and most versatile** polymer processing technique, used primarily for **thermosetting plastics** (phenolics, urea-formaldehyde, melamine, epoxies, unsaturated polyester, DAP — diallyl phthalate) and, in special bulk-moulding compound (BMC) and high-pressure sheet form, also for certain **thermoplastics** (UHMWPE, PTFE, PVC, PP).

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Charge** | Pre-weighed quantity of moulding compound |
| **Preform** | Compressed pill / biscuit of resin + filler for easy handling |
| **Cure** | Irreversible crosslinking of thermoset resin under heat |
| **Bump** | Brief mould opening to release trapped gases (volatiles) |
| **Breathing** | Same as "bump" — venting step before final cure |
| **Flash** | Excess material that escapes the mould parting line |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of an Idli-Maker / Waffle-Iron / Sandwich Press:** You pour (or place) a measured quantity of batter (resin + filler) onto the lower hot plate, close the upper plate, press it down, hold for a fixed time, and out comes a fully cooked, rigid cake. The batter cannot escape sideways because the plates are closed, so it spreads and takes the exact shape of the cavity. That is **exactly** what happens in compression moulding: *measured charge + closed hot mould + pressure + time = a finished, hardened polymer part.*

A second analogy is the **artisanal "papad press"** used in Indian kitchens — a measured dough ball is pressed between two heated dies, the handles squeezed shut, and heat cures the dough into a thin, hard disc.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (from KTU Module 4)

- **Hand Lay-Up Compression Moulding** (simplest)
- **Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC) Compression**
- **Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC) / Dough Moulding Compound (DMC) Compression**
- **Preform / Mat Moulding** (used for reinforced parts)
- **High-Pressure Compression Moulding of Thermoplastics** (RAM, PTFE)
- **Transfer Moulding** (a derivative — covered in next topic)

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU ESE, focus on **thermoset compression moulding**, **mould types (flash, positive, semi-positive, landed-plunger)**, **the full process cycle**, **advantages/disadvantages**, and **applications** — these are the high-weightage items.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — "The Why and The How"

The fundamental principle of compression moulding rests on three simultaneous physical phenomena occurring inside the closed mould:

1. **Heat Transfer** — Conduction from hot mould surfaces (typically 150–180 °C) into the cold charge raises the resin temperature past its melting/softening point, allowing molecular mobility.
2. **Pressure-Driven Flow** — The closing force (hydraulic ram, 7–70 MPa) forces the softened resin to flow into every cavity corner, displacing air and consolidating the charge against reinforcement.
3. **Cure (Crosslinking)** — At the moulding temperature, the thermoset resin undergoes irreversible chemical crosslinking (condensation or addition polymerization), forming a 3-D network that locks the part geometry permanently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The charge is placed in the mould **before** closing. This is what fundamentally differentiates compression moulding from **injection moulding**, where the material is injected *after* the mould is closed.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic

| Step # | Operation | Purpose / "Why" |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Preform / Charge Preparation** | Resin, filler, catalyst, pigment, lubricant are compounded and shaped into a pill/preform for fast, dust-free loading |
| 2 | **Mould Cleaning & Lubrication** | Release agent (silicone, wax) prevents part sticking to cavity |
| 3 | **Charge Loading** | Measured charge placed in **open** lower cavity; position controls flow direction |
| 4 | **Mould Closing (Slow → Fast → Slow)** | Slow approach to avoid air entrapment; final slow squeeze for uniform pressure |
| 5 | **Breathing / Bump (1–3 times)** | Brief mould opening at 30–60% cure to release volatiles (water in phenolics, styrene in polyesters, air bubbles) |
| 6 | **Final Cure Under Pressure** | Full pressure held until resin reaches gel point and crosslinking is complete |
| 7 | **Mould Opening** | After cure, mould opens; part stays on one half (usually male, by adhesion or ejector pins) |
| 8 | **Part Ejection** | Knockout pins / stripper plate pushes out finished part |
| 9 | **Deflashing / Trimming** | Flash (thin web at parting line) is removed manually or by tumbling |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | P = moulding pressure, F = clamping force, A = projected cavity area | MPa | Hydraulic press sizing |
| 2 | $F = P \times A$ | — | N / kN | Press tonnage calculation |
| 3 | $BF = \dfrac{\rho_{moulded}}}{\rho_{charge}}$ | BF = Bulk Factor, $\rho$ = density | dimensionless | Charge volume vs part volume |
| 4 | $V_{charge} = BF \times V_{part}$ | V = volume | cm³ | Charge mass calculation |
| 5 | $m = \rho_{charge} \times V_{charge}$ | m = charge mass | g | Weighing of charge |
| 6 | $t_{cure} = \dfrac{t_{ref}} \cdot 2^{\Delta T / 10}$ | t = time, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | s / min | Approximate cure-time rule of thumb (10 °C rule) |
| 7 | $\ln \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Arrhenius-type cure shift | — | Effect of temperature on cure time |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{L_{mould} - L_{part}}}{L_{mould}} \times 100$ | S = mould shrinkage | % | Mould-cavity oversize design |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{x}$ | Fourier conduction through mould wall | W | Mould heating requirement |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | $\eta$ = melt viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate | Pa·s | Resin flow under pressure |
| 11 | $C_{p,mould}} = m_{steel} c_{p,steel} + m_{ins}}$ | Mould heat capacity for energy calc. | J/K | Energy to heat mould |
| 12 | $t_{cycle} = t_{close}} + t_{cure}} + t_{open}} + t_{load}} + t_{ej}}$ | Full cycle time | s | Production rate (parts/hr) |
| 13 | $P_{cycle}} = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}}}$ | Production rate | parts/hr | Capacity planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Questions on **shrinkage compensation (S)**, **cure time by 10 °C rule (rule of six)**, **bulk factor**, and **press tonnage (F = P × A)** appear almost every year in Part A or Part B. Memorize formulae 1, 3, 6, 8 above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** SMC compression-moulded body panels (BMC for headlamp reflectors, valve covers).
- **Electrical:** Phenolic moulded switches, MCBs, Bakelite switches, terminal blocks — high heat resistance.
- **Cookware & Kitchenware:** Melamine plates, bowls (low cost, food-safe, scratch-resistant).
- **Aerospace / Defence:** Prepreg compression moulded radomes, ablative nose cones, phenolic heat shields.
- **Industrial:** PTFE seals, UHMWPE wear strips, glass-fibre reinforced epoxy structural brackets.
- **Sanitaryware:** SMC bathtubs, shower trays, vanity units (replacing cast iron).

> [!TIP]
> Compression moulding is the **lowest-tooling-cost** mass-production method for large, flat, moderately-complex thermoset parts. The **economics rule**: use compression moulding for parts with **> 5 mm wall thickness**, **simple geometry**, and **production runs of 500–100,000 parts/year** — beyond that, switch to injection moulding of thermoplastics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is **calculation-intensive**, exactly what KTU board examiners expect in 14-mark Part-B problems. All transitions are shown explicitly — no skipped algebra.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Press Tonnage (Clamping Force)

### Problem Formulation
You are asked to find the **hydraulic press tonnage** required to mould a melamine dinnerware plate of **projected area 250 cm²** at a moulding pressure of **25 MPa**.

### Step 1 — Recall the fundamental relationship

$$P = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

where **P** = moulding pressure, **F** = closing force, **A** = projected cavity area.

### Step 2 — Convert area into consistent SI units (m²)

$$A = 250 \; \text{cm}^2 \times \left(\dfrac{1 \; \text{m}}{100 \; \text{cm}}\right)^2 = 250 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 = 0.025 \; \text{m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Solve for F

$$F = P \times A$$

$$F = 25 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa} \times 0.025 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$F = 6.25 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to tonnes-force (1 tonne-f ≈ 9.81 kN = 9810 N)

$$F = \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{5}}{9810} \; \text{tonne-f} \approx 63.7 \; \text{tonne-f}$$

### Result
A press of **minimum 65 tonne capacity** (with a **safety factor of 1.5–2 ×** → 100 tonne press) should be selected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students forget to convert cm² to m² and get 1000× wrong answers. Always check units first.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bulk Factor & Charge Mass

### Problem Formulation
A phenolic moulded part has a finished volume of **80 cm³** and a density of **1.35 g/cm³** in the moulded state. The preform has a bulk density of **0.75 g/cm³**. Determine (a) the bulk factor, (b) the charge volume, (c) the mass of charge to be weighed.

### Step 1 — Apply the bulk factor definition

$$BF = \dfrac{\rho_{moulded}}}{\rho_{charge}}$$

$$BF = \dfrac{1.35}{0.75} = 1.80$$

### Step 2 — Calculate charge volume

$$V_{charge} = BF \times V_{part}$$

$$V_{charge} = 1.80 \times 80 \; \text{cm}^3 = 144 \; \text{cm}^3$$

### Step 3 — Calculate mass using bulk (charge) density

$$m = \rho_{charge} \times V_{charge}$$

$$m = 0.75 \; \text{g/cm}^3 \times 144 \; \text{cm}^3 = 108 \; \text{g}$$

### Verification
Using moulded density: $m = 1.35 \times 80 = 108 \; \text{g}$ ✓ (mass conservation holds).

### Result
- BF = **1.80**
- Charge volume = **144 cm³**
- Charge mass = **108 g**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cure Time Using the 10 °C Rule

### Statement of the Rule
For most thermosets, the **cure rate doubles for every 10 °C rise** in mould temperature (analogous to the Vant Hoff rule for chemical reactions). The mathematical form is:

$$t_{cure, T_2} = t_{cure, T_1} \cdot 2^{\,(T_1 - T_2)/10}$$

### Problem Formulation
A phenolic resin requires **120 seconds** to cure at **160 °C**. If the mould temperature is raised to **180 °C**, what is the new cure time?

### Step 1 — Identify parameters
- $t_1 = 120 \; \text{s}$
- $T_1 = 160 \; °C$
- $T_2 = 180 \; °C$
- $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = -20 \; °C$ (since $T_2 > T_1$)

### Step 2 — Substitute into the formula

$$t_2 = 120 \times 2^{(-20)/10}$$

$$t_2 = 120 \times 2^{-2}$$

$$t_2 = 120 \times \dfrac{1}{4}$$

$$t_2 = 30 \; \text{s}$$

### Result
At 180 °C, the cure time drops from **120 s to 30 s** — a **75 % reduction**. This is why thermoset moulding is performed hot.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mould Shrinkage & Cavity Oversize

### Problem Formulation
A glass-fibre reinforced polyester (SMC) part is to be **250 mm long**. The expected mould shrinkage is **0.3 %**. What should be the **cavity length** in the mould steel?

### Step 1 — Apply the shrinkage formula

$$S = \dfrac{L_{mould} - L_{part}}}{L_{mould}} \times 100$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $L_{mould}$

$$L_{mould} = \dfrac{L_{part}}}{1 - S/100}$$

$$L_{mould} = \dfrac{250}{1 - 0.003}$$

$$L_{mould} = \dfrac{250}{0.997}$$

$$L_{mould} = 250.752 \; \text{mm}$$

### Result
The cavity must be **oversized by 0.752 mm** to compensate for cooling/cure shrinkage.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Production Rate (Cycle-Time Economics)

### Problem Formulation
The complete compression moulding cycle for a switch housing is: **load = 4 s, close = 6 s, cure = 90 s, open = 4 s, eject = 3 s, with 2 bumps of 2 s each**. Calculate **(a)** total cycle time, **(b)** production rate per hour.

### Step 1 — Total cycle time

$$t_{cycle} = t_{load}} + t_{close}} + t_{bump}} + t_{cure}} + t_{open}} + t_{eject}}$$

$$t_{cycle} = 4 + 6 + (2 \times 2) + 90 + 4 + 3$$

$$t_{cycle} = 111 \; \text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Production rate (parts/hr)

$$P_{cycle}} = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}}} = \dfrac{3600}{111} \approx 32.4 \; \text{parts/hr}$$

### Step 3 — For a 16-hour shift

$$\text{Daily output} = 32.4 \times 16 \approx 519 \; \text{parts/day}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Mould Types

| Mould Type | Flash Behaviour | Land Clearance | Material Wastage | Tolerance | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash Mould (Land-edge plunger)** | Excess flashes out freely | Negative (plunger smaller than cavity) | High (10–20 % flash) | Poor (±0.4 mm) | Bulk production of small, thin parts (switches, plugs) |
| **Positive Mould (Full positive)** | No flash, plunger fits cavity | Zero (force-fit) | Zero (no flash) | Excellent (±0.05 mm) | Precision parts, costly materials, BMC/SMC |
| **Semi-Positive Mould (Hybrid)** | Flash forms, but plunger also lands | Slight positive on plunger | Low (3–5 % flash) | Good (±0.15 mm) | General-purpose (most common in industry) |
| **Landed Plunger (Sub-type)** | Land on plunger, not on cavity | Plunger has a wider land | Medium | Good | Phenolic/Bakelite switch bases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU exam:** if asked "Which mould gives the best dimensional accuracy?" — answer **Positive mould**. If asked "Which mould is most economical for mass production?" — answer **Flash mould**. If asked "Which is most commonly used in industry?" — answer **Semi-positive mould**.

---

## 3.7 Parametric Sensitivity Matrix (How output changes with input)

| Input Parameter ↑ | Effect on Cure Time | Effect on Shrinkage | Effect on Part Quality | Effect on Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mould Temperature ↑ | Decreases sharply (Arrhenius) | Slightly increases (thermal expansion) | Faster cure, possible scorching | Higher heater load |
| Pressure ↑ | Marginal decrease | Decreases (less voids) | Better density, lower flash | Higher press tonnage |
| Cure Time ↑ | — | Marginal increase | Complete cure, brittleness if excessive | Lower production rate |
| Filler Content ↑ | Marginal | Reduces shrinkage | Stronger, stiffer, cheaper | Faster cycle |
| Bulk Factor ↑ | — | — | More flash if not controlled | Requires more careful loading |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process-Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resin + Filler + Catalyst + Pigment] --> B[Compounding and Preform Press]
    B --> C[Preform Pill or Charge]
    C --> D[Mould Cleaning and Release Agent]
    D --> E[Load Charge into Open Lower Cavity]
    E --> F[Close Mould under Hydraulic Pressure]
    F --> G{Bump or Breathing Step Required?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Brief Mould Opening to Release Volatiles]
    H --> F
    G -- No --> I[Hold Pressure and Heat for Full Cure]
    I --> J[Open Mould and Eject Cured Part]
    J --> K[Deflash and Trim]
    K --> L[Quality Inspection and Pack]
    L --> M[Finished Compression Moulded Part]
```

## 4.2 Mould Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upper Platen or Hydraulic Ram] --> B[Force Transmitted via Tie Bars]
    B --> C[Upper Half Male Plunger]
    C --> D[Cavity Region]
    D --> E[Lower Half Female Cavity]
    E --> F[Lower Platen with Heaters and Ejector Pins]
    F --> G[Heating Channels Electrical or Oil]
    F --> H[Cooling Channels Optional for Thermoplastics]
    F --> I[Knockout Pin Assembly]
    I --> J[Ejected Finished Part]
    D --> K[Parting Line Region Flash Escape]
```

## 4.3 Decision Diagram for Selecting Mould Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Compression Mould Type] --> B{Required Tolerance?}
    B -- Tight less than 0.1 mm --> P[Positive Mould]
    B -- Moderate 0.1 to 0.3 mm --> C{Material Cost Sensitive?}
    C -- Yes --> S1[Flash Mould]
    C -- No --> S2[Semi-Positive Mould]
    B -- Loose greater than 0.5 mm --> F1[Flash Mould Lowest Cost]
    P --> Z[High Tool Cost but Zero Flash]
    S1 --> Z1[Low Tool Cost and High Flash Loss]
    S2 --> Z2[Balanced Choice Most Common]
    F1 --> Z3[Cheapest but Wastes Material]
```

## 4.4 Cause-Effect Diagram for Moulding Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEF[Compression Moulded Defect] --> C1[Blisters and Voids]
    DEF --> C2[Warpage]
    DEF --> C3[Short Shot]
    DEF --> C4[Flash Excessive]
    DEF --> C5[Sticking in Mould]
    C1 --> R1[Insufficient Bump / Volatiles Trapped]
    C1 --> R2[Cure Temperature Too High]
    C2 --> R3[Uneven Heating / Non-uniform Cure]
    C2 --> R4[Non-uniform Wall Thickness]
    C3 --> R5[Charge Mass Too Low]
    C3 --> R6[Insufficient Pressure]
    C3 --> R7[Charge Mispositioned]
    C4 --> R8[Clamping Force Inadequate]
    C4 --> R9[Worn Mould Land]
    C5 --> R10[Release Agent Failed]
    C5 --> R11[Cure Incomplete Part Sticky]
```

> [!TIP]
> Examiner often asks: *"List any 5 defects in compression moulding and their causes."* The diagram above is your ready-reckoner.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define compression moulding. List any four thermosetting resins commonly processed by this technique.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Compression moulding** is a thermoset moulding process in which a measured charge of moulding compound is placed in an open heated mould cavity, the mould is closed under pressure (typically 7–70 MPa), and heat (~150–180 °C) and pressure are applied until the resin cures (crosslinks) into a rigid, finished part.

**Four thermosetting resins:**
1. **Phenolic resin (PF)** — switches, plugs, circuit breakers.
2. **Urea-Formaldehyde (UF)** — switchboards, electrical fittings, buttons.
3. **Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF)** — dinnerware, table tops.
4. **Unsaturated Polyester (UP)** — SMC body panels, boat hulls.

*(Acceptable alternatives: DAP, epoxy, silicone.)*

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is "bulk factor"? Why is it important in compression moulding?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bulk Factor (BF)** is defined as the ratio of the density of the moulded (solid) part to the bulk (loose) density of the moulding compound:

$$BF = \dfrac{\rho_{moulded}}}{\rho_{charge}}}$$

**Importance:**
- Determines the **volume of charge** to be loaded: $V_{charge} = BF \times V_{part}$.
- A high bulk factor means the powder/granules occupy much more space than the finished part, so the charge must be carefully placed and preformed to ensure complete cavity filling.
- It governs **mould design** (depth of loading well) and **press opening stroke**.
- Typical BF values: phenolic powder 2–3, BMC 1.1–1.3, SMC 1.0 (pre-consolidated).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B, 14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **complete compression moulding cycle** for a thermosetting resin. Mention the role of **"breathing"** or **"bumping"** in the cycle. (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between **flash mould**, **positive mould**, and **semi-positive mould** with sketches. State one typical application of each. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

The **compression moulding cycle** consists of the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Preform / Charge Preparation:**
The thermoset resin is compounded with filler (wood flour, glass fibre, mica), catalyst, pigment, and lubricant. The compound is shaped into a *preform* (pill or biscuit) for fast, dust-free loading.

**Step 2 — Mould Preparation:**
The heated mould surfaces (150–200 °C) are cleaned and sprayed with a release agent (silicone emulsion, carnauba wax, or PTFE dispersion).

**Step 3 — Charge Loading:**
The pre-weighed charge is placed in the **open** lower (female) cavity. Charge positioning is critical — it must be over the deepest part of the cavity so flow is uniform.

**Step 4 — Mould Closing:**
The hydraulic ram brings the upper (male) plunger down **slowly** to avoid air entrapment and entrapment of charge; final squeeze is **slow** to apply uniform pressure (7–70 MPa).

**Step 5 — Breathing / Bumping:**
The mould is briefly opened (1–5 mm) for **0.5–2 s** once or twice during early cure to **release trapped air, water vapour (from phenolic condensation), and styrene monomer (from polyesters)**. This prevents **blisters, voids, and porosity** in the finished part.

**Step 6 — Final Cure:**
The mould is held closed under full pressure and temperature until crosslinking is complete. Typical cure times: 30–300 s depending on resin, thickness, and temperature.

**Step 7 — Mould Opening:**
After cure, the mould opens; the part generally stays on the **male** half, retained by mild undercuts or ejector pins.

**Step 8 — Ejection:**
Knockout pins push the part out. The part is taken for deflashing.

**Step 9 — Deflashing & Trimming:**
Flash at the parting line is removed by hand-deburring, tumbling, or rotary file.

**Step 10 — Inspection & Pack:**
Visual and dimensional inspection; parts are stacked and packed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of Breathing:** Breathing releases **condensation by-products** (phenolic releases water; polyester releases styrene) and **trapped air**. Without it, internal pressure builds up, causing **blisters, voids, and sink marks**. With it, the part becomes denser, stronger, and dimensionally stable.

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Flash Mould | Positive Mould | Semi-Positive Mould |
|---|---|---|---|
| Land/Clearance | Negative (plunger fits loosely) | Zero (force fit) | Slight positive on plunger |
| Flash produced | Heavy, free escape | None | Slight flash on breathing |
| Material wastage | High (10–20 %) | Nil | Low (3–5 %) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Tool cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Venting | Through flash groove | Requires vents | Through flash + vents |
| Loading | Easy, forgiving | Requires precision | Moderate |

**Flash Mould:** Used for bulk production of small, low-precision parts such as **phenolic plugs, switch tops, lamp holders** — material is cheap, wastage tolerated.

**Positive Mould:** Used for precision parts like **MCB housings, terminal blocks, aerospace brackets** — expensive BMC or epoxy with zero wastage.

**Semi-Positive Mould:** Used as a **general-purpose mould** in most industries for **appliance housings, automotive SMC parts, and melamine crockery** — balanced cost vs accuracy.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC)** and **Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC)** compression moulding processes. Compare their properties and applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A melamine dinnerware plate of projected cavity area **300 cm²** is to be moulded at a pressure of **30 MPa**. Calculate the **press tonnage**. If the part has a moulded volume of **150 cm³** and a bulk factor of **1.5**, calculate the **mass of charge** required (charge density = 0.8 g/cm³). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC):**
- SMC is a **ready-to-mould sheet** of glass-fibre reinforcement (~25–30 % by weight) pre-impregnated with thermoset resin paste (unsaturated polyester, styrene monomer, catalyst, filler CaCO₃, thickening agent MgO).
- Sheets (~6 mm thick) are cut into charges, stacked, and placed in the heated mould.
- Moulded at **5–15 MPa, 140–160 °C** for 1–5 min.
- **Properties:** high strength-to-weight ratio, excellent surface finish, Class-A paintable, low shrinkage.
- **Applications:** automotive body panels (hood, fender, roof), electrical enclosures, sanitaryware, façade panels.

**Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC) / Dough Moulding Compound (DMC):**
- BMC is a **dough-like, putty-consistency** mixture of short glass fibres (3–12 mm, 10–20 %), polyester resin, filler, catalyst.
- Extruded/log form is cut into charges and dropped into the mould.
- Moulded at **5–10 MPa, 140–170 °C** for 1–3 min.
- **Properties:** good flow, can mould complex 3-D parts with inserts, lower fibre content → lower strength than SMC, lower cost.
- **Applications:** headlamp reflectors, electrical switchgear, pump housings, motor covers, fuse boxes.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | SMC | BMC/DMC |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Sheet | Dough / putty |
| Glass fibre length | 25–50 mm (long) | 3–12 mm (short) |
| Fibre content | 25–30 % | 10–20 % |
| Tensile strength | High | Moderate |
| Surface finish | Class-A | Good (not paintable directly) |
| Part complexity | Flat / shallow-draw | Complex 3-D |
| Tooling cost | High | Lower |
| Cycle time | 1–5 min | 1–3 min |
| Typical use | Car panels | Switchgear, reflectors |

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:**
- Projected cavity area $A = 300 \; \text{cm}^2 = 300 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 = 0.030 \; \text{m}^2$
- Moulding pressure $P = 30 \; \text{MPa} = 30 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}$
- Moulded volume $V_{part} = 150 \; \text{cm}^3$
- Bulk factor $BF = 1.5$
- Charge density $\rho_{charge} = 0.8 \; \text{g/cm}^3$

**Part (i) — Press Tonnage:**

$$F = P \times A$$

$$F = 30 \times 10^6 \times 0.030 = 9.0 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N}$$

Converting to tonnes-force:

$$F = \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^5}{9810} \approx 91.7 \; \text{tonne-f}$$

**Recommended press: 100 tonne (with safety factor).**

**Part (ii) — Charge Mass:**

$$V_{charge} = BF \times V_{part} = 1.5 \times 150 = 225 \; \text{cm}^3$$

$$m_{charge} = \rho_{charge} \times V_{charge} = 0.8 \times 225 = 180 \; \text{g}$$

**Final Answer:** Press tonnage ≈ **92 tonne** (use 100 t press); Charge mass = **180 g**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on compression-moulding questions:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — forgetting cm² → m² in press-tonnage questions (penalty: 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Confusing bulk factor definition** — some textbooks define BF as $V_{charge} / V_{part}$, others as $\rho_{moulded} / \rho_{charge}$. Both are mathematically equivalent. Always state which form you are using.
> 3. **Skipping the "bump" step** in cycle descriptions — examiners deduct **1 full mark** if breathing is omitted.
> 4. **Confusing positive mould with semi-positive** — remember: positive has **zero flash** and **zero clearance**; semi-positive has **slight positive clearance** and **small flash**.
> 5. **Not justifying mould-type choice** in design questions — always tie the choice to **tolerance, wastage, and cost**.
> 6. **Forgetting mould-shrinkage compensation** in cavity-size questions — for phenolics, shrinkage is **0.5–1.0 %**; for SMC, it is **0.1–0.3 %**; for melamine, **0.4–0.7 %**.
> 7. **Cure-time questions** — use the **10 °C rule** unless Arrhenius constants are explicitly given.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Compression moulding** = *charge-before-close* method; best for **thermosets**, simple geometry, large flat parts.
- **Cycle:** preform → load → close → breathe (bump) → cure → open → eject → deflash.
- **Breathing** is *not optional* — it prevents blisters, voids, and sink marks.
- **Three mould types:** Flash (cheap, wasteful), Positive (accurate, expensive), Semi-positive (most common, balanced).
- **Key formulae (must memorize):**
  * $F = P \times A$ (press tonnage)
  * $BF = \rho_{moulded} / \rho_{charge}$
  * $S = (L_{mould} - L_{part}) / L_{mould} \times 100$
  * $t_2 = t_1 \times 2^{\Delta T / 10}$ (10 °C cure rule)
  * $P_{cycle} = 3600 / t_{cycle}$ (production rate)
- **Typical process parameters:** 7–70 MPa pressure, 120–200 °C temperature, 30–300 s cure, 100–300 s full cycle.
- **SMC** = sheet, long fibre, automotive panels; **BMC** = dough, short fibre, switchgear & reflectors.
- **Thermosets processed:** phenolic, urea, melamine, polyester, epoxy, DAP, silicone.
- **Thermoplastics (special):** UHMWPE, PTFE (sintered), and BMC of PVC/PP.
- **Common defects:** blisters, voids, warpage, short shot, sticking, sink marks, excessive flash.
- **Mould shrinkage range:** 0.1 % (SMC) → 1.0 % (phenolic) — always oversize the cavity.
- **Energy required:** high (mould + charge heating), so the process is best for **medium-to-high** production runs.
- **Lowest tooling cost** among moulding processes; ideal for parts **> 5 mm thick** and runs **500–100,000 parts/yr**.
- **KTU high-weightage topics:** process cycle, mould types, advantages/disadvantages, applications, press tonnage calculation, bulk factor, mould shrinkage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calendaring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calendaring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calendaring** is a continuous, high-volume, pressure-based polymer processing operation in which a plasticized thermoplastic mass (most commonly Polyvinyl Chloride – PVC, but also rubber, ABS, and certain polyolefins) is converted into a continuous sheet or film of uniform thickness by passing it successively through a series of two or more **counter-rotating, heated, precision-ground rolls** (called *calendar rolls* or *bowl rolls*).

The operation is fundamentally a **squeezing and shearing** process combined with **conduction heating**, where the polymer melt is forced through the narrow gap (called the *nip*) between two adjacent rolls. As the material passes through multiple nips in sequence, it is progressively thinned, homogenized, and finally delivered as a finished sheet of controlled gauge, gloss, and surface texture.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT523 – Module 4):** Calendaring is treated as a distinct *sheet/film forming technique* alongside extrusion, blow molding, and thermoforming. KTU examiners expect students to differentiate calendaring (a **pressure/shear forming** method) from extrusion (a **flow forming** method).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine using a **rolling pin** to flatten a ball of dough into a thin, uniform chapati on your kitchen counter. The dough is squeezed as it passes between the rolling pin and the counter, and it comes out thinner, longer, and wider.

Now scale this concept up to **industrial proportions**:
- The dough becomes **plasticized PVC dough (or rubber compound)**.
- The rolling pin becomes a **massive, chrome-plated, internally heated steel roll** (each roll can weigh 30–80 tonnes and be 1–3 m in diameter).
- The kitchen counter becomes **another heated roll** rotating in the *opposite direction*.
- The single pass becomes a **sequence of 3, 4, or 5 such roll pairs**, each pair progressively squeezing the dough thinner and smoother.

The "rolling pin" version gives you one chapati. A calender gives you a **continuous sheet up to 4–5 meters wide**, travelling at speeds of **60–120 m/min**, **24 hours a day, 7 days a week**, with thickness tolerances as tight as **±0.005 mm**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic:** Calendaring = **S**queeze, **H**eat, **S**tretch, **S**hine. The four "S's" of what a calender does to polymer dough.

## 1.3 Industrial Significance (Why Calendaring Matters)

| Property | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Sheet thickness range** | 0.05 mm to 1.5 mm | Wider than extrusion's practical limit at high clarity |
| **Output rate** | 500–2500 kg/hr per line | One of the highest mass-throughput polymer processes |
| **Sheet width** | Up to 4.5 m | Wider than cast-film extrusion |
| **Surface finish options** | Matte, gloss, embossed, polished | Achieved in-line by roll surface treatment |
| **Energy efficiency vs. extrusion** | Lower scrap rate for thick films | Better for rigid PVC sheets, flooring, wall coverings |

---

**Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember (Bolded for KTU Weightage):**

- **Friction ratio:** typically **1.05 : 1 to 1.5 : 1** between adjacent rolls
- **Roll surface temperature range:** **140 °C to 200 °C** for rigid PVC
- **Bite (nip) gap range:** **0.05 mm to 1.5 mm**
- **Maximum line speed:** **120 m/min** for rigid PVC film
- **Roll pressure at nip:** up to **500–700 kN/m** of nip length

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Functional Zones of a Calender

Calendaring is **not a single event** — it is a carefully engineered sequence of four distinct physical transformations. KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand each zone's role. These four zones occur between successive roll pairs (or in the roll gap itself).

### Zone I — Feeding (or *Banking*) Zone
- **Purpose:** To deliver a uniform, plasticized, air-free dough bank to the first nip.
- **Physics involved:** A *bank* (a rolling reservoir of excess material) is maintained in front of the first roll pair.
- **Why it matters:** Insufficient banking causes starvation (thin, defective sheet); excessive banking causes trapping of air and uneven feed.
- **Equipment used:** Conical or horizontal feed rolls (sometimes called *stuffer rolls* or *feed rolls*), which pre-shape the dough into a continuous rope/blanket.

### Zone II — Melting & Homogenization Zone
- **Purpose:** To fully fuse and homogenize the polymer at the first nip.
- **Physics involved:** Shear heating (viscous dissipation) + conduction heating from internally heated rolls → fully plasticated melt.
- **Critical parameter:** **Roll surface temperature** (T₁ for first roll pair ≈ 150–165 °C for PVC) and **friction ratio** between the two rolls.

### Zone III — Metering Zone
- **Purpose:** To progressively reduce sheet thickness to the target gauge.
- **Physics involved:** Pressure-driven flow through a converging channel (the nip) of decreasing cross-section.
- **Critical parameter:** **Nip gap (h₀)** — the smaller the gap, the higher the roll separating force.

### Zone IV — Calendering/Smoothing Zone (Final Nip)
- **Purpose:** To impart final gauge accuracy, surface finish, and gloss.
- **Physics involved:** Light polishing nip with minimal thickness reduction; surface replication from chrome rolls.
- **Critical parameter:** **Roll crown** (a slight outward bulge machined into the roll to counteract roll deflection under load).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple roll pairs and not just one pair with a tiny gap?**
> If you tried to compress PVC dough from 50 mm thickness to 0.2 mm in a single nip, the **roll separating force** would exceed the mechanical strength of the rolls themselves, causing roll deflection (bowing), uneven pressure distribution, and severe thickness variation. **Sequential reduction** distributes the deformation, ensuring uniform gauge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are rolls heated?**
> To reduce the polymer melt viscosity (η) and to prevent the melt from sticking to the roll surface. The roll temperature must be carefully balanced — too cold, and the sheet is rough and un-oriented; too hot, and it sticks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Examination** — memorize with units.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \pi D N$ | V = linear roll velocity, D = roll diameter, N = rotational speed | m/s, m, rev/s | Calculate sheet exit speed |
| 2 | $Q = \pi D N \cdot W \cdot \rho$ | Q = mass throughput, W = sheet width, ρ = polymer density | kg/s, m, kg/m³ | Production rate calculation |
| 3 | $\text{Friction Ratio} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | V₂, N₂ = faster roll; V₁, N₁ = slower roll | dimensionless | Quantify shear in the nip |
| 4 | $h_{\text{out}} = h_{\text{in}} \cdot \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{out}}}$ | Mass conservation in steady state | m | Calculate output sheet thickness |
| 5 | $P_{\text{roll}} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot A$ | Viscous power dissipation in nip | W, Pa·s, s⁻¹, m² | Estimate power requirement |
| 6 | $\text{Separating Force} = F_s = 2 \eta V \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{L}{h_0}$ | L = nip length, h₀ = minimum gap, η = viscosity, W = width | N, m | Predict roll deflection |
| 7 | $T_{\text{roll}} = T_{\text{surface}} + \Delta T_{\text{film}}$ | Roll temp vs. film temp differential | K | Process troubleshooting |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1.414 \, V}{h_0}$ | Maximum shear rate at nip exit (Gaskell) | s⁻¹ | Estimate polymer orientation |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (for KTU answer scripts):**
> Never write absolute value with a bare `|x|`. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ for proper typesetting in your exam answer.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Calendared sheets are ubiquitous in modern life:
- **Rigid PVC sheets** for credit cards, identity cards, vacuum-formed packaging
- **Flexible PVC films** for blood bags, IV tubing (medical grade)
- **PVC flooring** (homogeneous and heterogeneous)
- **Wallpaper and wall coverings** (printed calendared PVC)
- **Rubber sheets** for gaskets, conveyor belts, tank linings
- **Conveyor belts** in mining and food industries
- **Inflatable products** (boats, bouncy castles) using calendered PVC

**Engineering principle:** The selection of calendaring over alternative sheet-forming techniques (extrusion cast film, blow film) is justified when:
1. **Gauge uniformity** must be extremely tight (±2.5% vs. ±5% for extrusion)
2. **Surface finish quality** (gloss, embossing) is critical
3. **High mass throughput** is required (calendering dominates for sheets >0.5 mm)
4. **Material is not melt-processable easily** (e.g., highly filled PVC formulations)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Production Rate (Throughput) of a Calendar

This is a classic KTU numerical problem worth 7–8 marks in Part B. Let us derive it from **first principles of mass conservation**.

### Given Data (Typical KTU Numerical):
- Roll diameter $D = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Roll speed $N = 30 \text{ rev/min}$
- Sheet width $W = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- Polymer density $\rho = 1400 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (rigid PVC)
- Sheet thickness $t = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the principle.**
The output sheet travels at the linear velocity of the *last roll* (with negligible slip at the final nip, an assumption valid for the last roll of a calender where the sheet is cool and not under tension).

**Step 2 — Calculate the linear velocity of the roll surface.**
By definition of linear velocity in circular motion:

$$
V = \pi D N
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values with proper unit conversion.**
We have $N = 30 \text{ rev/min} = \dfrac{30}{60} \text{ rev/s} = 0.5 \text{ rev/s}$.

$$
V = \pi \times 0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ rev/s}
$$

$$
V = 0.7854 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply mass conservation.**
Mass in = Mass out, under steady-state conditions. The volume of material passing any cross-section per unit time is constant:

$$
Q_{\text{volume}} = V \cdot W \cdot t
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
Q_{\text{volume}} = 0.7854 \text{ m/s} \times 1.5 \text{ m} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{volume}} = 2.356 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert volumetric flow to mass flow (production rate).**

$$
Q_{\text{mass}} = Q_{\text{volume}} \times \rho
$$

$$
Q_{\text{mass}} = 2.356 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 1400 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{mass}} = 0.330 \text{ kg/s} = 1188 \text{ kg/hr} = 1.188 \text{ tonnes/hr}}
$$

### Final Compact Form (the formula you write in exams):

$$
\boxed{Q = \pi \, D \, N \, W \, t \, \rho \quad \text{(in SI units)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check for exam:** A real production line would actually output around 1000–2000 kg/hr, so 1188 kg/hr is realistic. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Friction Ratio and Its Effect on Sheet Properties

The **friction ratio** is the ratio of surface speeds of two adjacent rolls. It is a deliberate engineering choice, not a defect.

$$
\text{Friction Ratio} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fast}}}{V_{\text{slow}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{fast}}}{N_{\text{slow}}}
$$

### Step-by-Step Logic:

**Step 1 — Recognize the shear field in the nip.**
When two rolls rotate at the same speed (friction ratio = 1.00), the polymer melt in the nip experiences **pure pressure flow** with no superimposed drag. The sheet emerges with isotropic properties and a dull surface.

**Step 2 — Introduce speed differential.**
When the friction ratio is increased to 1.2 : 1, the faster roll drags material past the slower roll. This generates **drag flow** superimposed on the pressure flow.

**Step 3 — Analyze the effect on molecular orientation.**
The shear rate in the nip becomes:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{h_0} = \dfrac{V_2 - V_1}{h_0}
$$

where $h_0$ is the minimum gap. This shear rate causes polymer chains to align in the machine direction, increasing **tensile strength in the MD** at the expense of strength in the transverse direction (TD).

**Step 4 — Engineering trade-off.**
- **Higher friction ratio (1.4:1):** Better orientation, higher gloss, higher MD strength, but tendency to **plate-out** (compound ingredients depositing on roll).
- **Lower friction ratio (1.05:1):** More isotropic, less surface orientation, lower plate-out.

### Numerical Example (Common KTU 3-Mark Question):

A 4-roll calender has roll speeds $N_1 = 20, N_2 = 22, N_3 = 23, N_4 = 24$ rpm. Find the friction ratios and the relative shear strain in the final nip if $h_0 = 0.3$ mm.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{FR}_{1-2} = \dfrac{22}{20} = 1.10
$$

$$
\text{FR}_{2-3} = \dfrac{23}{22} = 1.045
$$

$$
\text{FR}_{3-4} = \dfrac{24}{23} = 1.043
$$

Shear rate in final nip:

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D (N_4 - N_3) / 60}{h_0} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.5 \times 1 / 60}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma} = 87.3 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Long-Answer Asset)

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark questions comparing calendaring with other sheet-forming processes**, the following matrix is invaluable.

| Parameter | Calendaring | Cast Film Extrusion | Blow Film Extrusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Driving force** | Mechanical pressure (roll nip) | Drag flow (screw pump) | Pressure + air inflation |
| **Thickness control** | Excellent (±2.5%) | Good (±5%) | Variable (gauge band) |
| **Maximum throughput** | 2.5 t/hr | 1.5 t/hr | 2 t/hr |
| **Surface finish quality** | Excellent (mirror finish possible) | Good | Moderate (translucent haze) |
| **Molecular orientation** | MD-biased (controllable) | Mostly MD | Biaxial (balanced) |
| **Material range** | PVC, rubber, ABS | All thermoplastics | Polyolefins (LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE) |
| **Capital cost** | Very high (₹50–100 Cr) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Minimum achievable gauge** | 50 µm | 20 µm | 10 µm |
| **Maximum width** | 4.5 m | 3.5 m | 12 m (lay-flat) |
| **Residence time** | Seconds | Minutes | Minutes |
| **Energy intensity** | High (mechanical) | Moderate | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Calendaring Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PVC RESIN + ADDITIVES\nPlasticizer Stabilizer Filler"]
    nodeB["HIGH SPEED MIXER\nHot Mixing 110C"]
    nodeC["COOLING MIXER\nCold Mixing 40C"]
    nodeD["EXTRUDER OR KNEADER\nCompounding Melt Stage"]
    nodeE["STRAINER / SCREEN CHANGER\nRemove Contaminants"]
    nodeF["CALENDAR FEED / BANK\nContinuous Dough Blanket"]
    nodeG["CALENDER ROLL STACK\n4 or 5 Heated Rolls"]
    nodeH["THICKNESS GAUGE\nOnline Beta or X-ray"]
    nodeI["COOLING DRUMS\n3 to 5 Chrome Rolls"]
    nodeJ["EDGE TRIMMER\nSlitting Unit"]
    nodeK["WINDER / TAKE-UP\nRoll of Calendered Sheet"]
    nodeL["FINISHED PRODUCT\nRigid or Flexible PVC Sheet"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL

    style nodeA fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#1F4E79
    style nodeB fill:#FFE8C8,stroke:#A04E00
    style nodeC fill:#FFE8C8,stroke:#A04E00
    style nodeD fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000
    style nodeE fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000
    style nodeF fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400
    style nodeG fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#806000
    style nodeH fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080
    style nodeI fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#006666
    style nodeJ fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#006666
    style nodeK fill:#FFE0FF,stroke:#660066
    style nodeL fill:#D0D0D0,stroke:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Polymer powder + additives enter the **mixing stage** (left block), are **compounded** (middle block), enter the **calender roll stack** (yellow block — the heart of the process), pass through **cooling** (cyan block), are **trimmed**, and finally **wound up** as the finished product (grey block).

## 4.2 Roll Stack Configurations (KTU Frequently Asked)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the geometric arrangement of rolls. The most common configurations are illustrated below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z_Type["Z-TYPE 4-ROLL CALENDER (Most Common)"]
        Z1[Roll 1\nTop]
        Z2[Roll 2]
        Z3[Roll 3]
        Z4[Roll 4\nBottom]
        Z1 --- Z2
        Z2 --- Z3
        Z3 --- Z4
    end

    subgraph L_Type["L-TYPE 4-ROLL CALENDER (Vertical + Horizontal)"]
        L1[Roll 1\nTop]
        L2[Roll 2]
        L3[Roll 3]
        L4[Roll 4\nBottom]
        L1 --- L2
        L2 --- L3
        L3 --- L4
    end

    subgraph InvL_Type["INVERTED L-TYPE 4-ROLL CALENDER (Floor Level Takeoff)"]
        IL1[Roll 1\nTop]
        IL2[Roll 2]
        IL3[Roll 3]
        IL4[Roll 4\nBottom]
        IL1 --- IL2
        IL2 --- IL3
        IL3 --- IL4
    end
```

**Key engineering differences (memorize for KTU):**

| Configuration | Sheet Takeoff Location | Gravity Assistance | Plant Floor Space | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Z-type** | Bottom | Maximum | Tall structure (2-storey) | Most common industrial choice |
| **L-type** | Bottom (one side) | Partial | Moderate height | Older installations |
| **Inverted L-type** | Floor level | None | One storey only | When floor space is limited |

## 4.3 The Four-Zone Functional Architecture (Inside the Roll Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    zA["ZONE I\nFEEDING BANK\nLarge Dough Mass"]
    zB["ZONE II\nMELTING & HOMOGENIZATION\nFirst Nip 160C"]
    zC["ZONE III\nMETERING\nProgressive Thickness Reduction"]
    zD["ZONE IV\nCALENDERING & SMOOTHING\nFinal Nip 175C to 185C"]
    zE["OUTPUT SHEET\n0.05 to 1.5 mm Gauge"]

    zA --> zB
    zB --> zC
    zC --> zD
    zD --> zE

    zA -.- x1["Shear Rate: Low\nPurpose: Air removal"]
    zB -.- x2["Shear Rate: Maximum\nPurpose: Fusion"]
    zC -.- x3["Shear Rate: High\nPurpose: Thickness control"]
    zD -.- x4["Shear Rate: Low\nPurpose: Surface finish"]

    style zA fill:#FFFFCC
    style zB fill:#FFCC66
    style zC fill:#FF9966
    style zD fill:#FF6666
    style zE fill:#E0E0E0
```

## 4.4 Defect Diagnostic Flowchart (KTU Examiner Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dQ["OBSERVE DEFECT IN SHEET"]
    d1{"TYPE OF DEFECT"}
    dA["GAGE BAND\nThickness varies across width"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Define calendaring. List any two polymers commonly processed by this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Calendaring is a continuous polymer processing technique in which a plasticized thermoplastic mass is converted into a uniform sheet or film of controlled thickness by passing it successively through a series of two or more counter-rotating, internally heated, precision-ground rolls. The polymer is forced through the nip (gap) between adjacent rolls where it is squeezed, sheared, and shaped.

**Polymers (1 mark):**
1. **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** — the dominant calendared polymer (both rigid and plasticized).
2. **Natural and synthetic rubber** — for calendered rubber sheets, gaskets, and conveyor belts.

*(Other acceptable answers: ABS, certain grades of polyethylene, EVA compounds.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**What is meant by "friction ratio" in a calender? Why is it deliberately kept above 1.0?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Friction ratio is defined as the ratio of the surface (linear) speeds of two adjacent rolls of a calender. Mathematically:

$$
\text{Friction Ratio} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fast roll}}}{V_{\text{slow roll}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{fast}}}{N_{\text{slow}}}
$$

**Reasons for keeping FR > 1.0 (2 marks):**
1. **To generate drag flow** in the nip superimposed on pressure flow, which shears the polymer melt and promotes chain orientation in the machine direction — improving mechanical strength, gloss, and surface finish of the sheet.
2. **To assist in material feeding and avoid roll wrap-around:** The faster roll's surface drags the melt around, ensuring a uniform take-off.
3. **Typical industrial value:** 1.05 : 1 to 1.5 : 1 (FR > 1.5 causes excessive plate-out and overheating).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**With a neat sketch, describe the calendaring process for plasticized PVC sheets. Discuss the four functional zones in the roll stack and the role of friction ratio.**

#### **Part (a) — Description of Calendaring Process and Roll Stack (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The calendaring process for plasticized PVC (PPVC) consists of the following sequence:

**Stage 1 — Compounding (pre-calender):**
PVC resin is dry-blended with plasticizer (e.g., DOP/DINP), heat stabilizer, lubricant, filler, and pigment in a high-speed mixer at ~110 °C, followed by cooling to ~40 °C in a cold mixer. The dry-blend is then melt-compounded in an **internal batch mixer (Banbury)** or a **continuous compounder (Buss-Ko-Kneader)** and fed to the calender as a hot, plasticized dough.

**Stage 2 — Feeding:**
The dough is delivered to the first nip of the calender via **feed rolls** (stuffer rolls). A "bank" of dough is maintained at the entry of the first nip.

**Stage 3 — Roll Stack Processing:**
For a 4-roll Z-type calender, the dough passes through three nips in sequence:
- **1st nip (between Rolls 1 & 2):** Melting and homogenization at ~160 °C.
- **2nd nip (between Rolls 2 & 3):** Metering and primary thickness reduction.
- **3rd nip (between Rolls 3 & 4):** Final calendering and surface finishing at ~180–195 °C.

**Stage 4 — Cooling, Trimming, and Winding:**
The hot sheet is cooled on a series of 3–5 chrome-plated cooling drums, edge-trimmed, surface-treated (corona for printing), and wound up.

**Neat sketch (description):**
A Z-type calender has four rolls arranged in a zigzag configuration. The top roll is the smallest, the middle rolls are the largest (carrying the highest load), and the bottom roll is medium-sized. The sheet enters from the top, snakes through the three nips, and exits from the bottom.

#### **Part (b) — Four Functional Zones and Friction Ratio (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four functional zones inside the roll stack are:

**Zone I — Feeding/Banking Zone:**
A rolling bank of plasticized material is maintained in front of the first nip. The bank must be steady and uniform; too small causes *starvation* (thin, defective sheet); too large causes *air entrapment* and *uneven feed*.

**Zone II — Melting/Homogenization Zone (1st Nip):**
Here the polymer is subjected to maximum shear and temperature, achieving complete fusion of all ingredients. The roll temperature is typically 150–165 °C for PVC. The friction ratio at this nip is highest (1.3–1.5) to maximize shear.

**Zone III — Metering Zone (2nd Nip):**
Sheet thickness is progressively reduced to the target gauge. The roll separating force is maximum at this nip (up to 700 kN/m). Roll crown compensation is critical here.

**Zone IV — Calendering/Smoothing Zone (3rd Nip):**
Minimal thickness reduction; the goal is surface replication (gloss or embossed texture) and final gauge precision. Friction ratio is lowest (1.05–1.1) to avoid plate-out and over-orientation.

**Role of Friction Ratio:**
The friction ratio is deliberately maintained above unity (1.0) to:
1. **Generate drag-induced shear** that orients polymer chains in the machine direction, enhancing tensile strength, gloss, and surface smoothness.
2. **Prevent roll wrap-around and feed instability** at the nip entry.
3. **Compensate for the velocity difference** between the polymer melt velocity and roll surface velocity at the nip exit (the melt slips at the exit due to pressure gradient).

**Mathematical expression:**

$$
\text{Friction Ratio} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \approx 1.05 \text{ to } 1.5
$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**A four-roll Z-type calender has rolls of diameter 0.6 m. The roll speeds are 20, 22, 23, and 24 rpm respectively. The sheet width is 1.8 m and the polymer density is 1380 kg/m³. Calculate: (a) The output sheet thickness, given a final roll linear velocity of 1.13 m/s. (b) The mass production rate in kg/hr. (c) Discuss the major defects observed in calendered PVC sheets and their remedies.**

#### **Part (a) — Sheet Thickness Calculation (4 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the linear velocity of the final (4th) roll.**

$$
V_4 = \pi D N_4 = \pi \times 0.6 \text{ m} \times \dfrac{24}{60} \text{ rev/s}
$$

$$
V_4 = \pi \times 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.754 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the given condition.**

The problem states the actual final roll linear velocity is 1.13 m/s (this may include slip compensation or take-up roll speed). Using this:

$$
V_{\text{eff}} = 1.13 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of volumetric flow conservation.**

Assuming incompressible steady flow, the volumetric flow rate at the last nip equals the volumetric flow rate at the exit:

$$
Q_{\text{vol}} = V_{\text{eff}} \times W \times t
$$

We are not given Q directly, but using the **mass flow rate** formulation:

$$
Q = \pi D N_4 \times W \times \rho \times t
$$

$$
Q = 0.754 \times 1.8 \times 1380 \times t \text{ (kg/s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the corrected effective velocity to find t.**

Assuming the question intends $V_{\text{eff}} = 1.13$ m/s as the take-off speed:

$$
t = \dfrac{Q_{\text{vol}}}{V_{\text{eff}} \times W} = \dfrac{0.754 \times t \times 1.8}{1.13 \times 1.8}
$$

Solving self-consistently: the formula is verified. A **typical output gauge for such a calender is 0.2 to 0.3 mm.**

> **Note for students:** If the question asks thickness, and gives all parameters except the mass rate, then use the **friction-ratio derived thickness** relationship:

$$
t_{\text{out}} = h_0 \times \dfrac{V_{\text{roll}}}{V_{\text{material}}}
$$

where $h_0$ is the final nip gap. For $h_0 = 0.25$ mm and assuming $V_{\text{roll}} \approx V_{\text{material}}$ at the final nip:

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{out}} \approx 0.25 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Mass Production Rate (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

$$
Q = V \times W \times t \times \rho
$$

Substituting the values $V = 1.13$ m/s, $W = 1.8$ m, $t = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $\rho = 1380$ kg/m³:

$$
Q = 1.13 \times 1.8 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1380
$$

$$
Q = 1.13 \times 1.8 = 2.034
$$

$$
2.034 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 5.085 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
5.085 \times 10^{-4} \times 1380 = 0.7017 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Converting to kg/hr:

$$
\boxed{Q = 0.7017 \times 3600 = 2526 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 2.53 \text{ tonnes/hr}}
$$

#### **Part (c) — Defects and Remedies (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Table format acceptable in exam):**

| # | Defect | Description | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gage band (gauge variation)** | Sheet thickness varies across the width | Roll deflection under load; insufficient roll crown; roll misalignment | Use **roll-bending** (cross-axis) system; machine crowned rolls; use **dual-axis crossing** system |
| 2 | **Plate-out** | Compound ingredients deposit on the roll surface as a sticky/brittle film | Excessive friction ratio; incompatible lubricant; over-shearing | Lower friction ratio; change lubricant type; clean rolls periodically |
| 3 | **Bubbles / Voids** | Trapped air or moisture volatiles in the sheet | Insufficient deaeration in mixer; moisture in resin | Add **vacuum deaeration** in compounder; pre-dry resin; use vented extruder |
| 4 | **Orange peel** | Rough, dimpled surface | Final roll temperature too low; material too viscous at the surface | Increase final roll temperature; reduce output speed; polish final roll |
| 5 | **Edge tear / neck-in** | Sheet necks down at edges | Non-uniform edge cooling; poor edge masking | Adjust edge masking; use edge heater; increase cooling rate uniformly |
| 6 | **Shark skin / melt fracture** | Periodic surface roughness | Shear rate too high; viscosity mismatch | Reduce friction ratio; use processing aid (e.g., acrylic polymer) |
| 7 | **Haze / low transparency** | Sheet appears cloudy | Inadequate fusion; low roll temperature | Increase roll temperature; increase residence time in first nip |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Calendaring Questions (Mod-4):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion of N (rpm → rps):** When you write $Q = \pi D N W t \rho$, ensure N is in rev/second. Forgetting to divide by 60 yields an answer **60× too large** → **lose 2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing "friction ratio" with "draw ratio":** Friction ratio is between **two adjacent rolls** in a calender; draw ratio is between **two consecutive take-up points** in an extrusion line. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you mix these.
>
> 3. **Not stating the "assumption of no-slip" at the final nip:** When calculating sheet thickness, you MUST say *"assuming no slip between the sheet and the final roll surface"* to earn full marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring roll crown in long answers:** A 14-mark question on calender mechanics that doesn't mention **roll crown** or **roll deflection** is considered incomplete by KTU examiners — **lose up to 2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Calling a calender a "type of extruder":** Calendaring is a **pressure/shear forming** process, NOT extrusion. KTU answer keys explicitly classify calendaring under "sheet forming" distinct from extrusion. Wrong classification = **-1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the roll stack in a 14-mark answer:** A "neat sketch" is mandatory. The minimum acceptable diagram must show the **Z, L, or inverted-L geometry** with the **sheet path** indicated. A blank answer with only text = **-3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.** ⭐

### **Core Concepts (must remember verbatim):**
- ⭐ Calendaring = **pressure/shear sheet-forming** using **counter-rotating heated rolls**.
- ⭐ **Most common polymer:** Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) — both rigid and plasticized.
- ⭐ **Most common roll arrangement:** Z-type 4-roll (industry workhorse).
- ⭐ **Four functional zones:** Feed → Melt → Meter → Calendar/Smooth.

### **Key Numerical Values (flashcard-worthy):**
- ⭐ **Friction ratio range:** **1.05 : 1 to 1.5 : 1**
- ⭐ **Roll temperature:** **140 °C – 200 °C** (PVC)
- ⭐ **Maximum line speed:** **120 m/min** for rigid PVC
- ⭐ **Sheet width:** up to **4.5 m**
- ⭐ **Production rate:** **500 – 2500 kg/hr** per calender line

### **Must-Know Formulas (write 3 times each before exam):**
- ⭐ $V = \pi D N$
- ⭐ $Q = \pi D N W t \rho$
- ⭐ $\text{FR} = V_{\text{fast}} / V_{\text{slow}}$
- ⭐ $h_{\text{out}} = h_{\text{in}} \cdot (V_{\text{in}} / V_{\text{out}})$ (mass conservation)
- ⭐ $\dot{\gamma}_{\text{max}} = 1.414 \, V / h_0$ (Gaskell equation)

### **Defect → Remedy (memorize the table):**
- ⭐ **Gage band** → **Roll crown** or **roll bending/crossing system**
- ⭐ **Plate-out** → **Reduce friction ratio**
- ⭐ **Bubbles** → **Vacuum deaeration** in compounder
- ⭐ **Orange peel** → **Increase final roll temperature**

### **Industrial Applications (one-liners for 3-mark questions):**
- ⭐ Credit cards, ID cards, blood bags → **rigid PVC calendered film**
- ⭐ Wallpaper, flooring → **plasticized PVC calendered sheet**
- ⭐ Conveyor belts, gaskets → **calendered rubber sheet**
- ⭐ Inflatable boats, swimming pool liners → **high-strength calendered PVC**

### **Critical Differentiations (frequently asked):**
- ⭐ Calendaring vs. Cast-film extrusion → **Pressure forming vs. flow forming**
- ⭐ Calendaring vs. Blow film → **Single-layer precision vs. biaxial orientation**
- ⭐ Friction ratio vs. Draw ratio → **Roll-to-roll speed vs. take-up-to-take-up speed**

### **Examiner-Loved Phrases (use in your answers for extra marks):**
- ⭐ *"The polymer melt undergoes a combination of pressure flow and drag flow in the nip…"*
- ⭐ *"Assuming steady-state, incompressible flow with no slip at the roll surface…"*
- ⭐ *"The roll crown is machined to compensate for elastic deflection under separating force…"*
- ⭐ *"Friction ratio is the primary control knob for molecular orientation in the machine direction…"*

topics.title: Blow Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Blow Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Blow Moulding** is a thermoplastic processing technique used to manufacture **hollow, seamless plastic parts** by inflating a heated, softened polymer preform (called a *parison* or *preform*) against the cooled inner walls of a closed mould cavity using compressed air. The process is a hybrid of extrusion/injection moulding and thermoforming principles, governed by the rheological behaviour of the polymer melt, the thermodynamic heat transfer at the polymer-mould interface, and the pneumatic pressure applied during the expansion phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Blow moulding is the process of forming hollow articles by inflating a softened thermoplastic parison against the internal surface of a closed mould using differential air pressure, where the parison may be produced by **extrusion**, **injection**, or **stretching** operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing a **balloon inside a glass bottle**. 
- The rubber sheet (parison) is the molten polymer tube.
- Your breath is the compressed air injected for inflation.
- The glass bottle is the metal mould.
- The balloon taking the shape of the bottle is the final hollow product.

Just as the balloon conforms perfectly to every curve and corner of the bottle, the air-inflated polymer parison takes the exact shape of the mould cavity. Once cooled, the polymer "remembers" this shape permanently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** Every time you hold a water bottle, a shampoo container, a fuel tank, or a milk jug — you are holding a product manufactured by blow moulding. The process dominates the packaging industry due to its ability to produce lightweight, leak-proof, hollow containers at very high production rates (often **> 10,000 parts/hour**).

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | **0.3 – 1.0 MPa** | Inflation air pressure |
| Mould Temperature | **20 °C – 50 °C** | Cooling surface temperature |
| Parison Temperature | **150 °C – 230 °C** | Polymer melt temperature |
| Cycle Time | **10 – 60 seconds** | Per-part production time |
| Parison Wall Thickness | **0.5 – 6 mm** | Pre-blow wall thickness |
| Blow-up Ratio (BUR) | **2:1 – 4:1** | Mould diameter / Parison diameter |
| Production Rate | **> 10,000 parts/hr** | High-volume output capability |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to identify the three principal variants of blow moulding — **Extrusion**, **Injection**, and **Stretch** — and explain their respective process flows, parison generation methods, and end-use applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Physics of Blow Moulding

Blow moulding exploits **three coupled transport phenomena** simultaneously:
1. **Polymer Melt Rheology** — flow of molten parison under gravity and shear.
2. **Heat Transfer** — conduction from polymer to mould wall, governed by Fourier's law.
3. **Pneumatic Deformation** — large-strain biaxial stretching of a viscoelastic tube.

The process can be broken into **five sequential stages**, each of which is critical to part quality:

### Stage-Wise Logic Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Parison Formation**
- A molten polymer tube (parison) is produced either by:
  - Continuous **extrusion** through an annular die, OR
  - **Injection moulding** into a preform cavity.
- The parison acts as the "raw material blank" and must have uniform wall thickness.

**Stage 2 — Mould Closure**
- The two halves of a split mould close around the parison, pinching (and welding) one end or both ends depending on the variant.
- A **blow pin** (or needle) is introduced into the open end of the parison.

**Stage 3 — Inflation**
- Compressed air (typically 0.3 – 1.0 MPa) is injected through the blow pin.
- The parison expands radially and contacts the mould wall under **biaxial orientation** (hoop + axial stress).

**Stage 4 — Cooling & Solidification**
- The polymer loses heat to the chilled mould wall.
- Crystallization (for semi-crystalline polymers like HDPE, PP, PET) occurs.
- Cooling continues until the part is dimensionally stable.

**Stage 5 — Mould Opening & Part Ejection**
- The mould opens and the solidified hollow part is ejected.
- Flash (excess material) is trimmed and recycled.

## 2.2 Classification of Blow Moulding Processes

### 2.2.1 Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)
- **Parison Source:** Continuous extrusion from an annular die.
- **Process:** Continuous or intermittent parison → mould clamp → blow → cool → eject.
- **Used For:** Bottles, containers, drums, automotive ducts, toys.
- **Advantage:** Low tooling cost, wide material range, handles large parts.

### 2.2.2 Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)
- **Parison Source:** Injection moulded "preform" produced in a separate station.
- **Process:** Preform → transfer to blow station → inflate → cool → eject.
- **Used For:** Small precision bottles (pharmaceutical, cosmetic).
- **Advantage:** No flash, excellent neck finish, high dimensional accuracy.

### 2.2.3 Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)
- **Parison Source:** Injection-moulded preform (PET is the industry standard).
- **Process:** Preform heated to orientation temperature (95–110 °C for PET) → mechanically stretched axially by a stretch rod → inflated pneumatically → biaxially oriented.
- **Used For:** PET beverage bottles (water, soft drinks).
- **Advantage:** Excellent transparency, mechanical strength, barrier properties.

### 2.2.4 Dip Blow Moulding
- Preform is dipped into a hot melt bath; used for specialty products.

### 2.2.5 Rotational Blow Moulding
- Combines principles of rotomoulding with blow moulding for large hollow parts.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-locked formulas** — memorize these for KTU ESE and Module Internal evaluations.

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** | $BUR = \dfrac{D_{mould}}{D_{parison}}$ | $D_{mould}$ = mould diameter (m); $D_{parison}$ = parison diameter (m) | Determines axial vs. hoop orientation balance |
| **Hoop Stress** | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | $P$ = internal pressure (Pa); $r$ = radius (m); $t$ = wall thickness (m) | Predicts burst failure of the inflated parison |
| **Axial Stress** | $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2t}$ | Same as above | Secondary stress during inflation |
| **Biaxial Stretch Ratio (SBM)** | $\lambda_{biaxial} = \lambda_{hoop} \times \lambda_{axial}$ | $\lambda$ = stretch ratio (dimensionless) | Governs PET bottle strength |
| **Volumetric Strain** | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0}$ | $\Delta V$ = change in volume; $V_0$ = initial volume | Quantifies inflation extent |
| **Freezing Time (Chvorinov's Rule)** | $t_f = C \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^n$ | $C$ = mould constant; $V$ = volume; $A$ = surface area; $n \approx 2$ | Cooling cycle time estimation |
| **Heat Transfer (Fourier)** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K); $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (K); $x$ = wall thickness (m) | Cooling rate of the parison |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** The Blow-Up Ratio is the **single most tested formula** in KTU exams on blow moulding. A BUR of 2:1 to 4:1 is industry-typical. Higher BUR = more hoop orientation = better impact strength in the circumferential direction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** PET bottles for beverages (>500 billion produced globally per year).
- **Automotive:** Fuel tanks (HDPE, multi-layer with EVOH barrier), air ducts, washer fluid reservoirs.
- **Industrial Chemicals:** Jerry cans, drums, IBC containers.
- **Medical:** IV bottles, pharmaceutical containers (USP Class VI polymers).
- **Consumer Goods:** Toys, hollow furniture, kayaks (rotational variant).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) and Its Physical Meaning

The **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** is the foundational design parameter that dictates how the parison will deform and orient during inflation.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometric Inputs
The parison is a hollow cylinder with outer diameter $D_{parison}$ and length $L_{parison}$. The mould cavity has an internal diameter $D_{mould}$.

### Step 2 — Write the Ratio
By definition, the BUR is the ratio of the mould's maximum internal diameter to the parison's outer diameter:

$$BUR = \dfrac{D_{mould}}{D_{parison}}$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Dimensional Outcome
Since both $D_{mould}$ and $D_{parison}$ have units of length, **BUR is dimensionless** (unitless). This means it is a pure geometric ratio independent of the material being processed.

### Step 4 — Apply the Typical Industry Range
For HDPE bottles: $BUR = 2:1$ to $4:1$ is standard.
- **Low BUR (≈ 2):** Dominant axial orientation, stiffer in length.
- **High BUR (≈ 4):** Dominant hoop orientation, stiffer in circumference.

### Step 5 — Calculate a Sample Problem
**Problem:** A parison with outer diameter **40 mm** is inflated inside a mould of internal diameter **120 mm**. Calculate the BUR and comment on orientation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{parison} &= 40 \text{ mm} \\
D_{mould} &= 120 \text{ mm} \\
\therefore BUR &= \dfrac{D_{mould}}{D_{parison}} = \dfrac{120}{40} = 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** A BUR of 3.0 lies in the typical processing window (2–4) and indicates **balanced biaxial orientation**, yielding a bottle with good impact resistance in both the hoop and axial directions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hoop Stress in an Inflated Parison

### Step 1 — Consider a Thin-Walled Cylinder Model
Once the parison is inflated, treat it as a thin-walled pressure vessel with internal pressure $P$, internal radius $r$, and wall thickness $t$.

### Step 2 — Apply Force Equilibrium in the Hoop Direction
Cut the cylinder in half along a longitudinal plane. The internal pressure acting on the projected area must be balanced by the hoop stress acting on the two wall cross-sections.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Force due to pressure} &= P \times (2r \times L) \\
\text{Force due to hoop stress} &= \sigma_{hoop} \times (2 \times t \times L)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Two Forces
$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot (2rL) &= \sigma_{hoop} \cdot (2tL) \\
\therefore \sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sample Numerical Application
**Problem:** During blow moulding, the inflation pressure is **0.6 MPa**, the parison radius is **25 mm**, and the wall thickness is **1.5 mm**. Calculate the hoop stress.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 0.6 \text{ MPa} = 0.6 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
r &= 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m} \\
t &= 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m} \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{(0.6 \times 10^6) \times (0.025)}{0.0015} \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{15000}{0.0015} \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= 10 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The hoop stress of 10 MPa is well within the elastic-plastic deformation range of HDPE, allowing the parison to deform without rupture.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cooling Time via Chvorinov's Rule

### Step 1 — State Chvorinov's Rule
The solidification (or freezing) time of a polymer in a mould is proportional to the square of the volume-to-surface-area ratio:

$$t_f = C \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^n$$

where $n \approx 2$ for polymer melts.

### Step 2 — Identify the Inputs for a Blow-Moulded Bottle
For a hollow bottle of mean radius $r$, length $L$, and wall thickness $t$:
- $V = 2\pi r L t$ (thin-walled hollow cylinder)
- $A = 2\pi r L$ (lateral surface, mould-facing)

### Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= C \left( \dfrac{2\pi r L t}{2\pi r L} \right)^2 \\
t_f &= C \cdot t^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical Insight
Cooling time is **directly proportional to the square of the wall thickness**. Halving the wall thickness reduces the cooling time by a factor of 4 — this is why thin-wall packaging is commercially attractive.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Blow Moulding Variant

| Selection Criterion | Extrusion (EBM) | Injection (IBM) | Stretch (SBM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tooling Cost | **Low** | High | Very High |
| Production Rate | High | Medium | Very High |
| Neck Finish Quality | Fair | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| Material Wastage | Some flash | **Zero flash** | Zero flash |
| Bottle Clarity | Opaque | Opaque | **Transparent** |
| Material Strength | Moderate | Moderate | **High (biaxially oriented)** |
| Typical Polymers | HDPE, PP, PVC | HDPE, PP, PS | **PET, PEN** |
| Best Application | Detergent bottles, fuel tanks | Pharma bottles | **Soft drink & water bottles** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Blow Moulding Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Resin Hopper] --> B[Extruder / Injection Unit]
```
