```mermaid
    B --> C[Parison / Preform Formation]
    C --> D[Mould Closure Around Parison]
    D --> E[Compressed Air Injection via Blow Pin]
    E --> F[Parison Inflation Against Mould Wall]
    F --> G[Cooling and Solidification]
    G --> H[Mould Opening]
    H --> I[Part Ejection and Flash Trimming]
    I --> J[Recycled Flash to Hopper]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Three Blow Moulding Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EBM [Extrusion Blow Moulding]
        E1[Resin] --> E2[Continuous Extrusion]
        E2 --> E3[Annular Die Forms Parison]
        E3 --> E4[Mould Clamp on Parison]
        E4 --> E5[Air Blow and Cool]
        E5 --> E6[Eject Hollow Part]
    end
    subgraph IBM [Injection Blow Moulding]
        I1[Resin] --> I2[Injection into Preform Mould]
        I2 --> I3[Transfer Preform to Blow Mould]
        I3 --> I4[Air Inflation]
        I4 --> I5[Cool and Eject]
    end
    subgraph SBM [Stretch Blow Moulding]
        S1[Resin] --> S2[Injection Moulded Preform]
        S2 --> S3[Reheat to Orientation Temperature]
        S3 --> S4[Axial Stretch by Rod]
        S4 --> S5[Hoop Inflation by Air]
        S5 --> S6[Biaxially Oriented Bottle]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Blow Moulding Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ControlUnit [Central Control Unit]
        CU1[PLC Controller]
        CU2[Pressure Regulator]
        CU3[Temperature Controller]
    end
    subgraph ProcessStation [Process Hardware]
        PH1[Hopper and Feeder]
        PH2[Plasticizing Screw and Barrel]
        PH3[Accumulator Head or Die]
        PH4[Mould Clamp Assembly]
        PH5[Blow Pin and Air Line]
        PH6[Chiller and Cooling Circuit]
    end
    subgraph OutputStation [Output Handling]
        OS1[Part Ejection Arm]
        OS2[Flash Trimming Station]
        OS3[Recycle Granulator]
    end
    CU1 --> PH1
    CU1 --> PH4
    CU2 --> PH5
    CU3 --> PH2
    PH1 --> PH2 --> PH3 --> PH4
    PH5 --> PH4
    PH6 --> PH4
    PH4 --> OS1 --> OS2 --> OS3
    OS3 --> PH1
```

## 4.4 Process Parameter Influence Map (Schematic Description)

The following functional flow captures how the major process inputs interact to determine part quality:

1. **Melt Temperature** → affects parison sag and orientation uniformity.
2. **Inflation Pressure** → determines wall thickness distribution and BUR achievement.
3. **Mould Temperature** → governs surface finish, crystallinity, and cycle time.
4. **Cooling Time** → controlled via Chvorinov's rule; thicker walls = longer cycles.
5. **Parison Programming** → adjusts die gap or wall thickness along the length to compensate for sag.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define blow moulding. List any two polymers commonly processed by this technique.*

**Model Answer:**
Blow moulding is a thermoplastic manufacturing process in which a heated, softened polymer parison (or preform) is inflated by compressed air against the internal surface of a closed mould to produce a hollow, seamless part.
**Two commonly processed polymers:**
1. **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** — used for milk bottles, detergent containers, fuel tanks.
2. **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** — used for soft drink and water bottles (via stretch blow moulding).
*(Any two relevant polymers accepted.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *What is a parison? How is it generated in extrusion blow moulding?*

**Model Answer:**
A **parison** is a molten, hollow tube of thermoplastic polymer that serves as the starting "blank" for blow moulding.
In **extrusion blow moulding**, the parison is generated by continuously forcing the polymer melt through an **annular (circular) die**. The die consists of a central mandrel and an outer ring; the gap between them determines the parison wall thickness. The extruded tube hangs vertically (or is guided downward) into the open mould, which then closes around it for the inflation step.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** process in detail with a neat process flow diagram. Discuss the role of the **parison programming** technique. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** and the **hoop stress** developed in an inflated parison. A parison of outer diameter 50 mm is blown into a mould of internal diameter 150 mm at an inflation pressure of 0.8 MPa. The parison wall thickness is 2 mm. Calculate the **BUR** and the **hoop stress** developed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM) is a continuous thermoplastic process used to manufacture hollow parts such as bottles, containers, and fuel tanks.

**Process Flow (in words — to be drawn in the answer script):**
1. Polymer resin (typically HDPE, PP, or PVC) is fed from a hopper into a heated extruder barrel.
2. A rotating screw plasticizes and conveys the melt forward.
3. The melt is forced through an **annular die** at the end of the extruder, forming a continuous hollow tube called the **parison**.
4. The mould closes around the parison, pinching the bottom (or top, depending on the design) to seal one end.
5. A **blow pin** injects compressed air (0.3 – 1.0 MPa) into the open end of the parison.
6. The parison expands and conforms to the mould cavity's internal surface.
7. The polymer cools in contact with the chilled mould wall (typically 20 – 50 °C) until it solidifies.
8. The mould opens, the part is ejected, and excess flash is trimmed and recycled.

**Role of Parison Programming:**
Parison programming is a **real-time thickness control technique** used to compensate for two key defects:
- **Parison Sag:** Gravity causes the extruded molten tube to stretch and thin unevenly along its length.
- **Non-uniform cooling:** Different regions of the mould experience different cooling rates.

A **programmable die** (with a moving mandrel or die ring driven by a hydraulic actuator) dynamically adjusts the die gap during extrusion. The parison is extruded **thicker at the top and thinner at the bottom** to counteract sag, ensuring that the final blown part has **uniform wall thickness** and minimal material waste.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Blow-Up Ratio Derivation**
The BUR is the dimensionless ratio of mould diameter to parison diameter:

$$BUR = \dfrac{D_{mould}}{D_{parison}}$$

**Step 2 — Hoop Stress Derivation**
For a thin-walled cylinder under internal pressure $P$, force equilibrium in the hoop direction gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot (2rL) &= \sigma_{hoop} \cdot (2tL) \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{mould} &= 150 \text{ mm} \\
D_{parison} &= 50 \text{ mm} \\
\therefore BUR &= \dfrac{150}{50} = 3.0 \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\
P &= 0.8 \text{ MPa} = 0.8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
r &= 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m} \\
t &= 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m} \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{(0.8 \times 10^6) \times (0.025)}{0.002} \\
\sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{20000}{0.002} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Results:** BUR = 3.0 (balanced biaxial orientation); Hoop Stress = 10 MPa (within safe deformation range for HDPE).

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary 2024]
>
> **(a)** Compare **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)**, **Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)**, and **Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** based on parison source, tooling cost, neck-finish quality, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** process for PET bottles. Discuss why **biaxial orientation** is essential for the strength and clarity of PET beverage containers. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | EBM | IBM | SBM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parison Source** | Continuous extrusion through annular die | Injection moulded preform | Injection moulded preform |
| **Tooling Cost** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Neck Finish Quality** | Fair (requires post-trimming) | Excellent (no flash) | Excellent (no flash) |
| **Typical Applications** | Detergent bottles, fuel tanks, drums, industrial containers | Pharmaceutical & cosmetic bottles, small precision containers | Soft drink bottles, water bottles, edible oil containers |

**EBM** is the most economical and versatile but produces flash. **IBM** yields high-precision necks suitable for screw caps. **SBM** is the only variant that achieves molecular biaxial orientation, which is essential for clear, strong PET beverage bottles.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM) for PET Bottles — Process Steps:**

1. **Preform Injection:** A short, thick-walled test-tube-shaped preform is injection moulded from PET resin. The preform is essentially a "small version" of the final bottle with a fully formed threaded neck.
2. **Conditioning:** The preform is stored and allowed to cool to room temperature.
3. **Reheating:** The preform is fed into the stretch blow moulding station and reheated to the **orientation temperature** (95 – 110 °C for PET, above its glass transition temperature of ~ 80 °C).
4. **Axial Stretching:** A **stretch rod** is inserted into the preform and moves axially, stretching it longitudinally (typically 2.0 – 2.5× its original length).
5. **Hoop Inflation:** High-pressure air (3 – 4 MPa) is injected, expanding the preform radially (hoop direction) to fill the mould cavity.
6. **Biaxial Orientation Achieved:** The polymer chains are simultaneously aligned in the axial and hoop directions, producing a **biaxially oriented** structure.
7. **Cooling & Ejection:** The bottle is cooled in the mould, ejected, and trimmed.

**Why Biaxial Orientation is Essential for PET:**

- **Mechanical Strength:** Stretching aligns the polymer chains along the stress directions, dramatically increasing tensile strength, impact resistance, and burst pressure resistance — critical for carbonated beverage bottles (which must withstand internal CO₂ pressure of ~ 0.5 – 0.9 MPa).
- **Optical Clarity:** Biaxial orientation suppresses the formation of large crystallites that would scatter light, yielding a **transparent, glass-like** appearance.
- **Barrier Properties:** Oriented PET has improved CO₂ and O₂ barrier performance, extending beverage shelf life.
- **Weight Reduction:** Higher strength per unit mass allows the use of thinner walls, reducing material consumption and cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing the parison with the preform:** The parison (EBM) is extruded continuously, while the preform (IBM/SBM) is injection-moulded in a separate step. Examiners award zero credit if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units in numerical answers:** The BUR is dimensionless, but hoop stress MUST carry the unit (MPa or Pa).
> 3. **Omitting the role of biaxial orientation in SBM:** Simply stating "PET is stretched" is incomplete; you must explicitly mention that the chains align in **both axial AND hoop directions**, and link this to improved strength + clarity.
> 4. **Drawing the process flow without labels:** Always label each block (e.g., "Hopper", "Die", "Blow Pin") — unlabelled diagrams lose at least 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning flash and recycling in EBM:** The flash-trimming step and the recyclability of the trim are part of the standard answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this checklist as a **last-minute revision sheet** before the KTU ESE or Module Internal exam.

- [ ] **Definition:** Blow moulding = inflating a heated polymer parison/preform with compressed air inside a closed mould to make a hollow part.
- [ ] **Three Main Variants:** EBM (extrusion), IBM (injection), SBM (stretch) — know the parison source for each.
- [ ] **Blow-Up Ratio:** $BUR = D_{mould} / D_{parison}$; typical range 2:1 to 4:1; dimensionless.
- [ ] **Hoop Stress:** $\sigma_{hoop} = Pr / t$; this is the dominant stress during inflation.
- [ ] **Parison Programming:** Dynamic die-gap adjustment to compensate for sag and ensure uniform wall thickness.
- [ ] **SBM Essentials:** PET is the workhorse polymer; orientation temperature 95–110 °C; biaxial orientation gives strength + clarity + barrier.
- [ ] **Cycle Time:** Governed by cooling (Chvorinov's rule: $t_f = C(V/A)^2$); wall thickness dominates.
- [ ] **Polymers:** HDPE, PP, PVC (EBM/IBM); PET (SBM); PS and PEN also used in specialty applications.
- [ ] **Defects to know:** Parison sag, non-uniform wall thickness, flash, poor neck finish, sink marks, poor clarity (in SBM).
- [ ] **Applications:** Beverage bottles (PET, SBM), detergent & chemical containers (HDPE, EBM), fuel tanks (HDPE multi-layer, EBM), pharma bottles (IBM).
- [ ] **Pressure Range:** Inflation air pressure typically **0.3 – 1.0 MPa** for EBM/IBM; **3 – 4 MPa** for SBM.
- [ ] **Cooling Temperature:** Mould is chilled to **20 – 50 °C**; melt enters at **150 – 230 °C**.
- [ ] **Advantages of Blow Moulding:** Hollow seamless parts, high production rate, low scrap, low tooling cost (especially EBM).
- [ ] **Limitations:** Limited to hollow parts; wall thickness control is challenging in EBM; high initial investment in SBM tooling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Extrusion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Extrusion** is a continuous, high-volume thermo-mechanical polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic material (in the form of granules, pellets, or powder) is plasticized, homogenized, and pressurized inside a heated barrel by a rotating or reciprocating screw, then forced through a shaped opening called a **die** to produce a continuous profile of uniform cross-section. The extrudate is subsequently cooled, dimensionally stabilized, and either coiled or cut to length. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, extrusion is treated as a *primary shaping process* alongside injection moulding and blow moulding, and is the most common technique for producing **films, sheets, pipes, tubes, rods, fibres, wire insulation, and complex profile sections** in thermoplastics. **Key Operating Principle (KTU Definition):** - The plasticating unit (extruder) converts **solid polymer → viscoelastic melt** and delivers it at a **steady, metered volumetric flow rate (Q)** against a die back-pressure (ΔP). --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) Think of extrusion as a giant, heated **meat grinder / pasta press** combined with a **cake-icing syringe**. | Component | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Function | |---|---|---| | Hopper | Flour funnel | Gravity feed of solid pellets | | Heated Barrel | Pressure cooker wall | Melting the polymer | | Rotating Screw | Rotating meat auger | Conveying, melting, pumping | | Die | Icing nozzle / pasta die | Shapes the cross-section | | Haul-off & Cooling | Conveyor in cold room | Solidifies and pulls the product | | Cutter / Winder | Scissors / spool | Sizes the final product | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition Pump:** You push dough through a star-shaped cookie press — the dough comes out as a continuous star-shaped strip. Polymer extrusion is the industrial, temperature-controlled, screw-driven version of exactly this concept, except the dough is replaced by molten polymer at **150 °C – 300 °C** and the pressure is regulated to within **± 2 %** for dimensional consistency. --- ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Extrusion > [!NOTE] > **Bold-Listed Core Constants/Parameters (KTU Board Favourites):** > - **L/D ratio (Barrel length to screw diameter):** typically **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** for thermoplastics, **> 30 : 1** for PVC and engineering plastics. > - **Compression ratio (CR):** feed-zone flight depth ÷ metering-zone flight depth, usually **2 : 1 to 4 : 1**. > - **Screw lead (λ):** usually **equal to 1 × D** (square pitch). > - **Melt temperature window:** **150 °C – 320 °C** depending on polymer (LDPE ≈ 160 °C, PP ≈ 220 °C, PVC ≈ 180 °C, PA66 ≈ 280 °C). > - **Die back-pressure (ΔP):** **5 – 30 MPa** typical. > - **Throughput (Q):** **10 – 2000 kg/h** industrial range. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single-Screw Extruder — Operational Logic The single-screw extruder is the workhorse of the polymer industry. Every zone of the screw performs a *distinct thermodynamic and rheological* duty: ### 2.1.1 Feed Zone (Solids Conveying) - **Length:** 2 – 8 D - **Function:** Compact and forward the solid pellets using *drag-induced conveying*. - **Mechanism:** Frictional drag between pellets and the hot barrel wall must exceed friction along the screw root (otherwise the solid bed rotates *with* the screw and no forward motion occurs — the **no-conveying condition**). - **Key Parameter:** Coefficient of friction (μ) at barrel wall — higher is better, hence barrels are internally roughened or grooved. ### 2.1.2 Compression / Melting Zone (Phase Change) - **Length:** 5 – 15 D - **Function:** Melt the polymer by *conduction from the barrel* and *mechanical shear heating*. - **Mechanism:** A **solid bed** is gradually eroded by a thin melt film; the **Tadmor melting model** describes the melt pool geometry. - **Key Parameter:** Compression ratio (CR) = h₁ / h₂ where h₁ = feed depth, h₂ = metering depth. ### 2.1.3 Metering Zone (Melt Pumping) - **Length:** 3 – 10 D - **Function:** Homogenize temperature and generate the **pressure head (ΔP)** that drives the melt through the die. - **Mechanism:** Drag flow in a deep channel treated as a **Newtonian flow between parallel plates** (the unwound screw channel). - **Key Parameter:** Metering depth (h₂) — controls the pumping capacity. ### 2.1.4 Mixing Zone (Optional) - **Function:** Disrupt laminar flow and equalize melt temperature/residence time. Devices include **Union Carbide (UC) mixers, Maddock (a.k.a. fluted) mixers, pineapple mixers, Saxton mixers**. --- ## 2.2 The Screw — Geometric Relationships The screw is a *helical flight on a cylindrical root*. The key dimensions are: - **D** = outer (flight-tip) diameter of screw - **Dᵣ** = root diameter - **h** = flight depth = (D − Dᵣ)/2 - **W** = channel width (perpendicular to flight) - **e** = flight width (land width) - **θ** = helix angle at the flight tip, given by tan θ = λ/(πD) - **λ** = axial lead of the screw (axial advance per turn) > [!IMPORTANT] > For a **square-pitch screw**, λ = πD, giving θ = 45° at the tip. This is the KTU design baseline. --- ## 2.3 Classification of Extrusion (KTU Board Classification) | Type | Drive | Polymer State | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Single-screw extruder** | Rotating screw | Thermoplastics | Films, pipes, sheets | | **Twin-screw extruder (co-/counter-rotating)** | Twin intermeshing screws | PVC, compounds, reactive blending | Compounding, profile, rigid PVC | | **Ram extruder** | Hydraulic piston | PTFE, UHMWPE, thermosets | PTFE rods, sintering preforms | | **Co-extruder** | Multiple extruders in series | Multilayer polymers | Multilayer films/sheets (food packaging) | | **Reactive extruder** | Twin-screw | Monomer + catalyst in melt | Bulk polymerisation / grafting | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table below, absolute value and modulus are written as `\vert ... \vert` to prevent breaking the markdown pipe syntax. | # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Variables / Units | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Helix angle (θ)** | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi D}$ | D (m), λ (m) | Screw geometry | | 2 | **Square-pitch condition** | $\lambda = \pi D \;\Rightarrow\; \theta = 45^{\circ}$ | — | Standard screw | | 3 | **Compression ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{h_{\text{feed}}}{h_{\text{meter}}}$ | h in m | Melting efficiency | | 4 | **L/D ratio** | $L/D$ | dimensionless | Quality indicator | | 5 | **Drag-flow (Newtonian, metering zone)** | $Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N \, h \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2}$ | Q (m³/s), N (rev/s), h (m) | Forward pumping | | 6 | **Pressure-flow** | $Q_p = -\dfrac{\pi D \, h^{3} \, \Delta P}{12 \, \eta \, L_{m}}\sin^{2}\theta$ | ΔP (Pa), η (Pa·s), Lₘ (m) | Back-pressure loss | | 7 | **Net throughput (Newtonian)** | $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ | m³/s | **Most asked KTU formula** | | 8 | **Apparent shear rate (Newtonian channel)** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h}\cos\theta$ | s⁻¹ | Die design | | 9 | **Power-law index correction (Power-law fluid)** | $Q_{d,\text{PL}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(2n+1)}{3n}$ | n = power-law index | Non-Newtonian correction | | 10 | **Die-swell (extrudate swell ratio)** | $B = \dfrac{D_{\text{ext}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$ | dimensionless | Quality control | | 11 | **Specific energy consumption** | $SEC = \dfrac{P}{Q\,\rho}$ (kWh/kg) | P = power, ρ = density | Energy efficiency | | 12 | **Volumetric flow through a die (Poiseuille, circular)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$ | R (m), L (m) | Pipe/tube die | | 13 | **Volumetric flow (slit die)** | $Q = \dfrac{W\, h^{3} \Delta P}{12\,\eta\, L}$ | W = width, h = gap | Sheet/film die | | 14 | **Residence time** | $\bar{t}_{res} = \dfrac{V_{\text{free}}}{Q}$ | s | Thermal degradation check | | 15 | **Melt-fracture onset (critical shear rate)** | $\dot{\gamma}_{c} \approx 100$–$1000$ s⁻¹ for HDPE | s⁻¹ | Processing window | --- ## 2.5 Why and How — The Engineering Logic Behind the Steps 1. **Drag-flow (Q_d)** is what the rotating screw *pushes* forward — it is **proportional to N and h**, the two levers an operator can change. 2. **Pressure-flow (Q_p)** is the back-leakage the die pressure *forces backwards* through the flight clearance — it is **proportional to ΔP/η**. 3. The **net throughput Q = Q_d − Q_p** is independent of die pressure *only* if we treat the polymer as Newtonian; in real thermoplastics (pseudoplastic), the exponent `n` enters, as shown in formula #9. 4. **Increasing die resistance (lower die land length or smaller orifice)** raises ΔP, which *reduces Q* (screw characteristic curve meets die characteristic curve at the operating point — the classical graphical method taught at KTU). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Q_d − Q_p framework is the foundation of **die balancing, profile extrusion calibration, and on-line rheometry** (the extruder itself is used as a capillary rheometer by varying N and measuring ΔP). --- ## 2.6 Types of Dies (Shaping Stage) | Die Type | Product | Salient Feature | |---|---|---| | **Coathanger die** | Flat film / sheet | Equalises flow path length → uniform thickness | | **Circular / spider die** | Blown film / pipe | Mandrel supported by spider legs; melt flows radially | | **Slot die** | Cast film / sheet | Precision gap; pressure-driven uniform output | | **Crosshead die** | Wire / cable insulation | Melt is drawn at 90° to extruder axis around a wire | | **Tubing die (spider type)** | Hose, drinking straws | Internal air pressure for bore sizing | | **Profile die** | Window sections, gaskets | Complex shape, requires **die-land length tuning** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **coathanger die** (used for flat products) with **spider die** (used for annular products). Remember: *Coat-hanger is for flat sheets; Spider is for hollow tubes/films.* # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Net Throughput in the Metering Zone (Newtonian Model) > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner *expects* the student to derive the **drag-flow** term from first principles. Below is the complete, exhaustive, board-ready derivation. **No step is skipped.** ### Step 1 — Unwrap the Helical Channel The screw channel is a *rectangular trough* cut into a cylinder. If we cut the cylinder along a line parallel to the flights and flatten it, we obtain a **rectangular channel** of: - width **W** (perpendicular to flight tip) - length **π D N t** = Lₘ (metering length along the helix unwound) - depth **h** (perpendicular to barrel inner wall) The barrel inner surface moves relative to the screw root at a velocity **v_b = π D N** tangentially. We resolve this into the channel: - **down-channel component:** v_dx = π D N cos θ - **cross-channel component:** v_dy = π D N sin θ ### Step 2 — Apply the Newtonian Viscosity Law For steady, fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid between two parallel plates separated by **h**, with the upper plate moving at **V = π D N cos θ**, the velocity profile in the down-channel direction is: $$ u(z) = \frac{\Delta P}{\eta L_{m}} \, z\,(h - z) \; + \; V \left( 1 - \frac{z}{h} \right) $$ where **z** is measured from the barrel wall (z = 0 at the wall, z = h at the screw root). The first term is the **Poiseuille (pressure) component**, the second is the **Couette (drag) component**. ### Step 3 — Integrate to Get Volumetric Flow Per Unit Channel Width The volumetric flow per unit width in the down-channel direction: $$ q_{\text{per width}} = \int_{0}^{h} u(z) \, dz $$ Substituting the velocity profile and integrating: $$ q_{\text{per width}} = \int_{0}^{h}\left[\frac{\Delta P}{\eta L_{m}} z(h - z) + V\!\left(1 - \frac{z}{h}\right)\right] dz $$ This gives: $$ q_{\text{per width}} = \frac{\Delta P \, h^{3}}{12 \, \eta \, L_{m}} + \frac{V\,h}{2} $$ **Conversion-logic explanation:** - The first term comes from the integral $\int_0^h z(h-z)\,dz = h^{3}/6$, divided by the denominator. - The second term is the *mean* velocity $\langle u \rangle = V/2$ (Couette parabolic-trapezoidal average) times the depth $h$. ### Step 4 — Multiply by Channel Width and Account for the Helix The volumetric flow rate in the down-channel direction is $Q_{\text{channel}} = W \cdot q_{\text{per width}}$. The down-channel flow must be projected onto the *axial* direction by the factor **sin θ** (geometry of helix). The down-channel direction makes angle **θ** with the cross-channel axis. So: $$ Q_{d} = W \sin\theta \cdot \frac{V h}{2}, \qquad Q_{p} = W \sin\theta \cdot \frac{\Delta P h^{3}}{12 \eta L_{m}} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute V and W - Drag velocity: $V = \pi D N \cos\theta$ - Channel width (perpendicular to flight): $W = \dfrac{\pi D \sin\theta - e}{\cos\theta} \approx \dfrac{\pi D \sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ if flight width $e$ is neglected. Substituting: $$ Q_{d} = \frac{\pi D \sin\theta}{\cos\theta} \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \frac{\pi D N \cos\theta}{2} \cdot h $$ Simplifying ($\sin\theta$ appears twice, $\cos\theta$ cancels once): $$ Q_{d} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin^{2}\theta \cos\theta}{2 \cos\theta} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin^{2}\theta}{2} $$ > [!NOTE] > Many textbooks (and KTU model answers) write the formula with $\sin\theta\cos\theta$ instead of $\sin^{2}\theta$. The discrepancy arises from different geometric definitions of *W*. The KTU-board-canonical form is **$Q_{d} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$**, which is the version to reproduce in the exam. ### Step 6 — Pressure-Flow Term For the metering channel, the axial pressure-flow component is: $$ Q_{p} = \frac{\pi D h^{3} \Delta P \sin^{2}\theta}{12 \eta L_{m}} $$ ### Step 7 — Net Throughput (Newtonian) $$ Q = Q_{d} - Q_{p} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D h^{3} \Delta P \sin^{2}\theta}{12 \eta L_{m}} $$ This is the **most-asked KTU derivation** for Part B questions. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Question:** A single-screw extruder has D = 60 mm, screw speed N = 60 rpm, metering depth h = 2 mm, helix angle θ = 17.65°, metering length L_m = 0.6 m. The polymer is assumed Newtonian with η = 500 Pa·s. Die back-pressure ΔP = 4 MPa. Compute (a) the drag-flow rate, (b) the pressure-flow rate, (c) the net throughput. **Given (in SI units):** - D = 0.060 m - N = 60 rpm = 1.0 rev/s - h = 0.002 m - sin θ = sin 17.65° ≈ 0.303 - cos θ = cos 17.65° ≈ 0.953 - L_m = 0.6 m - η = 500 Pa·s - ΔP = 4 × 10⁶ Pa ### Part (a) — Drag-flow rate $$ Q_{d} = \frac{\pi^{2} (0.060)^{2} (1.0) (0.002) (0.303) (0.953)}{2} $$ Step-by-step arithmetic: $$ \pi^{2} = 9.8696 $$ $$ (0.060)^{2} = 0.0036 $$ $$ 9.8696 \times 0.0036 = 0.03553 $$ $$ 0.03553 \times 1.0 = 0.03553 $$ $$ 0.03553 \times 0.002 = 7.106 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ 7.106 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.303 = 2.153 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ 2.153 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.953 = 2.052 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ 2.052 \times 10^{-5} \div 2 = 1.026 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ Therefore: $$ Q_{d} \approx 1.026 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 36.9 \;\text{cm}^{3}/\text{s} $$ ### Part (b) — Pressure-flow rate $$ Q_{p} = \frac{\pi (0.060) (0.002)^{3} (4 \times 10^{6}) (0.303)^{2}}{12 (500) (0.6)} $$ Step-by-step arithmetic: $$ \pi (0.060) = 0.1885 $$ $$ (0.002)^{3} = 8.0 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ 0.1885 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-9} = 1.508 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ (0.303)^{2} = 0.09181 $$ $$ 1.508 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.09181 = 1.385 \times 10^{-10} $$ $$ 1.385 \times 10^{-10} \times 4 \times 10^{6} = 5.539 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ 12 \times 500 \times 0.6 = 3600 $$ $$ Q_{p} = 5.539 \times 10^{-4} \div 3600 = 1.539 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ Therefore: $$ Q_{p} \approx 1.539 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 0.154 \;\text{cm}^{3}/\text{s} $$ ### Part (c) — Net throughput $$ Q = Q_{d} - Q_{p} = (1.026 \times 10^{-5}) - (1.539 \times 10^{-7}) $$ $$ Q \approx 1.011 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ For LDPE (ρ ≈ 920 kg/m³), mass throughput: $$ \dot{m} = Q \cdot \rho = 1.011 \times 10^{-5} \times 920 = 9.30 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg/s} \approx 33.5 \;\text{kg/h} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that **Q_p is only ~1.5 % of Q_d**. This shows that the metering zone of a *well-designed* screw operates almost purely as a drag pump, with die back-pressure causing only a small leakage loss — a classic KTU *commentary* line in Part B answers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Extrusion Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hopper
Solid pellets] --> B[Feed Throat
Water-cooled jacket] B --> C[Feed Zone
Solids conveying] C --> D[Compression Zone
Melting by shear and conduction] D --> E[Metering Zone
Melt pumping to high pressure] E --> F[Breaker Plate
and Screen Pack] F --> G[Adapter and
Die Body] G --> H[Die Land
Shapes extrudate] H --> I[Calibration and
Cooling Bath] I --> J[Haul-off and
Cutter or Winder] J --> K[Finished Profile
Pipe / Sheet / Film] subgraph Motors M1[Main Drive Motor
Screw rotation] -.-> C M1 -.-> D M1 -.-> E end subgraph Heaters H1[Heater Band 1] -.-> C H2[Heater Band 2] -.-> D H3[Heater Band 3] -.-> E end ``` ## 4.2 Screw-Zone Block Diagram (Geometry & Function) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Hopper Entry] --> B1[Feed Zone
Length 2 to 8 D
Deep flights
h equals 0.10 to 0.15 D] B1 --> C1[Compression Zone
Length 5 to 15 D
Tapered root
Melting takes place] C1 --> D1[Metering Zone
Length 3 to 10 D
Shallow flights
h equals 0.02 to 0.06 D] D1 --> E1[Mixing Zone Optional
Maddock or Saxton mixer
Temperature homogenisation] E1 --> F1[Die Adapter Region
Breaker plate and screen pack] B1 -.-> B2[Barrel wall temperature
Set point 1] C1 -.-> C2[Barrel wall temperature
Set point 2 higher] D1 -.-> D2[Barrel wall temperature
Set point 3 highest] ``` ## 4.3 Screw Characteristic Curve vs Die Characteristic Curve (Operating Point) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ScrewOutput S1[Drag flow Qd
independent of delta P] --> S2[Total screw curve
Qd minus Qp
slopes downward with delta P] end subgraph DieOutput D1[Die flow Qd_die
Qd_die equals delta P divided by die resistance
slopes upward with delta P] --> D2[Operating point OP
intersection of the two curves
defines Q and delta P] end S2 --> D2 ``` ## 4.4 Co-extrusion Line Architecture (Multilayer Films) ```mermaid flowchart LR E1[Extruder A
Outer skin layer] --> M1[Feedblock
or Multimanifold Die] E2[Extruder B
Core layer] --> M1 E3[Extruder C
Inner seal layer] --> M1 M1 --> C1[Combined Melt Stream
3 or more layers] C1 --> D1[Chill Roll or
Blown Film Tower] D1 --> W1[Trimming and Winding
Multilayer film roll] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers above (A, B, C, D, E, M, H, W) are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters — strictly compliant with the Mermaid compilation safeguards (no reserved keywords, no embedded markdown formatting inside quoted labels). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define extrusion. List any four products made by extrusion.** **Model Answer:** Extrusion is a continuous polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic material is plasticated, pressurised, and forced through a die to produce a continuous profile of uniform cross-section. Four products: 1. Plastic pipes and tubes 2. Polymer sheets and films 3. Wire and cable insulation 4. Window and door profiles (or fibre monofilaments) > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing only "shaping process" without mentioning *continuous* profile loses 1 mark. *Continuous* and *uniform cross-section* are the two key qualifying phrases in the KTU marking scheme. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the function of the breaker plate and screen pack in an extruder?** **Model Answer:** The breaker plate and screen pack are positioned at the end of the barrel before the die. Their functions are: 1. Convert the rotating spiral motion of the melt into a *straight axial flow*, eliminating tangential flow components (memory effect). 2. Filter out contaminants, unmelted gels, and carbonised particles, improving melt cleanliness. 3. Generate the **back-pressure ΔP** required for proper mixing and for stable output. 4. Support the screen mesh against the high melt pressure. Typically the screen pack consists of 20 – 400 mesh stainless-steel screens, sometimes preceded by a *breaker plate* with a pattern of drilled holes. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the working of a **single-screw extruder**. Explain the functions of the three functional zones of the screw. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **net volumetric throughput** of a Newtonian polymer in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder. #### Model Solution — Part A (a) **Diagram:** (Refer to the screw block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes — hopper, feed zone, compression zone, metering zone, die.) **Three Zones and their Functions:** | Zone | Length | Function | Physical Mechanism | |---|---|---|---| | **Feed Zone** | 2 – 8 D | Convey solid pellets forward | Frictional drag; barrel friction > root friction | | **Compression Zone** | 5 – 15 D | Melt the polymer; build pressure | Conducted heat + viscous dissipation; solid bed erodes into melt pool (Tadmor model) | | **Metering Zone** | 3 – 10 D | Homogenise and pump melt to die | Drag flow in a Newtonian deep channel; flow is essentially Poiseuille + Couette | **Auxiliary components:** - **Hopper:** gravity feeds pellets; often water-cooled throat to prevent premature melting. - **Heater bands:** provide external energy (3 – 5 zones, each independently controlled). - **Breaker plate + screen pack:** straightens flow and filters melt. - **Die:** shapes the extrudate. - **Cooling, haul-off, cutter:** solidifies, pulls, and sizes the product. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always state that the feed-zone mechanism is **drag-induced solids conveying** and the metering zone is **drag-flow of a viscous fluid**. These two phrases fetch 1 mark each. #### Model Solution — Part A (b) The derivation is presented in full in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student should reproduce: 1. Unwinding the helical channel into a flat rectangular trough of width W, depth h, length L_m. 2. Resolving the barrel velocity $\pi D N$ into a down-channel component $\pi D N \cos\theta$. 3. Writing the velocity profile $u(z)$ as the superposition of Couette and Poiseuille flows. 4. Integrating across depth h to get the volumetric flow per unit width. 5. Projecting on the axial direction using the factor $\sin\theta$. 6. Combining drag-flow and pressure-flow terms. **Final equation to be boxed in the answer book:** $$ Q = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} \; - \; \frac{\pi D h^{3} \Delta P \sin^{2}\theta}{12 \eta L_{m}} $$ --- ### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **screw characteristic curve** and **die characteristic curve**. Show graphically how the operating point of an extruder is determined. **(b) [7 Marks]** What is **die-swell (extrudate swell)**? List any four **defects** observed in extrusion and the remedies for each. #### Model Solution — Part B (a) **Screw Characteristic Curve:** - It is the graphical relationship between the **net throughput Q** of the extruder (y-axis) and the **die back-pressure ΔP** (x-axis). - Mathematically: $Q = Q_{d} - Q_{p}$, where $Q_{p} \propto \Delta P$, so the curve has a **negative slope**. - The intercept at ΔP = 0 is the **maximum drag-flow** $Q_{d}$, achieved with an open discharge (no die). **Die Characteristic Curve:** - It is the relationship between the **volumetric flow through the die** and the **pressure drop across the die**. - Mathematically: $Q = K \cdot \Delta P / \eta$ (Poiseuille law), where K is the die conductance. - The curve has a **positive slope** (linear for Newtonian, non-linear for power-law). **Operating Point:** - The two curves are plotted on the same axes; their **intersection** is the operating point (Q*, ΔP*). - It is the unique combination of throughput and pressure at which the screw pumping and die resistance are balanced. - Used by process engineers to predict the effect of changing **screw speed N** (shifts screw curve up) or changing the **die** (changes the die-curve slope). #### Model Solution — Part B (b) **Die-swell (Extrudate Swell or Barus Effect):** It is the phenomenon in which the diameter of the polymer extrudate emerging from the die is *larger* than the die diameter. It is quantified as the **swell ratio B = D_ext / D_die**. - It arises from the **elastic recovery (recoil) of the viscoelastic melt** upon leaving the die, plus the rearrangement of velocity profile. - Higher B is favoured by: higher shear rate, longer die-land length, higher molecular weight, broader MWD, lower temperature. **Four Defects and Remedies:** | # | Defect | Cause | Remedy | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sharkskin** (small-amplitude periodic ripples on the surface) | Wall slip at the die land, extensional stress at exit | Use processing aids (e.g., fluoroelastomers in LLDPE), raise die temperature, use landless die | | 2 | **Melt fracture** (gross, helical distortion) | Critical shear rate exceeded; elastic instability | Lower shear rate (larger die land), raise melt temperature, use lower-MFR grade | | 3 | **Die-lip build-up** (stringy deposits on die exit) | Degraded polymer or additive migration | Use proper stabilisers, polish die land, periodic cleaning | | 4 | **Voids or bubbles in extrudate** | Moisture, volatile degradation products, or air entrapment | Pre-dry polymer (e.g., PA, PET), vent the barrel, tighten hopper seal | | 5 | **Bambooing / surging** (diameter variations along extrudate) | Insufficient pressure; starved feed; partial melting | Increase L/D ratio, use grooved feed throat, control temperature profile | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Die-swell):** Students often confuse **sharkskin** (small ripples, periodic) with **melt fracture** (gross helical gross distortion). They are *different* defects with different critical shear rates — sharkskin starts at *lower* shear rate (~10 – 100 s⁻¹), melt fracture at *higher* shear rate (>500 s⁻¹). The examiner deducts 1 mark if these are merged or mis-ordered. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Loss — Question A(b) derivation:** Many students stop the derivation at the velocity profile and *forget* to project the down-channel flow onto the axial direction using $\sin\theta$. The examiner specifically looks for this projection — losing it costs 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Punch-List for KTU 2024 ESE:** - **Extrusion** = continuous, die-shaping process for thermoplastics, dominated by **single-screw extruders** (square-pitch, L/D = 20 – 30, CR = 2 – 4). - **Three zones** of the screw — **Feed (solids conveying)**, **Compression (melting)**, **Metering (pumping)**. Each has a distinct length, depth, and physics. - **Drag-flow $Q_d$** is proportional to **N, D², h, sinθ cosθ**. This is the *positive* pumping action. - **Pressure-flow $Q_p$** is proportional to **ΔP, h³ / η, sin²θ**. This is the *negative* back-leakage. - **Net throughput Q = Q_d − Q_p** — the *single most important* equation in extrusion theory. Must be reproduced from first principles in Part B. - **Screw characteristic** has a *negative* slope; **die characteristic** has a *positive* slope. The **operating point** is their intersection. - **Coathanger die** → flat film/sheet. **Spider die** → hollow/annular product. **Crosshead die** → wire coating. - **L/D ratio** governs residence time and melt uniformity. **Compression ratio** governs the melting efficiency. - **Die-swell (B)** is caused by elastic recovery of the viscoelastic melt. B > 1 always. - **Sharkskin** = low-shear, small ripples. **Melt fracture** = high-shear, gross distortion. - **Breaker plate + screen pack** straightens flow, filters melt, and provides back-pressure. - **Twin-screw extruders** are preferred for PVC, compounding, and reactive extrusion. They can be *co-rotating* or *counter-rotating*, *intermeshing* or *non-intermeshing*. - **Co-extrusion** uses multiple extruders feeding into a *feedblock* or *multimanifold die* to produce multilayer products (packaging films, barrier tubes). - **Critical operating parameters to state in any Part B answer:** melt temperature profile, screw speed N, back-pressure ΔP, throughput Q, residence time, and specific energy consumption. - **Units to remember:** Throughput in m³/s or kg/h; shear rate in s⁻¹; pressure in Pa or MPa; viscosity in Pa·s. - **Common KTU keywords to weave into answers:** *viscoelastic melt, drag flow, pressure flow, shear heating, isothermal Newtonian approximation, die land, calibration, haul-off, vent, breaker plate, gel, additive, stabiliser, processing aid.* > [!NOTE] > **Final Board Tip:** In 14-mark Part B questions, *always* include a labelled diagram (screw with three zones, or operating-point graph). A correct diagram with all labels fetches **2 – 3 free marks** even when the derivation has minor algebraic slips.

topics.title: Thermoforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermoforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: THERMOFORMING — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermoforming** is a secondary plastic processing (fabrication) technique in which a flat thermoplastic sheet (or film) is heated above its **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** for amorphous polymers, or above its **melting point ($T_m$)** for semi-crystalline polymers, until it reaches a soft, rubbery, and pliable state. The softened sheet is then forced against or over a mold by applying a pressure differential (vacuum, compressed air, or mechanical plug-assist), where it cools, solidifies, and retains the contour of the mold. Finally, the formed part is trimmed from the surrounding web.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-Equivalent):**
> *Thermoforming is a sheet-extrusion-based secondary shaping process in which a heat-softened polymer sheet is deformed into a three-dimensional article using vacuum, pressure, or matched molds, followed by cooling and trimming.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Idli-Maker" Logic

Imagine the South Indian culinary process of making **idlis**:

1. A liquid batter (the molten/softened polymer) is poured into a tray.
2. Steam heats the batter (analogous to the infrared/oven heater softening the polymer sheet).
3. The batter is sealed against an idli-mold's cavities (analogous to vacuum pulling the sheet into the female mold cavity).
4. After cooking/cooling, the solidified idli is removed and trimmed (analogous to trimming the finished part).

**In essence:** You take a *flat sheet*, *soften* it with heat, *push it into a shape*, *let it cool and harden*, and *cut it out*. That is the entire philosophy of thermoforming!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Injection Molding:** In injection molding, molten polymer is *injected* into a closed mold under high pressure (≈ 700–1400 bar). In thermoforming, a *pre-formed* sheet is merely *dragged* over a mold using low pressure (vacuum ≈ 0.9 atm, or compressed air ≈ 1–4 bar). This is why thermoforming is often called a **low-pressure, sheet-based** process.

### 1.3 The Six Pillars of Thermoforming (Foundation Concepts)

| # | Pillar | Core Idea |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **Sheet Stock** | Thermoplastic sheet (0.025 mm – 6.5 mm) is the raw material |
| 2 | **Heat** | IR heaters, convection, or contact heating |
| 3 | **Forming** | Vacuum, pressure, mechanical, or matched mold |
| 4 | **Cooling** | Air, water spray, or contact cooling |
| 5 | **Trimming** | Die-cutting, routing, or CNC knife cutting |
| 6 | **Mold** | Aluminum, wood, epoxy, or steel (single-sided) |

### 1.4 Common Engineering Polymers Used in Thermoforming

| Polymer | Typical $T_g$ (°C) | Typical $T_m$ (°C) | Forming Temp (°C) | Common Application |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **HIPS** (High-Impact Polystyrene) | 100 | — | 130–165 | Refrigerator liners, toys |
| **ABS** (Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene) | 105 | — | 140–175 | Luggage, automotive interior |
| **PVC** (Polyvinyl Chloride) | 80 | — | 100–150 | Blister packs, signage |
| **PET** (Polyethylene Terephthalate) | 70 | 245 | 120–160 | Trays, clamshells |
| **PMMA** (Polymethyl Methacrylate) | 105 | — | 140–180 | Aircraft canopies, displays |
| **PP** (Polypropylene) | −20 | 165 | 150–180 | Battery cases, cups |
| **PE** (Polyethylene) | −120 | 110–135 | 120–165 | Industrial liners |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The **forming temperature window** (the narrow range between the sag temperature and the degradation temperature) is a **favorite 3-mark question**. Always mention both the lower and upper limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Thermoforming Process — Stage-Wise Breakdown

The thermoforming process is operationally divided into **five sequential stages**. Each stage has its own physics, its own dominant parameters, and its own defects.

### **Stage 1: Sheet Clamping**
- The extruded thermoplastic sheet is loaded into a **clamping frame** that holds it in place.
- The frame must apply uniform pressure to prevent the sheet from sagging unevenly during heating.
- Clamping force is governed by the **gravitational sag load** of the softened sheet.
- Sag = the sheet's tendency to droop under its own weight once softened.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a *drum skin* stretched over a hoop. Before you heat the drum skin, you must *tighten the hoop* — that is the clamp.

### **Stage 2: Heating (The Softening Phase)**

The sheet is heated until it reaches an optimal forming temperature. Three heating modes are used:

| Heating Mode | Mechanism | Best For |
|--------------|-----------|----------|
| **Convection** | Hot air circulated over the sheet | Thick sheets, low-temp polymers |
| **Infrared (IR) Radiation** | Quartz/Tungsten IR lamps emit 1–4 µm wavelength | Most common; rapid & uniform |
| **Contact Heating** | Heated platens touch the sheet | Thin films, high-precision |

- The sheet is heated from **both sides simultaneously** to ensure uniform temperature distribution.
- The target temperature is **~5–10 °C below the degradation temperature** to avoid polymer breakdown.
- The polymer must be above **$T_g$** (amorphous) or just above **$T_m$** (semi-crystalline) to enter the rubbery state.

### **Stage 3: Forming (The Shaping Phase)**

This is the **heart of thermoforming**. The pliable sheet is forced into conformity with a mold using one (or a combination) of these methods:

#### A. **Vacuum Forming (Most Common)**
- A vacuum (≈ 0.08–0.09 MPa below atmospheric) is applied behind the mold.
- Atmospheric pressure (~0.1 MPa) pushes the sheet onto the mold surface.
- The sheet "snaps" into the cavity in milliseconds.

#### B. **Pressure Forming (Compressed Air)**
- Compressed air at **1–4 bar** pushes the sheet into the mold.
- Faster cycle times and sharper detail than vacuum forming.

#### C. **Mechanical Forming (Matched Dies / Plug-Assist)**
- A **male plug** physically pushes the sheet into a female cavity.
- Used for deep-draw parts (e.g., bathtubs, refrigerator liners).

#### D. **Drape Forming**
- The sheet is mechanically draped over a male mold without vacuum or pressure.
- Suited for simple hemispherical shapes.

### **Stage 4: Cooling**
- The formed part is allowed to cool below its $T_g$ (or $T_m$) to retain shape.
- Cooling methods: ambient air, forced air, water mist, or chilled mold.
- Typical cooling time = 50–70% of total cycle time.

### **Stage 5: Trimming**
- The formed part is separated from the surrounding flange (web).
- Methods: **die cutting, routing, laser cutting, or CNC knife cutting**.

---

## 2.2 Mold Types — Male vs Female

| Feature | **Female (Negative) Mold** | **Male (Positive) Mold** |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| Shape | Cavity (depression) | Protrusion (boss) |
| Part Outside | Matches mold surface | Matches mold surface |
| Wall Thickness | Thicker at base, thinner at top | Thicker at top, thinner at base |
| Detail | Sharp detail on outer surface | Sharp detail on inner surface |
| Examples | Tubs, containers | Display cases, helmets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wall-thickness distribution** is THE most asked concept in KTU exams. **Female molds** produce parts **thinner at the top (corners)**, while **male molds** produce parts **thinner at the base.**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Tensile stress in sheet under vacuum | Pa |
| 2 | $P_{atm} - P_{vac} = \Delta P$ | Driving pressure differential for vacuum forming | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta P \approx 0.9 \text{ atm} \approx 9 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$ | Typical vacuum differential | Pa |
| 4 | $t_{cool} \propto \dfrac{s^2}{\alpha}$ | Cooling time ∝ square of sheet thickness (s) | s |
| 5 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity of polymer | m²/s |
| 6 | $T_{form} = T_g + (10 \text{ to } 30)$ | Forming temperature for amorphous polymer | °C |
| 7 | $T_{form} = T_m + (5 \text{ to } 15)$ | Forming temperature for semi-crystalline polymer | °C |
| 8 | $h_{final} = h_{initial} \times \dfrac{A_{sheet}}{A_{part}}$ | Wall-thickness (mass conservation) | mm |
| 9 | $D_{draw} = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | Draw ratio (depth-to-width) | — |
| 10 | $\text{Strain} = \ln\left(\dfrac{L_{final}}{L_{initial}}\right)$ | True strain during stretching | — |
| 11 | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_{sheet} - T_{mold})}{s}$ | Heat transfer through sheet (Fourier) | W |
| 12 | $\text{Sag} \propto \dfrac{\rho g L^4}{E s^3}$ | Sag deflection (bending plate theory) | m |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Door panels, dashboards, bumper covers.
- **Packaging:** Blister packs, clamshells, food trays.
- **Medical:** Disposable trays, prosthesis housings.
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft interior panels (PP, PEI sheets).
- **Construction:** Bathtubs, shower stalls, skylights (PMMA, ABS).
- **Refrigeration:** Door liners (HIPS — *the most mass-produced thermoformed part in the world*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wall-Thickness Distribution (Mass Conservation)

When a flat sheet of initial thickness $s_0$ is drawn into a shaped mold, the **volume of polymer is conserved** (assuming negligible density change at the forming temperature). This is the single most important derivation in thermoforming.

**Given:**
- Initial sheet area = $A_0$
- Initial sheet thickness = $s_0$
- Final part area = $A_f$ (sum of the entire surface area covered by the part)
- Final (local) thickness = $s_f$

**Step 1:** Volume of polymer before forming.

$$
V_{initial} = A_0 \times s_0
$$

**Step 2:** Volume of polymer after forming (assuming the polymer fills the mold surface).

$$
V_{final} = A_f \times s_f
$$

**Step 3:** Apply conservation of mass (incompressibility assumption).

$$
A_0 \times s_0 = A_f \times s_f
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for final thickness.

$$
s_f = s_0 \times \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}
$$

**Step 5:** Express in terms of draw ratio.

$$
D = \dfrac{H}{W} \quad \text{(depth-to-width ratio)}
$$

$$
s_f \approx \dfrac{s_0}{1 + D}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The deeper the draw, the thinner the walls. This is why **deep-draw thermoforming** requires **thicker starting sheets** and often **plug-assist pre-stretching** to equalize thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooling Time for a Thermoplastic Sheet

Cooling is dominated by **conduction through the sheet thickness** and **convection at the mold surface**. A simplified lumped-capacity model:

**Step 1:** Fourier's Law of heat conduction through the sheet.

$$
Q = \dfrac{k A (T_{sheet} - T_{mold})}{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Heat removed to drop sheet temperature from $T_i$ to $T_f$.

$$
Q_{removed} = m \, c_p \, (T_i - T_f) = \rho \, V \, c_p \, (T_i - T_f)
$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two (steady-state balance).

$$
\dfrac{k A (T_{sheet} - T_{mold})}{s} = \rho \, A \, s \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT}{dt}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange and integrate.

$$
\dfrac{dT}{T_{sheet} - T_{mold}} = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p \, s^2} \, dt
$$

**Step 5:** Introduce thermal diffusivity $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$.

$$
\int_{T_i}^{T_f} \dfrac{dT}{T - T_{mold}} = -\dfrac{\alpha}{s^2} \int_0^{t_{cool}} dt
$$

**Step 6:** Solve the integral.

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{T_f - T_{mold}}{T_i - T_{mold}}\right) = -\dfrac{\alpha \, t_{cool}}{s^2}
$$

**Step 7:** Final closed-form expression for cooling time.

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{T_i - T_{mold}}{T_f - T_{mold}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cooling time scales as the **square of the sheet thickness**. Doubling the sheet thickness **quadruples** the cooling time — a key reason thick parts have long cycle times.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Draw Ratio (Vacuum Forming Limit)

The maximum depth a part can be drawn by vacuum alone is limited by the **atmospheric pressure driving force** vs. the **sheet's sag resistance**.

**Step 1:** Driving force per unit area = atmospheric pressure.

$$
F_{drive} = P_{atm} \approx 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2:** Sheet's resistance to stretching (stress at break).

$$
\sigma_{yield} \approx (10 \text{ to } 50) \times 10^6 \text{ Pa for softened thermoplastics}
$$

**Step 3:** The maximum depth of draw, $H_{max}$, is empirically given by:

$$
H_{max} \approx 0.5 \times W \times \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\sigma_{yield}}}
$$

where $W$ is the smaller mold dimension.

**Step 4:** Plug this into a numerical example. For a 200 mm wide mold with $\sigma_{yield} = 25$ MPa:

$$
H_{max} = 0.5 \times 0.2 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.013 \times 10^5}{25 \times 10^6}}
$$

$$
H_{max} = 0.1 \times \sqrt{4.052 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
H_{max} = 0.1 \times 0.0637 \approx 6.37 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
H_{max} \approx 6.4 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is why **pure vacuum forming** is limited to **shallow parts** (draw ratios < 0.5). For deeper parts, **plug-assist** or **pressure forming** must be used.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Defect Type

| Process Parameter | Defect if Too Low | Defect if Too High |
|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Sheet temperature | Incomplete forming, wrinkles, bridging | Sag, webbing, polymer degradation |
| Vacuum level | Poor detail, unfilled corners | Sheet rupture, excessive thinning |
| Mold temperature | Poor surface replication, warping | Long cycle, sticking to mold |
| Clamping force | Sheet slippage, uneven stretch | Sheet tearing at frame |
| Heating time | Cold spots, brittle fracture | Burn-through, oxidation |
| Cooling rate | Distortion on ejection | High residual stress, sink marks |
| Sheet thickness | Excessive thinning, puncture | Long cycle, poor detail |
| Draw ratio | — | Bottom thinning, webbing |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis: Thermoforming vs. Injection Molding

| Parameter | Thermoforming | Injection Molding |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Pressure | 0.1–0.4 MPa | 70–140 MPa |
| Tool cost | Low (aluminum) | High (hardened steel) |
| Lead time | Days | Weeks |
| Part size | Unlimited (limited by sheet width) | Limited by clamp tonnage |
| Wall thickness | Non-uniform | Uniform |
| Material waste | 10–30% (trim) | Minimal (sprue) |
| Cycle time | 4–60 s | 5–120 s |
| Polymer type | Thermoplastics only | Thermoplastics + thermosets |
| Production volume | Low–medium (50–100,000/yr) | High (>100,000/yr) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thermoforming Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete operational flow** of a thermoforming line, including the five canonical stages and the major sub-systems involved at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Extrusion or Pre-fab Stock] --> B[Stage 1 Sheet Clamping Frame]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Heating Module IR or Convection]
    C --> D[Temperature Sensor Pyrometer]
    D --> E{Is Sheet at Forming Temperature?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Stage 3 Forming Station]
    F --> G[Vacuum Pump Sub-system]
    F --> H[Compressed Air Sub-system]
    F --> I[Mechanical Plug-assist]
    G --> J[Mold Cavity Female or Male]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Stage 4 Cooling Module]
    K --> L[Stage 5 Trimming Station]
    L --> M[Finished Part]
    L --> N[Recycled Scrap Web]

    subgraph HeatingZone [HEATING ZONE]
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph FormingZone [FORMING ZONE]
        F
        G
        H
        I
        J
    end

    subgraph FinishingZone [FINISHING ZONE]
        K
        L
    end
```

## 4.2 Vacuum Forming — Sequence of Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step A Clamp Sheet] --> S2[Step B Heat to Forming Temp]
    S2 --> S3[Step C Seal Sheet Over Mold]
    S3 --> S4[Step D Apply Vacuum]
    S4 --> S5[Step E Sheet Conforms to Mold]
    S5 --> S6[Step F Cool Part Below Tg]
    S6 --> S7[Step G Release Vacuum]
    S7 --> S8[Step H Eject Formed Part]
    S8 --> S9[Step I Trim Flange]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Choosing a Thermoforming Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Thermoforming Method] --> Q1{Part Depth H vs Width W?}
    Q1 -- Draw Ratio Less Than 0.5 --> A1[Use Vacuum Forming]
    Q1 -- Draw Ratio 0.5 to 1.0 --> Q2{Detail Level Required?}
    Q1 -- Draw Ratio Greater Than 1.0 --> A2[Use Plug-assist or Matched Mold]

    Q2 -- Standard Detail --> A3[Use Pressure Forming]
    Q2 -- Sharp Edges and Texture --> A4[Use Pressure plus Plug-assist]

    A1 --> End[Optimal Method Selected]
    A2 --> End
    A3 --> End
    A4 --> End
```

## 4.4 Mold Comparison — Female vs Male Cavity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEMALE [FEMALE MOLD NEGATIVE CAVITY]
        F1[Sheet Heated and Sealed Over Cavity] --> F2[Vacuum Pulls Sheet Downward]
        F2 --> F3[Sheet Contacts Cavity Walls]
        F3 --> F4[Outer Surface is Detailed]
        F4 --> F5[Wall Thinner at Top Corners]
    end

    subgraph MALE [MALE MOLD POSITIVE BOSS]
        M1[Sheet Heated and Sealed Over Protrusion] --> M2[Vacuum Pulls Sheet Over Boss]
        M2 --> M3[Sheet Contacts Boss Surface]
        M3 --> M4[Inner Surface is Detailed]
        M4 --> M5[Wall Thinner at Base Corners]
    end
```

## 4.5 Heat Flow Through the Sheet — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR[IR Heater 1 micron to 4 micron] -- Radiation --> Top[Top Surface of Sheet]
    Top -- Conduction --> Mid[Sheet Core]
    Mid -- Conduction --> Bot[Bottom Surface]
    Bot -- Convection --> Mold[Mold Surface]
    Mold -- Water Channels --> Coolant[Chilled Coolant 15 deg C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for KTU Students:** Every Mermaid block above is a *visual answer* to a likely 7-mark question. For instance, Section 4.4 directly answers: *"With a neat sketch, differentiate between male and female molds in thermoforming."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define thermoforming. Mention any four thermoplastic materials commonly processed by this technique.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermoforming is a secondary plastic processing technique in which a pre-extruded thermoplastic sheet is heated to its forming temperature, made pliable, forced against a mold using vacuum, pressure, or mechanical means, and then cooled and trimmed to the desired shape.

Four common thermoplastic materials used are:
1. **HIPS** (High-Impact Polystyrene) — refrigerator liners.
2. **ABS** (Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene) — luggage shells.
3. **PVC** (Polyvinyl Chloride) — blister packaging.
4. **PET** (Polyethylene Terephthalate) — food trays.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional Question]**
**List the five main stages of the thermoforming process in the correct sequence.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Sheet clamping
2. Heating (to forming temperature)
3. Forming (vacuum/pressure/mechanical)
4. Cooling (below $T_g$ or $T_m$)
5. Trimming (removal of excess web)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Answer **any one full question** from this module. Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Total: 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **vacuum forming** process. Discuss the role of vacuum pressure, sheet temperature, and mold material. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare **male and female molds** in thermoforming. How does the choice of mold affect wall-thickness distribution? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Vacuum Forming — Block Diagram Explanation:**

| Step | Process Detail |
|------|---------------|
| 1 | A thermoplastic sheet is clamped in a holding frame. |
| 2 | IR heaters raise the sheet to a temperature **10–30 °C above $T_g$** (for amorphous polymers). |
| 3 | The heated sheet is positioned over a **female mold cavity**. |
| 4 | A vacuum pump evacuates the air between the sheet and the mold. |
| 5 | Atmospheric pressure (~**0.1 MPa**) pushes the sheet into the cavity. |
| 6 | The sheet conforms to the mold surface. |
| 7 | Cooling solidifies the part below $T_g$. |
| 8 | The vacuum is released, and the part is ejected and trimmed. |

**Role of Process Parameters:**
- **Vacuum Pressure:** A higher vacuum (≈ 0.09 MPa) gives sharper detail and faster forming; insufficient vacuum causes poor replication and bridging.
- **Sheet Temperature:** Must be in the rubbery state — too cold → brittle fracture; too hot → sag and degradation.
- **Mold Material:** Aluminum (most common — good conductivity, machinable), wood (cheap prototypes), epoxy (low-volume production), or steel (high-volume).

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

| Feature | Female (Negative) Mold | Male (Positive) Mold |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Geometry | Has a cavity (depression) | Has a protrusion (boss) |
| Part outside | Conforms to cavity | Conforms to boss |
| Detail | Outer surface has detail | Inner surface has detail |
| Wall thickness | **Thinner at top, thicker at base** | **Thinner at base, thicker at top** |
| Application | Tubs, containers, trays | Display cases, helmets |

**Wall-Thickness Logic (Mass Conservation):**
$$
s_f = s_0 \times \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}
$$

In a **female mold**, the polymer must stretch more to reach the top corners, so $A_f$ is greater there → $s_f$ decreases. In a **male mold**, the polymer must stretch more to wrap around the base of the boss → $s_f$ decreases at the base.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write *"thickness is uniform in thermoforming"* — this is **wrong**. Thermoforming inherently produces **non-uniform wall thickness** because the polymer stretches unevenly. Marks will be deducted if you say the contrary.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** What are the major **process parameters** that affect the quality of thermoformed products? Explain any four with their effect on final part quality. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of thermoforming as a polymer processing technique. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Major Process Parameters:**

1. **Sheet Temperature:**
   - Too low → incomplete forming, cracks, poor detail.
   - Too high → sag, degradation, webbing.
   - **Optimum:** 10–30 °C above $T_g$ (amorphous) or 5–15 °C above $T_m$ (semi-crystalline).

2. **Vacuum Level (or Pressure):**
   - Insufficient vacuum → unfilled corners, poor detail.
   - Excessive vacuum → sheet rupture, excessive thinning.
   - Typical vacuum: **0.08–0.09 MPa** below atmospheric.

3. **Heating Time and Method:**
   - IR radiation is preferred for speed and uniformity.
   - Uneven heating → warping, residual stress.
   - **Heating cycle:** typically 30–60% of total cycle.

4. **Mold Temperature:**
   - Cold mold → poor surface replication, warping on ejection.
   - Hot mold → better detail but longer cycle.
   - Typical: **50–80 °C** for thermoplastics like HIPS, ABS.

5. **Clamping Force:**
   - Insufficient → sheet slippage, uneven draw.
   - Excessive → sheet tearing at the frame.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Advantages:**
- Low tooling cost (aluminum molds).
- Short lead time for prototypes.
- Suitable for very large parts (e.g., bathtubs, refrigerator liners).
- Lower pressure equipment than injection molding.
- Wide material compatibility (HIPS, ABS, PVC, PET, PMMA, PP).
- Good for low-to-medium production runs.

**Limitations:**
- Non-uniform wall thickness.
- Limited to thermoplastics (not thermosets).
- High scrap rate (10–30% trimmings).
- Part detail inferior to injection molding.
- Limited to relatively shallow draws in pure vacuum forming.
- Residual stresses from cooling.

**Applications:**
- **Packaging:** Blister packs, food trays, clamshells (PET, PVC, HIPS).
- **Automotive:** Door panels, dashboards, headliners (ABS, PP).
- **Appliances:** Refrigerator liners, washing machine tops (HIPS, ABS).
- **Medical:** Sterile packaging, surgical trays (PET, PP).
- **Construction:** Bathtubs, shower stalls, skylights (PMMA, ABS).
- **Aerospace:** Interior panels (PEI, PPSU sheets).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. Confusing **primary** processes (extrusion, injection, blow molding) with **secondary** processes (thermoforming is secondary).
> 2. Stating that thermoforming is suitable for **thermosets** — **WRONG**. Thermosets cannot be re-softened.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **trimming** as a separate stage.
> 4. Drawing the diagram without labeling the **mold, vacuum port, clamp, and heater**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Thermoforming = secondary process** using **pre-extruded thermoplastic sheets** (not pellets, not melt).
- [x] **Heat the sheet** above $T_g$ (amorphous) or $T_m$ (semi-crystalline) by **10–30 °C**.
- [x] **Five stages:** Clamp → Heat → Form → Cool → Trim.
- [x] **Forming methods:** Vacuum (~0.09 MPa), Pressure (1–4 bar), Mechanical (plug-assist), Drape.
- [x] **Female mold → thinner at top corners; Male mold → thinner at base.**
- [x] **Wall thickness formula:** $s_f = s_0 \times (A_0 / A_f)$.
- [x] **Cooling time:** $t_{cool} = (s^2 / \alpha) \times \ln[(T_i - T_{mold})/(T_f - T_{mold})]$.
- [x] **Draw ratio** $D = H / W$ — for $D > 0.5$, use plug-assist or pressure forming.
- [x] **Tooling:** Aluminum is most common (cheap, machinable, good conductivity).
- [x] **Materials:** HIPS, ABS, PVC, PET, PMMA, PP, PE, PEI, PPSU.
- [x] **Applications:** Refrigerator liners (HIPS), blister packs (PVC), trays (PET), bathtubs (PMMA/ABS).
- [x] **Comparison key:** Pressure in thermoforming (0.1–0.4 MPa) ≪ Injection molding (70–140 MPa).
- [x] **Process type:** Low pressure, sheet-based, single-sided mold, thermoplastic only.
- [x] **Scrap rate:** 10–30% (trim waste is often recycled in-house).
- [x] **Cycle time:** Typically 4–60 seconds, dominated by cooling (50–70% of cycle).
- [x] **Most common defects:** Webbing, bridging, sag, thinning, wrinkles, cold spots, burn-through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *draw* a thermoforming diagram, always include these **7 labeled elements** for full marks: **(1) Sheet, (2) Clamp, (3) Heater, (4) Mold, (5) Vacuum port, (6) Vacuum pump, (7) Trimming station.** Examiners deduct up to 3 marks for missing labels!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry and Melt Spinning Methods for Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dry and Melt Spinning Methods for Fibres**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fibre Spinning?

**Fibre Spinning** is a polymer processing operation in which a viscous polymeric fluid (either a melt or a concentrated solution) is forced through a die called a *spinneret* containing one or more fine orifices, producing continuous filament(s) that are subsequently solidified by cooling or solvent evaporation and attenuated by drawing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The spinneret is the heart of any fibre spinning line. The cross-sectional shape of the orifice directly determines the cross-sectional shape of the final fibre (round, trilobal, hollow, etc.).

The three principal commercial spinning routes are:

| Method | State of Polymer | Solidification Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Melt Spinning** | Polymer melt (no solvent) | Cooling below melting point $T_m$ or glass transition $T_g$ |
| **Dry Spinning** | Polymer in volatile solvent | Solvent evaporation in a heated chamber |
| **Wet Spinning** | Polymer in non-volatile solvent | Coagulation in a chemical bath |

This module covers the **first two** of these methods in detail.

## 1.2 Dry Spinning — Formal Definition

**Dry Spinning** is a fibre formation process in which a polymer is dissolved in a volatile solvent to form a viscous *dope*, extruded through a spinneret into a heated chamber (the *spinning tower* or *cell*), where the solvent is rapidly evaporated by a counter-current or co-current flow of hot inert gas (usually air or nitrogen), leaving behind a solidified continuous filament.

**Typical polymers processed by dry spinning:** Polyacrylonitrile (PAN), cellulose acetate (acetate rayon), cellulose triacetate, modacrylic, and spandex (segmented polyurethane).

**Typical solvents used:**
- PAN: Dimethylformamide (DMF) or Dimethylacetamide (DMAc)
- Cellulose acetate: Acetone
- Spandex: DMF or dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** The word *dry* refers to the absence of any liquid coagulation bath — solidification happens purely by **mass transfer (solvent diffusion out of the filament)** rather than by heat transfer alone.

## 1.3 Melt Spinning — Formal Definition

**Melt Spinning** is a fibre formation process in which a thermoplastic polymer is heated above its melting temperature ($T_m$) (or, for amorphous polymers, well above the softening point) to form a viscous melt, metered by a precision gear pump through a spinneret into a cool air quench zone, where the molten filaments solidify by conductive and convective heat loss to the surrounding air.

**Typical polymers processed by melt spinning:** Polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyamide 6 (Nylon 6), polyamide 6,6 (Nylon 6,6), polypropylene (PP), polyethylene (PE), and most polyesters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Melt spinning is the **most economical and environmentally clean** of all spinning methods because it requires **no solvent recovery system** and is the preferred method whenever the polymer is thermally stable above its melting point.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Melt Spinning — The "Glowing Cheese Extruder" Analogy

Imagine a hot glue gun loaded with molten polymer. When you press the trigger, the viscous, glowing melt pushes out through the metal tip. As soon as the melt exits into the cool room air, the outside surface freezes into a skin. The filament is then pulled by a rotating winder (the *take-up roll*) which stretches it thinner, and by the time it reaches the winder it has cooled completely. This is **exactly** what happens in melt spinning — the polymer melt is extruded, quenched in air, and drawn.

### Dry Spinning — The "Honey Drying in a Warm Wind" Analogy

Imagine dissolving sugar in hot water to make thick syrup, then pushing the syrup through a small hole. The syrup strand is dropped into a tall chimney filled with rising hot air. As the strand falls, the hot air blows across it, the water evaporates, and what reaches the bottom of the chimney is a dry, thin, solid sugar filament. The hot air strips away the solvent, exactly as a hair dryer dries wet hair. This is **dry spinning** in essence.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick mental test:** If the polymer can be melted without decomposing → **Melt Spinning**. If the polymer decomposes before melting → use a solvent and **Dry Spinning**.

### The Two Key Physical Phenomena

| Spinning Type | Dominant Transport Mechanism | Energy Form Removed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Melt Spinning | Heat transfer (filament → air) | Sensible heat + latent heat of crystallization |
| Dry Spinning | Mass transfer (solvent → air) | Latent heat of vaporization of solvent |

This is the **fundamental thermodynamic distinction** that examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Generic Stages of Any Spinning Process

Whether dry or melt, every commercial fibre spinning line consists of the following five generic stages. Understanding these stages is essential for KTU descriptive answers.

1. **Polymer Preparation** — Melting (melt spinning) or dissolution in solvent (dry spinning) to form a processable fluid of the correct rheology.
2. **Metering and Filtration** — A precision gear pump delivers a constant volumetric throughput; the fluid is filtered to remove gels, dirt, and undissolved particles.
3. **Extrusion through Spinneret** — The fluid is forced through capillaries (typical diameter **0.05 – 0.5 mm**) to form fluid filaments.
4. **Solidification** — Heat loss (melt) or solvent loss (dry) converts the fluid filament into a solid filament.
5. **Drawing (attenuation) and Winding** — The filaments are stretched between the spinneret and a high-speed take-up roll to orient the molecules and develop tensile strength, then wound on bobbins.

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism — Melt Spinning

### 2.2.1 Sequence of Operations

The process flow is:

1. **Solid polymer chips/pellets** are fed from a hopper into the **extruder**.
2. The **screw extruder** (typically a single-screw extruder with length-to-diameter ratio $L/D = 24$ to $30$) conveys, compresses, melts, and homogenizes the polymer.
3. The melt passes through a **polymer filter** (sand pack or sintered metal) to remove contaminants.
4. A **metering gear pump** delivers a constant flow rate to the **spinneret pack**, which contains the spinneret (a stainless-steel disc with 1 to several thousand holes).
5. Molten filaments emerge downward into the **quench duct**, where a cross-flow of cool filtered air (typically $20-30 \, ^\circ C$) cools the filaments below their crystallization or glass transition temperature.
6. The solidified filaments converge at a **finish applicator** (a ceramic kiss roll applying spin finish oil), pass over **godets** (heated or unheated rollers controlling tension), and are finally wound on a **take-up winder** at speeds from $500 \, m/min$ (textile yarn) up to $6000-8000 \, m/min$ (POY — Partially Oriented Yarn for polyester and nylon).

### 2.2.2 Critical Process Parameters in Melt Spinning

- **Melt temperature:** typically $20-50 \, ^\circ C$ above $T_m$.
- **Spinneret temperature:** matched to melt temperature to avoid die-swell variations.
- **Mass throughput per hole ($Q$):** typically **0.5 – 10 g/min** per hole.
- **Take-up velocity ($V_L$):** up to $8000 \, m/min$ for industrial POY.
- **Draw-down ratio (DDR):** the ratio of take-up velocity to average extrusion velocity.
- **Quench air velocity:** $0.3 - 1.0 \, m/s$ cross-flow.

## 2.3 Detailed Mechanism — Dry Spinning

### 2.3.1 Sequence of Operations

1. **Polymer chips** are dissolved in a volatile solvent in a large stirred **dissolution tank** to form a viscous *spinning dope* (typical concentration **15 – 35 wt % polymer**, viscosity $50 - 500 \, Pa \cdot s$).
2. The dope is filtered, de-aerated (vacuum degassing to remove air bubbles that would break filaments), and pre-heated.
3. The dope is metered by a **gear pump** through a **spinneret** mounted at the top of a tall **spinning tower** (length $4 - 10 \, m$, diameter $0.3 - 1.5 \, m$).
4. Hot inert gas (typically nitrogen or air at $100-250 \, ^\circ C$) flows **co-current or counter-current** to the falling filaments.
5. As the filament falls under gravity through the tower, the solvent diffuses out of the filament surface and is carried away by the gas stream. The filament diameter shrinks progressively.
6. At the bottom of the tower, the **dry filament** passes through a **finish applicator**, over **draw rolls**, and is wound on bobbins.

### 2.3.2 Critical Process Parameters in Dry Spinning

- **Dope concentration and viscosity** — affects cohesion of the fluid jet and final fibre density.
- **Spinneret hole diameter** — typically larger than in melt spinning ($0.1 - 0.5 \, mm$) because the dope contains solvent that must evaporate.
