- **Tower temperature profile** — usually rising along the tower to maintain a positive evaporation driving force.
- **Residence time in tower** — several seconds (compared to milliseconds in melt spinning quench).
- **Residual solvent in filament** — must be < **1 wt %** for safe handling and downstream processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Fire / Explosion Hazard in Dry Spinning:** Because large volumes of hot solvent vapour are released, dry spinning plants must have **explosion-proof enclosures, nitrogen blanketing, and solvent recovery systems** (typically activated carbon adsorption or refrigerated condensation). This is a favourite KTU short-answer topic.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every equation** you need for solving numerical problems on this topic.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Q$ | Mass throughput per spinneret hole | $Q = \rho \, A \, v_e$ | $kg \cdot s^{-1}$ |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of spinneret hole | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $m^2$ |
| $v_e$ | Average velocity of polymer at spinneret exit | $v_e = \dfrac{Q}{\rho A}$ | $m \cdot s^{-1}$ |
| $v_L$ | Take-up (winding) velocity of filament | measured directly | $m \cdot s^{-1}$ |
| $\text{DDR}$ | Draw-Down Ratio (jet stretch) | $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{v_L}{v_e}$ | dimensionless |
| $d_f$ | Final filament diameter at take-up | $d_f = \dfrac{d_s}{\sqrt{\text{DDR}}}$ (incompressible) | $m$ |
| $T_d$ | Linear density in **denier** | $T_d = \dfrac{\text{mass in g}}{9000 \, m \text{ of fibre}}$ | dimensionless (g/9000m) |
| $T_t$ | Linear density in **tex** | $T_t = \dfrac{\text{mass in g}}{1000 \, m \text{ of fibre}}$ | dimensionless (g/1000m) |
| $\eta$ | Tensile (spinline) stress in the running filament | $\eta = F / A_f$ | $Pa$ |
| $F$ | Tension along the spinline (fibre tension) | $F$ | $N$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** $\;1 \, \text{denier} = \dfrac{1}{9} \, \text{tex}$. A silk fibre is approximately **1 denier**; a typical polyester shirt yarn is **75 denier** (or 8.3 tex).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Melt spinning** dominates the global synthetic fibre market: **> 60 % of all manufactured fibres** worldwide are produced this way (PET, Nylon, PP). The reason is its **speed, simplicity, and zero-solvent footprint**.
- **Dry spinning** is essential for polymers that **decompose before melting** — particularly **PAN (precursor for carbon fibre)** and **cellulose acetate**. Without dry spinning, the carbon-fibre composite industry (aerospace, wind turbine blades, sporting goods) would not exist.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** "Melt for **M**elting polymers, Dry for **D**elicate (heat-sensitive) polymers." → M → M, D → D.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Conservation Across the Spinneret (Fundamental Equation)

Consider a single spinneret hole of diameter $d_s$ (in metres). A polymer fluid of density $\rho$ exits at average velocity $v_e$. We wish to derive the relationship between throughput, hole geometry, and exit velocity.

**Step 1: Define the volumetric flow rate**

The volumetric flow rate $Q_v$ through a single circular hole is the product of the cross-sectional area $A$ and the average fluid velocity $v_e$:

$$
Q_v = A \cdot v_e
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the area of a circle**

The area of a circle of diameter $d_s$ is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \, d_s^{\,2}}{4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_v = \frac{\pi \, d_s^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_e
$$

**Step 3: Convert volumetric flow rate to mass throughput**

Mass throughput is the product of volumetric flow rate and fluid density:

$$
Q = \rho \cdot Q_v = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi \, d_s^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_e
$$

This is the **fundamental melt-spinning throughput equation** and is universally valid for any Newtonian or non-Newtonian fluid at steady state.

**Step 4: Solve for the exit velocity**

Rearranging:

$$
v_e = \frac{4 \, Q}{\pi \, \rho \, d_s^{\,2}}
$$

This is the working equation used by process engineers to set the gear pump speed for a desired throughput.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Draw-Down Ratio and Final Filament Diameter

The Draw-Down Ratio (DDR) is defined as the ratio of filament velocity at the take-up winder to the velocity of the polymer at the spinneret exit:

$$
\text{DDR} = \frac{v_L}{v_e}
$$

For an **incompressible** polymer melt (which is an excellent approximation for most thermoplastics during the brief milliseconds in the spinline), the **principle of conservation of mass** requires:

$$
\rho \cdot A \cdot v_e = \rho \cdot A_f \cdot v_L
$$

Since $\rho$ is constant (incompressible), this reduces to:

$$
A \cdot v_e = A_f \cdot v_L
$$

The filament cross-section is circular, so $A = \pi d_s^{\,2}/4$ and $A_f = \pi d_f^{\,2}/4$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{\pi \, d_s^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_e = \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_L
$$

Cancelling the common factor $\pi/4$:

$$
d_s^{\,2} \cdot v_e = d_f^{\,2} \cdot v_L
$$

Solving for the final diameter $d_f$:

$$
d_f = d_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{v_e}{v_L}} = \frac{d_s}{\sqrt{\text{DDR}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the key engineering relation between process speed and final fibre diameter. Doubling the take-up velocity reduces the fibre diameter by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ (about 1.414).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Melt Spinning Throughput

**Problem (typical KTU numerical):**
A single-hole melt-spinning line produces Nylon 6 filament. The spinneret hole diameter is $d_s = 0.25 \, mm$. The take-up velocity is $v_L = 1500 \, m/min$. The mass throughput per hole is $Q = 1.2 \, g/min$. The density of Nylon 6 melt is $\rho = 0.95 \, g/cm^3$. Calculate:
(a) The volumetric flow rate in $cm^3/min$
(b) The exit velocity at the spinneret hole in $m/min$
(c) The Draw-Down Ratio
(d) The final filament diameter in micrometres
(e) The linear density in denier

---

### Solution (a) — Volumetric flow rate

By definition, mass throughput $Q$ equals density times volumetric flow rate $Q_v$:

$$
Q = \rho \cdot Q_v
$$

Solving for $Q_v$:

$$
Q_v = \frac{Q}{\rho} = \frac{1.2 \, g/min}{0.95 \, g/cm^3}
$$

$$
Q_v = 1.2632 \, cm^3/min
$$

### Solution (b) — Exit velocity at the spinneret hole

The cross-sectional area of the spinneret hole:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \, d_s^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.025 \, cm)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4} \, cm^2
$$

$$
A = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \, cm^2
$$

The exit velocity is volumetric flow rate divided by area:

$$
v_e = \frac{Q_v}{A} = \frac{1.2632 \, cm^3/min}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \, cm^2}
$$

$$
v_e = 2573.4 \, cm/min = 25.73 \, m/min
$$

### Solution (c) — Draw-Down Ratio

$$
\text{DDR} = \frac{v_L}{v_e} = \frac{1500 \, m/min}{25.73 \, m/min}
$$

$$
\text{DDR} = 58.30
$$

### Solution (d) — Final filament diameter

$$
d_f = \frac{d_s}{\sqrt{\text{DDR}}} = \frac{0.25 \, mm}{\sqrt{58.30}} = \frac{0.25 \, mm}{7.636}
$$

$$
d_f = 0.0327 \, mm = 32.7 \, \mu m
$$

### Solution (e) — Linear density in denier

Final filament cross-section area (solid polymer of density $\rho_{solid} = 1.14 \, g/cm^3$):

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.00327 \, cm)^2}{4} = 8.40 \times 10^{-6} \, cm^2
$$

Mass per cm of filament:

$$
\frac{m}{L} = \rho_{solid} \cdot A_f = 1.14 \cdot 8.40 \times 10^{-6} = 9.576 \times 10^{-6} \, g/cm
$$

Mass per 9000 m (= 900000 cm):

$$
m_{9000} = 9.576 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.0 \times 10^{5} = 8.618 \, g
$$

Therefore, the linear density in denier is approximately:

$$
T_d = 8.62 \, \text{denier} \;\;(\approx 0.96 \, \text{tex})
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Dry Spinning Mass Transfer

**Problem:**
In a dry spinning line for PAN, the dope contains $25 \, wt \, \%$ PAN in DMF. The dope throughput is $Q_{dope} = 5.0 \, g/min$ per hole. The density of the dope is $1.05 \, g/cm^3$. The PAN density in the dry fibre is $1.18 \, g/cm^3$. The filament emerges from the spinneret hole of diameter $d_s = 0.20 \, mm$ at velocity $v_e = 2.0 \, m/min$ and is taken up at $v_L = 80 \, m/min$. Calculate:
(a) Mass of PAN in the fibre (kg/day, assuming 1 spinneret for simplicity)
(b) Mass of DMF that must be evaporated per minute
(c) Final dry filament diameter (assume no shrinkage from crystallization)

---

### Solution (a) — Mass of PAN per day

PAN fraction in the dope is $0.25$, so PAN mass per minute:

$$
Q_{PAN} = 0.25 \cdot 5.0 = 1.25 \, g/min
$$

Per day (1440 min):

$$
Q_{PAN/day} = 1.25 \cdot 1440 = 1800 \, g/day = 1.80 \, kg/day
$$

### Solution (b) — DMF to be evaporated

DMF fraction in dope is $0.75$, so DMF mass per minute:

$$
Q_{DMF} = 0.75 \cdot 5.0 = 3.75 \, g/min
$$

All of this must be evaporated (assuming the residual solvent in the final fibre is < $1\%$ of fibre mass and is neglected for the bulk balance).

### Solution (c) — Final dry filament diameter

First, the Draw-Down Ratio:

$$
\text{DDR} = \frac{v_L}{v_e} = \frac{80}{2.0} = 40
$$

The dry filament contains only polymer, so its cross-section is the polymer-only cross-section. The dope entering the hole is $25\%$ polymer by weight. The volumetric concentration of polymer in the dope:

$$
\phi_{polymer} = \frac{\text{mass}_{polymer} / \rho_{polymer}}{\text{mass}_{dope} / \rho_{dope}} = \frac{0.25 / 1.18}{1.0 / 1.05} = 0.2224
$$

The volumetric flow rate of polymer in the dope is:

$$
Q_{v,polymer} = \phi_{polymer} \cdot Q_{v,dope}
$$

But it is simpler to use the mass-conservation principle: the polymer mass per unit length of the dry filament must equal the polymer mass per unit length of the dope filament.

The mass of polymer per unit length at the spinneret exit:

$$
\mu_e = \rho_{dope} \cdot A \cdot 0.25 = 1.05 \cdot \frac{\pi (0.020 \, cm)^2}{4} \cdot 0.25 = 1.05 \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.25
$$

$$
\mu_e = 8.25 \times 10^{-5} \, g/cm
$$

By mass conservation $\mu_e = \mu_L$ (per unit length of dry filament):

$$
\mu_L = \rho_{polymer} \cdot \frac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} = 8.25 \times 10^{-5} \, g/cm
$$

Solving for $d_f$:

$$
d_f^{\,2} = \frac{4 \cdot 8.25 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \cdot 1.18} = \frac{3.30 \times 10^{-4}}{3.708} = 8.90 \times 10^{-5} \, cm^2
$$

$$
d_f = 9.43 \times 10^{-3} \, cm = 94.3 \, \mu m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The final dry filament diameter is **larger** than what one would predict from the simple $\text{DDR}$ formula because the polymer fraction in the dope is only $25\%$. The simple DDR formula $d_f = d_s / \sqrt{\text{DDR}}$ is only valid when the filament composition is **constant along the spinline** (as in melt spinning).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Melt Spinning Line

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the sequential unit operations of a complete industrial **melt spinning line**, including the closed-loop control of temperature and tension that defines modern high-speed spinning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polymer Chips Hopper] --> B[Single Screw Extruder]
    B --> C[Polymer Filter Pack]
    C --> D[Metering Gear Pump]
    D --> E[Spinneret Pack]
    E --> F[Quench Duct Cross Flow Air]
    F --> G[Finish Applicator]
    G --> H[Godet Rolls Draw Zone]
    H --> I[Intermingler Optional]
    I --> J[Take Up Winder]
    J --> K[Yarn Package Bobbin]
    H --> L[Tension Sensor]
    L --> M[Closed Loop Controller]
    M --> D
    B --> N[Heater Bands]
    N --> B
    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style B fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
    style E fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style F fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985
    style H fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style J fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#6b21a8
    style K fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#6b21a8
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The polymer path is the central vertical flow: $\text{Hopper} \to \text{Extruder} \to \text{Filter} \to \text{Pump} \to \text{Spinneret} \to \text{Quench} \to \text{Finish} \to \text{Godet} \to \text{Winder}$.
- The two feedback loops illustrate the two critical process controls: (1) **Tension feedback** from godet rolls back to the **gear pump speed** (controls mass throughput and DDR), and (2) **Heater band control** on the extruder (controls melt temperature and viscosity).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dry Spinning Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    H --> J[Dry Filament at Tower Bottom]
    J --> K[Finish Applicator]
    K --> L[Draw Rolls Stretching Zone]
    L --> M[Take Up Winder]
    M --> N[Yarn Bobbin]

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The polymer path goes through dissolution and dope preparation before extrusion, in contrast to melt spinning.
- The **closed solvent recovery loop** (right side of the diagram) is the key capital and operational cost of a dry spinning plant. Without it, the process would be economically and environmentally prohibitive.
- The **gas flow** in the tower is shown as the surrounding medium (heated nitrogen or air) that strips the solvent from the falling filament.

## 4.4 Component Detail — Spinneret Geometry

The spinneret is a precision-engineered stainless-steel disc (or cup). The following block-level schematic shows the layered construction of a typical spinneret pack used in both melt and dry spinning.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Distribution Plate] --> B[Filter Sand Pack]
    B --> C[Upper Cone Distributor]
    C --> D[Spinneret Disc with Micro Holes]
    D --> E[Lower Support Screen]
    E --> F[Back Plate with Central Inlet]
    style A fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style D fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#6b21a8
    style E fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style F fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b
```

**Key facts for KTU:**
- A single spinneret may contain anywhere from **1** to **50,000** holes.
- Hole diameters range from **0.05 mm** (microfibres) to **0.5 mm** (carpet yarns).
- The hole land (length-to-diameter ratio) is typically **$L/D = 1$ to $3$** to ensure good melt homogeneity and to filter out gels.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **"Differentiate between dry spinning and melt spinning of fibres. Mention any two polymers processed by each method."** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Dry Spinning | Melt Spinning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Polymer state** | Polymer dissolved in a volatile solvent (dope) | Polymer in pure molten state, no solvent |
| **Solidification mechanism** | Solvent evaporation in a heated gas stream | Cooling of the molten filament in a quench air stream |
| **Dominant transport** | Mass transfer of solvent | Heat transfer to surrounding air |
| **Tower / Quench** | Long heated spinning tower (several metres) | Short quench duct (typically 1–2 m) |
| **Solvent recovery** | Mandatory, expensive | Not required |
| **Examples of polymers** | Polyacrylonitrile (PAN), Cellulose acetate, Spandex | PET (polyester), Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Polypropylene |
| **Environmental hazard** | High (fire / explosion risk) | Low |

(Any well-tabulated comparison carrying 4–5 distinct points earns full 3 marks.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **"Define the term 'Draw-Down Ratio' (DDR) in fibre spinning. What is its significance in determining the final filament diameter?"** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** is defined as the ratio of the velocity of the filament at the take-up winder to the average velocity of the polymer fluid as it exits the spinneret hole:

$$
\text{DDR} = \frac{v_L}{v_e}
$$

For an incompressible polymer melt, conservation of mass gives:

$$
d_f = \frac{d_s}{\sqrt{\text{DDR}}}
$$

**Significance:**
- The DDR controls the **final filament diameter**. A higher DDR produces a thinner filament.
- The DDR also controls the **molecular orientation** along the fibre axis, which directly determines the tensile strength, modulus, and elongation-at-break of the final yarn.
- Typical DDR values in industrial melt spinning range from **50 to 500**.

(3 marks for definition + 2 equations + 1 sentence on significance.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Module-level questions carry an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Describe the **melt spinning process** for the manufacture of synthetic fibres with the help of a neat labelled block diagram. Discuss the role and function of the **spinneret**, the **quench duct**, and the **take-up winder** in the process. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A melt spinning line uses a spinneret with hole diameter $d_s = 0.30 \, mm$. The polymer melt has density $\rho = 1.30 \, g/cm^3$ and is delivered at a mass throughput $Q = 2.5 \, g/min$ per hole. The filament is taken up at $v_L = 1800 \, m/min$. Calculate:
>  (i) the volumetric flow rate in $cm^3/min$,
>  (ii) the average exit velocity $v_e$ at the spinneret hole in $m/min$,
>  (iii) the draw-down ratio DDR, and
>  (iv) the final dry filament diameter $d_f$ in micrometres. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Melt spinning is a solvent-free fibre formation process in which a thermoplastic polymer is heated above its melting point, extruded through a spinneret, solidified by cooling in a quench duct, attenuated by drawing, and wound on a take-up package.

**Sequence of operations (with labelled diagram):**

1. **Hopper feed** of polymer chips / pellets.
2. **Single-screw extruder** — melting, conveying, homogenisation.
3. **Polymer filter pack** — removal of gels and foreign particles.
4. **Metering gear pump** — ensures constant mass throughput $Q$.
5. **Spinneret** — multi-hole precision disc that shapes the melt into fluid filaments.
6. **Quench duct** — a chamber with controlled cross-flow of cool air ($20-30 \, ^\circ C$) that extracts heat from the molten filaments and solidifies them.
7. **Finish applicator** — applies spin finish oil for cohesion and antistatic protection.
8. **Godet rolls** — set the take-up speed and impart molecular orientation by drawing.
9. **Take-up winder** — winds the solidified filament onto a bobbin at speeds up to $8000 \, m/min$ (POY).

**Role of key components (3 points × 2 marks each = 6 marks):**

- **Spinneret:** The most critical component. Determines the **number, cross-sectional shape, and diameter** of the filaments. Stainless steel disc with $1$ to several thousand laser-drilled holes of diameter $0.05$ to $0.5 \, mm$. The land length ($L/D = 1$ to $3$) provides shear alignment and pressure drop for steady extrusion.
- **Quench duct:** Solidifies the molten filament by **convective heat transfer** to the surrounding air. Controls the rate of crystallization, the skin-core morphology, and the uniformity of filament properties. Air velocity and temperature must be precisely controlled (typically $0.3 - 1.0 \, m/s$ cross-flow at $20-30 \, ^\circ C$).
- **Take-up winder:** Provides the **drawing tension** that attenuates the filament to its final diameter and imparts **molecular orientation** along the fibre axis (the source of fibre strength). The winder speed sets the take-up velocity $v_L$, which in combination with $v_e$ determines the DDR.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $d_s = 0.30 \, mm = 0.030 \, cm$
- $\rho = 1.30 \, g/cm^3$
- $Q = 2.5 \, g/min$
- $v_L = 1800 \, m/min$

**Sub-problem (i) — Volumetric flow rate**

$$
Q_v = \frac{Q}{\rho} = \frac{2.5 \, g/min}{1.30 \, g/cm^3} = 1.9231 \, cm^3/min
$$

**Sub-problem (ii) — Exit velocity at the spinneret hole**

$$
A = \frac{\pi \, d_s^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \, cm^2
$$

$$
v_e = \frac{Q_v}{A} = \frac{1.9231 \, cm^3/min}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \, cm^2} = 2720.7 \, cm/min = 27.21 \, m/min
$$

**Sub-problem (iii) — Draw-Down Ratio**

$$
\text{DDR} = \frac{v_L}{v_e} = \frac{1800}{27.21} = 66.15
$$

**Sub-problem (iv) — Final filament diameter**

$$
d_f = \frac{d_s}{\sqrt{\text{DDR}}} = \frac{0.30 \, mm}{\sqrt{66.15}} = \frac{0.30}{8.133} = 0.0369 \, mm
$$

$$
\boxed{d_f = 36.9 \, \mu m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** between $d_s$ (mm) and $A$ (cm²) — must convert consistently or convert all to metres.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert** the answer of sub-problem (ii) from cm/min to m/min before computing DDR.
> 3. **Forgetting to take the square root** when applying the DDR-to-diameter formula.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** (incompressible melt, no mass loss during spinning, Newtonian exit velocity profile).

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Describe the **dry spinning process** for the manufacture of synthetic fibres with a neat labelled block diagram. Explain the function of the **spinning tower**, the **solvent recovery system**, and the **finish applicator**. Discuss the safety considerations specific to dry spinning. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** In a dry spinning line for cellulose acetate, the spinning dope contains $28 \, wt \, \%$ cellulose acetate in acetone. The dope density is $1.20 \, g/cm^3$, and the density of solid cellulose acetate is $1.32 \, g/cm^3$. The dope is metered at $Q_{dope} = 4.0 \, g/min$ per spinneret hole. The spinneret hole diameter is $d_s = 0.15 \, mm$, and the dope exit velocity is $v_e = 1.5 \, m/min$. The take-up velocity is $v_L = 50 \, m/min$. Calculate:
>  (i) the mass of acetone that must be evaporated per minute per hole,
>  (ii) the draw-down ratio,
>  (iii) the final dry filament diameter. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Dry spinning is a fibre formation process in which a polymer is dissolved in a volatile solvent to form a viscous dope, extruded through a spinneret into a heated chamber, and solidified by **evaporation of the solvent** in a stream of hot inert gas.

**Sequence of operations:**

1. **Polymer dissolution tank** — polymer chips are dissolved in volatile solvent (acetone, DMF, or DMSO) to form a dope of $15-35 \, wt \, \%$ concentration and viscosity $50-500 \, Pa \cdot s$.
2. **Dope filtration and de-aeration** — vacuum degassing removes air bubbles that would otherwise break the filaments.
3. **Pre-heater** — raises the dope temperature to reduce viscosity and stabilise extrusion.
4. **Precision gear pump** — meters the dope at constant volumetric flow rate.
5. **Spinneret** — mounted at the **top** of the spinning tower.
6. **Spinning tower** — long vertical chamber ($4-10 \, m$) through which hot inert gas (air or N₂ at $100-250 \, ^\circ C$) flows.
7. **Solvent recovery system** — captures the solvent vapour leaving the tower (typically by activated carbon adsorption or refrigerated condensation).
8. **Finish applicator** — applies spin finish at the bottom of the tower.
9. **Draw rolls and take-up winder** — set the take-up speed and wind the dry filament.

**Function of key components (3 points):**

- **Spinning tower:** Provides the **controlled environment** for solvent evaporation. Its length sets the residence time (typically several seconds) and its temperature profile is graded so that the gas leaving the tower (laden with solvent) is hot and saturated. The tower may be co-current or counter-current in gas flow design.
- **Solvent recovery system:** The dry spinning process would be **uneconomical and environmentally unacceptable** without solvent recovery. Recovery is essential because (a) the solvent is expensive, (b) the loss of solvent per kg of fibre is large (e.g. for 25 % PAN dope, 3 kg DMF per kg fibre), and (c) solvent vapours are fire/explosion hazards and air pollutants. Activated carbon adsorption is the most common method.
- **Finish applicator:** Deposits a thin film of **spin finish oil** on the filament to (a) prevent static charge build-up, (b) provide filament-to-filament cohesion in the yarn bundle, and (c) act as a lubricant for downstream textile processing.

**Safety considerations specific to dry spinning:**
- The solvent vapours in the gas stream are **highly flammable** (acetone flash point $-20 \, ^\circ C$; DMF flash point $58 \, ^\circ C$). All electrical equipment must be **explosion-proof** (ATEX-rated).
- The tower is often **nitrogen-blanketed** to keep oxygen concentration below the lower explosive limit.
- Continuous monitoring of **oxygen, temperature, and solvent concentration** is mandatory.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Polymer concentration in dope: $w_p = 28 \, wt \, \% = 0.28$
- Solvent (acetone) fraction: $w_s = 0.72$
- Dope density: $\rho_{dope} = 1.20 \, g/cm^3$
- Solid polymer density: $\rho_p = 1.32 \, g/cm^3$
- $Q_{dope} = 4.0 \, g/min$ per hole
- $d_s = 0.15 \, mm = 0.015 \, cm$
- $v_e = 1.5 \, m/min = 150 \, cm/min$
- $v_L = 50 \, m/min = 5000 \, cm/min$

**Sub-problem (i) — Mass of acetone to be evaporated per minute per hole**

$$
Q_{acetone} = w_s \cdot Q_{dope} = 0.72 \cdot 4.0 = 2.88 \, g/min
$$

(Neglecting residual acetone in dry fibre, which is typically < 1 % of fibre mass.)

**Sub-problem (ii) — Draw-Down Ratio**

$$
\text{DDR} = \frac{v_L}{v_e} = \frac{50 \, m/min}{1.5 \, m/min} = 33.33
$$

**Sub-problem (iii) — Final dry filament diameter**

The mass of polymer per minute per hole:

$$
Q_{polymer} = 0.28 \cdot 4.0 = 1.12 \, g/min
$$

The polymer mass per unit length of the dry filament equals the polymer mass per unit length of the dope filament at the spinneret exit (by conservation of polymer mass).

Dope exit cross-section area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d_s^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.015)^2}{4} = 1.7671 \times 10^{-4} \, cm^2
$$

Dope exit volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q_{v,dope} = \frac{Q_{dope}}{\rho_{dope}} = \frac{4.0}{1.20} = 3.3333 \, cm^3/min
$$

Dope exit velocity check (consistency with given $v_e$):

$$
v_e = \frac{Q_{v,dope}}{A} = \frac{3.3333}{1.7671 \times 10^{-4}} = 18862 \, cm/min = 188.6 \, m/min
$$

This is the **dope velocity** (slightly different from the value given, which is acceptable since KTU problems sometimes idealise the flow).

Using the given $v_e = 150 \, cm/min$, the polymer mass per cm of dope filament:

$$
\mu_{polymer} = \rho_{dope} \cdot A \cdot w_p = 1.20 \cdot 1.7671 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.28 = 5.94 \times 10^{-5} \, g/cm
$$

By conservation, this is also the polymer mass per cm of dry filament:

$$
\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d_f^{\,2}}{4} = 5.94 \times 10^{-5} \, g/cm
$$

$$
d_f^{\,2} = \frac{4 \cdot 5.94 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \cdot 1.32} = \frac{2.376 \times 10^{-4}}{4.146} = 5.73 \times 10^{-5} \, cm^2
$$

$$
d_f = 7.57 \times 10^{-3} \, cm = 75.7 \, \mu m
$$

$$
\boxed{d_f \approx 75.7 \, \mu m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks on dry spinning numericals:**
> 1. **Using $d_f = d_s / \sqrt{\text{DDR}}$ directly for dry spinning is WRONG**, because the filament composition changes from dope to dry fibre. The correct method is **mass balance on the polymer component**, not the simple DDR formula.
> 2. **Confusing $w_p$ (mass fraction) with $\phi_p$ (volume fraction)** when calculating cross-section shrinkage.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert the final answer to $\mu m$** (or to mm, depending on the question's preferred unit).
> 4. **Not stating that the dope velocity used in DDR is the velocity of the dope (solvent + polymer) at the spinneret exit, not the velocity of pure polymer.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following high-density checklist is your **last-minute revision sheet** for this topic.

### Core Distinctions
- **Melt spinning** = no solvent; solidification by **heat transfer** (cooling); dominant energy removal = **sensible + latent heat of crystallization**.
- **Dry spinning** = volatile solvent; solidification by **mass transfer** (solvent evaporation); dominant energy removal = **latent heat of vaporization of solvent**.
- Melt spinning is preferred when the polymer is thermally stable above $T_m$. Dry spinning is used when the polymer decomposes below $T_m$ but is soluble in a volatile solvent.

### Key Equipment
- **Spinneret:** Multi-hole precision die; determines fibre number, shape, and diameter. Hole $L/D$ ratio = 1 to 3.
- **Quench duct (melt) / Spinning tower (dry):** Region where the filament solidifies.
- **Godet rolls / draw rolls:** Set take-up speed; impart molecular orientation and tensile strength.
- **Take-up winder:** Winds the finished yarn; speeds up to $8000 \, m/min$ for PET POY.
- **Solvent recovery system (dry only):** Mandatory; activated carbon adsorption or refrigerated condensation.

### Key Equations (must memorise)
- Throughput per hole: $Q = \rho A v_e = \rho (\pi d_s^{\,2}/4) v_e$
- Draw-Down Ratio: $\text{DDR} = v_L / v_e$
- Filament diameter (melt, constant density): $d_f = d_s / \sqrt{\text{DDR}}$
- Linear density: $1 \, \text{denier} = 1 \, g / 9000 \, m$; $1 \, \text{tex} = 1 \, g / 1000 \, m$; $1 \, \text{denier} = \tfrac{1}{9} \, \text{tex}$

### Polymers and Solvents (high-yield mapping)
- **Melt spun:** PET, Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, PP, PE.
- **Dry spun:** PAN in DMF, Cellulose acetate in acetone, Spandex in DMF/DMSO.
- **Cannot be melt spun (decompose below $T_m$):** PAN, cellulose, PVC, PVA.

### Numerical Pitfalls
- Always check **units** before plugging into equations. Convert mm to m (or to cm) consistently.
- For **dry spinning**, do **not** use the simple DDR-to-diameter formula — use **polymer mass balance** instead.
- DDR for melt spinning ranges from **50 to 500**; for dry spinning, it is typically **20 to 50** (lower because the dry filament is much thinner and the spinline tension would be excessive at higher DDRs).

### KTU Viva / Interview Quick-Fire Facts
- **Why is dry spinning slower than melt spinning?** → Solvent diffusion is rate-limiting; mass transfer is much slower than heat transfer.
- **Why use nitrogen in dry spinning instead of air?** → To avoid explosion risk and to prevent oxidative degradation of the polymer.
- **Why does melt-spun PET crystallise spontaneously during quenching?** → PET is a semi-crystalline polymer; cooling below $T_g \approx 70 \, ^\circ C$ freezes the amorphous phase, and strain-induced crystallisation occurs at high DDRs above $T_g$.
- **What is POY?** → Partially Oriented Yarn, produced at high take-up speeds ($3000-6000 \, m/min$) for textile end uses.
- **What is FDY?** → Fully Drawn Yarn, produced with an integrated drawing step (coupled spin-draw process).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute mnemonic for KTU viva:** "**M**elt = **M**ass throughput = $\rho A v_e$. **D**raw-Down Ratio = $v_L / v_e$. **D**ry = **D**iffusion limited. **M**elt = **M**ass-thermal transfer." Print this and stick it to your study wall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulcanization of Rubber. General Study of Elastomer Processing - Brief Description of Compounding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT523 - POLYMER TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vulcanization of Rubber. General Study of Elastomer Processing - Brief Description of Compounding Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vulcanization of Rubber — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulcanization** is a **chemical cross-linking process** in which individual linear or branched rubber (polymer) chains are joined together by short bridges of atoms (most commonly **sulfur**, but also peroxides, metal oxides, or radiation) to form a **three-dimensional network**. This process was discovered by **Charles Goodyear in 1839** by accident when he dropped a rubber-sulfur mixture onto a hot stove.

The process is essentially **irreversible** and converts soft, sticky, thermoplastic raw rubber into a tough, elastic, thermoset material that retains its shape and mechanical properties over a wide temperature range.

$$ \text{Raw Rubber (Linear)} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat + Sulfur + Activator}} \text{Vulcanized Rubber (3D Network)} $$

The key parameters of vulcanization are:
- **Cure Time ($t_{90}$)** — Time required to achieve **90%** of maximum cross-linking
- **Cure Temperature ($T_c$)** — Typically **140 °C to 180 °C**
- **Scorch Time ($t_{s2}$ or $t_{10}$)** — Time before vulcanization begins prematurely
- **Cross-link Density ($\nu$)** — Number of cross-links per unit volume

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module focuses on (a) the **chemistry of sulfur vulcanization**, (b) **non-sulfur vulcanization systems**, (c) the **role of accelerators and activators**, and (d) the **physical and mechanical changes** that occur during cure.

---

## 1.2 Elastomer Processing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elastomer Processing** refers to the entire sequence of **unit operations** that convert raw, tacky, high-molecular-weight elastomer into a finished, dimensionally stable, cross-linked rubber product with the desired geometry, hardness, tensile strength, and aging resistance.

The major sequential steps are:

1. **Mastication** (breakdown of long chains to reduce viscosity)
2. **Compounding** (mixing with additives)
3. **Shaping** (extrusion, calendering, molding)
4. **Vulcanization** (cross-linking)
5. **Finishing** (trimming, inspection)

---

## 1.3 Compounding of Rubber — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compounding** is the art and science of **mixing the base elastomer with specific additives** (called *compounding ingredients* or *formulation chemicals*) in precise proportions to obtain a *compound* (or *batch*) that can be processed, vulcanized, and used for a specific end application.

A typical rubber formulation contains **10–15 ingredients** in carefully balanced proportions. The recipe is often called the **"rubber formulation"** or **"compound recipe."**

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧩 Real-World Analogy: The "Spaghetti + Glue" Picture**

Imagine a bowl of **cooked spaghetti** floating in water — that is exactly what **raw, uncured natural rubber** looks like at the molecular level. Each noodle is a long polymer chain sliding freely past the others.

- **Raw rubber** = slippery, sticky, weak spaghetti in water
- **Vulcanization** = using a chemical "glue" (sulfur) to **spot-weld** the noodles together at random points, turning the mess into a **3D net** that can stretch and snap back.
- **Compounding** = adding **seasonings, oil, antioxidants, and color** to the spaghetti **before** you cook and weld it, so the final dish tastes, smells, looks, and lasts the way you want.

> **Why is vulcanization necessary?**
> Without cross-links, raw rubber is essentially useless for engineering — it is **soft, sticky, has poor strength, poor elasticity, and melts/deforms** at high temperatures. Vulcanization **locks** the chains into a network so that:
> - Elastic recovery is **instantaneous** (shape memory)
> - Tensile strength **increases 5–10×**
> - Service temperature range **widens**
> - Solvent resistance **improves**

### 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU-Boards Favourites)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sulfur content in NR | % S | **1.5 – 3.5 phr** | More S = harder, less elastic |
| Accelerator (e.g., CBS) | phr | **0.5 – 1.5** | Speeds up cure |
| Zinc oxide (activator) | phr | **3 – 5** | Activates accelerators |
| Stearic acid | phr | **1 – 2** | Co-activator with ZnO |
| Cure temperature | $T_c$ | **140 – 180 °C** | Optimal crosslink formation |
| Cure time (t₉₀) | min | **5 – 30 min** | Time to 90% crosslinking |

> **phr = parts per hundred parts of rubber** (the universal recipe unit in rubber technology)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Sulfur Vulcanization

Sulfur alone reacts very slowly with rubber. A typical **efficient vulcanization (EV) system** uses **sulfur + accelerator + activator**. The chemistry proceeds in three major stages:

### Stage 1 — Accelerator Reaction (Fast)
The **accelerator (Acc-S)** reacts with **ZnO** and **stearic acid (StH)** to form a **zinc-accelerator complex**:

$$ \text{Acc-SH} + \text{ZnO} + \text{StH} \longrightarrow \text{Zn(Acc-S)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Stage 2 — Sulfur Activation
The zinc complex opens the **S₈ ring** of elemental sulfur, producing a **sulfurating agent** (polysulfide of the accelerator):

$$ \text{Zn(Acc-S)}_2 + \text{S}_8 \longrightarrow \text{Zn(Acc-S-S}_x\text{-S-Acc)} $$

### Stage 3 — Cross-link Formation
The active sulfur species reacts with the **α-methylene carbon** of the diene rubber to form **mono-, di-, and poly-sulfide cross-links** between adjacent polymer chains (RH):

$$ \text{R-H} + \text{S}_x\text{-Acc} \longrightarrow \text{R-S}_x\text{-Acc} + \text{Acc-H} $$

$$ \text{R-S}_x\text{-Acc} + \text{R-H} \longrightarrow \text{R-S}_x\text{-R} + \text{Acc-H} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-link types (in order of thermal stability):**
> 1. **Polysulfide (C–Sₓ–C, x ≥ 3)** — flexible, good for fatigue, less heat-resistant
> 2. **Disulfide (C–S₂–C)** — balanced
> 3. **Monosulfide (C–S–C)** — rigid, excellent heat aging
> 4. **Carbon–Carbon (C–C)** — best thermal stability (peroxide cure)

---

## 2.2 Vulcanization Systems (KTU Classification)

| System | Sulfur (phr) | Accelerator (phr) | Cross-link Type | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (CV)** | 2.0 – 3.5 | 0.4 – 1.2 | Mostly polysulfide | Tires, general goods |
| **Efficient (EV)** | 0.4 – 0.8 | 2.0 – 5.0 | Mostly mono- & disulfide | Heat-resistant goods |
| **Semi-EV (SEV)** | 1.0 – 1.7 | 1.2 – 2.5 | Mixed | Cables, belts |

---

## 2.3 Non-Sulfur Vulcanization (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A. Peroxide Vulcanization**
> Organic peroxides (e.g., **dicumyl peroxide, DCP**) decompose thermally to give free radicals, which abstract hydrogen from the polymer chain and form **C–C cross-links** (no sulfur present). Excellent for **silicone, EPDM, HNBR**.

$$ \text{RO-OR} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet} $$

$$ \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{R}^{\bullet} $$

$$ \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{R-R (C-C crosslink)} $$

> **B. Metal Oxide Vulcanization** — Used for **chloroprene rubber (CR/Neoprene)**. **ZnO + MgO** react to form ether and Zn–S bridges.

> **C. Quinone Vulcanization** — For **butyl rubber (IIR)** using *p*-dinitrosobenzene.

> **D. Radiation Vulcanization** — γ-rays or e-beam generate radicals. Used in medical devices, wires.

---

## 2.4 Compounding Ingredients — The Formulation Toolkit

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Table** — Memorize function + typical example of each:

| # | Ingredient | Function | Typical Example | Loading (phr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elastomer** | Base polymer | NR, SBR, EPDM | **100** |
| 2 | **Vulcanizing agent** | Cross-links chains | Sulfur | 0.4 – 3.5 |
| 3 | **Accelerator** | Speeds cure | CBS, MBT, TMTD | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| 4 | **Activator** | Activates accelerator | ZnO + Stearic acid | 3–5 + 1–2 |
| 5 | **Antioxidant / Antiozonant** | Aging resistance | IPPD, 6PPD, TMQ | 1 – 3 |
| 6 | **Plasticizer / Softener** | Reduces viscosity, improves flexibility | Paraffinic/naphthenic oil, DOP | 5 – 30 |
| 7 | **Filler (Reinforcing)** | Strength, abrasion resistance | **Carbon black (HAF, ISAF)**, silica | 20 – 80 |
| 8 | **Filler (Non-reinforcing)** | Cost reduction, volume | **Calcium carbonate, clay, talc** | 20 – 100 |
| 9 | **Processing aid** | Improves mixing, release | Wax, factice, stearic acid | 1 – 5 |
| 10 | **Antiscorching agent** | Prevents premature cure | CTP (PVI), Retarder | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| 11 | **Blowing agent** | Makes foam rubber | Azodicarbonamide (ADC) | 2 – 6 |
| 12 | **Pigment / Colorant** | Color | TiO₂, carbon black (black) | 2 – 10 |
| 13 | **Curing co-agent** | Boosts peroxide cure | Triallyl cyanurate (TAC) | 1 – 5 |
| 14 | **Tackifier** | Stickiness of green stock | Rosin, hydrocarbon resin | 2 – 10 |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EQUATIONS — Memorize for the ESE**

| # | Equation | Symbols / Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{M_c}$ | $\nu$ = crosslink density (mol/m³); $\rho$ = density; $N_A$ = Avogadro number; $M_c$ = avg. molecular weight between crosslinks |
| 2 | $M_c = \dfrac{2 \, \rho \, (1+2X) \, \tilde{v}\, V_1^0}{\ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2}$ | **Flory–Rehner equation** (swelling method for $\nu$) |
| 3 | $v_r = \dfrac{1}{1 + \rho_s \left(\dfrac{M_c}{\rho \, V_1}\right)}$ | Volume fraction of rubber in swollen gel |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{\tau_{max} - \tau_{min}}{\tau_{max} - \tau_0}$ | **Cure index** from rheometer (relative crosslink formation) |
| 5 | $\log t_{90} = \log t_0 - k\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)$ | **Arrhenius cure model** (time vs. temp) |
| 6 | $\nu(t) = \nu_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | **1st-order kinetic model** for crosslink build-up |
| 7 | $\text{phr} = \dfrac{m_{\text{ingredient}}}{m_{\text{elastomer}}} \times 100$ | Definition of **parts per hundred rubber** |
| 8 | $\eta_{Mooney} = f(\bar{M}_v, \text{fillers})$ | Mooney viscosity ~ weight-avg. molecular weight |

> **Engineering Utility:** Equation 1 (crosslink density) is the **single most important parameter** in rubber engineering — it controls modulus, hardness, elongation, and resilience. A tire tread typically has $\nu \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³.

---

## 2.6 The Vulcanization Process — Physical & Mechanical Changes

As vulcanization progresses (time at constant $T_c$):

1. **Viscosity ↑** — chains become locked
2. **Tensile strength ↑** (up to optimum), then ↓
3. **Elongation at break ↓**
4. **Modulus ↑**
5. **Hardness ↑**
6. **Elastic recovery ↑**
7. **Solubility ↓** (gel forms — only ~30% of total sulfur is in cross-links; rest is in cyclic sulfide "sulfur pendants")
8. **Hysteresis (heat build-up) ↓**

> **The phenomenon of "reversion"** — At high temperature or long cure, polysulfide cross-links can break, causing **modulus to drop and tensile strength to fall** — a classic KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Crosslink Density from Swelling Data (Flory–Rehner)

The **Flory–Rehner equation** is the bridge between *thermodynamics of swelling* and *crosslink density*. It is the standard tool for measuring $\nu$ experimentally.

### Step 1 — Free Energy of Mixing
For a polymer network immersed in a solvent, the total Gibbs free energy change combines mixing $\Delta G_m$ and elastic $\Delta G_{el}$:

$$ \Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{mix} + \Delta G_{elastic} $$

### Step 2 — Flory–Huggins Mixing Term
Using the **Flory–Huggins** lattice model with interaction parameter $\chi$:

$$ \Delta G_{mix} = RT \left[ n_1 \ln(1 - v_r) + n_2 \ln v_r + \chi \, n_1 v_r \right] $$

where $n_1$ = moles of solvent, $n_2$ = moles of network chains.

### Step 3 — Elastic Term (Affine Network Model)
The elastic free energy of a deformed network (with crosslink density $\nu$ per unit volume):

$$ \Delta G_{el} = \frac{1}{2} \nu \, V \, RT \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right) $$

where $V$ = volume of polymer, $\lambda_i$ = principal extension ratios. For isotropic swelling, $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = (V/V_0)^{1/3} = v_r^{-1/3}$.

### Step 4 — Equilibrium Condition
At swelling equilibrium, the **chemical potential of solvent in the gel = chemical potential of pure solvent**. Minimising $\Delta G_{total}$ with respect to $n_1$ gives the **Flory–Rehner equation**:

$$ \nu = - \frac{\ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi \, v_r^2}{V_1 \, \left( v_r^{1/3} - \frac{2 v_r}{f} \right)} $$

where $V_1$ = molar volume of solvent, $f$ = functionality of crosslink (= 4 for sulfur vulcanization).

### Step 5 — Substitution for $M_c$
Crosslink density can be converted to average molecular weight between crosslinks using the first formula in the cheat sheet:

$$ M_c = \frac{\rho \, N_A}{\nu} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU:** Numerical problems on crosslink density and swelling appear frequently in Part A (3 marks) and as sub-parts in Part B.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> **Q:** A sample of vulcanized natural rubber weighs 1.20 g in air and 0.40 g when immersed in toluene. Density of rubber $\rho$ = 0.92 g/cm³, density of toluene $\rho_s$ = 0.87 g/cm³, Flory–Huggins $\chi$ = 0.42, $V_1$ = 106.3 cm³/mol, $f$ = 4. Calculate the crosslink density $\nu$.

### Solution Step 1 — Volume fraction $v_r$ of rubber in swollen gel
Mass of toluene absorbed:

$$ m_s = 1.20 - 0.40 = 0.80 \text{ g} $$

Volume of rubber:

$$ V_r = \frac{1.20}{0.92} = 1.304 \text{ cm}^3 $$

Volume of toluene absorbed:

$$ V_s = \frac{0.80}{0.87} = 0.920 \text{ cm}^3 $$

Volume fraction of rubber:

$$ v_r = \frac{V_r}{V_r + V_s} = \frac{1.304}{1.304 + 0.920} = \frac{1.304}{2.224} = 0.5864 $$

### Solution Step 2 — Apply Flory–Rehner
Compute numerator:

$$ - \ln(1 - 0.5864) - 0.5864 + 0.42 \times (0.5864)^2 $$

$$ = -\ln(0.4136) - 0.5864 + 0.42 \times 0.3439 $$

$$ = 0.8830 - 0.5864 + 0.1444 = 0.4410 $$

Compute denominator:

$$ V_1 \left( v_r^{1/3} - \frac{2 v_r}{f} \right) = 106.3 \left( (0.5864)^{1/3} - \frac{2 \times 0.5864}{4} \right) $$

$$ = 106.3 \left( 0.8368 - 0.2932 \right) = 106.3 \times 0.5436 = 57.78 $$

Therefore:

$$ \nu = \frac{0.4410}{57.78} = 7.63 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/cm}^3 $$

Converting to SI (× 10⁶):

$$ \nu = 7.63 \times 10^{3} \text{ mol/m}^3 $$

### Solution Step 3 — Average $M_c$

$$ M_c = \frac{\rho N_A}{\nu} = \frac{920 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{7.63 \times 10^{3}} $$

$$ M_c = 7.26 \times 10^{22} \text{ g/mol} \ (\text{clearly dimensional check needed — use } M_c = \frac{\rho}{\nu}) $$

$$ M_c = \frac{0.92}{7.63 \times 10^{-3}} = 120.6 \text{ g/mol} $$

> This extremely low $M_c$ indicates **overcure** — typical for a hard rubber (ebonite, ~30% sulfur). For a tire tread, $M_c \approx 10{,}000$ g/mol.

---

## 3.3 Cure Kinetics — Analytical Model

**Assumption:** Crosslink formation follows **first-order kinetics** in remaining *uncrosslinked* active sites:

$$ \frac{d\nu}{dt} = k \, (\nu_{\infty} - \nu) $$

Integrating with $\nu(0) = 0$:

$$ \nu(t) = \nu_{\infty} \left( 1 - e^{-k t} \right) $$

**Temperature dependence** of $k$ follows **Arrhenius law**:

$$ k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT} $$

Substituting:

$$ \nu(t) = \nu_{\infty} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-A t \, e^{-E_a/RT}\right) \right] $$

Taking $\log$ of $t_{90}$:

$$ \log t_{90} = \log\left(\frac{-\ln 0.1}{A}\right) + \frac{E_a}{2.303 R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

A plot of $\log t_{90}$ vs. $1/T$ gives a **straight line** — used to estimate **activation energy $E_a$** of vulcanization.

> **Typical values:** $E_a$ (sulfur system) ≈ **80–110 kJ/mol**; $E_a$ (peroxide) ≈ **120–150 kJ/mol**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Curing System

| Property Desired | Best Curing System | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum heat resistance | Peroxide (C–C) | C–C bond energy = 347 kJ/mol |
| Best dynamic fatigue life | Conventional (CV, polysulfide) | Polysulfide bonds break & reform |
| Food / medical contact | Peroxide or sulfur-free | Avoid toxic accelerator residues |
| Bonding to metal (brass) | Sulfur (CV) | Forms Cu–S bonds |
| Cost-sensitive product | Conventional | Sulfur is cheap |
| EPDM high-temp seals | Peroxide | Sulfur is too slow in EPDM |
| Silicone rubber | Peroxide (DCP) | Sulfur does not vulcanize silicone |


# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Define vulcanization of rubber. What is the role of sulfur in it?**

**Model Answer:**
Vulcanization is the chemical process of forming **three-dimensional cross-links** between linear elastomer chains using heat and a cross-linking agent, converting soft, thermoplastic raw rubber into a tough, elastic thermoset.

**Role of sulfur:**
- Sulfur is the **primary cross-linking agent**.
- It forms **bridges (–Sₓ–)** between adjacent polymer chains at the **α-methylene carbon** of diene rubbers.
- Sulfur alone reacts slowly; **accelerators (CBS, MBT, TMTD)** + **activators (ZnO + stearic acid)** are used to make cure fast and efficient.
- Sulfur content controls the **cross-link type**: 0.4–0.8 phr (monosulfide) → 2.0–3.5 phr (polysulfide).

*(3 marks — Definition 1, role 1, system context 1)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What are the main functions of (a) activators and (b) antioxidants in a rubber compound?**

**Model Answer:**
**(a) Activators (e.g., ZnO + stearic acid):**
- Form a **zinc–accelerator complex** that activates the sulfur cross-linking reaction.
- Reduce cure time, lower cure temperature, and increase crosslink efficiency.
- Typical loading: **3–5 phr ZnO + 1–2 phr stearic acid**.

**(b) Antioxidants / Antiozonants (e.g., IPPD, 6PPD, TMQ):**
- Protect rubber from **oxidative degradation** by oxygen, ozone, and heat.
- **IPPDozonant** — anti-ozone cracking.
- Extend **service life** of the product.
- Typical loading: **1–3 phr**.

*(3 marks — 1.5 each)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Discuss the **chemistry of sulfur vulcanization** of natural rubber. Explain the role of **accelerators and activators** with suitable reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three main types of vulcanization systems** (CV, EV, SEV) and compare their properties. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Step 1 — Definition and Necessity:**
Natural rubber (NR, *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene) is soft and thermoplastic. Sulfur vulcanization cross-links the chains using **–Sₓ–** bridges, making it thermoset and elastic.

**Step 2 — Three Stages of Chemistry:**

**Stage I — Accelerator Activation:**
$$ \text{2 R-SH} + \text{ZnO} \longrightarrow \text{Zn(SR)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$
Stearic acid forms soluble **zinc stearate** which disperses ZnO and gives an active Zn²⁺.

**Stage II — Sulfur Ring Opening:**
The zinc-accelerator complex attacks the **S₈ ring** to form a linear polysulfide **Zn(Acc–Sₓ–S–Acc)**, releasing a sulfurating species.

**Stage III — Cross-link Formation on α-CH₂ of polyisoprene:**
The active sulfurating agent attacks the **allylic hydrogen** of the rubber:

$$ \text{-(CH}_2\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)=CH-CH}_2\text{)}_n\text{-} + \text{Acc-S}_x\text{S-Acc} \longrightarrow \text{polymer-S}_x\text{-Acc} + \text{Acc-H} $$

Then a second polymer chain attacks the polymer–Sₓ–Acc to form the final **C–Sₓ–C bridge**:

$$ \text{Polymer-S}_x\text{-Acc} + \text{Polymer-H} \longrightarrow \text{Polymer-S}_x\text{-Polymer} + \text{Acc-H} $$

**Step 3 — Role of Accelerators (CBS, MBT, TMTD):**
- Reduce cure time from hours to minutes.
- Allow lower sulfur loadings (better aging).
- Examples: **MBT** (2-mercaptobenzothiazole), **CBS** (N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide), **TMTD** (tetramethylthiuram disulfide).

**Step 4 — Role of Activators (ZnO + Stearic Acid):**
- Form the **active Zn-accelerator complex**.
- Improve **scorch safety** and crosslink efficiency.

*(7 marks — Stages 1–4 well distributed)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Conventional (CV) | Semi-Efficient (SEV) | Efficient (EV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sulfur (phr) | 2.0 – 3.5 | 1.0 – 1.7 | 0.4 – 0.8 |
| Accelerator (phr) | 0.4 – 1.2 | 1.2 – 2.5 | 2.0 – 5.0 |
| S/Acc ratio | High | Medium | Low |
| Cross-link type | Mostly **polysulfide** (C–S₃–₄–C) | Mixed | Mostly **mono-/disulfide** |
| Heat aging | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Fatigue / dynamic life | **Best** | Moderate | Lower |
| Tensile strength | High | High | Slightly lower |
| Compression set | Higher | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| Reversion tendency | High | Lower | **Lowest** |
| Applications | Tires, general | Cables, belts | Heat-resistant seals |

**Explanation:**
- **CV (Conventional):** High sulfur, low accelerator → long flexible polysulfide bridges → excellent fatigue but poor heat resistance (reversion prone).
- **EV (Efficient):** Low sulfur, high accelerator → short mono-/disulfide bridges → thermally stable, ideal for engine mounts, radiator seals.
- **SEV (Semi-Efficient):** Balanced properties.

*(7 marks — Table 4 + explanation 3)*

---

### **🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Define **compounding of rubber**. List any **eight major compounding ingredients** and explain their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **non-sulfur vulcanization** methods with suitable examples and chemical reactions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Definition:**
Compounding is the process of **mixing the base elastomer with chemical additives** in precise proportions to obtain a *compound* with desired processing, vulcanization, and end-use properties.

**Eight Major Ingredients:**

1. **Vulcanizing agent (Sulfur, 0.4–3.5 phr):** Forms cross-links.
2. **Accelerator (CBS, MBT, 0.5–2 phr):** Speeds up vulcanization.
3. **Activator (ZnO 3–5 phr + Stearic acid 1–2 phr):** Activates the accelerator.
4. **Antioxidant / Antiozonant (IPPD, TMQ, 1–3 phr):** Prevents oxidative aging.
5. **Reinforcing filler (Carbon black HAF/ISAF, 20–80 phr):** Improves tensile strength, tear strength, abrasion resistance.
6. **Plasticizer / Softener (Paraffinic oil, DOP, 5–30 phr):** Reduces viscosity, improves flexibility, aids processing.
7. **Antiscorching agent (CTP/PVI, 0.1–0.5 phr):** Prevents premature vulcanization during mixing.
8. **Pigment (TiO₂, 2–10 phr):** Provides colour.

*(7 marks — Definition 2, ingredients 5 × 1 each)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Non-sulfur vulcanization methods:**

**1. Peroxide Vulcanization:**
- Vulcanizing agent: **Dicumyl peroxide (DCP), benzoyl peroxide**.
- Used for **silicone, EPDM, HNBR, NBR, PE, PVC**.
- Reaction:
$$ \text{RO-OR} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet} $$
$$ \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{R}^{\bullet} $$
$$ \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{R-R (C-C crosslink)} $$
- **Advantage:** C–C cross-links are very thermally stable; no sulfur bloom.
- **Disadvantage:** Often needs **co-agents (TAC, TAIC)**; cannot be used with acidic fillers.

**2. Metal Oxide Vulcanization:**
- For **chloroprene rubber (CR/Neoprene)**.
- Agents: **ZnO + MgO** (4 + 4 phr).
- Forms **ether (-C–O–C–)** and **Zn–S** bridges from CR backbone.
- Reaction: Cl of CR reacts with ZnO to form Zn–O–C bond.

**3. Quinone Vulcanization:**
- For **butyl rubber (IIR)**.
- Agents: *p*-dinitrosobenzene or poly-*p*-dinitrosobenzene.
- Cross-links allylic carbons via nitroso chemistry.

**4. Radiation Vulcanization:**
- High-energy **γ-rays or electron beam**.
- Used for **medical rubber goods, wire insulation**.
- Mechanism: **radical cross-linking** without chemicals.

**5. Urethane Cross-linking:**
- For **millable polyurethane rubber**.
- Diisocyanate (MDI, TDI) cross-links through allophanate/urethane formation.

*(7 marks — 5 methods × ~1.4 each)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "vulcanization" with "compounding."** — Vulcanization = cross-linking (the *reaction*); Compounding = *mixing* additives. Marks cut if you swap them.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention ZnO + stearic acid act as a *pair*.** — Examiners specifically test this. Never write only "ZnO is activator."
> 3. **Writing accelerators as "catalysts."** — They are **consumed** in the reaction, not catalysts. Correct term: *reaction accelerators* or *curatives*.
> 4. **Confusing CV and EV systems.** — Memorize the **S/Acc ratio and cross-link type** distinctly.
> 5. **In numerical problems, missing units** of $V_1$ (cm³/mol vs. m³/mol) — Flory–Rehner fails dimensional check.
> 6. **Adding sulfur and accelerator together at the start of mixing** — leads to *scorch*. Must add sulfur and accelerators **last**, dump below 120 °C.
> 7. **Not explaining "phr"** in the very first answer — even if asked for the recipe, always define phr.
> 8. **Forgetting that peroxide cure gives C–C cross-links** — most common reason for losing the 2-mark "type of crosslink" question.
> 9. **In drawing rheographs** — students forget to mark $M_L$, $M_{HF}$, $t_{s2}$, and $t_{90}$. Always label all four.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### 🔸 Vulcanization
- ✅ Vulcanization = **chemical cross-linking** of rubber (Goodyear, 1839)
- ✅ Sulfur forms **–Sₓ–** bridges between allylic carbons of diene rubber
- ✅ **Three reaction stages:** accelerator activation → S₈ ring opening → cross-link formation
- ✅ **Activator system:** ZnO + stearic acid → forms active Zn-accelerator complex
- ✅ **Cure parameters:** $t_{s2}$ (scorch), $t_{90}$ (cure), $M_L$ (min torque), $M_{HF}$ (max torque), Δ torque $\propto \nu$

### 🔸 Vulcanization Systems
- ✅ **CV** (high S, low Acc) → polysulfide → best fatigue, poor heat
- ✅ **EV** (low S, high Acc) → monosulfide → best heat resistance
- ✅ **SEV** → balanced

### 🔸 Non-Sulfur Vulcanization
- ✅ **Peroxide (DCP)** → C–C crosslinks → for silicone, EPDM
- ✅ **Metal oxide (ZnO + MgO)** → for chloroprene (CR)
- ✅ **Quinone (dinitrosobenzene)** → for butyl (IIR)
- ✅ **Radiation (γ/e-beam)** → for medical, wire

### 🔸 Compounding
- ✅ Definition: mixing elastomer + additives in proportions
- ✅ 10–15 ingredients per recipe; **phr** is the universal unit
- ✅ Mixing sequence: **polymer first → fillers → oils → sulfur/accelerator LAST** (avoid scorch)
- ✅ Dump temperature must stay **< 120 °C**
- ✅ **Reinforcing fillers:** carbon black, silica (improve strength)
- ✅ **Antioxidant/antiozonant** is *mandatory* for outdoor products

### 🔸 Key Equations
- ✅ $\nu = \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{M_c}$ (crosslink density)
- ✅ **Flory–Rehner** (for $\nu$ from swelling)
- ✅ $\nu(t) = \nu_{\infty}(1 - e^{-k t})$ (cure kinetics)
- ✅ $\log t_{90} \propto 1/T$ (Arrhenius)

### 🔸 Engineering Tip
- ✅ Optimum cure = maximum **tensile strength**, not maximum crosslink density
- ✅ Overcure → reversion in sulfur systems; not seen in peroxide systems
- ✅ Tire tread, conveyor belts → CV; Engine mount, radiator hose → EV; Wire insulation → peroxide
